$$\boxed{Fo = \frac{\alpha t}{L_c^2} = \frac{k t}{\rho c_p L_c^2}}$$

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation of the Lumped System Equation

This derivation is critical because it ties $Bi$ and $Fo$ into a single working formula.

**Step 1 — Energy balance on a lumped body.**

The rate of heat loss by convection from the surface equals the rate of decrease of internal energy:

$$-h A_s (T - T_\infty) = \rho V c_p \frac{dT}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Define dimensionless temperature.**

$$\theta = \frac{T - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T - T_\infty = \theta (T_i - T_\infty)$$

Differentiating:

$$\frac{dT}{dt} = (T_i - T_\infty) \frac{d\theta}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the energy balance.**

$$-h A_s \theta (T_i - T_\infty) = \rho V c_p (T_i - T_\infty) \frac{d\theta}{dt}$$

Cancel $(T_i - T_\infty)$:

$$-h A_s \theta = \rho V c_p \frac{d\theta}{dt}$$

**Step 4 — Separate variables and integrate.**

$$\frac{d\theta}{\theta} = -\frac{h A_s}{\rho V c_p} dt$$

Integrating from $\theta = 1$ at $t = 0$ to $\theta$ at time $t$:

$$\ln \theta \bigg|_{1}^{\theta} = -\frac{h A_s}{\rho V c_p} t \bigg|_{0}^{t}$$

$$\ln \theta - \ln 1 = -\frac{h A_s}{\rho V c_p} t$$

$$\ln \theta = -\frac{h A_s}{\rho V c_p} t$$

$$\theta = \exp\left(-\frac{h A_s}{\rho V c_p} t\right)$$

**Step 5 — Express the exponent in terms of $Bi$ and $Fo$.**

$$\frac{h A_s}{\rho V c_p} t = \frac{h}{\rho c_p (V/A_s)} t = \frac{h L_c}{k} \cdot \frac{k t}{\rho c_p L_c^2} = Bi \cdot Fo$$

**Step 6 — Final result.**

$$\boxed{\frac{T(t) - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = e^{-Bi \cdot Fo}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **master equation** for KTU Module 1 lumped capacitance problems. Once $Bi \leq 0.1$ is verified, this single equation answers 90% of the questions.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Physical Significance of $Bi$ Ranges

| Range of $Bi$ | Internal Resistance | Temperature Field | KTU Recommendation |
|---------------|---------------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| $Bi \to 0$ | Negligible | Spatially uniform | **Lumped system analysis** (perfectly valid) |
| $Bi \leq 0.1$ | Very small | Approximately uniform | **Lumped system analysis** (acceptable error < 5%) |
| $0.1 < Bi < 1$ | Moderate | Mild gradients | **Lumped system borderline** — error may exceed 5% |
| $Bi \geq 1$ | Dominant | Significant gradients | **Distributed analysis required** (series solution) |
| $Bi \to \infty$ | Infinite | Surface reaches $T_\infty$ instantly | **Surface temp. boundary condition** ($T_s = T_\infty$) |

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Physical Significance of $Fo$ Ranges

| Range of $Fo$ | Transient Stage | Physical State |
|---------------|-----------------|----------------|
| $Fo \to 0$ | Process just started | $T = T_i$ everywhere (no change yet) |
| $Fo \ll 1$ | Early transient | Heat has barely penetrated |
| $Fo \sim 1$ | Intermediate | Significant change in temperature distribution |
| $Fo \gg 1$ | Late transient | Approaching steady state |
| $Fo \to \infty$ | Steady state | No further temperature change with time |

---

## 3.6 Worked Example (KTU Typical Style)

> **Problem:** A steel ball of diameter $D = 5 \text{ cm}$, initially at $T_i = 200°\text{C}$, is suddenly quenched in a large oil bath at $T_\infty = 30°\text{C}$. The convective coefficient is $h = 500 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$. Steel properties: $k = 45 \text{ W/mK}$, $\rho = 7800 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $c_p = 480 \text{ J/kgK}$. Determine (a) whether the lumped capacitance method is applicable, and (b) the temperature of the ball after 5 minutes.

**Step 1 — Compute characteristic length.**

For a sphere: $L_c = R/3 = (0.025)/3 = 0.00833 \text{ m}$

**Step 2 — Compute Biot number.**

$$Bi = \frac{h L_c}{k} = \frac{500 \times 0.00833}{45} = \frac{4.167}{45} = 0.0926$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $Bi = 0.0926 \leq 0.1$, the lumped capacitance method is **applicable** within engineering accuracy.

**Step 3 — Compute thermal diffusivity.**

$$\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho c_p} = \frac{45}{7800 \times 480} = \frac{45}{3.744 \times 10^6} = 1.202 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Fourier number at $t = 300$ s.**

$$Fo = \frac{\alpha t}{L_c^2} = \frac{1.202 \times 10^{-5} \times 300}{(0.00833)^2} = \frac{3.606 \times 10^{-3}}{6.939 \times 10^{-5}} = 51.96$$

**Step 5 — Apply the lumped equation.**

$$\frac{T - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = e^{-Bi \cdot Fo} = e^{-0.0926 \times 51.96} = e^{-4.812}$$

$$e^{-4.812} = 0.00807$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $T$.**

$$T - 30 = 0.00807 \times (200 - 30) = 0.00807 \times 170 = 1.372$$

$$T = 31.37°\text{C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** After 5 minutes, the steel ball has cooled to within **1.37°C** of the oil temperature — practically fully equilibrated. The high $Fo = 51.96$ confirms this.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Decision Logic for Lumped System Applicability

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Given h, k, geometry, T_i, T_infty"] --> B["Compute L_c from geometry"]
    B --> C["Compute Bi = h L_c / k"]
    C --> D{"Is Bi ≤ 0.1?"}
    D -- "YES" --> E["Lumped system method VALID"]
    D -- "NO" --> F["Use one-term series approximation"]
    E --> G["Compute alpha = k / rho c_p"]
    G --> H["Compute Fo = alpha t / L_c^2"]
    H --> I["Apply theta = exp to the power of -Bi Fo"]
    I --> J["Solve for T or t as required"]
    F --> K["Refer to Heisler charts or analytical series"]
    J --> L["End: Report Final Answer"]
    K --> L
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Physical Meaning of $Bi$ and $Fo$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Input Parameters"]
        A1["Convection coefficient h"]
        A2["Thermal conductivity k"]
        A3["Geometry L_c"]
        A4["Time t"]
        A5["Material density rho"]
        A6["Specific heat c_p"]
    end
    subgraph BIOT_COMP["Biot Number Formation"]
        B1["Internal Resistance R_cond = L_c / k"]
        B2["External Resistance R_conv = 1 / h"]
        B3["Bi = R_cond / R_conv = h L_c / k"]
        B1 --> B3
        B2 --> B3
    end
    subgraph FOURIER_COMP["Fourier Number Formation"]
        C1["Thermal diffusivity alpha = k / rho c_p"]
        C2["Fo = alpha t / L_c^2"]
        C3["Dimensionless time scale"]
        C1 --> C2
        C2 --> C3
    end
    subgraph RESULT["Lumped System Result"]
        D1["Temperature ratio theta = T - T_inf / T_i - T_inf"]
        D2["theta = exp of -Bi Fo"]
        D3["Final temperature T t"]
        D1 --> D2
        D2 --> D3
    end
    INPUT --> BIOT_COMP
    INPUT --> FOURIER_COMP
    BIOT_COMP --> RESULT
    FOURIER_COMP --> RESULT
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Timeline — $Fo$ and the Progression of a Transient Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["Fo = 0: t = 0. System at initial state. T equals T_i everywhere"]
    S1["Fo much less than 1: Early transient. Heat barely penetrates surface layer"]
    S2["Fo around 1: Intermediate stage. Significant temperature change in bulk"]
    S3["Fo much greater than 1: Late transient. Approaching steady state"]
    S4["Fo approaches infinity: Steady state reached. T distribution no longer changes with t"]
    S0 --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Concept Map — Coupling of $Bi$ and $Fo$ in Lumped Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Physical Process: Object cooling or heating in a fluid"]
    P2["Bi quantifies WHETHER object behaves as lumped mass"]
    P3["Fo quantifies HOW FAR the lumped process has progressed"]
    P4["Combined: Bi times Fo = h A_s t / rho V c_p = t / tau"]
    P5["tau = thermal time constant = rho V c_p / h A_s"]
    P1 --> P2
    P1 --> P3
    P2 --> P4
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Biot number. What is its physical significance? State the condition under which lumped system analysis is valid based on the Biot number.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> 
> **Definition (1 Mark):** The Biot number is a dimensionless group defined as:
> 
> $$Bi = \frac{h L_c}{k}$$
> 
> It represents the ratio of internal conductive thermal resistance of a solid to the external convective thermal resistance at its surface.
> 
> **Physical Significance (1 Mark):** The Biot number compares the heat conducted *through* the solid body to the heat convected *away from* its surface. A low $Bi$ indicates the surface and interior temperatures are nearly equal; a high $Bi$ indicates significant temperature gradients exist within the solid.
> 
> **Lumped Validity Criterion (1 Mark):** Lumped system analysis is valid when $Bi \leq 0.1$. Under this condition, the temperature variation within the solid is less than 5% and may be neglected.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Define Fourier number. Explain its significance with the help of an example.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> 
> **Definition (1 Mark):** The Fourier number is a dimensionless time defined as:
> 
> $$Fo = \frac{\alpha t}{L_c^2}$$
> 
> It represents the ratio of the rate of heat conduction to the rate of thermal energy storage in a solid.
> 
> **Significance (2 Marks):** The Fourier number indicates *how far* a transient heat conduction process has progressed. For example, when a metal sphere is quenched in a cooling bath, $Fo \to 0$ means the quench has just begun (no temperature change yet), $Fo \sim 1$ means the transient is in its intermediate stage, and $Fo \to \infty$ means steady state has been reached (temperature no longer changes with time). Thus, $Fo$ is a universal time scale for transient conduction, independent of the material's absolute size.

---

## Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Biot and Fourier Number Problems**
> 1. **Forgetting the characteristic length formula** for the given geometry. Marks lost: ~2 marks.
> 2. **Not verifying $Bi \leq 0.1$** before applying the lumped equation. If the criterion fails, the entire solution is invalid. Marks lost: ~3 marks.
> 3. **Unit inconsistency** between $h$ ($\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$), $k$ ($\text{W/mK}$), and $L_c$ (m). Always use SI units throughout. Marks lost: ~1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing $Fo$ with $Bi$**: $Bi$ compares resistances (geometry vs convection); $Fo$ scales time (transient progression). Marks lost: ~2 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A long steel cylinder of diameter $8 \text{ cm}$ is initially at a uniform temperature of $250°\text{C}$. It is suddenly immersed in a large oil bath maintained at $40°\text{C}$. The convective heat transfer coefficient is $h = 300 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$. Steel properties are $k = 50 \text{ W/mK}$, $\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$, and $c_p = 500 \text{ J/kgK}$.

**Calculate:**
1. The characteristic length of the cylinder.
2. The Biot number and check if the lumped system analysis is applicable.
3. The Fourier number after 10 minutes.
4. The temperature of the cylinder after 10 minutes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define the following terms with proper units: **(i)** Biot number, **(ii)** Fourier number, **(iii)** Thermal diffusivity, **(iv)** Characteristic length. Explain the significance of each in transient heat conduction analysis. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Step 1 — Geometry and Characteristic Length.**

Diameter $D = 8 \text{ cm} = 0.08 \text{ m}$, so radius $R = 0.04 \text{ m}$.

For a long cylinder:

$$L_c = \frac{R}{2} = \frac{0.04}{2} = 0.02 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Biot Number.**

$$Bi = \frac{h L_c}{k} = \frac{300 \times 0.02}{50} = \frac{6}{50} = 0.12$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Check:** $Bi = 0.12 > 0.1$. The lumped system analysis is **borderline invalid**. For an exact KTU answer, we should note that the error could exceed 5%, but since the problem still asks us to proceed (as a typical KTU problem does), we will apply the lumped equation with this caveat.

**Step 3 — Thermal Diffusivity.**

$$\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho c_p} = \frac{50}{7850 \times 500} = \frac{50}{3.925 \times 10^6} = 1.274 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Fourier Number at $t = 600$ s.**

$$Fo = \frac{\alpha t}{L_c^2} = \frac{1.274 \times 10^{-5} \times 600}{(0.02)^2} = \frac{7.643 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \times 10^{-4}} = 19.11$$

**Step 5 — Temperature Calculation.**

$$\frac{T - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = e^{-Bi \cdot Fo} = e^{-0.12 \times 19.11} = e^{-2.293}$$

$$e^{-2.293} = 0.1009$$

$$T - 40 = 0.1009 \times (250 - 40) = 0.1009 \times 210 = 21.18$$

$$T = 40 + 21.18 = 61.18°\text{C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** $L_c = 0.02 \text{ m}$, $Bi = 0.12$ (borderline), $Fo = 19.11$, $T(10 \text{ min}) \approx 61.2°\text{C}$.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**(i) Biot Number (1.5 Marks):**

$$Bi = \frac{h L_c}{k} \quad \text{(dimensionless)}$$

- **Units:** Dimensionless
- **Significance:** It is the ratio of internal conduction resistance to external convection resistance. When $Bi \leq 0.1$, the temperature within the solid is spatially uniform and lumped capacitance analysis can be used.

**(ii) Fourier Number (1.5 Marks):**

$$Fo = \frac{\alpha t}{L_c^2} = \frac{k t}{\rho c_p L_c^2} \quad \text{(dimensionless)}$$

- **Units:** Dimensionless
- **Significance:** It is the dimensionless time that scales transient heat conduction. A small $Fo$ means the process is in early transient; a large $Fo$ means steady state has been reached.

**(iii) Thermal Diffusivity (2 Marks):**

$$\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho c_p} \quad [\text{m}^2/\text{s}]$$

- **Units:** $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$
- **Significance:** It measures a material's ability to conduct thermal energy relative to its ability to store thermal energy. Materials with high $\alpha$ (e.g., metals) respond quickly to temperature changes; materials with low $\alpha$ (e.g., plastics) respond slowly.

**(iv) Characteristic Length (2 Marks):**

$$L_c = \frac{V}{A_s}$$

- **Units:** m
- **Significance:** It is the ratio of the solid's volume to its surface area. It represents the typical length scale for heat conduction within the body. For standard geometries: $L_c = L$ (plane wall), $L_c = R/2$ (cylinder), $L_c = R/3$ (sphere).

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** A thin aluminum plate of thickness $L = 4 \text{ cm}$ is removed from an oven at $T_i = 180°\text{C}$ and placed in a room where the air temperature is $T_\infty = 25°\text{C}$. The convective coefficient on both faces is $h = 15 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$. Aluminum properties: $k = 200 \text{ W/mK}$, $\rho = 2700 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $c_p = 900 \text{ J/kgK}$.

**Find:**
1. The Biot number and verify the lumped system validity.
2. The time required for the plate to cool to $50°\text{C}$.
3. The Fourier number at the moment the plate reaches $50°\text{C}$.
4. The temperature of the plate after 30 minutes of cooling. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the temperature distribution inside a solid during transient heat conduction for (i) low Biot number and (ii) high Biot number. Discuss the role of the Fourier number in determining the time required to reach steady state. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Step 1 — Characteristic Length.**

For a plane wall, $L_c = L = $ half-thickness $= 0.04/2 = 0.02 \text{ m}$.

**Step 2 — Biot Number.**

$$Bi = \frac{h L_c}{k} = \frac{15 \times 0.02}{200} = \frac{0.3}{200} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0015$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $Bi = 0.0015 \ll 0.1$, the lumped system analysis is **perfectly valid** (internal gradients are completely negligible).

**Step 3 — Thermal Diffusivity.**

$$\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho c_p} = \frac{200}{2700 \times 900} = \frac{200}{2.43 \times 10^6} = 8.23 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Time Required to Cool to $50°\text{C}$.**

Apply the lumped equation:

$$\frac{T - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = e^{-Bi \cdot Fo}$$

$$\frac{50 - 25}{180 - 25} = \frac{25}{155} = 0.1613$$

Taking natural log of both sides:

$$\ln(0.1613) = -Bi \cdot Fo$$

$$-1.825 = -0.0015 \times Fo \quad \Rightarrow \quad Fo = \frac{1.825}{0.0015} = 1216.7$$

Now solve for time using $Fo = \alpha t / L_c^2$:

$$t = \frac{Fo \cdot L_c^2}{\alpha} = \frac{1216.7 \times (0.02)^2}{8.23 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{1216.7 \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}{8.23 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$t = \frac{0.4867}{8.23 \times 10^{-5}} = 5913 \text{ s} \approx 98.6 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 5 — Fourier Number at $50°\text{C}$.**

$$Fo = 1216.7 \text{ (dimensionless)}$$

**Step 6 — Temperature After 30 minutes (1800 s).**

$$Fo_{1800} = \frac{\alpha t}{L_c^2} = \frac{8.23 \times 10^{-5} \times 1800}{4 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{0.1481}{4 \times 10^{-4}} = 370.3$$

$$\frac{T - 25}{180 - 25} = e^{-0.0015 \times 370.3} = e^{-0.5555} = 0.5736$$

$$T - 25 = 0.5736 \times 155 = 88.91$$

$$T = 113.91°\text{C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Summary of Answers:** $Bi = 0.0015$ (lumped valid), $Fo = 1216.7$ at $50°\text{C}$, cooling time $\approx 98.6$ min, $T(30 \text{ min}) \approx 113.9°\text{C}$.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Sketch — Temperature Distribution in Transient Conduction (Conceptual):**

- For **low $Bi$ ($Bi \leq 0.1$)**: The temperature is essentially **uniform throughout the body** at every instant. The body behaves as a single thermal mass. Only the *boundary* between the body and the fluid shows a temperature drop (convective resistance dominates).

- For **high $Bi$ ($Bi > 0.1$)**: The temperature varies significantly *within* the body. The **interior** lags behind the surface. The center of the body is still hot while the surface has already cooled, producing a **curved temperature profile** that flattens over time.

**Role of Fourier Number in Determining Time to Steady State (3.5 Marks):**

The Fourier number represents the dimensionless time required for the transient process to progress. As $Fo \to \infty$, steady state is achieved. The time $t_{ss}$ to reach steady state is estimated by the condition $Fo \geq$ some threshold value (typically $Fo \geq 0.2$ for the lumped system to reach within $\approx 82\%$ of the final temperature). 

For a general body:

$$t_{ss} \approx \frac{Fo_{ss} \cdot L_c^2}{\alpha}$$

Since $Fo_{ss}$ depends only on the geometry and the desired accuracy, the time to steady state scales **quadratically with the characteristic length** and **inversely with thermal diffusivity**. This explains why large objects take much longer to reach thermal equilibrium than small ones, and why metals (high $\alpha$) reach steady state faster than insulators (low $\alpha$).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1: $Bi$ and $Fo$**

- ✅ **Biot Number $Bi = h L_c / k$** — ratio of internal conduction resistance to external convection resistance. **Dimensionless**.
- ✅ **Fourier Number $Fo = \alpha t / L_c^2 = k t / (\rho c_p L_c^2)$** — dimensionless time. **Dimensionless**.
- ✅ **Thermal diffusivity $\alpha = k / (\rho c_p)$** — units: $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$.
- ✅ **Characteristic length** depends on geometry: $L$ (wall), $R/2$ (cylinder), $R/3$ (sphere), $V/A_s$ (general).
- ✅ **Lumped system valid when $Bi \leq 0.1$** — error in temperature prediction < 5%.
- ✅ **Lumped system equation:** $\dfrac{T - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = e^{-Bi \cdot Fo}$.
- ✅ **Thermal time constant:** $\tau = \rho V c_p / (h A_s) = \rho c_p L_c / h$.
- ✅ **Steady state corresponds to $Fo \to \infty$** for the time-dependent problem.
- ✅ **$Fo$ scales with $L_c^2$:** Doubling the size **quadruples** the time to reach the same thermal state.
- ✅ **$Bi$ decision flow:** Compute $Bi$ first; if $Bi > 0.1$, **do not use lumped** — use series solution or Heisler charts.
- ✅ **$Fo$ decision flow:** After confirming lumped validity, compute $Fo$ to determine transient progress.
- ✅ **KTU trap:** $L_c$ is the half-thickness for a wall, **not** the full thickness!
- ✅ **KTU trap:** Surface area $A_s$ includes **all** exposed faces (e.g., both sides of a plate).
- ✅ **Engineering insight:** Materials with **low $\alpha$** (plastics, wood) change temperature slowly even if $h$ is high; **$Bi$ remains low** because of low $k$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Numerical Problems of Practical Importance on the Above Topics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Numerical Problems of Practical Importance on the Above Topics # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What are "Numerical Problems of Practical Importance" in Heat Transfer? In the context of KTU Module 1, **Numerical Problems of Practical Importance** refers to the quantitative, calculation-based engineering questions that require the application of heat transfer fundamentals to real-world industrial equipment such as **insulated pipes, furnace walls, heat exchangers, reactor walls, and storage tanks**. These problems test the student's ability to translate physical setups into mathematical models and solve for unknown rates of heat transfer, interface temperatures, insulation thickness, and overall heat transfer coefficients. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Module 1 Scope Recap:** > The numerical problems are based on the following sub-topics: > 1. Steady-state conduction (plane walls, cylinders, spheres) > 2. Composite (multi-layer) systems > 3. Thermal resistance networks (analogous to electrical circuits) > 4. Critical radius of insulation > 5. Convective heat transfer & Newton's Law of Cooling > 6. Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient ($U$) & LMTD > 7. Fouling factors ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Heat as Current" Picture Imagine heat as water flowing through a pipe network. The **driving force** is the temperature difference ($\Delta T$) — analogous to voltage. The **flow resistance** is the *thermal resistance* ($R$) — analogous to electrical resistance. The **flow rate** is the heat transfer rate ($Q$) — analogous to current. $$Q = \frac{\Delta T}{R_{\text{total}}}$$ Just as in Ohm's law, the larger the temperature difference or the smaller the resistance, the greater the heat flow. This **thermal resistance analogy** is the single most powerful tool for solving Module 1 numerical problems. > [!NOTE] > **Three Modes of Heat Transfer — At a Glance:** > - **Conduction**: Heat transfer through a solid (or stationary fluid) by molecular vibration & free electron transport. Governed by **Fourier's Law**. > - **Convection**: Heat transfer between a solid surface and a moving fluid. Governed by **Newton's Law of Cooling**. > - **Radiation**: Heat transfer by electromagnetic waves (not in numerical scope of Module 1). ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold for Emphasis) | Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value | Units | |----------|--------|----------------|-------| | **Thermal conductivity of copper** | $k$ | **385** | W/(m·K) | | **Thermal conductivity of stainless steel** | $k$ | **16** | W/(m·K) | | **Thermal conductivity of insulating brick** | $k$ | **0.20** | W/(m·K) | | **Thermal conductivity of air (at 300 K)** | $k$ | **0.026** | W/(m·K) | | **Stephan-Boltzmann constant** | $\sigma$ | $5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ | W/(m²·K⁴) | | **Typical $h$ for natural convection (air)** | $h$ | **5–25** | W/(m²·K) | | **Typical $h$ for forced convection (water)** | $h$ | **500–10,000** | W/(m²·K) | | **Typical $h$ for condensing steam** | $h$ | **5,000–15,000** | W/(m²·K) | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Foundational Laws of Module 1 ### 2.1.1 Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction (Why? How?) - **Why does it exist?** Because heat spontaneously flows from hot to cold regions within a medium, and the rate depends on how easily the medium conducts heat. - **How is it applied?** It is used as a differential equation that, upon integration over a geometry, yields the heat transfer rate. The differential form is: $$q = -k \frac{dT}{dx}$$ where $q$ is the heat flux (W/m²), $k$ is thermal conductivity, and $\frac{dT}{dx}$ is the temperature gradient. ## 2.2 The KTU Module 1 Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **MANDATORY MEMORIZATION** — These formulas cover 95% of Module 1 numerical problems. ### 2.2.1 Conduction Through a Plane Wall (Slab) | Parameter | Formula | Units | |-----------|---------|-------| | Heat flux | $q = \dfrac{k(T_1 - T_2)}{x}$ | W/m² | | Heat transfer rate | $Q = \dfrac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{x}$ | W | | Thermal resistance | $R_{\text{cond}} = \dfrac{x}{kA}$ | K/W | | Temperature at distance $x_1$ | $T_{x_1} = T_1 - \dfrac{Q \cdot x_1}{kA}$ | K or °C | ### 2.2.2 Conduction Through a Hollow Cylinder | Parameter | Formula | Units | |-----------|---------|-------| | Heat transfer rate | $Q = \dfrac{2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$ | W | | Heat flux at radius $r$ | $q_r = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi r L}$ | W/m² | | Thermal resistance | $R_{\text{cyl}} = \dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L}$ | K/W | | Interface temperature | $T_{\text{interface}} = T_1 - Q \cdot R_{\text{layer}}$ | K or °C | ### 2.2.3 Conduction Through a Hollow Sphere | Parameter | Formula | Units | |-----------|---------|-------| | Heat transfer rate | $Q = \dfrac{4\pi k (T_1 - T_2)}{\left(\dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r_2}\right)}$ | W | | Thermal resistance | $R_{\text{sph}} = \dfrac{1}{4\pi k}\left(\dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r_2}\right)$ | K/W | ### 2.2.4 Composite Walls and Cylinders (Resistance in Series) For a composite system with $n$ layers: $$R_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i$$ And the heat transfer rate is: $$Q = \frac{\Delta T_{\text{overall}}}{R_{\text{total}}}$$ ### 2.2.5 Convective Heat Transfer — Newton's Law of Cooling $$Q = h A (T_s - T_\infty)$$ where $h$ is the convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K), $T_s$ is the surface temperature, and $T_\infty$ is the bulk fluid temperature. ### 2.2.6 Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U) For a wall with convective fluids on both sides (and conduction through a single layer): $$\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x}{k} + \frac{1}{h_o}$$ For a composite wall (multi-layer): $$\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \sum \frac{x_j}{k_j} + \frac{1}{h_o}$$ With **Fouling factors** ($R_{f,i}$ and $R_{f,o}$): $$\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + R_{f,i} + \sum \frac{x_j}{k_j} + R_{f,o} + \frac{1}{h_o}$$ ### 2.2.7 Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD) For a counter-current or co-current heat exchanger: $$\Delta T_{\text{LM}} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}$$ And the heat transfer rate: $$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{\text{LM}}$$ ### 2.2.8 Critical Radius of Insulation (Cylinder) $$r_{\text{cr}} = \frac{k_{\text{ins}}}{h_o}$$ - If $r_2 < r_{\text{cr}}$: Adding insulation **increases** heat loss. - If $r_2 > r_{\text{cr}}$: Adding insulation **decreases** heat loss. ### 2.2.9 Convective Heat Transfer Coefficient Correlation (Basics) For natural convection over a vertical plate (laminar): $$\text{Nu} = 0.59 \, (\text{Gr} \cdot \text{Pr})^{1/4}$$ For forced convection in a pipe (turbulent, Dittus-Boelter): $$\text{Nu} = 0.023 \, \text{Re}^{0.8} \, \text{Pr}^{n}$$ where $n = 0.4$ for heating and $0.3$ for cooling. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why are these formulas important to industry?** > - **Furnace wall design**: Selecting refractory thickness to minimize heat loss while keeping the cold-face temperature safe. > - **Insulated steam pipe design**: Determining insulation thickness to reduce energy loss and prevent personnel burns. > - **Heat exchanger design**: Sizing the area to achieve a target heat duty using $Q = U A \Delta T_{LM}$. > - **Reactor jacket design**: Calculating the coolant flow rate to maintain a desired reaction temperature. > - **Cryogenic storage**: Choosing multi-layer insulation to minimize boil-off of LNG/LH₂. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix (Worked Numerical Problems) ## 3.1 Problem 1 — Composite Plane Wall (KTU High-Priority Type) ### Problem Statement A furnace wall is constructed of **three layers**: - **Inner layer (refractory brick):** Thickness $x_1 = 0.10$ m, $k_1 = 1.05$ W/(m·K) - **Middle layer (insulating brick):** Thickness $x_2 = 0.08$ m, $k_2 = 0.21$ W/(m·K) - **Outer layer (building brick):** Thickness $x_3 = 0.10$ m, $k_3 = 0.69$ W/(m·K) The inner surface temperature is $T_1 = 1100$ °C and the outer surface temperature is $T_4 = 40$ °C. The wall area is $A = 1$ m². Calculate: 1. The rate of heat loss per m² of wall area. 2. The interface temperatures $T_2$ and $T_3$. ### Step 1 — Identify the Geometry and Mode This is **steady-state, 1-D conduction** through a **composite plane wall** (resistances in series). The driving force is $\Delta T = T_1 - T_4 = 1100 - 40 = 1060$ °C. ### Step 2 — Compute Individual Thermal Resistances $$R_1 = \frac{x_1}{k_1 A} = \frac{0.10}{1.05 \times 1} = 0.0952 \ \text{K/W}$$ $$R_2 = \frac{x_2}{k_2 A} = \frac{0.08}{0.21 \times 1} = 0.3810 \ \text{K/W}$$ $$R_3 = \frac{x_3}{k_3 A} = \frac{0.10}{0.69 \times 1} = 0.1449 \ \text{K/W}$$ ### Step 3 — Sum the Resistances $$R_{\text{total}} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 = 0.0952 + 0.3810 + 0.1449$$ $$R_{\text{total}} = 0.6211 \ \text{K/W}$$ ### Step 4 — Apply the Heat Transfer Equation $$Q = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{R_{\text{total}}} = \frac{1060}{0.6211}$$ $$\boxed{Q = 1706.6 \ \text{W}}$$ Since $A = 1$ m², the **heat flux** is also $q = 1706.6$ W/m². ### Step 5 — Compute Interface Temperatures Under steady-state, the **same** $Q$ flows through each layer. Therefore: $$T_1 - T_2 = Q \cdot R_1 = 1706.6 \times 0.0952 = 162.5 \ \text{°C}$$ $$T_2 = T_1 - 162.5 = 1100 - 162.5 = \boxed{937.5 \ \text{°C}}$$ $$T_2 - T_3 = Q \cdot R_2 = 1706.6 \times 0.3810 = 650.2 \ \text{°C}$$ $$T_3 = T_2 - 650.2 = 937.5 - 650.2 = \boxed{287.3 \ \text{°C}}$$ **Check:** $T_3 - T_4 = 287.3 - 40 = 247.3$ °C, and $Q \cdot R_3 = 1706.6 \times 0.1449 = 247.3$ °C. ✓ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Insight:** Notice that the **insulating brick (layer 2)** has the highest resistance (≈ 61% of total) and hence drops the largest temperature. This is precisely why we use insulation. --- ## 3.2 Problem 2 — Insulated Steam Pipe (Cylinder Geometry) ### Problem Statement A steam pipe of **outer diameter 80 mm** is insulated with a 40 mm thick layer of **85% magnesia** ($k = 0.058$ W/(m·K)). The outer surface of the insulation is at $30$ °C and the pipe outer surface is at $250$ °C. Calculate the **rate of heat loss per metre of pipe length**. ### Step 1 — Identify the Geometry This is a **hollow cylinder** (single layer) of conduction. Length $L = 1$ m. ### Step 2 — Establish Radii - Inner radius (pipe outer): $r_1 = 80/2 = 40$ mm $= 0.040$ m - Outer radius (insulation): $r_2 = 40 + 40 = 80$ mm $= 0.080$ m ### Step 3 — Apply the Cylinder Conduction Formula $$Q = \frac{2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$$ Substituting the values: $$Q = \frac{2\pi (0.058)(1.0)(250 - 30)}{\ln(0.080/0.040)}$$ ### Step 4 — Evaluate the Logarithm $$\ln(0.080/0.040) = \ln(2) = 0.6931$$ ### Step 5 — Final Calculation $$Q = \frac{2\pi (0.058)(1.0)(220)}{0.6931}$$ $$Q = \frac{80.169}{0.6931}$$ $$\boxed{Q = 115.65 \ \text{W per m length}}$$ ### Step 6 — Heat Flux at Pipe Surface (Optional) $$q_{r_1} = \frac{Q}{2\pi r_1 L} = \frac{115.65}{2\pi (0.040)(1)} = 460.2 \ \text{W/m}^2$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Insight:** The heat flux is **highest at the inner surface** and **lowest at the outer surface** of an insulated cylinder. This is because the area increases with radius. --- ## 3.3 Problem 3 — Critical Radius of Insulation ### Problem Statement A bare steel pipe of **outer diameter 50 mm** ($k_{\text{steel}} = 45$ W/(m·K)) is used to carry a hot fluid. The pipe is exposed to ambient air with $h_o = 10$ W/(m²·K). 1. Calculate the **critical radius** of insulation for $k_{\text{ins}} = 0.05$ W/(m·K). 2. Comment on what happens to heat loss when insulation is added. ### Step 1 — Critical Radius Formula $$r_{\text{cr}} = \frac{k_{\text{ins}}}{h_o} = \frac{0.05}{10} = 0.005 \ \text{m} = 5 \ \text{mm}$$ ### Step 2 — Compare with Pipe Outer Radius Pipe outer radius $r_1 = 50/2 = 25$ mm $= 0.025$ m. Since $r_1 = 25$ mm $\gg r_{\text{cr}} = 5$ mm, **adding any insulation will DECREASE heat loss**. This is the **favourable case**. ### Step 3 — Verify with a Numerical Check Heat loss with **no insulation** (convection from bare pipe): $$Q_{\text{bare}} = h_o \cdot (2\pi r_1 L) \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)$$ $$Q_{\text{bare}} = 10 \times 2\pi (0.025)(1) \times (T_s - T_\infty) = 1.571 (T_s - T_\infty) \ \text{W/m}$$ Heat loss with **insulation** (conduction + convection): $$R_{\text{total}} = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L} + \frac{1}{h_o \cdot 2\pi r_2 L}$$ Let $r_2 = 0.040$ m (15 mm of insulation): $$R_{\text{cond}} = \frac{\ln(0.040/0.025)}{2\pi (0.05)(1)} = \frac{0.4700}{0.3142} = 1.4959 \ \text{K/W}$$ $$R_{\text{conv}} = \frac{1}{10 \times 2\pi (0.040)(1)} = \frac{1}{2.513} = 0.3979 \ \text{K/W}$$ $$R_{\text{total}} = 1.4959 + 0.3979 = 1.8938 \ \text{K/W}$$ $$Q_{\text{ins}} = \frac{(T_s - T_\infty)}{1.8938} = 0.5281 (T_s - T_\infty) \ \text{W/m}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Comparison:** $Q_{\text{ins}} = 0.528$ vs $Q_{\text{bare}} = 1.571$. Heat loss dropped by a factor of **3**! ✓ Insulation worked. ### What if it were a thin wire? For an **electrical wire** (small $r_1$) where $r_1 < r_{\text{cr}}$, adding insulation initially **increases** heat loss until $r_2 = r_{\text{cr}}$, after which it decreases. This is why electric cables are not always insulated — the bare wire may dissipate more heat safely. --- ## 3.4 Problem 4 — Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient in a Heat Exchanger ### Problem Statement A double-pipe heat exchanger has: - **Inner pipe (steel):** $k_s = 45$ W/(m·K), inner diameter 30 mm, wall thickness 3 mm - **Inside convection coefficient:** $h_i = 1500$ W/(m²·K) (water) - **Outside convection coefficient:** $h_o = 50$ W/(m²·K) (air) - **Fouling factors:** $R_{f,i} = 0.0002$ m²·K/W, $R_{f,o} = 0.0004$ m²·K/W Calculate the **overall heat transfer coefficient** $U$ based on the **outer area** of the inner pipe. ### Step 1 — Geometry - $D_{1i} = 0.030$ m (inner diameter of steel pipe) - $D_{1o} = 0.036$ m (outer diameter of steel pipe) - Inner radius $r_i = 0.015$ m - Outer radius $r_o = 0.018$ m ### Step 2 — All Resistances on Outer Area Basis $$\frac{1}{U_o} = \frac{1}{h_o} + R_{f,o} + \frac{r_o \ln(r_o/r_i)}{k_s} + R_{f,i} \cdot \frac{r_o}{r_i} + \frac{1}{h_i} \cdot \frac{r_o}{r_i}$$ Wait — we need to be careful. For $U$ based on outer area, the conduction resistance becomes: $$R_{\text{cond}} = \frac{r_o \ln(r_o/r_i)}{k_s} = \frac{0.018 \times \ln(0.018/0.045)}{45}$$ Wait, let me recompute: $r_o = 0.018$ m, $r_i = 0.015$ m: $$R_{\text{cond}} = \frac{0.018 \times \ln(0.018/0.015)}{45} = \frac{0.018 \times 0.1823}{45} = 7.292 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^2\text{K/W}$$ ### Step 3 — Summing All Resistances $$\frac{1}{U_o} = \frac{1}{50} + 0.0004 + 7.292 \times 10^{-5} + \frac{0.0002 \times 0.018}{0.015} + \frac{0.018}{1500 \times 0.015}$$ $$= 0.02000 + 0.0004 + 0.0000729 + 0.000240 + 0.000800$$ $$= 0.02151 \ \text{m}^2 \text{K/W}$$ ### Step 4 — Final U $$U_o = \frac{1}{0.02151} = 46.49 \ \text{W/(m}^2 \cdot \text{K)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Insight:** The dominant resistance is the air-side convection ($1/50 = 0.02$ m²K/W), contributing ~93% of the total. This is typical for gas-liquid exchangers and explains why fins are added on the air side to increase the effective $h_o$. --- ## 3.5 Problem 5 — LMTD and Heat Exchanger Area ### Problem Statement A counter-current heat exchanger cools **oil (3.0 kg/s)** from $150$ °C to $80$ °C using **water entering at $30$ °C** and leaving at $50$ °C. The overall heat transfer coefficient is $U = 1500$ W/(m²·K) and the specific heat of oil is $c_p = 2.0$ kJ/(kg·K). Calculate the **heat exchanger area** required. ### Step 1 — Heat Duty $$Q = \dot{m}_{\text{oil}} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{\text{oil,in}} - T_{\text{oil,out}})$$ $$Q = 3.0 \times 2000 \times (150 - 80)$$ $$Q = 3.0 \times 2000 \times 70 = 420{,}000 \ \text{W} = 420 \ \text{kW}$$ ### Step 2 — Compute Temperature Differences at Both Ends For counter-current flow: - Hot fluid inlet (150 °C) ↔ Cold fluid outlet (50 °C): $\Delta T_1 = 150 - 50 = 100$ °C - Hot fluid outlet (80 °C) ↔ Cold fluid inlet (30 °C): $\Delta T_2 = 80 - 30 = 50$ °C ### Step 3 — Apply LMTD Formula $$\Delta T_{LM} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = \frac{100 - 50}{\ln(100/50)} = \frac{50}{\ln 2} = \frac{50}{0.6931}$$ $$\Delta T_{LM} = 72.13 \ \text{°C}$$ ### Step 4 — Solve for Area $$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LM}} = \frac{420{,}000}{1500 \times 72.13}$$ $$\boxed{A = 3.88 \ \text{m}^2}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Insight:** A useful sanity check — water side: $\dot{m}_w = Q / (c_{pw} \Delta T_w) = 420{,}000 / (4180 \times 20) = 5.02$ kg/s. ✓ Reasonable. --- ## 3.6 Problem 6 — Conduction Through a Sphere ### Problem Statement A hollow sphere of inner radius 50 mm and outer radius 100 mm is made of material with $k = 20$ W/(m·K). The inner surface is at $T_1 = 200$ °C and the outer surface is at $T_2 = 50$ °C. Find the heat loss. ### Step 1 — Apply the Sphere Formula $$Q = \frac{4\pi k (T_1 - T_2)}{\dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r_2}}$$ $$Q = \frac{4\pi (20)(200 - 50)}{\dfrac{1}{0.050} - \dfrac{1}{0.100}} = \frac{4\pi (20)(150)}{20 - 10}$$ $$Q = \frac{4\pi \times 3000}{10} = \frac{12{,}000 \pi}{10} = 1200 \pi$$ $$\boxed{Q = 3770 \ \text{W}}$$ --- ## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Tree for Students | If the Problem Involves… | Use the Formula… | Key Variables to Watch | |--------------------------|------------------|------------------------| | **Plane wall, single layer** | $Q = \dfrac{kA(T_1-T_2)}{x}$ | All areas equal | | **Plane wall, composite** | $Q = \dfrac{\Delta T}{\sum (x_i/k_iA)}$ | Resistances add | | **Hollow cylinder, single layer** | $Q = \dfrac{2\pi kL(T_1-T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$ | Length $L$ required | | **Hollow cylinder, composite** | $Q = \dfrac{2\pi L(T_1-T_n)}{\sum \ln(r_{i+1}/r_i)/k_i}$ | Multiple ln terms | | **Hollow sphere** | $Q = \dfrac{4\pi k(T_1-T_2)}{1/r_1-1/r_2}$ | Note the difference in geometry factor | | **Convection only** | $Q = hA(T_s-T_\infty)$ | Watch units of $h$ | | **Convection + Conduction (composite wall with fluids)** | $Q = \dfrac{\Delta T}{1/h_i + \sum x_i/k_i + 1/h_o}$ | All multiplied by appropriate area | | **Heat exchanger rating** | $Q = U A \Delta T_{LM}$ | LMTD must be computed first | | **Critical radius** | $r_{cr} = k/h$ | Compare with $r_1$ of cylinder | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Thermal Resistance Network — Composite Wall with Convection ```mermaid graph LR A[Hot Fluid
T infinity i] -->|1 / h sub i A| B[Inner Convective
Resistance] B -->|x sub 1 / k sub 1 A| C[Layer 1
Refractory] C -->|x sub 2 / k sub 2 A| D[Layer 2
Insulation] D -->|x sub 3 / k sub 3 A| E[Layer 3
Building Brick] E -->|1 / h sub o A| F[Outer Convective
Resistance] F --> G[Cold Fluid
T infinity o] style A fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style G fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style B fill:#FFE66D,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style C fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style D fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style E fill:#F38181,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style F fill:#FFE66D,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` ## 4.2 Heat Flow Path Through a Composite Cylinder (Insulated Pipe) ```mermaid graph TD A[Inner Hot Fluid
T i] -->|Convection h i| B[Pipe Inner Wall
T sub 1] B -->|Conduction ln r2/r1 / 2 pi k1 L| C[Pipe Outer Wall

T infinity] style A fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style E fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style B fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style C fill:#F38181,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style D fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` ## 4.3 Heat Exchanger Flow Configuration — Counter Current ```mermaid graph LR A[Hot Fluid In
150 deg C] -->|flows right to left| B[Heat Exchanger Shell] B -->|Hot Fluid Out

Beyond this, it falls] E --> H[Maximum heat loss point] style A fill:#FFE66D,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style B fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style D fill:#F38181,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style E fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Direct Conceptual) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define the term "thermal resistance" and state its units. Mention the thermal resistance for conduction through a plane wall and a hollow cylinder.** **Model Answer:** Thermal resistance is the opposition offered by a material or a system to the flow of heat, analogous to electrical resistance in Ohm's law. It is defined as the ratio of the temperature difference across the system to the steady-state heat transfer rate. $$R = \frac{\Delta T}{Q}$$ **Units:** K/W (Kelvin per Watt) or °C/W. - For a plane wall: $R_{\text{cond}} = \dfrac{x}{kA}$ - For a hollow cylinder: $R_{\text{cond}} = \dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L}$ ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is the critical radius of insulation? Under what condition does adding insulation increase heat loss?** **Model Answer:** The critical radius of insulation is the outer radius of insulation at which the heat transfer rate from a cylinder reaches a **maximum** value. Beyond this radius, adding more insulation decreases heat loss; below it, adding insulation paradoxically **increases** heat loss. $$r_{\text{cr}} = \frac{k_{\text{insulation}}}{h_{\text{outside}}}$$ **Condition for increased heat loss:** When the bare pipe radius $r_1 < r_{\text{cr}}$, adding insulation in the range $r_1 < r_2 < r_{\text{cr}}$ increases heat loss. This is commonly observed in **small-diameter electrical wires**. --- ## 5.2 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **A furnace wall consists of three layers as follows:** | Layer | Material | Thickness (mm) | k (W/m·K) | |-------|----------|----------------|-----------| | 1 | Fire brick | 150 | 1.40 | | 2 | Insulating brick | 100 | 0.20 | | 3 | Building brick | 150 | 0.70 | The inner surface temperature is $1200$ °C and the outer surface temperature is $60$ °C. Take area $A = 1$ m². #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** Calculate the rate of heat loss through the wall per m² of area and the temperature at each interface. **Solution:** **Step 1:** Compute individual resistances for $A = 1$ m²: $$R_1 = \frac{x_1}{k_1 A} = \frac{0.150}{1.40 \times 1} = 0.1071 \ \text{K/W}$$ $$R_2 = \frac{x_2}{k_2 A} = \frac{0.100}{0.20 \times 1} = 0.5000 \ \text{K/W}$$ $$R_3 = \frac{x_3}{k_3 A} = \frac{0.150}{0.70 \times 1} = 0.2143 \ \text{K/W}$$ **Step 2:** Total resistance: $$R_{\text{total}} = 0.1071 + 0.5000 + 0.2143 = 0.8214 \ \text{K/W}$$ **Step 3:** Heat loss: $$Q = \frac{\Delta T}{R_{\text{total}}} = \frac{1200 - 60}{0.8214} = \frac{1140}{0.8214} = 1387.7 \ \text{W}$$ **Step 4:** Interface temperatures: $$T_2 = T_1 - Q \cdot R_1 = 1200 - 1387.7 \times 0.1071 = 1200 - 148.6 = 1051.4 \ \text{°C}$$ $$T_3 = T_2 - Q \cdot R_2 = 1051.4 - 1387.7 \times 0.5000 = 1051.4 - 693.9 = 357.5 \ \text{°C}$$ **Check:** $T_4 = T_3 - Q \cdot R_3 = 357.5 - 1387.7 \times 0.2143 = 357.5 - 297.4 = 60.1$ °C ✓ #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** If the same wall is redesigned by interchanging the position of fire brick and building brick (i.e., fire brick outside, building brick inside, with the same insulator in the middle), comment on whether the new arrangement is safe. The melting point of building brick is $700$ °C. **Solution:** In the new arrangement, $T_1$ (hot side, 1200 °C) is now in contact with the building brick first, then insulating brick, then fire brick. The interface temperature between the building brick and insulation (i.e., $T_2$) must be computed and compared to the melting point. **New order:** Hot side → Building brick → Insulating brick → Fire brick → Cold side Using the same $Q$ value (since geometry and surface temperatures are unchanged): $$T_2 = T_1 - Q \cdot R_{\text{building}} = 1200 - 1387.7 \times 0.2143 = 1200 - 297.4 = 902.6 \ \text{°C}$$ Since $902.6$ °C $\gg 700$ °C (melting point), the **building brick will melt**! **Conclusion:** The new arrangement is **NOT safe**. The original arrangement is correct because the high-temperature side is lined with the most refractory (high $k$) material, and the insulating brick drops the temperature below the safe limit before the building brick is reached. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students assume "swap = same Q" without computing the new interface temperature. The golden rule: **the layer with the lowest $k$ produces the largest temperature drop, and its position determines whether downstream materials exceed their service limits.** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **A counter-current double-pipe heat exchanger is used to condense steam at $120$ °C (saturated) by cooling water. Water enters at $30$ °C and leaves at $70$ °C, with a flow rate of $2.0$ kg/s. The overall heat transfer coefficient based on the outer pipe surface is $U_o = 2000$ W/(m²·K). The outer pipe diameter is $D_o = 50$ mm.** **Steam condenses at constant temperature (i.e., $\Delta T_1 = 120 - 70 = 50$ °C at one end and $\Delta T_2 = 120 - 30 = 90$ °C at the other).** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** Calculate the LMTD and the heat transfer area. **Solution:** **Step 1:** Identify temperature differences (counter-current): - $\Delta T_1 = 120 - 70 = 50$ °C (steam end with water exit) - $\Delta T_2 = 120 - 30 = 90$ °C (steam end with water entry) **Step 2:** Apply LMTD: $$\Delta T_{LM} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = \frac{50 - 90}{\ln(50/90)} = \frac{-40}{\ln(0.5556)} = \frac{-40}{-0.5878} = 68.05 \ \text{°C}$$ **Step 3:** Heat duty: $$Q = \dot{m}_w c_{pw} \Delta T_w = 2.0 \times 4180 \times (70 - 30) = 2.0 \times 4180 \times 40 = 334{,}400 \ \text{W}$$ **Step 4:** Area: $$A_o = \frac{Q}{U_o \cdot \Delta T_{LM}} = \frac{334{,}400}{2000 \times 68.05} = \frac{334{,}400}{136{,}100}$$ $$\boxed{A_o = 2.457 \ \text{m}^2}$$ #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** If the heat exchanger length is $L = 16$ m, calculate the inner pipe diameter $D_i$ (neglecting pipe wall thickness). **Solution:** **Step 1:** Area of outer pipe surface: $$A_o = \pi D_o L = \pi (0.050)(16) = 2.513 \ \text{m}^2$$ **Step 2:** This is the area. Wait — the problem states "based on outer pipe surface." If the design is for $A_o = 2.457$ m², then we need: $$L = \frac{A_o}{\pi D_o} = \frac{2.457}{\pi (0.050)} = 15.64 \ \text{m}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Insight:** Note that LMTD works for **condensers** even though the hot fluid doesn't change temperature, because it is treated as a single-phase fluid with constant $T$. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A common mistake is to compute LMTD as arithmetic mean. Also, for a condenser (one fluid at constant $T$), the formula simplifies but is still valid. Students often get confused about which $\Delta T$ to use first. --- ## 5.3 Additional Practice Numerical (Self-Study) **Problem:** A composite cylindrical wall has: - Inner steel pipe: $r_1 = 0.040$ m, $r_2 = 0.050$ m, $k_1 = 45$ W/(m·K) - Outer insulation: $r_3 = 0.090$ m, $k_2 = 0.05$ W/(m·K) - Hot fluid inside: $h_i = 500$ W/(m²·K), $T_\infty i = 350$ °C - Cold fluid outside: $h_o = 25$ W/(m²·K), $T_\infty o = 30$ °C - Length $L = 5$ m **Find:** Heat loss, overall $U$ based on outer area, and outer surface temperature. **Solution Sketch:** - $R_i = 1/(h_i \cdot 2\pi r_1 L) = 1/(500 \cdot 2\pi \cdot 0.040 \cdot 5) = 1/628.3 = 0.001592$ K/W - $R_{\text{steel}} = \ln(0.050/0.040)/(2\pi \cdot 45 \cdot 5) = 0.2231/1413.7 = 0.000158$ K/W - $R_{\text{ins}} = \ln(0.090/0.050)/(2\pi \cdot 0.05 \cdot 5) = 0.5878/1.571 = 0.3741$ K/W - $R_o = 1/(25 \cdot 2\pi \cdot 0.090 \cdot 5) = 1/70.69 = 0.01415$ K/W - $R_{\text{total}} = 0.001592 + 0.000158 + 0.3741 + 0.01415 = 0.3900$ K/W - $Q = (350-30)/0.3900 = 820.5$ W - $T_{o,\text{surface}} = T_\infty o + Q \cdot R_o = 30 + 820.5 \times 0.01415 = 30 + 11.6 = 41.6$ °C --- ## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Module 1 Numerical Problems:** > 1. **Unit mismatch**: Forgetting to convert mm to m. 1 mm = 0.001 m. **Mark loss: 1–2 marks.** > 2. **Area inconsistency in composite walls**: Using outer area for one layer and inner area for another. **Mark loss: 2–3 marks.** > 3. **Cylinder vs. Sphere formula confusion**: Using $2\pi rL$ for a sphere. **Mark loss: full mark of subpart.** > 4. **Critical radius miscalculation**: Forgetting to compare $r_{\text{cr}}$ with the actual pipe radius. **Mark loss: 2 marks.** > 5. **LMTD vs. AMTD**: Using arithmetic mean $\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1 + \Delta T_2}{2}\right)$ instead of log mean. **Mark loss: 2 marks.** > 6. **Forgetting $A$ in plane wall resistance**: Writing $R = x/k$ instead of $x/(kA)$. **Mark loss: 1 mark.** > 7. **Fouling factor sign**: Forgetting to add $R_{f,i}$ and $R_{f,o}$ in the denominator. **Mark loss: 1–2 marks.** --- ## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Fourier's Law** is the foundation of all conduction problems; integrate it over the geometry (slab, cylinder, or sphere) to get the heat transfer rate. - **Thermal resistance analogy** is the most efficient problem-solving tool: convert the system to an electrical circuit of resistors in **series** (and sometimes **parallel**). - **Composite wall formula**: $Q = \dfrac{\Delta T_{\text{overall}}}{\sum R_i}$ — apply this to ANY series heat transfer path. - **Hollow cylinder heat transfer** requires the **logarithmic** term: $\ln(r_2/r_1)$, NOT a linear term. - **Hollow sphere** uses $\left(\dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r_2}\right)$ — different geometry constant ($4\pi k$ vs. $2\pi k L$). - **Critical radius of insulation** is $r_{\text{cr}} = k/h_o$. Compare with the **pipe outer radius** $r_1$ to decide whether insulation helps. - **Newton's Law of Cooling**: $Q = hA(T_s - T_\infty)$. Remember the **direction**: $T_s > T_\infty$ for heat to flow out of a hot surface. - **Overall heat transfer coefficient $U$** for a system with convection and conduction: $1/U = 1/h_i + \sum (x/k) + 1/h_o$, with **fouling factors added**. - **LMTD formula**: $\Delta T_{LM} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$. Note that the **order** of $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ doesn't affect the absolute value but must be consistent. - **Heat exchanger area**: $A = Q / (U \cdot \Delta T_{LM})$. - **Units check**: Always verify $k$ is in W/(m·K), $x$ in m, $A$ in m², $h$ in W/(m²·K) — and $\Delta T$ in K or °C (the difference is the same). - **Sanity check**: After computation, verify that interface temperatures are between the bulk fluid temperatures — if not, there is a sign error. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU Tip:** Practice at least 5 numerical problems of each type (plane wall composite, cylinder single/composite, critical radius, heat exchanger LMTD). Most KTU questions are direct applications of the formulas above with **plug-and-chug** numbers, so the key is **memorization of the formula and unit discipline**.

topics.title: Problem Solving in Conduction Using Excel/Matlab/Scilab/Python (Only for Self-Study/Microproject/Assignment)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Problem Solving in Conduction Using Excel/Matlab/Scilab/Python (Only for Self-Study/Microproject/Assignment)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Computational (Numerical) Heat Conduction** refers to the systematic discretization of the governing heat diffusion equation (PDE) — Fourier's law coupled with energy conservation — into a system of algebraic difference equations that can be solved iteratively on a digital platform (spreadsheet or numerical computing environment). The continuous spatial domain $x \in [0,L]$ and temporal domain $t \in [0,\tau]$ are replaced by a finite grid of nodes $(i,n)$, transforming the differential heat equation into a solvable numerical problem.

The general transient heat conduction equation solved numerically is:

$$\rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(k \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\!\left(k \frac{\partial T}{\partial y}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left(k \frac{\partial T}{\partial z}\right) + \dot{q}_{gen}$$

For constant thermal conductivity and no generation, this reduces to the **diffusion equation** in 1D:

$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}$$

where the thermal diffusivity $\alpha = k/(\rho c_p)$ with units $\mathbf{m^{2}/s}$ is the central physical property governing transient conduction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** This topic is designated for **Self-Study, Microproject, or Assignment only**. It will NOT appear as a direct ESE question, but the underlying numerical principles (Fourier number, grid spacing, stability) are examinable in conduction problems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

**Analogy — "The Coloring of a Frosted Glass Wall":**

Imagine a thick concrete wall, fully white (cold = 100°C), suddenly plunged on one face into a vat of red dye at 300°C. The red slowly seeps inward, node by node.

- **Analytical solution** (Chapter 2, Incropera): Tells you the *exact* color at *any* depth and time using infinite series and error functions — but only for very simple geometries (plane wall, cylinder, sphere) with simple BCs.
- **Numerical solution** (this topic): Breaks the wall into thin slices (Δx), and at each time step (Δt) tells each slice: "Take the average of your two neighbors and update your own color." Repeat thousands of times.

**Why use numerical methods at all?**
Real chemical engineering equipment — **shell-and-tube exchangers with fins, 3D reactor jackets, non-uniform cross-sections, variable $k(T)$, complex boundary conditions** — have **no closed-form analytical solution**. Excel/MATLAB/Scilab/Python give us the *only* practical route to a temperature distribution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Comparison for Students:**
> | Feature | Analytical | Numerical |
> |---|---|---|
> | Speed (small problem) | Fast | Slow |
> | Complex geometry | ❌ | ✅ |
> | Variable $k, h$ | ❌ | ✅ |
> | Internal generation | Sometimes | ✅ Always |
> | Accuracy | Exact | Approximate (error controllable) |
> | Engineering tool | Exam theory | Real design |
> 
> In KTU 2024 Scheme, you must know **both** — analytical for derivations, numerical for design projects.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Thermal diffusivity $\alpha$** — controls how fast temperature waves penetrate a body (units: $\mathbf{m^{2}/s}$). Copper ≈ $1.1 \times 10^{-4}$; Steel ≈ $2.3 \times 10^{-5}$; Brick ≈ $5.2 \times 10^{-7}$.
- **Fourier number $Fo = \alpha \Delta t / \Delta x^{2}$** — dimensionless time step, the **stability gatekeeper**.
- **Grid Biot number $Bi_{grid} = h \Delta x / k$** — controls how the convective BC is implemented.
- **Grid Peclet number** — for problems with advection.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Numerical Conduction

### Pillar 1: Domain Discretization
The continuous space–time is replaced by a **computational grid**:
- Spatial nodes: $i = 0, 1, 2, \dots, N$ with step $\Delta x = L/N$.
- Temporal nodes: $n = 0, 1, 2, \dots$ with step $\Delta t$.
- Node $(i, n)$ represents the point $x_i = i \Delta x$ at time $t_n = n \Delta t$.
- Temperature at that node: $T_i^{\,n} \approx T(x_i, t_n)$.

### Pillar 2: Approximation of Derivatives
Using **Taylor series expansion** (truncated at 2nd order):

$$\left.\frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}\right|_{i,n} \approx \frac{T_{i+1}^{\,n} - 2T_i^{\,n} + T_{i-1}^{\,n}}{\Delta x^{2}} + O(\Delta x^{2})$$

$$\left.\frac{\partial T}{\partial t}\right|_{i,n} \approx \frac{T_i^{\,n+1} - T_i^{\,n}}{\Delta t} + O(\Delta t)$$

The accuracy of the numerical solution is $O(\Delta x^{2}, \Delta t)$ — halving $\Delta x$ reduces the truncation error by a factor of **four**.

### Pillar 3: Algebraic Recurrence
Substituting the approximations into the PDE produces a **time-marching formula** that the spreadsheet/coding environment evaluates row by row.

## 2.2 The Three Numerical Schemes

### (A) Explicit (Forward Time, Central Space — FTCS)
Replace the time derivative with a **forward difference** and the spatial derivative with the current time level $n$:

$$T_i^{\,n+1} = T_i^{\,n} + Fo \left(T_{i+1}^{\,n} - 2T_i^{\,n} + T_{i-1}^{\,n}\right)$$

- ✅ Extremely simple — each new value depends only on *known* previous values.
- ✅ Perfect for Excel: one formula, copy-paste down a column.
- ❌ **Conditionally stable** — requires $Fo \le 0.5$. Violate this and the solution *blows up* (oscillates wildly).

### (B) Implicit (Backward Time, Central Space — BTCS)
Replace the time derivative with a **backward difference** and the spatial derivative with the *new* time level $n+1$:

$$-Fo\, T_{i-1}^{\,n+1} + (1+2Fo)\, T_i^{\,n+1} - Fo\, T_{i+1}^{\,n+1} = T_i^{\,n}$$

- ❌ The three unknowns $T_{i-1}, T_i, T_{i+1}$ are *all at the new time level* — you must solve a **system of simultaneous linear equations** (tridiagonal matrix).
- ✅ **Unconditionally stable** — any $Fo$ works.
- ✅ Required for stiff problems or long-time simulations.

### (C) Crank–Nicolson (Average of Explicit & Implicit)
Take the arithmetic mean of the FTCS and BTCS spatial operators:

$$- \tfrac{Fo}{2}\, T_{i-1}^{\,n+1} + (1+Fo)\, T_i^{\,n+1} - \tfrac{Fo}{2}\, T_{i+1}^{\,n+1} = \tfrac{Fo}{2}\, T_{i-1}^{\,n} + (1-Fo)\, T_i^{\,n} + \tfrac{Fo}{2}\, T_{i+1}^{\,n}$$

- ✅ **Second-order accurate in both space AND time** — the most accurate of the three.
- ✅ Unconditionally stable.
- ❌ Most algebraically involved.

## 2.3 Boundary Condition Implementation

### (1) Dirichlet (Specified Surface Temperature)
At $x = 0$ (node $i=0$): $T_0^{\,n} = T_s$ for all $n$. Just fix the value in the spreadsheet cell.

### (2) Convection (Robin / Newton Cooling) at $x = 0$
Energy balance on a half-control-volume of thickness $\Delta x/2$:

$$-k \frac{T_1^{\,n} - T_0^{\,n}}{\Delta x} = h \left(T_0^{\,n} - T_\infty\right)$$

Solving for the surface node $T_0$ (for explicit scheme):

$$T_0^{\,n+1} = T_0^{\,n} + \frac{2 Fo}{(k/h\Delta x)}\left(T_\infty - T_0^{\,n}\right) \quad \text{using} \quad Bi_{grid} = \frac{h \Delta x}{k}$$

For interior node adjacent to surface (using ghost-node approach or 1st-order):

$$T_0^{\,n+1} = \frac{T_1^{\,n} + Bi_{grid}\, T_\infty}{1 + Bi_{grid}}$$

### (3) Insulated (Adiabatic) Boundary
$\partial T / \partial x = 0$ at surface $\Rightarrow$ use a **mirror node**: $T_{-1}^{\,n} = T_{1}^{\,n}$. The recurrence then simplifies to:

$$T_0^{\,n+1} = T_0^{\,n} + 2 Fo \left(T_1^{\,n} - T_0^{\,n}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The convective BC implementation is the **#1 source of error** in numerical conduction. Always derive the half-volume energy balance, never just plug values blindly. Examiners love to test this!

## 2.4 Stability Analysis (von Neumann)

Substitute a Fourier-mode perturbation $T_i^{\,n} = G^{n} e^{j \beta i \Delta x}$ into the explicit scheme. The amplification factor is:

$$G = 1 - 4 Fo \sin^{2}\!\left(\frac{\beta \Delta x}{2}\right)$$

For **stability**, $\vert G \vert \le 1$ for all $\beta$, which is satisfied only when:

$$\boxed{\;Fo = \frac{\alpha \Delta t}{\Delta x^{2}} \le \frac{1}{2}\;}$$

This is the **famous Courant–Friedrichs–Lewy (CFL) condition** for the heat equation. The implicit and Crank–Nicolson schemes have $\vert G \vert \le 1$ for any $Fo$ — they are *unconditionally stable*.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Mandatory Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Always use `\vert` for absolute value in formulas inside tables** — to keep the markdown table syntax intact.

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Stability / Range | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1D Heat Diffusion PDE | $\partial T/\partial t = \alpha\,\partial^{2}T/\partial x^{2}$ | — | — |
| 2 | Thermal Diffusivity | $\alpha = k/(\rho c_p)$ | — | $\mathbf{m^{2}/s}$ |
| 3 | Fourier Number | $Fo = \alpha \Delta t / \Delta x^{2}$ | $\le 0.5$ (Explicit) | dimensionless |
| 4 | Grid Biot Number | $Bi_{grid} = h \Delta x / k$ | depends on problem | dimensionless |
| 5 | Explicit (FTCS) | $T_i^{n+1} = T_i^{n} + Fo(T_{i+1}^{n} - 2T_i^{n} + T_{i-1}^{n})$ | $Fo \le 0.5$ | °C or K |
| 6 | Implicit (BTCS) Matrix Row | $-Fo\,T_{i-1} + (1+2Fo)\,T_i - Fo\,T_{i+1} = T_i^{n}$ | Any $Fo$ | °C or K |
| 7 | Crank–Nicolson Matrix Row | $-(\tfrac{Fo}{2})\,T_{i-1} + (1+Fo)\,T_i - (\tfrac{Fo}{2})\,T_{i+1} = (\tfrac{Fo}{2})\,T_{i-1}^{n} + (1-Fo)\,T_i^{n} + (\tfrac{Fo}{2})\,T_{i+1}^{n}$ | Any $Fo$ | °C or K |
| 8 | Insulated BC | $T_{-1}^{n} = T_{+1}^{n}$ (mirror) | — | — |
| 9 | Convective Surface BC | $T_0^{n+1} = (T_1^{n} + Bi_{grid}\,T_\infty) / (1 + Bi_{grid})$ | — | °C or K |
| 10 | 2D Explicit | $T_{i,j}^{n+1} = T_{i,j}^{n} + Fo_x(T_{i+1,j} - 2T_{i,j} + T_{i-1,j}) + Fo_y(T_{i,j+1} - 2T_{i,j} + T_{i,j-1})$ | $Fo_x + Fo_y \le 0.5$ | °C or K |
| 11 | Truncation Error | $O(\Delta x^{2}, \Delta t)$ | — | — |
| 12 | Convergence (Richardson) | Error $\approx C \Delta x^{2}$ | Halve $\Delta x \Rightarrow$ quarter error | — |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Reactor wall design** (autoclaves, catalytic crackers): transient heating/cooling of thick steel walls — solves safety-shutdown thermal stress problems.
- **Fired heater tubes**: 2D conduction in tube wall with internal heat generation.
- **Heat exchanger fin design**: 1D conduction in fin with convective tip — no analytical solution for variable cross-section, so numerical is essential.
- **Glass/ceramic annealing**: slow cooling of glass sheets — long transient with radiation BC, must be solved numerically.
- **Microelectronics cooling**: 3D conduction in silicon chips with non-uniform heat generation.

In every industry placement (Reliance, BPCL, ISRO, DRDO, chemical plants), your first task as a Process Engineer will often be **building a numerical conduction model** in MATLAB/Python/Excel for a heat-exchange unit.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Explicit FTCS Scheme (1D)

### Starting Point
Governing PDE (1D, constant $\alpha$, no source):

$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}$$

### Step 1: Discretize the time derivative (forward difference)

Taylor expansion of $T$ about $(x_i, t_n)$ to the next time step:

$$T_i^{\,n+1} = T_i^{\,n} + \Delta t \left.\frac{\partial T}{\partial t}\right|_{i,n} + \frac{\Delta t^{2}}{2}\left.\frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial t^{2}}\right|_{i,n} + \cdots$$

Solving for the first time derivative:

$$\left.\frac{\partial T}{\partial t}\right|_{i,n} = \frac{T_i^{\,n+1} - T_i^{\,n}}{\Delta t} - \frac{\Delta t}{2}\left.\frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial t^{2}}\right|_{i,n} - \cdots \approx \frac{T_i^{\,n+1} - T_i^{\,n}}{\Delta t} + O(\Delta t)$$

The leading truncation error is $O(\Delta t)$ — first-order accurate in time.

### Step 2: Discretize the spatial second derivative (central difference)

Taylor expansion about node $i$ at fixed time $n$:

$$T_{i+1}^{\,n} = T_i^{\,n} + \Delta x \left.\frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\right|_{i,n} + \frac{\Delta x^{2}}{2}\left.\frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}\right|_{i,n} + \frac{\Delta x^{3}}{6}\left.\frac{\partial^{3} T}{\partial x^{3}}\right|_{i,n} + \cdots$$

$$T_{i-1}^{\,n} = T_i^{\,n} - \Delta x \left.\frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\right|_{i,n} + \frac{\Delta x^{2}}{2}\left.\frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}\right|_{i,n} - \frac{\Delta x^{3}}{6}\left.\frac{\partial^{3} T}{\partial x^{3}}\right|_{i,n} + \cdots$$

Adding the two expansions, the odd derivatives cancel:

$$T_{i+1}^{\,n} + T_{i-1}^{\,n} = 2 T_i^{\,n} + \Delta x^{2} \left.\frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}\right|_{i,n} + \frac{\Delta x^{4}}{12}\left.\frac{\partial^{4} T}{\partial x^{4}}\right|_{i,n} + \cdots$$

Solving for the second derivative:

$$\left.\frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}\right|_{i,n} = \frac{T_{i+1}^{\,n} - 2 T_i^{\,n} + T_{i-1}^{\,n}}{\Delta x^{2}} - \frac{\Delta x^{2}}{12}\left.\frac{\partial^{4} T}{\partial x^{4}}\right|_{i,n} - \cdots \approx \frac{T_{i+1}^{\,n} - 2 T_i^{\,n} + T_{i-1}^{\,n}}{\Delta x^{2}} + O(\Delta x^{2})$$

The leading truncation error is $O(\Delta x^{2})$ — second-order accurate in space.

### Step 3: Substitute into the PDE

$$\frac{T_i^{\,n+1} - T_i^{\,n}}{\Delta t} = \alpha \left[\frac{T_{i+1}^{\,n} - 2 T_i^{\,n} + T_{i-1}^{\,n}}{\Delta x^{2}}\right]$$

Rearranging:

$$T_i^{\,n+1} - T_i^{\,n} = \frac{\alpha \Delta t}{\Delta x^{2}}\left(T_{i+1}^{\,n} - 2 T_i^{\,n} + T_{i-1}^{\,n}\right)$$

Recognising $Fo = \alpha \Delta t / \Delta x^{2}$:

$$\boxed{\;T_i^{\,n+1} = T_i^{\,n} + Fo \left(T_{i+1}^{\,n} - 2 T_i^{\,n} + T_{i-1}^{\,n}\right)\;}$$

Total truncation error: $O(\Delta t, \Delta x^{2})$ — first-order in time, second-order in space.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Stability Criterion (Explicit Scheme)

### Step 1: Substitute a Fourier-mode perturbation
Let the solution contain an error of the form $T_i^{\,n} = G^{n} e^{j \beta i \Delta x}$, where $j = \sqrt{-1}$ and $\beta$ is the wavenumber.

### Step 2: Apply the recurrence
Substituting into the explicit formula and factoring out $G^{n} e^{j \beta i \Delta x}$:

$$G = 1 + Fo \left(e^{j \beta \Delta x} - 2 + e^{-j \beta \Delta x}\right)$$

### Step 3: Use the Euler identity
$e^{j\theta} + e^{-j\theta} = 2 \cos\theta$, hence:

$$G = 1 + 2 Fo (\cos(\beta \Delta x) - 1)$$

Using the double-angle identity $1 - \cos\theta = 2 \sin^{2}(\theta/2)$:

$$G = 1 - 4 Fo \sin^{2}\!\left(\frac{\beta \Delta x}{2}\right)$$

### Step 4: Impose the stability bound $\vert G \vert \le 1$
The worst case is when $\sin^{2}(\beta \Delta x / 2) = 1$ (i.e. $\beta \Delta x = \pi$), giving $G = 1 - 4 Fo$. For $\vert G \vert \le 1$:

$$-1 \le 1 - 4 Fo \le 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0 \le Fo \le \frac{1}{2}$$

Hence the **stability criterion**:

$$\boxed{\;Fo = \frac{\alpha \Delta t}{\Delta x^{2}} \le \frac{1}{2}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often quote $Fo \le 0.5$ without deriving it. The von Neumann analysis is what an examiner expects. If asked "Why 0.5?", the above derivation is the answer.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Convective Boundary Recurrence

Consider a semi-infinite slab with surface at $x=0$ exposed to a fluid at $T_\infty$ with $h$. The half-control-volume from $x = -\Delta x/2$ to $x = +\Delta x/2$ centred on the surface node.

### Step 1: Energy balance on the half-volume
Rate of energy in by conduction from the interior node (at $i=1$):

$$\dot{E}_{cond} = -k A \frac{T_1 - T_0}{\Delta x}$$

Rate of energy out by convection to the fluid:

$$\dot{E}_{conv} = h A (T_0 - T_\infty)$$

Rate of energy storage in the half-volume:

$$\dot{E}_{stor} = \rho c_p A \frac{\Delta x}{2} \frac{dT_0}{dt}$$

### Step 2: Conservation: $\dot{E}_{cond} - \dot{E}_{conv} = \dot{E}_{stor}$

$$-k \frac{T_1 - T_0}{\Delta x} - h (T_0 - T_\infty) = \rho c_p \frac{\Delta x}{2} \frac{dT_0}{dt}$$

### Step 3: Rearrange to standard form
Dividing by $k/\Delta x$ and grouping:

$$\frac{\rho c_p \Delta x}{2k} \frac{dT_0}{dt} = (T_1 - T_0) - \frac{h \Delta x}{k}(T_0 - T_\infty)$$

Define $Bi_{grid} = h \Delta x / k$ and recall $Fo = \alpha \Delta t / \Delta x^{2} = k \Delta t / (\rho c_p \Delta x^{2})$:

$$\frac{Fo}{2 Bi_{grid}} \frac{dT_0}{dt}\cdot \frac{\Delta t}{\text{()}} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{after forward differencing:}$$

$$T_0^{\,n+1} = T_0^{\,n} + \frac{2 Fo}{Bi_{grid}}\left(T_1^{\,n} - T_0^{\,n}\right) - \frac{2 Fo}{Bi_{grid}} Bi_{grid}(T_0^{\,n} - T_\infty)$$

Combining:

$$T_0^{\,n+1} = T_0^{\,n} + \frac{2 Fo}{Bi_{grid}}\left(T_1^{\,n} - T_0^{\,n}\right) - 2 Fo (T_0^{\,n} - T_\infty)$$

Equivalently, a more common practical form (after rearranging):

$$\boxed{\;T_0^{\,n+1} = \frac{T_1^{\,n} + Bi_{grid}\, T_\infty}{1 + Bi_{grid}}\;}$$

This is the form most easily implemented in Excel — it gives the *new* surface temperature in terms of the *old* interior neighbour and the fluid temperature.

## 3.4 Comprehensive Worked Example

**Problem [Adapted from Incropera Example 5.1, computational variant]:**

A stainless-steel plate of thickness $2L = 0.04$ m, thermal conductivity $k = 15$ W/m·K, $\rho = 7900$ kg/m³, $c_p = 500$ J/kg·K, is initially at $T_i = 200$ °C. It is suddenly immersed in an oil bath at $T_\infty = 20$ °C with $h = 100$ W/m²·K on **both** faces. Using 3 nodes (centre + 2 surfaces) with $\Delta x = 0.02$ m and explicit scheme, compute temperatures for the first 4 time steps with $\Delta t = 30$ s.

**Step 1: Compute thermal diffusivity and Fourier number.**

$$\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho c_p} = \frac{15}{7900 \times 500} = 3.797 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{2}/\text{s}$$

$$Fo = \frac{\alpha \Delta t}{\Delta x^{2}} = \frac{3.797 \times 10^{-6} \times 30}{(0.02)^{2}} = \frac{1.139 \times 10^{-4}}{4 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.2848$$

Stability check: $Fo = 0.285 < 0.5$ ✅ — scheme is stable.

**Step 2: Compute the grid Biot number for the surface nodes.**

$$Bi_{grid} = \frac{h \Delta x}{k} = \frac{100 \times 0.02}{15} = 0.1333$$

**Step 3: Set up the governing formulas.**
By symmetry, the centre node (i = 1) is treated as insulated. The two surfaces (i = 0 and i = 2) are convective and identical by symmetry.

- **Insulated centre node** (mirror BC): 

$$T_1^{\,n+1} = T_1^{\,n} + 2 Fo \left(T_0^{\,n} - T_1^{\,n}\right)$$

- **Convective surface node** (from §3.3): 

$$T_0^{\,n+1} = \frac{T_1^{\,n} + Bi_{grid}\, T_\infty}{1 + Bi_{grid}} = \frac{T_1^{\,n} + 0.1333 \times 20}{1.1333}$$

**Step 4: Time-step the calculation.**

*Time $n = 0$:* $T_0 = T_1 = T_2 = 200$ °C.

*Time $n = 1$:*

$$T_0^{1} = \frac{200 + 0.1333 \times 20}{1.1333} = \frac{200 + 2.667}{1.1333} = 178.82 \text{ °C}$$

$$T_1^{1} = 200 + 2(0.2848)(178.82 - 200) = 200 + 0.5696 \times (-21.18) = 200 - 12.06 = 187.94 \text{ °C}$$

*Time $n = 2$:*

$$T_0^{2} = \frac{187.94 + 2.667}{1.1333} = \frac{190.61}{1.1333} = 168.18 \text{ °C}$$

$$T_1^{2} = 187.94 + 0.5696 \times (168.18 - 187.94) = 187.94 + 0.5696 \times (-19.76) = 187.94 - 11.25 = 176.69 \text{ °C}$$

*Time $n = 3$:*

$$T_0^{3} = \frac{176.69 + 2.667}{1.1333} = \frac{179.36}{1.1333} = 158.26 \text{ °C}$$

$$T_1^{3} = 176.69 + 0.5696 \times (158.26 - 176.69) = 176.69 + 0.5696 \times (-18.43) = 176.69 - 10.50 = 166.19 \text{ °C}$$

*Time $n = 4$:*

$$T_0^{4} = \frac{166.19 + 2.667}{1.1333} = \frac{168.86}{1.1333} = 149.00 \text{ °C}$$

$$T_1^{4} = 166.19 + 0.5696 \times (149.00 - 166.19) = 166.19 - 9.79 = 156.40 \text{ °C}$$

**Summary Table (Temperatures in °C):**

| Time Step $n$ | $t$ (s) | $T_0 = T_2$ (Surface) | $T_1$ (Centre) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 200.00 | 200.00 |
| 1 | 30 | 178.82 | 187.94 |
| 2 | 60 | 168.18 | 176.69 |
| 3 | 90 | 158.26 | 166.19 |
| 4 | 120 | 149.00 | 156.40 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The surface cools roughly 3× faster than the centre in the early phase — exactly as expected physically. After 120 s, the surface has dropped 51 °C while the centre has dropped only 43.6 °C. Continuing the iterations in Excel for, say, 200 steps would produce the complete cooling curve, which can be plotted and compared to the analytical series solution (error function / infinite series from Module 1) to validate the numerical scheme.
