## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

*State and explain the significance of the Dittus–Boelter equation for turbulent flow heat transfer in a circular tube. Under what conditions is this correlation valid?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Dittus–Boelter equation for turbulent internal flow in a smooth circular tube is:

$$ \text{Nu}_D = 0.023 \, \text{Re}_D^{0.8} \, \text{Pr}^{n} $$

where $n = 0.4$ when the fluid is being heated ($T_s > T_b$) and $n = 0.3$ when the fluid is being cooled ($T_s < T_b$).

**Significance:** It provides a quick, simple estimate of the convective heat transfer coefficient in turbulent flow without requiring iteration. The exponent $0.8$ reflects the strong dependence of $h$ on velocity, while the Prandtl exponent accounts for the relative thickness of thermal vs. momentum boundary layers.

**Validity conditions (must be stated for full marks):**
1. $\text{Re}_D > 10{,}000$ (fully turbulent)
2. $0.7 \le \text{Pr} \le 160$
3. $L/D > 10$ (developed flow)
4. Property variations less than a factor of 1.3 between bulk and wall (otherwise use Sieder–Tate viscosity correction).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

*Distinguish between the laminar fully-developed values of the Nusselt number under (i) constant wall temperature and (ii) constant wall heat flux boundary conditions. Why do they differ?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Boundary Condition | Fully-Developed Laminar $\text{Nu}_D$ |
|---|---|
| Constant wall temperature ($T_s = $ const.) | $3.66$ |
| Constant wall heat flux ($q_s'' = $ const.) | $4.36$ |

**Reason for the difference (2 marks):** Under constant heat flux, the heat is supplied uniformly, which forces the wall temperature to rise along the tube. This axial temperature gradient enhances radial conduction near the wall relative to the isothermal case. Mathematically, the temperature profile is more "peaked" toward the wall for constant $q_s''$, producing a higher wall-to-bulk temperature difference per unit heat flux. The result is a $4.36/3.66 \approx 19\%$ higher Nusselt number for the constant-heat-flux case.

---

### PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Mark Loss #1 — Wrong property temperature:** Many students evaluate ALL properties at the inlet temperature, missing that $k$, $\rho$, $\mu_b$, $C_p$, $\text{Pr}$ must be at the bulk mean $T_b = (T_{b,in}+T_{b,out})/2$, and only $\mu_s$ at $T_s$.
> - **Mark Loss #2 — Forgetting the viscosity correction ratio** when Sieder–Tate is mandated for liquids with large $\Delta T$ (oils).
> - **Mark Loss #3 — Mixing Dittus–Boelter constants:** $n = 0.4$ for heating and $n = 0.3$ for cooling; using the wrong exponent costs a mark.
> - **Mark Loss #4 — Reporting units inconsistently:** $h$ in W/m²·K (not W/m·K), $Q$ in W or kW.
> - **Mark Loss #5 — Not stating the validity range of the chosen correlation**; always close your solution with a one-line check: "Since $\text{Re}_D = X$, $0.7 \le \text{Pr} \le 160$, $L/D = Y > 10$, the Dittus–Boelter equation is valid."

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the analytical value of the Nusselt number for **steady, laminar, fully-developed flow** with **constant wall heat flux** in a circular tube. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Engine oil flows through a 4-m-long tube of 30 mm inner diameter with a mass flow rate of 0.20 kg/s. The oil enters at $80\,^\circ\text{C}$ and leaves at $60\,^\circ\text{C}$. The tube wall is maintained at $40\,^\circ\text{C}$. Oil properties (evaluated at appropriate temperatures) are: $k_b = 0.143$ W/m·K, $\mu_b = 0.027$ N·s/m², $\mu_s = 0.085$ N·s/m², $\text{Pr}_b = 1200$. Estimate the rate of heat transfer from the oil to the wall. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Question A:**

**(a)** See the full derivation in **Section 3.1** above. The result is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Nu}_D = 4.36 \quad \text{(constant heat flux, fully developed laminar)} } $$

**Key assumptions (must list for full marks):**
1. Steady, incompressible, laminar flow.
2. Constant fluid properties.
3. Constant wall heat flux $q_s''$.
4. Fully developed velocity and temperature profiles.
5. No internal heat generation; negligible viscous dissipation.
6. Axisymmetric; no natural convection effects.

---

**(b)** Step-by-step solution:

**Step 1 — Flow regime check.**

$$ \text{Re}_D = \frac{4\dot{m}}{\pi D \mu_b} = \frac{4 \times 0.20}{\pi \times 0.030 \times 0.027} = \frac{0.80}{2.544 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 314.5 $$

Since $314.5 < 2300$, the flow is **laminar**.

**Step 2 — Select correlation.** Since $L/D = 4/0.030 = 133.3$ is large and $\text{Pr}$ is high, the entrance region is significant. Use **Sieder–Tate laminar (constant wall temperature)**:

$$ \text{Nu}_D = 1.86 \left(\frac{\text{Re}_D\,\text{Pr}}{L/D}\right)^{1/3}\left(\frac{\mu_b}{\mu_s}\right)^{0.14} $$

**Step 3 — Compute each bracket.**

$$ \frac{\text{Re}_D\,\text{Pr}}{L/D} = \frac{314.5 \times 1200}{133.3} = 2832 $$

$$ (2832)^{1/3} \approx 14.15 $$

$$ \left(\frac{0.027}{0.085}\right)^{0.14} = (0.3176)^{0.14} \approx 0.751 $$

**Step 4 — Nusselt number.**

$$ \text{Nu}_D = 1.86 \times 14.15 \times 0.751 \approx 19.77 $$

**Step 5 — Convection coefficient.**

$$ \bar{h} = \frac{\text{Nu}_D \cdot k}{D} = \frac{19.77 \times 0.143}{0.030} \approx 94.2 \;\text{W/m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{K} $$

**Step 6 — Surface area and $\Delta T_{\text{LMTD}}$.**

$$ A_s = \pi D L = \pi \times 0.030 \times 4 = 0.377 \;\text{m}^2 $$

$$ \Delta T_1 = T_{b,\text{in}} - T_s = 80 - 40 = 40\,^\circ\text{C} $$
$$ \Delta T_2 = T_{b,\text{out}} - T_s = 60 - 40 = 20\,^\circ\text{C} $$

$$ \Delta T_{\text{LMTD}} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)} = \frac{40 - 20}{\ln(40/20)} = \frac{20}{\ln 2} \approx 28.85\,^\circ\text{C} $$

**Step 7 — Heat transfer rate.**

$$ Q = \bar{h}\,A_s\,\Delta T_{\text{LMTD}} = 94.2 \times 0.377 \times 28.85 \approx 1024 \;\text{W} \approx \boxed{1.02\;\text{kW}} $$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** For turbulent flow inside a smooth circular tube, derive (or state) the **Gnielinski correlation** and explain how it differs from the simpler **Dittus–Boelter** equation. Discuss the role of the friction factor $f$ in the Gnielinski correlation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Water enters a 2-cm-ID smooth tube at $40\,^\circ\text{C}$ with a mean velocity of 3 m/s. The tube wall is maintained at $80\,^\circ\text{C}$. Using the **Gnielinski** correlation, determine the length of tube required to heat the water to $75\,^\circ\text{C}$. Assume water properties at bulk mean $T_b = 57.5\,^\circ\text{C}$: $k = 0.652$ W/m·K, $\mu_b = 5.0 \times 10^{-4}$ N·s/m², $C_p = 4180$ J/kg·K, $\rho = 985$ kg/m³, $\text{Pr} = 3.2$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Question B:**

**(a)** The Gnielinski correlation for turbulent flow in smooth tubes is:

$$ \text{Nu}_D = \frac{(f/8)(\text{Re}_D - 1000)\text{Pr}}{1 + 12.7\sqrt{f/8}\,(\text{Pr}^{2/3} - 1)} $$

with the smooth-tube friction factor:

$$ f = \left(0.790\,\ln\text{Re}_D - 1.64\right)^{-2} $$

**Comparison with Dittus–Boelter** (must list at least 3 differences for full marks):

| Aspect | Dittus–Boelter | Gnielinski |
|---|---|---|
| Validity range | $\text{Re}_D > 10^4$, $0.7 \le \text{Pr} \le 160$ | $\text{Re}_D > 2300$, $0.5 \le \text{Pr} \le 2000$ |
| Wall BC distinction | Has $n = 0.4$ (heating) and $n = 0.3$ (cooling) | Treats both BCs similarly via $f$ and the Pr term |
| Accuracy | ±25% typical | ±10% — much more accurate |
| Physics-based | Pure empirical power law | Built on momentum–heat transfer analogy (uses $f$) |

**Role of friction factor:** The friction factor $f$ appears because Gnielinski's correlation is built on the **Reynolds analogy** between momentum and heat transfer. By using $f$, the correlation inherits the correct boundary-layer physics at the wall; the same $f$ that governs pressure drop also controls how effectively eddies sweep heat away from the wall. This makes the correlation physically more grounded and consequently more accurate.

---

**(b)** Step-by-step:

**Step 1 — Compute $\text{Re}_D$.**

$$ \text{Re}_D = \frac{\rho \bar{u}_m D}{\mu_b} = \frac{985 \times 3 \times 0.020}{5.0 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{59.1}{5.0\times 10^{-4}} = 1.182 \times 10^5 $$

Regime: turbulent.

**Step 2 — Compute $f$.**

$$ f = \left[0.790 \ln(1.182 \times 10^5) - 1.64\right]^{-2} = \left[0.790(11.679) - 1.64\right]^{-2} $$
$$ = \left[9.227 - 1.64\right]^{-2} = (7.587)^{-2} \approx 0.01737 $$

So $f/8 = 0.002171$, $\sqrt{f/8} = 0.04660$.

**Step 3 — Compute $\text{Nu}_D$.**

$$ \text{Pr}^{2/3} = (3.2)^{0.667} \approx 2.182, \quad \text{Pr}^{2/3} - 1 = 1.182 $$

Numerator: $0.002171 \times (118200 - 1000) \times 3.2 = 0.002171 \times 117200 \times 3.2 \approx 814.0$

Denominator: $1 + 12.7 \times 0.04660 \times 1.182 = 1 + 0.6996 = 1.6996$

$$ \text{Nu}_D \approx \frac{814.0}{1.6996} \approx 478.9 $$

**Step 4 — Convection coefficient.**

$$ \bar{h} = \frac{478.9 \times 0.652}{0.020} \approx 15{,}612 \;\text{W/m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{K} $$

**Step 5 — Heat duty required.**

$$ Q = \dot{m} C_p (T_{b,\text{out}} - T_{b,\text{in}}) = (\rho \bar{u}_m A_c) C_p \Delta T $$

$$ A_c = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.020)^2}{4} = 3.142 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^2 $$

$$ \dot{m} = \rho \bar{u}_m A_c = 985 \times 3 \times 3.142 \times 10^{-4} = 0.9284 \;\text{kg/s} $$

$$ Q = 0.9284 \times 4180 \times (75 - 40) = 0.9284 \times 4180 \times 35 \approx 135{,}830 \;\text{W} $$

**Step 6 — Find required area and length.**

$$ \Delta T_{\text{LMTD}} = \frac{(75 - 80) - (40 - 80)}{\ln[(75-80)/(40-80)]} = \frac{-5 - (-40)}{\ln(5/40)} = \frac{35}{\ln(0.125)} = \frac{35}{-2.0794} \approx -16.83 $$

Taking magnitudes, $\Delta T_{\text{LMTD}} = 16.83\,^\circ\text{C}$.

$$ A_s = \frac{Q}{\bar{h}\,\Delta T_{\text{LMTD}}} = \frac{135{,}830}{15{,}612 \times 16.83} \approx 0.517 \;\text{m}^2 $$

**Step 7 — Convert area to length.**

$$ L = \frac{A_s}{\pi D} = \frac{0.517}{\pi \times 0.020} \approx \boxed{8.23\;\text{m}} $$

**Validity check (1 mark bonus in KTU valuations):**
- $\text{Re}_D = 1.18 \times 10^5$ ✓ (in range $10^4$–$5 \times 10^6$)
- $\text{Pr} = 3.2$ ✓ (in range $0.5$–$2000$)
- Smooth tube ✓
- $L/D \approx 412 > 10$ ✓
All Gnielinski validity conditions satisfied.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Reynolds number rule of thumb:** $\text{Re}_D < 2300$ (laminar), $2300$–$4000$ (transitional), $> 4000$ (turbulent).
- [x] **Fully-developed laminar Nusselt numbers are analytical constants:** $\text{Nu}_D = 3.66$ (constant $T_s$) and $\text{Nu}_D = 4.36$ (constant $q_s''$). Memorize these.
- [x] **Entrance-region laminar correlations:** **Hausen** for constant $T_s$; **Sieder–Tate** when viscosity correction is needed or for developing flow with entry effects.
- [x] **Most-used turbulent correlation:** **Gnielinski** is the modern standard; **Dittus–Boelter** is acceptable for quick estimates.
- [x] **Dittus–Boelter exponent rule:** $n = 0.4$ for heating ($T_s > T_b$), $n = 0.3$ for cooling ($T_s < T_b$).
- [x] **Viscosity correction ratio:** $(\mu_b/\mu_s)^{0.14}$ — evaluate $\mu_b$ at bulk mean and $\mu_s$ at wall temperature. Critical for engine oils, heavy hydrocarbons.
- [x] **Property temperature rule:** All properties in $k$, $C_p$, $\rho$, $\text{Pr}$ at **bulk mean** $T_b = (T_{b,\text{in}} + T_{b,\text{out}})/2$.
- [x] **Friction factor formula** for smooth tubes (Petukhov/Gnielinski): $f = (0.790 \ln \text{Re}_D - 1.64)^{-2}$.
- [x] **Heat duty formula:** $Q = \bar{h}\,A_s\,\Delta T_{\text{LMTD}}$, with $A_s = \pi D L$ for tubes.
- [x] **Always state validity checks** in your KTU answer — these are the "free 1-mark" boosters examiners reward.
- [x] **LMTD formula** when both ends have $\Delta T$ of the same sign: $\Delta T_{\text{LMTD}} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)/\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$.
- [x] **Transitional regime** in KTU papers: prefer Gnielinski (which gracefully extends down to $\text{Re}_D \approx 2300$ via the $-1000$ term).
- [x] **Units sanity check at the end:** $\bar{h}$ in W/m²·K, $Q$ in W or kW, $\text{Nu}_D$ dimensionless.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laminar and Turbulent Flow Over Flat Plates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Laminar and Turbulent Flow Over Flat Plates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Boundary Layer Flow Over a Flat Plate

When a uniform free-stream fluid flows over a flat plate held stationary in its path, a **boundary layer** develops along the plate. This is a thin region adjacent to the surface where the fluid velocity transitions from zero (at the wall, due to the **no-slip condition**) to the free-stream velocity $U_\infty$ in the transverse direction.

The flow regime is classified by the local **Reynolds number** at a distance $x$ from the leading edge:

$$Re_x = \frac{\rho U_\infty x}{\mu} = \frac{U_\infty x}{\nu}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme)**
> The plate flow problem is the foundational case study for all convective transport. KTU expects students to (a) identify laminar, turbulent, and mixed regimes, (b) apply Blasius-type local and mean correlations, and (c) compute both friction (drag) and heat-transfer coefficients.

### 1.2 Transition Criterion — The Critical Reynolds Number

- For $Re_x < Re_{x,c}$ → **Laminar boundary layer**
- For $Re_x > Re_{x,c}$ → **Turbulent boundary layer**

The accepted standard (Blasius/Schlichting) is:

$$Re_{x,c} = \frac{\rho U_\infty x_c}{\mu} \approx 5 \times 10^5$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 5 × 10⁵?** It is the empirical benchmark for a smooth, flat plate under low-turbulence free-stream conditions. For higher free-stream turbulence intensity, transition may occur earlier (e.g., $3 \times 10^5$).

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **"The River Pebble" Analogy**
> Imagine dropping a smooth, flat pebble into a slow, glassy stream.
> - **Near the leading edge (inlet):** The water touches the pebble gently. Its motion is *ordered*, layer upon layer — this is **laminar flow**. The fluid glides in smooth, parallel sheets.
> - **Further downstream:** Friction along the surface slows down more water, and the shear between slow and fast layers becomes unstable. Tiny disturbances grow. The motion becomes chaotic, with eddies and mixing — this is **turbulent flow**.
> - **Mixed zone:** The transition isn't instant — there's a short overlap region where both regimes coexist.
>
> The thin "skin" of slowed water hugging the pebble is the **boundary layer**. Its thickness grows downstream like a wedge of molasses spreading on a tilted plate.

---

## 1.4 Types of Boundary Layers on a Flat Plate

| Boundary Layer | Driving Force | Governing Equation | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hydrodynamic (Velocity) BL** | Momentum diffusion (viscosity $\mu$) | $\tau_w = \mu \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right)_{y=0}$ | Friction / drag coefficient |
| **Thermal (Temperature) BL** | Thermal diffusion (conductivity $k$) | $q_w'' = -k \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial y}\right)_{y=0}$ | Heat transfer coefficient |

> [!NOTE]
> The **thermal boundary layer thickness** $\delta_t$ is related to the **hydrodynamic boundary layer thickness** $\delta$ by the **Prandtl number**: $\delta_t / \delta \approx Pr^{-1/3}$ for $Pr \ge 0.6$.
> - For **liquid metals** ($Pr \ll 1$): $\delta_t \gg \delta$ (heat diffuses far ahead of velocity).
> - For **oils** ($Pr \gg 1$): $\delta_t \ll \delta$ (heat diffuses slowly).

### 1.5 Key Physical Constants (Bolded for Quick Reference)

- **Critical Reynolds number, $Re_{x,c} = 5 \times 10^5$** (laminar-to-turbulent transition)
- **Free-stream conditions:** $U_\infty$, $T_\infty$ (far-field reference)
- **Wall (surface) conditions:** $T_s$ or $T_w$ (imposed)
- **Kinematic viscosity:** $\nu = \mu/\rho$ in **m²/s**
- **Prandtl number:** $Pr = \nu/\alpha = c_p \mu/k$ (dimensionless)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Setup and Coordinate System

Consider a flat plate of length $L$ and width $W$ aligned with the $x$-axis. The free-stream uniform velocity is $U_\infty$ in the $+x$ direction. We fix the origin at the leading edge.

The governing **boundary-layer equations** (Prandtl, 1904) are simplifications of the full Navier–Stokes equations valid inside the thin BL where $y \ll x$:

$$u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \nu \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} \quad \text{(x-momentum)}$$

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0 \quad \text{(continuity)}$$

with boundary conditions:
- $u(x, 0) = 0$, $\; v(x, 0) = 0$ (no-slip, impermeable)
- $u(x, y) \to U_\infty$ as $y \to \infty$

---

## 2.2 Local Skin Friction Coefficient — $C_{f,x}$

The **wall shear stress** is $\tau_w(x) = \mu (\partial u / \partial y)_{y=0}$, and the local friction coefficient is:

$$C_{f,x} = \frac{\tau_w(x)}{\frac{1}{2} \rho U_\infty^2}$$

---

## 2.3 Local Heat Transfer Coefficient — $h_x$ and Stanton Number

The local convection coefficient is:

$$h_x = \frac{q_w''(x)}{T_s - T_\infty} = \frac{-k (\partial T/\partial y)_{y=0}}{T_s - T_\infty}$$

The **local Stanton number** is:

$$St_x = \frac{h_x}{\rho c_p U_\infty}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — MEMORIZE FOR KTU ESE**

### A. Friction Coefficient Correlations

| Regime | Local $C_{f,x}$ | Average $C_{f,L}$ | Valid Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Laminar** (Blasius, 1908) | $C_{f,x} = \dfrac{0.664}{Re_x^{0.5}}$ | $C_{f,L} = \dfrac{1.328}{Re_L^{0.5}}$ | $Re_x < 5 \times 10^5$ |
| **Turbulent** (1/7-power) | $C_{f,x} = \dfrac{0.0592}{Re_x^{0.2}}$ | $C_{f,L} = \dfrac{0.074}{Re_L^{0.2}}$ | $Re_x > 5 \times 10^5$ |
| **Mixed** (laminar + turbulent) | — | $C_{f,L} = \dfrac{0.074}{Re_L^{0.2}} - \dfrac{1700}{Re_L}$ | Full plate |

### B. Heat Transfer Correlations (Constant $T_s$)

| Regime | Local $Nu_x$ | Average $Nu_L$ | Valid Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Laminar** (Pohlhausen) | $Nu_x = 0.332 \, Re_x^{0.5} \, Pr^{1/3}$ | $Nu_L = 0.664 \, Re_L^{0.5} \, Pr^{1/3}$ | $Pr \ge 0.6$ |
| **Turbulent** | $Nu_x = 0.0296 \, Re_x^{0.8} \, Pr^{1/3}$ | $Nu_L = 0.037 \, Re_L^{0.8} \, Pr^{1/3}$ | $0.6 \le Pr \le 60$ |
| **Mixed** | — | $Nu_L = (0.037 \, Re_L^{0.8} - 871) \, Pr^{1/3}$ | Full plate |

> [!NOTE]
> The friction coefficient formulas **do NOT depend on Pr**, while the heat-transfer correlations have $Pr^{1/3}$ dependence. This is the essence of the **Reynolds Analogy**.

### C. Reynolds and Modified Reynolds Analogy

| Analogy | Equation | Validity |
|---|---|---|
| Reynolds (original) | $St_x \equiv C_{f,x}/2$ | $Pr = 1$ (gases) |
| **Modified** (Chilton–Colburn) | $St_x \, Pr^{2/3} = C_{f,x}/2$ | $0.6 \le Pr \le 60$ |

### D. Drag Force on the Plate

$$F_D = C_{f,L} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho U_\infty^2 \cdot A$$

where $A = L \cdot W$ is the wetted area (one side).

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where is this used in industry?**
> - **Aerospace:** Airfoil/wing drag estimation; rocket nose-cone heating.
> - **Heat exchangers:** Flat plate solar collectors, electronic chip cooling fins.
> - **Marine:** Ship-hull skin-friction resistance; biofilm formation.
> - **Process industry:** Cooling of rolled metal sheets, glass tempering, paper drying.
> - **Building HVAC:** Wind loading on flat roof panels.

The local Stanton number directly links fluid friction to heat transfer, enabling the designer to use cheap pressure-drop measurements to predict thermal performance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Prandtl's Equations to Blasius Solution (Laminar)

### Step 1 — Stream Function Formulation
Since the flow is 2-D and incompressible, define a **stream function** $\psi(x, y)$ such that $u = \partial \psi / \partial y$ and $v = -\partial \psi / \partial x$. This automatically satisfies continuity.

### Step 2 — Similarity Transformation
Blasius (1908) introduced the **similarity variable**:

$$\eta = y \sqrt{\frac{U_\infty}{\nu x}}$$

and assumed a single-variable stream function:

$$\psi(x, y) = \sqrt{\nu U_\infty x} \, f(\eta)$$

The velocity components transform as:

$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = U_\infty f'(\eta)$$

$$v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = \tfrac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{\nu U_\infty}{x}} \, [\eta f'(\eta) - f(\eta)]$$

### Step 3 — Reduction to the Blasius ODE
Substituting $u$ and $v$ into the Prandtl x-momentum equation:

$$u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \nu \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}$$

yields the famous **Blasius ordinary differential equation**:

$$f''' + \tfrac{1}{2} f \, f'' = 0$$

with boundary conditions:
- $f(0) = 0$ (no penetration)
- $f'(0) = 0$ (no slip)
- $f'(\eta) \to 1$ as $\eta \to \infty$ (free stream)

### Step 4 — Wall Shear Stress from the Solution
The numerical solution (Runge–Kutta or series) gives $f''(0) \approx 0.332056$. Therefore:

$$\tau_w = \mu \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right)_{y=0} = \mu U_\infty f''(0) \sqrt{\frac{U_\infty}{\nu x}}$$

Substituting into the definition of $C_{f,x}$:

$$C_{f,x} = \frac{2 \tau_w}{\rho U_\infty^2} = \frac{0.664}{\sqrt{Re_x}} = \frac{0.664}{Re_x^{0.5}} \quad \blacksquare$$

### Step 5 — Average Friction Coefficient over Length $L$
The average is obtained by integrating:

$$C_{f,L} = \frac{1}{L} \int_0^L C_{f,x} \, dx = \frac{1}{L} \int_0^L \frac{0.664 \, \nu^{0.5}}{U_\infty^{0.5} x^{0.5}} dx$$

Carrying out the integral:

$$C_{f,L} = \frac{0.664 \, \nu^{0.5}}{L \, U_\infty^{0.5}} \left[ 2 x^{0.5} \right]_0^L = \frac{1.328}{\sqrt{Re_L}} = \frac{1.328}{Re_L^{0.5}} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Turbulent Local Friction (1/7-Power Velocity Profile)

### Step 1 — Assume a Power-Law Velocity Profile
Inside a turbulent boundary layer, the time-averaged velocity profile is well-approximated by:

$$\frac{u}{U_\infty} = \left(\frac{y}{\delta}\right)^{1/7} \quad \text{for } 0 \le y \le \delta$$

This is the **Prandtl 1/7th-power law**, valid for $Re_x$ in the range $5 \times 10^5$ to $10^7$.

### Step 2 — Wall Shear Stress
Differentiate to find the gradient at the wall:

$$\tau_w = \mu \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right)_{y=0} \rightarrow \text{not used directly}$$

Instead, we apply the **momentum integral equation**:

$$\frac{\tau_w}{\rho U_\infty^2} = \frac{d \theta}{dx}$$

where the **momentum thickness** is:

$$\theta = \int_0^{\delta} \frac{u}{U_\infty} \left(1 - \frac{u}{U_\infty}\right) dy$$

With the 1/7-power profile:

$$\theta = \int_0^{\delta} \left[\left(\frac{y}{\delta}\right)^{1/7} - \left(\frac{y}{\delta}\right)^{2/7}\right] dy = \delta \left[\frac{7}{8} - \frac{7}{9}\right] = \frac{7 \delta}{72}$$

### Step 3 — Kármán Integral Equation

$$\frac{\tau_w}{\rho U_\infty^2} = \frac{d\theta}{dx}$$

Substituting $\theta = 7\delta/72$ and noting that $\tau_w$ for the turbulent case can be expressed via $\delta$:

$$\frac{0.0225 \rho U_\infty^2}{(U_\infty \delta / \nu)^{0.25}} = \frac{7}{72} \frac{d\delta}{dx}$$

After integration (using $\delta = 0$ at $x = 0$):

$$\delta(x) = \frac{0.382 \, x}{Re_x^{1/5}}$$

### Step 4 — Local Friction Coefficient
From $C_{f,x} = 2 \tau_w / (\rho U_\infty^2)$ and the integral form:

$$C_{f,x} = \frac{0.0592}{Re_x^{0.2}} \quad \blacksquare$$

### Step 5 — Average Coefficient
Integrating $C_{f,x}$ over $L$ from $x_c$ to $L$ (with the laminar part from $0$ to $x_c$):

$$C_{f,L} = \frac{0.074}{Re_L^{0.2}} - \frac{1700}{Re_L} \quad \blacksquare$$

(The "1700" term comes from the laminar contribution using $x_c$ such that $Re_{x_c} = 5 \times 10^5$.)

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Local Nusselt Number (Laminar, Constant $T_s$)

### Step 1 — Energy Boundary-Layer Equation
The thermal BL is governed by:

$$u \frac{\partial T}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial T}{\partial y} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial y^2}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Reynolds Analogy
For laminar flow, momentum and thermal BLs have the same dimensionless form when $Pr = 1$. For $Pr \ne 1$, the analogy can be extended using the **exact solution by Pohlhausen**. The result for a constant-wall-temperature plate is:

$$\frac{\delta_t}{\delta} = \frac{1}{1.026} Pr^{-1/3} \quad (Pr \gtrsim 0.6)$$

### Step 3 — Derive the Heat Flux
The thermal gradient at the wall is:

$$-k \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial y}\right)_{y=0} = k \frac{T_s - T_\infty}{\delta_t} \quad \text{(linear profile approximation)}$$

Therefore:

$$h_x = \frac{k}{\delta_t} = \frac{1.026 \, k \, Pr^{1/3}}{\delta}$$

Using $\delta \approx 5.0 \, x / Re_x^{0.5}$ for the laminar BL:

$$h_x = \frac{1.026 \, k \, Pr^{1/3}}{5.0 \, x / Re_x^{0.5}} = \frac{0.332 \, k}{x} Re_x^{0.5} Pr^{1/3}$$

### Step 4 — Local Nusselt Number
Dividing by $k/x$:

$$Nu_x = \frac{h_x x}{k} = 0.332 \, Re_x^{0.5} \, Pr^{1/3} \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> The same factor $0.332$ appears in $C_{f,x} = 0.664/Re_x^{0.5}$ — this is **not a coincidence**! It directly reflects the Reynolds analogy:
> $$St_x \, Pr^{2/3} = \frac{C_{f,x}}{2} = \frac{0.332}{Re_x^{0.5}}$$
> Hence $St_x = 0.332 \, Re_x^{-0.5} \, Pr^{-2/3}$ and $Nu_x = St_x \, Re_x \, Pr = 0.332 \, Re_x^{0.5} \, Pr^{1/3}$. ✓

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Model Style)

**Problem:** Air at $T_\infty = 300$ K and $U_\infty = 3$ m/s flows over a flat plate of length $L = 2$ m maintained at $T_s = 350$ K. Compute (a) $Re_L$, (b) regime, (c) $C_{f,L}$, (d) drag force per unit width, (e) average $h$.

**Air properties at 300 K:** $\nu = 15.89 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s, $k = 0.0263$ W/m·K, $Pr = 0.707$.

### Solution

**(a) Reynolds number at $L$:**
$$Re_L = \frac{U_\infty L}{\nu} = \frac{3 \times 2}{15.89 \times 10^{-6}} = 3.776 \times 10^5$$

**(b) Regime identification:** $Re_L = 3.776 \times 10^5 < 5 \times 10^5$ → **Fully laminar** throughout.

**(c) Average friction coefficient (laminar):**
$$C_{f,L} = \frac{1.328}{Re_L^{0.5}} = \frac{1.328}{(3.776 \times 10^5)^{0.5}} = \frac{1.328}{614.5} = 2.161 \times 10^{-3}$$

**(d) Drag per unit width ($W = 1$ m):**
$$F_D / W = C_{f,L} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho U_\infty^2 \cdot L = (2.161 \times 10^{-3})(0.5)(1.177)(9)(2) = 0.0229 \text{ N/m}$$

**(e) Average heat transfer coefficient:**
$$Nu_L = 0.664 \, Re_L^{0.5} Pr^{1/3} = 0.664 (614.5)(0.829) = 338.4$$
$$\bar{h} = Nu_L \cdot k / L = 338.4 \times 0.0263 / 2 = 4.45 \text{ W/m}^2 \cdot \text{K}$$

✅ This single problem covers **all four** KTU high-yield formulas.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Flow Regime Identification

This decision-flow diagram walks a student through the **regime classification** that is the first step in every KTU flat-plate problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Flat plate problem given] --> B[Compute Re sub L equals U infinity times L over nu]
    B --> C{Re sub L less than 5 times 10 to power 5?}
    C -- YES --> D[FULLY LAMINAR: Use Blasius correlations]
    C -- NO --> D2{Re sub L greater than 5 times 10 to power 5?}
    D2 -- YES --> E[Compute x sub c from Re sub xc equals 5 times 10 to power 5]
    E --> F[MIXED BOUNDARY LAYER: Laminar from 0 to x sub c and Turbulent from x sub c to L]
    F --> G[Use Mixed BL average correlation: Cf L equals 0.074 over Re sub L to 0.2 minus 1700 over Re sub L]
    D --> H[Local Cf x equals 0.664 over Re sub x to 0.5]
    D --> I[Local Nu x equals 0.332 Re sub x to 0.5 Pr to 1 over 3]
    G --> J[Apply Reynolds Analogy if Pr equals 1: St equals Cf over 2]
    I --> K[Compute drag force F sub D equals Cf L half rho U squared A]
    H --> K
    J --> L[Compute heat transfer q equals h A delta T]
    K --> M[END: Final answers in SI units]
    L --> M
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** A KTU problem is solved by first classifying the regime, *then* selecting the appropriate row from the Formula Sheet in SECTION 2.4. A common student error is applying the **turbulent** correlation to a **laminar** plate (or vice-versa) — the regime check in Box C is the safeguard.

## 4.2 Boundary Layer Structure (Schematic Block Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LEADING EDGE
        LE[Leading edge x equals 0]
    end
    LE -->|Laminar BL| Z1[Region 0 to x sub c: Smooth orderly flow]
    Z1 --> Z2[Transition Zone: Small unstable region]
    Z2 --> Z3[Region x sub c to L: Turbulent BL with chaotic eddies]
    Z3 --> Z4[Free stream U infinity at outer edge]
    Z1 -.->|delta grows as x over Re to 0.5| G1[delta laminar]
    Z3 -.->|delta grows as x over Re to 0.2| G2[delta turbulent]
    G2 --> G1
```

## 4.3 Analogy Map — Momentum ↔ Heat Transfer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Friction Coefficient Cf x] -- Reynolds Analogy Pr equals 1 --> S1[Stanton Number St x]
    M1 -- Modified Analogy Pr not equals 1 --> S2[St x Pr to 2 over 3 equals Cf x over 2]
    S1 --> H1[Heat Transfer Coefficient h x equals St x rho c p U infinity]
    S2 --> H2[h x equals Cf x over 2 times rho c p U infinity times Pr to negative 2 over 3]
    H1 --> N1[Local Nusselt Number Nu x equals h x x over k]
    H2 --> N2[Nu x equals Cf x over 2 Re x Pr to 1 over 3]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked to "use the Reynolds analogy to find $h_x$ given $C_{f,x}$," always write the **modified** analogy (with $Pr^{2/3}$), unless $Pr$ is explicitly stated as 1. Partial credit is lost if the $Pr^{2/3}$ term is omitted for $Pr \ne 1$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A, 3 Marks]
**Define the hydrodynamic and thermal boundary layer thicknesses over a flat plate. How are they related for a fluid with Prandtl number greater than 1?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **hydrodynamic boundary layer thickness** $\delta$ is the distance from the plate surface in the $y$-direction at which the fluid velocity reaches **99% of the free-stream velocity** $U_\infty$ (formally $u = 0.99\, U_\infty$).

The **thermal boundary layer thickness** $\delta_t$ is the distance at which the temperature difference $(T - T_s)$ reaches **99% of $(T_\infty - T_s)$**, i.e., the fluid has effectively reached the free-stream temperature.

For a fluid with $Pr > 1$ (e.g., oils), thermal diffusivity $\alpha$ is much smaller than momentum diffusivity $\nu$. Heat diffuses more slowly than momentum, so the thermal BL is **thinner** than the hydrodynamic BL:

$$\frac{\delta_t}{\delta} \approx Pr^{-1/3} < 1$$

✅ Award 1 mark each for: (a) definition of $\delta$, (b) definition of $\delta_t$, (c) correct $\delta_t/\delta$ relation with $Pr > 1$ implication.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, 3 Marks]
**State the Reynolds analogy between heat transfer and fluid friction. Under what conditions is the modified Chilton–Colburn analogy applicable?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Reynolds Analogy (1874):** The local heat transfer (Stanton number) is directly proportional to the local skin friction:

$$St_x = \frac{C_{f,x}}{2}$$

It is valid only when:
- The Prandtl number $Pr = 1$ (e.g., gases like air at moderate temperatures).
- No pressure gradient (flat plate geometry).
- No viscous dissipation.
- Constant fluid properties.

**Modified Analogy (Chilton–Colburn, 1933):** Extends the analogy to fluids with $Pr \ne 1$:

$$St_x \, Pr^{2/3} = \frac{C_{f,x}}{2}$$

Applicable in the range $\mathbf{0.6 \le Pr \le 60}$, encompassing gases, water, and light oils.

✅ 1 mark for original analogy equation, 1 mark for $Pr = 1$ condition, 1 mark for modified analogy with $Pr$ range.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Unit errors are the #1 mark-loser.** Always quote $\nu$ in m²/s, $h$ in W/m²·K, $F_D$ in N. Mixing CGS (cm, cal) with SI forfeits 1–2 marks.
> - **Regime misclassification:** Always compute $Re_L$ *first*. A student applying $C_{f,L} = 0.074/Re_L^{0.2}$ to a plate with $Re_L = 10^5$ (laminar) loses **all** 7 friction marks.
> - **Substituting $x$ instead of $L$ in average formulas:** $Nu_x$ and $C_{f,x}$ are LOCAL. To get averages, integrate OR use the "average" formula with $Re_L$. Markers scrutinize this.
> - **Forgetting the $Pr^{1/3}$ term** in the turbulent correlation = 2 marks lost.

---

### QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, 14 Marks]
**(a)** Air at $20°$C and $5$ m/s flows over a flat plate of length $3$ m maintained at $80°$C. Determine:
1. The critical distance $x_c$ where transition begins.
2. The local friction and heat transfer coefficients at $x = x_c$.
3. The average friction coefficient over the full plate.
4. The total drag force on one side of the plate (width $W = 1.5$ m).

**(b)** If the free-stream velocity is doubled to $10$ m/s while the plate length is reduced to $1$ m, recompute the average Nusselt number. Comment on the change.

**Given:** Air at 50°C (film temp): $\nu = 17.47 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s, $k = 0.02735$ W/m·K, $Pr = 0.7228$, $\rho = 1.092$ kg/m³, $c_p = 1006$ J/kg·K.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a):**

**(a.1) Critical distance $x_c$:**

$$x_c = \frac{Re_{x,c} \cdot \nu}{U_\infty} = \frac{5 \times 10^5 \times 17.47 \times 10^{-6}}{5} = 1.747 \text{ m}$$

**(a.2) Local coefficients at $x = x_c$:**

$$C_{f,x_c} = \frac{0.664}{(5 \times 10^5)^{0.5}} = \frac{0.664}{707.1} = 9.39 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$Nu_{x_c} = 0.332 (5 \times 10^5)^{0.5} (0.7228)^{1/3} = 0.332 \times 707.1 \times 0.8985 = 210.9$$

$$h_{x_c} = \frac{Nu_{x_c} \cdot k}{x_c} = \frac{210.9 \times 0.02735}{1.747} = 3.30 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$$

**(a.3) Average friction coefficient (MIXED):**
$Re_L = (5 \times 3)/17.47 \times 10^{-6} = 8.587 \times 10^5 > 5 \times 10^5$ → mixed BL.

$$C_{f,L} = \frac{0.074}{(8.587 \times 10^5)^{0.2}} - \frac{1700}{8.587 \times 10^5}$$

$$(8.587 \times 10^5)^{0.2} = 15.14$$

$$C_{f,L} = \frac{0.074}{15.14} - 1.98 \times 10^{-3} = 4.887 \times 10^{-3} - 1.98 \times 10^{-3} = 2.91 \times 10^{-3}$$

**(a.4) Total drag force:**

$$F_D = C_{f,L} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho U_\infty^2 \cdot (L \cdot W)$$

$$F_D = 2.91 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5 \times 1.092 \times 25 \times (3 \times 1.5)$$

$$F_D = 2.91 \times 10^{-3} \times 13.65 \times 4.5 = 0.179 \text{ N}$$

**Part (b):**

New conditions: $U_\infty = 10$ m/s, $L = 1$ m.

$$Re_L = \frac{10 \times 1}{17.47 \times 10^{-6}} = 5.724 \times 10^5 > 5 \times 10^5 \rightarrow \text{mixed}$$

Using the **mixed average Nusselt** correlation:

$$Nu_L = (0.037 \, Re_L^{0.8} - 871) Pr^{1/3}$$

$Re_L^{0.8} = (5.724 \times 10^5)^{0.8}$. Compute: $\ln(5.724 \times 10^5) = 13.257$, $\times 0.8 = 10.605$, $e^{10.605} = 4.01 \times 10^4$.

$$Nu_L = (0.037 \times 4.01 \times 10^4 - 871) \times 0.8985 = (1484 - 871) \times 0.8985 = 551$$

**Comment:** The Nusselt number **increases** with velocity (steeper velocity gradient → higher convection) but the **plate is shorter**. Net effect on $\bar{h}$:

$$\bar{h} = Nu_L \cdot k / L = 551 \times 0.02735 / 1 = 15.07 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$$

Compared to case (a) where $h_{x_c} = 3.30$ W/m²·K, the average $h$ has increased roughly **4.5 times**, confirming that velocity scaling dominates over length reduction in forced convection.

---

### QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – Sample, 14 Marks]
**(a)** A thin, heated flat plate of length $1.5$ m and width $1$ m is exposed to atmospheric air at $25°$C moving at $4$ m/s. The plate is at uniform temperature $T_s = 95°$C. Compute the **rate of heat loss** from the plate, assuming the boundary layer is fully turbulent.

**(b)** For the same plate, determine the **friction drag** and the **total pumping power** required to overcome it, assuming a fan with 60% efficiency supplies the air.

**Given (air at film temp 60°C):** $\nu = 18.9 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s, $k = 0.02808$ W/m·K, $Pr = 0.72$, $\rho = 1.059$ kg/m³, $c_p = 1008$ J/kg·K.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a):**

Step 1 — Check regime:
$$Re_L = \frac{U_\infty L}{\nu} = \frac{4 \times 1.5}{18.9 \times 10^{-6}} = 3.175 \times 10^5$$

❗ **Regime check:** $Re_L = 3.175 \times 10^5 < 5 \times 10^5$ → **LAMINAR**, not turbulent!

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Trap:** The question states "assuming turbulent," but the data gives $Re_L < 5 \times 10^5$. A careful student should *re-flag* this in the answer and proceed with the **laminar** correlation, noting the assumption. Award full marks for correct computation under the proper regime. *(If KTU genuinely intended turbulent, the assumed answer key uses turbulent formulas — present both for safety.)*

**Assuming turbulent (as per the question's directive):**

$$Nu_L = 0.037 \, Re_L^{0.8} Pr^{1/3}$$

$\ln(3.175 \times 10^5) = 12.67$, $\times 0.8 = 10.14$, $e^{10.14} = 2.51 \times 10^4$.

$$Nu_L = 0.037 \times 2.51 \times 10^4 \times (0.72)^{1/3} = 928.7 \times 0.896 = 832.0$$

$$\bar{h} = Nu_L \cdot k / L = 832.0 \times 0.02808 / 1.5 = 15.58 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$$

$$Q = \bar{h} \, A \, (T_s - T_\infty) = 15.58 \times (1.5 \times 1) \times 70 = 1636 \text{ W}$$

**Correct (laminar) alternative:**
$$Nu_L = 0.664 \, Re_L^{0.5} Pr^{1/3} = 0.664 \times 563.5 \times 0.896 = 335.2$$
$$\bar{h} = 335.2 \times 0.02808 / 1.5 = 6.27 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$$
$$Q = 6.27 \times 1.5 \times 70 = 658 \text{ W}$$

**Part (b):**

Friction coefficient (using turbulent average, as directed):
$$C_{f,L} = \frac{0.074}{Re_L^{0.2}} = \frac{0.074}{(3.175 \times 10^5)^{0.2}}$$

$Re_L^{0.2}$: $\ln(3.175 \times 10^5) \times 0.2 = 12.67 \times 0.2 = 2.534$, $e^{2.534} = 12.60$.

$$C_{f,L} = 0.074 / 12.60 = 5.87 \times 10^{-3}$$

Drag force:
$$F_D = C_{f,L} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho U_\infty^2 \cdot A = 5.87 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5 \times 1.059 \times 16 \times 1.5$$
$$F_D = 0.0746 \text{ N}$$

Power to overcome drag (pumping power for the moving air):
$$\dot{W}_{ideal} = F_D \cdot U_\infty = 0.0746 \times 4 = 0.298 \text{ W}$$

Accounting for fan efficiency $\eta = 0.6$:
$$\dot{W}_{fan} = \dot{W}_{ideal} / \eta = 0.298 / 0.6 = 0.497 \text{ W}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The drag power is **negligibly small** (~0.5 W) compared to the heat-transfer rate (~1636 W). This explains why flat-plate convective heat transfer is so widely used — the "fans cost" is trivial relative to the heat exchanged.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMOREMBER

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This)**

- ✅ **Transition Reynolds number:** $Re_{x,c} = 5 \times 10^5$ — never write $3 \times 10^5$ unless the problem specifies higher turbulence intensity.
- ✅ **Laminar local:** $C_{f,x} = 0.664/Re_x^{0.5}$, $Nu_x = 0.332\, Re_x^{0.5}\, Pr^{1/3}$.
- ✅ **Laminar average:** $C_{f,L} = 1.328/Re_L^{0.5}$, $Nu_L = 0.664\, Re_L^{0.5}\, Pr^{1/3}$.
- ✅ **Turbulent local:** $C_{f,x} = 0.0592/Re_x^{0.2}$, $Nu_x = 0.0296\, Re_x^{0.8}\, Pr^{1/3}$.
- ✅ **Turbulent average:** $C_{f,L} = 0.074/Re_L^{0.2}$, $Nu_L = 0.037\, Re_L^{0.8}\, Pr^{1/3}$.
- ✅ **Mixed plate (laminar + turbulent):**
  - $C_{f,L} = 0.074/Re_L^{0.2} - 1700/Re_L$
  - $Nu_L = (0.037\, Re_L^{0.8} - 871)\, Pr^{1/3}$
- ✅ **Memorize the $\mathbf{1700}$ and $\mathbf{871}$ constants** — they appear repeatedly in KTU papers.
- ✅ **Thermal vs. hydrodynamic BL:** $\delta_t/\delta \approx Pr^{-1/3}$ for $Pr \gtrsim 0.6$.
- ✅ **Reynolds analogy:** $St = C_f/2$ (for $Pr = 1$); $St\, Pr^{2/3} = C_f/2$ (for $0.6 \le Pr \le 60$).
- ✅ **Drag formula:** $F_D = C_{f,L} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho U_\infty^2 \cdot A_{wetted}$.
- ✅ **Always start with regime check:** Compute $Re_L$ *first*, then pick the row of formulas.
- ✅ **Use film temperature** $T_f = (T_s + T_\infty)/2$ to evaluate all fluid properties.
- ✅ **Unit check:** $Re$ is dimensionless; $Nu$ is dimensionless; $h$ is W/m²·K; $F_D$ is N.
- ✅ **"Average"** in the formula sheet means over the plate length $L$, NOT the time-average of turbulent fluctuations.
- ✅ **For liquid metals** ($Pr \ll 1$): use specialized correlations (e.g., $Nu_x = 0.565\, Re_x^{0.5}\, Pr^{0.5}$ for constant $q_w''$); the $Pr^{1/3}$ law breaks down.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Numerical Problems of Practical Interest
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Numerical Problems of Practical Interest

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of "Numerical Problems of Practical Interest" in Heat Transfer

In the context of **PCCHT402 – Heat Transfer Operations (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, *Numerical Problems of Practical Interest* refer to the application-oriented, multi-variable engineering calculations that integrate the three fundamental modes of heat transfer — **conduction**, **convection**, and **radiation** — into real-world equipment and process geometries. These problems are not purely academic; they are designed to mirror the calculations a chemical/thermal engineer would perform in industry while designing or rating heat exchange equipment, insulated pipelines, furnace walls, reactor jackets, reboilers, condensers, and radiating surfaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 2 Context):**
> Numerical Problems of Practical Interest include composite resistance problems (plane walls, cylinders, spheres, fins), problems involving the **critical radius of insulation**, determination of the **overall heat transfer coefficient (U)** for combined conduction-convection systems, two-dimensional steady-state conduction where applicable, and introductory heat-exchanger rating problems using **LMTD** and **overall ΔT**. The emphasis is on translating a physical word-problem into a thermal resistance network, solving it, and interpreting the result.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water-Flow Through Pipes" Picture

Imagine three kinds of "pipes" carrying a fluid called **heat**:

1. **Conduction pipes** — heat flows through a solid wall much like water flowing through a narrow pipe. The "pipe" has a fixed resistance ($R$) that depends on thickness ($L$) and the wall's thermal conductivity ($k$). A thin copper wall is a fat, low-resistance pipe; a thick insulating brick is a skinny, high-resistance pipe.

2. **Convection pipes** — heat must jump from a solid surface to a moving fluid (or vice versa). This is like a turbulent waterfall with splash losses. The "splash loss" is quantified by the **convective heat transfer coefficient, $h$**, with units $\text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$.

3. **Radiation pipes** — heat travels as electromagnetic waves, like a flashlight beam, even through a vacuum. This is governed by **view factors, emissivities, and the Stefan–Boltzmann constant $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}^4$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Big Idea (KTU Board Favourite):**
> Every practical problem in this module is solved by drawing a **thermal resistance network** — analogous to an electrical circuit where heat flow $q$ corresponds to current, temperature difference $\Delta T$ to voltage, and thermal resistance $R$ to electrical resistance ($q = \Delta T / R$). Students who master this analogy routinely score 14/14 on numerical parts.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Practical Heat Transfer Problems

| Pillar | Physical Picture | Governing Equation | Typical Industrial Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Conduction ($k$) | Heat diffusion through a stationary medium | Fourier's Law: $q = -kA\,\dfrac{dT}{dx}$ | Refractory furnace walls, pipe insulation |
| Convection ($h$) | Heat transfer between a surface and a moving fluid | Newton's Law of Cooling: $q = hA\,\Delta T$ | Boiler tubes, reactor cooling coils |
| Radiation ($\varepsilon, F$) | Heat transfer by EM waves | Stefan–Boltzmann: $q = \varepsilon \sigma A\,T^4$ | Furnace chambers, solar collectors |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Standard Constants (memorize these):**
> - Stefan–Boltzmann constant: $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}^4$
> - Most engineering calculations use absolute temperature in **Kelvin (K)**
> - Overall heat transfer coefficient $U$ has units $\text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Thermal Resistance Network Philosophy

Every practical heat transfer problem in Module 2 can be reduced to **one of two networks**:

- **Series network** → heat flows through multiple layers one after the other (e.g., furnace wall: refractory → insulating brick → steel casing → outside air film)
- **Parallel network** → heat splits and flows through different paths simultaneously (e.g., finned surface where fin and unfinned portions conduct in parallel)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use resistance networks?**
> Because once you reduce a problem to an equivalent resistance, you can write a single Ohm's-law-style equation: $q = \dfrac{\Delta T_{overall}}{R_{total}}$. This works for both steady-state and quasi-steady industrial designs.

## 2.2 Formula Cheat Sheet (KTU Board Exam Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** All absolute values and divisions in the table below use $\vert$ or $\mid$ instead of the bare pipe character to protect the markdown table.

| # | Geometry / Situation | Thermal Resistance Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Plane wall (conduction) | $R_{cond} = \dfrac{L}{kA}$ | $L$ = thickness [m], $k$ = W/m·K, $A$ = m² |
| 2 | Hollow cylinder (conduction) | $R_{cond} = \dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L}$ | $r_1, r_2$ = inner/outer radius [m], $L$ = length [m] |
| 3 | Hollow sphere (conduction) | $R_{cond} = \dfrac{1}{4\pi k}\left(\dfrac{1}{r_1}-\dfrac{1}{r_2}\right)$ | $r_1, r_2$ = inner/outer radius [m] |
| 4 | Convective film | $R_{conv} = \dfrac{1}{hA}$ | $h$ = W/m²·K, $A$ = m² |
| 5 | Radiation between surfaces | $R_{rad} = \dfrac{1}{h_r A_1}$ where $h_r = \varepsilon \sigma (T_1^2+T_2^2)(T_1+T_2)$ | $h_r$ = radiative coefficient [W/m²·K] |
| 6 | Plane composite wall (series) | $\dfrac{1}{UA} = \sum_i \dfrac{L_i}{k_i A}$ | Sum of individual layer resistances |
| 7 | Cylindrical composite | $\dfrac{1}{U_i A_i} = \sum_i \dfrac{\ln(r_{i+1}/r_i)}{2\pi k_i L}$ | Use **consistent area** reference |
| 8 | Critical radius of insulation (cylinder) | $r_{cr} = \dfrac{k_{ins}}{h_{outside}}$ | $k_{ins}$ = insulation conductivity, $h_o$ = outside $h$ |
| 9 | Heat loss from a fin | $q_{fin} = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b\,\tanh(mL)$ | $m = \sqrt{hP/kA_c}$ |
| 10 | Heat exchanger (LMTD) | $q = UA\,\Delta T_{LM}$ | $\Delta T_{LM} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$ |
| 11 | Log Mean Temperature Difference | $\Delta T_{LM} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$ | Counter-flow or parallel-flow $\Delta T$ values |
| 12 | Stefan–Boltzmann law | $q = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4$ | $T$ in Kelvin |

### 2.3 The "Overall U" Master Equation

For any composite system with **convection–conduction–convection** in series, the *overall heat transfer coefficient* $U$ is defined relative to a chosen reference area $A$:

$$\frac{1}{U\,A} = \frac{1}{h_i A_i} + \sum_{\text{layers}}\frac{L}{k A_{\text{layer}}} + \frac{1}{h_o A_o}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often compute $U$ at one area (say inside) and then multiply by a *different* area (say outside) when finding $q$. The product $UA$ is invariant; the individual terms $U_i A_i$ and $U_o A_o$ are **NOT** equal. Always pick one area reference and stay consistent.

## 2.4 Critical Radius of Insulation — A Counterintuitive Result

When you insulate a small-diameter pipe or wire, adding insulation *initially increases* heat loss, not decreases it! This happens because the outside surface area grows faster than the conductive resistance. The point of maximum heat loss occurs at:

$$r_{cr} = \frac{k_{\text{ins}}}{h_{outside}}$$

- If $r_{pipe} < r_{cr}$ → adding insulation **increases** $q$ (bad design for small wires)
- If $r_{pipe} > r_{cr}$ → adding insulation **decreases** $q$ (normal expected behavior)

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Implication:** Bare electrical heating elements (e.g., cartridge heaters) often have radii deliberately kept below $r_{cr}$ so that the small added insulation raises the heat dissipation rate — exactly opposite of intuition.

## 2.5 Heat Exchanger Rating Using LMTD

The **Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)** is the correct average driving force for a double-pipe or shell-and-tube exchanger:

$$\Delta T_{LM} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\!\left(\frac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}$$

where $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ are the temperature differences at the two ends of the exchanger (each end's hot-fluid-minus-cold-fluid difference). For a counter-flow arrangement, $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ are the temperature differences at opposite ends; for co-current (parallel) flow, they are at the same ends but with the streams moving in the same direction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Use:** Every chemical plant uses the LMTD method (or the equivalent NTU method) to *rate* an existing exchanger (find $q$ or outlet $T$) or to *size* a new one (find $A$ for a desired duty).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Heat Flow Through a Composite Cylindrical Wall (Insulated Steam Pipe)

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

A steam pipe of inner radius $r_1 = 0.05$ m and outer radius $r_2 = 0.055$ m carries saturated steam. It is lagged with two layers of insulation:

- **Layer 1 (inner insulation):** $r_2$ to $r_3 = 0.075$ m, $k_1 = 0.07$ W/m·K
- **Layer 2 (outer insulation):** $r_3$ to $r_4 = 0.10$ m, $k_2 = 0.10$ W/m·K

The inside convective coefficient (steam to pipe) is $h_i = 5000$ W/m²·K, the outside convective coefficient (outer air) is $h_o = 10$ W/m²·K. Steam temperature $T_{steam} = 453$ K, ambient air $T_\infty = 303$ K. Pipe length $L = 10$ m. Pipe wall $k_{steel} = 45$ W/m·K. Find the heat loss per metre of pipe.

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Identify the Resistance Network

Heat flows through **five resistances in series** (per unit length):

1. Inside convective film: $R_i = \dfrac{1}{h_i (2\pi r_1 L)}$
2. Steel pipe conduction: $R_{steel} = \dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k_{steel} L}$
3. Inner insulation conduction: $R_1 = \dfrac{\ln(r_3/r_2)}{2\pi k_1 L}$
4. Outer insulation conduction: $R_2 = \dfrac{\ln(r_4/r_3)}{2\pi k_2 L}$
5. Outside convective film: $R_o = \dfrac{1}{h_o (2\pi r_4 L)}$

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Plug Numerical Values

We will compute each resistance with $L = 10$ m.

**Resistance 1 — Inside film:**

$$R_i = \frac{1}{5000 \times 2\pi \times 0.05 \times 10} = \frac{1}{15\,707.96}$$

$$R_i = 6.366 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{K/W}$$

**Resistance 2 — Steel pipe:**

$$R_{steel} = \frac{\ln(0.055/0.05)}{2\pi \times 45 \times 10} = \frac{\ln(1.1)}{2827.43} = \frac{0.09531}{2827.43}$$

$$R_{steel} = 3.371 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{K/W}$$

**Resistance 3 — Inner insulation layer:**

$$R_1 = \frac{\ln(0.075/0.055)}{2\pi \times 0.07 \times 10} = \frac{\ln(1.3636)}{4.3982} = \frac{0.31015}{4.3982}$$

$$R_1 = 7.052 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{K/W}$$

**Resistance 4 — Outer insulation layer:**

$$R_2 = \frac{\ln(0.10/0.075)}{2\pi \times 0.10 \times 10} = \frac{\ln(1.3333)}{6.2832} = \frac{0.28768}{6.2832}$$

$$R_2 = 4.579 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{K/W}$$

**Resistance 5 — Outside film:**

$$R_o = \frac{1}{10 \times 2\pi \times 0.10 \times 10} = \frac{1}{62.832}$$

$$R_o = 1.5915 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{K/W}$$

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Total Resistance

$$R_{total} = R_i + R_{steel} + R_1 + R_2 + R_o$$

$$R_{total} = (0.0000637) + (0.0000337) + (0.07052) + (0.04579) + (0.01592)$$

$$R_{total} = 0.13232\ \text{K/W}$$

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Heat Loss Calculation

$$q = \frac{T_{steam} - T_\infty}{R_{total}} = \frac{453 - 303}{0.13232} = \frac{150}{0.13232}$$

$$q = 1133.6\ \text{W} \quad \text{(for 10 m pipe)}$$

Per metre of pipe:

$$q/L = 113.36\ \text{W/m}$$

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Interpretation

- The **steel pipe and inside film** resistances are *negligible* (combined $< 0.01\%$ of total).
- The **inner insulation** contributes the largest single resistance (53%).
- The **outer insulation and outside air film** together account for ~46%.
- This is why insulation thickness and outside $h_o$ (wind speed!) dominate the design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight for KTU students:** Always compute *all* resistances first, then identify the dominant one. Often the answer to "where is heat loss controlled?" lies in the largest resistance, not the thinnest layer.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Critical Radius of Insulation and Maximum Heat Loss

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup

A bare copper wire of radius $r_1 = 0.001$ m is to be insulated with rubber ($k_{ins} = 0.15$ W/m·K). The wire surface temperature is held at $T_1 = 400$ K, ambient $T_\infty = 300$ K, and $h_o = 20$ W/m²·K. Find (a) the critical radius, (b) the heat loss at $r = r_{cr}$, and (c) the heat loss at $r_2 = 0.005$ m.

### 3.2.2 Step 1 — Derive the Heat Loss Expression

For a long insulated cylinder with inside surface at $T_1$ (negligible inside resistance) and convection outside:

$$q = \frac{T_1 - T_\infty}{R_{total}} = \frac{2\pi L (T_1 - T_\infty)}{\dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k_{ins}} + \dfrac{1}{h_o r_2}}$$

For a thin wire, $r_1$ is fixed and the denominator depends on $r_2$:

$$f(r_2) = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k_{ins}} + \frac{1}{h_o r_2}$$

### 3.2.3 Step 2 — Find the Critical Radius

Set $\dfrac{df}{dr_2} = 0$:

$$\frac{1}{k_{ins} r_2} - \frac{1}{h_o r_2^2} = 0$$

$$\frac{1}{k_{ins} r_2} = \frac{1}{h_o r_2^2}$$

$$r_2 = \frac{k_{ins}}{h_o}$$

Substituting values:

$$r_{cr} = \frac{0.15}{20} = 0.0075\ \text{m} = 7.5\ \text{mm}$$

### 3.2.4 Step 3 — Maximum Heat Loss at Critical Radius

At $r_2 = r_{cr}$, the expression simplifies (a standard textbook result):

$$q_{max} = \frac{2\pi L (T_1 - T_\infty) h_o}{1 + h_o r_{cr}/k_{ins} \cdot \ln(r_{cr}/r_1)}$$

With $r_{cr}/k_{ins} = 1/h_o$ and $h_o r_{cr}/k_{ins} = 1$:

$$q_{max} = \frac{2\pi L (T_1 - T_\infty) h_o}{1 + \ln(r_{cr}/r_1)} = \frac{2\pi L \times 100 \times 20}{1 + \ln(7.5)}$$

$$\ln(7.5) = 2.0149$$

$$q_{max} = \frac{2\pi L \times 2000}{3.0149} = \frac{12\,566.4\,L}{3.0149}$$

$$q_{max} = 4168.4\ L\ \text{W}\quad(\text{for } L \text{ in metres})$$

For $L = 1$ m: $q_{max} \approx 4168$ W/m.

### 3.2.5 Step 4 — Heat Loss at $r_2 = 0.005$ m

$$q = \frac{2\pi L (100)}{\dfrac{\ln(0.005/0.001)}{0.15} + \dfrac{1}{20 \times 0.005}}$$

$$\ln(5) = 1.6094$$

$$q = \frac{628.32 L}{10.729 + 10.0} = \frac{628.32 L}{20.729} = 30.31 L\ \text{W}$$

For $L = 1$ m: $q \approx 30.3$ W/m.

### 3.2.6 Step 5 — Interpretation

| Case | Heat loss per metre | Observation |
|---|---|---|
| Bare wire (assume negligible conduction resistance) | $\sim 125.7$ W/m | Baseline |
| $r_2 = 0.005$ m (5 mm insulation) | 30.3 W/m | **Decreased** (good) |
| $r_2 = r_{cr} = 0.0075$ m | 4168 W/m | **Maximum** (catastrophic!) |
| $r_2 = 0.02$ m (large insulation) | decreases again | Normal behavior resumes |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Lesson:** For a thin wire ($r_1 = 1$ mm $\ll r_{cr} = 7.5$ mm), adding insulation up to 7.5 mm radius *increases* heat loss by more than 100×. Always check $r_{cr}$ before specifying insulation thickness on small wires and tubes.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Formula

| Situation | First Step | Key Formula | Common Mistake |
|---|---|---|---|
| Furnace wall, multi-layer | List $L_i$ and $k_i$ | $q = \dfrac{\Delta T}{\sum L_i/(k_i A)}$ | Forgetting to multiply $R$ by $A$ consistently |
| Lagged pipe | Identify radii | $R = \dfrac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{2\pi k L}$ | Using $r_o - r_i$ instead of $\ln(r_o/r_i)$ |
| Insulated small wire | Compute $r_{cr}$ | $r_{cr} = k_{ins}/h_o$ | Assuming more insulation always reduces $q$ |
| Fin efficiency | Compute $mL$ | $q = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b \tanh(mL)$ | Using $\tanh(mL)$ when $mL$ is large (use $\approx 1$) |
| Heat exchanger rating | Compute $\Delta T_{LM}$ | $q = UA\,\Delta T_{LM}$ | Taking arithmetic mean instead of log mean |
| Combined convection + radiation | Compute $h_r$ | $h_{total} = h_c + h_r$ | Forgetting to use absolute $T$ in $h_r$ formula |

---

## 4.1 Thermal Resistance Network — Composite Cylindrical Pipe

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["T_steam (Hot Fluid)"] -->|"Ri = 1/(hi·2π·r1·L)"| B["Inner Pipe Wall Surface"]
    B -->|"R_steel = ln(r2/r1)/(2π·k_steel·L)"| C["Outer Pipe Wall Surface"]
    C -->|"R1 = ln(r3/r2)/(2π·k1·L)"| D["Inner Insulation Outer Surface"]
    D -->|"R2 = ln(r4/r3)/(2π·k2·L)"| E["Outer Insulation Outer Surface"]
    E -->|"Ro = 1/(ho·2π·r4·L)"| F["T_ambient (Cold Fluid)"]

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each arrow represents one thermal resistance. The total driving force $T_{steam} - T_{ambient}$ is "dropped" across these five resistors in series, producing a heat flow rate $q$ that is the *same* through every element. The "size" of each resistance dictates the *temperature drop* across that element.

    B -->|"r2 is greater than r_cr"| E["Heat loss DECREASES with more insulation"]

flowchart TD

    S3 --> S4["Step 4: Write resistance expressions for each layer"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5: Sum series resistances or apply parallel rule"]

## 4.4 Schematic of a Double-Pipe Heat Exchanger (For LMTD Problems)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1["Hot Fluid Inlet T_hi"] --> H2["Hot Fluid Flows in Inner Pipe"]
    H2 --> H3["Hot Fluid Outlet T_ho"]
    C1["Cold Fluid Inlet T_ci"] --> C2["Cold Fluid Flows in Annulus"]
    C2 --> C3["Cold Fluid Outlet T_co"]
    H2 -. "Heat Transfer q" .-> C2
    style H1 fill:#ff6347
    style H3 fill:#ff6347
    style C1 fill:#b4d8ff
    style C3 fill:#b4d8ff
    style H2 fill:#ffe5b4
    style C2 fill:#d5e8d4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Two flow arrangements exist:**
> - **Counter-flow (most efficient):** hot fluid enters one end, cold enters the *opposite* end.
> - **Parallel-flow (co-current):** both fluids enter the *same* end and travel in the same direction.
> The temperature differences $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ used in the LMTD formula depend on which arrangement is used.
