# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A questions are direct, definition-based, or one-step formula applications. No internal choice. Each carries 3 marks. Write the formula, substitute values, and state the answer with units.

---

### Question 1: Define Critical Radius of Insulation

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Model Answer:**

The *critical radius of insulation* $r_{cr}$ is the outer radius of insulation on a cylindrical (or spherical) surface at which the heat loss rate is **maximum**. It occurs when the increase in external surface area exactly compensates for the increase in conductive resistance. The mathematical expression is:

$$r_{cr} = \frac{k_{ins}}{h_o}$$

where $k_{ins}$ is the thermal conductivity of the insulation and $h_o$ is the external convective heat transfer coefficient. For $r < r_{cr}$, adding insulation **increases** heat loss; for $r > r_{cr}$, adding insulation decreases heat loss as expected. This is industrially significant for small wires, heating elements, and thin tubes. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2: Write the LMTD Formula for a Heat Exchanger

`[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary Exam Pattern]`

**Model Answer:**

The Log Mean Temperature Difference is defined as:

$$\Delta T_{LM} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}$$

where $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ are the temperature differences between the hot and cold streams at the **two ends** of the exchanger. It is used in the rating equation:

$$q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LM}$$

The LMTD is preferred over the arithmetic mean because the true driving force for heat transfer decreases exponentially along the exchanger, not linearly. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks and offer an **internal choice** (you must attempt one out of two). Each Part B question has two sub-parts, typically (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. The sub-parts usually combine a derivation/formula question with a numerical problem.

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Module 2 Type]`

**(a)** A steam pipe of outer diameter 8 cm is covered with a 4 cm thick layer of insulation ($k = 0.08$ W/m·K), followed by a 2 cm thick second layer of insulation ($k = 0.05$ W/m·K). The inside steam-side convective coefficient is $h_i = 6000$ W/m²·K and the outside air-side convective coefficient is $h_o = 12$ W/m²·K. The pipe wall and steam-side temperature drop are negligible. Steam temperature is 470 K, ambient is 300 K. Calculate:
   1. The heat loss per metre of pipe. **(4 marks)**
   2. The outer surface temperature of the insulation. **(3 marks)**

**(b)** Derive an expression for the critical radius of insulation on a cylindrical pipe and explain its industrial significance. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Given (all per unit length, $L = 1$ m):**
- Pipe outer radius: $r_1 = 0.04$ m
- Layer 1 outer radius: $r_2 = 0.04 + 0.04 = 0.08$ m
- Layer 2 outer radius: $r_3 = 0.08 + 0.02 = 0.10$ m
- $k_1 = 0.08$ W/m·K, $k_2 = 0.05$ W/m·K
- $h_i = 6000$ W/m²·K, $h_o = 12$ W/m²·K
- $T_{steam} = 470$ K, $T_\infty = 300$ K

**Step 1 — Compute the three resistances (per metre):**

$$R_{cond,1} = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k_1 (1)} = \frac{\ln(0.08/0.04)}{2\pi \times 0.08} = \frac{\ln 2}{0.5027} = \frac{0.6931}{0.5027} = 1.3787\ \text{K·m/W}$$

$$R_{cond,2} = \frac{\ln(r_3/r_2)}{2\pi k_2 (1)} = \frac{\ln(0.10/0.08)}{2\pi \times 0.05} = \frac{\ln(1.25)}{0.3142} = \frac{0.2231}{0.3142} = 0.7102\ \text{K·m/W}$$

$$R_{conv,o} = \frac{1}{h_o (2\pi r_3)} = \frac{1}{12 \times 2\pi \times 0.10} = \frac{1}{7.5398} = 0.1326\ \text{K·m/W}$$

Neglecting pipe wall and $h_i$ as stated:

$$R_{total} = 1.3787 + 0.7102 + 0.1326 = 2.2215\ \text{K·m/W}$$

**Step 2 — Heat loss per metre:**

$$q = \frac{\Delta T}{R_{total}} = \frac{470 - 300}{2.2215} = \frac{170}{2.2215} = 76.52\ \text{W/m}$$

**Step 3 — Outer surface temperature:**

The temperature drop across the outside film:

$$\Delta T_{film} = q \times R_{conv,o} = 76.52 \times 0.1326 = 10.15\ \text{K}$$

$$T_{surface} = T_\infty + \Delta T_{film} = 300 + 10.15 = 310.15\ \text{K}$$

**Final Answers:**
1. Heat loss $q \approx 76.5$ W/m
2. Outer surface temperature $T_s \approx 310.2$ K

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Derivation of Critical Radius of Insulation:**

Consider a long cylindrical pipe of outer radius $r_1$ covered with insulation of outer radius $r_2$ and thermal conductivity $k_{ins}$. The pipe surface is held at temperature $T_1$ and the surrounding fluid at $T_\infty$, with convective coefficient $h_o$.

The total resistance per unit length is:

$$R_{total} = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k_{ins}} + \frac{1}{2\pi h_o r_2}$$

The heat loss per unit length is:

$$q = \frac{2\pi (T_1 - T_\infty)}{\dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k_{ins}} + \dfrac{1}{h_o r_2}}$$

For a thin wire/pipe where $r_1$ is fixed, the maximum $q$ corresponds to minimum $R_{total}$. Differentiating $R_{total}$ with respect to $r_2$:

$$\frac{dR_{total}}{dr_2} = \frac{1}{2\pi k_{ins} r_2} - \frac{1}{2\pi h_o r_2^2} = 0$$

Solving:

$$r_{2,cr} = \frac{k_{ins}}{h_o}$$

**Industrial Significance:**
- For small electrical heating elements, cartridge heaters, and instrument wires, $r_1 < r_{cr}$ initially. Adding insulation up to $r_{cr}$ actually *increases* heat dissipation, which is desirable for these heating applications.
- For large steam pipes and reactor vessels, $r_1 > r_{cr}$ always, so adding insulation monotonically reduces heat loss.
- The design rule: **never insulate a small wire up to a radius less than $r_{cr}$ if the goal is to reduce heat loss.**
- Examples: bare Nichrome heating coils intentionally avoid insulation; high-voltage transmission lines use specific minimum conductor sizes to avoid $r_{cr}$ issues.

**(7 marks — full marks require diagram, derivation, and at least two real-world examples.)**

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Module 2 Type]`

**(a)** A composite furnace wall consists of three layers:
   - 10 cm of firebrick ($k = 1.4$ W/m·K)
   - 15 cm of insulating brick ($k = 0.25$ W/m·K)
   - 5 cm of red brick ($k = 0.7$ W/m·K)

The inside (furnace-side) temperature is 1500 K and the outside (ambient-side) convective coefficient is $h_o = 20$ W/m²·K with ambient at 300 K. Calculate the heat loss per square metre of wall and the temperature at the interface between the insulating brick and the red brick. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A counter-flow double-pipe heat exchanger is used to cool oil (cp = 2.1 kJ/kg·K) from 420 K to 360 K using water (cp = 4.18 kJ/kg·K) entering at 300 K. The overall heat transfer coefficient is $U = 800$ W/m²·K, and the heat transfer area is $A = 1.2$ m². Calculate the LMTD, the heat duty, and the water flow rate required. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Given:**
- Layer 1 (firebrick): $L_1 = 0.10$ m, $k_1 = 1.4$ W/m·K
- Layer 2 (insulating brick): $L_2 = 0.15$ m, $k_2 = 0.25$ W/m·K
- Layer 3 (red brick): $L_3 = 0.05$ m, $k_3 = 0.7$ W/m·K
- $T_i = 1500$ K, $T_\infty = 300$ K, $h_o = 20$ W/m²·K
- Area reference: $A = 1$ m² (per m² of wall)

**Step 1 — Resistances:**

$$R_1 = \frac{L_1}{k_1 A} = \frac{0.10}{1.4 \times 1} = 0.07143\ \text{K/W}$$

$$R_2 = \frac{L_2}{k_2 A} = \frac{0.15}{0.25 \times 1} = 0.6000\ \text{K/W}$$

$$R_3 = \frac{L_3}{k_3 A} = \frac{0.05}{0.7 \times 1} = 0.07143\ \text{K/W}$$

$$R_o = \frac{1}{h_o A} = \frac{1}{20 \times 1} = 0.0500\ \text{K/W}$$

**Step 2 — Total resistance:**

$$R_{total} = 0.07143 + 0.6000 + 0.07143 + 0.0500 = 0.79286\ \text{K/W}$$

**Step 3 — Heat loss per m²:**

$$q = \frac{T_i - T_\infty}{R_{total}} = \frac{1500 - 300}{0.79286} = \frac{1200}{0.79286} = 1513.5\ \text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Interface temperature between insulating brick and red brick:**

The temperature drop across layers 3 and outside (from outside inward):

$$\Delta T_3 = q \times R_3 = 1513.5 \times 0.07143 = 108.1\ \text{K}$$

$$\Delta T_o = q \times R_o = 1513.5 \times 0.0500 = 75.7\ \text{K}$$

$$T_{interface} = T_\infty + \Delta T_o + \Delta T_3 = 300 + 75.7 + 108.1 = 483.8\ \text{K}$$

**Final Answers:**
- Heat loss $\approx 1514$ W/m²
- Interface temperature $\approx 484$ K (≈ 211 °C)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** The insulating brick ($R_2 = 0.60$) accounts for 76% of the total resistance. The firebrick is hot but conducts well; the thin red brick is at moderate temperature. This is a textbook example of why *insulating* layers go on the *cold* side of a furnace — it minimizes the temperature of the structural (load-bearing) red brick.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Oil: $T_{h,in} = 420$ K, $T_{h,out} = 360$ K, $c_{p,h} = 2.1$ kJ/kg·K
- Water: $T_{c,in} = 300$ K, $c_{p,c} = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K
- $U = 800$ W/m²·K, $A = 1.2$ m²
- Counter-flow arrangement

**Step 1 — Estimate cold-fluid outlet temperature to compute LMTD:**

Energy balance gives:

$$q = \dot{m}_h c_{p,h} (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c} (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$$

We need $q$ first from the rating equation, then back-calculate.

**Step 2 — Counter-flow end-temperature differences:**

For counter-flow:
- $\Delta T_1$ (at end where oil enters): $T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$
- $\Delta T_2$ (at end where oil exits): $T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$

We don't yet know $T_{c,out}$. Use iterative method or solve for $T_{c,out}$ using:

$$q = U A \Delta T_{LM} = \dot{m}_h c_{p,h} \Delta T_h$$

For counter-flow, a standard relation is:

$$\Delta T_{LM} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$$

With $\Delta T_1 = (420 - T_{c,out})$ and $\Delta T_2 = (360 - 300) = 60$ K.

**Step 3 — Iterative solution:**

Assume $T_{c,out} = 335$ K (estimate).

- $\Delta T_1 = 420 - 335 = 85$ K
- $\Delta T_2 = 360 - 300 = 60$ K
- $\Delta T_{LM} = \dfrac{85 - 60}{\ln(85/60)} = \dfrac{25}{0.347} = 72.0$ K
- $q = U A \Delta T_{LM} = 800 \times 1.2 \times 72.0 = 69\,120$ W
- $\dot{m}_h c_{p,h} = q / \Delta T_h = 69\,120 / 60 = 1152$ W/K → $\dot{m}_h = 1152 / 2100 = 0.549$ kg/s
- Energy balance: $T_{c,out} = T_{c,in} + q / (\dot{m}_c c_{p,c})$

For a first pass, choose $\dot{m}_c$ so that $T_{c,out} = 335$ K:

$$\dot{m}_c = \frac{q}{c_{p,c} (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})} = \frac{69\,120}{4180 \times 35} = \frac{69\,120}{146\,300} = 0.4724\ \text{kg/s}$$

The estimate is self-consistent. 

**Final Answers:**
- LMTD $\approx 72$ K
- Heat duty $q \approx 69.1$ kW
- Water flow rate $\dot{m}_c \approx 0.472$ kg/s

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** Check that $\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2 < 2$ for LMTD validity. Here $85/60 = 1.42$, which is acceptable. For ratios greater than 2, the arithmetic mean gives an error $<4\%$, but LMTD should still be used for KTU problems.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Loss-of-Marks Pitfalls (from KTU board examiner experience):**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing $r$ in cm and $k$ expecting m. Always convert all dimensions to **metres** before substitution.
> 2. **Area reference confusion:** For cylindrical problems, the thermal resistance at the inside film and outside film are referenced to *different* areas. Either use a single reference area and convert all terms, or work in resistances per unit length.
> 3. **Using $r_2 - r_1$ instead of $\ln(r_2/r_1)$:** This is the most common error. The conduction resistance in a cylinder is logarithmic, *not* linear.
> 4. **Forgetting $\Delta T_{LM}$ requires $\Delta T_1 \neq \Delta T_2$:** If they are equal, LMTD reduces to that value (no logarithm needed), but the problem is degenerate.
> 5. **Steady-state assumption not stated:** Always write "Under steady-state conditions, $q$ is constant through all resistances" at the start.
> 6. **No units in final answer:** KTU strictly penalizes answers without units. Always write "76.5 W/m" or "1.51 kW/m²".

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Every KTU Exam:**

- ✅ **The 3 modes of heat transfer:** conduction ($k$), convection ($h$), radiation ($\varepsilon, \sigma$)
- ✅ **The 5 standard resistance formulas:** plane wall, cylinder, sphere, convection, radiation
- ✅ **The critical radius formula:** $r_{cr} = k_{ins} / h_o$ — and its counter-intuitive behavior for small wires
- ✅ **LMTD formula:** $\Delta T_{LM} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2) / \ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)$
- ✅ **Stefan–Boltzmann constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴ — must use **Kelvin**
- ✅ **Units discipline:** lengths in **metres**, $k$ in **W/m·K**, $h$ in **W/m²·K**, $U$ in **W/m²·K**, $q$ in **Watts**
- ✅ **For cylindrical problems:** ALWAYS use $r$ (not $D$); ALWAYS use $\ln(r_o/r_i)$; ALWAYS be consistent with the area reference
- ✅ **Steady-state assumption:** implied in all Module 2 problems — heat flow is constant through all series resistances
- ✅ **Energy balance in heat exchangers:** $q = \dot{m}_h c_{p,h} \Delta T_h = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c} \Delta T_c = U A \Delta T_{LM}$
- ✅ **Counter-flow is more efficient** than parallel-flow because it maintains a higher $\Delta T_{LM}$ for the same inlet/outlet conditions
- ✅ **Practice the three-step method:** draw resistance network → sum resistances → apply $q = \Delta T / R$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always sketch the resistance network (even a rough one on rough paper) before substituting numbers. The visual check catches 80% of common errors. Examiner preference: showing the network diagram + clear formula + unit check typically earns full marks even if a small arithmetic slip is made later.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Natural Convection: Dimensional Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Natural Convection: Dimensional Analysis ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition **Natural (or Free) Convection** is the mode of heat transfer in a fluid where fluid motion is induced **solely by density differences** arising from temperature gradients within the fluid — no external pump, fan, or blower is used. The fluid in contact with a hot surface becomes less dense and rises due to **buoyancy**, while cooler, denser fluid descends to replace it, forming a self-sustaining circulation loop. **Dimensional Analysis** is a mathematical technique used to reduce a complex physical problem with many variables into a smaller set of **dimensionless groups** (π terms) by analyzing the fundamental dimensions (M, L, T, θ) of each governing variable. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Buckingham π-Theorem is the primary analytical engine used to derive the functional relationship for the natural convection heat-transfer coefficient: > $$h = \phi(L, \Delta T, \rho, \mu, k, C_p, \beta, g)$$ > yielding the canonical form: $Nu = f(Gr, Pr)$ ### 1.2 Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy — "The Hot Air Balloon Effect"** > Imagine a room with a **radiator** on one wall. The air touching the radiator warms up, becomes lighter (less dense), and rises to the ceiling. The cool air near the floor slides in to take its place. This self-generated circulation is **natural convection**. Now, if you wanted to mathematically *predict* how fast the room warms up, you would not write an equation with all 9 individual variables — instead, you would group them into **dimensionless clusters** (like Nusselt, Grashof, Prandtl) that universally describe the physics. This is exactly what **dimensional analysis** does. ### 1.3 Governing Variables & Their Physical Constants | Symbol | Variable | SI Units | Role | |:------:|:--------:|:--------:|:----:| | **h** | Heat transfer coefficient | **W/m²·K** | Unknown (to be correlated) | | **L** | Characteristic length | **m** | Geometry | | **ΔT** | Temperature driving force | **K** | Buoyancy cause | | **ρ** | Fluid density | **kg/m³** | Inertia | | **μ** | Dynamic viscosity | **kg/m·s** | Viscous resistance | | **k** | Thermal conductivity | **W/m·K** | Conduction | | **C_p** | Specific heat capacity | **J/kg·K** | Thermal storage | | **β** | Thermal expansion coeff. | **1/K** | Buoyancy strength | | **g** | Gravitational acceleration | **m/s²** = 9.81 | Buoyancy driver | **Total Variables: 9 | Fundamental Dimensions: 4 (M, L, T, θ) | π-terms: 9 − 4 = 5** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Buckingham π-Theorem — Operational Logic The Buckingham π-Theorem states that any physically meaningful equation involving $n$ variables expressed in terms of $k$ independent fundamental dimensions can be reduced to an equivalent relationship among $(n - k)$ independent dimensionless groups called $\pi$-terms. **Step-by-Step Logic for Natural Convection:** 1. **Identify variables:** $h, L, \Delta T, \rho, \mu, k, C_p, \beta, g$ → 9 variables. 2. **Count fundamental dimensions:** $[M, L, T, \theta]$ → 4 dimensions. 3. **Number of π-terms:** $9 - 4 = 5$ dimensionless groups expected. 4. **Select repeating variables:** $L, \rho, k, \mu$ - Must collectively contain all 4 dimensions - Must NOT include the dependent variable ($h$) or the response variable ($\Delta T$) - Must NOT be dimensionless 5. **Construct each π-term** by combining one non-repeating variable with the four repeating variables. 6. **Solve the linear system** of dimension equations for the exponents. 7. **Combine intermediate π-terms** if necessary to obtain physically meaningful groups. ### 2.2 Physical Significance of Each π-Group - **Nusselt Number (Nu):** Ratio of convective to conductive heat transfer across the boundary layer. Higher Nu ⇒ stronger convection. - **Grashof Number (Gr):** Ratio of buoyancy (lift) forces to viscous forces inside the fluid. Higher Gr ⇒ dominant natural-convection currents. - **Prandtl Number (Pr):** Ratio of momentum diffusivity ($\nu$) to thermal diffusivity ($\alpha$). It is a **fluid property only**. - **Rayleigh Number (Ra):** Product $Gr \cdot Pr$ — the combined criterion governing the onset of turbulence in natural convection. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these formulas — they appear in every KTU numerical on natural convection.** | # | Formula | Name | Typical Range / Constant | |:-:|:-------:|:----:|:------------------------:| | 1 | $Nu = \dfrac{h L}{k}$ | Nusselt Number | Dimensionless | | 2 | $Gr = \dfrac{g \, \beta \, \Delta T \, L^{3}}{\nu^{2}} = \dfrac{g \, \beta \, \Delta T \, L^{3} \, \rho^{2}}{\mu^{2}}$ | Grashof Number | Dimensionless | | 3 | $Pr = \dfrac{C_p \, \mu}{k} = \dfrac{\nu}{\alpha}$ | Prandtl Number | Fluid property | | 4 | $Ra = Gr \cdot Pr$ | Rayleigh Number | $Ra < 10^{9}$ ⇒ laminar | | 5 | $Nu = C \cdot (Gr \cdot Pr)^{n} = C \cdot Ra^{n}$ | Generic Correlation | Empirical | | 6 | $\beta = \dfrac{1}{T_{film}}$ | Vol. expansion (ideal gas) | $T_{film}$ in **K** | | 7 | $T_{film} = \dfrac{T_s + T_\infty}{2}$ | Film Temperature | Used for properties | ### 2.4 Standard KTU Correlations (Vertical Plates) | Regime | Ra Range | Correlation | |:------:|:--------:|:-----------:| | Laminar | $10^{4} < Ra < 10^{9}$ | $Nu = 0.59 \, Ra^{1/4}$ | | Turbulent | $10^{9} < Ra < 10^{13}$ | $Nu = 0.10 \, Ra^{1/3}$ | | Combined | $10^{4} < Ra < 10^{13}$ | $Nu = \left\{ 0.825 + \dfrac{0.387 \, Ra^{1/6}}{\left[1 + (0.492/Pr)^{9/16}\right]^{8/27}} \right\}^{2}$ | ### 2.5 Engineering Utility These correlations are used in the design of: - Heat-exchanger shells (no fan-assisted flow) - Building ventilation & HVAC ducts - Cooling of nuclear reactor cores (passive safety) - Solar thermal collectors - Electronic equipment cooling fins in still air - Refrigerator condenser coils ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Buckingham π-Theorem for Natural Convection **Step 1 — Statement of the functional dependence:** $$h = \phi(L,\, \Delta T,\, \rho,\, \mu,\, k,\, C_p,\, \beta,\, g)$$ **Step 2 — Tabulate dimensions of every variable in the M-L-T-θ system:** | Variable | M | L | T | θ | |:--------:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:--:| | h | 1 | 0 | −3 | −1 | | L | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | | ΔT | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | | ρ | 1 | −3 | 0 | 0 | | μ | 1 | −1 | −1 | 0 | | k | 1 | 1 | −3 | −1 | | C_p | 0 | 2 | −2 | −1 | | β | 0 | 0 | 0 | −1 | | g | 0 | 1 | −2 | 0 | **Step 3 — Number of π-terms:** $n = 9$ variables, $k = 4$ dimensions $$\text{Number of } \pi\text{-terms} = n - k = 9 - 4 = 5$$ **Step 4 — Choose repeating variables:** $L,\ \rho,\ k,\ \mu$ (they collectively cover M, L, T, θ). --- #### π₁-Term (with h — heat transfer coefficient) $$\pi_1 = h \cdot L^{a} \cdot \rho^{b} \cdot k^{c} \cdot \mu^{d}$$ For $\pi_1$ to be dimensionless, the sum of exponents of each fundamental dimension must vanish: $$\begin{aligned} \text{M:} \quad & 1 + b + c + d = 0 \\ \text{L:} \quad & 0 + a - 3b + c - d = 0 \\ \text{T:} \quad & -3 - 3c - d = 0 \\ \theta\text{:} \quad & -1 - c = 0 \end{aligned}$$ Solving the linear system: - From θ: $c = -1$ - From T: $-3 - 3(-1) - d = 0 \Rightarrow d = 0$ - From M: $1 + b - 1 + 0 = 0 \Rightarrow b = 0$ - From L: $a - 0 + (-1) - 0 = 0 \Rightarrow a = 1$ $$\boxed{\pi_1 = \frac{h L}{k} = Nu \quad \text{(Nusselt Number)}}$$ --- #### π₂-Term (with ΔT) $$\pi_2 = \Delta T \cdot L^{a} \cdot \rho^{b} \cdot k^{c} \cdot \mu^{d}$$ $$\begin{aligned} \text{M:} \quad & b + c + d = 0 \\ \text{L:} \quad & a - 3b + c - d = 0 \\ \text{T:} \quad & -3c - d = 0 \\ \theta\text{:} \quad & 1 - c = 0 \end{aligned}$$ Solving: - From θ: $c = 1$ - From T: $-3(1) - d = 0 \Rightarrow d = -3$ - From M: $b + 1 - 3 = 0 \Rightarrow b = 2$ - From L: $a - 6 + 1 + 3 = 0 \Rightarrow a = 2$ $$\pi_2 = \frac{\Delta T \cdot L^{2} \cdot \rho^{2} \cdot k}{\mu^{3}}$$ --- #### π₃-Term (with C_p) $$\pi_3 = C_p \cdot L^{a} \cdot \rho^{b} \cdot k^{c} \cdot \mu^{d}$$ $$\begin{aligned} \text{M:} \quad & b + c + d = 0 \\ \text{L:} \quad & a - 3b + c - d = 0 \\ \text{T:} \quad & -2 - 3c - d = 0 \\ \theta\text{:} \quad & -1 - c = 0 \end{aligned}$$ Solving: - From θ: $c = -1$ - From T: $-2 + 3 - d = 0 \Rightarrow d = 1$ - From M: $b - 1 + 1 = 0 \Rightarrow b = 0$ - From L: $a - 0 - 1 - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow a = 0$ $$\boxed{\pi_3 = \frac{C_p \, \mu}{k} = Pr \quad \text{(Prandtl Number)}}$$ --- #### π₄-Term (with β) $$\pi_4 = \beta \cdot L^{a} \cdot \rho^{b} \cdot k^{c} \cdot \mu^{d}$$ $$\begin{aligned} \text{M:} \quad & b + c + d = 0 \\ \text{L:} \quad & a - 3b + c - d = 0 \\ \text{T:} \quad & -3c - d = 0 \\ \theta\text{:} \quad & -1 - c = 0 \end{aligned}$$ Solving: - From θ: $c = -1$ - From T: $3 - d = 0 \Rightarrow d = 3$ - From M: $b - 1 + 3 = 0 \Rightarrow b = -2$ - From L: $a + 6 - 1 - 3 = 0 \Rightarrow a = -2$ $$\pi_4 = \frac{\beta \, \mu^{3}}{L^{2} \, \rho^{2} \, k}$$ --- #### π₅-Term (with g) $$\pi_5 = g \cdot L^{a} \cdot \rho^{b} \cdot k^{c} \cdot \mu^{d}$$ $$\begin{aligned} \text{M:} \quad & b + c + d = 0 \\ \text{L:} \quad & 1 + a - 3b + c - d = 0 \\ \text{T:} \quad & -2 - 3c - d = 0 \\ \theta\text{:} \quad & -c = 0 \end{aligned}$$ Solving: - From θ: $c = 0$ - From T: $-2 - d = 0 \Rightarrow d = -2$ - From M: $b - 2 = 0 \Rightarrow b = 2$ - From L: $1 + a - 6 + 0 + 2 = 0 \Rightarrow a = 3$ $$\pi_5 = \frac{g \, L^{3} \, \rho^{2}}{\mu^{2}}$$ --- #### Final Combination: Obtaining Grashof Number The product $\pi_2 \cdot \pi_4 \cdot \pi_5$ simplifies elegantly: $$\begin{aligned} \pi_2 \cdot \pi_4 \cdot \pi_5 &= \left( \frac{\Delta T \, L^{2} \, \rho^{2} \, k}{\mu^{3}} \right) \cdot \left( \frac{\beta \, \mu^{3}}{L^{2} \, \rho^{2} \, k} \right) \cdot \left( \frac{g \, L^{3} \, \rho^{2}}{\mu^{2}} \right) \\ &= \frac{\Delta T \cdot \beta \cdot g \cdot L^{3} \cdot \rho^{2}}{\mu^{2}} \end{aligned}$$ $$\boxed{\pi_2 \cdot \pi_4 \cdot \pi_5 = \frac{g \, \beta \, \Delta T \, L^{3} \, \rho^{2}}{\mu^{2}} = Gr \quad \text{(Grashof Number)}}$$ --- ### 3.2 The Master Functional Equation The Buckingham π-Theorem now reduces the original 9-variable problem to: $$\boxed{Nu = f(Gr,\ Pr) \quad \Rightarrow \quad Nu = C \, Gr^{a} \, Pr^{b}}$$ For **laminar** natural convection on a vertical plate, experiments confirm $a \approx b$, giving the elegant form: $$Nu = C \left( Gr \cdot Pr \right)^{n} = C \cdot Ra^{n}, \quad n \approx \tfrac{1}{4}$$ ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Variables vs. Dimensions (Summary View) | Group | Members | Final Dimensionless Name | |:-----:|:-------:|:------------------------:| | π₁ | h, L, k | Nusselt (Nu) | | π₂ | ΔT, L, ρ, k, μ | Intermediate | | π₃ | C_p, μ, k | Prandtl (Pr) | | π₄ | β, L, ρ, k, μ | Intermediate | | π₅ | g, L, ρ, μ | Intermediate | | π₂·π₄·π₅ | ΔT, β, g, L, ρ, μ | **Grashof (Gr)** | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Buckingham π-Theorem Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Step 1: Identify all 9 variables
h, L, DeltaT, rho, mu, k, Cp, beta, g] --> B[Step 2: Write dimensions in M-L-T-theta] B --> C[Step 3: Count pi-terms
9 minus 4 = 5 groups] C --> D[Step 4: Select repeating variables
L, rho, k, mu] D --> E[Step 5: Form 5 pi-terms
one per non-repeating variable] E --> F[Step 6: Solve linear dimension equations
for each exponent] F --> G[Step 7: Combine intermediates
pi2 times pi4 times pi5 = Gr] G --> H[Final Master Equation
Nu = f of Gr and Pr] H --> I[Laminar Regime
Nu = 0.59 Ra to power 1/4] H --> J[Turbulent Regime
Nu = 0.10 Ra to power 1/3] I --> K[Compute h
h = Nu times k divided by L] J --> K K --> L[Heat Flux
q = h times A times DeltaT] ``` ### 4.2 Functional Architecture: Heat-Transfer Process Map ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Hot Vertical Plate
Temperature Ts] --> B[Boundary Layer Forms
velocity and thermal] B --> C{Regime Check
Ra number} C -->|Ra less than 10 to power 9| D[Laminar Flow
Nu equals 0.59 Ra to power 0.25] C -->|Ra greater than 10 to power 9| E[Turbulent Flow
Nu equals 0.10 Ra to power 0.33] D --> F[Convective Coefficient h
h equals Nu times k divided by L] E --> F F --> G[Heat Loss Rate
q equals h times A times DeltaT] ``` ### 4.3 Conceptual Schematic: Layered π-Group Construction ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph INPUT[Input Variables] V1[h] V2[L] V3[DeltaT] V4[rho] V5[mu] V6[k] V7[Cp] V8[beta] V9[g] end subgraph REP[Repeating Variables] R1[L] R2[rho] R3[k] R4[mu] end INPUT --> PRO[Pi Theorem Engine] REP --> PRO PRO --> P1[pi1 equals Nu] PRO --> P2[pi2] PRO --> P3[pi3 equals Pr] PRO --> P4[pi4] PRO --> P5[pi5] P2 --> COMB[Combined pi2 times pi4 times pi5] P4 --> COMB P5 --> COMB COMB --> P6[Grashof Number Gr] P1 --> OUT[Final Equation
Nu equals f of Gr and Pr] P3 --> OUT P6 --> OUT ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define the Grashof number and Rayleigh number. What is the physical significance of each in natural convection? **Model Answer:** The **Grashof number** is defined as the ratio of buoyancy (lift) forces to viscous forces acting on a fluid element: $$Gr = \frac{g \, \beta \, \Delta T \, L^{3}}{\nu^{2}}$$ Physically, $Gr$ represents the relative importance of the buoyancy driving force (caused by density differences due to heating) against the viscous resistance that opposes fluid motion. A high $Gr$ indicates that buoyancy dominates and natural convection currents are strong. The **Rayleigh number** is the product of the Grashof number and the Prandtl number: $$Ra = Gr \cdot Pr = \frac{g \, \beta \, \Delta T \, L^{3}}{\nu \, \alpha}$$ $Ra$ is the governing dimensionless group for natural convection — it determines whether the flow regime is **laminar** ($Ra < 10^{9}$) or **turbulent** ($Ra > 10^{9}$). It encapsulates both the buoyancy driving force and the relative rates of momentum and thermal diffusion in the fluid. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** State the Buckingham π-Theorem. How many dimensionless groups are obtained when applied to derive the natural convection correlation for $h$? **Model Answer:** The **Buckingham π-Theorem** states that if a physical phenomenon depends on $n$ variables expressed in $k$ independent fundamental dimensions, the relationship can be expressed in terms of $(n - k)$ independent dimensionless groups called $\pi$-terms. For natural convection on a vertical plate, the variables are: $h,\, L,\, \Delta T,\, \rho,\, \mu,\, k,\, C_p,\, \beta,\, g$ (n = 9) Fundamental dimensions: M, L, T, θ (k = 4) $$\text{Number of } \pi\text{-terms} = n - k = 9 - 4 = 5$$ These reduce physically to: $Nu,\ Gr,\ Pr$ (and two intermediate groups that combine to form $Gr$). The final functional relationship is: $$Nu = f(Gr, Pr)$$ --- ### Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]* **(a)** Apply the Buckingham π-Theorem to derive the dimensionless groups governing natural convection heat transfer on a vertical plate. Identify the Nusselt, Grashof, and Prandtl numbers explicitly. (7 Marks) **(b)** Air at $20\,^\circ$C and $1\,atm$ flows past a vertical plate of height $0.4\,m$ maintained at $80\,^\circ$C. Calculate the convective heat transfer coefficient and the rate of heat transfer per unit width of the plate. (7 Marks) **Given Properties of air at film temperature (50 °C ≈ 323 K):** - $k = 0.02735 \text{ W/m·K}$ - $\nu = 1.798 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ - $Pr = 0.7228$ ##### Model Solution: **(a)** See the exhaustive derivation in **Section 3.1** above. The five π-terms are: $\pi_1 = \dfrac{hL}{k} = Nu$ ; $\pi_3 = \dfrac{C_p \mu}{k} = Pr$ ; $\pi_2 \cdot \pi_4 \cdot \pi_5 = \dfrac{g \beta \Delta T L^{3} \rho^{2}}{\mu^{2}} = Gr$ $$\boxed{Nu = f(Gr, Pr) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; Nu = C \cdot Ra^{n}}$$ **(b) Step 1 — Compute the film temperature and β:** $$T_{film} = \frac{T_s + T_\infty}{2} = \frac{80 + 20}{2} = 50\,^\circ C = 323 \text{ K}$$ $$\beta = \frac{1}{T_{film}} = \frac{1}{323} = 3.096 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$ **Step 2 — Compute ΔT and Grashof number:** $$\Delta T = T_s - T_\infty = 80 - 20 = 60 \text{ K}$$ $$Gr = \frac{g \, \beta \, \Delta T \, L^{3}}{\nu^{2}} = \frac{(9.81)(3.096 \times 10^{-3})(60)(0.4)^{3}}{(1.798 \times 10^{-5})^{2}}$$ $$Gr = \frac{(9.81)(3.096 \times 10^{-3})(60)(0.064)}{3.233 \times 10^{-10}} = \frac{0.1166}{3.233 \times 10^{-10}}$$ $$\boxed{Gr = 3.61 \times 10^{8}}$$ **Step 3 — Compute Rayleigh number and check regime:** $$Ra = Gr \cdot Pr = (3.61 \times 10^{8})(0.7228) = 2.61 \times 10^{8}$$ Since $10^{4} < Ra = 2.61 \times 10^{8} < 10^{9}$, the flow is **laminar**. **Step 4 — Apply the laminar correlation:** $$Nu = 0.59 \, Ra^{1/4} = 0.59 \times (2.61 \times 10^{8})^{0.25}$$ $$\begin{aligned} (2.61 \times 10^{8})^{0.25} &= (2.61)^{0.25} \times (10^{8})^{0.25} \\ &= 1.272 \times 10^{2} = 127.2 \end{aligned}$$ $$Nu = 0.59 \times 127.2 = 75.05$$ **Step 5 — Compute h:** $$h = \frac{Nu \cdot k}{L} = \frac{(75.05)(0.02735)}{0.4}$$ $$\boxed{h = 5.13 \text{ W/m}^{2}\text{K}}$$ **Step 6 — Heat transfer per unit width:** $$q' = h \cdot L \cdot \Delta T = (5.13)(0.4)(60) = 123.1 \text{ W/m}$$ $$\boxed{q' \approx 123 \text{ W per metre width}}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]* **(a)** Using dimensional analysis, show that the natural-convection heat transfer coefficient depends on Nusselt, Grashof, and Prandtl numbers. Clearly list the repeating and non-repeating variables used. (7 Marks) **(b)** A horizontal hot plate at $90\,^\circ$C is exposed to ambient air at $30\,^\circ$C. The plate dimensions are $0.6\,m \times 0.6\,m$. Using the appropriate correlation, calculate the total heat lost from the plate to the surroundings. (7 Marks) **Given Properties of air at film temperature (60 °C ≈ 333 K):** - $k = 0.02808 \text{ W/m·K}$ - $\nu = 1.892 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ - $Pr = 0.7202$ ##### Model Solution: **(a)** Refer to **Section 3.1** for the exhaustive derivation. **Summary of variable classification:** | Type | Variables | Count | |:----:|:---------:|:-----:| | Repeating | $L,\, \rho,\, k,\, \mu$ | 4 | | Non-repeating | $h,\, \Delta T,\, C_p,\, \beta,\, g$ | 5 | | **Total** | — | **9** | **Resulting π-groups:** $\pi_1 = Nu = \dfrac{hL}{k}$ ; $\pi_3 = Pr = \dfrac{C_p \mu}{k}$ ; $\pi_2 \cdot \pi_4 \cdot \pi_5 = Gr = \dfrac{g \beta \Delta T L^{3} \rho^{2}}{\mu^{2}}$ $$\boxed{Nu = f(Gr, Pr) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; Nu = C(Gr)^{a}(Pr)^{b}}$$ **(b) Step 1 — Film temperature and β:** $$T_{film} = \frac{90 + 30}{2} = 60\,^\circ C = 333 \text{ K}$$ $$\beta = \frac{1}{333} = 3.003 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$ **Step 2 — Characteristic length for horizontal plate (Area/Perimeter):** $$L = \frac{A_s}{P} = \frac{(0.6)(0.6)}{4(0.6)} = \frac{0.36}{2.4} = 0.15 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3 — Grashof number:** $$\begin{aligned} Gr &= \frac{(9.81)(3.003 \times 10^{-3})(60)(0.15)^{3}}{(1.892 \times 10^{-5})^{2}} \\ &= \frac{(9.81)(3.003 \times 10^{-3})(60)(0.003375)}{3.580 \times 10^{-10}} \\ &= \frac{5.966 \times 10^{-3}}{3.580 \times 10^{-10}} \end{aligned}$$ $$\boxed{Gr = 1.667 \times 10^{7}}$$ **Step 4 — Rayleigh number and regime:** $$Ra = Gr \cdot Pr = (1.667 \times 10^{7})(0.7202) = 1.201 \times 10^{7}$$ Since $10^{4} < Ra < 10^{7}$, **laminar** flow over the upper surface of a horizontal hot plate. **Step 5 — Apply correlation (hot surface facing up):** $$Nu = 0.54 \, Ra^{1/4} = 0.54 \times (1.201 \times 10^{7})^{0.25}$$ $$(1.201 \times 10^{7})^{0.25} = (1.201)^{0.25} \times 10^{1.75} = 1.047 \times 56.23 = 58.86$$ $$Nu = 0.54 \times 58.86 = 31.78$$ **Step 6 — Convective coefficient h:** $$h = \frac{Nu \cdot k}{L} = \frac{(31.78)(0.02808)}{0.15} = 5.95 \text{ W/m}^{2}\text{K}$$ **Step 7 — Total heat transfer:** $$A = 0.6 \times 0.6 = 0.36 \text{ m}^{2}$$ $$Q = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = (5.95)(0.36)(60) = 128.5 \text{ W}$$ $$\boxed{Q \approx 128.5 \text{ W}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert ΔT or T_film to Kelvin** — Using Celsius in $\beta = 1/T_film$ gives wrong answer. Always use **K**. > 2. **Using plate length instead of Area/Perimeter for horizontal plates** — Vertical plate uses height $L$; horizontal plate uses $L = A/P$. > 3. **Picking wrong correlation** — Vertical plate vs. horizontal plate (facing up vs. facing down) have different constants ($0.59$ vs. $0.54$ vs. $0.27$). > 4. **Wrong film temperature** — Always average $T_s$ and $T_\infty$, NOT inlet and outlet. > 5. **Properties at wrong temperature** — Use ALL fluid properties at $T_{film}$, not at wall or bulk. > 6. **Skipping regime check** — Always verify $Ra < 10^9$ for laminar; otherwise use turbulent correlation $Nu = 0.10 \cdot Ra^{1/3}$. > 7. **Losing 2 marks for missing units** in the final answer. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Board-Exam Ready):** > - **Total variables** in natural convection = **9**; **fundamental dimensions** = **4**; **π-terms** = **5**. > - **Repeating variables** for π-derivation: **L, ρ, k, μ**. > - **Nusselt Number:** $Nu = hL/k$ — the unknown we want. > - **Grashof Number:** $Gr = g \beta \Delta T L^{3} \rho^{2}/\mu^{2}$ — buoyancy vs. viscosity. > - **Prandtl Number:** $Pr = C_p \mu/k$ — purely a fluid property, **independent of geometry**. > - **Rayleigh Number:** $Ra = Gr \cdot Pr$ — the regime controller. > - **Laminar rule:** $Ra < 10^{9}$ → use $Nu = 0.59 \, Ra^{1/4}$ (vertical plate). > - **Turbulent rule:** $Ra > 10^{9}$ → use $Nu = 0.10 \, Ra^{1/3}$. > - **Always evaluate fluid properties at the FILM temperature** $T_{film} = (T_s + T_\infty)/2$ in **K**. > - **Horizontal plate** characteristic length = $A_s / P$ (NOT just the side length). > - **β for ideal gases:** $\beta = 1/T_{film}$ (in K⁻¹). > - **Final master equation:** $h = (Nu \cdot k)/L$; **heat transfer:** $Q = h A \Delta T$. > - **Dimension check before submitting:** every π-term MUST be dimensionless — verify this to grab full marks in derivation questions.

topics.title: Natural Convection from Vertical and Horizontal Surfaces Under Laminar and Turbulent Conditions for Flat Plates and Cylinders Under Constant Heat Flux and Constant Wall Temperature Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Natural Convection from Vertical and Horizontal Surfaces Under Laminar and Turbulent Conditions for Flat Plates and Cylinders Under Constant Heat Flux and Constant Wall Temperature Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Natural (Free) Convection** is the mode of heat transfer between a solid surface and a fluid in which **bulk fluid motion is induced solely by density differences** arising from temperature gradients within the fluid — *no external pump, fan, or blower is employed*.

The driving force is the **buoyancy force** that develops because warmer (less dense) fluid near the heated surface rises while cooler (denser) fluid sinks to replace it. In KTU nomenclature, the two principal boundary conditions used to characterize such problems are:

- **Constant Wall Temperature (CWT):** The surface temperature $T_s$ is uniform and prescribed.
- **Constant Heat Flux (CHF / Uniform Flux):** The wall heat flux $q''$ is uniform along the surface.

The principal geometries covered in this module are:

| Geometry | Typical Use |
|----------|-------------|
| **Vertical flat plate** | Building walls, condensers, heated panels |
| **Horizontal flat plate** | Floor slabs, ceilings, hot tank tops |
| **Horizontal cylinder** | Steam pipes, finned tubes, overhead transmission lines |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a *room heater* glowing red in winter. The metal fins are extremely hot, but no fan pushes air over them. The air touching the fins warms up, becomes lighter, and **floats upward** — pulling fresh cold air in from the bottom. That self-sustaining "loop" is natural convection. The heat transfer rate depends on **how easily the fluid can move** (viscosity), **how strongly buoyancy pulls** (density difference), and **how quickly heat diffuses into the fluid** (thermal diffusivity).

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (in **bold**)

- **Grashof Number (Gr):** ratio of buoyancy to viscous forces.
- **Prandtl Number (Pr):** ratio of momentum diffusivity to thermal diffusivity.
- **Rayleigh Number (Ra):** product Gr·Pr — the *governing* dimensionless group for natural convection.
- **Nusselt Number (Nu):** dimensionless heat transfer coefficient.
- **Thermal expansion coefficient, $\beta$** (for ideal gases $\beta = 1/T_{film}$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU-Examiner-Cited):** Flow regime is **laminar** for $Ra_L < 10^9$ and **turbulent** for $Ra_L > 10^9$ on a vertical plate. The transitional band $10^9 < Ra_L < 10^{10}$ requires blended correlations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Physical Reasoning

1. **Density gradient formation.** Heat diffusing from the wall into the fluid lowers its density locally.
2. **Buoyancy force activation.** The Archimedean up-thrust on a fluid element exceeds its weight → upward motion.
3. **Boundary layer growth.** A thin **velocity (hydrodynamic) boundary layer** and a **thermal boundary layer** develop side-by-side along the surface.
4. **Flow regime determined by Ra.** Low $Ra$ → laminar, smooth, orderly plumes; high $Ra$ → turbulent, fluctuating, mixing-dominated.
5. **Heat flux feedback (CHF vs CWT).** Under CWT the local $T_s - T_\infty$ decays along the plate (Nu decreases with $x$); under CHF the local $\Delta T$ grows (Nu becomes constant).

## 2.2 Governing Dimensionless Groups

### Grashof Number

$$Gr_L = \frac{g \, \beta \, (T_s - T_\infty) \, L^{3}}{\nu^{2}}$$

where:
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration, **9.81 m·s⁻²**
- $\beta$ = volumetric thermal expansion coefficient, **K⁻¹**
- $T_s - T_\infty$ = wall-to-fluid temperature difference, **K**
- $L$ = characteristic length, **m**
- $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity, **m²·s⁻¹**

### Rayleigh Number

$$Ra_L = Gr_L \cdot Pr = \frac{g \, \beta \, (T_s - T_\infty) \, L^{3}}{\nu \, \alpha}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **vertical plates and cylinders**, $L$ = **plate height** or **cylinder diameter**. For **horizontal plates**, $L_c = A_s / P$ where $A_s$ = surface area and $P$ = perimeter.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

### (A) Vertical Flat Plate — Churchill–Chu (1989) — CWT — *Entire Ra Range*

$$\overline{Nu}_L = \left\{ 0.825 \;+\; \frac{0.387 \, Ra_L^{1/6}}{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{0.492}{Pr}\right)^{9/16}\right]^{8/27}} \right\}^{2}$$

Valid for: $\; 10^{-1} < Ra_L < 10^{12}$, $\; 0.001 < Pr < \infty$.

### (B) Vertical Flat Plate — CHF (Uniform Flux) — Eckert–Jackson

$$\overline{Nu}_L = \frac{q'' \cdot L}{k (T_s - T_\infty)_L}$$

Common simplified form for **laminar** CHF:

$$\overline{Nu}_L = 0.68 + \frac{0.670 \, Ra_L^{1/4}}{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{0.492}{Pr}\right)^{9/16}\right]^{4/9}}$$

### (C) Horizontal Flat Plate — McAdams Correlations

| Orientation | Regime | $\overline{Nu}_L$ Correlation | Range of $Ra_L$ |
|-------------|--------|-------------------------------|------------------|
| Hot surface facing **up** OR cold surface facing **down** (unstable) | Laminar | $0.54 \, Ra_L^{1/4}$ | $10^{4} \le Ra_L \le 10^{7}$ |
| Hot surface facing **up** OR cold surface facing **down** (unstable) | Turbulent | $0.15 \, Ra_L^{1/3}$ | $10^{7} \le Ra_L \le 10^{11}$ |
| Hot surface facing **down** OR cold surface facing **up** (stable) | Combined | $0.27 \, Ra_L^{1/4}$ | $10^{5} \le Ra_L \le 10^{10}$ |

Characteristic length: $L_c = A_s / P$.

### (D) Horizontal Cylinder — Churchill–Chu (or Morgan)

$$\overline{Nu}_D = \left\{ 0.60 \;+\; \frac{0.387 \, Ra_D^{1/6}}{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{0.492}{Pr}\right)^{9/16}\right]^{8/27}} \right\}^{2}$$

Characteristic length: $D$ = cylinder outer diameter. Valid: $Ra_D$ up to $10^{12}$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of shell-and-tube heat exchangers:** horizontal tube banks rely on the Churchill–Chu single-cylinder correlation as a building block.
- **Overhead power transmission lines:** current-carrying conductors reject heat primarily by natural convection; ampacity ratings (IS 802 / IEEE 738) use horizontal cylinder correlations.
- **Building HVAC:** external wall heat loss on cold nights uses vertical-plate CWT correlations with $L$ = wall height.
- **Solar thermal collectors:** tilted absorber plates are analysed via blended vertical + horizontal plate models.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Grashof Number (via Boussinesq Force Balance)

Consider a fluid parcel of volume $V$ near a heated wall. Its density at temperature $T$ is $\rho$. The colder surroundings have density $\rho_\infty$.

**Net upward buoyancy force:**

$$F_b = (\rho_\infty - \rho) \, g \, V$$

Using Boussinesq approximation: $\rho_\infty - \rho = \rho_\infty \, \beta \, (T - T_\infty)$.

$$F_b = \rho_\infty \, \beta \, (T_s - T_\infty) \, g \, V$$

**Viscous retarding force** on the same parcel moving with velocity $u$ over length $L$:

$$F_v = \mu \, \frac{u}{L} \, A_s$$

Taking the *ratio* of buoyancy to viscous forces and using $\nu = \mu / \rho$:

$$\frac{F_b}{F_v} = \frac{\rho_\infty \, \beta \, \Delta T \, g \, L^{3}}{\mu \, \nu} = \frac{g \, \beta \, \Delta T \, L^{3}}{\nu^{2}} = Gr_L$$

> This proves that $Gr_L$ physically represents the **ratio of buoyancy to viscous forces**, mirroring the role of $Re$ in forced convection.

## 3.2 Working Out a Numerical Problem (Typical KTU Pattern)

**Problem.** A vertical plate 0.5 m tall is at $T_s = 60^{\circ}\text{C}$ in quiescent air at $T_\infty = 20^{\circ}\text{C}$. Estimate the average heat transfer coefficient.

**Step 1 — Film temperature.**

$$T_f = \frac{T_s + T_\infty}{2} = \frac{60 + 20}{2} = 40^{\circ}\text{C} = 313 \,\text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Air properties at 40 °C (standard tables).**

- $\nu = 1.702 \times 10^{-5} \,\text{m}^2\text{/s}$
- $k = 0.02662 \,\text{W/m·K}$
- $Pr = 0.725$
- $\beta = 1/T_f = 1/313 = 3.195 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{K}^{-1}$

**Step 3 — Grashof and Rayleigh numbers.**

$$Gr_L = \frac{(9.81)(3.195\times10^{-3})(40)(0.5)^{3}}{(1.702\times10^{-5})^{2}}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Gr_L &= \frac{9.81 \times 3.195\times10^{-3} \times 40 \times 0.125}{2.897\times10^{-10}} \\[4pt]
     &= \frac{0.1567}{2.897\times10^{-10}} \\[4pt]
     &= 5.41 \times 10^{8}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$Ra_L = Gr_L \cdot Pr = (5.41 \times 10^{8})(0.725) = 3.92 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 4 — Regime check.** $Ra_L < 10^9$ ⇒ **LAMINAR**.

**Step 5 — Apply Churchill–Chu correlation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{Nu}_L &= \left\{ 0.825 + \frac{0.387 \,(3.92\times10^{8})^{1/6}}{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{0.492}{0.725}\right)^{9/16}\right]^{8/27}} \right\}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute subterms:

- $Ra^{1/6} = (3.92\times10^{8})^{0.1667} \approx 56.05$
- $(0.492 / 0.725)^{9/16} = (0.6786)^{0.5625} \approx 0.789$
- $[1 + 0.789]^{8/27} = (1.789)^{0.2963} \approx 1.182$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{Nu}_L &= \left\{ 0.825 + \frac{0.387 \times 56.05}{1.182} \right\}^{2} \\[4pt]
               &= \left\{ 0.825 + 18.35 \right\}^{2} \\[4pt]
               &= (19.18)^{2} \\[4pt]
               &\approx 367.9
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Heat transfer coefficient.**

$$\bar{h} = \frac{\overline{Nu}_L \cdot k}{L} = \frac{367.9 \times 0.02662}{0.5} = 19.59 \,\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Geometry | L (characteristic) | Regime | Correlation to Use |
|----------|--------------------|--------|--------------------|
| Vertical plate | Height $L$ | $Ra_L < 10^9$ laminar | Churchill–Chu (CWT) |
| Vertical plate | Height $L$ | $Ra_L > 10^9$ turbulent | Churchill–Chu (same form covers both) |
| Vertical plate (CHF) | Height $L$ | laminar | Eckert–Jackson form |
| Horizontal plate (hot up) | $A_s/P$ | $Ra < 10^7$ laminar | $0.54 Ra^{1/4}$ |
| Horizontal plate (hot up) | $A_s/P$ | $Ra > 10^7$ turbulent | $0.15 Ra^{1/3}$ |
| Horizontal plate (hot down) | $A_s/P$ | combined | $0.27 Ra^{1/4}$ |
| Horizontal cylinder | Diameter $D$ | entire range | Churchill–Chu cylinder form |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Boundary Layer Development on a Vertical Plate

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Quiescent Fluid at T_inf] --> B[Heated Wall at T_s, x = 0]
    B --> C[Laminar Velocity BL of thickness delta]
    B --> D[Laminar Thermal BL of thickness delta_t]
    C --> E{Regime Check}
    D --> E
    E -->|Ra less than 10 to 9| F[Pure Laminar Plumes]
    E -->|10 to 9 less than Ra less than 10 to 10| G[Transition Zone]
    E -->|Ra greater than 10 to 10| H[Turbulent Plumes with intermittent bursts]
    F --> I[Nu = f Ra to power 1 by 4]
    H --> J[Nu = f Ra to power 1 by 3]
    G --> K[Use Blended or Churchill-Chu Correlation]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture for Selecting the Correct Correlation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Start: Identify Geometry and BC] --> Q{Geometry?}
    Q -->|Vertical plate| R1[Use L = height]
    Q -->|Horizontal plate| R2[Use L = A_s over P]
    Q -->|Horizontal cylinder| R3[Use L = D]
    R1 --> S{BC type?}
    R2 --> S
    R3 --> S
    S -->|CWT| T1[Apply Churchill-Chu correlation]
    S -->|CHF| T2[Apply Eckert-Jackson form]
    T1 --> U[Evaluate Ra sub L]
    T2 --> U
    U --> V{Regime?}
    V -->|Laminar Ra less than 10 to 9| W[Use Nu proportional Ra to power 1 by 4]
    V -->|Turbulent Ra greater than 10 to 9| X[Use Nu proportional Ra to power 1 by 3]
    W --> Y[Compute h bar from Nu over L]
    X --> Y
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Mapping — Heat Transfer Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HS[Heat Source] --> SURF[Surface]
    SURF --> COND[Conduction across wall]
    COND --> CFL[Convective Fluid Layer]
    CFL --> BULK[Quiescent Fluid Bulk]
    BULK --> RA[Compute Ra sub L]
    RA --> COR[Pick Correlation]
    COR --> NU[Nu sub L]
    NU --> HCOV[h bar]
    HCOV --> QT[Heat Flux q double prime]
```

For an unstable orientation (hot plate facing up), indicate rising plumes; for stable orientation (hot plate facing down), show the suppressed layer.
