**Interpretation:** Since Pr < 1 (but close to 1), the thermal boundary layer is **slightly thicker** than the velocity boundary layer, as expected for gases.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) Explain the physical significance of Prandtl Number with suitable examples. Discuss how it influences the relative thickness of hydrodynamic and thermal boundary layers. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Prandtl Number ($\text{Pr}$) physically represents the **ratio of momentum diffusivity to thermal diffusivity** in a fluid. It tells us whether heat or momentum spreads faster through the fluid.

**Case 1: $\text{Pr} \ll 1$ (e.g., Liquid Metals)** — Heat spreads much faster than momentum. The thermal boundary layer is much thicker than the velocity boundary layer. Used in nuclear reactor cooling.

**Case 2: $\text{Pr} \approx 1$ (e.g., Gases)** — Heat and momentum spread at nearly equal rates. Both boundary layers have approximately the same thickness. Common in air-conditioning and combustion applications.

**Case 3: $\text{Pr} \gg 1$ (e.g., Oils)** — Momentum spreads much faster than heat. The velocity boundary layer is much thicker than the thermal boundary layer, meaning heat is concentrated near the surface. Important in lubrication and high-viscosity processing.

The relation between boundary layers is:

$$\frac{\delta}{\delta_t} \approx \text{Pr}^{n}, \quad n = 1/3 \text{ (turbulent)}, \quad n = 1/2 \text{ (laminar)}$$

**(b) Engine oil at $40°\text{C}$ has $\mu = 0.2107 \text{ kg/(m}\cdot\text{s)}$, $C_p = 1965 \text{ J/(kg}\cdot\text{K)}$, and $k = 0.1444 \text{ W/(m}\cdot\text{K)}$. Find the Prandtl Number and explain the implication for heat transfer design. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

$$\text{Pr} = \frac{\mu \, C_p}{k} = \frac{(0.2107)(1965)}{0.1444}$$

Numerator: $0.2107 \times 1965 = 414.0255$

$$\text{Pr} = \frac{414.0255}{0.1444} = 2867.2$$

$$\boxed{\text{Pr} \approx 2867}$$

**Implication for Heat Transfer Design:**
This very high Prandtl number means that for engine oil, the **thermal boundary layer is extremely thin** compared to the velocity boundary layer:

$$\frac{\delta}{\delta_t} = \text{Pr}^{1/3} = (2867)^{1/3} \approx 14.2$$

In heat exchangers handling engine oil:
- Heat transfer is **limited by slow thermal diffusion** within the oil.
- The **Nusselt number** will be relatively small for the same Reynolds number.
- Designers must use **larger surface areas, turbulent promoters, or fins** to compensate.
- Pre-heating oil systems (e.g., lubricant warm-up in cold climates) takes longer because of poor thermal penetration.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often forget to convert units. If $C_p$ is given in $\text{kJ/(kg}\cdot\text{K)}$, it MUST be converted to $\text{J/(kg}\cdot\text{K)}$ before substituting into $\text{Pr} = \mu C_p / k$. Failure to do so yields an answer 1000× too small.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing **Prandtl Number (Pr)** with **Peclet Number (Pe)**. Remember: $\text{Pe} = \text{Re} \cdot \text{Pr}$. The Peclet number depends on the flow geometry; the Prandtl number is a pure fluid property.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Using $\text{Pr}^{1/2}$ instead of $\text{Pr}^{1/3}$ in turbulent flow correlations. KTU 2024 questions typically expect $\text{Pr}^{1/3}$ for turbulent boundary layer analysis.
> 4. **Mark-Loss Zone:** Many students write the formula but skip the **dimensional proof**. KTU examiners explicitly allot 2–3 marks for proving that Pr is dimensionless. Always show the unit cancellation.
> 5. **Boundary Layer Ratio Trap:** Writing $\delta_t / \delta$ instead of $\delta / \delta_t$. The convention is **velocity-to-thermal** ratio.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Prandtl Number**

- **Definition:** $\text{Pr} = \mu C_p / k = \nu / \alpha$. Dimensionless.
- **Physical Meaning:** Ratio of momentum diffusivity to thermal diffusivity.
- **Typical Values:**
  - Liquid Metals: $\text{Pr} \approx 0.001 \text{ to } 0.03$
  - Gases: $\text{Pr} \approx 0.6 \text{ to } 0.8$ (Air $\approx 0.71$)
  - Water: $\text{Pr} \approx 1 \text{ to } 10$ (typically $\approx 7$)
  - Oils: $\text{Pr} \approx 100 \text{ to } 10^4$
- **Boundary Layer Rule:** $\delta / \delta_t = \text{Pr}^{1/2}$ (laminar), $\text{Pr}^{1/3}$ (turbulent).
- **Key Implication:** $\text{Pr} > 1 \Rightarrow \delta > \delta_t$; $\text{Pr} < 1 \Rightarrow \delta < \delta_t$.
- **Use in Correlations:**
  - Dittus-Boelter: $\text{Nu} = 0.023 \, \text{Re}^{0.8} \, \text{Pr}^{n}$
  - Sieder-Tate: $\text{Nu} = 0.027 \, \text{Re}^{0.8} \, \text{Pr}^{1/3} (\mu / \mu_w)^{0.14}$
  - Colburn Analogy: $\text{St} \, \text{Pr}^{2/3} = C_f / 2$
- **Related Numbers to Remember:**
  - $\text{Pe} = \text{Re} \cdot \text{Pr}$ (Peclet Number)
  - $\text{St} = \text{Nu} / (\text{Re} \cdot \text{Pr})$ (Stanton Number)
- **Engineering Significance:** Liquid metals (low Pr) = excellent coolants; oils (high Pr) = poor heat transfer fluids requiring special design.
- **Exam Mantra:** "Pr = $\mu C_p / k$. Always show dimensional proof. Remember $\text{Pr}^{1/3}$ for turbulent."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Colburn Analogies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Colburn Analogies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Colburn Analogy** is an empirical correlation that relates convective heat transfer to fluid friction (momentum transfer) in **fully developed turbulent flow** inside conduits. It is an extension of the simpler **Reynolds Analogy** to fluids whose **Prandtl Number (Pr)** is not equal to unity.

Mathematically, the Colburn Analogy is expressed in its most widely used form as:

$$St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \frac{f}{2}$$

where:
- $St$ is the **Stanton Number** for heat transfer, defined as $St = \dfrac{h}{\rho \cdot u_m \cdot c_p}$
- $Pr$ is the **Prandtl Number**, defined as $Pr = \dfrac{\mu \cdot c_p}{k}$
- $f$ is the **Darcy Friction Factor** (also called Fanning-related when divided by 2)
- $h$ is the convective heat transfer coefficient
- $\rho$ is the fluid density
- $u_m$ is the mean fluid velocity
- $c_p$ is the specific heat at constant pressure
- $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity
- $k$ is the thermal conductivity

The dimensionless group $St \cdot Pr^{2/3}$ is conventionally called the **Colburn j-Factor for Heat Transfer**, denoted $j_H$. Hence the analogy is equivalently written as:

$$j_H = St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \frac{f}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 2):**
> The Colburn Analogy is grouped with Reynolds, Prandtl, and von Karman analogies under the topic "Analogies between Heat, Mass and Momentum Transfer." KTU examiners **frequently** ask derivations, limitations, and comparative questions on this family of analogies.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car on a highway during a cold, windy night. The wind blowing against the car creates two simultaneous effects:

1. **Frictional drag (momentum transfer):** Air molecules hit the car and slow it down. The faster you drive, the more drag you feel.
2. **Cooling of the car body (heat transfer):** The same wind carries heat away from the car bonnet, making it cold.

Notice that **the very same wind (turbulent eddies)** is responsible for *both* effects. The drag force on the car and the cooling rate of the bonnet are not independent — they are linked through the same fluid motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core Physical Idea:**
> In turbulent flow, **eddy motion** simultaneously transports momentum (causing friction) and thermal energy (causing heat transfer). Because the *same* turbulent eddies do both jobs, there must be a direct proportionality between the friction factor and the heat transfer coefficient. This proportionality is what the Colburn Analogy quantifies.

The constant of proportionality depends on the **Prandtl Number**:
- For air and gases ($Pr \approx 0.7$), momentum and thermal boundary layers have comparable thickness → proportionality is almost direct.
- For oils ($Pr \gg 1$), the thermal boundary layer is much thinner than the momentum boundary layer → an extra correction factor $Pr^{2/3}$ is needed.
- For liquid metals ($Pr \ll 0.1$), the thermal boundary layer is much thicker → the analogy breaks down and a different exponent is required.

The factor $Pr^{2/3}$ is the **empirical "correction patch"** that Colburn added in 1933 to make Reynolds's original analogy work for liquids as well as gases.

## 1.3 Why This Analogy Matters in KTU Examinations

- It is a **high-yield topic**: at least one sub-question (3 or 7 marks) appears in nearly every end-semester exam.
- It links three transport phenomena — momentum, heat, and (later) mass transfer — making it a **conceptual bridge** in the syllabus.
- It enables engineers to **predict heat transfer coefficients from pressure drop measurements alone**, which is invaluable in design.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Family of Heat-Momentum Analogies — A Hierarchy

The Colburn Analogy does not exist in isolation. It belongs to a family of progressively refined relationships. Understanding the **chain of reasoning** is essential for KTU answers.

### 2.1.1 Reynolds Analogy (Foundation — 1874)

Osborne Reynolds first proposed that in turbulent flow, the ratio of heat transferred to the fluid to the heat capacity rate equals the ratio of wall shear stress to dynamic pressure:

$$\frac{h \cdot (T_w - T_b)}{\rho \cdot u_m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_w - T_b)} = \frac{\tau_w}{\rho \cdot u_m^2}$$

Simplifying gives the classical **Reynolds Analogy**:

$$St = \frac{f}{2}$$

**Validity:** Strictly valid **only when Pr = 1** (e.g., specific gases under narrow conditions).

### 2.1.2 Colburn Analogy (Empirical Extension — 1933)

Colburn recognized that for most engineering fluids (water, oils, refrigerants), $Pr \neq 1$. He introduced the empirical correction factor $Pr^{2/3}$:

$$St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \frac{f}{2}$$

**Validity:** $0.6 \leq Pr \leq 100$ (covers water, air, oils, refrigerants).

### 2.1.3 Prandtl Analogy (1934)

A more theoretically rigorous form using a two-layer model:

$$\frac{St}{f/2} = \frac{1}{1 + 5 \sqrt{\frac{f}{2} \cdot (Pr - 1)}}$$

**Validity:** Better for gases ($Pr \approx 0.7$), but still fails for liquid metals.

### 2.1.4 von Kármán Analogy (1939)

A three-layer model (viscous sublayer, buffer layer, turbulent core) that uses universal velocity and temperature profiles. Most accurate but mathematically complex.

### 2.1.5 Liquid Metal (Modified Reynolds) Analogy

For $Pr \ll 1$ (liquid metals), the analogy reverses the form:

$$St = \frac{f/2}{\sqrt{f/2} \cdot (constant) - c}$$

## 2.2 Physical Derivation Logic — Why the Exponent is 2/3

The $Pr^{2/3}$ exponent arises from the following reasoning:

1. The **momentum boundary layer thickness** scales as $\delta_m \sim \dfrac{x}{Re_x^{1/5}}$ for turbulent flow.
2. The **thermal boundary layer thickness** scales as $\delta_t \sim \dfrac{\delta_m}{Pr^{1/3}}$ (the famous 1/3-power rule for turbulent thermal layers).
3. The heat transfer coefficient scales as $h \sim \dfrac{k}{\delta_t} \sim \dfrac{k \cdot Pr^{1/3}}{\delta_m}$.
4. The Stanton number becomes:

$$St = \frac{h}{\rho u c_p} = \frac{k \cdot Pr^{1/3}}{\delta_m \cdot \rho u c_p}$$

5. Since $Pr = \dfrac{\mu c_p}{k}$, we have $k = \dfrac{\mu c_p}{Pr}$, so:

$$St = \frac{\mu c_p \cdot Pr^{1/3}}{Pr \cdot \delta_m \cdot \rho u c_p} = \frac{\mu}{\rho u \cdot \delta_m \cdot Pr^{2/3}}$$

6. The friction factor for turbulent flow in smooth tubes scales as $f \sim \dfrac{\mu}{\rho u \delta_m}$ (proportional to wall shear).
7. Therefore:

$$St \sim \frac{f}{2 \cdot Pr^{2/3}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad St \cdot Pr^{2/3} \sim \frac{f}{2}$$

This is the **physical origin** of the famous 2/3 exponent.

## 2.3 Engineering Significance

| Engineering Application | How Colburn Analogy Helps |
|--------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Heat Exchanger Design** | Estimate tube-side $h$ from a single pressure drop measurement |
| **Condenser / Evaporator Sizing** | Use air-side friction data to predict heat transfer performance |
| **Process Plant Piping** | Correlate pump power (friction losses) with heat loss/gain to surroundings |
| **Compact Heat Exchangers** | j-factor remains constant across geometrically similar surfaces |
| **Chemical Reactor Cooling Jackets** | Predict coolant-side heat transfer coefficient from pressure drop tests |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Units / Range | Validity Range |
|---|---------|------------------|------------------------|----------------|
| 1 | $St = \dfrac{h}{\rho u_m c_p}$ | Stanton Number (heat transfer) | Dimensionless | All convective flows |
| 2 | $Pr = \dfrac{\mu c_p}{k}$ | Prandtl Number | Dimensionless | Fluid property |
| 3 | $f = \dfrac{2 \tau_w}{\rho u_m^2}$ | Darcy Friction Factor | Dimensionless | All conduit flows |
| 4 | $St = \dfrac{f}{2}$ | Reynolds Analogy | Dimensionless | $Pr = 1$ only |
| 5 | $St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \dfrac{f}{2}$ | **Colburn Analogy** | Dimensionless | $0.6 \leq Pr \leq 100$ |
| 6 | $j_H = St \cdot Pr^{2/3}$ | Colburn j-Factor for heat | Dimensionless | Turbulent flow only |
| 7 | $\dfrac{St}{f/2} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 5\sqrt{(f/2)(Pr-1)}}$ | Prandtl Analogy | Dimensionless | $Pr \approx 0.7$ gases |
| 8 | $St = \dfrac{f/2}{\sqrt{f/2} \cdot 5 \Pr - c}$ | Modified Analogy (liquid metals) | Dimensionless | $Pr < 0.1$ |
| 9 | $\delta_t \approx \delta_m \cdot Pr^{-1/3}$ | Turbulent layer thickness ratio | Length ratio | $Pr \geq 0.6$ |
| 10 | $Nu = \dfrac{f}{2} \cdot Re \cdot Pr^{1/3}$ | Colburn-type Nusselt Number | Dimensionless | Turbulent tubes |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always state the **validity range** of the analogy you are using. Examiners deduct marks when students write $St = f/2$ without specifying that this is the *Reynolds* form valid only for $Pr = 1$.

## 2.5 Why the 2/3 Exponent is Empirical (Not Theoretical)

The 1/3-power rule for $\delta_t / \delta_m$ comes from dimensional analysis and the universal turbulent velocity profile, so the derivation of the $Pr^{2/3}$ correction appears theoretical. However, Colburn found that the exponent **2/3 gave the best fit** to extensive experimental data on water, oils, and gases. The theoretical value can vary between 1/2 and 1 depending on the assumed velocity profile. The 2/3 exponent is therefore a **semi-empirical compromise** — this is a common interview and exam question.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Reynolds Analogy (From First Principles)

We start from the basic definitions and proceed algebraically.

**Step 1 — Wall Shear Stress Definition:**
The wall shear stress for flow in a circular pipe is:

$$\tau_w = \frac{f \cdot \rho \cdot u_m^2}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Convective Heat Flux Definition:**
The heat flux from the wall to the fluid is given by Newton's law of cooling:

$$q = h \cdot (T_w - T_b)$$

where $T_w$ is the wall temperature and $T_b$ is the bulk mean fluid temperature.

**Step 3 — Reynolds's Original Physical Postulate:**
Reynolds argued that the ratio of the *actual heat transferred* to the *maximum possible heat transfer* equals the ratio of the *friction force* to the *momentum flux*. Mathematically:

$$\frac{q}{\rho \cdot u_m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_w - T_b)} = \frac{\tau_w}{\rho \cdot u_m^2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the Definitions:**

$$\frac{h \cdot (T_w - T_b)}{\rho \cdot u_m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_w - T_b)} = \frac{f \cdot \rho \cdot u_m^2 / 2}{\rho \cdot u_m^2}$$

**Step 5 — Cancel the Common Temperature Difference and Density Terms:**

The $(T_w - T_b)$ terms cancel on the left, and $\rho u_m^2$ cancels on the right, leaving:

$$\frac{h}{\rho \cdot u_m \cdot c_p} = \frac{f}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Recognize the Stanton Number:**

By definition, $St = \dfrac{h}{\rho u_m c_p}$. Therefore:

$$St = \frac{f}{2}$$

This is the **Reynolds Analogy**, and the derivation shows that it relies on the assumption $Pr = 1$ implicitly (since the heat transfer rate was assumed to scale identically with momentum transfer).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Colburn Analogy (Extension to $Pr \neq 1$)

**Step 1 — Begin with the Reynolds Analogy:**

$$St = \frac{f}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Recognize the Limitation:**
For fluids with $Pr \neq 1$, the experimental values of $h$ deviate from this prediction. The deviation is systematic — fluids with $Pr > 1$ (oils) transfer *less* heat than predicted, while fluids with $Pr < 1$ (liquid metals) transfer *more* heat.

**Step 3 — Introduce the Prandtl Number Correction:**
Colburn empirically determined that multiplying the Stanton number by $Pr^{2/3}$ brings the predicted values into close agreement with experimental data across a wide range of $Pr$:

$$St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \frac{f}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Rewrite in Terms of the Nusselt Number:**
Using the identity $St \cdot Re \cdot Pr = Nu$, we obtain:

$$Nu = \frac{f}{2} \cdot Re \cdot Pr^{1/3}$$

This is a very useful engineering form because it directly gives the Nusselt number from friction data.

**Step 5 — Verify with Experimental Data:**

For water at 300 K:
- $Pr \approx 5.85$
- $Pr^{2/3} \approx 3.25$
- If $St = 0.005$ is measured, then $f/2 = 0.005 \times 3.25 = 0.01625$, giving $f \approx 0.0325$. This matches the Blasius correlation $f = 0.079 Re^{-0.25}$ for turbulent flow at $Re \approx 10^4$ within ~10%.

The agreement is excellent, which is why the analogy is so widely used.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Equivalence of the Two Forms

**Given:**
$$St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \frac{f}{2}$$

**Multiply both sides by $Re \cdot Pr$:**

$$St \cdot Re \cdot Pr \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \frac{f}{2} \cdot Re \cdot Pr$$

**Recognize that $St \cdot Re \cdot Pr = Nu$:**

$$Nu \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \frac{f}{2} \cdot Re \cdot Pr$$

**Divide both sides by $Pr^{2/3}$:**

$$Nu = \frac{f}{2} \cdot Re \cdot Pr^{1 - 2/3} = \frac{f}{2} \cdot Re \cdot Pr^{1/3}$$

**Final Result:**

$$Nu = \frac{f}{2} \cdot Re \cdot Pr^{1/3}$$

This is the **Dittus-Boelter-like** form derived from analogy considerations.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Analogy

| Fluid Type | Pr Range | Recommended Analogy | Reason |
|------------|----------|---------------------|--------|
| Gases (air, N₂, O₂) | 0.6 – 0.8 | Reynolds or Colburn | $Pr \approx 1$ → both work |
| Water (300–400 K) | 3 – 7 | **Colburn** | $Pr$ moderately > 1, $Pr^{2/3}$ correction needed |
| Engine oils | 100 – 1000 | **Colburn** (with caution) | $Pr^{2/3}$ works up to $Pr \approx 100$ |
| Liquid metals (Na, Hg) | 0.005 – 0.03 | Modified Reynolds (not Colburn) | $Pr^{2/3}$ fails badly; thermal BL >> momentum BL |
| Refrigerants (R-134a) | 3 – 5 | Colburn | Within validity range |
| Steam (gas phase) | 0.9 – 1.1 | Reynolds or Colburn | $Pr \approx 1$ |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Water flows through a smooth tube at a mean velocity of 2 m/s. The tube is 25 mm in diameter. The bulk water temperature is 350 K and the wall temperature is 360 K. Estimate the convective heat transfer coefficient using the Colburn Analogy, given that the Darcy friction factor is 0.0042.

**Given:**
- $u_m = 2$ m/s
- $D = 0.025$ m
- $T_b = 350$ K, $T_w = 360$ K
- $f = 0.0042$
- Water properties at 350 K: $\rho = 972$ kg/m³, $c_p = 4195$ J/(kg·K), $\mu = 3.65 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa·s, $k = 0.668$ W/(m·K), $Pr = 2.29$

**Step 1 — Calculate Prandtl Number (verification):**

$$Pr = \frac{\mu c_p}{k} = \frac{3.65 \times 10^{-4} \times 4195}{0.668} \approx 2.29$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Colburn Analogy:**

$$St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \frac{f}{2}$$

$$St = \frac{f/2}{Pr^{2/3}} = \frac{0.0021}{(2.29)^{2/3}}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate $Pr^{2/3}$:**

$$Pr^{2/3} = (2.29)^{0.6667} \approx 1.735$$

**Step 4 — Compute Stanton Number:**

$$St = \frac{0.0021}{1.735} \approx 0.00121$$

**Step 5 — Solve for Heat Transfer Coefficient:**

$$h = St \cdot \rho \cdot u_m \cdot c_p = 0.00121 \times 972 \times 2 \times 4195$$

$$h = 0.00121 \times 8{,}155{,}080 \approx 9867 \text{ W/(m²·K)}$$

**Step 6 — Result:**

$$\boxed{h \approx 9870 \text{ W/(m²·K)}}$$

This value is consistent with standard Dittus-Boelter predictions for water at this Reynolds number, confirming the validity of the Colburn Analogy for this case.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: The Family of Heat-Momentum Analogies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Turbulent Convective Heat Transfer] --> nodeB{Fluid Property Check: Prandtl Number}
    nodeB -- Pr equals 1 --> nodeC[Reynolds Analogy]
    nodeB -- 0.6 to 100 --> nodeD[Colburn Analogy]
    nodeB -- 0.6 to 0.8 gases --> nodeE[Prandtl Analogy]
    nodeB -- Three layer model --> nodeF[von Karman Analogy]
    nodeB -- Pr less than 0.1 --> nodeG[Modified Liquid Metal Analogy]
    nodeC --> nodeH[Result St equals f over 2]
    nodeD --> nodeI[Result St times Pr to 2/3 equals f over 2]
    nodeE --> nodeJ[Result St over f2 equals 1 over 1 plus 5 sqrt f2 times Pr minus 1]
    nodeF --> nodeK[Three Layer Model Result]
    nodeG --> nodeL[Result St equals f2 over constant]
    nodeH --> nodeM[Apply to Heat Exchanger Design]
    nodeI --> nodeM
    nodeJ --> nodeM
    nodeK --> nodeM
    nodeL --> nodeM
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Physical Reasoning Behind the Colburn 2/3 Exponent

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Turbulent Eddy Motion] --> node2[Momentum Transfer to Wall]
    node1 --> node3[Heat Transfer to Wall]
    node2 --> node4[Friction Factor f]
    node3 --> node5[Heat Transfer Coefficient h]
    node5 --> node6[Boundary Layer Thickness Ratio]
    node6 -- delta_t over delta_m equals Pr to power 1 over 3 --> node7[Stanton Number Scaling]
    node4 --> node8[Stanton Number Prediction]
    node7 --> node8
    node8 -- Empirical exponent 2 over 3 --> node9[Colburn Analogy Result]
    node9 --> node10[Valid for 0.6 to 100]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Engineering Application of Colburn Analogy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Measure Pressure Drop dP in Pipe] --> nodeB[Compute Friction Factor f]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Lookup Fluid Prandtl Number Pr]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Apply Colburn Equation]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Compute Stanton Number St]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Compute Nusselt Number Nu]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Compute Heat Transfer Coefficient h]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Size Heat Exchanger]
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Boundary Layer Comparison for Different Pr

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Pr less than 0.1 Liquid Metals] --> nodeB[Thick Thermal BL]
    nodeC[Pr equals 1 Gases] --> nodeD[Thermal BL equals Momentum BL]
    nodeE[Pr greater than 1 Oils Water] --> nodeF[Thin Thermal BL]
    nodeB --> nodeG[Modified Reynolds Analogy]
    nodeD --> nodeH[Classical Reynolds]
    nodeF --> nodeI[Colburn Pr to 2 over 3 Correction]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** These are functional block-flow schematics. In a KTU exam, when drawing a figure is not feasible, a clear labeled block diagram earns full credit. Focus on the *physical logic* and the *transitions* between blocks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**State the Colburn Analogy and explain its physical significance.**

**Model Answer:**
The Colburn Analogy is an empirical relationship that correlates convective heat transfer with fluid friction in fully developed turbulent flow. It is stated as:

$$St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \frac{f}{2}$$

where $St$ is the Stanton Number, $Pr$ is the Prandtl Number, and $f$ is the Darcy friction factor. The dimensionless group $St \cdot Pr^{2/3}$ is known as the Colburn j-factor. The analogy is valid for fluids with $0.6 \leq Pr \leq 100$ and for $Re > 10{,}000$ (fully turbulent regime).

**Physical Significance:** The same turbulent eddies that transport momentum from the fluid to the wall (causing friction) also transport thermal energy (causing heat transfer). The Colburn Analogy exploits this common transport mechanism to predict heat transfer coefficients from easily measurable pressure drop data, eliminating the need for direct temperature measurements in many design situations.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**Differentiate between Reynolds Analogy and Colburn Analogy.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Reynolds Analogy | Colburn Analogy |
|---------|------------------|-----------------|
| Equation | $St = f/2$ | $St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = f/2$ |
| Validity | $Pr = 1$ only | $0.6 \leq Pr \leq 100$ |
| Pr correction | None | $Pr^{2/3}$ factor |
| Origin | Theoretical (1874) | Empirical (1933) |
| Applicable fluids | Few specific gases | Water, oils, refrigerants, gases |
| Accuracy | Limited | Good for most engineering fluids |

The Reynolds Analogy is the simpler theoretical form, while the Colburn Analogy extends it through an empirical correction factor to cover a wider range of engineering fluids.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**a)** Starting from the basic definitions of wall shear stress and convective heat flux, derive the Reynolds Analogy. State clearly the assumptions made. (7 marks)

**b)** Explain the physical reasoning that led Colburn to introduce the $Pr^{2/3}$ correction factor. Discuss the validity range of the resulting Colburn Analogy and compare it with the Prandtl Analogy. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation of Reynolds Analogy:**

**Step 1:** Wall shear stress in a pipe:

$$\tau_w = \frac{f \cdot \rho \cdot u_m^2}{2}$$

**Step 2:** Convective heat flux from wall:

$$q = h \cdot (T_w - T_b)$$

**Step 3:** Reynolds's postulate equates the fraction of momentum transferred as friction to the fraction of heat transferred as convective flux:

$$\frac{\tau_w}{\rho \cdot u_m^2} = \frac{q}{\rho \cdot u_m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_w - T_b)}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute:

$$\frac{f \cdot \rho \cdot u_m^2 / 2}{\rho \cdot u_m^2} = \frac{h \cdot (T_w - T_b)}{\rho \cdot u_m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_w - T_b)}$$

**Step 5:** Cancel common terms:

$$\frac{f}{2} = \frac{h}{\rho \cdot u_m \cdot c_p} = St$$

**Result:** $St = f/2$

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady, fully developed turbulent flow
2. Fluid properties constant
3. Prandtl Number $Pr = 1$
4. No heat sources within the fluid
5. Negligible viscous dissipation

**Part (b) — Physical Reasoning for $Pr^{2/3}$ Correction:**

The Reynolds Analogy strictly holds only for $Pr = 1$. For real fluids:

- When $Pr > 1$ (oils, water), the **thermal boundary layer is thinner** than the momentum boundary layer. Heat diffuses slowly relative to momentum, so the heat transfer coefficient is *lower* than predicted by the simple Reynolds formula.
- When $Pr < 1$ (liquid metals), the thermal boundary layer is *thicker* than the momentum boundary layer, giving *higher* heat transfer than predicted.

Colburn observed that the ratio $\delta_t / \delta_m \approx Pr^{-1/3}$ for turbulent flow (from the 1/3-power rule). This leads to the correction $St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = f/2$, which makes the analogy work over the range $0.6 \leq Pr \leq 100$.

**Comparison with Prandtl Analogy:**

| Aspect | Colburn | Prandtl |
|--------|---------|---------|
| Theoretical basis | Empirical | Semi-theoretical |
| Equation | $St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = f/2$ | $\dfrac{St}{f/2} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 5\sqrt{(f/2)(Pr-1)}}$ |
| Accuracy for gases | Good | Better |
| Accuracy for liquids | Good | Moderate |
| Complexity | Simple | Moderate |

The Prandtl Analogy is more accurate for gases near $Pr = 0.7$ but reduces to the Reynolds form when $Pr = 1$, and agrees well with the Colburn form for moderate $Pr$ values.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**a)** A fluid flows through a smooth tube with a measured Darcy friction factor of 0.0055. The fluid has a Prandtl number of 4.2 and a Reynolds number of 25,000. Estimate the Nusselt number using:
(i) Reynolds Analogy
(ii) Colburn Analogy (7 marks)

**b)** Water at 60°C flows through a 50 mm diameter smooth tube at 3 m/s. Using the Colburn Analogy, determine the convective heat transfer coefficient. Given: $\rho = 983$ kg/m³, $c_p = 4185$ J/(kg·K), $\mu = 4.67 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa·s, $k = 0.654$ W/(m·K), $f = 0.0045$ (Darcy). (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Nusselt Number Estimates:**

**Given:** $f = 0.0055$, $Pr = 4.2$, $Re = 25{,}000$

**(i) Reynolds Analogy:**

$$St = \frac{f}{2} = \frac{0.0055}{2} = 0.00275$$

$$Nu = St \cdot Re \cdot Pr = 0.00275 \times 25{,}000 \times 4.2 = 288.75$$

**(ii) Colburn Analogy:**

$$St = \frac{f/2}{Pr^{2/3}} = \frac{0.00275}{(4.2)^{2/3}}$$

$$Pr^{2/3} = (4.2)^{0.6667} \approx 2.61$$

$$St = \frac{0.00275}{2.61} \approx 0.001054$$

$$Nu = St \cdot Re \cdot Pr = 0.001054 \times 25{,}000 \times 4.2 \approx 110.7$$

**Conclusion:** The Reynolds Analogy overpredicts the Nusselt number by a factor of $4.2^{2/3} \approx 2.61$ when applied to a fluid with $Pr = 4.2$. The Colburn Analogy gives the more realistic estimate.

---

**Part (b) — Heat Transfer Coefficient for Water:**

**Given:**
- $D = 0.050$ m, $u_m = 3$ m/s
- $\rho = 983$ kg/m³, $c_p = 4185$ J/(kg·K)
- $\mu = 4.67 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa·s, $k = 0.654$ W/(m·K)
- $f = 0.0045$

**Step 1 — Compute Prandtl Number:**

$$Pr = \frac{\mu c_p}{k} = \frac{4.67 \times 10^{-4} \times 4185}{0.654} \approx 2.99$$

**Step 2 — Apply Colburn Analogy:**

$$St = \frac{f/2}{Pr^{2/3}} = \frac{0.00225}{(2.99)^{2/3}}$$

$$Pr^{2/3} = (2.99)^{0.6667} \approx 2.08$$

$$St = \frac{0.00225}{2.08} \approx 0.001082$$

**Step 3 — Compute Heat Transfer Coefficient:**

$$h = St \cdot \rho \cdot u_m \cdot c_p = 0.001082 \times 983 \times 3 \times 4185$$

$$h = 0.001082 \times 12{,}341{,}385 \approx 13{,}353 \text{ W/(m²·K)}$$

**Result:** $h \approx 13{,}350$ W/(m²·K), consistent with literature values for water at 60°C in turbulent flow.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Writing the wrong analogy:** Students often write $St = f/2$ (Reynolds) when the question asks for Colburn. Always check the Prandtl Number range before choosing.
> 2. **Forgetting units:** The Stanton Number is dimensionless. The friction factor is dimensionless. Never carry units into the final answer for these.
> 3. **Confusing Darcy and Fanning friction factors:** The Colburn form uses the **Darcy friction factor** (4× the Fanning factor). Mixing these up leads to a 4× error.
> 4. **Omitting validity range:** Examiners specifically check whether you state that Colburn is valid for $0.6 \leq Pr \leq 100$. Omitting this costs 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Algebra errors in the exponent:** $(Pr)^{2/3}$ is NOT the same as $(Pr)^{0.3}$ or $(Pr)^{1.5}$. Compute carefully.
> 6. **Applying to liquid metals:** The Colburn Analogy fails badly for $Pr < 0.1$. Mentioning this shows conceptual depth and impresses examiners.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### ⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Reynolds Analogy:** $St = f/2$ — valid for $Pr = 1$ only, theoretical basis
- [ ] **Colburn Analogy:** $St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = f/2$ — valid for $0.6 \leq Pr \leq 100$, empirical
- [ ] **Prandtl Analogy:** More accurate for gases ($Pr \approx 0.7$), two-layer model
- [ ] **von Kármán Analogy:** Three-layer model, most accurate, most complex
- [ ] **Liquid Metal Analogy:** Different form, valid for $Pr < 0.1$
- [ ] **j-Factor:** $j_H = St \cdot Pr^{2/3}$ — the Colburn heat transfer j-factor
- [ ] **Nusselt form:** $Nu = (f/2) \cdot Re \cdot Pr^{1/3}$ — direct engineering form
- [ ] **Boundary layer ratio:** $\delta_t / \delta_m \approx Pr^{-1/3}$ — origin of 2/3 exponent
- [ ] **Engineering use:** Predict $h$ from pressure drop data alone
- [ ] **Limitation:** Fails for $Pr < 0.1$ (liquid metals) — must use modified analogy

### 🎯 Key Mnemonics for KTU Exam

| Mnemonic | Meaning |
|----------|---------|
| **"Reynolds = Raw"** | No Pr correction, raw form |
| **"Colburn = Corrected"** | Has the $Pr^{2/3}$ correction |
| **"Prandtl = Precise for gases"** | Better for $Pr \approx 0.7$ |
| **"von Kármán = K"omplex"** | Three-layer, mathematically intensive |

### 📊 Critical Numerical Anchors (Memorize for KTU)

- $Pr_{air} \approx 0.71$
- $Pr_{water, 300K} \approx 5.85$
- $Pr_{water, 350K} \approx 2.29$
- $Pr_{engine\,oil} \approx 100 - 1000$
- $Pr_{mercury} \approx 0.025$
- Colburn validity: $0.6 \leq Pr \leq 100$
- Darcy = 4 × Fanning (friction factor conversion)

### 🔑 Three Lines to Memorize for the Exam

1. **Definition:** "The Colburn Analogy is an empirical correlation $St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = f/2$ valid for $0.6 \leq Pr \leq 100$ in fully developed turbulent flow."

2. **Origin:** "Colburn introduced the $Pr^{2/3}$ correction to the Reynolds Analogy to account for the difference in thickness between thermal and momentum boundary layers, where $\delta_t / \delta_m \approx Pr^{-1/3}$."

3. **Application:** "It enables prediction of convective heat transfer coefficients from pressure drop measurements, which is invaluable in heat exchanger design."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** When a question mentions "Colburn Analogy," always (a) state the equation, (b) state the validity range, (c) explain the origin of the 2/3 exponent, and (d) compare briefly with Reynolds and Prandtl forms. This four-part structure covers 90% of marks available on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radiation Heat Transfer: Thermal Radiation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Radiation Heat Transfer: Thermal Radiation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Thermal Radiation** is the energy emitted by a body in the form of electromagnetic waves (or photons) as a consequence of its temperature alone, without requiring an intervening medium. It is the only mode of heat transfer that can occur across a perfect vacuum.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PCCHT402 – Heat Transfer Operations**, thermal radiation is treated as an electromagnetic phenomenon governed by the temperature of the emitting surface. The electromagnetic radiation emitted by a body because of its temperature occupies a continuous spectrum of wavelengths (typically $\lambda$ between **$10^{-7}$ m** to **$10^{-3}$ m** for engineering applications), with the visible region lying between **0.4 µm and 0.7 µm**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Definition (Blackbody):** A *blackbody* is an ideal surface that absorbs ALL incident radiation at every wavelength and at every temperature. It is the perfect emitter and absorber of thermal radiation.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — Real World Analogy

Think of thermal radiation like **sunlight striking your face on a cold winter morning**:

- There is no air movement (no convection) and no physical contact (no conduction), yet you *feel warmth*. That warmth is thermal radiation.
- A dark-colored shirt absorbs more sunlight than a white one — the same logic applies to surfaces in engineering: dark, dull surfaces **emit and absorb more** radiation than shiny, polished ones.
- A metal teapot left on a table cools slowly because its shiny surface **radiates poorly**, even though it is hot. A black kettle of the same temperature cools much faster because it is an *efficient radiator*.
- The hotter the object, the more energetic (and shorter wavelength) its radiation. A heated iron rod first glows dull red, then orange, then white as temperature rises — this is **Wien's Displacement Principle** in action.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Required for This Module**
> - **Stefan–Boltzmann constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$
> - **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c_0 = 2.998 \times 10^8 \ \text{m/s}$
> - **Planck's first constant:** $C_1 = 3.743 \times 10^{-16} \ \text{W·m}^2$
> - **Planck's second constant:** $C_2 = 1.4387 \times 10^{-2} \ \text{m·K}$
> - **Wien's displacement constant:** $C_3 = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m·K}$ (≈ 2898 µm·K)

## 1.3 Fundamental Quantities in Radiation

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Emissive Power (Emittance) | $E$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ | Total energy radiated per unit area per unit time |
| Spectral Emissive Power | $E_\lambda$ | $\text{W/m}^3$ | Emissive power per unit wavelength interval |
| Irradiation | $G$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ | Total radiation incident on a surface per unit area |
| Absorptivity | $\alpha$ | dimensionless | Fraction of incident radiation absorbed |
| Reflectivity | $\rho$ | dimensionless | Fraction of incident radiation reflected |
| Transmissivity | $\tau$ | dimensionless | Fraction of incident radiation transmitted |
| Emissivity | $\varepsilon$ | dimensionless | Ratio of actual emission to blackbody emission |

By conservation of energy for an opaque / semi-transparent surface:

$$\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$$

For an **opaque** surface ($\tau = 0$), this simplifies to:

$$\alpha + \rho = 1$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Thermal Radiation

Thermal radiation originates from **atomic and molecular vibrations** within matter. When a substance is heated:

1. Charged particles (electrons, ions, dipoles) oscillate due to thermal agitation.
2. These accelerated charges produce oscillating electromagnetic fields.
3. The energy propagates outward at the **speed of light** ($c_0 = 3 \times 10^8 \ \text{m/s}$).
4. Upon striking another body, the radiation is partially absorbed, reflected, or transmitted.

The radiation propagates as **discrete quanta (photons)** of energy:

$$E_p = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

where $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \ \text{J·s}$ is **Planck's constant**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Unlike conduction and convection, radiation does NOT require a medium — it is most efficient in **vacuum**, which is why the Sun's energy reaches Earth through the near-vacuum of space.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table compiles the **exam-critical formulas** for Module 2 — Radiation Heat Transfer. Memorize and practice each one.

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_b = \sigma T^4$ | **Stefan–Boltzmann Law** — blackbody emissive power ($\text{W/m}^2$) |
| 2 | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | Emissive power of a *gray* body |
| 3 | $E_\lambda = \dfrac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[\exp(C_2/\lambda T) - 1\right]}$ | **Planck's Spectral Distribution Law** |
| 4 | $\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = C_3 = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m·K}$ | **Wien's Displacement Law** |
| 5 | $E_{b,\lambda_{\max}} \propto T^5$ | Peak spectral emissive power scaling |
| 6 | $q = \varepsilon \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ | Net radiation heat exchange between two infinite parallel gray plates |
| 7 | $q_{12} = \dfrac{\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1} + \dfrac{1}{A_1 F_{12}} + \dfrac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A_2}}$ | **General radiation network equation** between two finite gray surfaces |
| 8 | $\varepsilon = \alpha$ | **Kirchhoff's Law** at thermal equilibrium |
| 9 | $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$ | Energy balance on a surface |
| 10 | $A_i F_{ij} = A_j F_{ji}$ | **Reciprocity Rule** for view factors |
| 11 | $\sum_{j=1}^{N} F_{ij} = 1$ | **Summation Rule** for a closed enclosure |
| 12 | $F_{ij} + F_{ik} = 1$ | (for 3-surface enclosure where $k$ is the rest) |
| 13 | $A_1 F_{12} = A_2 F_{21}$ | Equivalent to Reciprocity (alternate form) |
| 14 | $F_{ii} = 1 - \sum_{j \neq i} F_{ij}$ | View factor of a surface to itself |

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of Thermal Radiation

Thermal radiation analysis is critical in the design of:

- **Fired heaters** and **furnaces** (refineries, petrochemicals)
- **Boilers** and **steam generators** — heat transfer in the combustion chamber is dominated by radiation from flames and hot gases
- **Nuclear reactors** — radiation heat transfer across vacuum gaps
- **Solar collectors** — absorbing solar radiation for energy conversion
- **Re-entry vehicles and aerospace** — extreme radiative heating
- **Insulation engineering** — using low-emissivity (shiny) *reflective foils* to reduce radiative heat loss, e.g., multi-layer insulation (MLI) in cryogenic vessels and Thermos flasks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Design Rule:** For surfaces above **500 K**, radiation often becomes the *dominant* mode of heat loss. Below ~300 K, convection and conduction usually dominate.

## 2.4 Concept of Blackbody, Gray Body and Real Surfaces

### (a) Blackbody
A blackbody is a hypothetical body that:
- Absorbs **all** incident radiation at every wavelength ($\alpha = 1$)
- Emits the **maximum possible** radiation at every wavelength ($\varepsilon = 1$)
- Its emissive power depends **only on temperature**, given by Stefan–Boltzmann law.

### (b) Gray Body
A gray body has emissivity $\varepsilon < 1$, but it is **independent of wavelength**. Its emissive power at any temperature is:

$$E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$$

### (c) Real (Selective) Surfaces
For real surfaces, $\varepsilon_\lambda$ varies with wavelength — these are called *non-gray* or *selective* bodies (e.g., polished metals have low $\varepsilon$ in IR but high in UV).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stefan–Boltzmann Law from Planck's Distribution

The starting point is **Planck's Spectral Distribution Law**, which gives the spectral emissive power of a blackbody at wavelength $\lambda$ and absolute temperature $T$:

$$E_{b,\lambda} = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^{5}\,\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T}\right) - 1\right]}$$

The **total hemispherical emissive power** of a blackbody is obtained by integrating the spectral distribution over all wavelengths from $0$ to $\infty$:

$$E_b = \int_{0}^{\infty} E_{b,\lambda}\, d\lambda$$

Substituting Planck's expression:

$$E_b = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{C_1}{\lambda^{5}\,\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T}\right) - 1\right]} d\lambda$$

**Step 1 — Variable substitution:**
Let $x = \dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T}$, so that $\lambda = \dfrac{C_2}{x T}$ and $d\lambda = -\dfrac{C_2}{x^{2} T} dx$.

**Step 2 — Express $\lambda^{5}$ in terms of $x$:**

$$\lambda^{5} = \left(\frac{C_2}{x T}\right)^{5} = \frac{C_2^{5}}{x^{5} T^{5}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the integral:**

$$E_b = \int_{\infty}^{0} \frac{C_1}{\dfrac{C_2^{5}}{x^{5} T^{5}}\,\left[\exp(x) - 1\right]} \cdot \left(-\frac{C_2}{x^{2} T}\right) dx$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the constants:**

$$E_b = \frac{C_1\, T^{5}}{C_2^{5}} \cdot \frac{C_2}{T} \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{3}}{\exp(x) - 1}\, dx$$

$$E_b = \frac{C_1\, T^{4}}{C_2^{4}} \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{3}}{\exp(x) - 1}\, dx$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the definite integral:**
The integral $\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} \dfrac{x^{3}}{e^{x} - 1}\, dx$ is a classical result equal to $\dfrac{\pi^{4}}{15}$.

**Step 6 — Combine constants:**

$$E_b = \frac{C_1\,\pi^{4}}{15\, C_2^{4}} \cdot T^{4}$$

**Step 7 — Define Stefan–Boltzmann constant:**

$$\sigma = \frac{C_1 \pi^{4}}{15 C_2^{4}}$$

Therefore, we arrive at the **Stefan–Boltzmann Law**:

$$\boxed{\,E_b = \sigma T^{4}\,} \quad \text{where} \quad \sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Wien's Displacement Law

To find the wavelength at which $E_{b,\lambda}$ is maximum, differentiate Planck's distribution with respect to $\lambda$ and set it equal to zero:

$$\frac{dE_{b,\lambda}}{d\lambda} = 0$$

Starting from:

$$E_{b,\lambda} = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^{5}\,\left[\exp(C_2/\lambda T) - 1\right]}$$

**Step 1 — Set up the differentiation.**
After differentiation and rearrangement, the condition for the maximum leads to a transcendental equation:

$$5\left[1 - \exp(-x)\right] = x$$

where $x = \dfrac{C_2}{\lambda_{\max} T}$.

**Step 2 — Numerical solution.**
Solving the transcendental equation by iteration gives:

$$x = 4.9651$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\lambda_{\max}$:**

$$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{C_2}{x\, T} = \frac{1.4387 \times 10^{-2}}{4.9651 \cdot T}$$

$$\boxed{\,\lambda_{\max}\, T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m·K} \equiv C_3\,}$$

This is **Wien's Displacement Law**.

**Engineering interpretation:** As temperature increases, the wavelength at which the maximum emission occurs *shifts to shorter wavelengths*. This is why a heated iron rod first glows red, then yellow, then white.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Kirchhoff's Law

Consider a small body (1) of area $A_1$ enclosed in a much larger isothermal enclosure (2) at temperature $T$. At thermal equilibrium:

- Body 1 absorbs radiation from the enclosure: $q_{\text{abs}} = \alpha_1 \, A_1 \, G$
- Body 1 emits radiation: $q_{\text{em}} = \varepsilon_1 \, A_1 \, E_b$

**Step 1 — Energy balance at equilibrium.**
At steady-state thermal equilibrium, emitted energy = absorbed energy:

$$\alpha_1 \, A_1 \, G = \varepsilon_1 \, A_1 \, E_b$$

$$\frac{\varepsilon_1}{\alpha_1} = \frac{G}{E_b}$$

**Step 2 — Examine the enclosure.**
Since the enclosure is a blackbody (large cavity), by definition:

$$G = E_b \quad \text{(irradiation from a blackbody equals its emissive power)}$$

**Step 3 — Conclude Kirchhoff's identity:**

$$\frac{\varepsilon_1}{\alpha_1} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\,\varepsilon = \alpha\,}$$

**Physical meaning:** A good emitter is also a good absorber. Polished silver (low $\varepsilon$) reflects most radiation; lampblack (high $\varepsilon$) absorbs and emits strongly.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Radiation Between Two Infinite Parallel Plates (No Shielding)

Consider two large parallel plates, separated by a large distance compared to their dimensions, so that $F_{12} = 1$. Surface 1 at $T_1$ with emissivity $\varepsilon_1$; Surface 2 at $T_2$ with emissivity $\varepsilon_2$.

**Step 1 — Define radiosity $J$.**
Radiosity = emitted + reflected radiation leaving a surface:

$$J = \varepsilon E_b + \rho G$$

Since $\rho = 1 - \alpha = 1 - \varepsilon$ (gray, opaque):

$$J = \varepsilon E_b + (1 - \varepsilon) G$$

**Step 2 — Express net heat flow from surface 1:**

$$q_1 = J_1 - G_1$$

For two infinite parallel surfaces $G_1 = J_2$ and $G_2 = J_1$:

$$q = \frac{E_{b1} - E_{b2}}{\dfrac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2}}$$

Substituting $E_{bi} = \sigma T_i^4$:

$$\boxed{\,q = \frac{\sigma(T_1^{4} - T_2^{4})}{\dfrac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2}}\,}$$

This is the **net radiative heat flux** between two infinite gray parallel plates.

---

## 3.5 General Network Equation: Two Finite Gray Bodies

For two finite gray bodies inside an enclosure, the radiation **network analogy** (analogous to electrical circuit) yields:

$$q_{12} = \frac{\sigma(T_1^{4} - T_2^{4})}{\dfrac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1} + \dfrac{1}{A_1 F_{12}} + \dfrac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A_2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The three denominator terms correspond to **surface resistance of body 1**, **space (geometric) resistance**, and **surface resistance of body 2**, respectively — analogous to three resistances in a series electrical circuit.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — View Factor (Shape Factor) Rules

| Rule Name | Equation | Physical Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Summation Rule** | $\sum_{j} F_{ij} = 1$ | All view factors from surface $i$ must sum to unity |
| **Reciprocity Rule** | $A_i F_{ij} = A_j F_{ji}$ | Symmetric exchange of radiation between two surfaces |
| **Superposition Rule** | $F_{i,(j+k)} = \dfrac{A_j F_{ij} + A_k F_{ik}}{A_j + A_k}$ | Combined view factor when surfaces are merged |
| **Enclosure Rule** | $\sum_{j} A_i F_{ij} = \sum_{j} A_j F_{ji}$ | Summation weighted by area |

For simple geometries (KTU standard cases):

- Two infinite parallel plates: $F_{12} = F_{21} = 1$
- Concentric cylinders/spheres (outer at $i$, inner at $j$): $F_{ij} = 1$, $F_{ji} = A_i/A_j$
- Two perpendicular plates sharing common edge: $F_{12} = 0.5$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Electromagnetic Spectrum for Thermal Radiation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SOURCE OF THERMAL RADIATION]:::sourceNode --> B[Atomic and Molecular Vibrations in Matter]
    B --> C[Acceleration of Charged Particles]
    C --> D[Generation of Electromagnetic Waves]
    D --> E[Propagation Through Vacuum or Medium]
    E --> F[Incident on Receiving Surface]
    F --> G{INTERACTION WITH SURFACE}
    G -->|Absorbed alpha| H[ABSORPTION alpha]
    G -->|Reflected rho| I[REFLECTION rho]
    G -->|Transmitted tau| J[TRANSMISSION tau]
    H --> K[ENERGY CONVERSION TO HEAT]
    I --> L[RETURNED TO SURROUNDINGS]
    J --> M[PASSES THROUGH BODY]

    classDef sourceNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Radiation Heat Transfer System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HTR[HEAT SOURCE AT TEMPERATURE T1]:::sourceNode
    EB1[Blackbody Emissive Power Eb1 = sigma T1^4]:::processNode
    EPS1[Gray Surface 1 with Emissivity eps1]:::processNode
    RAD[Radiative Energy Transfer Through Medium]:::transferNode
    EPS2[Gray Surface 2 with Emissivity eps2]:::processNode
    EB2[Blackbody Emissive Power Eb2 = sigma T2^4]:::processNode
    HTS[Heat Sink at Temperature T2]:::sinkNode
    NET[Net Heat Transfer q12 = Net Radiation Equation]:::resultNode
    VF[View Factor F12 Geometric Coupling]:::paramNode

    HTR --> EB1 --> EPS1 --> RAD
    VF -.modulates.-> RAD
    RAD --> EPS2 --> EB2 --> HTS
    EPS1 --> NET
    EPS2 --> NET
    EB1 --> NET
    EB2 --> NET
    VF --> NET

    classDef sourceNode fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef sinkNode fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000080,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef processNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef transferNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef resultNode fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
    classDef paramNode fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#808000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

## 4.3 Radiation Exchange Between Two Gray Surfaces — Resistance Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Surface 1 at T1]:::surfaceNode
    R1[Surface Resistance 1-e1 over e1A1]:::resistorNode
    RS[Space Resistance 1 over A1F12]:::resistorNode
    R2[Surface Resistance 1-e2 over e2A2]:::resistorNode
    S2[Surface 2 at T2]:::surfaceNode
    DRV[Driving Potential sigma T1^4 minus sigma T2^4]:::driverNode

    S1 --> R1 --> RS --> R2 --> S2
    DRV -.drives.-> R1
    DRV -.drives.-> R2
    DRV -.drives.-> RS

    classDef surfaceNode fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef resistorNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef driverNode fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

## 4.4 Wavelength Distribution — Planck Curves at Different Temperatures

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Planck Distribution at Temperature T1 LOW]:::curveNode
    P2[Planck Distribution at Temperature T2 MEDIUM]:::curveNode
    P3[Planck Distribution at Temperature T3 HIGH]:::curveNode

    P1 -->|peak shifts| P2
    P2 -->|peak shifts left and rises| P3

    P1 -. lambda_max at T1 .-> L1[Wien Law lambda_max T1 equal to 2898 micrometer Kelvin]:::lawNode
    P2 -. lambda_max at T2 .-> L2[Wien Law lambda_max T2 equal to 2898 micrometer Kelvin]:::lawNode
    P3 -. lambda_max at T3 .-> L3[Wien Law lambda_max T3 equal to 2898 micrometer Kelvin]:::lawNode

    P1 --> I1[Area under curve proportional to T to power 4 Stefan Law]:::lawNode
    P2 --> I2[Area under curve proportional to T to power 4 Stefan Law]:::lawNode
    P3 --> I3[Area under curve proportional to T to power 4 Stefan Law]:::lawNode

    classDef curveNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef lawNode fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** Each block in the diagrams above represents a *physical or mathematical step* in the radiation heat transfer process. The arrows indicate the logical flow of energy or information. In KTU board exams, schematic diagrams of this type (especially the resistance network in §4.3) are awarded **2–3 marks** if drawn correctly with all three resistances and the driving potential clearly labelled.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

### Question A1
**State and explain Stefan–Boltzmann Law. Also write the value of Stefan–Boltzmann constant.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The total hemispherical emissive power of a blackbody is proportional to the fourth power of its absolute temperature:

$$E_b = \sigma T^{4}$$

where $E_b$ is the emissive power in $\text{W/m}^2$, $T$ is absolute temperature in K, and $\sigma$ is the **Stefan–Boltzmann constant** $= 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$.

This law states that radiation heat transfer is *highly sensitive to temperature* — a small increase in temperature produces a large increase in radiation heat loss. **(3 Marks – Definition 1M, Equation 1M, Constant & interpretation 1M)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

### Question A2
**Define (i) Emissivity and (ii) Absorptivity. State Kirchhoff's Law.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**(i) Emissivity ($\varepsilon$):** The ratio of the emissive power of a real surface to that of a blackbody at the same temperature: $\varepsilon = E/E_b$. **(1 Mark)**

**(ii) Absorptivity ($\alpha$):** The fraction of incident radiation that is absorbed by a surface, $0 \le \alpha \le 1$. **(1 Mark)**

**Kirchhoff's Law:** At thermal equilibrium, the emissivity of a surface equals its absorptivity: $\varepsilon = \alpha$. Hence a good absorber is a good emitter, and a poor absorber (shiny, reflective surface) is a poor emitter. **(1 Mark)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose 2–4 marks by:
> 1. **Forgetting units** in the final answer (KTU deducts 0.5–1 mark per missing unit).
> 2. **Confusing $\varepsilon$ with $\alpha$** in Kirchhoff's Law — they are equal only at thermal equilibrium.
> 3. **Missing the view factor** $F_{12}$ in the network equation for *finite* surfaces (they assume $F_{12}=1$).
> 4. **Not drawing the radiation network** in problems involving multiple surfaces — examiners award 2 marks for the schematic alone.
> 5. **Expressing temperatures in °C instead of K** — this is a *fatal* error in Stefan–Boltzmann calculations.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) — OPTION 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Model Paper]**

**Two large parallel plates, placed very close to each other, exchange radiation. The temperatures of the surfaces are 800 K and 500 K respectively. If the emissivities are 0.6 and 0.4 respectively, calculate:**
**(a)** The net heat transfer per unit area between the plates. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** The percentage reduction in heat transfer if a polished aluminum radiation shield ($\varepsilon_s = 0.05$) is placed between the plates. **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution B-A (a):

For two infinite parallel plates with $F_{12} = 1$:

$$q = \frac{\sigma(T_1^{4} - T_2^{4})}{\dfrac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2}}$$

**Step 1 — Compute surface resistance terms:**

$$\frac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1} = \frac{1-0.6}{0.6} = \frac{0.4}{0.6} = 0.6667$$

$$\frac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2} = \frac{1-0.4}{0.4} = \frac{0.6}{0.4} = 1.5000$$

**Step 2 — Compute temperature difference term:**

$$T_1^{4} - T_2^{4} = (800)^{4} - (500)^{4}$$

$$T_1^{4} = 4.096 \times 10^{11} \ \text{K}^{4}$$

$$T_2^{4} = 6.25 \times 10^{10} \ \text{K}^{4}$$

$$T_1^{4} - T_2^{4} = 4.096 \times 10^{11} - 6.25 \times 10^{10} = 3.471 \times 10^{11} \ \text{K}^{4}$$

**Step 3 — Final computation:**

$$q = \frac{5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 3.471 \times 10^{11}}{0.6667 + 1.5000}$$

$$q = \frac{5.67 \times 3.471}{2.1667} = \frac{19.681}{2.1667}$$

$$\boxed{q = 9084 \ \text{W/m}^{2} \approx 9.08 \ \text{kW/m}^{2}} \quad \textbf{(7 Marks)}$$

#### Solution B-A (b):

With a radiation shield between the plates, the heat flux is reduced because there are now *two* radiative gaps in series.

$$q_{\text{shielded}} = \frac{\sigma(T_1^{4} - T_2^{4})}{\left(\dfrac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1-\varepsilon_s}{\varepsilon_s}\right) + \left(\dfrac{1-\varepsilon_s}{\varepsilon_s} + \dfrac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2}\right)}$$

**Step 1 — Shield resistance term:**

$$\frac{1-\varepsilon_s}{\varepsilon_s} = \frac{1-0.05}{0.05} = \frac{0.95}{0.05} = 19.0$$

**Step 2 — Compute new denominator:**

$$\text{Denominator} = (0.6667 + 19.0) + (19.0 + 1.5) = 19.6667 + 20.5 = 40.1667$$

**Step 3 — Compute new heat flux:**

$$q_{\text{shielded}} = \frac{5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 3.471 \times 10^{11}}{40.1667}$$

$$q_{\text{shielded}} = \frac{19.681}{40.1667} = 490.0 \ \text{W/m}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Percentage reduction:**

$$\%\text{reduction} = \frac{9084 - 490.0}{9084} \times 100 = 94.6\%$$

$$\boxed{\,\text{Heat transfer is reduced by } 94.6\%\,} \quad \textbf{(7 Marks)}$$

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — OPTION 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the **Stefan–Boltzmann Law** from **Planck's spectral distribution law**. State clearly all the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A blackbody at 2000 K emits radiation. Compute: **(i)** Wavelength of maximum emission, **(ii)** Total emissive power, **(iii)** Spectral emissive power at the wavelength of maximum emission. **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution B-B (a):

**Assumptions:**
1. Radiation is emitted by a blackbody surface at uniform temperature $T$.
2. The emitting surface is diffuse (Lambertian) and isotropic.
3. The system is in thermodynamic equilibrium.

**Starting equation — Planck's Law:**

$$E_{b,\lambda} = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^{5}\,\left[\exp(C_2/\lambda T) - 1\right]}$$

**Step 1:** Total emissive power is the integral over all wavelengths:

$$E_b = \int_{0}^{\infty} E_{b,\lambda}\, d\lambda = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{C_1}{\lambda^{5}\,\left[\exp(C_2/\lambda T) - 1\right]}\, d\lambda$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $x = C_2/(\lambda T)$, so $\lambda = C_2/(xT)$ and $d\lambda = -C_2\, dx/(x^{2} T)$:

$$\lambda^{5} = \frac{C_2^{5}}{x^{5} T^{5}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute in the integral and flip limits:

$$E_b = \frac{C_1\, T^{4}}{C_2^{4}} \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{3}}{e^{x} - 1}\, dx$$

**Step 4:** The integral is a classical result:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{3}}{e^{x} - 1}\, dx = \frac{\pi^{4}}{15} \approx 6.494$$

**Step 5:** Substituting back:

$$E_b = \frac{C_1 \pi^{4}}{15\, C_2^{4}}\, T^{4} = \sigma T^{4}$$

where $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$. $\quad \textbf{(7 Marks)}$

---

#### Solution B-B (b):

**Given:** $T = 2000$ K (blackbody)

**(i) Wavelength of maximum emission — Wien's Law:**

$$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{C_3}{T} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{2000}$$

$$\boxed{\,\lambda_{\max} = 1.449 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m} = 1.449 \ \mu\text{m}\,} \quad \textbf{(2 Marks)}$$

**(ii) Total emissive power — Stefan–Boltzmann Law:**

$$E_b = \sigma T^{4} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (2000)^{4}$$

$$E_b = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.6 \times 10^{13}$$

$$\boxed{\,E_b = 9.072 \times 10^{5} \ \text{W/m}^{2} = 907.2 \ \text{kW/m}^{2}\,} \quad \textbf{(2 Marks)}$$

**(iii) Spectral emissive power at $\lambda_{\max}$:**

Using Planck's law at $\lambda = 1.449 \times 10^{-6}$ m:

$$E_{b,\lambda_{\max}} = \frac{C_1}{\lambda_{\max}^{5}\,\left[\exp(C_2/\lambda_{\max} T) - 1\right]}$$

Compute $C_2/(\lambda_{\max} T) = 1.4387 \times 10^{-2}/(1.449 \times 10^{-6} \times 2000) = 4.965$

$$e^{4.965} - 1 = 143.3 - 1 = 142.3$$

$$\lambda_{\max}^{5} = (1.449 \times 10^{-6})^{5} = 6.39 \times 10^{-30} \ \text{m}^{5}$$

$$E_{b,\lambda_{\max}} = \frac{3.743 \times 10^{-16}}{6.39 \times 10^{-30} \times 142.3}$$

$$E_{b,\lambda_{\max}} = \frac{3.743 \times 10^{-16}}{9.094 \times 10^{-28}}$$

$$\boxed{\,E_{b,\lambda_{\max}} = 4.116 \times 10^{11} \ \text{W/m}^{2\text{·m}}\,} \quad \textbf{(3 Marks)}$$

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam**

### Core Definitions
- **Thermal Radiation:** Energy transfer by EM waves due to temperature alone; no medium needed.
- **Blackbody:** Absorbs all incident radiation at every wavelength; emits maximum possible.
- **Emissivity ($\varepsilon$):** Ratio of actual to blackbody emissive power, $0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$.
- **Absorptivity ($\alpha$):** Fraction of incident radiation absorbed; $\varepsilon = \alpha$ at equilibrium (Kirchhoff).
- **Gray Body:** $\varepsilon < 1$ but wavelength-independent.
- **View Factor ($F_{ij}$):** Fraction of radiation leaving surface $i$ that strikes surface $j$.

### Critical Numerical Values
- $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$
- $C_1 = 3.743 \times 10^{-16} \ \text{W·m}^2$
- $C_2 = 1.4387 \times 10^{-2} \ \text{m·K}$
- $\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = 2898 \ \mu\text{m·K}$

### Essential Laws (must memorize with units)
1. **Stefan–Boltzmann:** $E_b = \sigma T^4$ (W/m²)
2. **Wien's Displacement:** $\lambda_{\max} T = 2898 \ \mu\text{m·K}$
3. **Kirchhoff's Law:** $\varepsilon = \alpha$
4. **Planck's Distribution:** $E_{b,\lambda} = \dfrac{C_1}{\lambda^5[e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1]}$
5. **Reciprocity:** $A_i F_{ij} = A_j F_{ji}$
6. **Summation:** $\sum_j F_{ij} = 1$

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- ❌ Using °C instead of K in Stefan–Boltzmann → **Wrong answer, full marks lost**.
- ❌ Forgetting that gray-body emissive power is $\varepsilon \sigma T^4$, not $\sigma T^4$.
- ❌ Applying $F_{12} = 1$ for *finite* surfaces — that only holds for *infinite* parallel plates.
- ❌ Omitting the space resistance $1/(A_1 F_{12})$ in the network equation.
- ❌ Confusing emissive power (W/m²) with total emitted power (W).

### Problem-Solving Strategy (KTU Pattern)
1. **Identify** the geometry (parallel plates, concentric cylinders, enclosure).
2. **Draw** the radiation resistance network.
3. **Compute** each resistance term.
4. **Apply** the network equation: $q = \sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)/\sum R_i$.
5. **Verify** units and check physical sense (heat should flow from hot to cold).

### Real-World Engineering Connections
- Boiler furnace design — flame radiation to tubes.
- Cryogenic vessel insulation — multi-layer reflective foils with low $\varepsilon$.
- Solar thermal collectors — maximizing $\alpha$ and minimizing $\varepsilon$ via selective coatings.
- Aerospace — re-entry thermal protection systems rely on radiative cooling.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spectral Emissive Power
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Spectral Emissive Power

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Spectral Emissive Power** (also called **monochromatic emissive power**) of a surface at a given temperature $T$ is defined as the rate of radiation energy emitted per unit area of the surface per unit wavelength interval at a specified wavelength $\lambda$. It is denoted as $E_\lambda$ or $E_{b\lambda}$ (for a blackbody).

Mathematically, it is expressed as the derivative of the total emissive power $E$ with respect to wavelength $\lambda$ at constant temperature $T$:

$$E_\lambda = \left(\frac{\partial E}{\partial \lambda}\right)_T$$

**SI Units:** $\text{W/m}^2 \cdot \text{m}$ or equivalently $\text{W/m}^3$. When wavelength is expressed in micrometers ($\mu m$), the units become $\text{W/m}^2 \cdot \mu m$.

### 1.2 Related Definitions for Context

- **Emissive Power ($E$):** The total radiant energy emitted per unit time per unit surface area over **all wavelengths** (from 0 to $\infty$). Units: **W/m²**.
- **Blackbody:** An idealized surface that absorbs **all** incident radiation at all wavelengths and emits the **maximum possible** radiation at any given temperature.
- **Gray Body:** A surface whose **monochromatic emissivity is constant** across all wavelengths.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Spectral emissive power is the foundation upon which Planck's distribution, Wien's Displacement Law, and Stefan–Boltzmann Law are mathematically constructed. Mastering this concept is essential for solving radiation heat transfer problems in KTU ESE.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Heat Rainbow"

Imagine heating a piece of iron in a forge. At low temperatures, it glows a **dull red**. As you increase the temperature, it turns **orange**, then **yellow-white**, and eventually becomes a bluish-white at very high temperatures. Why?

Every object, by virtue of its temperature, emits a "rainbow" of thermal radiation. However, this rainbow is **not uniform** — certain wavelengths dominate. **Spectral emissive power** tells us *how much* energy is being emitted at *each individual color* (wavelength) of that rainbow.

- The **total emissive power** ($E$) is like measuring the *total brightness* of the heated metal — summing the entire rainbow.
- The **spectral emissive power** ($E_\lambda$) is like measuring the brightness *at one specific color* — for example, just the green portion.

Just as a rainbow has more energy concentrated in some bands than others, $E_\lambda$ varies dramatically with $\lambda$, and this variation is described by **Planck's Law**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** $E_\lambda$ is a *function of both temperature $T$ and wavelength $\lambda$*. Total emissive power $E$ is simply the *area under the spectral distribution curve* $E_\lambda$ vs $\lambda$.

### 1.4 Physical Constants Referenced in this Module

| Constant | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Planck's Constant | $h$ | $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s |
| Boltzmann Constant | $k$ | $1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K |
| Speed of Light | $c$ | $2.998 \times 10^{8}$ m/s |
| Stefan–Boltzmann Constant | $\sigma$ | $5.670 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴ |
| Wien's Displacement Constant | $b$ | $2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Spectral Distribution Concept

When a body is heated, its atoms and molecules undergo accelerated thermal motion. Charged particles accelerate and decelerate, producing electromagnetic radiation across a continuous spectrum. The intensity of emission is **not uniform** — it follows a characteristic distribution governed by quantum mechanics (Planck's Law).

The shape of the spectral distribution curve $E_{b\lambda}$ vs $\lambda$ depends **only on temperature**:

- At **low temperatures**, the peak emission occurs at long wavelengths (infrared region).
- At **high temperatures**, the peak shifts to shorter wavelengths (visible/UV region).
- The **area under the curve** = total emissive power $E_b$ (Stefan–Boltzmann).

### 2.2 Relationship Between Spectral and Total Emissive Power

The total emissive power is obtained by integrating the spectral emissive power over the entire wavelength spectrum (from 0 to $\infty$):

$$E = \int_{0}^{\infty} E_\lambda \, d\lambda$$

This is the **master integral** of radiation heat transfer. All major radiation laws (Stefan–Boltzmann, Wien's Displacement) are derived from this integral combined with Planck's spectral distribution.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Law | Mathematical Expression | Units | Key Insight |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Spectral Emissive Power Definition | $E_\lambda = \left(\dfrac{\partial E}{\partial \lambda}\right)_T$ | $\text{W/m}^2 \cdot \mu m$ | Energy emitted at one specific wavelength |
| 2 | Total Emissive Power (from Spectral) | $E = \int_{0}^{\infty} E_\lambda \, d\lambda$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ | Area under $E_\lambda$ vs $\lambda$ curve |
| 3 | Planck's Distribution (Blackbody) | $E_{b\lambda} = \dfrac{2 \pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{hc}{\lambda k T}\right) - 1\right]}$ | $\text{W/m}^2 \cdot \mu m$ | Spectral distribution of a blackbody |
| 4 | Wien's Displacement Law | $\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K | m·K | Peak wavelength inversely proportional to $T$ |
| 5 | Stefan–Boltzmann Law | $E_b = \sigma T^{4}$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ | Total blackbody emissive power |
| 6 | Kirchhoff's Law | $\dfrac{\alpha_\lambda}{E_{b\lambda}} = \dfrac{1}{E_{b\lambda}} \Rightarrow \varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$ | Dimensionless | Good absorbers are good emitters |
| 7 | Emissivity | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{E}{E_b}$ | Dimensionless | Ratio of actual to blackbody emission |
| 8 | Gray Body Spectral Emissivity | $\varepsilon_\lambda = \varepsilon = \text{constant}$ | Dimensionless | Independent of wavelength |
| 9 | Gray Body Total Emissivity | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ | Modified Stefan–Boltzmann |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** Always distinguish between $\varepsilon$ (total emissivity), $\varepsilon_\lambda$ (spectral/monochromatic emissivity), and $\alpha$ (absorptivity). KTU examiners frequently test whether students know that *for a gray body* these quantities are equal.

### 2.4 Engineering Significance

Spectral emissive power analysis is critical in the design of:

- **Furnaces and Boilers:** To calculate radiative heat flux in combustion chambers.
- **Solar Collectors:** Solar radiation peaks near $0.5\ \mu m$ (visible range), and surfaces must be tuned to absorb these wavelengths.
- **Infrared Thermography & Thermal Imaging:** Cameras detect specific IR bands; emissivity calibration depends on $E_\lambda$.
- **Spacecraft Thermal Protection:** Re-entry vehicles experience radiation at extreme wavelengths — coating emissivity is wavelength-specific.
- **Glass & Ceramic Industries:** Colored glasses transmit/absorb based on spectral profiles.

### 2.5 The Three Cardinal Curves of Radiation

A blackbody's spectral distribution exhibits **three key features** that students must memorize for KTU exams:

1. **Maximum Emitted Wavelength** — given by Wien's Displacement Law ($\lambda_{\max} T = b$).
2. **Maximum Spectral Emissive Power** — given by substituting $\lambda_{\max}$ into Planck's law:
$$E_{b\lambda_{\max}} \propto T^{5}$$
3. **Total Emitted Power** — given by Stefan–Boltzmann Law ($E_b = \sigma T^{4}$).

These three relationships between $E_{b\lambda_{\max}}$, $E_b$, and $T$ are the most tested items in Part A of KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: Total Emissive Power from Spectral Emissive Power

**Statement:** Prove that the total emissive power is the integral of the spectral emissive power over all wavelengths.

**Starting Point:**

By definition, the spectral emissive power is the rate of energy emission per unit area per unit wavelength at wavelength $\lambda$.

**Step 1:** The energy emitted by a surface per unit area per unit time in a small wavelength interval $d\lambda$ centered at wavelength $\lambda$ is:

$$dE = E_\lambda \, d\lambda$$

**Step 2:** This means $E_\lambda$ represents the *intensity* of radiation at that particular wavelength. To obtain the total energy emitted across the *entire spectrum* (from $\lambda = 0$ to $\lambda \to \infty$), we must sum (integrate) all such small contributions:

$$E = \int_{0}^{\infty} dE$$

**Step 3:** Substituting $dE = E_\lambda \, d\lambda$:

$$E = \int_{0}^{\infty} E_\lambda \, d\lambda$$

**Step 4 (Interpretation):** This integral physically represents the **area under the curve** when $E_\lambda$ is plotted as a function of $\lambda$. This area equals the total emissive power.

**Conclusion:** The total emissive power is the continuous sum (integral) of spectral emissive power across all wavelengths from 0 to $\infty$. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation II: Stefan–Boltzmann Law from Planck's Law

**Statement:** Starting from Planck's spectral distribution, derive the Stefan–Boltzmann Law $E_b = \sigma T^4$.

**Step 1:** Recall Planck's spectral distribution for a blackbody:

$$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{2 \pi h c^2}{\lambda^{5} \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{hc}{\lambda k T}\right) - 1\right]}$$

**Step 2:** The total blackbody emissive power is the integral of the spectral distribution over all wavelengths:

$$E_b = \int_{0}^{\infty} E_{b\lambda} \, d\lambda = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{2 \pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5} \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{hc}{\lambda k T}\right) - 1\right]} \, d\lambda$$

**Step 3:** Introduce the substitution to simplify the integral. Let:

$$x = \frac{hc}{\lambda k T} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = \frac{hc}{x k T}$$

Differentiating with respect to $x$:

$$d\lambda = -\frac{hc}{k T} \cdot \frac{1}{x^{2}} \, dx$$

When $\lambda \to 0$, $x \to \infty$; when $\lambda \to \infty$, $x \to 0$. The negative sign reverses the limits:

$$E_b = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{2 \pi h c^{2}}{\left(\dfrac{hc}{x k T}\right)^{5} \left(e^{x} - 1\right)} \cdot \frac{hc}{k T} \cdot \frac{1}{x^{2}} \, dx$$

**Step 4:** Simplify the denominator:

$$\left(\frac{hc}{x k T}\right)^{5} = \frac{h^{5} c^{5}}{x^{5} k^{5} T^{5}}$$

Substituting back:

$$E_b = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{2 \pi h c^{2} \cdot x^{5} k^{5} T^{5}}{h^{5} c^{5} \left(e^{x} - 1\right)} \cdot \frac{h c}{k T \, x^{2}} \, dx$$

**Step 5:** Group constants. Collect $h$, $c$, $k$, $T$ powers:

$$E_b = 2 \pi k^{4} T^{4} \cdot \frac{1}{h^{3} c^{2}} \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{3}}{e^{x} - 1} \, dx$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate the standard integral (a known result from statistical mechanics):

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{3}}{e^{x} - 1} \, dx = \frac{\pi^{4}}{15}$$

**Step 7:** Substitute back:

$$E_b = 2 \pi k^{4} T^{4} \cdot \frac{1}{h^{3} c^{2}} \cdot \frac{\pi^{4}}{15} = \frac{2 \pi^{5} k^{4}}{15 h^{3} c^{2}} \cdot T^{4}$$

**Step 8:** Define the Stefan–Boltzmann constant $\sigma$:

$$\sigma = \frac{2 \pi^{5} k^{4}}{15 h^{3} c^{2}} = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/m}^{2} \cdot \text{K}^{4}$$

**Step 9:** Therefore:

$$\boxed{E_b = \sigma T^{4}}$$

**Conclusion:** The Stefan–Boltzmann law is a direct consequence of integrating Planck's spectral distribution over all wavelengths. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Derivation III: Wien's Displacement Law

**Statement:** Show that the wavelength at which $E_{b\lambda}$ is maximum satisfies $\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = b$ (constant).

**Step 1:** To find $\lambda_{\max}$, differentiate $E_{b\lambda}$ with respect to $\lambda$ and set the result to zero:

$$\frac{d E_{b\lambda}}{d \lambda} = 0$$

**Step 2:** Express Planck's distribution using the substitution $y = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda k T}$. Then:

$$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{2 \pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5} (e^{y} - 1)}$$

**Step 3:** Take the derivative. After applying chain rule and product rule (and using $dy/d\lambda = -hc/(\lambda^{2} k T)$):

$$\frac{d E_{b\lambda}}{d \lambda} = \frac{2 \pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{6} (e^{y} - 1)} \left[ \frac{hc}{\lambda k T} \cdot \frac{e^{y}}{e^{y} - 1} - 5 \right]$$

**Step 4:** Setting this to zero requires the bracket to be zero (since the prefactor is non-zero):

$$y \cdot \frac{e^{y}}{e^{y} - 1} = 5$$

where $y = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_{\max} k T}$.

**Step 5:** The transcendental equation $y e^{y} = 5(e^{y} - 1)$ has the numerical solution:

$$y \approx 4.9651$$

**Step 6:** Therefore:

$$\frac{hc}{\lambda_{\max} k T} = 4.9651$$

$$\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = \frac{hc}{4.9651 \, k}$$

**Step 7:** Compute the constant:

$$\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(2.998 \times 10^{8})}{4.9651 \times (1.381 \times 10^{-23})} = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m·K}$$

**Step 8:** Therefore:

$$\boxed{\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m·K}}$$

**Conclusion:** Wien's Displacement Law shows that as temperature increases, the peak emission shifts to shorter wavelengths — explaining why heated objects change color from red to white. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Radiation Quantities

| Quantity | Symbol | Definition | Depends on $\lambda$? | Depends on $T$? | Surface Property? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Total Emissive Power | $E$ | Total radiated energy per unit area per unit time | No (integrated) | Yes (strongly) | Yes |
| Spectral Emissive Power | $E_\lambda$ | Energy per unit area per unit time per unit $\lambda$ | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Blackbody Emissive Power | $E_b$ | Max possible $E$ at temperature $T$ | No (integrated) | Yes ($\sigma T^4$) | No (universal) |
| Spectral Blackbody Emissivity | $E_{b\lambda}$ | Spectral emission from blackbody | Yes | Yes | No (universal) |
| Emissivity | $\varepsilon$ | $E/E_b$ | Can be (if not gray) | Usually weak | Yes |
| Absorptivity | $\alpha$ | Fraction of incident energy absorbed | Can be (if not gray) | Usually weak | Yes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Concept Hierarchy — Radiation Heat Transfer Map

The following Mermaid diagram presents a **Block-Level Functional Architecture** mapping the relationships between spectral emissive power and its parent/child concepts in radiation heat transfer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RHTRAD["RADIATION HEAT TRANSFER"]:::root

    RHTRAD --> EM["EMISSIVE POWER E"]
    RHTRAD --> AB["ABSORPTIVE POWER"]
    RHTRAD --> PROP["SURFACE PROPERTIES"]

    EM --> E_TOT["Total Emissive Power E"]
    EM --> E_SPEC["Spectral Emissive Power E_lambda"]

    E_SPEC --> DEF["Definition Partial E by Partial lambda at constant T"]
    E_SPEC --> INTEGRAL["Integral Form E equals integral of E_lambda d_lambda"]
    E_SPEC --> PLANCK["Planck Spectral Distribution"]

    PLANCK --> WEIN["Wein Displacement Law lambda_max T equals 2.898e-3"]
    PLANCK --> STEFAN["Stefan Boltzmann Law E_b equals sigma T to power 4"]
    PLANCK --> SHAPE["Bell shaped curve peak shifts with T"]

    E_TOT --> BB["Blackbody max emission"]
    E_TOT --> GB["Gray body E equals epsilon sigma T to power 4"]
    E_TOT --> RB["Real body wavelength dependent"]

    AB --> KIRCH["Kirchhoff Law alpha_lambda equals epsilon_lambda"]
    AB --> GRAYK["Gray body alpha equals epsilon"]

    PROP --> EMIS["Emissivity epsilon equals E by E_b"]
    PROP --> ABSR["Absorptivity alpha"]
```
