| **Specular (mirror-like)** | $\alpha$ strongly direction-dependent | Polished aluminum, mirrors |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** A **gray surface** has $\alpha_\lambda = \alpha$ (constant) for *all wavelengths*. This is the most commonly assumed model in KTU numerical problems because it simplifies radiation network calculations dramatically.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Factors Affecting Absorptivity

The absorptivity of a real engineering surface is a function of:

1. **Nature of the material** — Dielectrics (non-metals) generally have higher $\alpha$ than polished metals.
2. **Surface finish / roughness** — A rough, oxidized, or painted surface has higher $\alpha$ than a polished one.
3. **Wavelength ($\lambda$) of incident radiation** — Most surfaces are *selective*; e.g., white paint has $\alpha \approx 0.1$ in the visible band but $\alpha \approx 0.9$ in the infrared.
4. **Temperature of the surface** — Although weak, $\alpha$ does vary with $T_s$.
5. **Angle of incidence** — For most diffuse surfaces, the directional effect is negligible; for specular surfaces, it dominates.

## 2.2 Absorptivity, Reflectivity & Transmissivity — The Energy Balance

For any semi-transparent (or opaque) body receiving incident radiation, conservation of energy demands:

$$\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = absorptivity
- $\rho$ = reflectivity (fraction reflected)
- $\tau$ = transmissivity (fraction transmitted through)

> [!NOTE]
> For an **opaque surface** (most engineering metals and thick coatings), $\tau = 0$, so:
> $$\alpha + \rho = 1$$
> This is a powerful shortcut used repeatedly in KTU Board problems.

For a **blackbody**, $\alpha = 1$, so $\rho = \tau = 0$ — it absorbs everything.

## 2.3 Kirchhoff's Law — The Link Between Absorptivity and Emissivity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kirchhoff's Law** is one of the *highest-weightage* topics in KTU Heat Transfer (Module 2). The law states that at thermal equilibrium, the monochromatic emissivity of a surface equals its monochromatic absorptivity at the same wavelength and temperature:
> $$\varepsilon_\lambda(T) = \alpha_\lambda(T)$$

For a **gray surface** (where $\varepsilon_\lambda$ and $\alpha_\lambda$ are independent of $\lambda$):
$$\varepsilon = \alpha$$

For a **selective (non-gray) surface**:
$$\varepsilon \neq \alpha \quad \text{(in general)}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | SI Units |
|---|---------|-------------|----------|
| 1 | $\alpha = \dfrac{E_{abs}}{E_{inc}}$ | Definition of total absorptivity | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\alpha_\lambda = \dfrac{E_{\lambda,abs}}{E_{\lambda,inc}}$ | Monochromatic absorptivity | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\alpha = \dfrac{\int_0^\infty \alpha_\lambda E_{\lambda,i}\, d\lambda}{\int_0^\infty E_{\lambda,i}\, d\lambda}$ | Total absorptivity from spectral data | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$ | Energy balance (semi-transparent) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\alpha + \rho = 1$ | Energy balance (opaque surface) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$ | Kirchhoff's law (spectral) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\varepsilon = \alpha$ | Kirchhoff's law (gray surface) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $J = \varepsilon E_b + \rho G$ | Radiosity for opaque body | $\text{W/m}^2$ |
| 9 | $\alpha_{sol} \neq \alpha_{therm}$ | Solar vs. thermal absorptivity distinction | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When the source temperature is *not specified* in the problem, students should assume the source is a blackbody at the indicated temperature, and apply Kirchhoff's law only when the *surface* is in equilibrium with the source. For non-equilibrium problems (typical in solar collectors), $\alpha$ and $\varepsilon$ are treated as *independent* properties.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Solar Thermal Collectors** — Use *selective coatings* (e.g., black chrome, cermet) with **high $\alpha_{sol}$** (≈ 0.95 in visible/NIR) and **low $\varepsilon_{therm}$** (≈ 0.05 in IR) to maximize energy capture and minimize radiative losses.
2. **Spacecraft Thermal Control** — Multi-layer insulation (MLI) and specialized paints (like Z-93 white) have $\alpha_{sol} / \varepsilon_{therm} \approx 0.15/0.92$ to *reject* solar heat in orbit.
3. **Building Energy Design** — Cool roofs use high-albedo materials to lower $\alpha_{sol}$, reducing air-conditioning loads.
4. **Furnace Design** — Hearth refractories are designed for high $\alpha$ at combustion-gas wavelengths to maximize heat absorption.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Absorptivity from Spectral Data

**Statement:** The total absorptivity of a surface is the incident-spectrum-weighted average of the monochromatic absorptivity.

**Step 1 — Define monochromatic absorbed energy.**
The energy absorbed per unit area, per unit wavelength interval, at wavelength $\lambda$ is:

$$E_{\lambda,abs} = \alpha_\lambda(\lambda) \cdot E_{\lambda,i}(\lambda)$$

**Step 2 — Integrate over the entire spectrum to obtain the total absorbed energy.**

$$E_{abs} = \int_0^\infty E_{\lambda,abs}\, d\lambda = \int_0^\infty \alpha_\lambda(\lambda)\, E_{\lambda,i}(\lambda)\, d\lambda$$

**Step 3 — The total incident radiation (irradiation) is similarly integrated.**

$$G = E_{inc} = \int_0^\infty E_{\lambda,i}(\lambda)\, d\lambda$$

**Step 4 — Apply the definition of absorptivity as the ratio.**

$$\alpha = \frac{E_{abs}}{E_{inc}} = \frac{\int_0^\infty \alpha_\lambda(\lambda)\, E_{\lambda,i}(\lambda)\, d\lambda}{\int_0^\infty E_{\lambda,i}(\lambda)\, d\lambda}$$

$$\boxed{\alpha = \frac{\displaystyle\int_0^\infty \alpha_\lambda E_{\lambda,i}\, d\lambda}{\displaystyle\int_0^\infty E_{\lambda,i}\, d\lambda}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical Significance:** This expression reveals that $\alpha$ is a **weighted average** of $\alpha_\lambda$. The *weighting function* is the spectral distribution of the incident radiation, $E_{\lambda,i}(\lambda)$. This is why the same surface can have different $\alpha$ values under sunlight, furnace radiation, or laser exposure.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Kirchhoff's Law from Thermodynamic Equilibrium

**Statement:** At thermal equilibrium, $\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$ for every wavelength and direction.

**Step 1 — Consider two large parallel surfaces** in vacuum, both at the same temperature $T$.
Surface 1 (a real body) faces Surface 2 (a blackbody cavity).

**Step 2 — Radiative heat exchange analysis.**
The net heat transfer between them must be zero at equilibrium. From the second law, energy exchange must balance.

**Step 3 — Surface 2 (blackbody) emits** $E_b$ (total) or $E_{b,\lambda}$ (monochromatic), all of which strikes Surface 1.

**Step 4 — Surface 1 absorbs** a fraction $\alpha_\lambda$ of this radiation per unit wavelength. So absorbed power per unit area per unit wavelength:

$$q_{\lambda,abs} = \alpha_\lambda(\lambda, T)\, E_{b,\lambda}(\lambda, T)$$

**Step 5 — In the reverse direction**, Surface 1 emits $E_{\lambda,1}(\lambda, T)$ per unit area, all of which is absorbed by the blackbody (since $\alpha_{bb} = 1$).

**Step 6 — Net heat flux at equilibrium = 0:**

$$E_{\lambda,1}(\lambda, T) - \alpha_\lambda(\lambda, T)\, E_{b,\lambda}(\lambda, T) = 0$$

**Step 7 — Rearrange:**

$$E_{\lambda,1}(\lambda, T) = \alpha_\lambda(\lambda, T)\, E_{b,\lambda}(\lambda, T)$$

**Step 8 — Divide both sides by $E_{b,\lambda}$:**

$$\frac{E_{\lambda,1}(\lambda, T)}{E_{b,\lambda}(\lambda, T)} = \alpha_\lambda(\lambda, T)$$

**Step 9 — By definition, the left-hand side is the monochromatic emissivity $\varepsilon_\lambda$:**

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_\lambda(\lambda, T) = \alpha_\lambda(\lambda, T)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** This derivation shows that Kirchhoff's law holds *only* when the surface and source are in **thermal equilibrium**. In practical problems (e.g., solar collector absorbing sun's radiation at 5800 K while operating at 400 K), the law does **not** strictly apply, and $\alpha$ and $\varepsilon$ are treated as independent.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Opaque Surface Energy Balance

**Step 1 — Conservation of energy for incident radiation.**

$$E_{inc} = E_{abs} + E_{ref} + E_{trans}$$

**Step 2 — Divide throughout by $E_{inc}$:**

$$1 = \frac{E_{abs}}{E_{inc}} + \frac{E_{ref}}{E_{inc}} + \frac{E_{trans}}{E_{inc}}$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the radiative property ratios.**

$$1 = \alpha + \rho + \tau$$

**Step 4 — Apply the opaque condition** $\tau = 0$ (no transmission through the body).

$$1 = \alpha + \rho \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho = 1 - \alpha$$

> [!TIP]
> **Quick KTU Problem-Solving Tip:** Whenever you see a problem mentioning "polished," "oxidized," or "thick" surfaces, immediately set $\tau = 0$, then compute $\rho = 1 - \alpha$. This single equation unlocks most one-line radiation problems.

## 3.4 Solved Analytical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A thick metal plate has a spectral absorptivity distribution given as:
- $\alpha_{\lambda,1} = 0.2$ for $0 \leq \lambda \leq 2 \,\mu\text{m}$ with incident spectral energy $E_{\lambda,i,1} = 1500 \,\text{W/m}^2\cdot\mu\text{m}$
- $\alpha_{\lambda,2} = 0.8$ for $2 \leq \lambda \leq 5 \,\mu\text{m}$ with incident spectral energy $E_{\lambda,i,2} = 500 \,\text{W/m}^2\cdot\mu\text{m}$

Calculate the total absorptivity of the plate.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute absorbed energy in each band.**

$$E_{abs,1} = \alpha_{\lambda,1} \cdot E_{\lambda,i,1} = 0.2 \times 1500 = 300 \,\text{W/m}^2$$

$$E_{abs,2} = \alpha_{\lambda,2} \cdot E_{\lambda,i,2} = 0.8 \times 500 = 400 \,\text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Total absorbed energy.**

$$E_{abs,total} = 300 + 400 = 700 \,\text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Total incident energy.**

$$E_{inc,total} = 1500 + 500 = 2000 \,\text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Total absorptivity.**

$$\alpha = \frac{700}{2000} = 0.35$$

$$\boxed{\alpha = 0.35}$$

**Step 5 — Reflectivity (opaque plate, $\tau = 0$).**

$$\rho = 1 - \alpha = 1 - 0.35 = 0.65$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Energy Partitioning on an Irradiated Surface

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Incident Radiation E_in] --> B[Surface Interaction]
    B --> C[Absorbed Fraction alpha E_in]
    B --> D[Reflected Fraction rho E_in]
    B --> E[Transmitted Fraction tau E_in]
    C --> F[Heating of Body]
    D --> G[Returned to Environment]
    E --> H[Passes Through Body]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Every photon arriving at the surface is partitioned into one of three fates — absorbed, reflected, or transmitted. Their fractional shares are precisely $\alpha$, $\rho$, and $\tau$, summing to unity.

## 4.2 Spectral Behavior: Gray vs. Selective vs. Blackbody

```mermaid
graph TD
    L0[Wavelength axis lambda] --> L1[0.1 micrometer]
    L1 --> L2[1.0 micrometer]
    L2 --> L3[10 micrometer]
    L3 --> L4[100 micrometer]
    style L0 fill:#f9f,stroke:#333
    B[Blackbody curve: alpha = 1 horizontal at top] --> L0
    G[Gray surface: alpha constant horizontal line at mid level] --> L0
    S[Selective surface: alpha step function rises with lambda] --> L0
```

> [!TIP]
> **Concept Map Insight:**
> - A **blackbody** is a flat horizontal line at $\alpha = 1$ (absorbs every wavelength equally and completely).
> - A **gray surface** is a flat horizontal line at some constant $\alpha < 1$.
> - A **selective surface** is a curve that varies with $\lambda$ — the defining feature of materials like solar absorber coatings.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Solar Absorber Coating

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Sun[Sun source at 5800 K] -->|Visible NIR spectrum| A1[Outer Selective Coating]
    A1 -->|High alpha_sol 0.95| A2[Absorbed solar energy]
    A1 -->|Low alpha in IR| A3[Reflected longwave radiation]
    A2 --> Pipe[Heat transfer fluid in tubes]
    A3 --> Out[Back to sky, minimal loss]
    Pipe --> Working[Useful thermal energy output]
    Working --> System[Solar collector efficiency]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Translation:** A selective coating simultaneously has *high absorptivity* in the solar band (where $\lambda$ is short) and *low emissivity* in the thermal band (where $\lambda$ is long). This dual behavior — impossible for a gray surface — is the heart of selective surface design and a frequently asked concept in KTU Module 2.

## 4.4 Kirchhoff's Law Logic Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Thermal Equilibrium State] --> Check{Surface type}
    Check -->|Monochromatic| Eq1[epsilon_lambda = alpha_lambda at all lambda and T]
    Check -->|Gray body| Eq2[epsilon = alpha at all lambda]
    Check -->|Selective non gray| Eq3[epsilon differs from alpha, no simple equality]
    Eq1 --> Apply[Used in radiation network analysis]
    Eq2 --> Apply
    Eq3 --> Apply
    Apply --> Exam[KTU Board Exam Focus]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Recognition:** When a problem mentions "in thermal equilibrium" or "small cavity inside a large body," apply $\varepsilon = \alpha$ (gray) or $\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$ (general). When the source and surface temperatures differ (e.g., solar, furnace, or space), $\alpha$ and $\varepsilon$ are **independent** properties.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define absorptivity. State Kirchhoff's law of radiation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Absorptivity (α):** It is the ratio of the radiation energy absorbed by a surface to the total radiation energy incident upon it. It is a dimensionless quantity lying between 0 and 1.

$$\alpha = \frac{\text{Radiation energy absorbed}}{\text{Radiation energy incident}}$$

**Kirchhoff's Law:** At thermal equilibrium, the monochromatic emissivity of a surface equals its monochromatic absorptivity at the same wavelength and temperature.

$$\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$$

For a gray body, this simplifies to $\varepsilon = \alpha$. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for Kirchhoff's statement, 1 for gray-body form)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between monochromatic absorptivity and total absorptivity. When are they equal?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Monochromatic Absorptivity ($\alpha_\lambda$) | Total Absorptivity ($\alpha$) |
|--------|----------------------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Definition | Fraction absorbed at a *specific* wavelength | Fraction absorbed over the *entire* spectrum |
| Wavelength dependence | A function of $\lambda$ | A wavelength-averaged quantity |
| Formula | $\alpha_\lambda = E_{\lambda,a}/E_{\lambda,i}$ | $\alpha = \int_0^\infty \alpha_\lambda E_{\lambda,i} d\lambda \,/\, \int_0^\infty E_{\lambda,i} d\lambda$ |

They are **equal** only when the surface is **gray**, i.e., $\alpha_\lambda$ is constant for all wavelengths. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### **Q.A. [KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** A thick metal plate has the following spectral absorptivity data:
- For $0 \le \lambda \le 3 \,\mu\text{m}$: $\alpha_{\lambda,1} = 0.30$ and incident spectral energy $E_{\lambda,i,1} = 2000 \,\text{W/m}^2\cdot\mu\text{m}$
- For $3 \le \lambda \le 8 \,\mu\text{m}$: $\alpha_{\lambda,2} = 0.70$ and incident spectral energy $E_{\lambda,i,2} = 800 \,\text{W/m}^2\cdot\mu\text{m}$

Calculate: (i) Total absorptivity of the plate, (ii) Reflectivity of the plate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** State and prove Kirchhoff's law for a gray surface. Explain its physical significance with an example. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Given:**
- Band 1: $\alpha_{\lambda,1} = 0.30$, $E_{\lambda,i,1} = 2000 \,\text{W/m}^2\cdot\mu\text{m}$, $\Delta\lambda_1 = 3 - 0 = 3 \,\mu\text{m}$
- Band 2: $\alpha_{\lambda,2} = 0.70$, $E_{\lambda,i,2} = 800 \,\text{W/m}^2\cdot\mu\text{m}$, $\Delta\lambda_2 = 8 - 3 = 5 \,\mu\text{m}$

**Step 1 — Absorbed energy in band 1.**

$$E_{abs,1} = \alpha_{\lambda,1} \cdot E_{\lambda,i,1} \cdot \Delta\lambda_1 = 0.30 \times 2000 \times 3 = 1800 \,\text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Absorbed energy in band 2.**

$$E_{abs,2} = \alpha_{\lambda,2} \cdot E_{\lambda,i,2} \cdot \Delta\lambda_2 = 0.70 \times 800 \times 5 = 2800 \,\text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Total absorbed energy.**

$$E_{abs,total} = 1800 + 2800 = 4600 \,\text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Total incident energy.**

$$E_{inc,total} = (E_{\lambda,i,1} \times \Delta\lambda_1) + (E_{\lambda,i,2} \times \Delta\lambda_2) = 6000 + 4000 = 10000 \,\text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Total absorptivity.**

$$\alpha = \frac{4600}{10000} = 0.46$$

**Step 6 — Reflectivity** (opaque surface, $\tau = 0$):

$$\rho = 1 - \alpha = 1 - 0.46 = 0.54$$

**Final Answer:** $\alpha = 0.46$, $\rho = 0.54$ *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Statement of Kirchhoff's Law:**
At thermal equilibrium, the emissivity of a surface equals its absorptivity at the same temperature. For a gray surface:

$$\varepsilon = \alpha$$

**Proof:**
Consider two large parallel infinite surfaces in vacuum. Let:
- Surface A = a real (gray) body at temperature $T$
- Surface B = a blackbody at the same temperature $T$

The system is in thermal equilibrium, so net heat exchange = 0.

Energy emitted by Surface B and absorbed by Surface A:

$$q_{B \to A,abs} = \alpha \cdot \sigma T^4 \quad \text{(per unit area, since B is a blackbody)}$$

Energy emitted by Surface A and absorbed by Surface B (B absorbs everything):

$$q_{A \to B,abs} = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma T^4$$

For thermal equilibrium, both must be equal:

$$\varepsilon \cdot \sigma T^4 = \alpha \cdot \sigma T^4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\varepsilon = \alpha}$$

**Physical Significance:**
Kirchhoff's law tells us that a good emitter is also a good absorber. For example, a blackened copper plate ($\alpha = 0.95$) emits almost as much as a blackbody ($\varepsilon = 0.95$), while a polished silver surface ($\alpha = 0.02$) emits very little ($\varepsilon = 0.02$). This principle guides the design of selective surfaces in solar collectors. *(7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks)

### **Q.B. [KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Define the following terms with their mathematical expressions: (i) Absorptivity, (ii) Reflectivity, (iii) Transmissivity. Derive the energy balance equation for an opaque surface. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A solar collector uses a selective absorber coating with $\alpha_{sol} = 0.92$ and $\varepsilon_{therm} = 0.12$. If solar irradiation is $800 \,\text{W/m}^2$ and the surface temperature is $60\,^\circ\text{C}$ ($333\,\text{K}$), calculate: (i) Solar energy absorbed per $\text{m}^2$, (ii) Radiative heat loss per $\text{m}^2$, (iii) Net useful heat gain per $\text{m}^2$. Take $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \,\text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}^4$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**(i) Absorptivity (α):** Ratio of radiation absorbed to radiation incident.

$$\alpha = \frac{E_{abs}}{E_{inc}}, \quad 0 \le \alpha \le 1$$

**(ii) Reflectivity (ρ):** Ratio of radiation reflected to radiation incident.

$$\rho = \frac{E_{ref}}{E_{inc}}, \quad 0 \le \rho \le 1$$

**(iii) Transmissivity (τ):** Ratio of radiation transmitted through the body to radiation incident.

$$\tau = \frac{E_{trans}}{E_{inc}}, \quad 0 \le \tau \le 1$$

**Energy Balance Derivation for Opaque Surface:**

By conservation of energy:

$$E_{inc} = E_{abs} + E_{ref} + E_{trans}$$

Dividing by $E_{inc}$:

$$1 = \alpha + \rho + \tau$$

For an **opaque surface**, transmissivity $\tau = 0$ (no radiation passes through). Therefore:

$$1 = \alpha + \rho \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\rho = 1 - \alpha}$$ *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- $\alpha_{sol} = 0.92$
- $\varepsilon_{therm} = 0.12$
- $G_{solar} = 800 \,\text{W/m}^2$
- $T_s = 333 \,\text{K}$
- $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \,\text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}^4$

**(i) Solar energy absorbed per $\text{m}^2$:**

$$q_{abs} = \alpha_{sol} \cdot G_{solar} = 0.92 \times 800 = 736 \,\text{W/m}^2$$

**(ii) Radiative heat loss per $\text{m}^2$:**

$$q_{loss} = \varepsilon_{therm} \cdot \sigma T_s^4$$

$$q_{loss} = 0.12 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (333)^4$$

$$(333)^4 = 333 \times 333 \times 333 \times 333 = 1.229 \times 10^{10}$$

$$q_{loss} = 0.12 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.229 \times 10^{10}$$

$$q_{loss} = 0.12 \times 696.78 = 83.61 \,\text{W/m}^2$$

**(iii) Net useful heat gain:**

$$q_{net} = q_{abs} - q_{loss} = 736 - 83.61 = 652.39 \,\text{W/m}^2$$

**Final Answers:**
- $q_{abs} = 736 \,\text{W/m}^2$
- $q_{loss} \approx 83.6 \,\text{W/m}^2$
- $q_{net} \approx 652.4 \,\text{W/m}^2$ *(7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing solar and thermal absorptivity:** Students often use $\alpha$ in the Stefan-Boltzmann formula, leading to massive errors. Always use $\alpha_{sol}$ for incident solar flux and $\varepsilon_{therm}$ for emitted thermal radiation.
> 2. **Forgetting temperature conversion:** $T$ in Stefan-Boltzmann must be in **Kelvin**. Using Celsius gives a negative value, which is a guaranteed zero.
> 3. **Mixing up $\alpha$ and $\varepsilon$ in Kirchhoff's law:** Kirchhoff's law holds at *thermal equilibrium*; it does NOT apply when source and surface temperatures differ.
> 4. **Sign errors in heat-loss calculations:** Radiative loss is subtracted from absorbed gain, not added.
> 5. **Omitting units in the final answer:** KTU examiners often deduct **0.5 mark** for missing units.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Absorptivity (Module 2)**

- **Definition:** $\alpha = E_{abs}/E_{inc}$, dimensionless, $0 \le \alpha \le 1$.
- **Monochromatic absorptivity** ($\alpha_\lambda$) varies with wavelength; **total absorptivity** ($\alpha$) is the spectral average.
- **Energy balance:** $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$. For opaque bodies, $\tau = 0$, so $\rho = 1 - \alpha$.
- **Kirchhoff's law:** $\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$ (general); $\varepsilon = \alpha$ (gray surface). Valid *only* at thermal equilibrium.
- **Blackbody:** $\alpha = 1$, $\rho = 0$, $\tau = 0$. **Gray body:** $\alpha$ constant for all $\lambda$. **Selective surface:** $\alpha$ strongly wavelength-dependent.
- **Solar vs. thermal absorptivity:** $\alpha_{sol}$ (for sun ≈ 5800 K) and $\varepsilon_{therm}$ (for surface emission) are independent properties for selective surfaces.
- **Total absorptivity formula:** Weighted by the *incident spectrum*, not the surface's own emission.
- **Most-tested KTU problem type:** Two-band numerical with $\alpha$ calculation + reflectivity computation using $\rho = 1 - \alpha$.
- **High-weightage concept:** Selective coatings in solar collectors — high $\alpha_{sol}$, low $\varepsilon_{therm}$.
- **Common errors to avoid:** Mixing $\alpha$ with $\varepsilon$, ignoring units, missing $T$ in Kelvin, applying Kirchhoff outside equilibrium.
- **Energy units:** Always report in $\text{W/m}^2$ for fluxes, dimensionless for properties.
- **Engineering applications:** Solar collectors, spacecraft thermal control, building cool roofs, furnace refractories.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reflectivity and Transmissivity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Reflectivity and Transmissivity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the discipline of **Radiation Heat Transfer**, when a beam of thermal radiation strikes a medium or a surface, its energy is partitioned into three fundamental physical outcomes. Each outcome is quantified by a dimensionless fraction (ranging from **0 to 1**) known as a **Radiative Property** of the material.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCCHT402 - Module 2: Radiation Heat Transfer)**
> The three primary radiative properties of a material surface or medium are:
> 1. **Absorptivity (α)** — Fraction of incident radiation energy absorbed by the body.
> 2. **Reflectivity (ρ)** — Fraction of incident radiation energy reflected by the body.
> 3. **Transmissivity (τ)** — Fraction of incident radiation energy transmitted through the body.

**Reflectivity (ρ)** is therefore the ratio of the radiant energy reflected by a body to the total radiant energy incident upon it. It quantifies the *mirror-like* or *diffuse* bouncing-back behavior of a surface.

**Transmissivity (τ)** is the ratio of the radiant energy transmitted through a body to the total radiant energy incident upon it. It quantifies how *transparent* a medium is to thermal radiation (e.g., glass, quartz, atmospheric air).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Sunlight Striking a Window Pane**
> 
> Imagine you are standing inside a closed room and the Sun's rays hit a window. Three things can happen to the sunlight:
> - **Part of it is absorbed (α):** The glass heats up slightly because it soaks up some energy.
> - **Part of it is reflected (ρ):** You see a bright glare bouncing off the glass back to the outside.
> - **Part of it is transmitted (τ):** The sunlight passes *through* the glass and lights up the room.
> 
> Sum of all three fractions = **100% of the incident light (Energy Conservation)**.
> 
> - A **mirror** has very high **ρ ≈ 0.95** (almost all reflected).
> - A **brick wall** has high **α ≈ 0.90** (most absorbed, no transmission).
> - A **clear glass window** has high **τ ≈ 0.85** (most passes through).

## 1.3 Special Terminology for KTU Examinations

| Term | Definition | Example Material |
|---|---|---|
| **Opaque Body** | A body that does **not** allow radiation to pass through it. | **τ = 0** (Brick, Metal, Wood) |
| **Transparent Body** | A body that allows most radiation to pass through it. | **τ → 1** (Dry Air, Quartz) |
| **Black Body** | A body that absorbs **all** incident radiation. | **α = 1, ρ = 0, τ = 0** |
| **White Body / Diffuse Reflector** | A body that reflects all incident radiation. | **ρ = 1, α = 0, τ = 0** |
| **Diathermanous Body** | Body that transmits all incident radiation. | **τ = 1, α = 0, ρ = 0** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** Reflectivity and Transmissivity are *surface-level* properties when dealing with opaque bodies, but they are *bulk volumetric* properties when dealing with semi-transparent media (e.g., glass, water, exhaust gases). This distinction frequently appears as a Part A question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Law of Conservation of Energy for Radiation

The most fundamental governing equation for these three properties arises from the **First Law of Thermodynamics applied to a radiation control volume**. Since the incident radiation must either be absorbed, reflected, or transmitted, and no other fate is possible (assuming no internal energy storage in a steady-state sense), the following holds:

$$\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = Absorptivity (dimensionless, $0 \le \alpha \le 1$)
- $\rho$ = Reflectivity (dimensionless, $0 \le \rho \le 1$)
- $\tau$ = Transmissivity (dimensionless, $0 \le \tau \le 1$)

## 2.2 Spectral (Monochromatic) vs. Total Properties

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Concept:** All three radiative properties ($\alpha, \rho, \tau$) are functions of **two variables**:
> 1. **Wavelength ($\lambda$)** of the incident radiation.
> 2. **Direction (angle of incidence, $\theta$)** at which the radiation strikes the surface.

### 2.2.1 Monochromatic (Spectral) Properties
When the property is evaluated at a *single wavelength* $\lambda$, it is called the **monochromatic property** and is denoted with a subscript $\lambda$:

$$\alpha_\lambda = \frac{G_{\lambda, \text{abs}}}{G_{\lambda, \text{inc}}}, \quad \rho_\lambda = \frac{G_{\lambda, \text{ref}}}{G_{\lambda, \text{inc}}}, \quad \tau_\lambda = \frac{G_{\lambda, \text{tra}}}{G_{\lambda, \text{inc}}}$$

Conservation at any single wavelength:
$$\alpha_\lambda + \rho_\lambda + \tau_\lambda = 1$$

### 2.2.2 Total (Hemispherical, Integrated) Properties
The **total property** is the wavelength-averaged value, obtained by integrating the spectral property over the entire wavelength spectrum of interest, weighted by the spectral intensity of the incident radiation $G_\lambda$:

$$\alpha = \frac{\displaystyle\int_0^\infty \alpha_\lambda \, G_\lambda \, d\lambda}{\displaystyle\int_0^\infty G_\lambda \, d\lambda}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often write $\alpha = \int_0^\infty \alpha_\lambda \, d\lambda$, which is mathematically wrong. The correct averaging requires weighting by the **incident spectral irradiation $G_\lambda$**, not a simple integration.

## 2.3 Directional Dependence

The properties are also functions of the **angle of incidence** $\theta$:
- **Hemispherical Property:** Averaged over all incident directions on a complete hemisphere.
- **Directional Property:** Evaluated at one specific angle $\theta$.
- For **diffuse surfaces** (assumption in most KTU problems), the surface is *direction-independent* with respect to *incoming* radiation.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **high-yield formulas** for KTU Part B (14-mark) numerical and theoretical problems.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Energy Conservation | $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$ | All dimensionless ($0 \le$ each $\le 1$) |
| 2 | Spectral Conservation | $\alpha_\lambda + \rho_\lambda + \tau_\lambda = 1$ | Valid at any $\lambda$ |
| 3 | Opaque Surface | $\tau = 0 \Rightarrow \alpha + \rho = 1$ | Valid for solids (metals, wood) |
| 4 | Reflectivity from Absorptivity (Opaque) | $\rho = 1 - \alpha$ | Used in solar collector problems |
| 5 | Transmissivity from Absorptivity (Reflective) | $\tau = 1 - \alpha$ | Valid for glass at specific $\lambda$ |
| 6 | Total Absorptivity (weighted avg) | $\alpha = \frac{\int \alpha_\lambda G_\lambda d\lambda}{\int G_\lambda d\lambda}$ | $G_\lambda$ is spectral irradiation in $W/m^2 \cdot \mu m$ |
| 7 | Beam Transmission (Beer's Law) | $\tau_\lambda = e^{-\kappa_\lambda L}$ | $\kappa_\lambda$ = absorption coefficient ($m^{-1}$), $L$ = path length ($m$) |
| 8 | Kirchhoff's Law (Thermal Equilibrium) | $\alpha_\lambda = \varepsilon_\lambda$ | $\varepsilon_\lambda$ is spectral emissivity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula 7 (Beer's Law)** is the most commonly used expression for transmissivity in *gaseous media* (combustion gases, furnace atmospheres) in KTU numerical problems. We will derive it in Section 3.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The classification of materials based on $\alpha$, $\rho$, and $\tau$ is the foundation for designing:

1. **Solar Thermal Collectors** — Need high $\alpha$ in solar spectrum ($\lambda = 0.3\text{–}2.5 \, \mu m$), low $\alpha$ in IR (selective surfaces).
2. **Greenhouse Design** — Need glass with high $\tau$ for visible light, high $\rho$ for UV/IR.
3. **Furnace Linings (Refractories)** — Need high $\rho$ to retain heat, low $\tau$ (typically opaque).
4. **Green Building Windows** — Low-E coatings adjust $\rho$ and $\tau$ for energy efficiency.
5. **Optical Fibers / IR Thermography** — Engineering specific $\tau_\lambda$ profiles for filtering.
6. **Reactor Vessel Jackets** — Radiation shields use alternating high-$\rho$ layers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Conservation Equation $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$

**Statement:** *The sum of the three radiative properties of any material body is exactly unity, at any wavelength and at any direction of incidence.*

**Step 1 — Energy Balance Setup:**
Consider a control volume of a small, thin slab of material of area $A$ and infinitesimal thickness $dL$. A monochromatic radiation beam of spectral irradiation $G_\lambda$ is incident on the surface. Energy is conserved within the CV (no accumulation, no generation of radiation energy).

**Step 2 — Energy In:**
Spectral incident energy per unit area per unit wavelength:
$$G_{\lambda, \text{inc}}$$

**Step 3 — Energy Out (Three Pathways):**
The total energy leaving the incident side or absorbed within the body is divided into:

- Absorbed fraction: $\alpha_\lambda \cdot G_{\lambda, \text{inc}}$
- Reflected fraction: $\rho_\lambda \cdot G_{\lambda, \text{inc}}$
- Transmitted fraction: $\tau_\lambda \cdot G_{\lambda, \text{inc}}$

**Step 4 — Apply Conservation:**
By the First Law of Thermodynamics (steady state, no internal source):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Energy In} &= \text{Energy Out} \\
G_{\lambda, \text{inc}} &= \alpha_\lambda G_{\lambda, \text{inc}} + \rho_\lambda G_{\lambda, \text{inc}} + \tau_\lambda G_{\lambda, \text{inc}} \\
G_{\lambda, \text{inc}} &= G_{\lambda, \text{inc}} \left( \alpha_\lambda + \rho_\lambda + \tau_\lambda \right) \\
\therefore \quad \alpha_\lambda + \rho_\lambda + \tau_\lambda &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Generalize to Total Properties:**
Integrating the spectral conservation over all wavelengths (with weighting), the same relationship holds for total (hemispherical) properties:

$$\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$$

This is the **most important equation** in radiation properties and is universally valid for any material in any state.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Beer's Law for Transmissivity Through a Gaseous Medium

**Statement:** *For a homogeneous, non-scattering absorbing-emitting medium, the monochromatic transmissivity decreases exponentially with the path length $L$ and the spectral absorption coefficient $\kappa_\lambda$.*

**Step 1 — Differential Energy Balance on a Slab:**
Consider a slab of gas of thickness $dL$. The intensity of radiation $I_\lambda$ decreases as it passes through due to absorption.

$$dI_\lambda = -I_\lambda \, \kappa_\lambda \, dL$$

The negative sign indicates a loss; $\kappa_\lambda$ is the **spectral absorption coefficient** (units: $m^{-1}$).

**Step 2 — Separate Variables:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dI_\lambda}{I_\lambda} &= -\kappa_\lambda \, dL
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate Across the Slab of Thickness L:**
Limits: at $L=0$, intensity is incident $I_{\lambda,0}$; at $L=L$, intensity is $I_{\lambda,L}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{I_{\lambda,0}}^{I_{\lambda,L}} \frac{dI_\lambda}{I_\lambda} &= -\int_0^L \kappa_\lambda \, dL \\
\ln \left( \frac{I_{\lambda,L}}{I_{\lambda,0}} \right) &= -\kappa_\lambda L \\
\frac{I_{\lambda,L}}{I_{\lambda,0}} &= e^{-\kappa_\lambda L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Identify Transmissivity:**
By definition, the monochromatic transmissivity is the ratio of transmitted to incident intensity:

$$\boxed{\tau_\lambda = e^{-\kappa_\lambda L}}$$

**Step 5 — Use Conservation to Get Absorptivity and Reflectivity:**
For gases, we typically assume $\rho_\lambda = 0$ (no reflection from gaseous interfaces at moderate pressure):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_\lambda &= 1 - \tau_\lambda = 1 - e^{-\kappa_\lambda L} \\
\rho_\lambda &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Total Transmissivity from Spectral Values

**Step 1 — Spectral Irradiation Definition:**
The total incident irradiation is the integral of spectral irradiation:
$$G = \int_0^\infty G_\lambda \, d\lambda$$

**Step 2 — Transmitted Spectral Energy:**
The transmitted spectral energy is $\tau_\lambda G_\lambda d\lambda$.

**Step 3 — Total Transmitted Energy:**
$$G_{\text{tra}} = \int_0^\infty \tau_\lambda G_\lambda \, d\lambda$$

**Step 4 — Total Transmissivity (Definition):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau &= \frac{G_{\text{tra}}}{G_{\text{inc}}} = \frac{\displaystyle\int_0^\infty \tau_\lambda G_\lambda \, d\lambda}{\displaystyle\int_0^\infty G_\lambda \, d\lambda}
\end{aligned}
$$

This confirms the *weighted-average* nature of total properties and explains why the KTU warns against using simple unweighted integration.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Radiation Interaction Block Diagram

This block diagram illustrates the energy partitioning logic of a thermal radiation beam incident on a generic material body.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incident Radiation G_lambda] --> B{Material Body CV}
    B --> P1[Absorbed Fraction alpha_lambda times G_lambda]
    B --> P2[Reflected Fraction rho_lambda times G_lambda]
    B --> P3[Transmitted Fraction tau_lambda times G_lambda]
    P1 --> C1[Internal Energy / Heating of Body]
    P2 --> C2[Back to Surroundings]
    P3 --> C3[Passes Through Body]
    B -.Check.-> D{alpha_lambda + rho_lambda + tau_lambda = 1}
    D --> E[Conservation Satisfied]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Material Classification Decision Tree

A KTU-style classification tree for materials based on their dominant radiative property.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Material Sample] --> Q1{Is tau > 0?}
    Q1 -- No --> OP[Opaque Body: tau = 0]
    OP --> Q2{Is alpha near 1?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> BB[Black Body: alpha = 1, rho = 0, tau = 0]
    Q2 -- No --> GR[Grey / Real Body: alpha + rho = 1]
    Q1 -- Yes --> TR[Transparent / Semi-Transparent Body]
    TR --> Q3{Is alpha near 1?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> ABS[Strong Absorber: e.g. soot cloud]
    Q3 -- No --> CL[Clear Medium: e.g. dry air, quartz]
    CL --> Q4{Apply Beer Law?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> BL[tau_lambda = exp of minus kappa_lambda L]
    Q4 -- No --> ID[Ideal Transparent: tau = 1]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Solar Collector Design

Maps the practical engineering decisions driven by the choice of $\alpha$, $\rho$, and $\tau$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SUN[Sunlight Spectrum G_lambda] --> GLASS[Glass Cover]
    GLASS -- tau_solar high --> ABS[Absorber Plate]
    GLASS -- rho_solar low --> SUN
    ABS -- alpha_solar high --> HEAT[Thermal Energy]
    ABS -- alpha_IR low --> RAD[Re-radiation Loss]
    RAD --> GLASS
    GLASS -- rho_IR high --> ABS
    ABS --> FLUID[Heat Transfer Fluid]
    FLUID --> OUT[Hot Fluid Output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The glass cover is designed to have *high* transmissivity $\tau$ in the solar spectrum (so sunlight reaches the plate) but *high* reflectivity $\rho$ in the infrared spectrum (so heat radiation from the hot plate is reflected back down). The absorber plate has *high* absorptivity $\alpha$ in the solar spectrum but *low* absorptivity (i.e., low emissivity) in the IR. This is the principle of *selective surfaces*.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Absorptivity, Reflectivity, and Transmissivity. State the law of conservation as applied to these three properties. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Absorptivity ($\alpha$):** The fraction of incident thermal radiation energy that is absorbed by a body. Mathematically, $\alpha = \frac{G_{\text{abs}}}{G_{\text{inc}}}$.

**Reflectivity ($\rho$):** The fraction of incident thermal radiation energy that is reflected by a body. Mathematically, $\rho = \frac{G_{\text{ref}}}{G_{\text{inc}}}$.

**Transmissivity ($\tau$):** The fraction of incident thermal radiation energy that is transmitted through a body. Mathematically, $\tau = \frac{G_{\text{tra}}}{G_{\text{inc}}}$.

**Law of Conservation:**
$$\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$$

This is derived from the First Law of Thermodynamics. For an opaque body, $\tau = 0$, so $\alpha + \rho = 1$.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between monochromatic and total radiative properties with one example. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Q]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Monochromatic (Spectral) Property | Total (Hemispherical) Property |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Property evaluated at a *single* wavelength $\lambda$ | Property averaged over the *entire* spectrum of incident radiation |
| **Notation** | $\alpha_\lambda, \rho_\lambda, \tau_\lambda$ | $\alpha, \rho, \tau$ |
| **Dependency** | Function of $\lambda$ only | Function of the *spectral distribution* of the incident source |
| **Mathematical Form** | Ratio of spectral energies at one $\lambda$ | Weighted average using $G_\lambda$ |
| **Example** | Solar glass has $\tau_\lambda = 0.92$ at $\lambda = 0.55 \, \mu m$ | Same glass has total $\tau = 0.85$ over the entire solar spectrum |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Module 2, Model Q]`

**(a)** Derive the relationship $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$ for radiation properties of a body. Explain clearly what happens to this relation for (i) an opaque body, (ii) a black body, and (iii) a transparent body. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A polished aluminum sheet has a solar absorptivity of $0.15$ and a thermal emissivity of $0.05$. Calculate the reflectivity in the solar range and the reflectivity in the thermal IR range. Comment on the suitability of this material as a solar absorber. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to A(a):

**Step 1 — Energy Conservation (3 Marks):**
Apply the First Law to an infinitesimally thin layer of material. The incident spectral irradiation $G_{\lambda,\text{inc}}$ is partitioned into absorbed, reflected, and transmitted components. Following the derivation in Section 3.1:

$$G_{\lambda,\text{inc}} = \alpha_\lambda G_{\lambda,\text{inc}} + \rho_\lambda G_{\lambda,\text{inc}} + \tau_\lambda G_{\lambda,\text{inc}}$$

$$\therefore \alpha_\lambda + \rho_\lambda + \tau_\lambda = 1$$

Integrating over all wavelengths yields the same relation for total properties:
$$\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$$

**Step 2 — Special Cases (4 Marks):**

**(i) Opaque Body** — No transmission occurs:
$$\tau = 0 \implies \alpha + \rho = 1$$

**(ii) Black Body** — Absorbs all incident radiation:
$$\alpha = 1 \implies \rho = 0, \quad \tau = 0$$

**(iii) Transparent (Diathermanous) Body** — Transmits all radiation:
$$\tau = 1 \implies \alpha = 0, \quad \rho = 0$$

---

#### Model Solution to A(b):

**Given:**
- Solar absorptivity, $\alpha_s = 0.15$
- Thermal (IR) emissivity, $\varepsilon = 0.05$

**Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Law (2 Marks):**
At thermal equilibrium, $\alpha = \varepsilon$ at each wavelength. So the IR absorptivity is $\alpha_{\text{IR}} = 0.05$.

**Step 2 — Solar Range Reflectivity (2 Marks):**
Polished aluminum is opaque in the solar range, so $\tau = 0$ and $\alpha_s + \rho_s = 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_s &= 1 - \alpha_s = 1 - 0.15 = 0.85
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — IR Range Reflectivity (2 Marks):**
Similarly, for the opaque aluminum in the IR range:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{\text{IR}} &= 1 - \alpha_{\text{IR}} = 1 - 0.05 = 0.95
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Comment (1 Mark):**

> [!NOTE]
> Polished aluminum is a **poor solar absorber** because $\alpha_s = 0.15$ (only 15% of solar energy is captured). The high reflectivity $\rho_s = 0.85$ means 85% of the incident solar radiation is reflected away. It is, however, an excellent *thermal reflector* and is therefore used as a *back-layer reflector* in solar collectors, not as the primary absorber plate.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Module 2, Model Q]`

**(a)** Explain the terms (i) Spectral (monochromatic) and total (hemispherical) absorptivity, reflectivity, and transmissivity. State the conditions under which a property is called a "total" property. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A furnace has an atmosphere of thickness $1.5 \, m$ containing combustion gases. The spectral absorption coefficient is $\kappa_\lambda = 0.6 \, m^{-1}$ at the dominant wavelength of radiation. Assuming a non-scattering, non-reflecting medium, calculate: (i) the spectral transmissivity, (ii) the spectral absorptivity, and (iii) the total emissivity of the gas at this wavelength. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B(a):

**Step 1 — Definitions (4 Marks):**

- **Spectral/Monochromatic Property:** The radiative property of a material evaluated at a *specific* wavelength $\lambda$ of incident radiation. Example: $\alpha_\lambda$ at $\lambda = 2 \, \mu m$.
- **Total/Hemispherical Property:** The radiative property integrated and weighted-averaged over the *entire* wavelength spectrum of the incident source and over all directions of the hemisphere. Example: total absorptivity $\alpha$ integrated from $\lambda = 0$ to $\infty$.

**Step 2 — Conditions for "Total" Property (3 Marks):**

A property is classified as a "total" property when:
1. It is **averaged over all wavelengths** of the incident radiation, weighted by the spectral distribution $G_\lambda$ of the source.
2. It is **averaged over all directions** of incidence (hemispherical integration).
3. The property is independent of the angle of incidence (diffuse surface assumption).

**Mathematical Form:**
$$\alpha = \frac{\int_0^\infty \alpha_\lambda G_\lambda \, d\lambda}{\int_0^\infty G_\lambda \, d\lambda}$$

---

#### Model Solution to B(b):

**Given:**
- Path length, $L = 1.5 \, m$
- Spectral absorption coefficient, $\kappa_\lambda = 0.6 \, m^{-1}$
- Non-scattering, non-reflecting: $\rho_\lambda = 0$

**Step 1 — Spectral Transmissivity via Beer's Law (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_\lambda &= e^{-\kappa_\lambda L} \\
\tau_\lambda &= e^{-(0.6)(1.5)} \\
\tau_\lambda &= e^{-0.9} \\
\tau_\lambda &= 0.4066
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Spectral Absorptivity (2 Marks):**

Since $\rho_\lambda = 0$ (no reflection), conservation gives:
$$\alpha_\lambda = 1 - \tau_\lambda = 1 - 0.4066 = 0.5934$$

**Step 3 — Total Emissivity (2 Marks):**

By **Kirchhoff's Law**, the spectral emissivity equals the spectral absorptivity at thermal equilibrium:
$$\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda = 0.5934$$

At a *single dominant wavelength*, this is also the total emissivity:
$$\varepsilon = 0.5934 \approx 0.593$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Beer's Law:** Students frequently write $\tau = e^{+\kappa L}$, which is physically impossible. The exponent must be **negative**.
> 2. **Kirchhoff Misapplication:** Kirchhoff's law ($\alpha_\lambda = \varepsilon_\lambda$) is valid at **thermodynamic equilibrium** and at a *single wavelength*. Do not equate total $\alpha$ with total $\varepsilon$ unless the surface is a **grey body**.
> 3. **Unit Errors:** Always check that $\kappa$ has units of $m^{-1}$ and $L$ in meters; otherwise, the exponent $\kappa L$ is dimensionless. Mixing cm and m is a common source of lost marks.
> 4. **Reflectivity Mistake in Gases:** For *gaseous* media, $\rho_\lambda = 0$ is the standard assumption. Do not apply $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$ and compute $\rho$ as a non-zero value for gas layers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist:**

- **Core Equation (MUST memorize):** $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$. Valid for any material, at any wavelength.
- **Opaque Body shortcut:** If $\tau = 0$, then $\rho = 1 - \alpha$. This is the **most-tested** simplified form.
- **Spectral vs. Total:** Spectral = single wavelength; Total = weighted average over all wavelengths using $G_\lambda$.
- **Beer's Law for gases:** $\tau_\lambda = e^{-\kappa_\lambda L}$ — always negative exponent; $\kappa$ in $m^{-1}$; $L$ in $m$.
- **Kirchhoff's Law:** $\alpha_\lambda = \varepsilon_\lambda$ at thermal equilibrium. Connects absorptivity to emissivity.
- **Material Categories to Remember:**
  - Black body: $\alpha = 1, \rho = 0, \tau = 0$
  - White body: $\rho = 1, \alpha = 0, \tau = 0$
  - Diathermanous: $\tau = 1, \alpha = 0, \rho = 0$
  - Opaque solid: $\tau = 0$, so $\alpha + \rho = 1$
- **Surface vs. Medium:** $\rho$ and $\tau$ are *surface* properties for opaque solids but *bulk (volumetric)* properties for semi-transparent media (gases, glass, water).
- **Engineering Application:** Solar collectors use *selective surfaces* — high $\alpha$ in solar spectrum, low $\varepsilon$ in IR range.
- **High-Yield Numerical Setup:** Always check if the problem is *wavelength-specific* (use $\alpha_\lambda, \rho_\lambda, \tau_\lambda$) or *broadband* (use total $\alpha, \rho, \tau$).
- **Common Error:** Writing $\alpha = \int \alpha_\lambda d\lambda$ instead of the *weighted* form using $G_\lambda$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Black and Gray Bodies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Concept of Black and Gray Bodies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **Radiation Heat Transfer**, real surfaces are classified based on how they emit and absorb thermal radiation across the electromagnetic spectrum. The two foundational idealizations governing Module 2 are:

#### **Black Body (Ideal Radiator)**
A **Black Body** is defined as a perfect absorber and a perfect emitter of thermal radiation at all wavelengths and at all temperatures. By definition, a black body absorbs **100%** of the incident radiation falling upon it and emits the **maximum possible radiation energy** per unit area at any given thermodynamic temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Property of a Black Body:**
> - Monochromatic Emissivity: $\varepsilon_\lambda = 1$ (for all $\lambda$ and all $T$)
> - Monochromatic Absorptivity: $\alpha_\lambda = 1$ (for all $\lambda$ and all $T$)
> - Total Emissivity: $\varepsilon = 1$
> - Total Absorptivity: $\alpha = 1$

#### **Gray Body**
A **Gray Body** is defined as a surface whose monochromatic emissivity is constant and independent of wavelength over the entire spectrum of interest, but is **less than unity**. In simple terms, a gray body emits and absorbs radiation uniformly across all wavelengths, but at a fraction of the black body intensity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Property of a Gray Body:**
> - $\varepsilon_\lambda = \varepsilon = \text{constant} < 1$
> - $\alpha_\lambda = \alpha = \text{constant} < 1$
> - A gray body is a **selective but uniform** radiator.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine a **pitch-dark room** where every wall is painted with a special coating:

- A **Black Body** is like a wall painted with the deepest **Vantablack** paint. It absorbs every single photon of light (or heat radiation) that strikes it, regardless of color (wavelength). Conversely, when heated, it glows with the **brightest possible intensity** for that temperature. **Nature's ultimate light sponge and brightest glow stick.**

- A **Gray Body** is like a wall painted with a uniform matte **medium-gray paint**. It absorbs a *fixed fraction* (say, 60%) of all incoming light, no matter what color the light is. When heated, it glows, but it is always **60% as bright** as a black body at the same temperature — and crucially, it is equally dim across every color of the spectrum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Insight:** Most engineering materials (polished metals, oxidized steel, refractory bricks) are *selective* — they emit and absorb differently at different wavelengths. The **Gray Body assumption** is the most widely used simplification in industrial furnace and boiler design because it allows engineers to replace complex spectral integrals with a single constant emissivity value.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants (KTU High-Yield)

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|-------|------|
| Stefan-Boltzmann Constant | $\sigma$ | $\mathbf{5.67 \times 10^{-8}}$ | $\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}^4$ |
| Absolute Zero | $0\,\text{K}$ | $-273.15$ | $^\circ\text{C}$ |
| Wien's Displacement Constant | $b$ | $2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ | $\text{m·K}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Why and How

**Step 1: Monochromatic Quantities**
Every radiative property is fundamentally a *function of wavelength* ($\lambda$). For any real surface, the monochromatic emissivity is defined as the ratio of the radiation emitted by the real surface to the radiation emitted by a black body at the *same wavelength and same temperature*:

$$\varepsilon_\lambda = \frac{E_\lambda}{E_{b,\lambda}}$$

**Step 2: Kirchhoff's Law of Thermal Radiation**
This is the cornerstone theorem that links *emission* to *absorption* at thermal equilibrium. It states that **the emissivity of a body equals its absorptivity at the same wavelength and temperature**:

$$\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$$

For a black body: $\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda = 1$.

For a gray body: $\varepsilon = \alpha < 1$ (constant over all wavelengths).

**Step 3: The Concept of a Gray Surface — Why it Matters**
- **Real surfaces** are *selective* — emissivity changes with wavelength.
- This selectivity makes heat-transfer integrals **mathematically intractable** (you must integrate over the entire Planck spectrum).
- The **Gray Body assumption** $\varepsilon_\lambda = \text{constant}$ pulls the emissivity *outside* the integral, dramatically simplifying Stefan-Boltzmann style calculations.
- In KTU problems, unless specified otherwise, all surfaces are treated as **gray bodies**.

**Step 4: Total Emissivity and Total Emittance**
The total emissivity is the integrated (bolometric) emissivity across the entire spectrum:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\int_0^\infty \varepsilon_\lambda E_{b,\lambda}\, d\lambda}{\int_0^\infty E_{b,\lambda}\, d\lambda}$$

For a gray body: $\varepsilon_\lambda$ is constant, so it factors out: $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_\lambda$.

**Step 5: Stefan-Boltzmann Law for Real Surfaces**

- **Black Body:** $E_b = \sigma T^4$
- **Gray Body:** $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$

The factor $\varepsilon$ acts as a **correction multiplier** that scales the black body result down to the real surface behavior.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---------|-------------|------|
| 1 | $E_b = \sigma T^4$ | Stefan-Boltzmann law for **black body** | $\text{W/m}^2$ |
| 2 | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | Emissive power of **gray body** | $\text{W/m}^2$ |
| 3 | $\varepsilon_\lambda = \frac{E_\lambda}{E_{b,\lambda}}$ | Monochromatic emissivity definition | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\alpha_\lambda = \frac{E_{a,\lambda}}{E_{i,\lambda}}$ | Monochromatic absorptivity definition | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$ | **Kirchhoff's Law** | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\varepsilon = \alpha$ (gray body) | Gray body identity | dimensionless |
| 7 | $E = \alpha E_b$ | Absorbed energy from black body source | $\text{W/m}^2$ |
| 8 | $\lambda_{\max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ | **Wien's Displacement Law** | $\text{m·K}$ |
| 9 | $E_{\lambda,b} = \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 [e^{hc/\lambda kT} - 1]}$ | **Planck's Law** (black body) | $\text{W/m}^2\text{·}\mu\text{m}$ |
| 10 | $q = \varepsilon \sigma (T_s^4 - T_{\text{sur}}^4)$ | Net radiation heat flux (gray body) | $\text{W/m}^2$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kirchhoff's Law** is *not* an assumption — it is a thermodynamic consequence of detailed balance at equilibrium. It is the single most tested identity in KTU Module 2 problems.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Furnace & Boiler Design:** Gray body assumption enables sizing of refractory linings and heat-transfer surfaces.
- **Solar Thermal Collectors:** Selective coatings are designed by *breaking* the gray body assumption to maximize absorption at solar wavelengths while minimizing emission at infrared wavelengths.
- **Re-entry Vehicle Thermal Protection:** Black body concepts underpin the design of heat shields (Space Shuttle tiles).
- **Building Energy Auditing:** Emissivity of walls, roofs, and windows governs radiative cooling/heating loads.
- **Thermography & Pyrometry:** Temperature measurement of hot bodies *requires* an emissivity correction (otherwise you get massive errors).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Planck's Law to Stefan-Boltzmann for a Gray Body

We begin with **Planck's spectral distribution** for a black body and systematically extend it to a gray body.

---

**Step 1: Start with Planck's spectral emissive power**

Planck's law gives the monochromatic emissive power of a black body at wavelength $\lambda$ and temperature $T$:

$$E_{\lambda,b} = \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 \left[ e^{hc/(\lambda k T)} - 1 \right]}$$

where:
- $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}\,\text{J·s}$ (Planck's constant)
- $c = 2.998 \times 10^8\,\text{m/s}$ (speed of light)
- $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}\,\text{J/K}$ (Boltzmann constant)

---

**Step 2: Total emissive power of a black body (Stefan-Boltzmann Law)**

To find the total emissive power, integrate Planck's distribution over all wavelengths from $0$ to $\infty$:

$$E_b = \int_0^\infty E_{\lambda,b}\, d\lambda = \int_0^\infty \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 \left[ e^{hc/(\lambda k T)} - 1 \right]}\, d\lambda$$

Evaluating this integral (using the standard substitution $u = hc/(\lambda kT)$) yields the famous Stefan-Boltzmann Law:

$$E_b = \sigma T^4$$

where $\sigma$ is the Stefan-Boltzmann constant:

$$\sigma = \frac{2 \pi^5 k^4}{15 h^3 c^2} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}^4$$

---

**Step 3: Define the monochromatic emissivity**

For a real (non-black) surface, the monochromatic emissive power is the black body value scaled by the monochromatic emissivity:

$$E_\lambda = \varepsilon_\lambda \cdot E_{\lambda,b}$$

This definition comes from $\varepsilon_\lambda \equiv E_\lambda / E_{\lambda,b}$.

---

**Step 4: Total emissive power of a real surface**

The total emissive power is the integral of the spectral emissive power:

$$E = \int_0^\infty E_\lambda\, d\lambda = \int_0^\infty \varepsilon_\lambda \cdot E_{\lambda,b}\, d\lambda$$

Because $\varepsilon_\lambda$ is a *function* of $\lambda$ in general, this integral is **not** trivially solvable.

---

**Step 5: Apply the Gray Body assumption**

The gray body assumption states that $\varepsilon_\lambda$ is **independent of wavelength**:

$$\varepsilon_\lambda = \varepsilon = \text{constant}$$

Substituting into the integral:

$$E = \int_0^\infty \varepsilon \cdot E_{\lambda,b}\, d\lambda = \varepsilon \int_0^\infty E_{\lambda,b}\, d\lambda$$

---

**Step 6: Factor the constant outside the integral**

Since $\varepsilon$ is a constant, it can be pulled outside:

$$E = \varepsilon \cdot E_b$$

---

**Step 7: Substitute the Stefan-Boltzmann result**

Substituting $E_b = \sigma T^4$:

$$\boxed{E = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, T^4}$$

This is the **fundamental gray body emission formula** used throughout KTU Module 2 problems.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Kirchhoff's Law from Thermodynamic Equilibrium

**Step 1: Consider a small cavity at temperature $T$**

Suppose we have a perfectly insulated enclosure with internal walls and place a small test body inside. The system is in thermal equilibrium at temperature $T$.

**Step 2: Apply the Second Law of Thermodynamics**

If a body emitted more energy than it absorbed at any wavelength, the body would continuously lose energy, violating the equilibrium condition. Therefore, at every wavelength:

$$\text{Energy emitted at }\lambda = \text{Energy absorbed at }\lambda$$

**Step 3: Write the balance**

$$E_{\lambda, \text{emitted}} = E_{\lambda, \text{absorbed}}$$

$$E_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda E_{i,\lambda}$$

where $E_{i,\lambda}$ is the incident radiation on the body at wavelength $\lambda$.

**Step 4: Substitute the definition of emissivity**

$$E_\lambda = \varepsilon_\lambda E_{b,\lambda}$$

Equating the two expressions:

$$\varepsilon_\lambda E_{b,\lambda} = \alpha_\lambda E_{i,\lambda}$$

**Step 5: Apply the condition that the surroundings behave as a black body at equilibrium**

Inside an isothermal cavity, the incident radiation on the body equals the black body radiation at temperature $T$:

$$E_{i,\lambda} = E_{b,\lambda}$$

**Step 6: Final result**

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda}$$

This is **Kirchhoff's Law**. For a gray body, since both are wavelength-independent, $\varepsilon = \alpha$.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Body Types

| Property | Black Body | Gray Body | Real (Selective) Body |
|----------|------------|-----------|----------------------|
| $\varepsilon_\lambda$ vs $\lambda$ | $= 1$ (constant) | $= \varepsilon < 1$ (constant) | varies with $\lambda$ |
| $\alpha_\lambda$ vs $\lambda$ | $= 1$ (constant) | $= \alpha < 1$ (constant) | varies with $\lambda$ |
| Absorptivity | $1$ | $< 1$ | $< 1$ |
| Reflectivity | $0$ | $> 0$ | $> 0$ |
| Transmissivity | $0$ | $0$ (opaque) | often $0$ (opaque) |
| Emissive Power | $\sigma T^4$ | $\varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | $\int \varepsilon_\lambda E_{b,\lambda}\, d\lambda$ |
| Kirchhoff applies? | Trivially | $\varepsilon = \alpha$ | $\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$ |
| KTU treatment | Reference standard | **Default assumption** | Requires spectral integration |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Concept Map: Radiation Properties of Bodies

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Radiative Surfaces] --> B[Black Body]
    A --> C[Gray Body]
    A --> D[Real Selective Body]

    B --> B1[eps_lambda = 1]
    B --> B2[alpha_lambda = 1]
    B --> B3[Eb = sigma T4]
    B --> B4[Perfect Absorber and Emitter]

    C --> C1[eps_lambda = eps constant less than 1]
    C --> C2[alpha_lambda = alpha constant less than 1]
    C --> C3[E = eps sigma T4]
    C --> C4[Uniform Emitter across all wavelengths]

    D --> D1[eps_lambda varies with lambda]
    D --> D2[alpha_lambda varies with lambda]
    D --> D3[Requires Planck Integration]
    D --> D4[Eg: Selective Solar Coatings]

    C1 -.Kirchhoff Law.-> C2
    B1 -.Kirchhoff Law.-> B2
```

### 4.2 Process Flow: Gray Body Energy Balance

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Incident Radiation Ei] --> B[Surface Interaction]
    B --> C[Absorbed Part alpha times Ei]
    B --> D[Reflected Part rho times Ei]
    B --> E[Transmitted Part tau times Ei]
    C --> F[Energy Gain to Body]
    D --> G[Returned to Environment]
    E --> H[Passed Through Body]
    F --> I[Body Re-emits E = eps sigma T4]
    I --> J[Thermal Equilibrium Check]
    J --> K[Kirchhoff Verification eps equals alpha]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Black Body as Reference Standard

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph Reference [BLACK BODY REFERENCE BENCHMARK]
        R1[Input: Temperature T] --> R2[Planck Spectral Distribution]
        R2 --> R3[Integrate over all wavelengths]
        R3 --> R4[Output: Eb = sigma T4]
    end

    subgraph RealSurface [REAL GRAY SURFACE]
        S1[Input: Temperature T] --> S2[Apply emissivity eps less than 1]
        S2 --> S3[Scale down by factor eps]
        S3 --> S4[Output: E = eps sigma T4]
    end

    R4 -.Multiplier eps.-> S2
    R4 --> VALIDATE[Engineering Validation and Design Calculations]
    S4 --> VALIDATE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The black body acts as the **universal reference**. Every real gray surface calculation is simply the black body result multiplied by a single constant $\varepsilon$. This is the entire essence of the gray body simplification.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

Define a **black body** and a **gray body**. State **two** distinguishing properties of each.

**Model Answer:**

A **black body** is an ideal surface that absorbs all incident thermal radiation at all wavelengths and at all temperatures. Its monochromatic emissivity $\varepsilon_\lambda = 1$ and its emissive power follows the Stefan-Boltzmann law: $E_b = \sigma T^4$.

A **gray body** is a surface whose monochromatic emissivity is constant and independent of wavelength, but less than unity: $\varepsilon_\lambda = \varepsilon < 1$. Its emissive power is $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$.

**Distinguishing properties:**

| Property | Black Body | Gray Body |
|----------|------------|-----------|
| Emissivity | $\varepsilon = 1$ (maximum) | $\varepsilon < 1$ (constant) |
| Absorptivity | $\alpha = 1$ (absorbs all) | $\alpha = \varepsilon < 1$ (absorbs fraction) |
| Reflectivity | $\rho = 0$ | $\rho > 0$ |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

State **Kirchhoff's Law** of thermal radiation. Mention its significance in heat transfer calculations.

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** *At thermal equilibrium, the monochromatic emissivity of a body equals its monochromatic absorptivity at the same wavelength and temperature.*

$$\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$$

**Significance:**
1. It allows the *emission* property of a surface to be computed from its *absorption* property (or vice versa), which is easier to measure experimentally.
2. It establishes that **good absorbers are good emitters** — a deep physical insight.
3. For gray bodies, it simplifies to $\varepsilon = \alpha$, making the gray body assumption extremely useful in industrial furnace and boiler design where complex spectral integration would otherwise be required.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Many students **write $\varepsilon = 1$ for black body but forget to state that this holds for *all* wavelengths** — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for this omission.
> - In Kirchhoff's Law derivations, students often skip the step showing *why* $E_{i,\lambda} = E_{b,\lambda}$ inside an isothermal cavity. This is the **key thermodynamic step** — losing it costs 2–3 marks.
> - In numerical problems, students frequently confuse the surface temperature $T_s$ with the surroundings temperature $T_{\text{sur}}$ in net heat flux formulas. Always **define both temperatures clearly** at the start of the solution.
> - **Unit consistency:** Temperature in Stefan-Boltzmann law *must* be in **Kelvin (K)**, not °C. Examiners deduct a full mark for this conversion error.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** for a gray body starting from the definition of monochromatic emissivity. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A furnace wall has an emissivity of $\varepsilon = 0.85$ and a surface temperature of $T_s = 1200\,\text{K}$. The surroundings are at $T_{\text{sur}} = 800\,\text{K}$. Calculate the **net radiative heat flux** leaving the wall. Take $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}^4$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Refer to Section 3.1, Steps 1 through 7 of these notes for the complete step-by-step derivation. The final boxed result is:

$$E = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, T^4$$

**Key assumptions to state explicitly (1 mark each):**
1. The surface is a **gray body** ($\varepsilon_\lambda = \text{constant}$).
2. The surface is **opaque** ($\tau = 0$).
3. The surface is **diffuse** (emission/absorption is uniform in all directions).
4. The medium between surfaces is **non-participating** (no absorption/emission in the medium).

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $\varepsilon = 0.85$
- $T_s = 1200\,\text{K}$
- $T_{\text{sur}} = 800\,\text{K}$
- $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}^4$

**Formula (net radiative heat flux for a gray surface enclosed in a much larger surrounding):**

$$q = \varepsilon \, \sigma \left( T_s^4 - T_{\text{sur}}^4 \right)$$

**Step 1: Compute $T_s^4$**

$$T_s^4 = (1200)^4$$

$$1200^2 = 1.44 \times 10^6$$

$$1200^4 = (1.44 \times 10^6)^2 = 2.0736 \times 10^{12}\,\text{K}^4$$

**Step 2: Compute $T_{\text{sur}}^4$**

$$T_{\text{sur}}^4 = (800)^4$$

$$800^2 = 6.4 \times 10^5$$

$$800^4 = (6.4 \times 10^5)^2 = 4.096 \times 10^{11}\,\text{K}^4$$

**Step 3: Compute the difference**

$$T_s^4 - T_{\text{sur}}^4 = 2.0736 \times 10^{12} - 0.4096 \times 10^{12} = 1.6640 \times 10^{12}\,\text{K}^4$$

**Step 4: Substitute and solve**

$$q = 0.85 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.6640 \times 10^{12}$$

$$q = 0.85 \times 5.67 \times 1.6640 \times 10^{4}$$

$$q = 0.85 \times 9.4349 \times 10^{4}$$

$$q = 8.0197 \times 10^{4}\,\text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 5: Final answer**

$$\boxed{q \approx 80.2\,\text{kW/m}^2}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** State and prove **Kirchhoff's Law** of thermal radiation. Mention its physical significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A tungsten filament has an emissivity of $\varepsilon = 0.30$ and operates at a temperature of $T = 2500\,\text{K}$. Find: (i) the total emissive power of the filament, and (ii) the wavelength at which the emission is maximum. Take $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}^4$ and Wien's constant $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m·K}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Proof of Kirchhoff's Law (7 marks):**

Refer to Section 3.2, Steps 1 through 6 for the complete derivation. The final result:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda}$$

**Physical significance to mention (1 mark each):**
1. **Good absorbers are good emitters** at the same wavelength.
2. A surface that is selective in absorption is *equally* selective in emission.
3. At thermal equilibrium, the energy budget of every wavelength band is balanced.
4. It enables indirect measurement of emissivity through absorptivity experiments.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $\varepsilon = 0.30$
- $T = 2500\,\text{K}$
- $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}^4$
- $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m·K}$

**(i) Total emissive power of the gray filament (3 marks):**

**Formula:**

$$E = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, T^4$$

**Step 1: Compute $T^4$**

$$T^4 = (2500)^4$$

$$2500^2 = 6.25 \times 10^6$$

$$2500^4 = (6.25 \times 10^6)^2 = 3.90625 \times 10^{13}\,\text{K}^4$$

**Step 2: Substitute**

$$E = 0.30 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 3.90625 \times 10^{13}$$

$$E = 0.30 \times 5.67 \times 3.90625 \times 10^{5}$$

$$E = 0.30 \times 22.148 \times 10^{5}$$

$$E = 6.644 \times 10^{5}\,\text{W/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 664.4\,\text{kW/m}^2}$$

**(ii) Wavelength of maximum emission (4 marks):**

**Wien's Displacement Law:**

$$\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = b$$

$$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{b}{T} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{2500}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_{\max} = 1.1592 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m} = 1.159\,\mu\text{m}}$$

This wavelength lies in the **near-infrared region**, which is why incandescent lamp filaments glow with a yellowish-white light.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Concept of Black and Gray Bodies**

- **Black Body:** Perfect emitter and absorber. $\varepsilon = \alpha = 1$. Emits $E_b = \sigma T^4$.
- **Gray Body:** Constant emissivity $\varepsilon = \alpha < 1$ across all wavelengths. Emits $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$.
- **Real Selective Body:** Emissivity varies with wavelength — requires full spectral integration.
- **Kirchhoff's Law:** $\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$ (most heavily tested identity).
- **Gray Body Simplification:** Pulls $\varepsilon$ out of the integral — *the* key engineering shortcut.
- **Stefan-Boltzmann Constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}^4$ — *always required in numericals*.
- **Temperature Units:** Must use **Kelvin (K)** in all radiation formulas; convert from °C by adding 273.15.
- **Wien's Displacement Law:** $\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m·K}$ — used to find peak emission wavelength.
- **Net Heat Flux:** $q = \varepsilon \sigma (T_s^4 - T_{\text{sur}}^4)$ — for a gray surface enclosed in a much larger surrounding.
- **Opaque Body Assumption:** $\tau = 0$ → $\alpha + \rho = 1$ → standard KTU problem setting.
- **Diffuse Surface Assumption:** Radiation is uniform in all directions — required for $\varepsilon = \alpha$ to apply in directional form.
- **Top Mistakes to Avoid:** Forgetting to convert to Kelvin; mixing up $T_s$ and $T_{\text{sur}}$; dropping the $\varepsilon$ factor in gray body formulas; omitting the "all wavelengths" qualifier in black body definitions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Black Body Radiation: Laws of Black Body Radiation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Black Body Radiation: Laws of Black Body Radiation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Black Body Radiation** is the theoretical electromagnetic radiation emitted by a perfect black body — an idealized physical object that absorbs **all** incident electromagnetic radiation, regardless of frequency or angle of incidence, and re-emits energy at the maximum possible rate per unit area for a given thermodynamic temperature.

A **Black Body** is therefore a perfect absorber ($\alpha = 1$) and a perfect emitter ($\varepsilon = 1$). In radiation heat transfer, all real surfaces are compared against this theoretical benchmark to quantify their deviation from ideal behavior.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus (Module 2):** Students are expected to master the four foundational laws governing black body radiation:
> 1. **Planck's Law of Black Body Radiation** (Quantum origin)
> 2. **Wien's Displacement Law**
> 3. **Stefan–Boltzmann Law**
> 4. **Kirchhoff's Law of Thermal Radiation**

The **Spectral Emissive Power**, $E_{b\lambda}$, is the rate of energy emitted per unit area of a black surface per unit wavelength interval (W/m²·m or W/m³). When integrated over the entire spectrum, it gives the total hemispherical emissive power $E_b$ (W/m²).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Physical Constants Required for This Module**
> - **Stefan–Boltzmann constant:** $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴
> - **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8}$ m/s
> - **Planck's constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
> - **Boltzmann constant:** $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
> - **Wien's displacement constant:** $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Perfect Heater" Imagery

Imagine a small, perfectly blackened oven with a tiny pinhole. Light entering the pinhole bounces inside repeatedly, and because the walls are pitch-black, **every single photon gets absorbed** before it can ever escape back out. The hole then appears perfectly black — the darkest black you can ever see.

Now heat this oven to a high temperature. Because it absorbed *all* the radiation that hit it, by the laws of thermodynamics it must also **emit the maximum possible radiation** at every wavelength. This is exactly a black body: a surface that is the ultimate absorber, and therefore the ultimate emitter, of thermal radiation.

In simple terms:

> **Black Body = Universe's most efficient "energy sponge" + equally efficient "energy radiator"**

This is why a real cooling fin, a satellite panel, or a furnace wall is *never* as efficient as a black body — they reflect some radiation, making them imperfect absorbers and imperfect emitters.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of Black Body Radiation

#### **Pillar 1: Planck's Law (The Quantum Foundation)**

Planck's Law describes the *spectral distribution* of black body radiation. Unlike classical physics, it introduces energy quantization: radiation is emitted in discrete packets called **photons** with energy $E = h\nu$.

**Key Insights:**
- Each mode of electromagnetic oscillation of frequency $\nu$ carries energy $\varepsilon = h\nu$.
- The average energy follows Bose–Einstein statistics: $\bar{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{h\nu}{e^{h\nu/k_BT} - 1}$.
- Multiplying by the density of modes per unit area per unit frequency gives the spectral emissive power.
- Classical Rayleigh–Jeans law is recovered in the limit $h\nu \ll k_B T$ (low frequency).
- Classical Wien's approximation is recovered in the limit $h\nu \gg k_B T$ (high frequency).

#### **Pillar 2: Wien's Displacement Law (Peak Wavelength Shift)**

This law states that as the temperature of a black body **increases**, the wavelength at which emission peaks **shifts toward shorter (higher-energy) wavelengths**.

- A heated iron rod first glows **dull red** (~700 nm), then **orange** (~600 nm), then **white-hot** (~500 nm) as T rises.
- Astronomers use this law backwards: by measuring the peak wavelength of a star's spectrum, they can compute its surface temperature.

#### **Pillar 3: Stefan–Boltzmann Law (Total Power Scaling)**

The total energy radiated per unit area scales with the **fourth power of absolute temperature**.

- Doubling the temperature increases emission by $2^4 = 16$ times.
- A small change in T causes a disproportionately large change in heat loss — critical for furnace design, re-entry physics, and solar collectors.

#### **Pillar 4: Kirchhoff's Law (Absorptivity = Emissivity)**

At thermal equilibrium, for any surface, the **spectral emissivity equals the spectral absorptivity** at every wavelength and temperature.

- A surface that strongly absorbs a particular color of light will equally strongly emit that same color when heated.
- This is what links absorption and emission — and it is the fundamental reason **good absorbers are good emitters** and **good reflectors are poor emitters**.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Law | Governing Equation | Form of Variable | Standard SI Units |
|---|-----|--------------------|------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Planck's Spectral Distribution | $E_{b\lambda} = \dfrac{2\pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}\left(e^{hc/\lambda k_B T} - 1\right)}$ | Spectral emissive power per unit wavelength | W/m³ |
| 2 | Planck's Spectral Distribution (frequency form) | $E_{b\nu} = \dfrac{2\pi h \nu^{3}}{c^{2}\left(e^{h\nu/k_B T} - 1\right)}$ | Spectral emissive power per unit frequency | W/m²·Hz |
| 3 | Rayleigh–Jeans Limit ($hc \ll \lambda k_B T$) | $E_{b\lambda} \approx \dfrac{2\pi c k_B T}{\lambda^{4}}$ | Classical low-frequency limit | W/m³ |
| 4 | Wien's Approximation ($hc \gg \lambda k_B T$) | $E_{b\lambda} \approx \dfrac{2\pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}} e^{-hc/\lambda k_B T}$ | Quantum high-frequency limit | W/m³ |
| 5 | Stefan–Boltzmann Law (Total Power) | $E_b = \sigma T^{4}$ | Total hemispherical emissive power | W/m² |
| 6 | Stefan–Boltzmann Constant | $\sigma = \dfrac{2\pi^{5} k_B^{4}}{15 h^{3} c^{2}}$ | Universal constant | W/m²·K⁴ |
| 7 | Wien's Displacement Law | $\lambda_{max} \cdot T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K | Peak wavelength of emission | m·K |
| 8 | Maximum Spectral Emissive Power | $E_{b\lambda_{max}} \propto T^{5}$ | Peak height scales with T⁵ | W/m³ |
| 9 | Kirchhoff's Law | $\varepsilon_\lambda(\theta, \phi, T) = \alpha_\lambda(\theta, \phi, T)$ | Spectral and directional equality | dimensionless |
| 10 | Diffuse Gray Surface Identity | $\varepsilon = \alpha$ (integrated form) | Total hemispherical equality | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Real-World Utility of These Equations**
