# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper Pattern)]
**Define the term "Onset of Nucleate Boiling" (ONB). What physical condition at the wall triggers the ONB?**

**Model Answer:**
The Onset of Nucleate Boiling (ONB) is the surface superheat (ΔT_e) at which the first vapor bubble is observed to nucleate on the heated surface immersed in a saturated liquid. Below ONB, the wall temperature is too low to activate the trapped gas/vapor cavities; heat transfer occurs only by single-phase natural convection. ONB is triggered when the wall superheat exceeds a critical value ΔT_e,ONB that is sufficient to overcome the surface tension and viscous suppression of the trapped embryo, allowing it to grow unstably. The corresponding temperature T_w,ONB is typically only a few degrees (≈ 4 °C for water at 100 °C) above the saturation temperature, marking the transition from Regime A (natural convection) to Regime B (nucleate boiling) on the Nukiyama curve.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper Pattern)]
**Distinguish clearly between "Critical Heat Flux" (CHF) and "Minimum Heat Flux" (MHF). Why does the heat flux fall between CHF and MHF?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Critical Heat Flux (CHF)** is the maximum heat flux a boiling surface can sustain in the nucleate boiling regime. Beyond CHF, the surface cannot remain liquid-wetted and a vapor blanket begins to form. The Zuber correlation gives q"_CHF = 0.149 h_fg ρ_v^{1/2} [σ g (ρ_l − ρ_v)]^{1/4}.
- **Minimum Heat Flux (MHF)**, also called the **Leidenfrost point**, is the lowest heat flux at which a continuous, stable vapor film can be maintained on the surface. Below MHF, the film collapses and the system jumps back to nucleate boiling.
- Between CHF and MHF, the system is in the **transition boiling** regime. The heat flux falls because the wall is partially covered by an insulating vapor film, which dramatically reduces the effective thermal conductance. The wall is essentially "starved" of liquid contact. This negative-slope branch is intrinsically unstable and is usually traversed rapidly during a transient burnout.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3 Choice Pattern]

**(a)** Sketch and explain the **Nukiyama curve** for saturated pool boiling. Identify all four regimes and the salient transition points (ONB, CHF, MHF). Indicate the stable and unstable regions.

**(b)** A horizontal polished copper surface boils saturated water at 1 atm. Using **Rohsenow's correlation**, determine the heat flux when the wall superheat is 10 °C. Comment on whether the system is operating safely relative to CHF. Data: C_sf (water–copper) = 0.0130, n = 1.0, μ_l = 279 × 10⁻⁶ N·s/m², h_fg = 2257 × 10³ J/kg, ρ_l = 957.9 kg/m³, ρ_v = 0.5956 kg/m³, σ = 0.0589 N/m, c_p,l = 4217 J/kg·K, Pr_l = 1.76.

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

A neatly labelled sketch of the Nukiyama curve (q" vs. ΔT_e) with the following features:

- **Axes:** Ordinate = heat flux q" (W/m²), Abscissa = ΔT_e (°C). Often drawn with log(q") on the ordinate.
- **Regime A (Free Natural Convection):** Low ΔT_e (0 to ≈ 4 °C), gentle upward slope. Single-phase, no bubbles.
- **ONB point:** Marked at ΔT_e,ONB ≈ 4 °C for water at 1 atm. Start of Regime B.
- **Regime B (Nucleate Boiling):** Two sub-regimes — isolated bubble regime (B-1) and slug/column bubble regime (B-2). Heat flux rises as q" ∝ (ΔT_e)³ per Rohsenow.
- **CHF peak:** q"_CHF ≈ 1.26 × 10⁶ W/m². Maximum of the curve.
- **Regime C (Transition Boiling):** Negative slope — unstable, drawn dashed. Not accessible in steady state.
- **MHF point:** Lowest heat flux in film boiling ≈ 0.18 × 10⁶ W/m².
- **Regime D (Stable Film Boiling):** Positive slope at low ΔT_e, leveling off as radiation takes over. Use Berenson/Bromley correlations.
- **Hysteresis loop** between the nucleate boiling (rising) branch and the film boiling (falling) branch — clearly indicated with arrows.

> Each of the above should be marked and labelled explicitly. Award full 7 marks for a complete sketch with all labels and a brief (1–2 sentence) description of each regime.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

Apply Rohsenow's correlation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q'' &= \mu_l \, h_{fg} \left[ \dfrac{g (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\sigma} \right]^{1/2} \left[ \dfrac{c_{p,l} \, \Delta T_e}{C_{sf} \, h_{fg} \, Pr_l^{\,n}} \right]^{3} \\[8pt]
\text{Group A} &= (279 \times 10^{-6})(2257 \times 10^{3}) = 629.7 \; \text{W/m} \\[4pt]
\text{Group B} &= \left[ \dfrac{(9.81)(957.9 - 0.5956)}{0.0589} \right]^{1/2} = 399.3 \; \text{m}^{-1} \\[4pt]
\text{Group C} &= \left[ \dfrac{(4217)(10)}{(0.0130)(2257 \times 10^{3})(1.76)} \right]^{3} = \left[ \dfrac{42170}{51637.6} \right]^{3} = (0.8166)^{3} = 0.5446 \\[4pt]
q'' &= 629.7 \times 399.3 \times 0.5446 \approx 1.369 \times 10^{5} \; \text{W/m}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** Using Zuber correlation, q"_CHF = 0.149 h_fg ρ_v^{1/2} [σ g (ρ_l − ρ_v)]^{1/4} ≈ 1.26 × 10⁶ W/m².
Since q" (1.37 × 10⁵) << q"_CHF (1.26 × 10⁶), the system is operating **well within the safe nucleate boiling regime** with a DNBR of approximately 9.2. No risk of burnout at this operating point.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**(a)** With the help of a clear sketch, explain the **mechanism of bubble nucleation, growth and departure** during nucleate pool boiling. Define the bubble departure diameter using Fritz's correlation.

**(b)** Derive the **Zuber correlation for Critical Heat Flux** in saturated pool boiling. For water at 1 atm, calculate the CHF and compare it with the experimentally reported value of 1.26 × 10⁶ W/m².

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

A clean **mermaid-style sketch** showing a heated surface with a cavity, an embryo, growth, necking, and bubble departure — followed by liquid re-flooding. The explanation should cover:

- Liquid at saturation temperature is in contact with the wall. **Trapped gas/vapor embryos** exist in microscopic cavities on the wall.
- When the wall superheat ΔT_e exceeds a critical value (≈ 4–5 °C for water at 1 atm), the embryo grows unstably inside the cavity and emerges as a bubble.
- The bubble grows by **evaporation of superheated liquid** at the bubble-liquid interface, fed by latent heat conducted from the wall through the superheated liquid layer.
- Buoyancy acts on the bubble; once buoyancy exceeds surface tension at the cavity mouth, the bubble **detaches**.
- Fritz's correlation gives the departure diameter:
$$D_d = 0.0208 \, \theta \left[ \dfrac{\sigma}{g (\rho_l - \rho_v)} \right]^{1/2}$$
where θ is the contact angle (≈ 50° for water on most clean surfaces).
- After departure, **cold bulk liquid rushes in** to fill the void, contacts the hot wall, and the cycle repeats. The frequency f ≈ 1/(t_w + t_r), where t_w is the waiting period and t_r is the residence time.
- This **pumping action** of bubbles is the source of the very high heat transfer coefficients in nucleate boiling.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Derivation (as in Section 3.2):** Use Rayleigh–Taylor instability to obtain the most-unstable Taylor wavelength λ:
$$\lambda = 2\pi \left[ \dfrac{\sigma}{g (\rho_l - \rho_v)} \right]^{1/2}$$
The vapor escape velocity is:
$$u_v = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda} \left[ \dfrac{\sigma g (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_l^{2}} \right]^{1/4}$$
Setting q"_CHF = ρ_v h_fg u_v and substituting the constants gives:
$$q''_{CHF} = 0.149 \, h_{fg} \, \rho_v^{1/2} \left[ \sigma \, g (\rho_l - \rho_v) \right]^{1/4}$$

**Numerical calculation (same data as Section 3.4 solution):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
q''_{CHF} &= 0.149 \times (2257 \times 10^{3}) \times (0.5956)^{1/2} \times [0.0589 \times 9.81 \times 957.3]^{1/4} \\[4pt]
&\approx 1.256 \times 10^{6} \; \text{W/m}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison:** The calculated value (1.256 MW/m²) matches the experimental value (1.26 MW/m²) to within 0.4%, confirming the accuracy of the Zuber correlation for water at atmospheric pressure.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students most commonly lose marks on "Regimes of Boiling" questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to label ONB, CHF, and MHF on the Nukiyama sketch.** Even a beautifully drawn curve with no labels scores zero on the descriptive part. (Penalty: 2–3 marks.)
> 2. **Confusing C_sf values across fluid-surface pairs.** C_sf for water–copper is **0.0130**, for water–platinum is **0.0130**, for water–stainless steel is **0.0132**, for water–nickel is **0.0060**. A wrong C_sf will produce a ΔT_e that is off by an order of magnitude.
> 3. **Using 0.149 vs. 0.125 in Zuber.** Modern textbooks (and KTU 2024) accept 0.149 (Zuber) for saturated pool boiling and 0.125 (Lienhard–Witte) for the corrected buoyancy-aware form. Pick one and stick with it; do not mix in the same solution.
> 4. **Mixing units:** σ in N/m, ρ in kg/m³, g in m/s², h_fg in J/kg. A common error is using h_fg in kJ/kg while leaving all other SI units unchanged.
> 5. **Calling the unstable branch "stable film boiling."** Regime C is *transition* boiling. Regime D is the *stable film* regime. Examiners explicitly deduct for this confusion.
> 6. **Omitting the MHF (Leidenfrost point).** Many answers stop at CHF. MHF is a separate transition that the KTU 2024 syllabus specifically lists.
> 7. **Skipping the physical interpretation in numerical problems.** A pure number without "operating safely in nucleate regime, well below CHF" loses the "engineering judgment" marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam.)**

- ☐ Boiling = vaporization at a heated surface submerged in a liquid, T_w > T_sat.
- ☐ The Nukiyama curve is the master plot: q" (log) vs. ΔT_e.
- ☐ **Sequence of regimes:** Natural Convection → Nucleate Boiling → Transition Boiling → Stable Film Boiling.
- ☐ **ONB** = Onset of Nucleate Boiling (transition from A to B).
- ☐ **CHF** = Critical Heat Flux (peak of curve, ≈ 1.26 MW/m² for water at 1 atm).
- ☐ **MHF** = Minimum Heat Flux (Leidenfrost point, valley between C and D).
- ☐ Nucleate boiling is the **most efficient** regime — preferred in industrial design.
- ☐ **Rohsenow's correlation** — q" ∝ (ΔT_e)³ — valid in lower/upper nucleate regime.
- ☐ **Zuber's CHF correlation** — q"_CHF = 0.149 h_fg ρ_v^{1/2} [σ g (ρ_l − ρ_v)]^{1/4}.
- ☐ **Berenson's film boiling correlation** — q" ∝ (ΔT_e)^{1/4} — valid in stable film regime.
- ☐ **Bubble departure diameter (Fritz):** D_d = 0.0208 θ [σ / g (ρ_l − ρ_v)]^{1/2}.
- ☐ **Bromley's correction:** h_fg' = h_fg + 0.68 c_p,v ΔT_e in film boiling.
- ☐ **C_sf** values: water–Cu ≈ 0.0130, water–SS ≈ 0.0132, water–Ni ≈ 0.0060, water–Pt ≈ 0.0130.
- ☐ Hysteresis exists between nucleate (rising ΔT_e) and film (falling ΔT_e) branches.
- ☐ Industrial design rule: always operate at q" < 0.7 × q"_CHF (DNBR > 1.3) to leave safety margin.
- ☐ Three energy-transport mechanisms: (1) latent heat via bubbles, (2) convection in superheated liquid layer, (3) radiation at high ΔT_e.
- ☐ Above MHF, the negative-slope transition regime is **unstable** — never designed for steady operation.
- ☐ Zuber constant for saturated pool boiling: 0.149. Lienhard–Witte improved form: 0.125.
- ☐ The Nukiyama curve has **two positive-slope branches** (natural convection at low ΔT_e and stable film boiling at high ΔT_e) separated by the **CHF peak** and the **MHF valley**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Correlation for Critical Heat Flux for Nucleate Pool Boiling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Correlation for Critical Heat Flux for Nucleate Pool Boiling ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Critical Heat Flux (CHF)** in nucleate pool boiling is defined as the **maximum heat flux** that can be transferred from a heated surface to a saturated liquid pool, beyond which the boiling regime undergoes a **catastrophic transition** from highly efficient nucleate boiling to inefficient film boiling. At this critical point, the discrete vapor bubbles generated at active nucleation sites coalesce into a continuous insulating vapor blanket that blankets the heater surface, drastically reducing the heat transfer coefficient. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The CHF (also termed the **burnout point** in reactor engineering or the **boiling crisis**) represents the upper operational limit of the **Nucleate Boiling Regime** on the classical Nukiyama curve. It separates the **Nucleate Boiling** region (high heat transfer, moderate surface superheat) from the **Transition Boiling** region (unstable, hysteresis-laden) and finally the **Film Boiling** region (stable, but very low heat flux). Mathematically, the CHF is the peak point on the boiling curve where: $$ \left(\frac{\partial q''}{\partial \Delta T_{sat}}\right) = 0 $$ where $q''$ is the heat flux and $\Delta T_{sat} = T_{wall} - T_{sat}$ is the surface superheat. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Popcorn Overload" Effect Imagine heating a pan of oil in your kitchen. Initially, small oil-vapor bubbles form at the bottom and pop at the surface, transferring heat very efficiently (this is **nucleate boiling**). As you turn up the stove, more and more bubbles form until they begin to merge into a thick vapor layer that **insulates** the pan from the oil — the temperature of the pan shoots up dangerously, but heat transfer to the oil **plummets**. This exact phenomenon is the **Critical Heat Flux**. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Implications:** > - In **nuclear reactor design**, exceeding the CHF on fuel rods causes a **fuel cladding failure** (the famous "burnout" in pressurized water reactors). > - In **rocket engine cooling**, exceeding CHF leads to **chugging instability** and potential engine failure. > - In **electronics cooling** of high-power chips, exceeding CHF causes **thermal runaway**. ### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Key Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value/Unit | |---|---|---| | Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** | | Liquid-to-vapor density ratio | $\rho_l / \rho_v$ | **1000 : 1** for water at 100°C | | Surface tension of water at 100°C | $\sigma$ | **0.0589 N/m** | | Latent heat of vaporization (water) | $h_{fg}$ | **2257 × 10³ J/kg** | > [!TIP] > For the KTU 2024 exam, you will typically be provided saturated water properties at the given pressure. Memorize the **structural form** of the correlations, not numerical constants. ### 1.4 Physical Mechanism of CHF The widely accepted physical explanation is the **Hydrodynamic Instability Theory** (Taylor-Helmholtz instability): 1. Vapor bubbles are generated at discrete nucleation sites on the heated surface. 2. As heat flux increases, the bubble departure frequency and density increase. 3. A **vapor-jet column** forms above the heated surface. 4. At the CHF, this jet becomes **unstable** — the buoyancy-driven vapor column can no longer be sustained, and the interface between the rising vapor column and the surrounding liquid becomes **hydrodynamically unstable**. 5. The vapor **blankets** the surface, initiating transition boiling. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Zuber Hydrodynamic Instability **Zuber (1959)** was the first to derive a CHF correlation from first principles, modeling the vapor-liquid interface as a **Taylor instability** between two fluid layers. The critical condition corresponds to the most rapidly growing wavelength of the instability. The **dimensional analysis** of the problem identifies the following governing variables: $$ q''_{CHF} = f(h_{fg}, \rho_v, \rho_l, \sigma, g, L, R) $$ Through Buckingham's Pi theorem, the CHF for a large horizontal flat plate reduces to: $$ q''_{CHF} = C_{cr} \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \rho_v \left[\frac{\sigma \cdot g (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^{\,2}}\right]^{1/4} $$ The constant $C_{cr}$ depends on the heater geometry and finite-size effects. ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Flow of the Hydrodynamic Theory 1. **Vapor Column Formation:** Bubbles merge into a continuous vapor layer of thickness $\delta$ above the heater. 2. **Buoyancy Drive:** The vapor rises due to buoyancy with characteristic velocity $u \sim \sqrt{g \delta (\rho_l - \rho_v)/\rho_v}$. 3. **Interface Instability:** A perturbation of wavelength $\lambda$ at the interface becomes unstable when the inertial restoring force is overcome by the buoyancy-driven destabilizing force. 4. **Critical Wavelength Selection:** The most rapidly growing wavelength is $\lambda_c = 2\pi \sqrt{3} \left[\sigma/(g(\rho_l - \rho_v))\right]^{1/2}$. 5. **CHF Threshold:** The instability grows fastest when the vapor mass flux equals the critical value, leading to the CHF formula. ### 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > This is the **definitive formula bank** for CHF problems in the KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize the structure and applicability of each correlation. | # | Correlation Name | Equation | Applicability | KTU Frequency | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Zuber (1959)** | $q''_{CHF} = 0.131 \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \rho_v \left[\dfrac{\sigma \, g (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^{\,2}}\right]^{1/4}$ | Large flat horizontal plate | **Very High** | | 2 | **Lienhard-Witte (1985)** | $q''_{CHF} = 0.149 \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \rho_v \left[\dfrac{\sigma \, g (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^{\,2}}\right]^{1/4}$ | Large flat horizontal plate (revised constant) | High | | 3 | **Berenson (1961)** | $q''_{CHF} = 0.18 \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \rho_v \left[\dfrac{\sigma \, g (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^{\,2}}\right]^{1/4}$ | Large flat horizontal plate | Medium | | 4 | **Kutateladze (1951)** | $q''_{CHF} = 0.16 \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \rho_v^{1/2} \left[\dfrac{\sigma \, g (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_l}\right]^{1/4}$ | Vertical surfaces / empirical | Medium | | 5 | **Lienhard-Gallego (Small Surface Correction)** | $\dfrac{q''_{CHF,small}}{q''_{CHF,large}} = \left[\dfrac{1}{1 + 1/L^*}\right]^{1/2}$ | Heaters with $L^* < 9.1$ | **High** | | 6 | **Horizontal Cylinder (Rohsenow)** | $q''_{CHF} = q''_{CHF,\infty} \cdot \left[1 + \dfrac{\sigma}{R^2 \, g (\rho_l - \rho_v)}\right]^{1/4}$ | Cylinder of radius $R$ | Medium | | 7 | **Characteristic Length** | $L^* = L \left[\dfrac{g (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\sigma}\right]^{1/2}$ | Non-dimensional length | **Very High** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Validity Rule:** The "large surface" correlations (Zuber, Lienhard-Witte) are valid **only** when $L^* = L \sqrt{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)/\sigma} \geq 9.1$. For smaller heaters, the **Lienhard-Gallego correction** factor $\left[\dfrac{1}{1+1/L^*}\right]^{1/2}$ **MUST** be applied. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | Application of CHF Correlation | |---|---| | **Nuclear Power** | Designing fuel rod safety margins in PWRs and BWRs | | **Rocketry** | Regenerative cooling of combustion chamber walls (e.g., V-2, Ariane) | | **Electronics** | Two-phase cooling of high-power CPUs and power electronics | | **Process Industry** | Reboiler design in distillation columns | | **Cryogenics** | LNG vaporization systems and cryocooler design | ### 2.5 Why and How of Each Parameter - **$h_{fg}$** — Latent heat of vaporization: Higher $h_{fg}$ means more energy is required to form vapor, increasing the CHF. - **$\rho_v$** — Vapor density: Denser vapor at higher pressures **reduces** CHF (vapor blanket is "heavier" and harder to lift off). - **$\sigma$** — Surface tension: Higher $\sigma$ stabilizes the vapor-liquid interface, **increasing** CHF. - **$g$** — Gravity: Higher $g$ enhances buoyancy-driven vapor removal, **increasing** CHF. - **$L$** — Heater size: For very small heaters, lateral vapor escape paths reduce the effective CHF. > [!TIP] > **KTU Memory Aid:** The CHF is **inversely** proportional to the square root of pressure (since $\rho_v$ rises sharply with pressure). At 1 atm, $q''_{CHF} \approx 1.25 \text{ MW/m}^2$ for water. At 10 atm, it drops to ~ 0.9 MW/m². ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Zuber Correlation (Hydrodynamic Instability) The Zuber CHF correlation is the **single most important** derivation for KTU 2024 Module 3. Below is the complete step-by-step derivation. #### Step 1: Setup of the Physical Model Consider a large horizontal flat heater of characteristic length $L$ immersed in a stagnant saturated liquid. A vapor layer of thickness $\delta$ forms above the heater with vapor density $\rho_v$, surrounded by liquid of density $\rho_l$. The interface between the two fluids is perturbed by a sinusoidal wave of wavelength $\lambda$ and amplitude $\epsilon$. The vapor rises with a characteristic velocity determined by buoyancy balance. By a steady-state momentum balance, the buoyancy force per unit area is balanced by the inertial drag: $$ g(\rho_l - \rho_v) \sim \rho_v \frac{u^2}{\delta} $$ Solving for the characteristic vapor velocity $u$: $$ u = \sqrt{\frac{g \delta (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v}} $$ #### Step 2: Mass Conservation of the Vapor Layer The vapor mass flux leaving the surface at the CHF equals the convective flux in the vapor column: $$ \dot{m}''_v = \rho_v u = \rho_v \sqrt{\frac{g \delta (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v}} = \sqrt{g \delta \rho_v (\rho_l - \rho_v)} $$ The corresponding heat flux at CHF is the vapor mass flux multiplied by the latent heat: $$ q''_{CHF} = \dot{m}''_v \cdot h_{fg} = h_{fg} \sqrt{g \delta \rho_v (\rho_l - \rho_v)} $$ #### Step 3: Dimensional Analysis via Buckingham's Pi Theorem For a large flat plate, the layer thickness $\delta$ is set by the most unstable Taylor wavelength. The natural length scale from the instability analysis is: $$ \lambda_c = 2\pi \sqrt{3} \left[\frac{\sigma}{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}\right]^{1/2} $$ The vapor layer thickness is of the same order: $$ \delta \sim \lambda_c / 2\pi $$ Substituting into the expression for $q''_{CHF}$: $$ q''_{CHF} \sim h_{fg} \left[g \cdot \rho_v (\rho_l - \rho_v) \cdot \frac{\sigma}{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}\right]^{1/2} = h_{fg} \left[\rho_v \cdot \sigma\right]^{1/2} $$ #### Step 4: Determining the Dimensionless Constant The exact hydrodynamic stability analysis (Zuber, 1959) yields: $$ q''_{CHF} = \frac{\pi}{24} h_{fg} \rho_v \left[\frac{\sigma g (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^{\,2}}\right]^{1/4} $$ Numerical evaluation of the constant: $$ \frac{\pi}{24} = 0.1309 \approx 0.131 $$ #### Step 5: Final Result — Zuber Correlation $$ \boxed{q''_{CHF} = 0.131 \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \rho_v \left[\frac{\sigma g (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^{\,2}}\right]^{1/4}} $$ **Validity:** Large flat horizontal plate where $L^* \geq 9.1$. ### 3.2 Derivation of the Lienhard-Gallego Small-Surface Correction For small heaters where $L^* < 9.1$, the vapor blanket is **no longer constrained** to form a one-dimensional column. Vapor can escape laterally, reducing the effective CHF. The correction factor is obtained by considering the change in the vapor escape geometry: $$ \frac{q''_{CHF,small}}{q''_{CHF,large}} = \left[\frac{1}{1 + 1/L^*}\right]^{1/2} $$ where $L^* = L \sqrt{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)/\sigma}$ is the **non-dimensional characteristic length**. The **critical transition value** $L^* = 9.1$ is obtained by setting the correction factor to 1: $$ \left[\frac{1}{1 + 1/L^*}\right]^{1/2} = 1 \implies 1 + 1/L^* = 1 \implies L^* \to \infty $$ In practice, the correction is within 5% of unity for $L^* > 9.1$, hence the threshold. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Demonstration > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example:** Saturated water at **1 atm (100°C)** is boiling on a **30 mm × 30 mm** flat horizontal plate. Calculate the CHF. **Given Properties at 1 atm (100°C):** - $h_{fg} = 2257 \times 10^3 \text{ J/kg}$ - $\rho_v = 0.5956 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - $\rho_l = 957.9 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - $\sigma = 0.0589 \text{ N/m}$ **Step 1: Check the validity of the large-surface correlation.** For a square plate, use the equivalent radius: $L = 30 \text{ mm} = 0.030 \text{ m}$. $$ L^* = L \sqrt{\frac{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\sigma}} = 0.030 \sqrt{\frac{9.81 \times (957.9 - 0.5956)}{0.0589}} $$ $$ L^* = 0.030 \sqrt{\frac{9.81 \times 957.3}{0.0589}} = 0.030 \sqrt{159{,}401} = 0.030 \times 399.25 = 11.98 $$ Since $L^* = 11.98 > 9.1$, the large-surface correlation is valid (correction factor $< 5\%$). **Step 2: Apply Zuber Correlation.** $$ q''_{CHF} = 0.131 \times h_{fg} \times \rho_v \left[\frac{\sigma g (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^{\,2}}\right]^{1/4} $$ Substituting values: $$ q''_{CHF} = 0.131 \times (2.257 \times 10^6) \times 0.5956 \times \left[\frac{0.0589 \times 9.81 \times 957.3}{(0.5956)^2}\right]^{1/4} $$ **Step 3: Evaluate the bracket.** $$ \frac{0.0589 \times 9.81 \times 957.3}{(0.5956)^2} = \frac{553.10}{0.3547} = 1559.2 $$ $$ (1559.2)^{1/4} = \sqrt{\sqrt{1559.2}} = \sqrt{39.49} = 6.284 $$ **Step 4: Compute the final CHF.** $$ q''_{CHF} = 0.131 \times 2.257 \times 10^6 \times 0.5956 \times 6.284 $$ $$ q''_{CHF} = 0.131 \times 8.444 \times 10^5 $$ $$ \boxed{q''_{CHF} \approx 1.106 \times 10^6 \text{ W/m}^2 = 1.106 \text{ MW/m}^2} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This value matches the accepted experimental CHF for water at 1 atm ($1.25 \text{ MW/m}^2 \pm 15\%$). The small discrepancy is due to surface condition and pressure effects. ### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — All CHF Correlations | Correlation | Numerical CHF (Water, 1 atm) | Deviation from Experiment | |---|---|---| | Zuber (1959) | 1.106 MW/m² | $-11.5\%$ | | Lienhard-Witte (1985) | 1.258 MW/m² | $+0.6\%$ | | Berenson (1961) | 1.520 MW/m² | $+21.6\%$ | **Conclusion:** Lienhard-Witte is the most accurate for water at moderate pressures; Zuber is more conservative and preferred in safety-critical design. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Boiling Curve and CHF Location (Nukiyama Curve) The Nukiyama curve is the **foundational schematic** for understanding CHF. The following block-level functional architecture maps the entire boiling regime with CHF as the central feature. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph RegionI["REGION I — Natural Convection (Below Onset of Boiling)"] NC["q'' low, T_wall < T_sat
Single-phase liquid convection
h increases slowly with T_wall"] end subgraph RegionII["REGION II — Nucleate Boiling (ONB to CHF)"] NB1["Subcooled Nucleate Boiling
Bubbles condense in subcooled liquid"] NB2["Saturated Nucleate Boiling
q'' rises rapidly, T_wall ≈ const"] CR["CRITICAL HEAT FLUX (CHF)
Maximum q'', point of crisis"] end subgraph RegionIII["REGION III — Transition Boiling (UNSTABLE)"] TB["Boiling Hysteresis Region
q'' DECREASES as T_wall rises
Vapor blankets form intermittently"] end subgraph RegionIV["REGION IV — Film Boiling (Stable)"] FB1["Minimum Heat Flux (Leidenfrost Point)"] FB2["Stable Film Boiling
Vapor film fully insulates surface"] end NC --> NB1 NB1 --> NB2 NB2 --> CR CR -->|"CRITICAL:Boiling Crisis"| TB TB --> FB1 FB1 --> FB2 style CR fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff style RegionII fill:#fff5e6,stroke:#e67e22 style RegionIII fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#c0392b ``` ### 4.2 Physical Mechanism of CHF — Hydrodynamic Instability ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Stage1["Stage 1: Bubble Formation (q'' < CHF)"] A1["Heated Surface
T_wall > T_sat"] A2["Discrete Bubble Nucleation
at Active Cavities"] A3["Buoyancy-Driven Bubble
Departure & Rise"] A1 --> A2 --> A3 end subgraph Stage2["Stage 2: Vapor Column Formation (q'' approaching CHF)"] B1["Vapor Jet Coalescence
Bubbles merge into column"] B2["Vapor Column Above Heater
Velocity u ~ sqrt(g δ Δρ/ρ_v)"] B1 --> B2 end subgraph Stage3["Stage 3: CHF — Helmholtz/Taylor Instability"] C1["Critical Wavelength λ_c
= 2π√3 √[σ/g(ρ_l-ρ_v)]"] C2["Interface becomes UNSTABLE
Vapor blanket detaches"] C3["TRANSITION BOILING BEGINS
h drops, T_wall rises sharply"] C1 --> C2 --> C3 end Stage1 --> Stage2 --> Stage3 style C3 fill:#ff4444,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style C2 fill:#ffaa44,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.3 Decision Tree for Selecting the Correct CHF Correlation ```mermaid flowchart TB Q["Given: Boiling on a Heated Surface
Saturation Properties Provided"] Q --> Q1{"Heater Geometry?"} Q1 -->|"Large Flat Plate"| Q2{"L* = L√[g(ρ_l-ρ_v)/σ]?"} Q1 -->|"Horizontal Cylinder"| QCY["Use Rohsenow Cylinder Correlation
q''_CHF = 0.131 h_fg ρ_v [σg(ρ_l-ρ_v)/ρ_v²]^(1/4)
× [1 + σ/R²g(ρ_l-ρ_v)]^(1/4)"] Q1 -->|"Vertical Plate"| QVT["Use Kutateladze Correlation
q''_CHF = 0.16 h_fg ρ_v^(1/2)
× [σg(ρ_l-ρ_v)/ρ_l]^(1/4)"] Q2 -->|"L* ≥ 9.1"| Q3["Apply ZUBER or LIENHARD-WITTE
q''_CHF = C_cr h_fg ρ_v [σg(ρ_l-ρ_v)/ρ_v²]^(1/4)
C_cr = 0.131 (Zuber) or 0.149 (L-W)"] Q2 -->|"L* < 9.1"| Q4["Apply LIENHARD-GALLEGO Correction
q''_CHF,small = q''_CHF,large × [1/(1+1/L*)]^(1/2)"] style Q3 fill:#90ee90 style Q4 fill:#ffeb99 style QCY fill:#87ceeb style QVT fill:#dda0dd ``` ### 4.4 Effect of Pressure on CHF — Conceptual Map ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["Pressure ↑ (P_sat increases)"] P1 --> P2["T_sat ↑"] P2 --> P3["ρ_v ↑↑ (Exponentially)"] P2 --> P4["σ ↓"] P2 --> P5["h_fg ↓"] P3 --> P6["q''_CHF ↓↓↓ (Strongly)"] P4 --> P6 P5 --> P6 P6 --> P7["CHF drops from ~1.25 MW/m²
at 1 atm to ~0.4 MW/m² at 100 atm"] style P1 fill:#ffe4b5 style P7 fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question A1:** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]` **Define Critical Heat Flux (CHF) in nucleate pool boiling. Why does the heat transfer coefficient drop suddenly after CHF?** **Model Answer:** > **Critical Heat Flux (CHF)** is the maximum heat flux that can be transferred from a heater surface to a saturated liquid pool during nucleate boiling, beyond which the boiling regime transitions from efficient nucleate boiling to film boiling. It is the peak of the Nukiyama boiling curve. > At CHF, the discrete vapor bubbles **coalesce** into a continuous vapor blanket that covers the heater surface. Since the thermal conductivity of vapor ($\sim 0.025$ W/m·K) is roughly **20 times lower** than that of liquid water ($\sim 0.6$ W/m·K), the heat transfer coefficient drops drastically. Simultaneously, the surface temperature **rises sharply** to compensate for the reduced heat transfer, often leading to **burnout** of the heater. **[3 Marks — KTU Mark Distribution: Definition 1M, Mechanism 1.5M, Consequence 0.5M]** #### **Question A2:** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]` **State Zuber correlation for CHF in pool boiling. What is the physical basis (theory) on which it is derived?** **Model Answer:** > **Zuber Correlation (1959):** > $$q''_{CHF} = 0.131 \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \rho_v \left[\frac{\sigma g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^{\,2}}\right]^{1/4}$$ > **Physical Basis:** Zuber derived the correlation from the **Hydrodynamic Instability Theory** (Taylor-Helmholtz instability). He modeled the vapor column rising above the heater as a fluid layer and analyzed the stability of the vapor-liquid interface. The CHF occurs at the most rapidly growing instability wavelength $\lambda_c = 2\pi\sqrt{3}\sqrt{\sigma/[g(\rho_l - \rho_v)]}$, beyond which the vapor blanket detaches from the heater. **[3 Marks — KTU Mark Distribution: Equation 1.5M, Theory explanation 1.5M]** --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Always** check the characteristic length $L^*$ before applying any CHF correlation. Using the "large surface" formula for $L^* < 9.1$ is the **#1 mistake** in KTU exams and costs **2–3 marks**. > 2. **Always** verify the consistency of units. $h_{fg}$ must be in J/kg, $\rho_v$ in kg/m³, $\sigma$ in N/m, $g$ in m/s². > 3. **Always** specify which correlation you are using and its validity. Examiners allocate marks for **justification of formula selection**. > 4. Do **not** confuse the CHF correlation with the **Nucleate Boiling Heat Transfer** correlation (Rohsenow, Forster-Zuber). They are different topics. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 3]`** **(a) Derive the Zuber correlation for critical heat flux in nucleate pool boiling on a large horizontal flat plate. Explain the underlying hydrodynamic instability theory. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Physical Setup** Consider a saturated liquid at temperature $T_{sat}$ boiling on a large horizontal flat plate of characteristic length $L$. As bubbles rise and coalesce, a vapor column of thickness $\delta$ forms above the plate. **Step 2: Buoyancy-Dominated Vapor Velocity** The vapor rises with characteristic velocity from a buoyancy-inertia balance: $$ g(\rho_l - \rho_v) \sim \rho_v \frac{u^2}{\delta} \implies u = \sqrt{\frac{g \delta (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v}} $$ **Step 3: Vapor Mass Flux and Heat Flux** The vapor mass flux at CHF is $\dot{m}''_v = \rho_v u$, and the heat flux is: $$ q''_{CHF} = h_{fg} \cdot \rho_v u = h_{fg} \sqrt{g \delta \rho_v (\rho_l - \rho_v)} $$ **Step 4: Most Unstable Wavelength (Taylor Instability)** The vapor-liquid interface is unstable to perturbations. The most rapidly growing wavelength is: $$ \lambda_c = 2\pi\sqrt{3} \left[\frac{\sigma}{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}\right]^{1/2} $$ The vapor layer thickness $\delta$ scales as $\lambda_c / (2\pi)$. **Step 5: Substitution and Final Result** $$ \delta \sim \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot 2\pi\sqrt{3} \left[\frac{\sigma}{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}\right]^{1/2} = \sqrt{3} \left[\frac{\sigma}{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}\right]^{1/2} $$ Substituting into the $q''_{CHF}$ expression: $$ q''_{CHF} = h_{fg} \sqrt{g \rho_v (\rho_l - \rho_v) \cdot \sqrt{3} \left[\frac{\sigma}{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}\right]^{1/2}} $$ After dimensional simplification, the constant from stability analysis gives: $$ \boxed{q''_{CHF} = 0.131 \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \rho_v \left[\frac{\sigma g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^{\,2}}\right]^{1/4}} $$ **Validity:** Large flat plate with $L^* \geq 9.1$. **[Marks: Setup 1M, Velocity 1.5M, Wavelength 1.5M, Substitution 1.5M, Final equation 1M, Validity 0.5M]** --- **(b) Saturated water at atmospheric pressure (1 atm) boils on a 50 mm × 50 mm horizontal flat plate. Given the following properties at 100°C: $h_{fg} = 2257$ kJ/kg, $\rho_v = 0.5956$ kg/m³, $\rho_l = 957.9$ kg/m³, $\sigma = 0.0589$ N/m, calculate the critical heat flux using the Zuber correlation. Verify whether the large-surface assumption is valid. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Verify the Large-Surface Assumption** Characteristic length $L = 50$ mm $= 0.050$ m. $$ L^* = L \sqrt{\frac{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\sigma}} = 0.050 \sqrt{\frac{9.81 \times (957.9 - 0.5956)}{0.0589}} $$ $$ L^* = 0.050 \sqrt{\frac{9.81 \times 957.3}{0.0589}} = 0.050 \sqrt{159{,}401} = 0.050 \times 399.25 = 19.96 $$ Since $L^* = 19.96 \gg 9.1$, the large-surface Zuber correlation is **valid**. **Step 2: Evaluate the Zuber Correlation** $$ q''_{CHF} = 0.131 \times h_{fg} \times \rho_v \left[\frac{\sigma g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^{\,2}}\right]^{1/4} $$ $$ = 0.131 \times 2257 \times 10^3 \times 0.5956 \times \left[\frac{0.0589 \times 9.81 \times 957.3}{(0.5956)^2}\right]^{1/4} $$ **Step 3: Compute the Bracket** $$ \frac{0.0589 \times 9.81 \times 957.3}{(0.5956)^2} = \frac{553.10}{0.3547} = 1559.2 $$ $$ (1559.2)^{1/4} = 6.284 $$ **Step 4: Final Result** $$ q''_{CHF} = 0.131 \times 2.257 \times 10^6 \times 0.5956 \times 6.284 $$ $$ \boxed{q''_{CHF} = 1.106 \times 10^6 \text{ W/m}^2 = 1.106 \text{ MW/m}^2} $$ **[Marks: $L^*$ calculation 2M, Bracket evaluation 2M, Final answer 2M, Validity conclusion 1M]** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]`** **(a) A horizontal cylindrical heater of radius $R = 5$ mm is immersed in saturated water at 100°C. Using the Rohsenow correlation for a horizontal cylinder, determine the CHF. Given: $h_{fg} = 2257$ kJ/kg, $\rho_v = 0.5956$ kg/m³, $\rho_l = 957.9$ kg/m³, $\sigma = 0.0589$ N/m. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Rohsenow Correlation for Horizontal Cylinder** $$ q''_{CHF,cyl} = q''_{CHF,\infty} \cdot \left[1 + \frac{\sigma}{R^2 g (\rho_l - \rho_v)}\right]^{1/4} $$ where $q''_{CHF,\infty} = 0.131 \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \rho_v \left[\dfrac{\sigma g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^{\,2}}\right]^{1/4}$ is the large-flat-plate limit (Zuber). **Step 2: Compute the Flat-Plate Limit** From Section 3.3, $q''_{CHF,\infty} = 1.106 \text{ MW/m}^2$ for water at 1 atm. **Step 3: Compute the Cylinder Correction Factor** $$ \left[1 + \frac{\sigma}{R^2 g (\rho_l - \rho_v)}\right]^{1/4} = \left[1 + \frac{0.0589}{(0.005)^2 \times 9.81 \times 957.3}\right]^{1/4} $$ $$ = \left[1 + \frac{0.0589}{0.2349}\right]^{1/4} = [1 + 0.2507]^{1/4} = (1.2507)^{1/4} $$ $$ (1.2507)^{1/4} = 1.0579 $$ **Step 4: Final Result** $$ q''_{CHF,cyl} = 1.106 \times 1.0579 = 1.170 \text{ MW/m}^2 $$ $$ \boxed{q''_{CHF,cyl} = 1.170 \text{ MW/m}^2} $$ The CHF on a small cylinder is **5.8% higher** than on a flat plate, because the curved surface allows lateral vapor escape. **[Marks: Correlation 1.5M, Flat-plate value 1.5M, Correction factor 2.5M, Final answer 1.5M]** --- **(b) A small flat heater of characteristic length $L = 3$ mm is used for nucleate pool boiling of water at 100°C. Calculate the CHF using the Lienhard-Gallego correction. Compare it with the large-plate Zuber value and comment. Given: same properties as above. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Compute the Non-Dimensional Length $L^*$** $$ L^* = L \sqrt{\frac{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\sigma}} = 0.003 \times 399.25 = 1.198 $$ Since $L^* = 1.198 < 9.1$, the small-surface correction **must be applied**. **Step 2: Compute the Correction Factor** $$ f = \left[\frac{1}{1 + 1/L^*}\right]^{1/2} = \left[\frac{1}{1 + 1/1.198}\right]^{1/2} $$ $$ = \left[\frac{1}{1 + 0.835}\right]^{1/2} = \left[\frac{1}{1.835}\right]^{1/2} = (0.5449)^{1/2} = 0.7381 $$ **Step 3: Apply the Correction** $$ q''_{CHF,small} = q''_{CHF,large} \times f = 1.106 \times 0.7381 $$ $$ \boxed{q''_{CHF,small} = 0.816 \text{ MW/m}^2} $$ **Step 4: Comment** The CHF on the small heater is **26.2% lower** than on the large plate. This is because for small heaters, the vapor blanket can form more easily due to limited lateral escape, leading to premature boiling crisis. This has critical implications for **microelectronic cooling** and **MEMS devices**, where CHF is significantly reduced. **[Marks: $L^*$ 1.5M, Correction factor 2.5M, Final answer 1.5M, Comment 1.5M]** --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 3 (CHF in Nucleate Pool Boiling):** - **Definition:** CHF = peak heat flux at which nucleate boiling transitions to film boiling. - **Nukiyama Curve:** CHF is the **peak point** between nucleate boiling and transition boiling. - **Physical Mechanism:** Hydrodynamic (Taylor-Helmholtz) instability of the rising vapor column. - **Most Unstable Wavelength:** $\lambda_c = 2\pi\sqrt{3}\sqrt{\sigma/[g(\rho_l - \rho_v)]}$. - **Zuber (1959):** $q''_{CHF} = 0.131 \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \rho_v \left[\dfrac{\sigma g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^{\,2}}\right]^{1/4}$ — for large flat plates ($L^* \geq 9.1$). - **Lienhard-Witte (1985):** Same form with constant 0.149 — better accuracy. - **Berenson (1961):** Same form with constant 0.18 — for large plates, slightly overpredicts. - **Kutateladze (1951):** $q''_{CHF} = 0.16 \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \rho_v^{1/2} \left[\dfrac{\sigma g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_l}\right]^{1/4}$ — for vertical surfaces. - **Small-Surface (Lienhard-Gallego):** Multiply large-plate CHF by $\left[1/(1+1/L^*)\right]^{1/2}$ when $L^* < 9.1$. - **Characteristic Length:** $L^* = L\sqrt{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)/\sigma}$. - **Horizontal Cylinder (Rohsenow):** $q''_{CHF,cyl} = q''_{CHF,\infty}\left[1 + \sigma/(R^2 g(\rho_l - \rho_v))\right]^{1/4}$. - **Pressure Effect:** $q''_{CHF}$ **decreases** as pressure increases (because $\rho_v$ rises exponentially). - **Typical Value:** $q''_{CHF} \approx 1.25$ MW/m² for water at 1 atm on a large plate. - **Engineering Critical Applications:** Nuclear reactor fuel rods, rocket engine cooling, high-power electronics. - **Burnout:** Exceeding CHF leads to catastrophic heater failure due to vapor film insulation. - **Mark-Saver:** Always **mention validity** of the correlation used — examiners award marks for this. - **Common Mistake:** Confusing CHF correlation (this topic) with nucleate boiling heat transfer correlation (Rohsenow, Forster-Zuber) — they are different!

topics.title: Condensation: Drop and Film Type Condensation – Nusselt Equation to Determine the Heat Transfer Coefficient in Film Condensation (No Derivation)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Condensation: Drop and Film Type Condensation – Nusselt Equation to Determine the Heat Transfer Coefficient in Film Condensation (No Derivation)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 : Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Condensation?

**Condensation** is the process by which a vapour changes its phase to a liquid when its temperature is reduced below its saturation temperature corresponding to its partial pressure. It is the reverse of vaporisation and is a fundamental mode of **latent heat transfer** encountered in condensers, distillation columns, refrigeration systems, and power-plant steam cycles.

From a thermodynamic standpoint, when a vapour comes in contact with a surface whose temperature $T_s$ is lower than the saturation temperature $T_{sat}$ of the vapour, the vapour loses its latent heat of vaporisation $h_{fg}$ to the cooler surface and condenses. The energy released per unit mass is exactly the **latent heat of condensation**, which numerically equals the latent heat of vaporisation at the same saturation pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standard):**
> Condensation is the process of change of phase from vapour to liquid, accompanied by the removal of latent heat. When a saturated or superheated vapour contacts a surface whose temperature is below the saturation temperature of the vapour, condensation occurs and the liberated latent heat is transferred to the surface.

---

## 1.2 The Two Physical Modes of Condensation

Condensation on a cold surface is **not** a single uniform phenomenon. Depending on the wettability of the surface by the condensate liquid, condensation occurs in one of two fundamentally distinct modes:

### (a) Film-Wise (Film-Type) Condensation
- A continuous, unbroken liquid film covers the entire cooling surface.
- The condensate **wets** the surface (contact angle $\theta < 90^\circ$).
- Heat must first conduct through this liquid film before reaching the wall — the film itself acts as a **thermal resistance barrier**.
- Heat transfer coefficients are comparatively **lower**.
- This is the **most common** mode in industrial practice on clean metallic surfaces (copper, brass, steel, stainless steel).

### (b) Drop-Wise (Drop-Type) Condensation
- The condensate forms **discrete, tiny droplets** instead of a continuous film.
- The surface is **non-wettable** (contact angle $\theta > 90^\circ$); the droplets coalesce, grow, and roll/slide off under gravity, exposing fresh surface.
- Heat transfer coefficients can be **5 to 10 times higher** than film condensation.
- Difficult to maintain in practice; usually requires surface treatment with hydrophobic promoters such as **stearic acid, oleic acid, or PTFE coatings**.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Chilled Beverage Glass:**
> Take two identical glass tumblers straight out of a freezer. Leave them on a humid afternoon table. Within seconds, you will see water droplets fogging the outer surface.
>
> - **Glass A (clean, untreated):** A clear, uniform water film coats the glass. The film thickens at the bottom and slowly drips. This is **film condensation**.
> - **Glass B (rubbed with a thin coat of coconut oil — hydrophobic):** You see distinct scattered beads of water that rapidly merge into large drops that quickly slide down, exposing dry surface momentarily. This is **drop-wise condensation**.
>
> Glass B loses heat faster because droplets keep exposing fresh cold surface. This is precisely why drop-wise condensation gives **higher heat transfer coefficients** but is hard to sustain.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Properties

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Units (SI) | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Latent heat of condensation | $h_{fg}$ | $\text{J/kg}$ | $2.26 \times 10^{6}$ (water at 100 °C) |
| Thermal conductivity of condensate | $k_\ell$ | $\text{W/(m·K)}$ | $0.6$ – $0.68$ (water film) |
| Density of condensate | $\rho_\ell$ | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | $\approx 960$ (water at 100 °C) |
| Dynamic viscosity of condensate | $\mu_\ell$ | $\text{Pa·s}$ or $\text{kg/(m·s)}$ | $2.82 \times 10^{-4}$ (water at 100 °C) |
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | $\text{m/s}^2$ | $9.81$ |
| Surface tension | $\sigma$ | $\text{N/m}$ | $\approx 0.059$ (water at 100 °C) |

All liquid-film thermo-physical properties ($\rho_\ell$, $k_\ell$, $\mu_\ell$) are evaluated at the **mean film temperature** defined as:

$$
T_f = \frac{T_{sat} + T_s}{2}
$$

except $h_{fg}$, which is evaluated at $T_{sat}$ (with a small correction factor sometimes applied for sensible subcooling of the liquid — see advanced corrections).

---

## 1.5 Industrial Significance

Condensation heat transfer is the *workhorse* mechanism in:

- **Steam surface condensers** in thermal power plants (turbine exhaust).
- **Refrigeration and air-conditioning condensers** (R-134a, R-717, R-410A).
- **Petrochemical distillation column overhead condensers**.
- **Cryogenic LNG vapor recovery**.
- **Waste-heat recovery boilers and process vapour reboilers**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In nearly **all** of these industrial hardware, designers use **Nusselt's film condensation theory** because film-wise condensation is the *default, guaranteed* mode in service, even when drop-wise was initially intended.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 : Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Film Condensation — Nusselt's Physical Picture

In 1916, **Wilhelm Nusselt** (a German engineer) published the first rigorous analytical model of laminar film condensation on a vertical plate. His theory treats the condensate as a thin, smooth, laminar liquid film flowing under gravity along the cold surface, with the following **physical reality**:

1. Saturated vapour approaches the cold surface and condenses on the outer face of the liquid film.
2. As the condensate flows downward under gravity, **more** vapour condenses onto the free surface, so the film thickness $\delta(x)$ **grows** with distance $x$ from the leading edge (top of the plate).
3. The wall heat flux is governed by **conduction through the liquid film**:

$$
q''_{wall} = \frac{k_\ell \, \Delta T}{\delta(x)}
$$

4. Since $\delta$ increases with $x$, the **local** heat transfer coefficient $h_x$ **decreases** with $x$. Hence the **average** coefficient is the appropriate design parameter.

---

## 2.2 Nusselt's Underlying Assumptions (Mandatory for KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> These assumptions are *favourite KTU two-mark question territory*. Memorise verbatim.

1. **Pure, saturated vapour** at uniform temperature $T_{sat}$ far from the surface.
2. **Laminar, smooth, and continuous** condensate film.
3. The **liquid film is the only thermal resistance** (vapour side offers negligible resistance).
4. The **wall temperature** $T_s$ is **uniform** along the surface (i.e., wall thermal resistance is negligible compared to film resistance, or $T_s$ is maintained by external means).
5. The **vapour velocity** normal to the surface is negligible (no shear-driven thinning).
6. **Constant fluid properties** throughout the film, evaluated at $T_f$.
7. The **rate of condensation** is governed by conduction across the film (no convection inside the film; gravity drives the flow as a falling film).
8. The film is **thin**, and the curvature effects of the free surface are negligible (valid for $\delta \ll L$).
9. **Inertia and energy convection** in the film are negligible compared with conduction and viscous/gravity terms (lubrication theory approximation).

---

## 2.3 Master Equations — Nusselt's Film Condensation Formulae (NO DERIVATION REQUIRED FOR KTU)

### (A) **Vertical Plate** — Average Heat Transfer Coefficient (Laminar Film)

$$
\boxed{\; \bar{h}_L \;=\; 0.943 \left[ \dfrac{\rho_\ell^{\,2} \, g \, h_{fg} \, k_\ell^{\,3}}{\mu_\ell \, \Delta T \, L} \right]^{1/4} \;}
$$

where $\Delta T = T_{sat} - T_s$, and $L$ is the total height of the plate.

### (B) **Vertical Plate** — Local Heat Transfer Coefficient at Distance $x$ from Top

$$
h_x \;=\; 0.707 \left[ \dfrac{\rho_\ell^{\,2} \, g \, h_{fg} \, k_\ell^{\,3}}{\mu_\ell \, \Delta T \, x} \right]^{1/4}
$$

Relation between local and average:

$$
\bar{h}_L \;=\; \tfrac{4}{3} \, h_x \;=\; \tfrac{4}{3} \, h_{x=L}
$$

### (C) **Single Horizontal Tube** (Outside Surface) — Average Heat Transfer Coefficient

$$
\boxed{\; \bar{h}_D \;=\; 0.725 \left[ \dfrac{\rho_\ell^{\,2} \, g \, h_{fg} \, k_\ell^{\,3}}{\mu_\ell \, \Delta T \, D} \right]^{1/4} \;}
$$

where $D$ is the outside diameter of the horizontal tube.

### (D) **Vertical Tier of $N$ Horizontal Tubes** (Stacked One Above the Other)

$$
h_N \;=\; h_1 \, N^{-1/4}
$$

Equivalently, treating $L = N \cdot D$ as effective vertical length in the vertical-plate equation, the same result is obtained. Each successive tube sees a thicker film, so $h$ decreases weakly as $N^{-1/4}$.

---

## 2.4 Condensate Reynolds Number — Regimen Boundary

The condensate Reynolds number is defined based on the **mass flow rate per unit width** $\Gamma$ (kg/s per metre of width):

$$
\Gamma \;=\; \dfrac{\dot{m}}{W} \quad (\text{for vertical plate, W = width perpendicular to flow})
$$

$$
\boxed{\; Re \;=\; \dfrac{4 \Gamma}{\mu_\ell} \;=\; \dfrac{4 \dot{m}}{\mu_\ell \, W} \;}
$$

Regime boundaries (universally tested in KTU numericals):

| Regime | Reynolds Number | Governing Equation |
|---|---|---|
| **Laminar** | $Re < 1800$ | Nusselt equation (valid) |
| **Wavy / Transition** | $1800 < Re < 3600$ | Nusselt equation with caution |
| **Turbulent** | $Re > 3600$ | **Kirkbride's correlation** recommended |

**Kirkbride's Turbulent Film Correlation** (for vertical surface):

$$
h \left( \dfrac{\mu_\ell^{\,2}}{k_\ell^{\,3} \rho_\ell^{\,2} g} \right)^{1/3} \;=\; \dfrac{Re}{1.08 \, Re^{1.22} - 5.2}
$$

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Master Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All quantities $\rho_\ell$, $k_\ell$, $\mu_\ell$ are evaluated at the **mean film temperature** $T_f = (T_{sat} + T_s)/2$. The latent heat $h_{fg}$ is evaluated at $T_{sat}$.

| # | Scenario | Governing Equation | Key Variables | Critical Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Vertical plate — average $h$ | $\bar{h}_L = 0.943 \left[ \rho_\ell^{2} g h_{fg} k_\ell^{3} / (\mu_\ell \Delta T L) \right]^{1/4}$ | $L$ = plate height | Laminar film, $Re < 1800$ |
| 2 | Vertical plate — local $h$ at $x$ | $h_x = 0.707 \left[ \rho_\ell^{2} g h_{fg} k_\ell^{3} / (\mu_\ell \Delta T x) \right]^{1/4}$ | $x$ = distance from top edge | Same as #1 |
| 3 | Relation between local & average | $\bar{h}_L = \tfrac{4}{3} h_{x=L}$ | — | — |
| 4 | Single horizontal tube | $\bar{h}_D = 0.725 \left[ \rho_\ell^{2} g h_{fg} k_\ell^{3} / (\mu_\ell \Delta T D) \right]^{1/4}$ | $D$ = O.D. of tube | — |
| 5 | $N$ horizontal tubes (vertical stack) | $h_N = h_1 \, N^{-1/4}$ | $h_1$ = single tube value | — |
| 6 | Condensate Reynolds number | $Re = 4 \Gamma / \mu_\ell$ | $\Gamma$ = mass per unit width | Laminar limit: $Re < 1800$ |
| 7 | Total heat transfer rate | $Q = \bar{h} \, A \, \Delta T$ | $A$ = total surface area | — |
| 8 | Mean film temperature | $T_f = (T_{sat} + T_s)/2$ | Used for property eval | — |
| 9 | Modified latent heat (subcooling corrn) | $h_{fg}^{\,'} = h_{fg} + 0.68 \, c_{p,\ell} \, \Delta T$ | Optional correction | For higher accuracy |

> **Engineering Utility:** Nusselt's equation is the *backbone of every condenser design calculation*. Without it, sizing of tube bundles in power plant condensers and process plant overhead condensers would be impossible. The $N^{-1/4}$ correction is what prevents over-design when multiple tubes are stacked vertically in a shell-and-tube condenser.

---

## 2.6 Comparison — Drop-Wise vs. Film-Wise Condensation

| Feature | Drop-Wise | Film-Wise |
|---|---|---|
| Surface wetting | Non-wetting ($\theta > 90^\circ$) | Wetting ($\theta < 90^\circ$) |
| Liquid distribution | Discrete droplets | Continuous film |
| Heat transfer coefficient | **High** ($h_{drop} \sim 5$–$10 \, h_{film}$) | **Lower** (governed by Nusselt) |
| Stability | Difficult to maintain | Stable, predictable |
| Industrial prevalence | Rare | **Dominant** |
| Typical $h$ value (water at $\Delta T \approx 10$ K) | $\sim 50{,}000$–$100{,}000 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$ | $\sim 5{,}000$–$10{,}000 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$ |
| Heat transfer mechanism | Direct droplet-surface conduction + latent release | Conduction through stagnant liquid film |
| Design basis used | Empirical (rarely in practice) | **Nusselt's analytical equation** |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 : Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly mandates **"No Derivation"** for this topic. However, for engineering clarity and partial-mark protection, the **logical flow** of Nusselt's solution is presented below as a structured walk-through. The full mathematical derivation has been **omitted** to comply with the syllabus directive — but the boundary/initial conditions and key result-checks are shown in full.

## 3.1 Outline of Nusselt's Analytical Flow (Logical Map, Not Full Derivation)

### Stage 1 — Geometry & Coordinate System
- Vertical plate of height $L$ and width $W$.
- $x$-axis measured **downward** from the leading (top) edge.
- Film thickness $\delta(x)$ at any $x$ is small; film is laminar, smooth, and gravity-driven.

### Stage 2 — Momentum Balance (Thin Falling Film)
For a fully developed laminar falling film (zero pressure gradient, gravity as body force), the velocity profile is **half-parabolic** (Hagen–Poiseuille analogue):

$$
u(y) \;=\; \dfrac{\rho_\ell \, g}{\mu_\ell} \, \delta^{\,2}(x) \left[ \dfrac{y}{\delta(x)} \;-\; \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{y}{\delta(x)}\right)^{\!2} \right]
$$

with **no-slip at wall** ($u=0$ at $y=0$) and **zero shear at free surface** ($\partial u/\partial y = 0$ at $y=\delta$).

### Stage 3 — Local Mass Flow Rate Per Unit Width

$$
\Gamma(x) \;=\; \int_{0}^{\delta(x)} \rho_\ell \, u(y) \, dy
$$

Carrying out the integration (using the parabolic profile above) yields the celebrated **one-third power law** for film thickness:

$$
\delta(x) \;=\; \left[ \dfrac{4 \mu_\ell \, \Gamma(x)}{\rho_\ell^{\,2} \, g} \right]^{1/3}
$$

### Stage 4 — Energy Balance (Conduction Through Film)
The wall heat flux at position $x$ is conduction across the film:

$$
q''(x) \;=\; \dfrac{k_\ell \, \Delta T}{\delta(x)}
$$

From an energy balance on an elemental film slice $\mathrm{d}x$, the local condensation rate is:

$$
h_{fg} \, \dfrac{\mathrm{d}\Gamma}{\mathrm{d}x} \;=\; q''(x) \;=\; \dfrac{k_\ell \, \Delta T}{\delta(x)}
$$

### Stage 5 — Solve for $\delta(x)$ and $\Gamma(x)$
Substituting the $\Gamma$–$\delta$ relation into the energy equation and integrating with **boundary condition** $\delta(0) = 0$ at the leading edge:

$$
\delta(x) \;=\; \left[ \dfrac{4 \mu_\ell \, k_\ell \, \Delta T \, x}{\rho_\ell^{\,2} \, g \, h_{fg}} \right]^{1/4}
$$

$$
\Gamma(x) \;=\; \left[ \dfrac{\rho_\ell^{\,2} \, g \, h_{fg} \, k_\ell^{\,3} \, \Delta T^{\,3} \, x}{4 \mu_\ell} \right]^{1/4}
$$

### Stage 6 — Local Heat Transfer Coefficient
By definition, $h_x = q''(x)/\Delta T = k_\ell/\delta(x)$. Therefore:

$$
h_x \;=\; \left[ \dfrac{\rho_\ell^{\,2} \, g \, h_{fg} \, k_\ell^{\,3}}{4 \mu_\ell \, \Delta T \, x} \right]^{1/4} \;\approx\; 0.707 \left[ \dfrac{\rho_\ell^{\,2} \, g \, h_{fg} \, k_\ell^{\,3}}{\mu_\ell \, \Delta T \, x} \right]^{1/4}
$$

### Stage 7 — Average Heat Transfer Coefficient
Integrating over the plate length $L$:

$$
\bar{h}_L \;=\; \dfrac{1}{L} \int_{0}^{L} h_x \, \mathrm{d}x \;=\; \tfrac{4}{3} \, h_{x=L}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\; \bar{h}_L \;=\; 0.943 \left[ \dfrac{\rho_\ell^{\,2} \, g \, h_{fg} \, k_\ell^{\,3}}{\mu_\ell \, \Delta T \, L} \right]^{1/4} \;}
$$

This matches the master equation from §2.3.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Solved to Validate the Formula)

**Problem Statement [KTU University Exam - July 2022 Type]:**
Saturated steam at atmospheric pressure ($T_{sat} = 100 ^\circ\text{C}$) condenses on a vertical plate of height $L = 0.5 \text{ m}$ and width $W = 0.3 \text{ m}$. The plate surface temperature is $T_s = 80 ^\circ\text{C}$. Estimate:
1. The average heat transfer coefficient $\bar{h}_L$.
2. The total condensation rate $\dot{m}$.
3. The condensate Reynolds number at the bottom of the plate.

**Fluid Properties of Water at Mean Film Temperature $T_f = (100+80)/2 = 90 ^\circ\text{C}$:**

- $\rho_\ell = 965.3 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\mu_\ell = 3.15 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/(m·s)}$
- $k_\ell = 0.675 \text{ W/(m·K)}$
- $h_{fg} = 2.257 \times 10^{6} \text{ J/kg}$ (at $100 ^\circ\text{C}$)

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** $\Delta T = T_{sat} - T_s = 100 - 80 = 20 \text{ K}$.

**Step 2:** Compute the bracketed group for $\bar{h}_L$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
X &= \dfrac{\rho_\ell^{\,2} \, g \, h_{fg} \, k_\ell^{\,3}}{\mu_\ell \, \Delta T \, L} \\[6pt]
  &= \dfrac{(965.3)^2 \times 9.81 \times (2.257 \times 10^6) \times (0.675)^3}{(3.15 \times 10^{-4}) \times 20 \times 0.5}
\end{aligned}
$$

Let us evaluate piece by piece:

$$
\rho_\ell^{\,2} = (965.3)^2 = 9.318 \times 10^{5} \text{ kg}^2/\text{m}^6
$$

$$
k_\ell^{\,3} = (0.675)^3 = 3.075 \times 10^{-1} \text{ W}^3/(\text{m}^3\text{·K}^3)
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 9.318 \times 10^{5} \times 9.81 \times 2.257 \times 10^{6} \times 3.075 \times 10^{-1}
$$

$$
= 9.318 \times 10^{5} \times 9.81 = 9.141 \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
\times \; 2.257 \times 10^{6} = 2.063 \times 10^{13}
$$

$$
\times \; 3.075 \times 10^{-1} = 6.345 \times 10^{12}
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 3.15 \times 10^{-4} \times 20 \times 0.5 = 3.15 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
X = \dfrac{6.345 \times 10^{12}}{3.15 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.015 \times 10^{15}
$$

$$
X^{1/4} = (2.015 \times 10^{15})^{1/4} \approx 2.118 \times 10^{3.75} \approx 6.71 \times 10^{3}
$$

(More precisely: $\log_{10}(2.015 \times 10^{15}) = 15.304$, divided by 4 = $3.826$, so $10^{3.826} = 6696$.)

$$
\bar{h}_L = 0.943 \times 6696 \approx 6314 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}
$$

**Step 3:** Total heat transfer rate.

$$
A = L \times W = 0.5 \times 0.3 = 0.15 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
Q = \bar{h}_L \, A \, \Delta T = 6314 \times 0.15 \times 20 = 18{,}942 \text{ W} \approx 18.94 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4:** Condensation mass rate.

$$
\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q}{h_{fg}} = \dfrac{18{,}942}{2.257 \times 10^{6}} = 8.39 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 5:** Condensate Reynolds number.

$$
\Gamma = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{W} = \dfrac{8.39 \times 10^{-3}}{0.3} = 2.80 \times 10^{-2} \text{ kg/(s·m)}
$$

$$
Re = \dfrac{4 \Gamma}{\mu_\ell} = \dfrac{4 \times 2.80 \times 10^{-2}}{3.15 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 355.5
$$

Since $Re = 355.5 \ll 1800$, the **laminar film assumption is validated** ✓.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Equation

| Physical Situation | KTU-Expected Equation | Common Mistake |
|---|---|---|
| Vertical wall / plate, laminar film | $\bar{h}_L = 0.943 \left[ \rho^2 g h_{fg} k^3 / (\mu \Delta T L) \right]^{1/4}$ | Using tube equation |
| Horizontal tube (single), laminar film | $\bar{h}_D = 0.725 \left[ \rho^2 g h_{fg} k^3 / (\mu \Delta T D) \right]^{1/4}$ | Using plate equation with $L=D$ |
| $N$ horizontal tubes stacked vertically | $h_N = h_1 N^{-1/4}$ | Forgetting the $-1/4$ correction |
| Turbulent condensate film ($Re > 3600$) | Kirkbride correlation | Continuing to use Nusselt |
| Inclined plate (angle $\phi$ from vertical) | Replace $g$ with $g \sin \phi$ in Nusselt eqn. | Forgetting the correction factor |
| Vertical plate with non-condensable gas | Nusselt no longer valid — must use Dehbi / correlations | Blindly applying Nusselt |

# SECTION 4 : Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Condensation Mode Comparison — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Steam at T_sat contacts surface] --> B{Is the surface wettable?}
    B -- Yes theta less than 90 deg --> C[Film-wise condensation]
    B -- No theta greater than 90 deg --> D[Drop-wise condensation]
    C --> E[Continuous liquid film forms]
    E --> F[Film thickens downward due to gravity]
    F --> G[Heat must conduct through film]
    G --> H[Lower heat transfer coefficient]
    H --> I[Use Nusselt equation]
    D --> J[Discrete droplets nucleate]
    J --> K[Droplets grow and coalesce]
    K --> L[Drops roll off, exposing fresh surface]
    L --> M[Much higher heat transfer coefficient]
    M --> N[Empirical correlations only]
```

## 4.2 Falling-Film Condensation on a Vertical Plate — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Top edge x = 0] --> P2[Film thickness delta of x is zero]
    P2 --> P3[Vapour condenses on free surface]
    P3 --> P4[Gravity drives film downward]
    P4 --> P5[Film thickness delta grows as x to power 1/4]
    P5 --> P6[Local h_x decreases as x to power -1/4]
    P6 --> P7[Bottom edge x = L]
    P7 --> P8[Compute average h_L equals 4/3 times h_x at L]
    P8 --> P9[Validate Re less than 1800 laminar]
    P9 --> P10[Compute total condensation rate m dot]
```

## 4.3 Condensation Calculation Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Given T_sat, T_s, geometry, fluid] --> S2{Geometry?}
    S2 -- Vertical plate --> S3[Use Nusselt vertical plate eqn]
    S2 -- Horizontal tube single --> S4[Use horizontal tube eqn]
    S2 -- N horizontal tubes --> S5[Compute h_1 then apply N power -1/4]
    S3 --> S6[Evaluate properties at T_f]
    S4 --> S6
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7[Compute Delta T and h]
    S7 --> S8[Compute m dot and Q]
    S8 --> S9[Compute Re]
    S9 --> S10{Re less than 1800?}
    S10 -- Yes --> S11[Laminar valid - DONE]
    S10 -- No --> S12[Use Kirkbride turbulent correlation]
```

## 4.4 Film Thickness Profile (Schematic Representation)

```
     x = 0  (top)
     |
     |   delta(x) grows as  x^(1/4)
     |   h_x  drops as  x^(-1/4)
     v

     +----------------------------+
     | Saturated vapour (T_sat)   |
     | ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ |  <- free surface
     |                            |
     |   Laminar condensate       |  delta(x) small
     |   film falling downward    |
     |                            |
     +----------------------------+  <- cold wall at T_s
     | Solid plate                |
     +----------------------------+

   At x = L (bottom):
     delta(L) maximum
     h_local(L) minimum
     h_average = (4/3) * h_local(L)
```
