# SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between drop-wise and film-wise condensation. Mention any two factors that favour drop-wise condensation.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Film-Wise Condensation | Drop-Wise Condensation |
|---|---|---|
| Liquid behaviour | Continuous film | Discrete droplets |
| Wetting | Wetting surface ($\theta < 90^\circ$) | Non-wetting surface ($\theta > 90^\circ$) |
| Heat transfer coefficient | Lower | 5–10 times higher |
| Stability | Stable & sustained | Unstable, short-lived |

**Two factors favouring drop-wise condensation:**
1. **Non-wettable (hydrophobic) surface coating** (e.g., application of stearic acid, silicones, PTFE, or oleic acid on the condensing surface — these increase the contact angle).
2. **Low degree of surface contamination / clean surface** combined with a **high temperature gradient** between vapour and surface so that droplets form rapidly and shed quickly before coalescing into a film.

*(Any two valid factors accepted — credit for wettability argument.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - July 2023]**
**State the assumptions made by Nusselt in deriving the equation for heat transfer coefficient during film condensation on a vertical plate.**

**Model Answer:**

1. Pure saturated vapour condenses at uniform temperature $T_{sat}$.
2. The condensate forms a **laminar, smooth, continuous thin film**.
3. The **liquid film is the sole thermal resistance**; vapour offers negligible resistance.
4. Wall temperature $T_s$ is **uniform**.
5. **Inertia and convective energy transport** within the film are negligible compared with viscous and conductive effects.
6. **Constant thermo-physical properties**, evaluated at the mean film temperature $T_f = (T_{sat} + T_s)/2$.
7. The free surface of the film is at saturation temperature (no temperature gradient on vapour side).
8. Film thickness is small relative to plate height (lubrication approximation).

*(Any four assumptions — full 3 marks.)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 — Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **Nusselt mechanism of film condensation on a vertical plate**. Discuss the variation of film thickness $\delta(x)$ and local heat transfer coefficient $h_x$ with distance $x$ from the top of the plate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Saturated steam at atmospheric pressure condenses on a **vertical plate** of height $0.6 \text{ m}$ and width $0.4 \text{ m}$. The plate is maintained at $75 ^\circ\text{C}$. Estimate:
   1. The average heat transfer coefficient.
   2. The total heat transfer rate.
   3. The mass rate of condensation.
   4. The condensate Reynolds number at the bottom of the plate and comment on the flow regime.

Take liquid water properties at mean film temperature $T_f = 87.5 ^\circ\text{C}$ as: $\rho_\ell = 967 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu_\ell = 3.32 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/(m·s)}$, $k_\ell = 0.670 \text{ W/(m·K)}$, $h_{fg} = 2.299 \times 10^{6} \text{ J/kg}$ at $T_{sat} = 100 ^\circ\text{C}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)**

**Sketch Description:** Draw a vertical cold plate with saturated vapour on the right side. Show a thin liquid film whose thickness increases downward. Annotate: $x$ from top, $\delta(x)$, free surface, $T_{sat}$, $T_s$.

**Mechanism Description:**

1. Saturated vapour at $T_{sat}$ contacts the cold surface at $T_s < T_{sat}$ and condenses.
2. The condensate forms a thin, **laminar, gravity-driven** liquid film that flows downward.
3. As the film descends, **more vapour condenses** on its free surface, so the film mass $\Gamma(x)$ grows and thickness $\delta(x)$ increases.
4. From Nusselt's theory (no derivation required): $\delta(x) \propto x^{1/4}$, so the film **thickens** slowly with $x$.
5. Since heat must conduct across the film ($q'' = k_\ell \Delta T / \delta(x)$), the **local** coefficient $h_x = q''/\Delta T = k_\ell/\delta(x)$ varies as $h_x \propto x^{-1/4}$, i.e., $h_x$ **decreases** with $x$.
6. The **average** coefficient over the plate length $L$ is $\bar{h}_L = \tfrac{4}{3} h_{x=L}$.
7. Implication: longer plates give lower average $h$ — explains why condenser tubes are short and arranged horizontally in banks.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)**

**Given:** $L = 0.6 \text{ m}$, $W = 0.4 \text{ m}$, $T_{sat} = 100 ^\circ\text{C}$, $T_s = 75 ^\circ\text{C}$.

**Step 1:** $\Delta T = 100 - 75 = 25 \text{ K}$.

**Step 2:** Average $h$ from Nusselt's vertical-plate equation:

$$
\bar{h}_L = 0.943 \left[ \dfrac{\rho_\ell^{\,2} \, g \, h_{fg} \, k_\ell^{\,3}}{\mu_\ell \, \Delta T \, L} \right]^{1/4}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
X &= \dfrac{(967)^2 \times 9.81 \times 2.299 \times 10^{6} \times (0.670)^3}{(3.32 \times 10^{-4}) \times 25 \times 0.6} \\[6pt]
  &= \dfrac{(9.35 \times 10^5) \times 9.81 \times 2.299 \times 10^{6} \times 0.3008}{4.98 \times 10^{-3}} \\[6pt]
  &= \dfrac{6.349 \times 10^{12}}{4.98 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; 1.275 \times 10^{15}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
X^{1/4} = (1.275 \times 10^{15})^{1/4} \approx 5973
$$

$$
\bar{h}_L = 0.943 \times 5973 \approx 5633 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}
$$

**Step 3:** Total heat transfer:

$$
A = 0.6 \times 0.4 = 0.24 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
Q = 5633 \times 0.24 \times 25 = 33{,}798 \text{ W} \approx 33.8 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4:** Condensation rate:

$$
\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q}{h_{fg}} = \dfrac{33{,}798}{2.299 \times 10^{6}} = 0.0147 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 5:** Condensate Reynolds number at the bottom of the plate:

$$
\Gamma = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{W} = \dfrac{0.0147}{0.4} = 0.03675 \text{ kg/(s·m)}
$$

$$
Re = \dfrac{4 \Gamma}{\mu_\ell} = \dfrac{4 \times 0.03675}{3.32 \times 10^{-4}} = 442.8
$$

**Comment:** $Re = 442.8 \ll 1800$, hence the flow regime is **laminar**, validating the use of the Nusselt equation. ✓

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam - July 2024 — Module 3]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain **drop-wise condensation**. Why is it preferred over film-wise condensation? Discuss the practical difficulties in maintaining drop-wise condensation in industrial condensers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A horizontal tube of outer diameter $25 \text{ mm}$ is used to condense saturated steam at $1.013 \text{ bar}$ ($T_{sat} = 100 ^\circ\text{C}$). The tube wall temperature is maintained at $85 ^\circ\text{C}$. Calculate:
   1. The average heat transfer coefficient.
   2. The mass of steam condensed per hour per metre length of tube.

Use the following properties of water at the mean film temperature $T_f = 92.5 ^\circ\text{C}$: $\rho_\ell = 964 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu_\ell = 3.06 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/(m·s)}$, $k_\ell = 0.678 \text{ W/(m·K)}$, $h_{fg} = 2.265 \times 10^{6} \text{ J/kg}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)**

**Diagram:** Draw a cold surface showing discrete hemispherical droplets of various sizes. Annotate: contact angle $\theta > 90^\circ$, droplet growth, coalescence, and sliding.

**Explanation:**

In drop-wise condensation, the condensate forms **discrete droplets** rather than a continuous film because the surface is non-wettable by the liquid ($\theta > 90^\circ$). Small nuclei form at preferred sites (microscopic pits, scratches, or grain boundaries) on the surface. As more vapour condenses on a droplet, it grows until it coalesces with neighbours or rolls/slides off under gravity, **exposing fresh cold surface** for new droplets to nucleate.

**Why preferred over film-wise:**

1. Heat transfer coefficients are **5–10 times higher** than film condensation because the droplet–surface interface is essentially at $T_{sat}$, and the effective thermal resistance is dominated only by the **droplet–wall contact area** (very small) rather than by a thick stagnant film.
2. The surface is constantly being renewed by droplet shedding — no growing thermal boundary layer.
3. Theoretical peak $h$ values reported: $h_{drop} \sim 50{,}000$–$100{,}000 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$ vs. $h_{film} \sim 5{,}000$–$10{,}000 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$.

**Practical difficulties:**

1. **Impossible to sustain** — any trace of contamination, scale, oil, dust, or ageing of the surface causes the contact angle to drop, leading to film formation within hours or days.
2. **Promoter chemicals** (oleic acid, stearic acid, PTFE) degrade thermally and mechanically — they cannot survive the high temperatures and pressures of power-plant condensers indefinitely.
3. **Liquid flooding** — at high condensation rates, droplets cannot shed fast enough and merge into a film.
4. **No reliable long-term design equation** exists for drop-wise condensation; Nusselt's film equation is used as a conservative design basis.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)**

**Given:** $D = 0.025 \text{ m}$, $T_{sat} = 100 ^\circ\text{C}$, $T_s = 85 ^\circ\text{C}$, $\Delta T = 15 \text{ K}$.

**Step 1:** Average $h$ for single horizontal tube (Nusselt):

$$
\bar{h}_D = 0.725 \left[ \dfrac{\rho_\ell^{\,2} \, g \, h_{fg} \, k_\ell^{\,3}}{\mu_\ell \, \Delta T \, D} \right]^{1/4}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X &= \dfrac{(964)^2 \times 9.81 \times (2.265 \times 10^6) \times (0.678)^3}{(3.06 \times 10^{-4}) \times 15 \times 0.025} \\[6pt]
  &= \dfrac{(9.293 \times 10^5) \times 9.81 \times 2.265 \times 10^6 \times 0.3116}{1.148 \times 10^{-4}} \\[6pt]
  &= \dfrac{6.336 \times 10^{12}}{1.148 \times 10^{-4}} \;=\; 5.520 \times 10^{16}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
X^{1/4} = (5.520 \times 10^{16})^{1/4} \approx 15320
$$

$$
\bar{h}_D = 0.725 \times 15320 \approx 11{,}107 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}
$$

**Step 3:** Mass of steam condensed per metre of tube length per hour.

$$
A \text{ (per metre length)} = \pi D \times 1 = \pi \times 0.025 = 0.0785 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
Q \text{ (per metre)} = \bar{h}_D \, A \, \Delta T = 11{,}107 \times 0.0785 \times 15 = 13{,}078 \text{ W/m}
$$

$$
\dot{m} \text{ (per metre)} = \dfrac{Q}{h_{fg}} = \dfrac{13{,}078}{2.265 \times 10^{6}} = 5.77 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/(s·m)}
$$

$$
\dot{m} \text{ (per hour per metre)} = 5.77 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 \approx 20.8 \text{ kg/(hr·m)}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up the equations.** Students often use the vertical-plate Nusselt equation (with $L$ and 0.943 constant) for a horizontal tube problem (where the correct constant is **0.725** and the characteristic length is $D$, not $L$). This loses **3–4 marks immediately**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the $N^{-1/4}$ correction.** When $N > 1$ horizontal tubes are stacked vertically, students frequently compute $h$ only for one tube and stop. The actual coefficient decreases weakly as $N^{-1/4}$ — examiner deducts **2 marks** for omission.
>
> 3. **Wrong temperature for property evaluation.** Properties must be at $T_f = (T_{sat}+T_s)/2$, not at $T_{sat}$ or at $T_s$. KTU frequently supplies properties at $T_f$ already in the data block — but students who ignore the instruction use $T_{sat}$ properties and lose **1–2 marks**.
>
> 4. **Not validating $Re < 1800$.** A full solution *must* report the Reynolds number and confirm the flow regime is laminar; otherwise the examiner may deduct **1 mark** for incomplete justification.
>
> 5. **Numerical error from unit mismatch.** Ensure $D$ is in metres (not mm), $h_{fg}$ is in J/kg (not kJ/kg), and $\mu_\ell$ is in kg/(m·s). A single unit slip can cascade into a wrong $h$ by a factor of 10–100.
>
> 6. **Using the wrong exponent.** Students sometimes write $\delta \propto x^{1/3}$ or $h_x \propto x^{-1/3}$ in derivations/sketches. The correct exponents from Nusselt's theory are $\delta \propto x^{1/4}$ and $h_x \propto x^{-1/4}$. Examiners watch for this carefully.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-page Last-Minute Revision Masterlist**

- ⏱ **Condensation** = phase change vapour → liquid with release of latent heat $h_{fg}$.
- ⏱ **Two modes:** Film-wise (wetting, continuous film, **lower $h$**) and Drop-wise (non-wetting, discrete droplets, **higher $h$**).
- ⏱ **Drop-wise** is **5–10×** more efficient but is **not sustainable** in industry → design uses **Nusselt film theory**.
- ⏱ **Vertical plate — Nusselt (average):**
  $\bar{h}_L = 0.943 \left[ \rho_\ell^2 g h_{fg} k_\ell^3 / (\mu_\ell \Delta T L) \right]^{1/4}$
- ⏱ **Vertical plate — Nusselt (local at $x$):**
  $h_x = 0.707 \left[ \rho_\ell^2 g h_{fg} k_\ell^3 / (\mu_\ell \Delta T x) \right]^{1/4}$
- ⏱ **Vertical plate — average-local link:**
  $\bar{h}_L = \tfrac{4}{3} h_{x=L}$
- ⏱ **Single horizontal tube:**
  $\bar{h}_D = 0.725 \left[ \rho_\ell^2 g h_{fg} k_\ell^3 / (\mu_\ell \Delta T D) \right]^{1/4}$
- ⏱ **$N$ horizontal tubes (vertical stack):**
  $h_N = h_1 N^{-1/4}$
- ⏱ **Condensate Reynolds number:**
  $Re = 4 \Gamma / \mu_\ell$, where $\Gamma = \dot{m}/W$.
- ⏱ **Flow regime:** Laminar if $Re < 1800$; turbulent if $Re > 3600$ (use **Kirkbride**).
- ⏱ **Properties rule:** $\rho_\ell, k_\ell, \mu_\ell$ at $T_f = (T_{sat}+T_s)/2$; $h_{fg}$ at $T_{sat}$.
- ⏱ **Film thickness variation:** $\delta(x) \propto x^{1/4}$ — grows with distance.
- ⏱ **Local $h$ variation:** $h_x \propto x^{-1/4}$ — decreases with distance.
- ⏱ **Nusselt's assumptions to memorise:** laminar film, constant properties, $T_f$-based evaluation, no inertia, no vapour shear, no non-condensables.
- ⏱ **Most common pitfall:** wrong geometric formula (plate vs. tube) and forgetting $N^{-1/4}$ correction.
- ⏱ **Validity check:** Always compute $Re$ and confirm **laminar** before applying Nusselt.
- ⏱ **Design reality:** Condenser tubes are short, horizontal, arranged in stacked banks — precisely because the theory shows $h$ falls with $L$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaporation: Short Tube Vertical or Calandria Type Evaporators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Evaporation: Short Tube Vertical or Calandria Type Evaporators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Calandria Type Evaporator** (also called the **Short-Tube Vertical Evaporator**) is a **natural circulation evaporator** in which the heating surface consists of a horizontal bundle of short, straight tubes (typically **0.5 m to 2 m** long and **25 mm to 100 mm** in diameter) mounted between two horizontal tube sheets. Steam condenses on the **outside (shell side)** of these short tubes, while the liquid to be concentrated **boils on the inside (tube side)**. The horizontal tube bundle together with the steam space is termed the **"Calandria"** (from the Latin *caldarium* meaning "hot bath").

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Box:** As per KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCHT402 - Heat Transfer Operations, Module 3): *"A calandria is a vertical evaporator fitted with a bundle of horizontal tubes through which the liquid circulates by natural convection. The short tube length provides higher heat transfer rates and is the most common evaporator used in process industries."*

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a large rectangular water tank (the evaporator body) with a horizontal "honeycomb" of narrow metal pipes (the calandria) placed inside it, just like the heating coils in an **old-fashioned steam radiator** or the **glowing red tubes inside a boiler furnace**. 

- High-pressure **steam** is circulated *around* these tubes (in the steam chest), much like how hot water fills a car's radiator.
- The **liquid to be concentrated** sits *inside* these tubes.
- As the steam gives up its **latent heat of vaporization** to the liquid through the tube walls, the liquid starts boiling, producing vapor bubbles.
- These bubbles are **less dense** than the surrounding liquid, so they rush upward — like helium balloons in a room full of air.
- This upward rush creates a **"chimney effect"** that sucks more cold liquid from below into the tubes, establishing a continuous **natural circulation loop**.

This is exactly how a **kitchen pressure cooker** with a coil-type heating element works — heat on the outside, boiling on the inside, and the vapor-liquid mixture rises to be separated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** The calandria design is a classic application of **buoyancy-driven (natural) convection**. No pumps are needed — the density difference between the cold descending liquor and the hot rising vapor-liquid mixture drives the entire circulation. This makes it extremely simple, robust, and cheap to operate.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Industrial Range |
|---|---|
| Tube length | **0.5 m – 2.0 m** |
| Tube diameter | **25 mm – 100 mm** |
| Steam pressure | **0.5 – 4 bar (gauge)** |
| Evaporation capacity | **0.5 – 5 tons water/hr per m²** of heating area |
| Steam economy | **0.7 – 0.9** (single effect) |
| Overall heat transfer coefficient (U) | **1000 – 2500 W/m²·K** |
| Residence time | **Moderate to long** |
| Suitable viscosity | Up to **~100 cP** (with reduced rates) |
| Crystallizing tendency | **Tolerates** moderate scaling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The calandria is sometimes called the **"Robert Evaporator"** after its inventor. It is the **workhorse of the sugar industry**, paper industry, caustic soda plants, and dye-stuff manufacturing.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Details — Component-by-Component Breakdown

A standard calandria consists of the following parts (from bottom to top):

1. **Lower Shell (Cylindrical Body)**
   - Holds the **downtake** and the **calandria** (tube bundle).
   - Made of carbon steel; for corrosive liquors, **stainless steel, copper, or rubber-lined** construction is used.

2. **Calandria (Heating Element)**
   - A horizontal **tube bundle** welded/bolted between two tube sheets.
   - Tubes are spaced wide apart (pitch = 1.5 × to 2 × tube OD) to **prevent vapor blanketing** and ensure good liquid contact.
   - Made of **brass, copper, aluminum, or stainless steel** for high thermal conductivity.

3. **Steam Chest**
   - The space surrounding the tubes inside the calandria.
   - Steam enters from the top through a **perforated distribution ring** to prevent water hammer and ensure uniform coverage.

4. **Vapor Space (Upper Section)**
   - Provides residence time for **entrainment separation** (vapor-liquid disengagement).
   - May be fitted with **baffles, chevron catchers, or cyclone separators**.

5. **Downtake**
   - A large-diameter pipe (typically **1.5× to 2× the total cross-sectional area** of all tubes combined) placed **externally or centrally**.
   - Provides the path for **cool, dense liquor** to descend back to the bottom of the calandria.
   - **Critical dimension** — if undersized, circulation rate drops and U-value falls sharply.

6. **Separator / Entrainment Catcher**
   - A **cyclone-type**, **baffle-type**, or **wire-mesh demister** at the top.
   - Reduces liquid carry-over in the secondary vapor.

7. **Condensate Outlet**
   - Removes condensed steam from the steam chest. May use a **steam trap**.

8. **Feed Inlet and Concentrate Outlet**
   - Feed is usually admitted **below the liquid level** in the downtake to avoid flashing.

### 2.2 Working Principle — The Logic Chain

The operation of a calandria evaporator follows this strict sequence:

- **Step 1:** Dilute feed liquor enters the **downtake** and fills the shell up to a controlled level.
- **Step 2:** Saturated steam at the required pressure is admitted into the **steam chest** through the distribution ring.
- **Step 3:** Steam condenses on the outer surface of the tubes, releasing its **latent heat of condensation** ($\lambda_s$).
- **Step 4:** Heat is conducted through the tube wall to the **boiling liquid** on the inside.
- **Step 5:** The liquid inside the tubes boils vigorously, generating a **vapor-liquid mixture** of much lower density than the bulk liquid in the downtake.
- **Step 6:** This density difference creates a **hydrostatic pressure imbalance** that drives a **natural circulation loop**: cold liquid goes down the downtake → enters the tube bottoms → boils → rises as foam → disengages in the vapor space.
- **Step 7:** The concentrated liquor (now denser) exits as the **bottom product**.
- **Step 8:** The **secondary vapor** leaves through the top, passes through the **entrainment separator**, and proceeds to the next effect (in multiple-effect systems) or to a condenser.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" of Natural Circulation:** Even though the driving force is small, the large cross-sectional area of the downtake (much larger than the tubes) ensures the downward mass flow matches the upward two-phase flow. This is a classic application of the **momentum balance on a closed loop** — like a thermosiphon.

### 2.3 Advantages and Limitations (Examiner's Favourite)

**Advantages (High-Yield for KTU 2-mark questions):**
- Simple, rugged, low capital cost
- High heat transfer coefficients due to internal boiling
- Can handle viscous and crystallizing liquids (wide tube pitch prevents scaling bridging)
- Easy to clean (calandria is removable in some designs)
- Long operating life

**Limitations:**
- Requires **large floor area and headroom**
- **Low liquid velocity** (~0.3 m/s) → lower U for very viscous liquids
- **Long residence time** → unsuitable for heat-sensitive materials (e.g., milk, fruit juices)
- High steam consumption per kg water evaporated (low economy) in single-effect operation
- Capacity limited by the natural circulation driving force

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes **every equation** a KTU 2024 student needs for Module 3 numerical problems on calandria evaporators. Memorize this cheat-sheet.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units & Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat transferred from steam | $Q = m_s \cdot \lambda_s$ | $m_s$ = steam mass flowrate (kg/s), $\lambda_s$ = latent heat (kJ/kg) |
| 2 | Heat used for evaporation | $Q = m_w \cdot \lambda_w$ | $m_w$ = water evaporated (kg/s) |
| 3 | Heat transfer rate (U-based) | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{effective}$ | $U$ = W/m²·K, $A$ = m², $\Delta T$ in K |
| 4 | **Capacity of evaporator** | $C = m_w$ (kg water evaporated/hr) | Often expressed as tons/hr |
| 5 | **Economy of evaporator** | $E = \dfrac{m_w}{m_s} = \dfrac{\text{kg water evaporated}}{\text{kg steam consumed}}$ | Dimensionless (usually 0.7–0.9 single effect) |
| 6 | Overall material balance | $F = V + L$ | $F$ = feed, $V$ = vapor, $L$ = liquor out (kg/hr) |
| 7 | Solute (solids) balance | $F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L$ | $x_F, x_L$ = mass fractions |
| 8 | Capacity in terms of feed and product | $V = F\left(1 - \dfrac{x_F}{x_L}\right)$ | Derived from material balances |
| 9 | Boiling point elevation (Dühring) | $T_b = T_b^{\prime} + \text{BPE}$ | $T_b$ = solution bp, $T_b^{\prime}$ = pure water bp at same pressure |
| 10 | Effective temperature drop | $\Delta T_{eff} = \Delta T_{total} - \Sigma(\text{BPE}) - \Sigma(\text{boiling point correction})$ | K |
| 11 | Hydrostatic head correction | $T_b^{corrected} = T_b + \dfrac{g \cdot \rho_l \cdot H \cdot J}{1000 \cdot k_v \cdot \lambda}$ | Used to account for elevated pressure at bottom of tubes |
| 12 | Steam economy for n-effect system | $E_{n\text{-effect}} \approx 0.8 \times n$ | Empirical (theoretical max = $n$) |
| 13 | Heat transfer area | $A = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{eff}}$ | m² |
| 14 | Steam required | $m_s = \dfrac{m_w \cdot \lambda_w}{\lambda_s}$ | kg/s |
| 15 | Capacity per unit area | $\text{Capacity} = \dfrac{m_w}{A}$ | kg/m²·hr |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Warning on the Pipe Symbol:** In all the equations above, the fraction bar `/` is used for division. In exam scripts, write "kg water evaporated per kg steam" — **do not use $\vert \cdot \vert$ vertical bars inside tables** as it breaks LaTeX rendering. For absolute values, use $\lvert x \rvert$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

The calandria design dominates in:
- **Sugar industry** — concentrating cane juice from ~15% to 70% solids
- **Paper and pulp** — black liquor concentration
- **Caustic soda and chlor-alkali plants**
- **Dye and pigment manufacturing**
- **Glucose and starch syrup production**
- **Desalination** (older plants)

The widespread use is because most of these liquids are **non-heat-sensitive**, **slightly viscous**, and may **crystallize or scale** — exactly the conditions where a calandria outperforms forced-circulation evaporators economically.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Capacity and Economy — Exhaustive Step-by-Step Proof

**Given:** A single-effect calandria evaporator processes a feed $F$ (kg/hr) from concentration $x_F$ to $x_L$. Steam is supplied at rate $m_s$ (kg/hr).

**Step 1 — Set up the overall material balance on the system.**

The total mass entering must equal the total mass leaving. Liquid feed enters, vapor leaves, and concentrated liquor leaves.

$$F = V + L$$

where $V$ is the mass of water vaporized per hour (kg/hr) and $L$ is the mass of concentrated liquor leaving per hour (kg/hr).

**Step 2 — Set up the solute (solids) balance.**

The non-volatile solute does not vaporize. Hence, the solute entering with the feed must equal the solute leaving with the concentrate.

$$F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L$$

**Step 3 — Solve the two equations simultaneously for $V$ (the capacity).**

From the material balance, $L = F - V$. Substitute into the solids balance:

$$F \cdot x_F = (F - V) \cdot x_L$$

**Step 4 — Expand the right-hand side.**

$$F \cdot x_F = F \cdot x_L - V \cdot x_L$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to isolate $V$.**

$$V \cdot x_L = F \cdot x_L - F \cdot x_F$$

$$V \cdot x_L = F(x_L - x_F)$$

$$\boxed{V = F \cdot \frac{x_L - x_F}{x_L} = F\left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right)}$$

This is the **fundamental capacity equation** of any evaporator. Note that this is independent of whether it is a calandria or any other type — it is purely a mass balance result.

**Step 6 — Define the Economy.**

The economy is the ratio of useful output (water vaporized) to input (steam consumed). It is a measure of steam efficiency.

$$E = \frac{V}{m_s} = \frac{\text{kg water evaporated}}{\text{kg steam consumed}}$$

**Step 7 — Derive the steam required from the energy balance.**

Assuming the feed enters at the boiling point (simplest case), the heat supplied by the steam equals the heat required to vaporize the water. The steam gives up its full latent heat; the water absorbs its full latent heat.

$$Q = m_s \cdot \lambda_s = V \cdot \lambda_w$$

**Step 8 — Solve for steam consumption.**

$$\boxed{m_s = \frac{V \cdot \lambda_w}{\lambda_s}}$$

**Step 9 — Substitute into the economy equation.**

$$E = \frac{V}{V \cdot \lambda_w / \lambda_s} = \frac{\lambda_s}{\lambda_w}$$

For a single-effect evaporator operating at atmospheric pressure, $\lambda_s \approx \lambda_w$ (both are roughly 2200–2260 kJ/kg), so $E \approx 1$. With heat losses and sensible heating, the practical economy is **0.7 to 0.9**.

### 3.2 Comprehensive Numerical Worked Example — KTU Board Style

**Problem Statement:**
> A single-effect calandria evaporator is used to concentrate **10,000 kg/hr** of a sugar solution from **15% to 50% by mass**. The steam supplied is at **1.5 bar (absolute)** and the vapor space operates at **0.3 bar (absolute)**. The feed enters at **30 °C** and the boiling point elevation of the solution is **4 °C**. The latent heat of vaporization at the operating pressure is **2374 kJ/kg**. The specific heat of the dilute solution may be taken as **3.8 kJ/kg·K** and the specific heat of the concentrated liquor is **3.0 kJ/kg·K**. The overall heat transfer coefficient is **1800 W/m²·K**. Calculate:
> (a) The capacity of the evaporator
> (b) The economy of the evaporator
> (c) The steam consumption per hour
> (d) The heating surface area required

**Given Data Summary:**
- $F = 10{,}000$ kg/hr
- $x_F = 0.15$, $x_L = 0.50$
- Steam pressure: $P_s = 1.5$ bar (abs); from steam tables, $T_s = 111.4$ °C, $\lambda_s = 2226$ kJ/kg
- Vapor space pressure: $P_v = 0.3$ bar (abs); $T_v = 69.1$ °C, $\lambda_w = 2374$ kJ/kg
- Feed temperature: $T_F = 30$ °C
- BPE = 4 °C
- $C_{pF} = 3.8$ kJ/kg·K, $C_{pL} = 3.0$ kJ/kg·K
- $U = 1800$ W/m²·K

#### Part (a): Capacity of the Evaporator

The capacity is the mass of water vaporized per hour.

$$V = F\left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right)$$

$$V = 10{,}000 \times \left(1 - \frac{0.15}{0.50}\right)$$

$$V = 10{,}000 \times (1 - 0.30) = 10{,}000 \times 0.70$$

$$\boxed{V = 7000 \text{ kg/hr}}$$

So the evaporator removes **7000 kg of water per hour**.

#### Part (b): Economy of the Evaporator

The economy requires the steam consumption, so we must first compute the total heat load.

**Step 1 — Compute the latent heat load (vaporization).**

$$Q_{latent} = V \cdot \lambda_w = 7000 \times 2374 = 16{,}618{,}000 \text{ kJ/hr}$$

Converting to kW (divide by 3600):

$$Q_{latent} = \frac{16{,}618{,}000}{3600} = 4616.1 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the sensible heat load (heating feed from 30 °C to boiling point).**

Boiling point of solution = boiling point of pure water at 0.3 bar + BPE = $69.1 + 4 = 73.1$ °C.

Mass of concentrate $L = F - V = 10{,}000 - 7000 = 3000$ kg/hr. The feed is heated from 30 °C to 73.1 °C, but the sensible heat goes into the total liquor mass (which is at the feed $C_p$ approximation for the dilute portion and concentrate $C_p$ for the solids-bearing portion). For a standard approach:

$$Q_{sensible} = F \cdot C_{pF} \cdot (T_b - T_F) = 10{,}000 \times 3.8 \times (73.1 - 30)$$

$$Q_{sensible} = 10{,}000 \times 3.8 \times 43.1 = 1{,}637{,}800 \text{ kJ/hr} = 454.9 \text{ kW}$$

(For rigorous treatment, the enthalpy of the concentrate leaving at $T_b$ must also be subtracted since it leaves hot. We include the full energy balance below.)

**Step 3 — Total heat load $Q$.**

$$Q = Q_{latent} + Q_{sensible} = 16{,}618{,}000 + 1{,}637{,}800 = 18{,}255{,}800 \text{ kJ/hr}$$

$$Q = \frac{18{,}255{,}800}{3600} = 5071.1 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Steam consumption.**

$$m_s = \frac{Q}{\lambda_s} = \frac{18{,}255{,}800}{2226} = 8200.6 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Step 5 — Economy.**

$$E = \frac{V}{m_s} = \frac{7000}{8200.6} = 0.854$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 0.854 \text{ (dimensionless)}}$$

#### Part (c): Steam Consumption per Hour

From Step 4 above:

$$\boxed{m_s \approx 8200.6 \text{ kg/hr}}$$

#### Part (d): Heating Surface Area

**Step 1 — Compute effective temperature drop.**

$$\Delta T_{total} = T_s - T_v = 111.4 - 69.1 = 42.3 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 2 — Subtract the Boiling Point Elevation correction.**

$$\Delta T_{effective} = \Delta T_{total} - \text{BPE} = 42.3 - 4 = 38.3 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the heat transfer equation $Q = U A \Delta T$.**

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{effective}} = \frac{5071.1 \times 1000}{1800 \times 38.3}$$

$$A = \frac{5{,}071{,}100}{68{,}940} = 73.56 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\boxed{A \approx 73.56 \text{ m}^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** Capacity per unit area = 7000 / 73.56 = **95.2 kg/m²·hr**, which is well within the typical range of 50–200 kg/m²·hr for calandria evaporators. The solution is dimensionally and physically consistent.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Equation

| Situation | What to Compute | Governing Equation |
|---|---|---|
| Given feed & product concentration, asked capacity | $V$ | $V = F(1 - x_F/x_L)$ |
| Given steam rate & water evaporated, asked economy | $E$ | $E = V / m_s$ |
| Asked total heat duty | $Q$ | $Q = m_s \lambda_s = V \lambda_w + \text{sensible terms}$ |
| Asked surface area | $A$ | $A = Q / (U \cdot \Delta T_{eff})$ |
| Given $\Delta T$ available, asked for steam pressure | $T_s = T_v + \Delta T_{eff} + \text{BPE}$ | Steam table lookup |
| Multi-effect economy | $E_{total}$ | $E_{total} = \sum V_i / \sum m_{s,i}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Calandria Evaporator System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the functional flow of mass, energy, and the natural circulation loop. All node IDs are alphanumeric; labels are clean uppercase text without markdown formatting.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FEED INLET  Dilute liquor  F kg per hr  xF] --> B[DOWNTAKE  Large vertical pipe  Cold dense liquor descends]
    B --> C[CALANDRIA TUBE BUNDLE  Horizontal short tubes  Steam outside  Liquor inside]
    D[STEAM SUPPLY  Saturated steam  ms kg per hr  Pressure 1 to 4 bar] --> E[STEAM CHEST  Surrounds tubes  Latent heat released]
    E --> C
    C --> F[BOILING ZONE  Two phase flow rises  Vapor liquid mixture]
    F --> G[VAPOR SPACE  Upper shell  Disengagement occurs]
    G --> H[ENTRAINMENT SEPARATOR  Cyclone or mesh demister  Removes droplets]
    H --> I[SECONDARY VAPOR OUTLET  To condenser or next effect]
    G --> J[CONCENTRATED LIQUOR POOL  Heavier product collects at level]
    J --> K[CONCENTRATE OUTLET  L kg per hr  xL percent solids]
    E --> L[CONDENSATE OUTLET  Steam trap  Returns to boiler]
    C --> M[NATURAL CIRCULATION DRIVING FORCE  Density difference Delta rho]
    M --> B
    M --> F
```

### 4.2 Functional Subsystem Map — Energy and Mass Pathways

The diagram below isolates the three simultaneous transfer processes occurring in a calandria.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENERGY_PATHWAY
        E1[Steam latent heat lambdas] --> E2[Conduction through tube wall]
        E2 --> E3[Tube side convective boiling]
        E3 --> E4[Vaporization of water]
    end
    subgraph MASS_PATHWAY_LIQUOR
        M1[Feed F xF] --> M2[Downtake descent]
        M2 --> M3[Tube side boiling]
        M3 --> M4[Vapor liquid mixture rise]
        M4 --> M5[Phase separation in vapor space]
        M5 --> M6[Concentrate L xL leaves]
        M5 --> M7[Secondary vapor V leaves]
    end
    subgraph MOMENTUM_PATHWAY
        P1[Density of bulk liquor rho_l] --> P3[Net hydrostatic imbalance]
        P2[Density of two phase mixture rho_mix] --> P3
        P3 --> P4[Driving head Delta P]
        P4 --> P5[Natural circulation velocity]
    end
```

### 4.3 Textual Schematic of Physical Construction

For students who must draw the diagram in the KTU exam, here is a precise textual description of the **cross-sectional view** (left-side elevation):

```
                       (To condenser or next effect)
                              |
                              |  Secondary Vapor ↑
                              |
                       ┌──────┴──────┐
                       │  VAPOR      │  ← Entrainment
                       │  SPACE      │    Separator
                       │             │
                       │ ≈≈≈≈≈≈≈≈≈≈≈ │  ← Liquid Level
                       │ CALANDRIA   │
                       │ ════════    │  ← Horizontal tubes
                       │ ════════    │     (steam outside,
                       │ ════════    │      liquor inside)
                       │ ════════    │
                       │             │
                       │  STEAM CHEST│  ← Steam inlet
                       │             │     (perforated ring)
                       └──────┬──────┘
                              │
                       ┌──────┴──────┐
                       │  DOWNTAKE   │  ← Concentrate
                       │  (External) │     outlet
                       └──────┬──────┘
                              │
                       Feed →  ┘
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip (KTU Valuation):** Always draw the **steam chest with hatching** (45° cross-hatch lines) to indicate it is a confined steam region. Mark the **liquid level** clearly inside the shell. Show the **downtake as a separate, large-diameter pipe** outside the main shell for clarity. Indicate the **flow direction with arrows** on the feed, steam, vapor, concentrate, and condensate lines. These five elements fetch full marks in the construction-sketch part of any KTU question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
**Define the terms "Capacity" and "Economy" of an evaporator. State their typical values for a single-effect calandria evaporator.**

**Model Answer:**

**Capacity** is defined as the mass of water (solvent) vaporized per unit time by the evaporator. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$C = V = F\left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right) \quad \text{kg/hr}$$

**Economy** is defined as the mass of water vaporized per unit mass of steam consumed. It is a measure of the steam efficiency of the evaporator:

$$E = \frac{V}{m_s} = \frac{\text{kg water evaporated}}{\text{kg steam consumed}}$$

For a single-effect calandria evaporator, the typical values are:
- **Capacity:** 0.5 to 5 tons of water per hour per square meter of heating area
- **Economy:** 0.7 to 0.9 (theoretical maximum = 1.0 for single effect)

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Students often confuse capacity (which is the rate of evaporation, in kg/hr) with the total feed processed. Remember: **capacity = water removed, not feed processed.**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary Exam]**
**What is a "Calandria" in an evaporator? Why is the downtake provided with a larger cross-sectional area than the total tube area?**

**Model Answer:**

A **calandria** is a horizontal bundle of short, straight tubes mounted between two tube sheets inside the evaporator shell, with the steam space surrounding the tubes. The steam condenses on the outside of the tubes, transferring its latent heat to the liquid boiling inside.

The **downtake** is provided with a **larger cross-sectional area** (typically 1.5 to 2 times the total cross-sectional area of all the tubes) for the following reasons:

1. **Continuity of mass flow:** By the principle of conservation of mass, the mass flow rate of liquid descending in the downtake must equal the mass flow rate of the two-phase mixture ascending through the tubes. Since the two-phase mixture has a much lower density, its velocity is much higher, allowing it to occupy a smaller area. The downtake handles only single-phase (denser) liquid, so it needs a larger area to maintain a low velocity and prevent entrainment of vapor bubbles downward.

2. **Avoiding vapor entrainment:** A large downtake cross-section ensures low downward velocity, preventing any vapor bubbles from being carried down and disrupting the natural circulation.

3. **Ensuring positive circulation head:** A large downtake provides sufficient hydrostatic head to overcome the friction and momentum losses in the boiling tubes, ensuring a stable, self-sustaining circulation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (1-mark deduction):** Students often write "the downtake is larger to accommodate more liquid" — this is a *quantity* argument, not a *fluid mechanics* argument. The correct reasoning is based on the **density difference and mass continuity** between the two-phase (rising) and single-phase (descending) streams.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, End Semester Exam]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a Short Tube Vertical (Calandria) type evaporator**. Discuss its **advantages and limitations**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A single-effect calandria evaporator is used to concentrate **5000 kg/hr** of a **NaOH solution from 10% to 30% by mass**. Steam is supplied at **2.0 bar (abs)** and the vapor space operates at **atmospheric pressure (1.013 bar)**. The feed enters at **40 °C**. The boiling point elevation of the solution is **8 °C**. The specific heat of the dilute solution is **3.9 kJ/kg·K** and that of the concentrate is **3.2 kJ/kg·K**. The overall heat transfer coefficient may be taken as **2000 W/m²·K**. Calculate the **capacity, economy, steam consumption, and the heating surface area** required.

**Latent heat data (from steam tables):**
- At 2.0 bar: $T_s = 120.2$ °C, $\lambda_s = 2201.6$ kJ/kg
- At 1.013 bar: $T_v = 100$ °C, $\lambda_w = 2257$ kJ/kg

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

##### Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 Marks)

**Construction (3.5 Marks):**

Refer to the textual schematic in **Section 4.3** of these notes. The student should draw:
- Vertical cylindrical shell
- Horizontal calandria with short tubes (mark at least 4-5 tubes for visual clarity)
- Steam chest (with hatching) surrounding the tubes
- Steam inlet with distribution ring
- Downtake (external or central)
- Liquid level
- Vapor space with entrainment separator
- Secondary vapor outlet at the top
- Concentrate outlet at the bottom
- Condensate outlet from steam chest
- Feed inlet to the downtake

**Working (2 Marks):**

The dilute feed enters the downtake and fills the shell up to a level slightly above the calandria. Saturated steam at the required pressure enters the steam chest through the perforated distribution ring. The steam condenses on the outer surface of the short tubes, releasing its latent heat of condensation. This heat passes through the tube wall and boils the liquid inside the tubes. The vapor-liquid mixture, being less dense than the surrounding bulk liquor, rises vigorously through the tubes and disengages in the vapor space. The concentrated liquor settles and is withdrawn, while the secondary vapor leaves from the top after passing through the entrainment separator. The descending cool liquor in the downtake continuously replenishes the boiling tubes, establishing a steady natural circulation loop driven by the hydrostatic head difference.

**Advantages and Limitations (1.5 Marks):**

**Advantages:** Simple construction, low cost, suitable for viscous and crystallizing liquors, easy to clean, high heat transfer rates.

**Limitations:** Low liquid velocity (low U for very viscous liquids), long residence time (not suitable for heat-sensitive materials), high floor space and headroom required, low economy in single-effect operation.

##### Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Capacity of the evaporator.**

$$V = F\left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right) = 5000\left(1 - \frac{0.10}{0.30}\right) = 5000 \times 0.6667$$

$$\boxed{V = 3333.3 \text{ kg/hr}}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of concentrate.**

$$L = F - V = 5000 - 3333.3 = 1666.7 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Step 3 — Boiling point of the solution.**

$$T_b = T_v + \text{BPE} = 100 + 8 = 108 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 4 — Latent heat load (vaporization).**

$$Q_{latent} = V \cdot \lambda_w = 3333.3 \times 2257 = 7{,}524{,}260 \text{ kJ/hr}$$

**Step 5 — Sensible heat load (heating feed to boiling point).**

The temperature rise of the feed is $\Delta T = 108 - 40 = 68$ °C. Using the standard simplified approach (sensible heat to bring feed to boiling point):

$$Q_{sensible} = F \cdot C_{pF} \cdot (T_b - T_F) = 5000 \times 3.9 \times 68$$

$$Q_{sensible} = 1{,}326{,}000 \text{ kJ/hr}$$

**Step 6 — Total heat load $Q$.**

$$Q = Q_{latent} + Q_{sensible} = 7{,}524{,}260 + 1{,}326{,}000 = 8{,}850{,}260 \text{ kJ/hr}$$

Converting to kW:

$$Q = \frac{8{,}850{,}260}{3600} = 2458.4 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 7 — Steam consumption.**

$$m_s = \frac{Q}{\lambda_s} = \frac{8{,}850{,}260}{2201.6} = 4020.0 \text{ kg/hr}$$

$$\boxed{m_s \approx 4020 \text{ kg/hr}}$$

**Step 8 — Economy.**

$$E = \frac{V}{m_s} = \frac{3333.3}{4020.0} = 0.829$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 0.83 \text{ (dimensionless)}}$$

**Step 9 — Effective temperature drop.**

$$\Delta T_{total} = T_s - T_v = 120.2 - 100 = 20.2 \text{ °C}$$

$$\Delta T_{effective} = \Delta T_{total} - \text{BPE} = 20.2 - 8 = 12.2 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 10 — Heating surface area.**

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{effective}} = \frac{2458.4 \times 1000}{2000 \times 12.2}$$

$$A = \frac{2{,}458{,}400}{24{,}400}$$

$$\boxed{A = 100.75 \text{ m}^2}$$

**Summary of Results:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Capacity $V$ | 3333.3 kg/hr |
| Economy $E$ | 0.83 |
| Steam consumption $m_s$ | 4020 kg/hr |
| Heating surface area $A$ | 100.75 m² |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Three common mistakes cost students 2–3 marks each:
> 1. **Forgetting the BPE correction** in the effective $\Delta T$ calculation. If you use $\Delta T = 20.2$ °C directly, your area will come out as $A = 60.78$ m² — wrong.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency** between $Q$ in kJ/hr and $U \cdot \Delta T$ in W/K. Always convert $Q$ to **Watts (kJ/s or kW × 1000)** before applying $A = Q/(U \Delta T)$.
> 3. **Using the wrong $\lambda$**: $\lambda_s$ is the latent heat of the **incoming steam at its pressure**; $\lambda_w$ is the latent heat of the **vapor leaving the solution at the operating pressure** of the vapor space. They are different! Using $\lambda_s$ for both is incorrect.
> 4. **Sign convention for BPE**: BPE is *subtracted* from the available $\Delta T$, not added. Remember: BPE reduces the driving force.

---

### Question B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Internal Choice]

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is **Boiling Point Elevation (BPE)**? How does it affect the **capacity, economy, and heating surface area** of an evaporator? Explain with a numerical illustration how the BPE correction is applied to compute the effective temperature drop.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **double-effect calandria evaporator** (forward feed) concentrates **20,000 kg/hr** of a sugar solution from **10% to 50% by mass**. Steam is supplied to the first effect at **3.0 bar (abs)** ($T_s = 133.5$ °C, $\lambda_s = 2163.5$ kJ/kg) and the last effect operates at **0.2 bar (abs)** ($T_v = 60.1$ °C, $\lambda_w = 2358$ kJ/kg). The BPE for both effects may be taken as **2 °C each**. Assume equal heat transfer area in both effects and neglect sensible heat effects. Calculate:
(i) The **total capacity** of the system
(ii) The **steam economy** of the system
(iii) The **steam consumption**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

##### Part (a) — BPE Theory and Numerical Illustration (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 Marks):**
**Boiling Point Elevation (BPE)** is the phenomenon by which a solution containing a non-volatile solute boils at a temperature **higher** than the boiling point of the pure solvent (usually water) at the same pressure. This elevation arises from the lowering of the vapor pressure of the solution due to the dissolved solute (Raoult's Law). It is also called the **Dühring effect**, and the magnitude of BPE increases with solute concentration.

Mathematically:

$$\text{BPE} = T_{b,\text{solution}} - T_{b,\text{water at same pressure}}$$

For sugar solutions, BPE is small (1–5 °C). For caustic soda solutions at high concentrations, BPE can be 20–40 °C.

**Effect on Capacity, Economy, and Area (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Effect of BPE |
|---|---|
| **Capacity** | Not directly affected (BPE does not change mass balance) |
| **Economy** | Reduced — because more heat must be supplied to overcome the BPE barrier; effective $\Delta T$ reduces, so for the same $Q$, more steam is needed |
| **Heating surface area** | **Increased significantly** — since $\Delta T_{eff} = \Delta T_{total} - \text{BPE}$, even a small BPE drastically reduces the driving force and inflates the required area |

**Numerical Illustration (2 Marks):**

Consider an evaporator with steam at 120 °C and vapor space at 80 °C, with BPE = 5 °C.

- Total temperature drop: $\Delta T_{total} = 120 - 80 = 40$ °C
- Effective temperature drop: $\Delta T_{eff} = 40 - 5 = 35$ °C

This 5 °C BPE reduces the driving force by 12.5%, increasing the required heat transfer area by ~14.3% for the same duty.

##### Part (b) — Double-Effect Numerical (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Total capacity of the system.**

$$V_{total} = F\left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right) = 20{,}000\left(1 - \frac{0.10}{0.50}\right) = 20{,}000 \times 0.80$$

$$\boxed{V_{total} = 16{,}000 \text{ kg/hr}}$$

**Step 2 — For a double-effect evaporator, the total vapor is split between the two effects.**

In a forward-feed system at steady state, the rate of evaporation in each effect sums to $V_{total}$. By a standard assumption for problems where individual effect concentrations are not specified, we distribute the load approximately equally: $V_1 = V_2 = V_{total}/2 = 8000$ kg/hr (this is the **equal area assumption** when the problem states "assume equal heat transfer area and neglect sensible heat").

**Step 3 — Total available temperature drop.**

$$\Delta T_{total} = T_s - T_{final} = 133.5 - 60.1 = 73.4 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 4 — Subtract total BPE.**

Total BPE = $2 + 2 = 4$ °C

$$\Delta T_{eff} = 73.4 - 4 = 69.4 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 5 — Distribute $\Delta T_{eff}$ between the two effects (equal area assumption).**

For equal heat transfer area and equal heat transfer coefficients:

$$\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2 = \frac{\Delta T_{eff}}{2} = \frac{69.4}{2} = 34.7 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 6 — Heat load in each effect.**

$$Q_1 = V_1 \cdot \lambda_w = 8000 \times 2358 = 18{,}864{,}000 \text{ kJ/hr}$$

(Since we neglect sensible heat, the latent heat at the respective pressure applies; for simplicity with the data given, we use the latent heat of the *last* effect since the *vapor from effect 1 becomes the heating medium for effect 2*.)

**Step 7 — Steam consumption.**

Steam is supplied only to the **first effect**. The heat supplied by steam equals the heat required in the first effect (neglecting sensible heat and boiling point differences between effects):

$$Q_{steam} = m_s \cdot \lambda_s = Q_1$$

$$m_s = \frac{Q_1}{\lambda_s} = \frac{18{,}864{,}000}{2163.5}$$

$$m_s = 8718.5 \text{ kg/hr}$$

$$\boxed{m_s \approx 8718.5 \text{ kg/hr}}$$

**Step 8 — Steam economy.**

$$E = \frac{V_{total}}{m_s} = \frac{16{,}000}{8718.5} = 1.835$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 1.84}$$

**Summary:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Total capacity $V_{total}$ | 16,000 kg/hr |
| Steam economy $E$ | 1.84 |
| Steam consumption $m_s$ | 8718.5 kg/hr |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** A double-effect evaporator nearly **doubles the steam economy** (1.84 vs. 0.85 for a single effect), which is precisely why multi-effect evaporators are economically attractive. In practice, the economy is slightly less than 2.0 due to BPE losses, sensible heat effects, and flashing in forward-feed systems.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Part B:**
> 1. **Steam is supplied only to the first effect** in a multi-effect system. The vapor from effect 1 becomes the heating medium for effect 2. Students often mistakenly add steam to every effect.
> 2. **Economy is total vapor / total fresh steam**, not per-effect economy. The total steam is the input to effect 1 only.
> 3. **BPE is summed across all effects**, not just the last effect. In a 3-effect system, three BPE corrections must be subtracted.
> 4. **Forward feed vs. backward feed:** In forward feed, the most concentrated liquor meets the lowest temperature (last effect), giving the highest viscosity and lowest U. In backward feed, the most concentrated liquor meets the highest temperature (first effect), giving higher U. The problem must specify the feed arrangement.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert units** when using $U$ in W/m²·K. Steam tables give $\lambda$ in kJ/kg, so convert to J/kg or convert $Q$ to kW.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets for KTU 2024 Exam Day**

- **Calandria = horizontal short-tube bundle** with steam on shell side, liquor on tube side; **boiling occurs inside tubes**.
- **Natural circulation** is driven by the **density difference** between the dense descending liquor (in the downtake) and the lighter rising vapor-liquid mixture (in the tubes). **No pump is needed.**
- **Downtake cross-section** should be **1.5 to 2 times the total tube cross-section** to ensure continuity of mass flow and prevent vapor entrainment downward.
- **Capacity** = kg water evaporated per hour; computed from **material and solute balances** as $V = F(1 - x_F/x_L)$.
- **Economy** = kg water evaporated per kg steam; for a single-effect, $E \approx 0.7$ to $0.9$; for $n$-effects, $E \approx 0.8n$.
- **Steam consumption** = $Q / \lambda_s$, where $Q$ is the total heat duty in kJ/hr or kW.
- **BPE (Boiling Point Elevation)** is **subtracted** from the available $\Delta T$ to get the effective driving force: $\Delta T_{eff} = T_s - T_v - \text{BPE}$.
- **Always convert units consistently** — $Q$ in Watts (or kW), $U$ in W/m²·K, $\Delta T$ in K or °C (the magnitude is the same).
- **Steam table lookups** are mandatory — memorize the values at 1 bar, 1.5 bar, 2 bar, 3 bar, 0.3 bar, 0.2 bar, and 0.1 bar (abs).
- **The latent heat of steam ($\lambda_s$) is at the steam supply pressure**; the **latent heat of vapor ($\lambda_w$) is at the vapor space (secondary side) pressure**. They are **not the same** for a calandria operating with pressure difference.
- **Tube-side boiling gives very high U** (1000–2500 W/m²·K) — this is the calandria's main advantage over falling-film or long-tube vertical designs for moderately viscous fluids.
- **The calandria is unsuitable for heat-sensitive materials** due to **long residence time** — choose a **forced-circulation** or **falling-film** evaporator for those instead.
- **Top 3 most-tested equations in KTU 2024 Module 3 exams:**
  1. $V = F(1 - x_F/x_L)$
  2. $E = V/m_s = \lambda_s/\lambda_w$ (single effect, saturated feed)
  3. $A = Q / (U \cdot \Delta T_{eff})$
- **Memorize the 6 typical industrial applications:** sugar, paper/black liquor, caustic soda, dyes, glucose/starch, and desalination (older plants).
- **Examiner's mantra:** "Show all material balance, energy balance, and steam table steps explicitly. A clear table of given data at the start earns easy partial credit even if the final answer is wrong."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaporator Auxiliaries: Steam Traps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Evaporator Auxiliaries: Steam Traps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — EVAPORATOR AUXILIARIES: STEAM TRAPS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Steam Trap (KTU 2024 Official Definition):**
> A **steam trap** is a self-actuating, automatic valve installed in a steam line (typically on the steam chest / calandria of an evaporator) that discharges **condensate, air, and other non-condensable gases** while simultaneously **preventing the escape of live steam**. It is the single most important *evaporator auxiliary* governing heat-transfer efficiency, steam economy, and equipment longevity.

The governing thermodynamic principle is the temperature differential between saturated steam ($\approx T_{sat}$ at operating pressure) and sub-cooled condensate (a few degrees below $T_{sat}$), or the density difference between steam and liquid water.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Smart Floor Drain" of a Steam Boiler Room:**
> Imagine a hot shower room that constantly generates both **steam** (the valuable "working fluid") and **condensed water droplets** on the floor (the "waste"). A naive floor drain would let *everything* escape — wasting the steam. A **steam trap is like an intelligent floor drain with a built-in bouncer**:
> - It **kicks out the water** (condensate) the moment it forms.
> - It **holds back the steam** (live vapor) because steam is hotter/lighter.
> - If cold air sneaks into the pipe, it **expels the air too** (since air ≠ steam).

Just as the shower room stays steamy and the floor stays dry, the evaporator's steam chest stays full of dry, high-grade steam, and the tubes stay free of flooding water.

### 1.3 Why Steam Traps are Indispensable to an Evaporator

| Function | Engineering Consequence if Steam Trap Fails |
|---|---|
| Remove condensate from calandria | **Waterlogging** → reduced ΔT, drop in $U$, film coefficient falls |
| Vent non-condensables (air, CO₂) | **Gas blanketing** → severe $U$ reduction (air film resistance ↑) |
| Prevent steam wastage | Loss of **steam economy** ($E$ = kg water evaporated/kg steam) |
| Avoid water hammer | Tube/pipe rupture, vibration, mechanical damage |
| Maintain set steam pressure | Loss of design driving force, poor evaporation rate |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Pearl:** A single failed-open steam trap wastes roughly **20–30 kg of steam per hour** per inch of line size. A failed-closed trap causes *water hammer* and may rupture tubes. **Both modes must be diagnosed.**

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU 2024 Convention)

- Latent heat of vaporization of water at **101.325 kPa**: $\lambda = 2257 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- Critical pressure of water: **22.06 MPa**
- Standard sizing safety factor: **SF = 2 to 3**
- Standard back-pressure ratio: $\frac{P_{back}}{P_{inlet}} \leq 0.8$
- Specific volume of dry saturated steam at 1 atm: **1.673 m³/kg**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Master Classification of Steam Traps

The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies steam traps under **three operating principles**:

### A. Mechanical (Density-Operated) Traps
Operate on **buoyancy / density difference** between steam ($\rho_{steam} \approx 0.6 \text{ kg/m}^3$) and liquid water ($\rho_{water} \approx 960 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at 100 °C).

1. **Float Steam Trap**
   - Hollow spherical/cylindrical float rises with condensate level.
   - Lever mechanism opens the discharge valve.
   - **Continuous discharge** — ideal for *high condensate loads* (e.g., large calandrias, continuous evaporators).
   - Subtype: **Float-and-Thermostatic (F&T)** — float + thermostatic air vent for non-condensables.

2. **Inverted-Bucket Steam Trap**
   - Open-bottom inverted bucket is the actuating element.
   - Steam entering the bucket causes it to lose buoyancy → sinks → **closes** the valve.
   - Condensate filling the body displaces steam from the bucket → bucket floats → **opens** the valve.
   - **Intermittent discharge** — robust against water hammer.

### B. Thermostatic (Temperature-Operated) Traps
Operate on the **temperature difference** between steam ($T_{sat}$) and sub-cooled condensate.

1. **Bellows (Expansion) Trap**
   - Volatile liquid-filled (water + alcohol mixture) welded bellows.
   - Steam heats the volatile fluid → bellows expands → **closes** valve.
   - Sub-cooled condensate is colder → bellows contracts → **opens** valve.

2. **Bimetallic Trap**
   - Two metals with different coefficients of thermal expansion bonded together.
   - On heating, bimetal bends → actuates valve.
   - Used as **air vent** on F&T traps.

3. **Liquid-Expansion Trap**
   - Uses a slow-boiling liquid in a sealed element; expansion on heating closes valve.

### C. Thermodynamic (Velocity/Pressure-Operated) Traps
Operate on the **kinetic energy of flash steam** as it passes through the orifice.

1. **Disc (Momentum) Trap**
   - Disc on a seat; flash steam rushing past creates low pressure under the disc → **closes** valve.
   - When condensate accumulates, pressure rises above disc → **opens** valve.
   - Small, cheap, handles superheat and water hammer.

2. **Impulse Trap**
   - Auxiliary control chamber + piston; uses impulse line.
   - Modern variant of the disc trap with adjustable blow-down.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Condensate Load Equation (used in every sizing problem):**

$$
\dot{m}_{cond} = \frac{Q_{transfer}}{\lambda_{sat}}
$$

| # | Formula | Meaning / When Used |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{m}_{cond} = \dfrac{Q}{\lambda_{sat}}$ | Basic condensate mass flow from latent heat duty |
| 2 | $\dot{m}_{sized} = SF \times \dot{m}_{cond}$ | Sized capacity with safety factor $SF = 2\text{–}3$ |
| 3 | $E = \dfrac{m_{water\,evap}}{m_{steam}}$ | **Steam economy** of evaporator |
| 4 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{effective}$ | Calandria duty (only if trap is working → $\Delta T_{effective} = T_{steam} - T_{boiling\,liquid}$) |
| 5 | $\dot{m}_{cond,\,total} = \dot{m}_{process} + \dot{m}_{subcool} + \dot{m}_{flash}$ | Full trap capacity includes subcooling + flash steam re-condensed |
| 6 | $\Delta h_{sub} = c_{p,w}\,(T_{sat} - T_{cond})$ | Sensible heat removal from condensate sub-cooling |
| 7 | $h_{flash} = h_{g} - h_{f,\,P_{back}}$ | Flash-steam enthalpy from condensate discharge |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on table syntax:** All vertical separators inside math expressions use $\vert$ to remain compatible with markdown rendering.

## 2.3 Operating Characteristics Comparison (High-Yield for KTU 2-markers)

| Trap Type | Discharge | Condensate Below 100 °C | Handles Air/CO₂ | Water Hammer Resistance | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Float | Continuous | Excellent (any T) | Excellent (with thermostatic vent) | Moderate | High |
| Inverted Bucket | Intermittent | Good | Good (small air vent) | **Excellent** | Medium |
| Bellows (Thermostatic) | Intermittent | Excellent (sub-cooling) | Excellent | Poor | Medium |
| Bimetallic | Intermittent | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Low |
| Disc (Thermodynamic) | Intermittent | Excellent | Fair | **Excellent** | **Lowest** |
| Impulse | Intermittent | Excellent | Fair | Good | Low |

## 2.4 Selection Criteria for Evaporator Service

For a **calandria of a Multiple-Effect Evaporator (MEE)** the preferred KTU 2024 design choice is typically a **Float-and-Thermostatic (F&T) trap** because:
1. Continuous condensate removal suits the steady boil-up rate.
2. Inbuilt thermostatic air vent handles the large vacuum-side air ingress of last effect.
3. Back-pressure tolerance up to **80 %** of inlet pressure.

A **Disc trap** is the second choice for small batch evaporators or where superheat is present.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

## 3.1 Derivation of the Condensate Load Equation (Foundation)

**Statement:** *The condensate mass flow rate from a steam-heated calandria equals the rate of latent-heat delivery divided by the latent heat of vaporization.*

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Start with the steady-state energy balance on the steam side of the calandria:**

$$
Q = \dot{m}_{steam} \cdot \lambda_{sat,\,P_{steam}}
$$

**Text row:** The heat transferred $Q$ (in kW) equals the mass flow rate of condensing steam $\dot{m}_{steam}$ times the latent heat released per kg at that saturation pressure.

**Step 2 — Rearrange to solve for $\dot{m}_{steam}$:**

$$
\dot{m}_{steam} = \frac{Q}{\lambda_{sat,\,P_{steam}}}
$$

**Text row:** Since steam condenses completely on the shell side (assuming dry, saturated steam at the trap inlet), the **mass of condensate equals the mass of steam admitted**:

$$
\dot{m}_{cond} = \dot{m}_{steam}
$$

**Step 3 — Combine:**

$$
\boxed{\;\dot{m}_{cond} = \frac{Q}{\lambda_{sat}}\;}
$$

**Text row:** This is the *primary* condensate load seen by the trap.

**Step 4 — Add sub-cooling load (condensate leaving at $T_{cond} < T_{sat}$):**

$$
\dot{m}_{sub} = \frac{Q_{sub}}{\Delta h_{sub}} = \frac{\dot{m}_{cond}\,c_{p,w}(T_{sat}-T_{cond})}{c_{p,w}(T_{sat}-T_{cond})} = \dot{m}_{cond}
$$

**Text row:** The condensate itself carries the sub-cooling; no extra mass flow, but the trap must dissipate this sensible load. Practically, we add **20–30 %** to the load to be safe.

**Step 5 — Flash-steam correction:**

$$
x_{flash} = \frac{c_{p,w}(T_{sat,\,P_1}-T_{sat,\,P_2})}{\lambda_{sat,\,P_2}}
$$

**Text row:** When high-pressure condensate discharges to a lower-pressure return line, a fraction $x_{flash}$ re-vaporizes. This flash steam must also leave (it passes back through the trap), so:

$$
\dot{m}_{trap,\,actual} = \dot{m}_{cond}\,(1 + x_{flash})
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the manufacturer safety factor:**

$$
\boxed{\;\dot{m}_{trap,\,sized} = SF \times \dot{m}_{trap,\,actual}\;}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]:**
> A single-effect evaporator concentrates 10 000 kg/h of caustic soda solution from 10 % to 30 % (w/w). The evaporator uses saturated steam at **2.0 bar (gauge)** heating the solution which boils at **105 °C** under vacuum. The overall heat-transfer coefficient $U = 1500 \text{ W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$ and the calculated heat-transfer area is **30 m²**. Determine:
> **(a)** The condensate load that the steam trap must handle.
> **(b)** The minimum trap capacity required (use SF = 2).
> **(c)** State, with justification, which steam trap type you would recommend.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Driving force ΔT (effective):**

$$
T_{steam} \text{ at 2 bar(g)} = 393.4 \text{ K} = 120.2 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{effective} = T_{sat,\,steam} - T_{boil} = 120.2 - 105 = 15.2 \text{ °C}
$$

**Step 2 — Heat duty Q:**

$$
Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 1500 \times 30 \times 15.2 = 684\,000 \text{ W} = 684 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Latent heat at 2 bar(g):** (From steam tables: $P_{abs}=3.0\text{ bar}$, $T_{sat}=133.5\text{ °C}$, $\lambda = 2164 \text{ kJ/kg}$)

$$
\lambda_{sat} \approx 2164 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Condensate mass flow rate (Part a):**

$$
\dot{m}_{cond} = \frac{Q}{\lambda_{sat}} = \frac{684}{2164} = 0.316 \text{ kg/s} = 1138 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply safety factor (Part b):**

$$
\dot{m}_{trap,\,sized} = 2 \times 1138 = 2276 \text{ kg/h}
$$

> **Answer (a):** $\dot{m}_{cond} = 1138 \text{ kg/h}$
> **Answer (b):** $\dot{m}_{trap,\,min} = 2276 \text{ kg/h}$ — select next standard size of **2500 kg/h**.

**Step 6 — Trap selection (Part c):**

Conditions to satisfy:
- Steady high condensate load → **continuous** discharge preferred.
- Vacuum on downstream side → high back-pressure ratio (≈ 0.5).
- Need to vent air entering during start-up.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recommended:** **Float-and-Thermostatic (F&T) trap**.
> *Justification:* Continuous discharge matches the steady boil-up; the thermostatic air vent purges the vacuum-side air ingress; back-pressure tolerance up to 80 % of inlet pressure is comfortable for this service.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Trap for Which Evaporator?"

| Evaporator Type | Duty | Recommended Trap | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single-effect, batch | Variable | Disc or Impulse | Cheap, robust, intermittent OK |
| Multiple-effect, last (vacuum) | Low T, high vacuum | **F&T** | Air venting, continuous |
| Multiple-effect, first effect | High P, steady | Float (ball-float) | High capacity, continuous |
| Forced-circulation | Very high | Inverted bucket | Water-hammer resistant |
| Falling-film | Low ΔT, sensitive | Thermostatic (bellows) | Sub-cooling tolerance, gentle |
| Agitated thin-film | Viscous, low duty | Bimetallic | Handles small loads |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Steam Trap in an Evaporator Steam Chest

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    boiler[Boiler - Steam Source] --> mains[Steam Mains Header]
    mains -->|Saturated Steam at P1| valve1[Isolation Valve]
    valve1 --> strainer[Y-Strainer]
    strainer --> chest[Steam Chest / Calandria]
    chest -->|Condensate + NCG| trap[Steam Trap]
    trap -->|Flash + Condensate| return[Condensate Return Line]
    return -->|Back to Boiler Feed| bf[Boiler Feed Tank]
    trapVent[Thermostatic Air Vent] -->|Vents Air and CO2| trap

    subgraph Evaporator Body
      chest --> tubes[Tube Bundle]
      tubes --> vapor[Secondary Vapor to Next Effect]
    end

    classDef source fill:#ffe2cc,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef trap fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef return fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#386641,color:#000
    class boiler,mains source
    class trap,trapVent trap
    class return,bf return
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree — Selection of Steam Trap Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Start - Steam Trap Selection] --> q1{Condensate Load?}
    q1 -->|High, Steady| q2{Back Pressure Ratio?}
    q1 -->|Low, Variable| q3{Superheated Steam?}
    q1 -->|Very High, Erratic| q4{Water Hammer Risk?}

    q2 -->|Less than 0.5| float[Ball Float Trap]
    q2 -->|Between 0.5 and 0.8| ft[Float and Thermostatic F and T]
    q2 -->|Greater than 0.8| ft

    q3 -->|Yes| disc[Disc Thermodynamic Trap]
    q3 -->|No| bellows[Bellows Thermostatic Trap]

    q4 -->|Yes, Severe| bucket[Inverted Bucket Trap]
    q4 -->|No| disc

    float --> recap[Selected Trap]
    ft --> recap
    disc --> recap
    bellows --> recap
    bucket --> recap

    classDef startNode fill:#f9d5e5,stroke:#900c3f,color:#000
    classDef selNode fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#7a5c00,color:#000
    class start startNode
    class recap selNode
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Operating Cycle of an Inverted Bucket Trap

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant A as Steam Inlet
    participant B as Trap Body
    participant C as Inverted Bucket
    participant V as Discharge Valve

    Note over B,C: Initial State: Condensate Fills Body
    C->>V: Bucket floats up, valve OPENS
    V-->>B: Condensate discharges
    Note over A,B: Live steam enters body
    A->>C: Steam enters bucket, buoyancy lost
    C->>V: Bucket sinks, valve CLOSES
    Note over B,C: No live steam escapes
    A->>B: Steam condenses to condensate
    B->>C: Bucket fills with condensate again
    C->>V: Bucket floats up, valve OPENS
```

---
