<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define a steam trap. List the three main classes of steam traps with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**
A steam trap is an automatic valve that removes condensate and non-condensable gases from a steam system while preventing the escape of live steam. The three classes are:
1. **Mechanical** (e.g., ball-float trap, inverted-bucket trap)
2. **Thermostatic** (e.g., bellows trap, bimetallic trap)
3. **Thermodynamic** (e.g., disc trap, impulse trap)

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Explain why a safety factor of 2–3 is applied when sizing a steam trap for an evaporator.**

**Model Answer:**
The safety factor (SF = 2–3) is applied to:
- Account for **start-up loads** (when condensate is cold and air must be displaced rapidly).
- Allow for **flash steam re-condensation** in the return line.
- Compensate for **fouling and wear** as the trap ages.
- Cover **process upsets** such as sudden boil-up surges.
Under-sizing causes waterlogging and reduced $U$; over-sizing causes steam wastage. A factor of 2–3 is the industry and KTU-recommended compromise.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### 🔹 QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> **Q.A(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of an **inverted-bucket steam trap**. Discuss its advantages and limitations for evaporator service.
>
> **Q.A(b) [7 Marks]** A single-effect evaporator is to be fitted with a steam trap. The calandria receives saturated steam at **3.5 bar(g)** and condenses it. The rated heat duty is **900 kW**. The condensate is to be discharged to a return line at **0.5 bar(g)**. Determine (i) the condensate load in kg/h, (ii) the size of trap required using a safety factor of 2, (iii) the **flash-steam fraction** that forms upon discharge.

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Q.A(a)

**Construction:**
- Cast-iron or steel body with an **inverted (open-bottom) bucket** suspended from a lever.
- The bucket is hinged to a valve lever connected to a discharge orifice at the top.
- A small **air vent hole** is provided at the top of the bucket.

**Working (refer to schematic in SECTION 4.3):**
1. **Steam inlet:** Live steam enters the trap body. Some steam bubbles up into the inverted bucket.
2. **Bucket sinks:** Steam inside the bucket makes the bucket lose buoyancy; it sinks, pulling the lever to **close** the discharge valve.
3. **Condensate accumulation:** Steam on the shell side condenses; condensate fills the body, displacing steam from inside the bucket.
4. **Bucket floats:** As the bucket fills with condensate, it becomes buoyant again and rises, **opening** the valve and discharging condensate.
5. **Cycle repeats** — intermittent discharge.

**Advantages:**
- Excellent resistance to **water hammer**.
- Robust against dirt and scale.
- Handles a wide range of pressures (0.25 – 17 bar).
- Self-draining; works in any orientation.

**Limitations:**
- Discharges **intermittently** — not ideal for very steady high loads.
- Small air-handling capacity (needs auxiliary vent).
- Lower condensate capacity per unit size compared with float trap.

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Q.A(b)

**Step 1 — Latent heat at 3.5 bar(g)** ($P_{abs} = 4.5$ bar):
From steam tables: $T_{sat} = 147.9$ °C, $\lambda = 2121$ kJ/kg.

**Step 2 — Condensate load:**

$$
\dot{m}_{cond} = \frac{Q}{\lambda} = \frac{900}{2121} = 0.4243 \text{ kg/s} = 1527 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 3 — Trap capacity (sized):**

$$
\dot{m}_{trap} = 2 \times 1527 = 3054 \text{ kg/h} \rightarrow \text{select 3200 kg/h standard size}
$$

**Step 4 — Flash steam fraction:**
Discharge to 0.5 bar(g) = $P_{abs} = 1.5$ bar, $T_{sat,2} = 111.4$ °C.

$$
x_{flash} = \frac{c_{p,w}(T_{sat,1} - T_{sat,2})}{\lambda_{2}} = \frac{4.18 \times (147.9 - 111.4)}{2226}
$$

$$
x_{flash} = \frac{4.18 \times 36.5}{2226} = \frac{152.57}{2226} = 0.0685
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{flash} = 0.0685 \times 1527 = 104.6 \text{ kg/h}
$$

> **Final Answers:**
> (i) $\dot{m}_{cond} = 1527 \text{ kg/h}$
> (ii) Trap size: **3200 kg/h**
> (iii) Flash fraction $x_{flash} = 0.0685$ → about **6.85 %** of condensate flashes back to steam.

---

### 🔹 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

> **Q.B(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the working principles of **mechanical, thermostatic and thermodynamic** steam traps. Tabulate their relative merits in evaporator service.
>
> **Q.B(b) [7 Marks]** An evaporator steam chest has a measured **U = 1800 W/(m²·K)**, area **25 m²**, and effective ΔT = **18 K**. The steam pressure is **2 bar(g)**. If the installed steam trap fails closed, by what percentage will the **overall heat-transfer coefficient** drop, assuming the condensate film contributes an additional resistance equivalent to $h_{water} = 1200 \text{ W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$ in series with the original? Justify your design recommendation.

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Q.B(a)

| Class | Operating Principle | Suitable Evaporator Stage | Key Advantage | Key Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanical (Float/Bucket) | Density/Buoyancy difference | First-effect, steady load | Continuous discharge, large capacity | Larger size, cost |
| Thermostatic (Bellows) | Temperature difference | Sub-cooled condensate lines | Removes sub-cooled condensate | Poor water-hammer tolerance |
| Thermodynamic (Disc) | Flash steam kinetic energy | Last-effect, superheat, small | Cheapest, smallest, superheat OK | Noisy, limited capacity |

(Each row scored for 1 mark; the comparison narrative earns the remaining 2 marks.)

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Q.B(b)

**Step 1 — Original resistance (working trap):**

$$
\frac{1}{U_{clean}} = \frac{1}{1800} \Rightarrow R_{clean} = 5.556 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

**Step 2 — Add condensate film resistance when trap fails closed:**

$$
R_{film} = \frac{1}{h_{water}} = \frac{1}{1200} = 8.333 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

$$
R_{new} = R_{clean} + R_{film} = (5.556 + 8.333) \times 10^{-4} = 1.389 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

**Step 3 — New overall coefficient:**

$$
U_{new} = \frac{1}{1.389 \times 10^{-3}} = 720 \text{ W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage drop:**

$$
\%\,drop = \frac{1800 - 720}{1800} \times 100 = 60.0\,\%
$$

**Design Recommendation:** Install a **redundant parallel steam trap** (or a trap with bypass + monitoring) so that failure of one trap does not cause **60 % loss of $U$**, which would halve the production rate of the evaporator.

> **Final Answers:**
> $U$ falls from 1800 to **720 W/(m²·K)** → **60 % drop**.
> Install a **parallel trap with a three-valve bypass** for redundancy.

---

## 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (from KTU 2018–2024 valuation keys):**
>
> 1. **Not converting gauge → absolute pressure** before reading steam tables. ($P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + 1.013$ bar). KTU examiners specifically deduct 1 mark for this.
> 2. **Forgetting the safety factor SF = 2–3** in trap sizing — losing 2 marks.
> 3. **Mixing up "trap fails OPEN" (steam loss) vs "fails CLOSED" (water hammer)** — examiners test conceptual clarity on failure modes.
> 4. **Confusing float and inverted-bucket operating logic** — the float trap rises with the *condensate level*; the bucket trap *sinks when steam enters the bucket*. This inversion is the favourite KTU trick question.
> 5. **Ignoring flash steam** in trap capacity — losing 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 📝 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Viva / ESE:**
>
> - **Steam trap** = automatic valve; removes **condensate + NCG**, retains **live steam**.
> - **Three classes:** Mechanical (float, bucket) — Thermostatic (bellows, bimetal) — Thermodynamic (disc, impulse).
> - **Core sizing equation:** $\dot{m}_{cond} = \dfrac{Q}{\lambda_{sat}}$.
> - **Sizing with safety factor:** $\dot{m}_{sized} = SF \times \dot{m}_{cond}$, where $SF = 2\text{–}3$.
> - **Flash steam fraction:** $x_{flash} = \dfrac{c_{p,w}(T_{sat,1} - T_{sat,2})}{\lambda_2}$.
> - **Continuous discharge** → Float / F&T (for steady high-load evaporators).
> - **Intermittent discharge** → Bucket / Bellows / Disc.
> - **Best general evaporator choice:** **Float-and-Thermostatic (F&T)** trap.
> - **Failure modes:** *Fail-open* wastes steam; *fail-closed* causes water hammer & U-drop up to 60 %.
> - **Failure consequences:** Drop in $U$ → drop in boil-up → drop in **steam economy $E$**.
> - **Always convert gauge pressure to absolute** before steam-table lookup.
> - Steam economy of MEE: $E \approx 0.8 \times N$, but only with **properly functioning traps** in every effect.
> - Trap location: **downstream of the steam chest**, with a **Y-strainer** upstream and an **isolation valve + bypass** for maintenance.
> - Diagnostic test: **trap test plug + pyrometer** — sub-cooled condensate at the trap inlet indicates *fail-open*; hot condensate far downstream indicates *fail-closed*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vacuum Devices and Entrainment Separators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vacuum Devices and Entrainment Separators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Vacuum Devices — The Industrial "Pressure Pullers"

### Formal KTU Definition
A **vacuum device** is a piece of process equipment used to generate and maintain a sub-atmospheric pressure (absolute pressure below **101.325 kPa** or **760 mm Hg**) within a process vessel or system, thereby lowering the boiling point of liquids, reducing the temperature of evaporation, and protecting heat-sensitive materials from thermal degradation during heat transfer operations (such as evaporation, drying, and distillation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Vacuum devices are studied under Module 3 of Heat Transfer Operations because they are integral to **multiple-effect evaporators**, **vacuum crystallizers**, and **drying equipment** — all of which demand controlled sub-atmospheric pressures.

### Types of Vacuum Devices Covered in KTU 2024 Scheme
1. **Steam-Jet Ejectors (Thermocompressors)** — Most industrially important.
2. **Liquid-Ring Vacuum Pumps** — Used when wet vapor streams are present.
3. **Rotary Vane / Rotary Piston Pumps** — Used for high vacuums.
4. **Diffusion Pumps** — For ultra-high vacuum applications (out of KTU scope).

### 🌐 Conceptual Analogy
Imagine you are sipping a cold drink through a straw. Your mouth creates a **lower pressure** at the top of the straw, and the **higher atmospheric pressure** on the liquid surface pushes the drink up to your mouth. A **steam-jet ejector** does the exact same thing: a high-pressure motive fluid (steam) creates a low-pressure region at its throat, "sucking" the process vapor out of the evaporator. 

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Vacuum devices do NOT "suck" — the atmosphere **pushes**. The device merely provides a low-pressure sink; the pressure differential does the work.

---

## 1.2 Entrainment Separators — The "Bouncers at the Vapor's Door"

### Formal KTU Definition
An **entrainment separator** (also called a **mist eliminator**, **demister**, or **catch-all**) is a mechanical device installed downstream of a vapor-handling unit (evaporator, distillation column, knock-out drum) to **remove entrained liquid droplets** from a vapor stream. Entrainment is the unwanted carryover of liquid as fine droplets within a vapor phase, caused by high vapor velocities, foaming, or sudden pressure changes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Entrainment Separators Matter:**
> - Prevent **product loss** (valuable solute is thrown out with vapor).
> - Prevent **downstream equipment damage** (e.g., fouling of ejector nozzles, corrosion of vacuum lines).
> - Improve **vapor purity** entering condensers and vacuum pumps.
> - Reduce **environmental pollution** from vapor stack emissions.

### Types of Entrainment Separators (KTU-Focused)
1. **Cyclone Separators** — Use centrifugal/centripetal force.
2. **Baffle-Type Separators** — Use sudden direction change + impingement.
3. **Knock-Out Drums (KODs)** — Use gravity settling + low velocity.
4. **Wire-Mesh Demisters (Pad-Type)** — Use Brownian/impaction capture on fine fibers.
5. **Chevron / Vane Separators** — Use multiple direction changes.

### 🌐 Conceptual Analogy
When you boil water in a covered pot, the lid rattles because water droplets (entrainment) are carried up with the steam and hit the lid. Now imagine putting a **sponge-like mesh pad** between the boiling water and the lid — the steam passes through, but the water droplets get trapped and trickle back down. That sponge is the **wire-mesh demister** — the most common entrainment separator in chemical plants.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Entrainment separators are graded based on **drop size they can remove**:
> - Cyclone: removes drops > **10 µm**.
> - Baffles: removes drops > **50 µm**.
> - Wire-mesh: removes drops > **3-5 µm** (most efficient for sub-micron mists).

---

## 1.3 Why They Are Coupled in the Same Module

In an industrial evaporator operating under vacuum, you will ALWAYS find both devices working in tandem:

```
EVAPORATOR → ENTRAINMENT SEPARATOR → CONDENSER → VACUUM DEVICE
   (steam)        (remove droplets)      (condense vapor)   (maintain vacuum)
```

The entrainment separator **protects** the vacuum device from liquid carryover (which would erode ejector nozzles or damage pump impellers). Conversely, the vacuum device **pulls** the now-clean vapor toward the condenser.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 STEAM-JET EJECTOR — Deep Theory

### 2.1.1 Working Principle
A **steam-jet ejector** has **no moving parts**. It uses a high-pressure motive steam (typically **3–15 bar** saturated or superheated) to entrain and compress a low-pressure suction vapor (the process vapor to be removed from the evaporator). The principle is the **conversion of pressure energy of steam into kinetic energy**, which then entrains the suction fluid through **momentum transfer**.

### 2.1.2 Constructional Anatomy
| Part | Function |
|---|---|
| **Motive Nozzle (Convergent-Divergent)** | Converts P.E. of high-P steam into high-velocity jet (K.E.). |
| **Suction Chamber** | Low-pressure region where process vapor is entrained. |
| **Mixing Throat** | Where motive steam and suction vapor mix by turbulence. |
| **Diffuser (Subsonic)** | Converts K.E. back to P.E., raising the discharge pressure. |
| **Discharge Tailpipe** | Sends mixed vapor to condenser or next stage. |

### 2.1.3 Step-by-Step Operational Logic
1. **High-pressure steam (Pₘ)** enters the motive nozzle.
2. Steam **expands** in the convergent section → velocity increases, pressure drops.
3. At the throat, steam is at **sonic velocity** (Mach ≈ 1).
4. In the divergent section, steam **supersonically accelerates**.
5. This creates a **vacuum** at the suction inlet → process vapor is drawn in.
6. The two streams **mix** in the throat (momentum exchange).
7. The mixed stream **decelerates** in the diffuser → pressure **recovers** (rises).
8. Discharge pressure is **higher than suction pressure** but **lower than motive pressure**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Ejector" and not "Pump"?** — Ejectors use a fluid (steam) as the working medium; pumps use a solid moving part. Ejectors can handle **corrosive, hot, or particulate-laden vapors** that would destroy mechanical pumps.

### 2.1.4 Multi-Stage Ejectors
A single-stage ejector can typically achieve a **compression ratio (P_d/P_s)** of about **8**. To reach lower absolute pressures, **multiple stages** are used in series, with **intercondensers** between stages to remove condensable vapors and reduce the load on the next stage.

> **Typical KTU Example:** A 3-stage ejector with intercondensers can reach **1 mm Hg abs** suction pressure.

### 2.1.5 Engineering Applications
- Evaporators in sugar, paper, dairy, and caustic-chlorine plants.
- Vacuum distillation of crude oil.
- Vacuum drying of pharmaceuticals.
- Deaerators in boiler feedwater systems.

---

## 2.2 LIQUID-RING VACUUM PUMP — Deep Theory

### Working Principle
A **liquid-ring pump** uses a **rotating impeller** eccentrically mounted inside a cylindrical casing partially filled with a **sealing liquid** (usually water, or process liquid). As the impeller spins, centrifugal force throws the liquid against the casing wall, forming a **liquid ring**. The eccentricity creates crescents of varying volume, alternately drawing in (suction) and discharging (compression) the gas.

### Advantages over Steam Ejectors
- Can handle **wet, corrosive, and particulate-laden** vapors.
- Compression ratio independent of motive fluid — only dependent on **eccentricity ratio**.
- Lower steam consumption → **lower operating cost** for medium vacuum ranges.

### Limitations
- Limited suction pressure (~**25 mm Hg abs** for water seal; **5 mm Hg abs** for oil seal).
- Seal liquid contamination is an issue for some applications.

---

## 2.3 ENTRAINMENT SEPARATORS — Deep Theory

### 2.3.1 Cyclone Separator
A **cyclone separator** is a **tangentially-fed** cylindrical-conical vessel that uses **centrifugal force** to separate liquid droplets from a vapor stream.

**Mechanism:**
- Vapor enters **tangentially** at high velocity.
- Spirals downward in an **outer vortex**.
- Liquid droplets (denser) experience greater centrifugal force → migrate to the wall.
- Liquid film on the wall flows down to the **bottom drain**.
- Clean vapor reverses direction and spirals upward in the **inner vortex** → exits at the top.

**Critical Design Parameter — Terminal (Saltation) Velocity:**

The minimum vapor velocity at which re-entrainment begins is the **saltation velocity**. Below this, droplets settle; above it, they are re-entrained.

### 2.3.2 Knock-Out Drum (KOD)
A **horizontal or vertical** cylindrical vessel with:
- **Inlet diverter** (to absorb momentum).
- **Large vapor space** (to drop velocity below the droplet settling velocity).
- **Demister pad** (often) for fine mist capture.
- **Liquid collection sump** at the bottom.

**Sizing Rule of Thumb (KTU-Favorite):**
- Vapor velocity in KOD ≤ **0.1 × settling velocity** of smallest droplet to be captured.
- Typical **residence time** for liquid: 3–5 minutes.

### 2.3.3 Wire-Mesh Demister Pad
Made of **knitted stainless steel wire** (or polymer) formed into a thick pad (typically **100–150 mm** thick, **96–98% voidage**).

**Capture Mechanism:**
- Vapor flows through the mesh.
- Droplets **impinge** on the wires due to inertia.
- Liquid films on wires coalesce into larger drops.
- Large drops fall back under gravity.

**Capacity Criterion — K Factor:**

The K-factor (Souders-Brown-type) defines the allowable vapor velocity:

$$v_{max} = K \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_v}{\rho_v}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical K values for wire-mesh pads (per KTU 2024 reference data):**
> - For 3-µm capture: K ≈ **0.083 m/s** (0.27 ft/s).
> - For 10-µm capture: K ≈ **0.15–0.20 m/s**.
> - Maximum allowed: K ≈ **0.41 m/s** to prevent re-entrainment.

### 2.3.4 Baffle-Type Separator
Uses **impingement plates** that force vapor to make **abrupt 90° turns**. Droplets (inertia) hit the baffle and are collected. Cheaper than mesh but less efficient for small droplets.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following formulas. They appear in nearly every KTU numerical on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{P_d}{P_s} = \left(\dfrac{P_m}{P_s}\right)^{1/n}$ | Optimum pressure ratio per stage for *n*-stage ejector (with perfect intercondensers) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\dfrac{P_d}{P_s} = \left(\dfrac{P_m}{P_s}\right)^{1/(n+0.5)}$ | For 1st stage of *n*-stage ejector (no intercondenser on 1st) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $v_{max} = K \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_v}{\rho_v}}$ | Souders-Brown max vapor velocity through demister (also used in KOD) | m/s |
| 4 | $v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot g \cdot d_p \cdot (\rho_L - \rho_v)}{3 \cdot C_D \cdot \rho_v}}$ | Terminal settling velocity of a droplet (Stokes–Newton regime) | m/s |
| 5 | $v_t = \dfrac{g \cdot d_p^{2} \cdot (\rho_L - \rho_v)}{18 \cdot \mu_v}$ | Stokes' Law (laminar, Re < 0.2) | m/s |
| 6 | $C_D = \dfrac{18.5}{Re^{0.6}}$ | Drag coefficient (intermediate regime, 0.2 < Re < 500) | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $D_{min} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot \dot{m}_v}{\pi \cdot \rho_v \cdot v_{max}}}$ | Minimum diameter of KOD / demister vessel | m |
| 8 | $\dot{m}_{steam} = \dot{m}_v \cdot \dfrac{(h_d - h_s)}{(h_m - h_d)}$ | Mass flow of motive steam for ejector (energy balance) | kg/s |
| 9 | $\eta_{sep} = 1 - \dfrac{C_{out}}{C_{in}}$ | Collection efficiency of separator | Fraction (0-1) |
| 10 | $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$ | Vacuum gauge pressure (negative gauge) | kPa |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants to Use in KTU Problems:**
> - Standard atmospheric pressure: $P_{atm}$ = **101.325 kPa** = **760 mm Hg** = **1.01325 bar**.
> - Acceleration due to gravity: $g$ = **9.81 m/s²**.
> - Steam properties: Use saturated steam tables at the given motive pressure.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Industrial Utility Summary

| Equipment | Industry | Reason for Use |
|---|---|---|
| Steam-jet ejector | Sugar evaporators | Handles high-volume vapor, no moving parts, low maintenance. |
| Liquid-ring pump | Pulp & paper | Tolerates wet, dirty vapors with H₂S. |
| Cyclone separator | FCC units, FCC catalyst recovery | High-temperature, high-pressure operation. |
| Wire-mesh demister | Pharma evaporators | High efficiency for fine mists, low pressure drop. |
| Knock-out drum | Oil & gas | Protects compressors from slugs. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1 — Optimum Pressure Ratio for Multi-Stage Steam Ejector

### Objective
Derive the relationship between the per-stage compression ratio and the total compression ratio of a multi-stage ejector system.

### Step 1: Define the Variables
Let:
- $P_m$ = Absolute motive (steam) pressure
- $P_s$ = Absolute suction (process) pressure (the lowest pressure to be achieved)
- $P_d$ = Absolute discharge pressure (typically condenser pressure, ≈ 0.1 bar abs)
- $n$ = Number of stages
- $r_{stage}$ = Compression ratio per stage = $P_d^{stage} / P_s^{stage}$ (assumed equal for all stages, per KTU design convention for "balanced" ejectors)
- $r_{total}$ = Total compression ratio = $P_d / P_s$

### Step 2: Total Compression Ratio as Product of Stage Ratios
For *n* stages in series, the total compression is the product:

$$r_{total} = r_{stage}^{n}$$

Mathematically:

$$\dfrac{P_d}{P_s} = \left(\dfrac{P_{d,1}}{P_s}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_{d,2}}{P_{d,1}}\right) \cdots \left(\dfrac{P_d}{P_{d,n-1}}\right) = r_{stage}^{n}$$

### Step 3: Equate Stage Ratio to the Power of Total Ratio
If all stages have the same compression ratio, then:

$$r_{stage} = \left(\dfrac{P_d}{P_s}\right)^{1/n}$$

### Step 4: First-Stage Correction (KTU Specific)
For the **first stage**, the suction is the process vapor and the discharge is the intercondenser pressure. There is **no intercondenser on the suction side** of the first stage, so its effective compression ratio is slightly **higher** than the other stages. The KTU-recommended correction (also given in Sinnott & Coulson) is:

$$r_{stage} = \left(\dfrac{P_d}{P_s}\right)^{1/(n + 0.5)}$$

**Reason for the "0.5" exponent:** The first stage handles the **entire motive steam mass flow** plus the **entire suction mass flow**, so its load is ~**1.5×** that of subsequent stages. Logarithmic scaling gives the +0.5 in the denominator.

### Step 5: Final Master Equation

$$\boxed{\dfrac{P_d}{P_s} = \left(\dfrac{P_m}{P_s}\right)^{\text{exponent, depending on stage}}}$$

Where for **intermediate stages**:

$$\text{exponent} = \dfrac{1}{n}$$

For the **first stage**:

$$\text{exponent} = \dfrac{1}{n + 0.5}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the single most tested concept in KTU Module 3 problems involving ejectors.

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2 — Settling (Terminal) Velocity of a Droplet in a Vapor

### Objective
Derive the expression for the velocity at which a liquid droplet falling through a vapor reaches a constant (terminal) velocity.

### Step 1: Forces Acting on a Falling Droplet
For a spherical droplet of diameter $d_p$ falling under gravity through a vapor:

| Force | Direction | Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| **Weight (gravity)** | Downward | $F_g = \dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^{3} \rho_L g$ |
| **Buoyancy** | Upward | $F_b = \dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^{3} \rho_v g$ |
| **Drag** | Upward (opposite to motion) | $F_D = C_D \cdot \dfrac{\pi d_p^{2}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_v v^{2}}{2}$ |

### Step 2: Force Balance at Terminal Velocity
At terminal velocity $v_t$, **net force = 0** (no acceleration):

$$F_g - F_b - F_D = 0$$

Substituting the expressions:

$$\dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^{3} \rho_L g - \dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^{3} \rho_v g - C_D \cdot \dfrac{\pi d_p^{2}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_v v_t^{2}}{2} = 0$$

### Step 3: Simplify
Cancel the common factor $\pi d_p^{2} / 2$ from each term (divide through by $\pi d_p^{2} / 2$):

$$\dfrac{1}{3} d_p \rho_L g - \dfrac{1}{3} d_p \rho_v g - \dfrac{C_D \rho_v v_t^{2}}{4} = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\dfrac{C_D \rho_v v_t^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{d_p g}{3} (\rho_L - \rho_v)$$

Multiply both sides by 4:

$$C_D \rho_v v_t^{2} = \dfrac{4 d_p g}{3} (\rho_L - \rho_v)$$

### Step 4: Solve for $v_t$

$$v_t^{2} = \dfrac{4 d_p g (\rho_L - \rho_v)}{3 C_D \rho_v}$$

Taking the square root:

$$\boxed{v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 g d_p (\rho_L - \rho_v)}{3 C_D \rho_v}}}$$

This is the **general settling velocity equation**, valid in all flow regimes.

### Step 5: Special Case — Stokes' Law (Laminar Regime, $Re < 0.2$)
In the **laminar regime**, the drag coefficient is given by Stokes' Law:

$$C_D = \dfrac{24}{Re} = \dfrac{24 \mu_v}{\rho_v v_t d_p}$$

Substituting into the general equation:

$$v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 g d_p (\rho_L - \rho_v)}{3 \cdot \left(\dfrac{24 \mu_v}{\rho_v v_t d_p}\right) \cdot \rho_v}}$$

Simplify the denominator:

$$3 \cdot \dfrac{24 \mu_v}{\rho_v v_t d_p} \cdot \rho_v = \dfrac{72 \mu_v}{v_t d_p}$$

So:

$$v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 g d_p (\rho_L - \rho_v) v_t d_p}{72 \mu_v}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 g d_p^{2} (\rho_L - \rho_v) v_t}{72 \mu_v}}$$

Square both sides:

$$v_t^{2} = \dfrac{4 g d_p^{2} (\rho_L - \rho_v) v_t}{72 \mu_v}$$

Divide both sides by $v_t$:

$$v_t = \dfrac{4 g d_p^{2} (\rho_L - \rho_v)}{72 \mu_v} = \dfrac{g d_p^{2} (\rho_L - \rho_v)}{18 \mu_v}$$

$$\boxed{v_t = \dfrac{g d_p^{2} (\rho_L - \rho_v)}{18 \mu_v}} \quad \text{(Stokes' Law)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most-tested** settling velocity formula in KTU problems. Always check the Reynolds number first to confirm laminar flow.

### Step 6: Reynolds Number Check
The Reynolds number for the falling droplet:

$$Re = \dfrac{\rho_v v_t d_p}{\mu_v}$$

- If $Re < 0.2$: Use **Stokes' Law**.
- If $0.2 < Re < 500$: Use **intermediate** $C_D$ (e.g., $C_D = 18.5/Re^{0.6}$).
- If $500 < Re < 2 \times 10^{5}$: Use **Newton's Law** ($C_D \approx 0.44$).

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3 — Required Diameter of a Knock-Out Drum (or Demister Vessel)

### Objective
Derive the minimum vessel diameter to prevent re-entrainment of droplets.

### Step 1: Mass Balance on the Vapor Stream
Vapor mass flow rate is $\dot{m}_v$ [kg/s]. The maximum allowed superficial velocity through the vessel cross-section is $v_{max}$ [m/s], given by the Souders-Brown criterion:

$$v_{max} = K \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_v}{\rho_v}}$$

### Step 2: Continuity Equation
For vapor flowing through a vessel of cross-sectional area $A$ [m²]:

$$\dot{m}_v = \rho_v \cdot v_{max} \cdot A = \rho_v \cdot v_{max} \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$$

### Step 3: Solve for Diameter $D$

$$\dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_v}{\rho_v v_{max}}$$

$$D^{2} = \dfrac{4 \dot{m}_v}{\pi \rho_v v_{max}}$$

$$\boxed{D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \dot{m}_v}{\pi \rho_v v_{max}}}}$$

### Step 4: Application to Demister Pads
When sizing a vessel containing a **wire-mesh demister**, the calculated $D$ is the **inside diameter of the vessel at the demister location**, and $v_{max}$ is found using the K-factor for the required drop removal efficiency.

---

## 4.3 Cyclone Separator — Internal Flow Paths

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Tangential Vapor Inlet] -->|High Velocity| B[Outer Vortex Downward]
    B --> C[Wall Liquid Film]
    C --> D[Bottom Sump Liquid Drain]

```

**Engineering Notes:**
- The **outer vortex** carries liquid droplets outward by centrifugal force.
- The **inner vortex** (low-pressure core) carries clean vapor upward.

# 🎯 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define entrainment. List any two entrainment separators used in chemical process industries.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Entrainment is the carryover of liquid droplets along with vapor in process equipment such as evaporators, distillation columns, and condensers. It occurs due to high vapor velocities, foaming, or sudden pressure changes.

Two entrainment separators:
1. **Cyclone separator** — uses centrifugal force to separate droplets from vapor.
2. **Wire-mesh demister** — uses impingement on fine wire mesh to coalesce and capture droplets.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for two devices.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a steam-jet ejector and a liquid-ring vacuum pump (any three points).**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per point):**

| Feature | Steam-Jet Ejector | Liquid-Ring Pump |
|---|---|---|
| Working principle | Momentum transfer from high-P steam to suction vapor (no moving parts). | Mechanical impeller with liquid seal (rotating parts). |
| Achievable vacuum | Very high (up to 0.05 mm Hg with multi-stage). | Moderate (5–25 mm Hg abs). |
| Suitability for wet vapors | Poor; requires upstream entrainment separator. | Excellent; seal liquid handles wet streams. |

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Recommended Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Module 3, Q8(a)]**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**A single-stage steam-jet ejector is used to maintain a vacuum of 700 mm Hg (vacuum gauge) in an evaporator. The discharge pressure is 100 mm Hg (absolute) and the motive steam pressure is 8 bar (gauge). The saturated steam properties are: enthalpy of motive steam $h_m = 2776$ kJ/kg, enthalpy of mixed discharge vapor $h_d = 2625$ kJ/kg, enthalpy of suction vapor $h_s = 2300$ kJ/kg. If the mass flow rate of vapor to be removed is 0.05 kg/s, calculate: (i) the compression ratio, (ii) the mass flow rate of motive steam required.**

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a wire-mesh demister pad. State the Souders-Brown criterion and give the typical K-value for 3-micron droplet capture.**

---

### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION A:**

#### **Part (a) — Full Step-by-Step Solution [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Convert all pressures to absolute (kPa) for uniformity. (1 mark)**

$$P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$$

$$P_{vac,gauge} = 700 \text{ mm Hg} = \dfrac{700}{760} \times 101.325 = 93.32 \text{ kPa (gauge)}$$

$$P_{suction,abs} = P_{atm} - P_{vac,gauge} = 101.325 - 93.32 = 8.0 \text{ kPa (abs)}$$

$$P_{discharge,abs} = 100 \text{ mm Hg abs} = \dfrac{100}{760} \times 101.325 = 13.33 \text{ kPa (abs)}$$

$$P_{motive,abs} = 8 \text{ bar gauge} + 1.01325 \text{ bar atm} = 9.01325 \text{ bar} = 901.325 \text{ kPa (abs)}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the compression ratio. (1 mark)**

$$r = \dfrac{P_{d}}{P_{s}} = \dfrac{13.33}{8.0} = 1.667$$

**Step 3: Write the steam mass flow rate equation. (1 mark)**

From the energy balance on the ejector (assuming no heat loss and adiabatic mixing):

$$\dot{m}_{steam} \cdot h_m + \dot{m}_{vapor} \cdot h_s = (\dot{m}_{steam} + \dot{m}_{vapor}) \cdot h_d$$

**Step 4: Rearrange to solve for motive steam mass flow. (1.5 marks)**

$$\dot{m}_{steam} \cdot h_m - \dot{m}_{steam} \cdot h_d = \dot{m}_{vapor} \cdot h_d - \dot{m}_{vapor} \cdot h_s$$

$$\dot{m}_{steam} \cdot (h_m - h_d) = \dot{m}_{vapor} \cdot (h_d - h_s)$$

$$\dot{m}_{steam} = \dot{m}_{vapor} \cdot \dfrac{h_d - h_s}{h_m - h_d}$$

**Step 5: Substitute the numerical values. (1.5 marks)**

$$\dot{m}_{steam} = 0.05 \times \dfrac{2625 - 2300}{2776 - 2625}$$

$$\dot{m}_{steam} = 0.05 \times \dfrac{325}{151}$$

$$\dot{m}_{steam} = 0.05 \times 2.1523$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{steam} = 0.1076 \text{ kg/s} \approx 0.108 \text{ kg/s}}$$

**Step 6: State the result. (1 mark)**
- Compression ratio = **1.667** (dimensionless).
- Motive steam required = **0.108 kg/s** (≈ 6.46 kg/min).

---

#### **Part (b) — Full Solution [7 marks]**

**Construction (2.5 marks):**
A wire-mesh demister is a pad of knitted metal (or plastic) wire formed into a dense, multilayered mat, typically 100–150 mm thick, with **96–98% voidage**. It is installed horizontally inside a vessel, perpendicular to the vapor flow. The mesh layers are sandwiched between top and bottom support grids.

**Working (2.5 marks):**
1. Wet vapor enters the bottom of the vessel and rises.
2. Vapor flows through the mesh, but droplets (because of inertia) **impinge** on the wire surfaces.
3. Liquid films on the wires **coalesce** into larger droplets.
4. The larger drops become heavy enough to fall back against the upward vapor flow.
5. The collected liquid drains to a sump and is returned to the process.
6. The cleaned vapor exits at the top.

**Souders-Brown Criterion (1 mark):**
$$v_{max} = K \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_v}{\rho_v}}$$

**Typical K-value for 3-µm capture (1 mark):**
$$K = 0.083 \text{ m/s} \quad (\text{approx. } 0.27 \text{ ft/s})$$

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, Q8(b)]**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**A vertical knock-out drum is to handle 0.2 kg/s of vapor with density 2.5 kg/m³. The liquid to be removed has density 1100 kg/m³, and the smallest droplet to be captured is 100 µm. The vapor viscosity is $1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s. Using Stokes' law, calculate: (i) the terminal settling velocity of the droplet, (ii) the maximum allowable vapor velocity in the drum (using a safety factor of 0.15), (iii) the minimum diameter of the drum.**

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Describe the working of a three-stage steam-jet ejector system with intercondensers. Why are intercondensers used between stages?**

---

### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION B:**

#### **Part (a) — Full Step-by-Step Solution [7 marks]**

**Given Data:**
- $\dot{m}_v = 0.2$ kg/s
- $\rho_v = 2.5$ kg/m³
- $\rho_L = 1100$ kg/m³
- $d_p = 100 \text{ µm} = 100 \times 10^{-6} = 1 \times 10^{-4}$ m
- $\mu_v = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s
- Safety factor (fraction of settling velocity) = **0.15**

**Step 1: Apply Stokes' Law to find terminal velocity. (2 marks)**

$$v_t = \dfrac{g \cdot d_p^{2} \cdot (\rho_L - \rho_v)}{18 \cdot \mu_v}$$

$$v_t = \dfrac{9.81 \times (1 \times 10^{-4})^{2} \times (1100 - 2.5)}{18 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$v_t = \dfrac{9.81 \times 1 \times 10^{-8} \times 1097.5}{3.24 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$v_t = \dfrac{9.81 \times 1097.5 \times 10^{-8}}{3.24 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$v_t = \dfrac{1.0765 \times 10^{-4}}{3.24 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$v_t = 0.3323 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v_t = 0.332 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step 2: Verify Stokes' Law is valid (Reynolds number check). (1 mark)**

$$Re = \dfrac{\rho_v v_t d_p}{\mu_v} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 0.3323 \times 1 \times 10^{-4}}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$Re = \dfrac{8.31 \times 10^{-5}}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}} = 4.62$$

Since $Re > 0.2$, **Stokes' law is NOT strictly valid**. We should use the intermediate regime formula. However, for a **3-marks KTU sub-question**, the Stokes' calculation is the expected approach. (Note: KTU may accept a comment that the answer is approximate.)

**Step 3: Calculate maximum allowable vapor velocity. (1 mark)**

$$v_{max} = \text{Safety factor} \times v_t = 0.15 \times 0.3323 = 0.0499 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v_{max} \approx 0.05 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step 4: Calculate minimum drum diameter. (2 marks)**

$$D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot \dot{m}_v}{\pi \cdot \rho_v \cdot v_{max}}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 0.2}{\pi \times 2.5 \times 0.0499}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.8}{0.3920}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{2.041}$$

$$\boxed{D = 1.428 \text{ m} \approx 1.43 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 5: State final answer. (1 mark)**
- Terminal velocity: **0.332 m/s**
- Maximum allowable velocity: **0.05 m/s**
- Minimum drum diameter: **1.43 m**

---

#### **Part (b) — Full Solution [7 marks]**

**Working of 3-Stage Steam-Jet Ejector (5 marks):**

1. **First Stage:** Motive steam (typically 8–10 bar) expands through the motive nozzle, creating a vacuum that draws vapor from the process (e.g., an evaporator). The mixed stream discharges at an intermediate pressure.
2. **First Intercondenser:** The discharge from stage 1 passes through a **shell-and-tube heat exchanger** using cooling water. The condensable water vapor (from the motive steam) condenses out and drains to a hot well. Only non-condensable gases + any uncondensed vapor pass to stage 2.
3. **Second Stage:** Uses fresh motive steam to compress the remaining gases from the intercondenser pressure to a higher intermediate pressure.
4. **Second Intercondenser:** Condenses water vapor from stage 2.
5. **Third Stage:** Final compression to atmospheric (or slightly above) for discharge.
6. **Final Discharge:** To a vent stack or to a final after-condenser.

**Why Intercondensers Are Used (2 marks):**
1. **Reduce load on subsequent stages:** By removing condensable vapor early, each downstream stage handles a smaller gas volume → smaller ejector, lower steam consumption.
2. **Improve overall efficiency:** Compression of liquids is more efficient than compression of vapors; condensation reduces the work needed in later stages.
3. **Recover pure water:** The condensate from intercondensers is distilled water that can be reused as boiler feedwater.
4. **Achieve lower absolute pressures:** Multi-staging with intercondensation is the only practical way to reach very high vacuums (e.g., 1 mm Hg abs) at industrial scale.

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **Pressure Unit Mixing:** Conflating gauge, absolute, and vacuum gauge pressures. Always convert to **absolute kPa or mm Hg** before applying any logarithmic or ratio-based formula. (Penalty: -2 marks per sub-question.)
> 2. **Stokes' Law Validity:** Applying Stokes' Law without checking the Reynolds number. For droplets > 50 µm, $Re$ usually exceeds 0.2, and the **intermediate regime formula** is required. (Penalty: -1 to -2 marks.)
> 3. **Ejector Pressure Ratio Direction:** Students often write the ratio inverted. The convention is $P_d / P_s$ where $P_d > P_s$ → ratio > 1. Reversing gives nonsensical results.
> 4. **Forgetting the n+0.5 Correction for the First Stage:** When the question asks for **per-stage compression ratio** in a multi-stage ejector, do NOT use $1/n$ for the first stage; use $1/(n+0.5)$.
> 5. **Using the Wrong K-Value:** For wire-mesh demisters, the K-value depends on the **required droplet removal size**. Do not use the maximum K (0.41 m/s) unless the question specifically allows it.
> 6. **Diameter Units:** Final answer for diameter must be in **meters** (or consistent SI units). Do not leave it in cm or mm.
> 7. **Missing Sketch in Descriptive Questions:** KTU deducts up to **2 marks** if a "neat sketch" is asked but not provided. Always include a labeled diagram, even if simple.

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 3: Vacuum Devices & Entrainment Separators**

### 🔑 Core Concepts
- **Vacuum devices** create and maintain sub-atmospheric pressure to lower boiling points, protecting heat-sensitive materials.
- **Entrainment separators** remove liquid droplets from vapor streams to protect downstream equipment (especially vacuum devices).
- The two are **always coupled** in industrial evaporators.

### 🔑 Key Formulas (Memorize All)

| Formula | Purpose |
|---|---|
| $\dfrac{P_d}{P_s} = \left(\dfrac{P_m}{P_s}\right)^{1/n}$ | Per-stage compression ratio in multi-stage ejector |
| $v_{max} = K \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_v}{\rho_v}}$ | Souders-Brown max vapor velocity |
| $v_t = \dfrac{g d_p^{2} (\rho_L - \rho_v)}{18 \mu_v}$ | Stokes' Law (laminar) settling velocity |
| $v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 g d_p (\rho_L - \rho_v)}{3 C_D \rho_v}}$ | General settling velocity |
| $\dot{m}_{steam} = \dot{m}_v \cdot \dfrac{h_d - h_s}{h_m - h_d}$ | Motive steam requirement |
| $D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \dot{m}_v}{\pi \rho_v v_{max}}}$ | Minimum KOD/demister diameter |

### 🔑 Equipment Selection Rules
- **Steam ejector:** No moving parts, highest vacuum, needs dry vapor.
- **Liquid-ring pump:** Handles wet vapor, moderate vacuum.
- **Cyclone:** For 10+ µm drops, low pressure drop.
- **Wire-mesh demister:** For 3+ µm drops, highest efficiency.
- **Knock-out drum:** For bulk liquid removal at low velocity.
- **Intercondensers:** Used between ejector stages to reduce load and recover distilled water.

### 🔑 Constants to Remember
- $P_{atm}$ = **101.325 kPa** = **760 mm Hg** = **1.01325 bar**
- $g$ = **9.81 m/s²**
- Stokes' Law valid for **Re < 0.2**
- Intermediate regime for **0.2 < Re < 500** ($C_D = 18.5/Re^{0.6}$)
- Newton's regime for **500 < Re < 2×10⁵** ($C_D ≈ 0.44$)
- K-factor for 3-µm mesh: **0.083 m/s**; max: **0.41 m/s**

### 🔑 Conversion Table (Frequently Needed)
- 1 bar = **100 kPa** = **750.06 mm Hg**
- 1 atm = **101.325 kPa** = **760 mm Hg**
- Vacuum gauge: $P_{abs} = P_{atm} - P_{vac}$

### 🔑 Common KTU Question Patterns
1. **"Calculate compression ratio + motive steam rate"** → Use the energy balance formula.
2. **"Determine KOD diameter for X vapor flow"** → Use Souders-Brown + continuity.
3. **"Explain with sketch"** → Always include a labeled diagram.
4. **"Compare two devices"** → Use a 3-column or 4-column table.
5. **"Why are intercondensers used?"** → 4 reasons (load reduction, efficiency, water recovery, deeper vacuum).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single Effect and Multiple Effect Evaporators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Single Effect and Multiple Effect Evaporators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Single Effect and Multiple Effect Evaporators

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Evaporation — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Evaporation** is a unit operation in which a volatile solvent (usually water) is vaporised from a non-volatile solute solution by supplying heat, thereby producing a concentrated liquor (thick liquor) and a vapour stream. It is fundamentally a **heat-transfer-controlled mass-transfer process** occurring at the boiling point of the solution.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, the term *evaporator* refers to an indirect-heat, shell-and-tube (or calandria-type) exchanger in which the heating medium is saturated steam and the process fluid is a non-volatile solution. The **vapour produced is almost always water vapour** when the solvent is water.

### 1.2 Single Effect Evaporator

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single Effect Evaporator** — An evaporator in which the vapour produced from the boiling solution is condensed and discarded (or sent elsewhere) after a single pass through **only one** heating surface (calandria). Steam is supplied once, vapour leaves once, and the process is completed in one stage.

- The latent heat of the vapour is **NOT reused** to vaporise more solvent.
- Economical only for small-scale operations or viscous, fouling liquors.
- Operating pressure may be atmospheric or vacuum.

### 1.3 Multiple Effect Evaporator

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multiple Effect Evaporator** — A cascade of *n* (where n ≥ 2) single-effect evaporators operated at successively lower pressures, in which the **vapour produced in one effect (the *ith* effect) is used as the heating medium in the next effect (the *(i+1)th* effect)**. Steam is fed only to the first effect.

- The driving force is the **temperature drop** between the first effect (highest pressure, condensing steam) and the last effect (lowest pressure, connected to condenser/vacuum).
- This arrangement conserves steam dramatically and is the standard industrial configuration.

### 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — Boiling Rice at Home**
>
> Imagine you must cook and reduce 10 L of milk to 1 L. You can do it in a **single pot** on the stove (single effect) — you keep adding heat, and the steam rises out of your kitchen window. The energy escapes unused.
>
> Now imagine a **multi-storey distillation column** in a sugar refinery: the vapour from the bottom tray rises, warms the liquid on the tray above, condenses, and releases its latent heat. Each tray "recycles" the heat. This is exactly what a multiple-effect evaporator does — each effect reuses the latent heat of the vapour produced in the previous effect, dramatically cutting the fresh steam requirement.

### 1.5 Key Terminology and Standard Metrics

| Term | Symbol | Standard Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Feed rate | $F$ | **kg h⁻¹** | Dilute solution entering |
| Feed concentration | $x_F$ | mass fraction | Solute mass fraction in feed |
| Exit (thick) liquor rate | $L$ | **kg h⁻¹** | Concentrated solution leaving |
| Exit liquor concentration | $x_L$ | mass fraction | Solute mass fraction in product |
| Vapour produced | $V$ | **kg h⁻¹** | Solvent removed as vapour |
| Steam consumed | $S$ | **kg h⁻¹** | Fresh heating steam |
| Steam pressure / temperature | $T_s$ | **kPa (abs) / °C** | Heating medium conditions |
| Last-effect pressure / temperature | $T_v$ | **kPa (abs) / °C** | Vacuum conditions |
| Heat-transfer area | $A$ | **m²** | Calandria heat-transfer surface |
| Overall heat-transfer coefficient | $U$ | **W m⁻² K⁻¹** | Typically 1500–3000 for evaporators |
| Capacity | — | **kg water h⁻¹** | Total water evaporated per hour |
| Economy | $e$ | dimensionless | kg water evaporated per kg steam |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle of a Single Effect Evaporator

A dilute solution enters the evaporator, **saturated steam** condenses on the outer side of the calandria tubes, gives up its latent heat $\lambda_s$, and the heat is transferred through the tube wall to the boiling solution. The solvent vapour leaves the top, while the concentrated liquor is withdrawn from the bottom. A **vapour space** above the boiling liquid allows separation of entrained droplets.

**Operating Steps (Logic Sequence):**

1. Feed $F$ at $t_F$ enters the evaporator.
2. Steam $S$ at $T_s$ condenses on the calandria, releasing latent heat $S \cdot \lambda_s$.
3. Heat is transferred through the wall at rate $Q = U A (T_s - T_b)$ where $T_b$ is the boiling point of the solution.
4. Solvent vapour $V$ leaves at $T_b$ with enthalpy $H_v$.
5. Concentrated liquor $L$ leaves at $T_b$ (assumed to leave at the boiling point).
6. Steam condensate leaves at $T_s$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumption (Standard KTU assumption):** The boiling point of the solution at the operating pressure is taken as the **reference temperature** for the vapour and the leaving liquor. This simplifies energy balances considerably.

## 2.2 Operating Principle of Multiple Effect Evaporators

A *multiple effect* is a *series* of single-effect units operating at progressively lower pressures. The total available temperature drop $\Delta T_{total} = T_{s1} - T_{vn}$ is distributed over the $n$ effects.

**Why does it work?**
- In the first effect, steam condenses and vaporises some solvent. The vapour then enters the calandria of the second effect (where pressure is lower) and itself condenses, releasing its latent heat to vaporise solvent in the second effect.
- The process repeats, with each effect producing vapour from the previous effect's vapour.
- **The total water evaporated is the sum of the vapour from each effect** (excluding the first effect's vapour which is partly condensed in the next effect's heating side).

**Net result:** Approximately 0.85 to 1.0 kg of water evaporated per kg of fresh steam **per effect**, giving an economy of roughly $0.85n$ to $1.0n$ for $n$ effects.

## 2.3 Feed Configurations in Multiple Effect Evaporators

The manner in which the feed liquor is introduced defines three standard arrangements:

### (a) Forward Feed (Co-current with steam and vapour)

> [!NOTE]
> Feed and steam/vapour flow in the **same direction** — from effect 1 to effect n. Pressure is highest in effect 1 and lowest in effect n.

- **Advantage:** No pumps required between effects — flow is driven by the natural pressure gradient.
- **Disadvantage:** Cold feed meets the hottest effect, and the most viscous (concentrated) liquor leaves the coldest effect — viscosity and hence resistance is high at the end, where temperature is low.

### (b) Backward Feed (Counter-current with steam and vapour)

> [!NOTE]
> Feed enters the **last (coldest) effect** and is pumped from one effect to the next, finally leaving the **hottest first effect**. Steam still enters the first effect, vapour flows 1 → n.

- **Advantage:** Hot, concentrated liquor meets the hottest surface — viscosity is lower, heat-transfer coefficients are higher.
- **Disadvantage:** Pumps required between every effect because the feed must be moved from a low-pressure region to a high-pressure region.

### (c) Parallel Feed

> [!NOTE]
> Fresh feed is supplied **separately to each effect**; thick liquor is withdrawn separately from each effect.

- **Advantage:** Used when the solution is highly viscous or when crystallisation occurs.
- **Disadvantage:** Lowest economy because the full latent heat cannot be cascaded; thick liquor is removed from each effect at its own boiling point.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master these equations — they cover ~90% of all KTU Part B numerical problems on evaporators.**

| # | Equation | Description / Conditions |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V = F\!\left(1 - \dfrac{x_F}{x_L}\right)$ | **Vapour produced** (overall material + solute balance) |
| 2 | $L = F - V$ | Thick liquor rate |
| 3 | $S \cdot \lambda_s = F \cdot C_{pF}(T_b - t_F) + V \cdot \lambda_v$ | **Energy balance** (feed at $t_F$, vapour and liquor at $T_b$); $\lambda_v$ = latent heat of vapour at $T_b$ |
| 4 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Rate of heat transfer through calandria |
| 5 | $Q = S \cdot \lambda_s$ | Heat supplied by condensing steam |
| 6 | $e = \dfrac{V}{S}$ | **Steam economy** of single effect |
| 7 | $e_{n} = n \cdot e_{single} \approx (0.85 \text{ to } 1.0)\,n$ | Approximate economy of *n*-effect system (water-based feed, no BPE) |
| 8 | $\Delta T_{avail} = T_{s1} - T_{vn}$ | Gross available temperature drop |
| 9 | $\Delta T_{true} = \Delta T_{avail} - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta'_i - \Delta T_{loss}$ | Net available $\Delta T$ after BPE and losses |
| 10 | $\Delta T_i = \dfrac{\Delta T_{true}}{n}$ (for equal areas) | Equal-area design assumption |
| 11 | $A_i = \dfrac{Q_i}{U_i \cdot \Delta T_i}$ | Area of each effect |
| 12 | $Q_1 = U_1 A_1 \Delta T_1 = Q_2 = \dots = Q_n$ (equal area) | **Equal-area constraint** |
| 13 | $\dfrac{C_a}{C_i} = \left(\dfrac{\Delta T_{true}}{\Delta T_{avail}}\right)^{0.9}$ | **Capacity correction factor** due to BPE |
| 14 | $\dfrac{e_a}{e_i} = 0.9 \cdot \dfrac{\Delta T_{true}}{\Delta T_{avail}}$ | **Economy correction factor** (approximate) |
| 15 | $T_{bp} = f(P)$ via **Dühring's rule** | Boiling point of solution vs water |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Multiple-effect evaporators are critical in: **sugar industry** (juice concentration), **paper & pulp** (black liquor), **dairy industry** (milk concentration), **caustic-chlor-alkali** (NaOH concentration), **desalination** (multi-stage flash is conceptually similar), and **pharma/fermentation broths** (downstream concentration). Mastering this topic directly maps to industrial energy-optimisation practice.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Vapour Produced in an Evaporator (Material Balance)

**Total material balance over the evaporator (steady state):**

$$F = L + V$$

**Solute balance** (solute is non-volatile; it neither leaves with the vapour nor accumulates):

$$F \, x_F = L \, x_L$$

Since $L = F - V$, substitute into the solute balance:

$$F \, x_F = (F - V) \, x_L$$

$$F \, x_F = F \, x_L - V \, x_L$$

Rearrange for $V$:

$$V \, x_L = F \, x_L - F \, x_F$$

$$V \, x_L = F \, (x_L - x_F)$$

Dividing both sides by $x_L$:

$$\boxed{\,V = F\!\left(1 - \dfrac{x_F}{x_L}\right)\,}$$

**Logical interpretation:** The fraction $\dfrac{x_F}{x_L}$ represents the mass of feed that remains as thick liquor; therefore $1 - \dfrac{x_F}{x_L}$ is the mass fraction of feed that becomes vapour.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy Balance for a Single Effect Evaporator

**Reference state:** Take the boiling point $T_b$ of the solution as the reference temperature, and take liquid water at $0°C$ as the reference enthalpy.

Enthalpies of all streams:
- Feed enters at $t_F$ with enthalpy $h_F = C_{pF}(t_F - 0) = C_{pF}\,t_F$
- Steam enters at $T_s$ with enthalpy $H_s = \lambda_s + C_{pw}(T_s - 0)$; we use saturated steam so $H_s = \lambda_s$
- Vapour leaves at $T_b$ with enthalpy $H_v = \lambda_v$ (latent heat of vapour at $T_b$)
- Thick liquor leaves at $T_b$ with enthalpy $h_L = C_{pL}(T_b - 0) \approx C_{pL} \, T_b$
- Condensate leaves at $T_s$ with enthalpy $h_c = C_{pw} \, T_s \approx 0$ if taken as saturated liquid at $T_s$ and reference is liquid water at $0°C$ — we treat the enthalpy of saturated liquid as $\approx 0$ for simplicity at this level (a stricter analysis would retain it, but KTU problems accept the simplified form).

**Energy in = Energy out + Accumulation (steady state ⇒ 0)**

$$F \, h_F + S \, \lambda_s = L \, h_L + V \, H_v + S \, h_c$$

Substituting enthalpies, with $h_c \approx 0$ (saturated liquid at $T_s$, ref. at $0°C$ approximated), and recognising $L \, h_L$ is small relative to the other terms for dilute aqueous solutions, the standard KTU form simplifies to:

$$F \, C_{pF}(T_b - t_F) + S \, \lambda_s = V \, \lambda_v$$

Rearranging for steam consumption:

$$\boxed{\,S = \dfrac{F \, C_{pF}(T_b - t_F) + V \, \lambda_v}{\lambda_s}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reasoning of simplification:** The term $L \, h_L$ has been absorbed into the temperature-difference $T_b - t_F$, because the liquor leaves at $T_b$ and the feed enters at $t_F$. The sensible heat to raise the feed from $t_F$ to $T_b$ is $F C_{pF} (T_b - t_F)$, and the latent heat to vaporise water is $V \lambda_v$. Together they constitute the heat load $Q$.

The heat-transfer rate is therefore:

$$Q = S \, \lambda_s = F \, C_{pF}(T_b - t_F) + V \, \lambda_v$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Capacity and Economy Definitions

**Capacity** of an evaporator (in kg of water evaporated per hour):

$$\text{Capacity} = V \;\; [\text{kg h}^{-1}]$$

**Economy** (also called **steam economy**) is the ratio of water evaporated to steam consumed:

$$e = \dfrac{V}{S}$$

**Combining with energy balance:**

Since $Q = S \lambda_s$ and $Q = V \lambda_v + F C_{pF}(T_b - t_F)$, we have:

$$S \, \lambda_s = V \, \lambda_v + F \, C_{pF}(T_b - t_F)$$

For the limiting case of a **saturated liquid feed at the boiling point** ($t_F = T_b$), the sensible-heat term vanishes:

$$S \, \lambda_s = V \, \lambda_v \quad\Rightarrow\quad \dfrac{V}{S} = \dfrac{\lambda_s}{\lambda_v}$$

For water near atmospheric pressure, $\lambda_s \approx \lambda_v$, giving $e \to 1$ for a single effect. In practice, $e \approx 0.8$ for cold-feed single effect.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Temperature Distribution in an *n*-Effect Evaporator

**Total available temperature drop** between the steam of the first effect and the vapour space of the last effect:

$$\Delta T_{avail} = T_{s1} - T_{vn}$$

**The first effect is at the highest pressure** (condensing steam gives the highest temperature). The last effect is at the lowest pressure (condenser/vacuum).

**Equal-area assumption:** For an *optimal* design, all effects have the **same heat-transfer area**, and the heat load on each effect is equal ($Q_1 = Q_2 = \dots = Q_n$).

**Step 1 — Total heat load equals total steam energy supplied:**

$$Q_{total} = S \, \lambda_{s1} = n \, Q_{per\,effect}$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad Q_{per\,effect} = \dfrac{S \, \lambda_{s1}}{n}$$

**Step 2 — Equal-area constraint gives equal $\Delta T$ scaled by $U$:**

Since $A_i = A$ for all $i$ and $Q_i = U_i A \Delta T_i$ is constant, then:

$$U_1 \, \Delta T_1 = U_2 \, \Delta T_2 = \dots = U_n \, \Delta T_n = \text{const}$$

If $U_i = U$ (uniform coefficients), the temperature drop across each effect is:

$$\Delta T_i = \dfrac{\Delta T_{avail}}{n} = \dfrac{T_{s1} - T_{vn}}{n}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **If $U_i$ varies between effects, the temperature distribution is NOT equal.** In that case, one must assume an arbitrary distribution, compute the areas, and iterate. KTU problems almost always assume equal areas and equal $U$ unless stated otherwise.

**Step 3 — Boiling Point Elevation (BPE) correction:**

The "true" temperature drop available for heat transfer is reduced by BPE in each effect:

$$\Delta T_{true} = \Delta T_{avail} - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta'_i - \Delta T_{loss}$$

where $\Delta'_i$ is the boiling-point elevation of the solution in the *i*th effect (obtained from Dühring's rule), and $\Delta T_{loss}$ accounts for friction, static-head, and other minor losses (often taken as 1–2°C total).

The corrected equal-area temperature drop per effect:

$$\Delta T_{i,\,true} = \dfrac{\Delta T_{true}}{n} = \dfrac{\Delta T_{avail} - \sum \Delta'_i - \Delta T_{loss}}{n}$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Effect of BPE on Capacity and Economy

### Capacity

The **ideal capacity** (no BPE, no losses) is the total water evaporated when the full $\Delta T_{avail}$ is used:

$$C_i = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{\lambda_v} = \dfrac{U A \Delta T_{avail}}{\lambda_v}$$

The **actual capacity** with BPE uses only the *true* temperature drop:

$$C_a = \dfrac{U A \Delta T_{true}}{\lambda_v} = \dfrac{U A (\Delta T_{avail} - \sum \Delta'_i - \Delta T_{loss})}{\lambda_v}$$

Ratio:

$$\dfrac{C_a}{C_i} = \dfrac{\Delta T_{true}}{\Delta T_{avail}}$$

A more accurate empirical correction (used in industry) is:

$$\dfrac{C_a}{C_i} = \left(\dfrac{\Delta T_{true}}{\Delta T_{avail}}\right)^{0.9}$$

This is the **capacity correction factor (CCF)**.

### Economy

The economy of an *n*-effect system is:

$$e = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i}{S}$$

In the ideal case (saturated feed, no BPE, $U$ constant), $\sum V_i \approx n \cdot V_{1} \approx 0.85\,nS$ to $1.0\,nS$.

With BPE, the average latent heat of evaporation $\bar{\lambda_v}$ increases (lower pressures), and the temperature drops per effect shrink, reducing the vapour per effect. The empirical correction:

$$\dfrac{e_a}{e_i} \approx 0.9 \cdot \dfrac{\Delta T_{true}}{\Delta T_{avail}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** A higher BPE means a *larger* portion of the gross temperature drop is "wasted" in raising the solution to its boiling point rather than driving evaporation. Hence, both capacity and economy decrease as BPE increases.

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Optimum Number of Effects (Cost-Based Criterion)

Define:
- $C_{steam}$ = cost of steam per kg, **₹/kg**
- $C_{cap}$ = fixed + operating cost per effect per year, **₹/effect·yr**
- $D$ = total water to be evaporated per year, **kg/yr**
- $n$ = number of effects
- Economy of *n*-effect system: $e_n \approx 0.85 n$ (typical, for water)

**Annual cost of steam:**

$$\text{Cost}_{steam} = D \cdot \dfrac{C_{steam}}{e_n} = \dfrac{D \cdot C_{steam}}{0.85\,n}$$

**Annual capital + operating cost (proportional to $n$):**

$$\text{Cost}_{cap+op} = n \cdot C_{cap}$$

**Total annual cost:**

$$\text{Cost}_{total}(n) = \dfrac{D \cdot C_{steam}}{0.85\,n} + n \cdot C_{cap}$$

**Optimum $n$** is found by differentiating with respect to $n$ and equating to zero:

$$\dfrac{d(\text{Cost}_{total})}{dn} = -\dfrac{D \cdot C_{steam}}{0.85\,n^2} + C_{cap} = 0$$

$$\dfrac{D \cdot C_{steam}}{0.85\,n^2} = C_{cap}$$

Solving for the optimum $n$:

$$\boxed{\,n_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{D \cdot C_{steam}}{0.85 \, C_{cap}}}\,}$$

> [!TIP]
> In practice, $n_{opt}$ for aqueous solutions in KTU problems is **typically 4 to 6**. Beyond 6 effects, capital cost outweighs steam savings for most industrial salts.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Single Effect Evaporator — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph sIn[Inlet Streams]
        nFeed[Feed F at temperature tF and concentration xF]
        nSteam[Steam S at temperature Ts and latent heat lambdas]
    end
    subgraph sCal[Calandria Heat Exchanger]
        nCal[Calandria Tubes Wall resistance 1 over U]
    end
    subgraph sOut[Outlet Streams]
        nVap[Vapour V at boiling point Tb latent heat lambdav]
        nLiq[Thick Liquor L at Tb concentration xL]
        nCond[Steam Condensate at Ts]
        nVent[Non condensable Vent]
    end
    nFeed --> nCal
    nSteam --> nCal
    nCal --> nVap
    nCal --> nLiq
    nCal --> nCond
    nCal --> nVent
    nFeed -.Solutes remain in solution.-> nLiq
    nSteam -.Latent heat released.-> nCond
    nCal -.Sensible plus latent heat.-> nVap
```

> [!NOTE]
> The thick-liquor stream carries all the **solute**; the vapour stream is essentially **pure solvent**. The steam condensate carries no solute and no solvent that wasn't already present in the steam supply. This is the essence of evaporator design.

## 4.2 Multiple Effect Forward Feed — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nS[Steam S into Effect 1] --> nE1[Effect 1 Highest Pressure Ts1]
    nF1[Feed F to Effect 1] --> nE1
    nE1 --> nL1[Thick liquor L1 to Effect 2]
    nE1 --> nV1[Vapour V1 to Calandria of Effect 2]
    nL1 --> nE2[Effect 2]
    nV1 --> nE2
    nE2 --> nL2[Thick liquor L2 to Effect 3]
    nE2 --> nV2[Vapour V2 to Calandria of Effect 3]
    nL2 --> nE3[Effect n Lowest Pressure]
    nV2 --> nE3
    nE3 --> nLn[Final Thick Liquor Ln]
    nE3 --> nVn[Final Vapour Vn to Condenser]
    nVn --> nCond[Condenser Vacuum]
```

## 4.3 Backward Feed — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nS[Steam S into Effect 1] --> nE1[Effect 1 Highest T Highest P]
    nF[Feed F] --> nEn[Effect n Lowest T Vacuum]
    nEn --> nLn1[Thick liquor to Effect n minus 1]
    nLn1 --> nEmid[Effect n minus 1]
    nEmid --> nLm1[Thick liquor to Effect 2]
    nLm1 --> nE2[Effect 2]
    nE2 --> nL1[Thick liquor to Effect 1]
    nL1 --> nE1
    nE1 --> nLfinal[Final Thick Liquor L Hot concentrated]
    nE1 --> nV1[Vapour V1 to Calandria of Effect 2]
    nV1 --> nE2
    nE2 --> nV2[Vapour V2 to Calandria of Effect 3]
    nEn --> nVn[Final Vapour Vn to Condenser]
    nVn --> nCond[Condenser]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **backward feed**, the feed moves **opposite** to the steam/vapour flow. Pumps are required between effects because the liquor must be pushed from a low-pressure region to a high-pressure region. The benefit is that the **most concentrated, most viscous liquor meets the hottest surface**, raising heat-transfer coefficients.

## 4.4 Parallel Feed — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nF[Feed F distributed] --> nE1[Effect 1]
    nF --> nE2[Effect 2]
    nF --> nE3[Effect n]
    nS[Steam S] --> nE1
    nE1 --> nV1[Vapour V1 to Calandria of Effect 2]
    nE1 --> nL1[Thick liquor L1 discharged]
    nV1 --> nE2
    nE2 --> nV2[Vapour V2 to Calandria of Effect 3]
    nE2 --> nL2[Thick liquor L2 discharged]
    nEn --> nVn[Vapour Vn to Condenser]
    nEn --> nLn[Thick liquor Ln discharged]
```

## 4.5 Temperature & Pressure Profile Across Multiple Effects

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph prof[Pressure and Temperature Profile Effect 1 to n]
        nP1[Effect 1 Highest P Ts1] --> nP2[Effect 2 P2 less than P1 Ts2]
        nP2 --> nP3[Effect 3 P3 less than P2 Ts3]
        nP3 --> nPn[Effect n Lowest P Tvn Vacuum]
    end
    nP1 -.Steam enters here.-> nS
    nPn -.Vapour to condenser.-> nC
```

> [!TIP]
> The **temperature decreases monotonically** from effect 1 to effect n. The vapour from each effect is at a temperature **higher** than the boiling point of the next effect (by an amount equal to the available $\Delta T$ for that effect), which is precisely what allows it to act as the heating medium for the next effect.

## 4.6 Block Diagram — Energy Cascade in Multiple Effect

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nSS[Fresh Steam S] --> nH1[Latent heat lambdas released in Effect 1]
    nH1 --> nQ1[Q1 vaporises water in Effect 1]
    nQ1 --> nV1[V1 condenses in Effect 2 calandria]
    nV1 --> nH2[Latent heat lambdav1 released in Effect 2]
    nH2 --> nQ2[Q2 vaporises water in Effect 2]
    nQ2 --> nV2[V2 condenses in Effect 3 calandria]
    nV2 --> nHn[Latent heat released in Effect n]
    nHn --> nQn[Qn vaporises water in Effect n]
    nQn --> nVn[Final Vn to Condenser]
    nV1 -.Partial re evaporation.-> nH2
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Define boiling point elevation (BPE) of a solution. Explain how Dühring's rule is used to estimate the boiling point of an aqueous solution at a given pressure.**

**Model Answer:**

Boiling point elevation (BPE), denoted $\Delta'$, is the difference between the boiling point of a solution and the boiling point of pure water at the **same pressure**. It arises from the colligative effect of dissolved non-volatile solute on the vapour pressure of the solvent.

$$\Delta' = T_{bp,\;solution} - T_{bp,\;water} \quad \text{at the same } P$$

For a solution of given concentration, $\Delta'$ is a function of pressure (it generally decreases with pressure, hence increases under vacuum).

**Dühring's Rule** states that the boiling point of an aqueous solution at one pressure is a **linear function** of the boiling point of pure water at another pressure. For a fixed concentration, one plots:

- X-axis: $T_{bp,\;water}$ at pressure $P$ (in °C)
- Y-axis: $T_{bp,\;solution}$ at pressure $P$ (in °C)

The plot is a straight line. Given the water's boiling point at the operating pressure (read from steam tables) and the solution concentration, the solution's boiling point is read directly from the Dühring chart. The BPE is then $\Delta' = T_{bp,\;solution} - T_{bp,\;water}$.

In KTU numerical problems, BPE values are often provided directly via Dühring charts in the appendix or a data table.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Distinguish between *capacity* and *economy* of an evaporator. Why does economy improve when going from a single effect to a multiple effect?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Capacity | Economy |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Rate of water (solvent) evaporated, in **kg h⁻¹** | Steam consumed per kg of water evaporated, **dimensionless** |
| **Formula** | $\text{Capacity} = V$ | $e = V/S$ |
| **Significance** | How *much* solvent is removed per unit time | How *efficiently* steam is used |
| **Single effect (water, sat. feed)** | $V$ | $\approx 1$ |
| **n-effect (ideal)** | $n \cdot V_{single}$ | $0.85\,n$ to $1.0\,n$ |

**Why economy improves in multiple effect:**

In a single effect, fresh steam supplies ALL the latent heat of vaporisation, and the vapour produced is *discarded* (sent to a condenser). In a multiple effect, the vapour from effect 1 condenses in effect 2 and provides the latent heat there — the *same heat* is reused. Hence, fresh steam is needed only in effect 1, but the total water evaporated is the **sum** of the vapours from all effects. For $n$ effects, the same 1 kg of steam (with cascading) can evaporate $0.85n$ to $n$ kg of water, multiplying the economy by $n$.

---

## Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 marks, with internal choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** In multiple-effect numericals, students routinely:
> (i) Forget to subtract the **sum of BPEs** from the available $\Delta T$ — losing 2–3 marks.
> (ii) Distribute $\Delta T$ *equally* across effects without first converting to **true $\Delta T$** after BPE — losing 2 marks.
> (iii) Use the wrong latent heat in the energy balance — $\lambda_s$ for steam and $\lambda_v$ for vapour are at *different temperatures*. Always check.
> (iv) Forget that thick liquor and vapour leave at the **boiling point of the solution** ($T_b$, not $T_{water}$).

---

### Question A (14 marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**A single-effect evaporator is used to concentrate 10,000 kg h⁻¹ of a 10% (by mass) NaOH solution to 30% (by mass). The feed enters at 30°C. Saturated steam is supplied at 120 kPa (abs). The evaporator operates at atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa). The overall heat-transfer coefficient is $U = 2000\;\text{W m}^{-2}\text{K}^{-1}$. The boiling point elevation (BPE) of the 30% NaOH solution at atmospheric pressure is 11°C.**

**Given:** $C_{pF} = 3.77\;\text{kJ kg}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$ for the feed; $\lambda_s$ at 120 kPa (abs) = **2201 kJ kg⁻¹**; $\lambda_v$ at the boiling temperature of the solution = **2260 kJ kg⁻¹**.

#### Part (a) — 7 marks
**Calculate the rate of water evaporated, the steam consumption, the steam economy, and the heat-transfer area required.**

**Step 1 — Vapour produced (Material balance):**

$$V = F\!\left(1 - \dfrac{x_F}{x_L}\right) = 10000\!\left(1 - \dfrac{0.10}{0.30}\right) = 10000 \times \dfrac{2}{3}$$

$$V = 6666.7 \;\text{kg h}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Thick liquor rate:**

$$L = F - V = 10000 - 6666.7 = 3333.3 \;\text{kg h}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Boiling point of water at 101.325 kPa (atmospheric):**

From steam tables: $T_{w,\,101.325} = 100°C$

**Step 4 — Boiling point of the solution:**

$$T_b = T_w + \Delta' = 100 + 11 = 111°C$$

**Step 5 — Energy balance to find steam:**

$$S \, \lambda_s = F \, C_{pF}(T_b - t_F) + V \, \lambda_v$$

$$S \times 2201 = 10000 \times 3.77 \times (111 - 30) + 6666.7 \times 2260$$

Compute each term:
- Sensible heat: $10000 \times 3.77 \times 81 = 3{,}053{,}700 \;\text{kJ h}^{-1}$
- Latent heat: $6666.7 \times 2260 = 15{,}066{,}742 \;\text{kJ h}^{-1}$
- Total $Q$: $18{,}120{,}442 \;\text{kJ h}^{-1}$

$$S = \dfrac{18{,}120{,}442}{2201} = 8233\;\text{kg h}^{-1}$$

**Step 6 — Steam economy:**

$$e = \dfrac{V}{S} = \dfrac{6666.7}{8233} = 0.810$$

**Step 7 — Heat-transfer area:**

$$Q = 18{,}120{,}442 \;\text{kJ h}^{-1} = \dfrac{18{,}120{,}442}{3600}\;\text{kW} = 5033.5\;\text{kW}$$

$$\Delta T = T_s - T_b = T_{s,\,120\,kPa} - 111 = 120.2 - 111 = 9.2°C \;(\text{using } T_s = 120.2°C \text{ from steam table at 120 kPa})$$

$$A = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T} = \dfrac{5033.5 \times 1000}{2000 \times 9.2} = 273.6\;\text{m}^2$$

#### Part (b) — 7 marks
**If the same evaporator is converted to a forward-feed triple-effect system with the same steam and the same final pressure, calculate (i) the new economy, (ii) the new capacity, and (iii) the BPE in each effect if the concentrations are approximately 14%, 21% and 30% with corresponding BPEs of 4°C, 7°C and 11°C. Assume equal areas and neglect temperature losses.**

**Step 1 — Gross available ΔT:**

$$T_{s1} = 120.2°C, \quad T_{vn} = 100°C \;(\text{atmospheric})$$

$$\Delta T_{avail} = 120.2 - 100 = 20.2°C$$

**Step 2 — Sum of BPEs:**

$$\sum \Delta' = 4 + 7 + 11 = 22°C$$

**Step 3 — True ΔT (here, negative — illustrating the impossibility of operation):**

$$\Delta T_{true} = 20.2 - 22 - 0 = -1.8°C$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The negative $\Delta T_{true}$ indicates that with these BPEs and this steam pressure, the triple-effect configuration CANNOT operate at atmospheric last-effect pressure.** To make it work, the **last effect must be under vacuum** (i.e., $T_{vn}$ must be lower than 100°C).

**Step 4 — Operational modification — assume last effect is at 50°C (≈ 12.3 kPa abs):**

$$\Delta T_{avail} = 120.2 - 50 = 70.2°C$$

$$\Delta T_{true} = 70.2 - 22 = 48.2°C$$

$$\Delta T_{each} = \dfrac{48.2}{3} = 16.07°C$$

**Step 5 — Total heat load equals the original single-effect $Q$** (since the *same* steam is used and the *same* work is done on the feed ideally):

$$Q_{total} = 18{,}120{,}442 \;\text{kJ h}^{-1} = 5033.5\;\text{kW}$$

**Step 6 — Approximate new capacity** (assume vapour produced is the same total, but distributed across effects):

$$\text{Capacity} \approx 6666.7 \;\text{kg h}^{-1}$$

**Step 7 — New economy (with capacity correction for BPE):**

$$e_{ideal} = 3 \times 0.85 = 2.55 \;\;(\text{or up to }3)$$

$$e_{actual} = e_{ideal} \times 0.9 \times \dfrac{\Delta T_{true}}{\Delta T_{avail}} = 2.55 \times 0.9 \times \dfrac{48.2}{70.2}$$

$$e_{actual} = 2.55 \times 0.9 \times 0.687 = 1.577$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Discussion:** The triple-effect economy of ≈1.58 represents a **2× improvement** over the single-effect economy of 0.81, with the *same* steam consumption. This is the central economic argument for multiple-effect operation.

---

### Question B (14 marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

#### Part (a) — 7 marks
**Describe with neat sketches the three standard feed arrangements in multiple-effect evaporators. Compare their relative advantages and disadvantages.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Forward Feed (Co-current with steam/vapour)**

The dilute feed enters the *first* effect (highest pressure) and the thick liquor leaves the *last* effect (lowest pressure). Vapour from effect *i* is the heating medium for effect *(i+1)*.

| Aspect | Forward Feed |
|---|---|
| **Direction of feed** | Effect 1 → Effect 2 → ... → Effect n |
| **Direction of steam/vapour** | Effect 1 → Effect 2 → ... → Effect n |
| **Pump requirement** | None (natural flow due to $\Delta P$) |
| **Temperature-viscosity match** | Poor: cold feed meets hot surface, then hot liquor meets cold surface in last effect |
| **Capacity** | Lower (viscous liquor in cold last effect gives low $U$) |
| **Best suited to** | Dilute, non-viscous feeds |

**2. Backward Feed (Counter-current with steam/vapour)**

The dilute feed enters the *last* effect (lowest pressure) and is pumped to progressively higher-pressure effects. Thick liquor leaves the *first* effect (highest pressure).

| Aspect | Backward Feed |
|---|---|
| **Direction of feed** | Effect n → Effect (n-1) → ... → Effect 1 |
| **Direction of steam/vapour** | Effect 1 → Effect 2 → ... → Effect n |
| **Pump requirement** | Pumps between every effect (liquor must be pumped to higher pressure) |
| **Temperature-viscosity match** | Excellent: cold feed meets cold surface; hot, less-viscous liquor meets hot surface in effect 1 |
| **Capacity** | Higher (good $U$ in first effect) |
| **Best suited to** | Viscous, concentrated feeds |

**3. Parallel Feed**

Fresh feed is supplied *separately* to each effect, and thick liquor is withdrawn *separately* from each effect. Steam enters effect 1; vapour from each effect (except the last) heats the next.

| Aspect | Parallel Feed |
|---|---|
| **Direction of feed** | Independent to each effect |
| **Direction of steam/vapour** | Effect 1 → Effect 2 → ... → Effect n |
| **Pump requirement** | None |
| **Temperature-viscosity match** | Inconsistent (vapour condensation on each effect's surface) |
| **Capacity** | Lowest economy (no full cascade of feed sensible heat) |
| **Best suited to** | Crystallising solutions, slurries |

#### Part (b) — 7 marks
**"The optimum number of effects in a multiple-effect evaporator is governed by an economic balance between steam cost and capital cost."** Derive the relationship for the optimum number of effects and discuss the practical limit.

**Model Answer:**

(Refer to **Derivation 6 — Optimum Number of Effects** in Section 3 of these notes.)

The total annual cost of an evaporator system comprises two competing terms:

- **Steam cost**, which *decreases* with $n$ (more reuse, less fresh steam).
- **Capital + operating cost**, which *increases* with $n$ (more effects = more calandrias, more vacuum equipment, more piping).

$$C_{total}(n) = \dfrac{D \cdot C_{steam}}{0.85\,n} + n \cdot C_{cap}$$

Differentiating with respect to $n$ and setting to zero:

$$-\dfrac{D \cdot C_{steam}}{0.85\,n^2} + C_{cap} = 0$$

$$\boxed{\,n_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{D \cdot C_{steam}}{0.85\,C_{cap}}}\,}$$

**Discussion of practical limits:**

1. **Physical feasibility:** $\Delta T_{true} = \Delta T_{avail} - \sum \Delta'$ must remain positive. As $n$ increases, $\Delta T_{each} = \Delta T_{true}/n$ shrinks. When $\Delta T_{each}$ becomes too small (say, < 5°C), heat-transfer areas balloon, and the design becomes impractical.

2. **Capital reality:** Beyond 4–6 effects, the capital cost grows faster than steam savings, especially for low-cost steam (coal-fired, waste-heat).

3. **Special industries:** In **sugar** industry, $n_{opt} = 4$ to 5; in **desalination** (multi-stage flash concept), $n$ can exceed 20 because steam is essentially free (waste heat from a power plant).

4. **Operation:** $n$ is always an integer; $n_{opt}$ from the equation is rounded to the nearest feasible integer (1, 2, 3, 4, 5 or 6).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3, Single & Multiple Effect Evaporators**

**Single Effect — Core Concepts:**
- ☑ Evaporation = concentration of a non-volatile solute solution by vaporising the solvent.
- ☑ Vapour produced: $V = F(1 - x_F/x_L)$ — material balance.
- ☑ Energy balance (KTU standard form): $S \lambda_s = F C_{pF}(T_b - t_F) + V \lambda_v$.
- ☑ Steam economy: $e = V/S \approx 0.8$ for water with cold feed; $\approx 1.0$ for saturated feed.
- ☑ Heat-transfer area: $A = Q / (U \Delta T)$, where $\Delta T = T_s - T_b$.

**Boiling Point Elevation (BPE) — Critical Detail:**
- ☑ BPE = $\Delta' = T_{bp,solution} - T_{bp,water}$ at the same pressure.
- ☑ BPE is estimated using **Dühring's rule** (linear chart of $T_{bp,solution}$ vs $T_{bp,water}$ at fixed concentration).
- ☑ BPE increases with concentration; decreases with pressure.
- ☑ BPE **reduces** the effective $\Delta T$ available for heat transfer.

**Multiple Effect — Core Concepts:**
- ☑ Vapour from one effect becomes the heating medium of the next.
- ☑ **Economy** of *n*-effect: $e_n \approx 0.85n$ to $1.0n$ (for water-based systems).
- ☑ **Equal-area design assumption** ⇒ $Q_1 = Q_2 = ... = Q_n = Q_{total}/n$.
- ☑ $\Delta T_{each} = (\Delta T_{avail} - \sum \Delta' - \Delta T_{loss})/n$.
- ☑ **Capacity correction factor**: $C_a/C_i = (\Delta T_{true}/\Delta T_{avail})^{0.9}$.
- ☑ **Economy correction factor**: $e_a/e_i \approx 0.9 \cdot (\Delta T_{true}/\Delta T_{avail})$.

**Feed Configurations — Quick Mnemonic:**
- ☑ **Forward** = "Feed follows the steam" → no pumps, but viscous liquor in cold end.
- ☑ **Backward** = "Feed fights the steam" → pumps needed, but better $U$ in hot end.
- ☑ **Parallel** = "Each effect is independent" → best for crystallising/viscous slurries.

**Optimum Number of Effects — Economic Criterion:**
- ☑ $n_{opt} = \sqrt{(D \cdot C_{steam}) / (0.85 \cdot C_{cap})}$.
