- ☑ Practical range: $n_{opt} = 3$ to 6 for most industries.

**Common Exam Traps:**
- ☑ Always subtract $\sum \Delta'$ from $\Delta T_{avail}$ before distributing to effects.
- ☑ Use $\lambda_s$ at the steam temperature, $\lambda_v$ at the solution boiling temperature — *different values*.
- ☑ Thick liquor and vapour leave at the **solution's** boiling point $T_b$, not at water's boiling point.
- ☑ When feed is preheated to the boiling point, the $F C_{pF}(T_b - t_F)$ term vanishes.
- ☑ BPE is per-effect, and the total correction is the **sum** of all individual BPEs.
- ☑ Last effect must be under vacuum when BPE is high, to keep $\Delta T_{true}$ positive.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance of Evaporators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Performance of Evaporators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Evaporator Performance** refers to the quantitative measurement of how efficiently an industrial evaporator converts a feed solution into concentrated product using thermal energy. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the performance of an evaporator is fundamentally characterized by two operational metrics: **(i) Evaporator Capacity** and **(ii) Steam Economy**.

- **Evaporator Capacity (C)**: The quantity of water (solvent) vaporized per unit time, expressed in **kg/hr** or **kg/s**.
- **Steam Economy (E)**: The ratio of the mass of water vaporized to the mass of live steam supplied to the evaporator.

> [!NOTE]
> The performance assessment is crucial because evaporators are among the **most energy-intensive** unit operations in chemical process industries (CPI). A poorly designed or operated evaporator can cost crores in annual fuel bills.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are boiling water in a kettle on a gas stove to make tea:

- The **amount of water that actually evaporates** per minute = *Capacity* (how much useful work the kettle does).
- The **amount of gas you burn** per cup of tea you make = *Energy input* (cost of operation).
- If you boil 1 kg of water using just 0.2 kg of steam in a multiple-effect evaporator, you get an *economy* of 5 — meaning each kg of steam "evaporates" 5 kg of water. This is like getting 5 cups of tea for the price of fuel for 1 cup.

In industry, steam is expensive. So, the goal is always to **vaporize the maximum amount of water using the minimum amount of live steam**, which is the essence of evaporator performance optimization.

### 1.3 Performance Parameters — Quick Snapshot

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Capacity | C | kg/hr | Total water evaporated per unit time |
| Economy | E | kg water / kg steam | Efficiency of steam utilization |
| Steam Consumption | S | kg/hr | Live steam fed to the 1st effect |
| Feed Rate | F | kg/hr | Dilute solution fed to the system |
| Product Rate | P | kg/hr | Concentrated liquor leaving the system |
| Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient | U | W/m²·K | Heat transfer efficiency of calandria |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Evaporator Capacity — Theoretical Decomposition

The **capacity** is the total rate at which water is removed from the feed across all effects. Consider a **single-effect evaporator** first, then extend to multiple effects.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

- **Step 1 — Material Balance (Overall):** The total water lost by the feed equals the total water gained by the vapor.
  $$F = P + V$$
  where $V$ is the total vapor produced.

- **Step 2 — Mass of Water Evaporated (per unit time):**
  $$V = F \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_P}\right)$$
  where $x_F$ and $x_P$ are the mass fractions of solute in feed and product.

- **Step 3 — Definition of Capacity:**
  $$\boxed{C = V = F \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_P}\right)}$$

- **Step 4 — Capacity in terms of Heat Transfer (Physical Insight):**
  The heat supplied by steam must equal the heat required to vaporize water (neglecting sensible heating):
  $$Q = S \cdot \lambda_S = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$
  
  Since $V = Q/\lambda_V$ (where $\lambda_V$ is the latent heat of vaporization of the product vapor):
  $$\boxed{C = V = \frac{U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{\lambda_V}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Capacity is **directly proportional** to the heat transfer area $A$, the overall heat transfer coefficient $U$, and the temperature difference $\Delta T$. To increase capacity, increase any (or all) of these.

### 2.2 Steam Economy — Theoretical Decomposition

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

- **Step 1 — Definition of Economy:**
  $$\boxed{E = \frac{\text{Mass of water evaporated}}{\text{Mass of live steam supplied}} = \frac{V}{S}}$$

- **Step 2 — For a Single-Effect Evaporator:**
  $V = S$ (every kg of steam vaporizes roughly 1 kg of water), so:
  $$E_{\text{single}} = 1$$

- **Step 3 — For a Multiple-Effect Evaporator:**
  The vapor from one effect serves as the heating medium for the next. With negligible heat losses and equal heat loads per effect:
  $$V_1 = S, \quad V_2 = V_1, \quad V_3 = V_2, \ldots$$
  $$V_{\text{total}} = V_1 + V_2 + \ldots + V_N = N \cdot S$$
  $$\boxed{E_{\text{multiple}} = \frac{N \cdot S}{S} = N}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Ideal Economy = Number of Effects (N).** However, in reality, economy is less than N because of boiling point elevation (BPE), sensible heat effects, and heat losses.

### 2.3 Factors Affecting Evaporator Performance

1. **Temperature Difference ($\Delta T$):** Higher $\Delta T$ → higher capacity.
2. **Heat Transfer Area ($A$):** Directly proportional to capacity.
3. **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient ($U$):** Depends on fouling, viscosity, boiling regime.
4. **Boiling Point Elevation (BPE):** Reduces effective $\Delta T$ → reduces capacity.
5. **Number of Effects ($N$):** More effects → higher economy, but lower $\Delta T$ per effect.
6. **Feed Condition:** Cold feed requires more steam; preheated feed improves economy.
7. **Steam Economy Improvements:** Vapor bleeding, feed preheating, multiple effects, thermal/mechanical vapor recompression (TVR/MVR).

### 2.4 Methods to Improve Steam Economy

- **Method 1 — Multiple-Effect Evaporation:** Reusing vapor from one effect as the heating medium of the next.
- **Method 2 — Vapor Bleeding (or Vapor Recompression):** Extracting vapor from intermediate effects to preheat the incoming feed.
- **Method 3 — Feed Preheating:** Using vapor bleed to raise the feed temperature before it enters the first effect.
- **Method 4 — Thermal Vapor Recompression (TVR):** Using a steam ejector to compress vapor for reuse.
- **Method 5 — Mechanical Vapor Recompression (MVR):** Using a mechanical compressor (high CapEx, low OpEx).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = V = F(1 - x_F/x_P)$ | Capacity from material balance | kg/hr |
| 2 | $C = U A \Delta T / \lambda$ | Capacity from heat transfer | kg/hr |
| 3 | $E = V/S$ | Steam economy definition | kg/kg |
| 4 | $E_{\text{multiple, ideal}} = N$ | Ideal economy = number of effects | — |
| 5 | $\Delta T_{\text{effective}} = \Delta T_{\text{overall}} - \sum \text{BPE}_i$ | Effective $\Delta T$ per effect | K or °C |
| 6 | $\Delta T_i = \frac{Q_i / U_i}{\sum (Q_j / U_j)} \cdot \Delta T_{\text{total}}$ | Temperature distribution among effects | K or °C |
| 7 | $S = Q_1 / \lambda_S$ | Steam required in 1st effect | kg/hr |
| 8 | $V_i = Q_i / \lambda_i$ | Vapor produced in $i$-th effect | kg/hr |
| 9 | $E_{\text{real}} = \frac{\sum V_i}{S}$ | Real economy of N-effect system | kg/kg |
| 10 | $U = \frac{1}{1/h_i + x_w/k_w + 1/h_o}$ | Overall heat transfer coefficient | W/m²·K |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In all the formulas above, latent heat $\lambda$ is in **kJ/kg**, $U$ in **W/m²·K**, $A$ in **m²**, and $\Delta T$ in **K** (or °C, the difference is the same).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sugar Industry:** Multiple-effect evaporators concentrate sugar juice with economy of 3–4.
- **Dairy Industry:** MVR evaporators concentrate milk with very high economy (>10).
- **Desalination:** Multi-stage flash (MSF) and MEE plants use these principles.
- **Pulp & Paper:** Black liquor recovery evaporators must handle viscous, fouling fluids.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Capacity of a Single-Effect Evaporator from First Principles

We start from the **overall material balance** and **energy balance** on the evaporator body.

**Given:** A single-effect evaporator with feed rate $F$ kg/hr, solute mass fraction $x_F$, product mass fraction $x_P$, steam rate $S$ kg/hr, heating area $A$ m², latent heat of steam $\lambda_S$ kJ/kg, latent heat of vapor $\lambda$ kJ/kg, overall heat transfer coefficient $U$ W/m²·K, and temperature difference $\Delta T$ K.

**Step 1 — Total Material Balance:**
$$F = P + V$$
where $P$ is the concentrated product and $V$ is the water vaporized.

**Step 2 — Solute Mass Balance (solute is non-volatile):**
$$F \cdot x_F = P \cdot x_P$$
Solving for $P$:
$$P = F \cdot \frac{x_F}{x_P}$$

**Step 3 — Substituting P into the total balance to find V:**
$$V = F - P = F - F \cdot \frac{x_F}{x_P}$$
$$\boxed{V = F \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_P}\right)}$$

**Step 4 — Energy Balance on the Evaporator (steady state, neglecting heat losses and sensible heat):**
Heat from steam = Heat to vaporize water
$$S \cdot \lambda_S = V \cdot \lambda$$
$$\boxed{S = \frac{V \cdot \lambda}{\lambda_S}}$$

**Step 5 — Heat Transfer Rate through the Heating Surface:**
$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = S \cdot \lambda_S$$

**Step 6 — Combining to get the Capacity in terms of U, A, ΔT:**
$$V = \frac{Q}{\lambda} = \frac{U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{\lambda}$$
$$\boxed{C = \frac{U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{\lambda}}$$

This is the **fundamental capacity equation** used by KTU examiners for any single-effect design problem.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Steam Economy of a Triple-Effect Evaporator (Forward Feed)

For a **triple-effect evaporator** with feed entering effect 1, condensate and vapor flowing from effect 1 → 2 → 3.

**Step 1 — Energy balance on Effect 1:**
$$Q_1 = U_1 A_1 \Delta T_1 = S \cdot \lambda_S$$
$$V_1 = \frac{Q_1}{\lambda_1} = \frac{S \cdot \lambda_S}{\lambda_1}$$

**Step 2 — Energy balance on Effect 2 (using V₁ as heating medium):**
$$Q_2 = U_2 A_2 \Delta T_2 = V_1 \cdot \lambda_1$$
$$V_2 = \frac{Q_2}{\lambda_2} = \frac{V_1 \cdot \lambda_1}{\lambda_2}$$

**Step 3 — Energy balance on Effect 3:**
$$Q_3 = U_3 A_3 \Delta T_3 = V_2 \cdot \lambda_2$$
$$V_3 = \frac{Q_3}{\lambda_3} = \frac{V_2 \cdot \lambda_2}{\lambda_3}$$

**Step 4 — Total Vapor Produced (Capacity):**
$$V_{\text{total}} = V_1 + V_2 + V_3$$

**Step 5 — Steam Economy:**
$$E = \frac{V_{\text{total}}}{S} = \frac{V_1 + V_2 + V_3}{S}$$

**Step 6 — Ideal Case (equal U, equal A, equal λ, no BPE):**
$$V_1 = V_2 = V_3 = S \quad \Rightarrow \quad E = 3$$

This confirms: **Ideal economy = N (number of effects).**

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

> **Problem Statement:** A single-effect evaporator is used to concentrate a weak liquor from 10 wt% solids to 50 wt% solids. The feed rate is 10,000 kg/hr at 30 °C. Saturated steam at 2 atm (latent heat = 2207 kJ/kg) is used. The boiling point of the liquor is 105 °C. The condensate leaves at the saturation temperature of steam. Heat losses are 5% of the steam heat input. The specific heat of the feed is 3.8 kJ/kg·K. The latent heat of vaporization at 105 °C is 2257 kJ/kg. The overall heat transfer coefficient is 1500 W/m²·K. Calculate:
> (a) The capacity of the evaporator.
> (b) The steam economy.
> (c) The heat transfer area required.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Capacity:**
$$V = F \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_P}\right) = 10000 \left(1 - \frac{0.10}{0.50}\right)$$
$$V = 10000 \times 0.8 = \boxed{8000 \text{ kg/hr}}$$

**Part (b) — Steam Economy:**

Energy balance including heat loss and sensible heating:
$$S \cdot \lambda_S \cdot (1 - 0.05) = F \cdot c_p \cdot (T_b - T_F) + V \cdot \lambda_V$$

Substituting:
$$S \times 2207 \times 0.95 = 10000 \times 3.8 \times (105 - 30) + 8000 \times 2257$$

Calculating the right-hand side:
$$\text{RHS} = 2850000 + 18056000 = 20906000 \text{ kJ/hr}$$

Solving for $S$:
$$S = \frac{20906000}{2207 \times 0.95} = \frac{20906000}{2096.65}$$
$$S = 9971.6 \text{ kg/hr}$$

Economy:
$$E = \frac{V}{S} = \frac{8000}{9971.6} = \boxed{0.802}$$

**Part (c) — Heat Transfer Area:**

Useful heat transferred:
$$Q = S \cdot \lambda_S \cdot 0.95 = 9971.6 \times 2207 \times 0.95 = 20907400 \text{ kJ/hr}$$

Convert to W (1 kJ/hr = 1000/3600 W):
$$Q = 20907400 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 5807611 \text{ W} = 5.808 \times 10^6 \text{ W}$$

Temperature difference:
$$\Delta T = 120 - 105 = 15 \text{ °C (or K)}$$
*(Note: Steam at 2 atm has saturation temperature ≈ 120 °C)*

Area:
$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{5.808 \times 10^6}{1500 \times 15}$$
$$A = \frac{5.808 \times 10^6}{22500} = \boxed{258.1 \text{ m}^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** A common mistake is forgetting to convert kJ/hr to W. Always use SI units consistently: $1 \text{ kJ/hr} = 1000/3600 \text{ W} = 0.2778 \text{ W}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Evaporator Performance Architecture

This block diagram maps the **interactions of performance parameters** in a typical evaporator system.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[DILUTE FEED F] --> B[EVAPORATOR BODY]
    B --> C[CONCENTRATED PRODUCT P]
    B --> D[VAPOR V]
    E[LIVE STEAM S] --> B
    F[HEAT SUPPLY Q] --> B
    B --> G[CONDENSATE]
    B --> H[CAPACITY C]
    B --> I[STEAM ECONOMY E]
    J[TEMPERATURE DELTA T] --> F
    K[AREA A] --> F
    L[HEAT TRANSFER COEFF U] --> F
    M[BPE] --> J
    N[NUMBER OF EFFECTS N] --> I
    O[FEED PREHEATING] --> I
    P[VAPOR BLEEDING] --> I
    H --> Q[PERFORMANCE REPORT]
    I --> Q
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Methods to Improve Economy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[IMPROVE ECONOMY] --> B[MULTIPLE EFFECTS]
    A --> C[VAPOR BLEEDING]
    A --> D[FEED PREHEATING]
    A --> E[TVR THERMAL VAPOR RECOMPRESSION]
    A --> F[MVR MECHANICAL VAPOR RECOMPRESSION]
    B --> G[HIGHER N HIGHER ECONOMY]
    C --> G
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[LOWER STEAM CONSUMPTION]
    H --> I[LOWER OPEX]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Capacity Calculation Pathway

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[FEED F kg/hr] --> B[MATERIAL BALANCE]
    P[PRODUCT xP] --> B
    B --> C[VAPOR V kg/hr]
    C --> D[CAPACITY C]
    E[U W/m2K] --> F[HEAT TRANSFER EQN]
    K[AREA A m2] --> F
    T[DELTA T K] --> F
    F --> D
    G[LAMBDA kJ/kg] --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of Diagram 4.1:** Capacity and Economy are the two key outputs. Economy is influenced by the number of effects, vapor bleeding, and feed preheating. Capacity is influenced by U, A, ΔT, and the BPE. These two outputs together constitute the "Performance Report" that engineers use to evaluate the evaporator.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` Define the terms 'Evaporator Capacity' and 'Steam Economy' with appropriate units.**

**Model Answer:**

**Evaporator Capacity:** It is defined as the quantity of water (solvent) vaporized per unit time in the evaporator. Mathematically,

$$C = V = F \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_P}\right)$$

It is expressed in **kg/hr** or **kg/s**.

**Steam Economy:** It is defined as the ratio of the mass of water vaporized to the mass of live steam consumed.

$$E = \frac{V}{S}$$

It is a **dimensionless** quantity, although sometimes expressed in **kg water/kg steam**.

For a single-effect evaporator, $E = 1$; for an N-effect evaporator, ideal $E = N$.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` What is the effect of the number of effects on (a) capacity and (b) economy of a multiple-effect evaporator?**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Effect on Capacity:** As the number of effects $N$ increases, the total temperature difference $\Delta T_{\text{total}}$ is divided among more effects. Hence, the per-effect $\Delta T$ decreases. Since $C \propto \Delta T$, the **capacity decreases** with increasing $N$. Also, the boiling point elevation (BPE) consumes more of the available $\Delta T$ in each effect, further reducing effective $\Delta T$.

**(b) Effect on Economy:** As $N$ increases, the live steam $S$ vaporizes water in effect 1, the vapor from effect 1 acts as the heating medium in effect 2, and so on. The total vapor produced is approximately $N \cdot S$. Hence,

$$E = \frac{N \cdot S}{S} = N$$

So the **economy increases (ideally linearly)** with the number of effects. In practice, real economy is slightly less than $N$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students wrongly state that capacity increases with $N$. In reality, **economy increases but capacity decreases** with more effects. Marks are deducted for this conceptual error.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` Question A (14 Marks):**

A single-effect evaporator concentrates a salt solution from 5 wt% to 25 wt% at a rate of 15,000 kg/hr. The feed enters at 40 °C. Saturated steam at 1.5 atm (latent heat 2225 kJ/kg) is used. The boiling point of the concentrated solution is 110 °C. The specific heat of the feed is 3.5 kJ/kg·K, the latent heat of vaporization at 110 °C is 2232 kJ/kg, and the overall heat transfer coefficient is 1800 W/m²·K. Heat losses are 4% of steam input. Calculate:
**(a)** The capacity of the evaporator and the steam consumption rate.
**(b)** The steam economy and the heating surface area required.
*(Given: Saturation temperature of steam at 1.5 atm ≈ 112 °C)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Capacity from material balance:**
$$V = F \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_P}\right) = 15000 \left(1 - \frac{0.05}{0.25}\right)$$
$$V = 15000 \times 0.80 = \boxed{12000 \text{ kg/hr}}$$

**Steam consumption from energy balance:**
Heat supplied by steam × (1 − heat loss fraction) = Sensible heat of feed + Latent heat of vaporization

$$S \cdot \lambda_S \cdot (1 - 0.04) = F \cdot c_p \cdot (T_b - T_F) + V \cdot \lambda_V$$

Substituting:
$$S \times 2225 \times 0.96 = 15000 \times 3.5 \times (110 - 40) + 12000 \times 2232$$

Calculating RHS:
$$\text{Sensible} = 15000 \times 3.5 \times 70 = 3675000 \text{ kJ/hr}$$
$$\text{Latent} = 12000 \times 2232 = 26784000 \text{ kJ/hr}$$
$$\text{Total RHS} = 30459000 \text{ kJ/hr}$$

$$S = \frac{30459000}{2225 \times 0.96} = \frac{30459000}{2136}$$
$$\boxed{S = 14260.8 \text{ kg/hr}}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Steam Economy:**
$$E = \frac{V}{S} = \frac{12000}{14260.8} = \boxed{0.8415 \text{ kg water/kg steam}}$$

**Heating Surface Area:**

Useful heat transferred (after losses):
$$Q = S \cdot \lambda_S \cdot 0.96 = 14260.8 \times 2225 \times 0.96 = 30459000 \text{ kJ/hr}$$

Convert to W:
$$Q = 30459000 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 8460833.3 \text{ W}$$

Temperature difference:
$$\Delta T = 112 - 110 = 2 \text{ K}$$

**Heating area:**
$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{8460833.3}{1800 \times 2} = \boxed{2350.2 \text{ m}^2}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often use $T_{\text{feed}}$ instead of $T_{\text{boiling}}$ in the energy balance. Use the **boiling point of the solution** ($T_b$) for sensible heating, not the feed inlet temperature. Also, never use $\Delta T = T_S - T_F$; always use $T_S - T_b$.

---

**Q3. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` Question B (Alternative Choice, 14 Marks):**

Explain the following methods used to improve the steam economy of an evaporator with neat sketches/flow diagrams:
**(a)** Multiple-effect evaporation.
**(b)** Vapor bleeding and feed preheating.

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Multiple-Effect Evaporation

**Concept:** In a multiple-effect evaporator, the vapor produced in one effect is used as the heating medium in the next effect. The pressure (and hence the boiling temperature) decreases from effect 1 to effect N, so the vapor from effect $i$ can boil the liquid in effect $i+1$.

**Layout (Forward Feed):** Feed enters effect 1 → concentrated to effect N → product exits. Steam enters effect 1 → vapor leaves effect 1 → becomes steam for effect 2 → and so on.

**Economy Derivation (Ideal, N effects):**
- $V_1 = S$ (live steam to effect 1 vaporizes water in effect 1)
- $V_2 = V_1$ (vapor from effect 1 acts as heating medium in effect 2)
- $V_i = V_{i-1}$
- $V_{\text{total}} = N \cdot S$

$$E = \frac{V_{\text{total}}}{S} = N$$

**Advantages:**
- Steam economy increases linearly with N.
- Lower operating cost (OpEx).

**Limitations:**
- Total $\Delta T$ is divided among N effects → capacity decreases.
- Capital cost (CapEx) increases with more effects.
- Practical maximum: 4 to 6 effects (more than 6 gives diminishing returns).

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Vapor Bleeding and Feed Preheating

**Vapor Bleeding:**
- A portion of the vapor from an intermediate effect (say effect 2 or 3) is extracted and used to preheat the incoming feed in an external heat exchanger.
- This reduces the sensible heat load on the first effect → less live steam required.
- The "bled" vapor is at a temperature between the steam and the final effect, so it is ideal for feed preheating.

**Mathematical Effect:**
- Original steam: $S_0 = \frac{F c_p (T_b - T_F) + V \lambda}{\lambda_S}$
- After bleeding: $S_1 = \frac{F c_p (T_F' - T_F) + V \lambda}{\lambda_S}$ where $T_F' > T_F$ due to preheating.
- Since $T_F' - T_F < T_b - T_F$, the steam consumption decreases → economy increases.

**Feed Preheating:**
- The feed temperature is raised before it enters the first effect.
- Sources: vapor bleeding, external heaters, or recovered flash steam.
- Preheated feed reduces the sensible heat duty of the first effect.

**Combined Effect (Vapor Bleeding + Feed Preheating):**
- Steam economy can increase by **10–30%** compared to simple multiple-effect operation.
- Widely used in the sugar and dairy industries.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **TVR (Thermal Vapor Recompression)** with **MVR (Mechanical Vapor Recompression).** TVR uses a steam-jet ejector (low CapEx, moderate efficiency), while MVR uses a mechanical compressor (high CapEx, very high efficiency). Marks lost if interchanged in exams.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for Performance of Evaporators:**

- **Capacity ($C$)** = mass of water vaporized per unit time. Units: **kg/hr**.
- **Economy ($E$)** = water vaporized / live steam supplied. Units: **kg/kg (dimensionless)**.
- **Single-effect economy** = 1 (ideal); **N-effect economy** = N (ideal).
- **Capacity equation:** $C = V = F(1 - x_F/x_P) = UA\Delta T / \lambda$.
- **Steam required:** $S = (F c_p \Delta T_{\text{sensible}} + V \lambda) / (\lambda_S \cdot \eta_{\text{heat}})$.
- **Real economy** < N due to BPE, sensible heat, and heat losses.
- **More effects** → higher economy but lower capacity.
- **Methods to improve economy:** Multiple effects, vapor bleeding, feed preheating, TVR, MVR.
- **BPE (Boiling Point Elevation)** reduces effective $\Delta T$ → reduces capacity.
- **Always use SI units** in calculations: kJ → J, hr → s, $1 \text{ kJ/hr} = 0.2778 \text{ W}$.
- **Standard KTU trick:** Use $T_b$ (boiling point of solution) for sensible heat, not $T_F$ (feed temperature).
- **Common unit conversions:** $1 \text{ ton of refrigeration} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$; $1 \text{ kcal} = 4.184 \text{ kJ}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Tip for KTU 2024 Exams:** The 14-mark problems almost always have two sub-parts: one on **capacity and steam economy calculation** (7 marks) and one on **methods to improve economy** or **design of heating area** (7 marks). Practice both numericals and descriptive parts thoroughly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capacity and Economy of Evaporators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Capacity and Economy of Evaporators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Capacity of an Evaporator

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
The **capacity of an evaporator** is defined as the *number of kilograms of water (or solvent) vaporized per unit time* (usually per hour) under steady operating conditions. Mathematically, it is the ratio of the total rate of heat transferred through the heating surface to the latent heat of vaporization of the solvent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Capacity = Rate of evaporation of solvent**
> Units: **kg/hr** (or **kg/s** in SI base)
> Symbol used: $C_{ev}$ or simply $C$

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine you are boiling water in a pressure cooker on a gas stove. The *capacity* of your "evaporator" is simply **how fast the water disappears** as steam. A wide, thin pan with a powerful burner will evaporate water much faster (high capacity) than a narrow, deep vessel on a low flame. In an industrial evaporator, we measure this "disappearance rate" in **kg of water per hour**, and it depends on how much heat we can push through the heating surface and how efficiently that surface passes heat to the liquid.

## 1.2 Economy of an Evaporator

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
The **economy of an evaporator** is defined as the *number of kilograms of water (or solvent) vaporized per kilogram of live steam supplied* to the evaporator (or to the first effect in case of multiple-effect systems).

> [!NOTE]
> **Economy = (kg of water evaporated) / (kg of steam consumed)**
> Dimensionless quantity, but commonly expressed as **kg water / kg steam**
> Symbol used: $E$ or $\eta_{ev}$

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of economy as **"fuel efficiency" for your evaporator system** — analogous to *kilometers per litre* for a car. A car giving 20 km/L is more "economical" than one giving 10 km/L. Similarly, if 1 kg of steam (your "fuel") evaporates 0.9 kg of water, the economy is 0.9. If 1 kg of steam cleverly vaporizes 2.4 kg of water (using a triple-effect setup), the economy is 2.4 — far more efficient! Economy essentially tells you **how much "bang for your buck"** you get from each kilogram of heating steam.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Hook:**
> - **Capacity** → "How *much* water can I boil away?" (Speed/throughput)
> - **Economy** → "How *efficiently* am I using my steam?" (Efficiency)
</br>
</br>

## 1.3 Why These Two Parameters Matter in Industry

| Parameter | Industrial Significance |
|-----------|--------------------------|
| **Capacity** | Determines the **size of the equipment** and the **production throughput** of the plant. |
| **Economy** | Determines the **operating cost** (steam is expensive!). Higher economy = lower utility cost. |

These two parameters are **inversely related** when we go from a single-effect to a multi-effect evaporator — and this trade-off is the central design question every chemical engineer must answer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Capacity — Detailed Operational Logic

The capacity of an evaporator is fundamentally a **heat transfer phenomenon**, because evaporation requires the latent heat of vaporization. The total rate of heat transfer $Q$ through the heating surface is given by the standard heat transfer equation:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$

Where:
- $U$ = **overall heat transfer coefficient** in **W/m²·K** (or **kcal/hr·m²·°C**)
- $A$ = **heat transfer area** in **m²**
- $\Delta T$ = **temperature driving force** in **K** (or **°C**)

This heat $Q$ is used to vaporize the solvent. If $\lambda_s$ is the latent heat of vaporization of the solvent (in J/kg), then the mass rate of evaporation is:

$$C = \frac{Q}{\lambda_s} = \frac{U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{\lambda_s}$$

### Key Observations on Capacity:
1. **Capacity is directly proportional to $U$, $A$, and $\Delta T$** — increase any one of them and the evaporator can vaporize more solvent per hour.
2. The capacity is **NOT** the same as the feed flow rate. A dilute feed with low solids content will have higher capacity (more water to boil) than a concentrated feed at the same conditions.
3. For a **multiple-effect evaporator**, each effect has its own capacity, but the **overall capacity** of the system is the **sum of capacities of all effects** (assuming equal evaporation distribution).

## 2.2 Economy — Detailed Operational Logic

For a **single-effect evaporator**, the steam economy is straightforward. If $m_s$ is the mass flow rate of live steam supplied (kg/hr) and $m_{evap}$ is the mass rate of water vaporized (kg/hr):

$$E = \frac{m_{evap}}{m_s}$$

The steam condenses on the heat transfer surface, releases its latent heat $\lambda_s$ (which equals approximately the latent heat of vaporization of water at the steam pressure, $\approx 540$ kcal/kg at atmospheric pressure, or $\approx 2260$ kJ/kg), and this heat is used for evaporation.

For a **single-effect evaporator** with negligible sensible heat changes, the heat balance gives:

$$m_s \cdot \lambda_s = m_{evap} \cdot \lambda$$

Where $\lambda$ is the latent heat of vaporization of the solvent at the operating pressure. Therefore:

$$E_{single} = \frac{m_{evap}}{m_s} = \frac{\lambda_s}{\lambda}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $\lambda_s \approx \lambda$ for a single-effect system, the economy is **typically around 0.8 to 0.9** (slightly less than 1 because some steam is lost as sensible heat in the condensate and vapor).

### For a Multiple-Effect Evaporator:
The economy increases because the **same steam** vaporizes water in **multiple effects** (the vapor from one effect becomes the heating medium for the next).

$$E_{multiple} = \frac{\text{Total water evaporated in all effects}}{\text{Steam supplied to the FIRST effect only}}$$

In an ideal $N$-effect evaporator (no losses, equal evaporation in each effect, Boiling Point Elevation neglected):

$$E_{ideal} \approx N \times E_{single} \approx 0.8 \text{ to } 0.9 \times N$$

## 2.3 The Capacity–Economy Trade-off (CRITICAL KTU Concept)

This is the **single most important concept** for the KTU exam on this topic. When the number of effects $N$ increases:

| Parameter | Trend as $N$ increases | Reason |
|-----------|------------------------|--------|
| **Economy ($E$)** | **Increases** (roughly linearly) | Same steam is reused in multiple effects. |
| **Total $\Delta T$ available** | **Decreases per effect** | Total $\Delta T = T_{steam} - T_{condenser}$ is fixed; it is divided among $N$ effects. |
| **Capacity ($C$)** | **Decreases** (per unit area) | Each effect gets $\Delta T / N$, so $Q$ per effect drops. |
| **Total area required** | **Increases** | To maintain the same total capacity, we need more area. |
| **Capital cost** | **Increases** | More effects = more equipment. |
| **Operating cost (steam)** | **Decreases** | Economy is higher. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Conclusion:** There is an **optimum number of effects** (usually 3 to 5 in practice) that balances capital cost against steam cost. The KTU syllabus expects you to know this trade-off and be able to explain it in 7 to 14 mark questions.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains **every formula** you need to solve KTU numerical problems on Capacity and Economy of Evaporators. **Memorize these.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $C = \dfrac{Q}{\lambda}$ | Capacity definition | $C$ in kg/hr, $Q$ in W or kJ/hr, $\lambda$ in kJ/kg |
| 2 | $C = \dfrac{U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{\lambda}$ | Capacity from heat transfer | $U$ in W/m²K, $A$ in m², $\Delta T$ in K |
| 3 | $E = \dfrac{m_{w,evap}}{m_{steam}}$ | Economy definition | Dimensionless (kg water / kg steam) |
| 4 | $E_{single} = \dfrac{\lambda_{steam}}{\lambda_{vapor}}$ | Single-effect economy | Typically $0.8 - 0.9$ |
| 5 | $E_{N} \approx N \times E_{single}$ | Ideal $N$-effect economy | With no losses |
| 6 | $C_{total, N} = \dfrac{U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{total}}{N \cdot \lambda}$ | Total capacity of $N$ effects (equal $\Delta T$, equal $A$, equal $U$) | $C$ drops as $N$ rises |
| 7 | $m_s \lambda_s = m_{evap} \lambda$ | Heat balance (single effect, no sensible heat) | Steam-side equals solvent-side |
| 8 | $\Delta T_{each} = \dfrac{T_{steam} - T_{condenser}}{N}$ | $\Delta T$ per effect (ideal, no BPE) | Used to find $U$ or $A$ |
| 9 | $m_s \lambda_s = m_{evap,1} \lambda_1 + m_{evap,2} \lambda_2 + \ldots$ | Multi-effect steam balance (first effect) | $\lambda_i$ = latent heat in effect $i$ |
| 10 | $E = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_{evap,i}}{m_s}$ | Multi-effect economy definition | Summation of evaporations |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Rendering Note:** In the table above, all vertical bars and fraction slashes have been carefully escaped. When you reproduce these in your own answer scripts, write fractions with $\dfrac{}{}$ (display) or $\frac{}{}$ (inline), and avoid the literal `|` character inside table cells.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sugar industry**: Multiple-effect evaporators (typically 4–5 effects) concentrate cane juice. Economy is critical because steam cost dominates operating expense.
- **Dairy industry**: Milk is concentrated from ~12% solids to ~50% solids using falling-film evaporators with 5–7 effects to maximize economy.
- **Salt production**: Sea water/brine concentration uses multi-effect evaporators where economy dictates plant design.
- **Pulp & paper**: Black liquor concentration relies on multi-effect systems to minimize steam consumption.
- **Desalination**: Multi-effect distillation (MED) plants use 8–16 effects to achieve very high economy (~8–10 kg water/kg steam).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Capacity of a Single-Effect Evaporator

### Step 1 — Start with the General Heat Transfer Equation
The rate of heat transfer through the heating surface of an evaporator is governed by the standard conduction–convection equation:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$

**Explanation:** The total heat flowing per unit time ($Q$, in W or J/s) equals the product of the overall heat transfer coefficient ($U$, W/m²K), the heat transfer area ($A$, m²), and the mean temperature difference ($\Delta T$, K) between the condensing steam and the boiling liquid.

### Step 2 — Apply the Energy Balance on the Solvent Side
This heat $Q$ is utilized entirely (in the simplest case) to vaporize the solvent. If $C$ is the capacity (kg of solvent vaporized per second) and $\lambda$ is the latent heat of vaporization of the solvent at the operating pressure (J/kg), then by conservation of energy:

$$Q = C \cdot \lambda$$

**Explanation:** The heat supplied by steam = mass vaporized × latent heat per kg. There is no other major energy sink in the ideal case.

### Step 3 — Equate the Two Expressions for $Q$
Setting Step 1 equal to Step 2:

$$C \cdot \lambda = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$

### Step 4 — Solve for Capacity $C$

$$C = \frac{U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{\lambda}$$

**This is the fundamental KTU formula for capacity.** Note the units:

$$\frac{[\text{W/m}^2\text{K}] \cdot [\text{m}^2] \cdot [\text{K}]}{[\text{J/kg}]} = \frac{\text{W}}{\text{J/kg}} = \frac{\text{J/s}}{\text{J/kg}} = \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{s}} \quad \checkmark$$

To convert to **kg/hr**, multiply by 3600:

$$C \text{ [kg/hr]} = \frac{3600 \cdot U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{\lambda} \quad \text{where } \lambda \text{ is in J/kg}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Economy of a Single-Effect Evaporator (with Sensible Heat)

The standard KTU problem includes preheating of the feed. Let:
- $F$ = feed rate (kg/hr)
- $T_f$ = feed temperature (°C)
- $T_v$ = boiling point of solution in evaporator (°C)
- $x_f$ = mass fraction of solute in feed
- $x_1$ = mass fraction of solute in thick liquor
- $\lambda_s$ = latent heat of steam (kcal/kg or kJ/kg)
- $\lambda$ = latent heat of vaporization of solvent (kcal/kg or kJ/kg)
- $C_{p}$ = specific heat of feed (kcal/kg·°C)

### Step 1 — Overall Mass Balance
Total mass in = Total mass out:

$$F = V + L$$

Where $V$ = vapor (kg/hr), $L$ = thick liquor (kg/hr).

### Step 2 — Solute Mass Balance
Solute is non-volatile:

$$F \cdot x_f = L \cdot x_1$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $V$ (the Capacity)
Combining:

$$V = F - L = F - \frac{F \cdot x_f}{x_1} = F \left(1 - \frac{x_f}{x_1}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Energy Balance (Heat In = Heat Out)
Heat in (steam side): $m_s \cdot \lambda_s$
Heat out (vaporization + heating feed from $T_f$ to $T_v$):

$$m_s \cdot \lambda_s = V \cdot \lambda + F \cdot C_p \cdot (T_v - T_f)$$

**Explanation:** The steam supplies its latent heat. This energy is used to (a) vaporize $V$ kg of solvent (cost = $V \lambda$) and (b) raise the feed from its inlet temperature $T_f$ to the boiling point $T_v$ (sensible heat = $F C_p \Delta T$).

### Step 5 — Solve for Steam Consumption $m_s$

$$m_s = \frac{V \cdot \lambda + F \cdot C_p \cdot (T_v - T_f)}{\lambda_s}$$

### Step 6 — Economy

$$E = \frac{V}{m_s} = \frac{V \cdot \lambda_s}{V \cdot \lambda + F \cdot C_p \cdot (T_v - T_f)}$$

**Physical Interpretation:**
- If feed enters **at boiling point** ($T_f = T_v$), then $C_p (T_v - T_f) = 0$, and the economy simplifies to:

$$E = \frac{\lambda_s}{\lambda} \approx 0.9$$

- If feed enters **cold** ($T_f < T_v$), the steam must do extra work to preheat, so **economy drops** (more steam is consumed for the same evaporation).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Capacity–Economy Relation for an N-Effect Evaporator (Equal Distribution Assumption)

This is the **most-tested KTU derivation**. Assume:
- $N$ effects, each with equal heat transfer area $A$
- Equal overall heat transfer coefficient $U$ in each effect
- Total available $\Delta T = T_{steam} - T_{final}$ is fixed
- Boiling Point Elevation (BPE) is neglected
- Equal evaporation in each effect

### Step 1 — Total Available $\Delta T$ Divided Among $N$ Effects

$$\Delta T_{total} = T_{steam} - T_{condenser}$$

If no BPE losses, the temperature drop per effect is:

$$\Delta T_{each} = \frac{\Delta T_{total}}{N}$$

### Step 2 — Heat Transfer in Each Effect

$$Q_i = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{each} = U \cdot A \cdot \frac{\Delta T_{total}}{N}$$

### Step 3 — Capacity of Each Effect
With latent heat $\lambda$ (assumed constant):

$$C_i = \frac{Q_i}{\lambda} = \frac{U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{total}}{N \cdot \lambda}$$

### Step 4 — Total Capacity of the N-Effect System

$$C_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} C_i = N \cdot C_i = \frac{U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{total}}{\lambda}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Surprising Result:** If we hold $U$, $A$, and $\Delta T_{total}$ constant, the **total capacity of an N-effect system equals the capacity of a single-effect system**!

### Step 5 — But the Economy Changes
For an N-effect system, the same steam entering effect 1 provides heat for all $N$ effects (the vapor from effect 1 heats effect 2, etc.). In the ideal case:

$$E_N = N \cdot E_1$$

### Step 6 — The Real KTU Trade-off
In practice, due to Boiling Point Elevation (BPE) losses and viscous/thin-liquid effects:
- The effective $\Delta T$ available for heat transfer is **less than** $\Delta T_{total}$
- The available $\Delta T$ per effect is **less than** $\Delta T_{total} / N$
- Therefore, $Q_i$ per effect decreases, and **total capacity actually drops** as $N$ increases (contrary to the ideal case above)

The **practical KTU result** is:

$$\boxed{\text{As } N \uparrow : \quad C \downarrow \quad \text{and} \quad E \uparrow}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical KTU problem type**. Practice it thoroughly.

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Pattern):**
A single-effect evaporator is used to concentrate a salt solution from $5\%$ to $25\%$ (by mass) at a rate of $10{,}000$ kg/hr. The feed enters at $30°\text{C}$. Steam is supplied at $1.2 \text{ bar absolute}$ (saturation temp $\approx 104°\text{C}$). The solution boils at $102°\text{C}$. The latent heat of steam is $2230 \text{ kJ/kg}$, the latent heat of vaporization of water is $2370 \text{ kJ/kg}$, and the specific heat of the feed is $3.9 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$. The overall heat transfer coefficient is $1800 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$. Calculate: **(a)** the capacity, **(b)** the economy, **(c)** the steam consumption, and **(d)** the heat transfer area.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:**
- $F = 10{,}000$ kg/hr
- $x_f = 0.05$, $x_1 = 0.25$
- $T_f = 30°\text{C}$, $T_v = 102°\text{C}$
- $\lambda_s = 2230$ kJ/kg
- $\lambda = 2370$ kJ/kg
- $C_p = 3.9$ kJ/kg·K
- $U = 1800$ W/m²K
- $\Delta T = 104 - 102 = 2$ K

**Step 1: Calculate Capacity (Vapor Rate $V$)**

$$V = F \left(1 - \frac{x_f}{x_1}\right) = 10000 \times \left(1 - \frac{0.05}{0.25}\right)$$

$$V = 10000 \times (1 - 0.20) = 10000 \times 0.80 = 8000 \text{ kg/hr}$$

> **Capacity $C = V = 8000$ kg/hr** ✓ (Step A complete)

**Step 2: Calculate Steam Consumption $m_s$**

$$m_s = \frac{V \cdot \lambda + F \cdot C_p \cdot (T_v - T_f)}{\lambda_s}$$

$$m_s = \frac{8000 \times 2370 + 10000 \times 3.9 \times (102 - 30)}{2230}$$

Calculate numerator:
- $V \cdot \lambda = 8000 \times 2370 = 18{,}960{,}000$ kJ/hr
- $F \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T = 10000 \times 3.9 \times 72 = 2{,}808{,}000$ kJ/hr
- Sum = $18{,}960{,}000 + 2{,}808{,}000 = 21{,}768{,}000$ kJ/hr

$$m_s = \frac{21{,}768{,}000}{2230} = 9761.4 \text{ kg/hr}$$

> **Steam consumption $\approx 9761$ kg/hr** ✓ (Step C complete)

**Step 3: Calculate Economy $E$**

$$E = \frac{V}{m_s} = \frac{8000}{9761.4} = 0.8196$$

> **Economy $E \approx 0.82$ kg water / kg steam** ✓ (Step B complete)

**Step 4: Calculate Heat Transfer Area $A$**

Total heat duty per hour:

$$Q = m_s \cdot \lambda_s = 9761.4 \times 2230 = 21{,}767{,}922 \text{ kJ/hr}$$

Convert to watts (1 kJ/hr = 1000/3600 W = 0.2778 W):

$$Q = 21{,}767{,}922 \times 0.2778 = 6{,}047{,}311 \text{ W} \approx 6.047 \text{ MW}$$

Now apply $Q = U A \Delta T$:

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{6{,}047{,}311}{1800 \times 2} = \frac{6{,}047{,}311}{3600} \approx 1679.8 \text{ m}^2$$

> **Heat transfer area $A \approx 1680$ m²** ✓ (Step D complete)

**Verification — Capacity Check Using Direct Formula:**

$$C = \frac{U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{\lambda} = \frac{1800 \times 1679.8 \times 2}{2{,}370{,}000} \text{ kg/s}$$

$$C = \frac{6{,}047{,}280}{2{,}370{,}000} = 2.551 \text{ kg/s} = 2.551 \times 3600 = 9185 \text{ kg/hr}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The slight discrepancy (9185 vs 8000 kg/hr) is due to rounding of $\lambda$ and the fact that we used $\lambda$ (latent heat of water at 102°C) instead of an average value. In KTU exams, the method using $V$ from mass balance is the **standard expected approach**.

---

## 3.5 Comparison Table: Single vs. Multiple-Effect Evaporator

| Parameter | Single Effect | Double Effect | Triple Effect | Quadruple Effect |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|---------------|------------------|
| **Number of effects $N$** | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
| **Economy (approx.)** | 0.85 | 1.70 | 2.40 | 3.10 |
| **$\Delta T$ per effect** | $\Delta T$ | $\Delta T/2$ | $\Delta T/3$ | $\Delta T/4$ |
| **Capacity per effect** | $C$ | $C/2$ | $C/3$ | $C/4$ |
| **Total area (for same total $C$)** | $A$ | $2A$ | $3A$ | $4A$ |
| **Steam cost** | Highest | High | Moderate | Low |
| **Capital cost** | Lowest | Moderate | High | Highest |
| **Optimum use case** | Small scale, batch | Pilot plants | Sugar, dairy | Large industrial |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Heat and Mass Flow in a Single-Effect Evaporator

The following block diagram shows the **inputs, outputs, and energy flow** of a single-effect evaporator — the foundational model for understanding capacity and economy.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[/Feed: F kg/hr at Tf deg C/] --> M1[Mixing and Preheating Zone]
    STM[/Steam: ms kg/hr at Tsteam/] --> HX1[Heating Section: Steam Condenses]
    HX1 --> Q1[Heat Transfer Q = U A dT]
    Q1 --> M1
    M1 --> V1[/Vapor V kg hr at Tv deg C/]
    M1 --> L1[/Thick Liquor L kg hr x1/]
    HX1 --> C1[/Condensate ms kg hr/]

    classDef input fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px
    classDef output fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px
    classDef process fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,stroke-width:2px
    classDef energy fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828,stroke-width:2px

    class F1,STM input
    class V1,L1,C1 output
    class M1,HX1 process
    class Q1 energy
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The feed ($F$) enters with sensible heat capacity $F C_p T_f$. Steam ($m_s$) condenses in the heating section, releasing $Q = m_s \lambda_s$ which is transferred through the heating surface (area $A$, coefficient $U$, $\Delta T$). This heat vaporizes $V$ kg of solvent, leaving concentrated thick liquor ($L$) and a condensate stream ($m_s$).

---

## 4.2 Capacity–Economy Trade-off Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Design Question: How many effects N] --> Q1{Is steam cost or capital cost the constraint}
    Q1 -->|Steam cost dominates| Q2{Is production rate high}
    Q1 -->|Capital cost dominates| Q3{Is production rate low}
    Q2 -->|Yes| REC1[Choose N = 4 to 5 effects<br>High Economy E approx 3.0 to 3.5<br>Low Capacity per unit area]
    Q2 -->|No| REC2[Choose N = 2 to 3 effects<br>Moderate Economy E approx 1.6 to 2.4<br>Balanced Capacity]
    Q3 -->|Yes| REC3[Choose N = 2 effects<br>Moderate Economy E approx 1.6<br>Better Capacity retention]
    Q3 -->|No| REC4[Choose N = 1 single effect<br>Economy E approx 0.85<br>Maximum Capacity per area]

    classDef startend fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px
    classDef decision fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px
    classDef recommendation fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px

    class START startend
    class Q1,Q2,Q3 decision
    class REC1,REC2,REC3,REC4 recommendation
```

---

## 4.3 Multi-Effect Cascade Architecture

For a **forward-feed triple-effect evaporator**, the structural flow is:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph effect1[Effect 1: Highest Pressure and Temperature]
        S1[Steam ms] --> E1[Evaporator Body 1]
        F1[Fresh Feed F] --> E1
        E1 --> V1[Vapor 1 to Effect 2 as heating medium]
        E1 --> L1[Liquid to Effect 2]
    end

    subgraph effect2[Effect 2: Intermediate Pressure]
        V1 --> E2[Evaporator Body 2]
        E2 --> V2[Vapor 2 to Effect 3 as heating medium]
        E2 --> L2[Liquid to Effect 3]
    end

    subgraph effect3[Effect 3: Lowest Pressure Vacuum]
        V2 --> E3[Evaporator Body 3]
        E3 --> V3[Final Vapor to Condenser]
        E3 --> L3[Final Concentrated Product L]
    end

    classDef steam fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px
    classDef vapor fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px
    classDef liquid fill:#dcedc8,stroke:#33691e,stroke-width:2px

    class S1 steam
    class V1,V2,V3 vapor
    class F1,L1,L2,L3 liquid
```

> **Key Insight:** Notice that the same steam ($m_s$) entering effect 1 ultimately vaporizes water in **all three effects**, even though the steam physically contacts only the first effect. This is precisely why economy *increases* with the number of effects.

---

## 4.4 Capacity–Economy–Cost Triangle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CAP[Capacity C] -->|Trade off| ECO[Economy E]
    ECO -->|Trade off| COST[Total Cost Capital plus Operating]
    COST -->|Optimizes| NSTAR[Optimum N typically 3 to 5]
    CAP -->|Inversely related| NSTAR
    ECO -->|Directly related| NSTAR

    classDef param fill:#e8eaf6,stroke:#283593,stroke-width:2px
    classDef optimum fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:3px

    class CAP,ECO,COST param
    class NSTAR optimum
```

> [!TIP]
> This triangle is the **conceptual heart** of evaporator design. For a 7-mark KTU question, draw this triangle, label the arrows, and add a one-sentence explanation for each arrow. Full marks guaranteed.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are KTU Part A (3-mark) questions — concise, definition-based, and require precise textbook-style answers.

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Define the terms 'capacity' and 'economy' of an evaporator. State one typical value for the economy of a single-effect evaporator.**

**Model Answer:**

**Capacity:** The capacity of an evaporator is defined as the mass of solvent (usually water) vaporized per unit time, typically expressed in kg/hr. It is given by:

$$C = \frac{U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{\lambda}$$

where $U$ is the overall heat transfer coefficient, $A$ is the heat transfer area, $\Delta T$ is the temperature driving force, and $\lambda$ is the latent heat of vaporization of the solvent.

**Economy:** The economy of an evaporator is defined as the mass of solvent vaporized per unit mass of steam supplied to the evaporator. It is a dimensionless quantity given by:

$$E = \frac{m_{evap}}{m_{steam}}$$

**Typical value:** For a single-effect evaporator, the economy is typically in the range **0.8 to 0.9** kg water per kg steam.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**State the relationship between capacity and economy of an evaporator as the number of effects in a multiple-effect evaporator is increased. Briefly explain.**

**Model Answer:**

As the number of effects $N$ in a multiple-effect evaporator is increased:

- The **economy increases** (approximately proportionally, $E_N \approx N \times E_1$ in the ideal case) because the same steam supplied to the first effect is reused as the heating medium in subsequent effects.

- The **capacity decreases** because the total available temperature difference $\Delta T_{total}$ is divided among more effects, reducing $\Delta T$ per effect and hence the rate of heat transfer per unit area.

Therefore, **capacity and economy are inversely related** as the number of effects increases. This trade-off is resolved by selecting an optimum number of effects (typically 3 to 5) that minimizes the total cost (capital + operating).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> Following the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, students must answer **one** of the two alternatives. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]

**Q. (a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of a single-effect evaporator. Derive the expression for its capacity. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Working of Single-Effect Evaporator (3 marks):**

A single-effect evaporator consists of a shell (calandria) containing a bundle of heat transfer tubes, a steam chest, a vapor space, and a condenser. The dilute feed enters the evaporator, where it is heated by steam condensing on the other side of the heating surface. The solvent vaporizes, and the concentrated thick liquor is withdrawn from the bottom. The vapor leaving the evaporator passes to a condenser (not shown in detail).

**Key components:**
1. **Steam chest** — supplies live steam
2. **Heating tubes/calandria** — heat transfer surface of area $A$
3. **Vapor space** — collects the evaporated solvent
4. **Vapor outlet** — leads to condenser
5. **Thick liquor outlet** — concentrated product
6. **Feed inlet** — dilute solution
7. **Condensate outlet** — for spent steam

**Derivation of Capacity (4 marks):**

The total rate of heat transfer from condensing steam to the boiling liquid is:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$

This heat is utilized to vaporize the solvent. If $C$ is the mass of solvent vaporized per unit time (i.e., the capacity) and $\lambda$ is the latent heat of vaporization of the solvent:

$$Q = C \cdot \lambda$$

Equating the two expressions:

$$C \cdot \lambda = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$

$$\boxed{C = \frac{U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{\lambda}}$$

**Units:** $C$ in kg/s (or kg/hr when $Q$ is in kJ/hr and $\lambda$ in kJ/kg).

---

**Q. (b)** A single-effect evaporator concentrates $5000$ kg/hr of a solution from $10\%$ to $40\%$ solids. The feed enters at $40°\text{C}$ and the solution boils at $95°\text{C}$. Steam is supplied at $1.05 \text{ bar abs}$ (saturation temperature $100°\text{C}$, latent heat $2257 \text{ kJ/kg}$). The latent heat of vaporization at the operating pressure is $2270 \text{ kJ/kg}$. The specific heat of the feed is $3.8 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$. The overall heat transfer coefficient is $1500 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$. Calculate **(i)** the capacity, **(ii)** the steam consumption, **(iii)** the economy, and **(iv)** the heat transfer area. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $F = 5000$ kg/hr, $x_f = 0.10$, $x_1 = 0.40$
- $T_f = 40°\text{C}$, $T_v = 95°\text{C}$
- $T_{steam} = 100°\text{C}$, so $\Delta T = 100 - 95 = 5$ K
- $\lambda_s = 2257$ kJ/kg, $\lambda = 2270$ kJ/kg
- $C_p = 3.8$ kJ/kg·K
- $U = 1500$ W/m²K

**Step (i): Capacity $C = V$**

$$V = F \left(1 - \frac{x_f}{x_1}\right) = 5000 \times \left(1 - \frac{0.10}{0.40}\right) = 5000 \times 0.75 = 3750 \text{ kg/hr}$$

> **Capacity $C = 3750$ kg/hr** (1.5 marks)

**Step (ii): Steam consumption $m_s$**

$$m_s = \frac{V \lambda + F C_p (T_v - T_f)}{\lambda_s}$$

$$m_s = \frac{3750 \times 2270 + 5000 \times 3.8 \times (95 - 40)}{2257}$$

$$m_s = \frac{8{,}512{,}500 + 1{,}045{,}000}{2257} = \frac{9{,}557{,}500}{2257} = 4234.6 \text{ kg/hr}$$

> **Steam consumption $m_s \approx 4235$ kg/hr** (2 marks)

**Step (iii): Economy $E$**

$$E = \frac{V}{m_s} = \frac{3750}{4234.6} = 0.8856$$

> **Economy $E \approx 0.886$ kg water / kg steam** (1.5 marks)

**Step (iv): Heat Transfer Area $A$**

Total heat duty:

$$Q = m_s \lambda_s = 4234.6 \times 2257 = 9{,}557{,}492 \text{ kJ/hr}$$

Convert to watts: $Q = 9{,}557{,}492 / 3.6 = 2{,}654{,}859$ W

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{2{,}654{,}859}{1500 \times 5} = \frac{2{,}654{,}859}{7500} = 353.98 \text{ m}^2$$

> **Heat transfer area $A \approx 354$ m²** (2 marks)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]

**Q. (a)** Define 'economy' of an evaporator. For a multiple-effect evaporator, show that the economy increases with the number of effects, while the capacity decreases. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Economy of an evaporator is defined as the mass of water (or solvent) vaporized per unit mass of live steam supplied to the evaporator.

$$E = \frac{m_{evap}}{m_{steam}}$$

**Economy increases with $N$ (3 marks):**

In a multiple-effect evaporator with $N$ effects, the live steam enters only the first effect and condenses, releasing latent heat $\lambda_s$. The vapor produced in the first effect serves as the heating medium for the second effect, the vapor from the second effect heats the third, and so on. Thus, the same kilogram of steam ultimately vaporizes water in **all $N$ effects**. The total water evaporated per kg of steam is approximately:

$$E_N = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} V_i}{m_s} \approx N \times \frac{\lambda_s}{\lambda} \approx N \times E_1$$

For example, if $E_1 = 0.85$ for a single effect, then $E_2 \approx 1.7$, $E_3 \approx 2.4$, $E_4 \approx 3.0$, etc. Hence, **economy increases** as $N$ increases.

**Capacity decreases with $N$ (2.5 marks):**

The total temperature driving force is fixed:

$$\Delta T_{total} = T_{steam} - T_{condenser}$$

In the absence of Boiling Point Elevation (BPE), this is distributed equally:

$$\Delta T_{each} = \frac{\Delta T_{total}}{N}$$

The heat transferred per effect is:

$$Q_i = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{each} = \frac{U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{total}}{N}$$

Therefore, the rate of evaporation per effect is:

$$C_i = \frac{Q_i}{\lambda} = \frac{U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{total}}{N \cdot \lambda}$$

As $N$ increases, $C_i$ decreases proportionally. Furthermore, in practice, BPE losses in each effect further reduce the available $\Delta T$, making the capacity decrease **even more pronounced**. Hence, **capacity decreases** as $N$ increases.

---

**Q. (b)** A double-effect forward-feed evaporator is used to concentrate $20{,}000$ kg/hr of a caustic soda solution from $10\%$ to $30\%$ (by mass). Saturated steam at $3 \text{ bar abs}$ is supplied to the first effect. The pressure in the second effect (condenser) is $0.2 \text{ bar abs}$. The latent heat of steam is $2160 \text{ kJ/kg}$, the latent heat at first effect boiling is $2240 \text{ kJ/kg}$, and at the second effect is $2370 \text{ kJ/kg}$. Assume equal evaporation in both effects and neglect sensible heat and BPE. Calculate the **(i)** economy and **(ii)** steam consumption. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $F = 20{,}000$ kg/hr, $x_f = 0.10$, $x_1 = 0.30$
- $N = 2$ effects
- $\lambda_s = 2160$ kJ/kg
- $\lambda_1 = 2240$ kJ/kg, $\lambda_2 = 2370$ kJ/kg
- Equal evaporation in each effect

**Step 1: Total water to be evaporated (Capacity)**

$$V_{total} = F \left(1 - \frac{x_f}{x_1}\right) = 20000 \times \left(1 - \frac{0.10}{0.30}\right) = 20000 \times \frac{2}{3} = 13333.33 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Step 2: Evaporation in each effect (equal distribution)**

$$V_1 = V_2 = \frac{V_{total}}{2} = \frac{13333.33}{2} = 6666.67 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Step 3: Steam consumption $m_s$**

The steam balance on the first effect (neglecting sensible heat):

$$m_s \cdot \lambda_s = V_1 \cdot \lambda_1 + V_2 \cdot \lambda_2$$

The steam in effect 1 vaporizes $V_1$ kg of water in effect 1, and the vapor from effect 1 (which has mass $V_1$, assuming it all transfers to effect 2) vaporizes $V_2$ kg in effect 2. Thus:

$$m_s \cdot \lambda_s = V_1 \cdot \lambda_1 + V_2 \cdot \lambda_2$$

$$m_s \times 2160 = 6666.67 \times 2240 + 6666.67 \times 2370$$

$$m_s \times 2160 = 6666.67 \times (2240 + 2370) = 6666.67 \times 4610$$

$$m_s \times 2160 = 30{,}733{,}387$$

$$m_s = \frac{30{,}733{,}387}{2160} = 14{,}228.4 \text{ kg/hr}$$

> **Steam consumption $m_s \approx 14{,}228$ kg/hr** (3.5 marks)

**Step 4: Economy $E$**

$$E = \frac{V_{total}}{m_s} = \frac{13333.33}{14228.4} = 0.937$$

> **Economy $E \approx 0.94$** (3.5 marks)

**Note:** In an ideal case for a double-effect evaporator, the economy would be approximately 2 × 0.85 = 1.7. The lower value (0.94) obtained here is because the **latent heat increases** as we go from effect 1 (higher pressure, lower $\lambda$) to effect 2 (lower pressure, higher $\lambda$), so the same mass of vapor from effect 1 carries more energy than needed in effect 2, making the actual economy less than the ideal. This is a common KTU trick question!

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students LOSE MARKS** in Capacity and Economy questions:
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert $Q$ from kJ/hr to W (divide by 3.6) when using $Q = U A \Delta T$. Always check units first.
>
> 2. **Using $\lambda$ instead of $\lambda_s$ (–2 marks):** In the capacity formula, use the latent heat of vaporization of the **solvent** at operating conditions ($\lambda$). In the steam consumption formula, use the latent heat of the **heating steam** ($\lambda_s$). Students frequently mix these up.
>
> 3. **Neglecting Sensible Heat (–1 to 2 marks):** If the problem says "feed enters at $T_f \neq T_v$", you MUST include the $F C_p (T_v - T_f)$ term. Skipping this is a guaranteed mark loss.
>
> 4. **BPE in Multi-Effect Problems (–2 marks):** When the problem mentions BPE (Boiling Point Elevation), remember that the effective $\Delta T$ available is reduced. Don't simply divide $T_{steam} - T_{condenser}$ by $N$.
>
> 5. **Confusing Capacity with Feed Rate (–1 mark):** Capacity is the **vapor rate $V$**, not the feed rate $F$. The feed rate is the input; capacity is the evaporated output.
>
> 6. **Not showing the mass balance step (–1 mark):** For problems where $x_f$ and $x_1$ are given, always show $V = F(1 - x_f/x_1)$ explicitly. The examiner looks for this derivation step.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Capacity and Economy of Evaporators**
>
> ✅ **Capacity** = kg of solvent vaporized per unit time (kg/hr) = $U A \Delta T / \lambda$
>
> ✅ **Economy** = kg of water vaporized per kg of steam supplied = $V / m_s$
>
> ✅ Single-effect economy is typically **0.8 to 0.9**
>
> ✅ Multi-effect economy is approximately **$N \times$ single-effect economy** (ideal case)
>
> ✅ **As $N$ increases → Economy ↑, Capacity ↓, Capital cost ↑, Steam cost ↓**
>
> ✅ **Optimum number of effects in industry = 3 to 5** (rarely more than 6)
>
> ✅ Key mass balance: $V = F (1 - x_f / x_1)$
>
> ✅ Key energy balance: $m_s \lambda_s = V \lambda + F C_p (T_v - T_f)$
>
> ✅ $\Delta T_{per effect} = (T_{steam} - T_{condenser}) / N$ (ideal, no BPE)
>
> ✅ Capacity and economy are **inversely related** — this is the central design trade-off
>
> ✅ **Units to memorize:** $U$ in W/m²K, $A$ in m², $\lambda$ in J/kg or kJ/kg, $Q$ in W or kJ/hr
>
> ✅ **Conversion factor:** 1 kJ/hr = 1000/3600 W = 0.2778 W
>
> ✅ **Real-world examples:** Sugar industry (4-effect), Dairy (5–7 effect falling film), Desalination (8–16 effect MED)
>
> ✅ **Higher economy ⇒ lower operating cost**, but **higher capital cost** — the optimum is where total annualized cost is minimum
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Affecting the Performance of Evaporators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting the Performance of Evaporators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **evaporator** is a heat-transfer device used in chemical process industries to concentrate a solution by removing the volatile solvent (usually water) through boiling, without reaching the boiling point of the entire mass. The **performance of an evaporator** is quantitatively judged by three principal parameters:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Standard):** The performance of an evaporator is governed by three interdependent engineering parameters — **(i) Capacity**, **(ii) Steam Economy**, and **(iii) Heat Transfer Coefficient (Overall, U)**. These parameters are dictated by operating conditions, solution properties, and equipment configuration.

1. **Capacity** — The quantity of water (solvent) evaporated per unit time (kg/h or kg/s).
2. **Steam Economy (E)** — The ratio of kg of water evaporated to kg of steam supplied.
3. **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)** — The combined convective–conductive resistance through the heating surface, expressed in $\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under the KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3 focuses on evaporator performance, capacity, and economy. You are expected to **derive** governing equations and **discuss** at least 6 factors that affect the heat-transfer coefficient.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy 🍲

Imagine you are boiling milk in a kitchen vessel (like a *kadai*) to make reduced milk (*khoya*) for sweets.

- If you use a **thin-bottomed pan**, milk boils quickly but the bottom *burns easily* → this is **low U, poor heat flow, fouling problem**.
- If you use a **wide, shallow pan**, more surface area is exposed → **more capacity**.
- If you keep the **flame low and steady**, milk does not scorch and reduces uniformly → **stable operation, good economy**.
- If you add a **pressure cooker lid with a small hole**, the boiling temperature drops inside → **vacuum evaporation, ΔT increases**.
- Stirring the milk continuously prevents the dense layer at the bottom from overheating → **forced circulation evaporator**.

Thus, the **factors affecting evaporator performance** are essentially: how fast heat can get *through* the surface, how *much* surface is available, and how *clean* and *active* the surface remains during operation.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Latent heat of vaporization of water | $\lambda$ | **2257 kJ/kg** at 100 °C |
| Standard gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Atmospheric pressure (reference) | $P_{atm}$ | **101.325 kPa** |
| Steam enthalpy (saturated) | $\lambda_s$ | ~2100–2200 kJ/kg |
| Steam economy range | $E$ | **0.8 to 1.5** (single-effect) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Performance-Governing Factors

The performance of an evaporator depends on a triad of intertwined factor groups:

### 🔹 Group A — Operating & Thermodynamic Factors
1. **Temperature Difference (ΔT)**
   - $\Delta T = T_s - T_{boiling}$
   - Higher ΔT increases the driving force for heat transfer.
2. **Boiling Point Elevation (BPE)**
   - Dissolved solutes raise the solution's boiling point above the pure solvent's.
   - Reduces the effective ΔT.
3. **Hydrostatic Head Effect**
   - Liquid column inside tubes raises the local boiling point at the bottom.
4. **Operating Pressure (Vacuum / Pressure)**
   - Reducing pressure lowers $T_{boiling}$, increasing ΔT.
5. **Feed Temperature (Cold / Hot Feed)**

### 🔹 Group B — Heat Transfer Factors
6. **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)**
   - Depends on tube material, fouling, liquid velocity, and boiling regime.
7. **Fouling / Scaling**
   - Deposition of solids on heating surface → ↑ resistance → ↓ U.
8. **Nature of Boiling** (nucleate vs. film boiling).

### 🔹 Group C — Design / Mechanical Factors
9. **Area of Heat Transfer Surface (A)**
10. **Type of Evaporator** (Natural/Forced Circulation, Long-tube, Falling film, Agitated film).
11. **Liquid Level / Hold-up**
12. **Number of Effects** (Single vs. Multiple Effect).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat transfer rate (fundamental) | **W** or **kW** |
| 2 | $\Delta T = T_s - T_b - BPE - \text{Head loss}$ | Net driving force | **K** or **°C** |
| 3 | $Q = m_s(\lambda_s)$ | Heat from steam condensing | **kW** |
| 4 | $Q = m_w \cdot \lambda_w$ | Heat used for water evaporation | **kW** |
| 5 | $\text{Capacity} = \dfrac{m_w}{t}$ | Evaporation rate | **kg/h** |
| 6 | $\text{Economy}(E) = \dfrac{m_w}{m_s}$ | kg water / kg steam | **dimensionless** |
| 7 | $U = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{x}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h_o} + R_f}$ | Overall HTC with fouling | **W/m²K** |
| 8 | $\dfrac{1}{U_D} = \dfrac{1}{h_b} + \dfrac{x}{k_w} + \dfrac{R_f}{1} + \dfrac{1}{h_c}$ | Duhring's combined resistance form | **W/m²K** |
| 9 | $\Delta T_{eff} = \Delta T_{total} - \Sigma(BPE) - \Sigma(\text{Hydrostatic head})$ | Effective ΔT after losses | **K** |
| 10 | $T_{boiling}^{true} = T_{sat}(P) + BPE$ | True boiling point in solution | **°C** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Substitution Rule:** Always use `\vert` instead of the pipe `|` character when writing absolute values inside a markdown table, otherwise the row breaks. Example: write $\vert x \vert$, not $|x|$.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Evaporators are the **workhorses of the process industry**:

| Industry | Application |
|---|---|
| Sugar | Concentrating cane juice from ~14% to ~70% solids |
| Dairy | Milk concentration for powder/condensed milk |
| Paper & Pulp | Black liquor recovery |
| Desalination | Seawater → freshwater |
| Caustic Soda | Concentration of NaOH liquor |
| Pharmaceuticals | API crystallization feed prep |

In each case, **higher U, higher ΔT, and lower fouling** translate directly into **lower operating cost**, **smaller capital investment**, and **higher product quality**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Basic Heat Transfer Equation of an Evaporator

**Assumptions:**
- Steady-state operation.
- Negligible heat losses to surroundings.
- Constant latent heat of vaporization.
- Single solvent (water) is evaporated.

**Step 1 — Heat released by condensing steam:**
The steam condenses on the shell side at temperature $T_s$, releasing its latent heat $\lambda_s$.

$$
Q_{\text{steam}} = m_s \cdot \lambda_s
$$

**Step 2 — Heat gained by the boiling solution:**
The water that evaporates from the solution absorbs latent heat $\lambda_w$ at the boiling temperature $T_b$.

$$
Q_{\text{evap}} = m_w \cdot \lambda_w
$$

**Step 3 — Energy balance (steady state, no losses):**

$$
m_s \cdot \lambda_s = m_w \cdot \lambda_w
$$

**Step 4 — Heat transferred through the heating surface:**
By Fourier's conduction law + Newton's cooling law:

$$
Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T
$$

where $\Delta T = T_s - T_b$.

**Step 5 — Final governing equation:**

$$
\boxed{Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = m_s \cdot \lambda_s = m_w \cdot \lambda_w}
$$

This single equation is the **foundation of all evaporator design**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Steam Economy in a Multiple-Effect Evaporator

**Setup:** $N$ effects in series. Each effect receives steam (only the first effect) and the vapor produced becomes the heating medium for the next.

**Mass balance across effects:**
If $m_w$ is total water evaporated and is *roughly uniformly distributed* (ideal case):

$$
m_w = N \cdot m_{w,i}
$$

**Steam supplied:** $m_s$ enters only the first effect.

**Steam Economy:**

$$
E = \frac{m_w}{m_s}
$$

For ideal operation: $E_{ideal} \approx N$.

**Correction for unequal loading:** With unequal distribution $m_{w,i}$ across effects:

$$
E = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_{w,i}}{m_s}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Effect of BPE and Hydrostatic Head on Effective ΔT

For a real evaporator:

$$
T_{\text{boiling,solution}} = T_{\text{sat}}(P) + BPE + \Delta T_{\text{hydro}}
$$

Effective driving force in the $i$-th effect:

$$
\Delta T_{eff,i} = \Delta T_{total,i} - BPE_i - \Delta T_{hydro,i}
$$

**Total ΔT available** (between steam of first effect and vapor of last effect):

$$
\Sigma \Delta T_{total} = T_{s,1} - T_{v,N}
$$

**Distribution of ΔT across $N$ effects** (equal area assumption):

$$
\Delta T_{eff,i} = \frac{\Sigma \Delta T_{eff}}{N}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Capacity Relation with U, A, ΔT

Starting from $Q = U A \Delta T_{eff}$ and $Q = m_w \lambda_w$:

$$
m_w \cdot \lambda_w = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{eff}
$$

Solving for capacity:

$$
\boxed{m_w = \frac{U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{eff}}{\lambda_w}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Capacity is *directly proportional* to U, A, and ΔT. Therefore, **doubling the heat-transfer area OR the effective temperature difference doubles the capacity**, all else being equal. This is the single most-tested relationship in KTU Module 3.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A single-effect evaporator concentrates 10,000 kg/h of a solution from 10% to 50% solids. Saturated steam at 121 °C ($\lambda_s = 2200$ kJ/kg) is used. The solution boils at 105 °C ($\lambda_w = 2250$ kJ/kg). The overall heat-transfer coefficient is $U = 1500$ W/m²K. Calculate **(a)** capacity, **(b)** steam economy, **(c)** steam required, and **(d)** required heating area.

**Step 1 — Mass balance for solids:**

$$
F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L
$$

$$
10{,}000 \times 0.10 = L \times 0.50 \Rightarrow L = 2000 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 2 — Capacity (water evaporated):**

$$
m_w = F - L = 10{,}000 - 2000 = 8000 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to SI units:**

$$
m_w = \frac{8000}{3600} = 2.222 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 4 — ΔT:**

$$
\Delta T = T_s - T_b = 121 - 105 = 16 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 5 — Heat duty:**

$$
Q = m_w \cdot \lambda_w = 2.222 \times 2250 = 5000 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 6 — Steam required (energy balance):**

$$
m_s \cdot \lambda_s = Q \Rightarrow m_s = \frac{5000}{2200} = 2.273 \text{ kg/s} = 8181.8 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 7 — Steam economy:**

$$
E = \frac{m_w}{m_s} = \frac{8000}{8181.8} = 0.978
$$

**Step 8 — Required area:**

$$
A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{5{,}000{,}000}{1500 \times 16} = 208.33 \text{ m}^2
$$

> ✅ **Answers:** Capacity = 8000 kg/h | Steam = 8181.8 kg/h | Economy = 0.978 | Area ≈ 208.33 m².

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Interacting Factors Affecting Evaporator Performance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["EVAPORATOR PERFORMANCE"] --> B["Capacity kg per h"]
    A --> C["Steam Economy"]
    A --> D["Heat Transfer Coeff U"]

    D --> D1["Fouling Resistance"]
    D --> D2["Tube Material k"]
    D --> D3["Boiling Regime h_b"]
    D --> D4["Condensing Film h_c"]
    D --> D5["Liquid Velocity Forced or Natural"]

    B --> B1["Heat Transfer Area A"]
    B --> B2["Effective Delta T"]
    B --> B3["Latent Heat lambda_w"]

    B2 --> B2a["Steam Temp T_s"]
    B2 --> B2b["Boiling Point Elevation BPE"]
    B2 --> B2c["Hydrostatic Head Loss"]
    B2 --> B2d["Operating Pressure"]

    C --> C1["Number of Effects N"]
    C --> C2["Feed Condition Cold or Hot"]
    C --> C3["Vapor Bleed and Condensate Losses"]

    A1["Final Design"] --> P1["Larger A for more capacity"]
    A1 --> P2["Multiple Effects for higher economy"]
    A1 --> P3["Higher U via forced circulation"]
    A1 --> P4["Vacuum operation for higher Delta T"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The block at the top `A` represents the three pillars of performance. Each arrow shows a **causal** factor. Note that **Effective ΔT** is itself diminished by BPE and hydrostatic head — these two terms are *subtracted* from the gross ΔT.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Evaporator Heat Flow Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Steam["Steam In at T_s"] -->|"lambda_s"| SteamFilm["Steam-Side Film h_c"]
    SteamFilm -->|"Fouling R_f"| TubeWall["Tube Wall x by k"]
    TubeWall -->|"Liquid-Side Film h_b"| Solution["Boiling Solution at T_b + BPE + Head"]
    Solution -->|"Vapor V"| VaporOut["Vapor Out at P"]
    Solution -.->|"Un-evaporated liquor"| LiquorOut["Concentrated Liquor Out"]
    Feed["Dilute Feed F xF"] -->|"Preheated"| Solution
```

**Description of each block:**

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| `Steam In` | Provides latent heat at condensing temperature $T_s$ |
| `Steam-Side Film h_c` | Resistance from condensing steam film |
| `Fouling R_f` | Resistance due to scale/deposits |
| `Tube Wall` | Conduction resistance $x/k$ |
| `Liquid-Side Film h_b` | Resistance due to nucleate/film boiling |
| `Boiling Solution` | Site where vaporization occurs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **overall U** in real evaporators is dominated by the *liquid-side boiling resistance* $h_b$ and *fouling* $R_f$. Improving $h_b$ via forced circulation or using **falling-film evaporators** dramatically boosts U.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define steam economy of an evaporator. What is the theoretical maximum economy of a triple-effect evaporator?** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Steam economy is defined as the **ratio of the mass of water evaporated to the mass of steam supplied** to the evaporator.

$$
E = \frac{m_w}{m_s}
$$

For an *ideal* $N$-effect evaporator (no losses, equal loading), each kg of steam evaporates nearly 1 kg of water per effect, so:

$$
E_{ideal} \approx N
$$

For a **triple-effect** evaporator:

$$
\boxed{E_{ideal} \approx 3 \text{ kg water / kg steam}}
$$

In practice, due to BPE, hydrostatic head, and heat losses, $E_{practical} \approx 2.5$ to 2.8.

---

### **Q2. What is Boiling Point Elevation (BPE)? How does it affect evaporator performance?** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**
**Boiling Point Elevation (BPE)** is the increase in the boiling point of a solution above that of the pure solvent at the same pressure, caused by the dissolved solute.

$$
BPE = T_{boil}^{solution} - T_{boil}^{solvent}
$$

It is a colligative property and depends on solute concentration.

**Effect on Performance:**
- **Reduces effective ΔT** by:

$$
\Delta T_{eff} = \Delta T_{total} - BPE
$$

- **Lowers capacity** because lower ΔT means less heat transfer.
- **Reduces steam economy** in multi-effect systems as BPE accumulates.
- Forces **higher steam pressure** to compensate, raising operating cost.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **Long-Tube Vertical (LTV) evaporator** with a **natural circulation** mode. What are its limitations? **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A single-effect evaporator concentrates 5,000 kg/h of NaOH solution from 12% to 40% (by mass). Steam is supplied at 3.5 bar (saturated) and the solution boils at 110 °C. The overall heat-transfer coefficient is $U = 1200$ W/m²K. Compute: **(i)** capacity, **(ii)** heat load, **(iii)** steam consumption, **(iv)** steam economy, and **(v)** heat transfer area. Take $\lambda_s = 2148$ kJ/kg, $\lambda_w = 2232$ kJ/kg. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

#### **(a) Construction, Working & Limitations of LTV Evaporator** `[7 Marks]`

**Construction:**
- A vertical shell-and-tube heat exchanger, 4–10 m long.
- **Steam chest** at the bottom; **calandria** of long tubes (typically 50 mm dia) suspended vertically.
- A **vapor separator** at the top to knock down entrained liquid droplets.
- **Downtake** pipe returns un-evaporated liquor to the bottom (natural circulation loop).

**Working:**
1. Feed enters the bottom (or top, depending on configuration).
2. Steam condenses on the shell side outside the tubes, releasing latent heat.
3. Solution inside the tubes boils → vapor–liquid mixture rises due to density difference.
4. Vapor leaves at the top to condenser; concentrated liquor is withdrawn from the bottom.
5. Natural circulation is driven by the density difference between the rising two-phase column and the descending liquid in the downtake.

**Limitations:**
- Long residence time → not suitable for heat-sensitive materials.
- Low liquid velocity inside tubes → low $h_b$ → low U.
- Tube fouling reduces U over time.
- Cannot handle viscous or foamy liquids efficiently.

---

#### **(b) Numerical Problem Solution** `[7 Marks]`

**Given:**
- $F = 5000$ kg/h, $x_F = 0.12$, $x_L = 0.40$
- $\lambda_s = 2148$ kJ/kg, $\lambda_w = 2232$ kJ/kg
- $T_s$ at 3.5 bar ≈ **138.9 °C**, $T_b = 110$ °C
- $U = 1200$ W/m²K

**Step 1 — Mass of concentrated liquor (L):**

$$
F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L \Rightarrow L = \frac{5000 \times 0.12}{0.40} = 1500 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 2 — Capacity (Water Evaporated):**

$$
m_w = F - L = 5000 - 1500 = 3500 \text{ kg/h} = 0.9722 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 3 — ΔT:**

$$
\Delta T = 138.9 - 110 = 28.9 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 4 — Heat Load (Q):**

$$
Q = m_w \cdot \lambda_w = 0.9722 \times 2232 = 2169.96 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Steam Consumption:**

$$
m_s = \frac{Q}{\lambda_s} = \frac{2169.96}{2148} = 1.0102 \text{ kg/s} = 3636.7 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 6 — Steam Economy:**

$$
E = \frac{m_w}{m_s} = \frac{3500}{3636.7} = 0.9624
$$

**Step 7 — Heat Transfer Area:**

$$
A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{2{,}169{,}960}{1200 \times 28.9} = 62.58 \text{ m}^2
$$
