> ✅ **Final Answers:** Capacity = 3500 kg/h | Q = 2169.96 kW | Steam = 3636.7 kg/h | Economy = 0.962 | A ≈ 62.58 m².

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the various **factors affecting the performance of an evaporator**. How does each factor influence **capacity** and **steam economy**? **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A double-effect evaporator with **forward feed** processes 20,000 kg/h of a feed containing 8% solids to a final concentration of 40% solids. Saturated steam at 2 bar is used in the first effect. Assuming equal water evaporation in each effect and equal heating areas, calculate: **(i)** water evaporated per effect, **(ii)** overall steam economy, and **(iii)** approximate ΔT per effect, given that total ΔT available = 50 K. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

#### **(a) Factors Affecting Performance of an Evaporator** `[7 Marks]`

| Factor | Influence on Capacity | Influence on Economy |
|---|---|---|
| **ΔT** (temperature difference) | ∝ capacity directly | Higher ΔT may demand higher pressure steam → cost trade-off |
| **Overall U** | ∝ capacity directly | Higher U → lower steam area → better economy |
| **Fouling / Scaling** | ↓ U → ↓ capacity | Increases steam consumption per kg water |
| **BPE** | ↓ effective ΔT → ↓ capacity | ↑ steam load per effect |
| **Hydrostatic Head** | ↓ effective ΔT → ↓ capacity | ↓ economy |
| **Number of Effects** | Slight ↓ capacity per effect (lower T) | ↑ economy (≈ N) |
| **Feed Temperature** | Hot feed reduces steam load → ↑ capacity | ↑ economy |
| **Type of Evaporator** | Forced circulation → ↑ U → ↑ capacity | Higher capital but ↑ economy per kg steam |

**Discussion Highlights:**

1. **ΔT:** The dominant driver; any reduction due to BPE, hydrostatic head, or pressure drops directly hits capacity via $m_w = U A \Delta T / \lambda_w$.
2. **Overall U:** Falls with time due to fouling; the major *practical* limitation. Forced-circulation units achieve $U > 3000$ W/m²K, while natural-circulation ones stay near $1000$ W/m²K.
3. **BPE:** Grows with concentration; the last effect of a multi-effect system suffers most.
4. **Hydrostatic Head:** In tall-tube evaporators, the hydrostatic pressure at the tube bottom raises the local boiling point by 2–5 K.
5. **Number of Effects:** Economy ↑, but capacity ↓ marginally due to total ΔT being shared.
6. **Feed Temperature:** A hot feed (above boiling) acts as a *flash* source, reducing steam demand.
7. **Type:** Falling-film and agitated-film evaporators provide the highest U for viscous/heat-sensitive fluids.

---

#### **(b) Double-Effect Evaporator Calculations** `[7 Marks]`

**Given:**
- $F = 20{,}000$ kg/h, $x_F = 0.08$, $x_L = 0.40$
- Total ΔT available = 50 K
- Equal evaporation per effect (assumption)

**Step 1 — Mass of concentrated liquor:**

$$
L = \frac{F \cdot x_F}{x_L} = \frac{20{,}000 \times 0.08}{0.40} = 4000 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 2 — Total water evaporated:**

$$
m_w^{total} = F - L = 20{,}000 - 4000 = 16{,}000 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 3 — Per effect (equal loading):**

$$
m_w^{per\ effect} = \frac{16{,}000}{2} = 8000 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 4 — Steam economy (ideal double effect):**

$$
E = \frac{m_w^{total}}{m_s} \approx 2 \text{ kg water/kg steam}
$$

(For forward feed, with no losses, $m_s \approx m_w^{per\ effect} = 8000$ kg/h)

**Step 5 — ΔT per effect (assuming equal area and ignoring BPE/head):**

$$
\Delta T_{per\ effect} = \frac{50}{2} = 25 \text{ K}
$$

> ✅ **Final Answers:** Per-effect evaporation = 8000 kg/h | Steam Economy ≈ 2 | ΔT per effect = 25 K.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in Module 3 Evaporator Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion** between kg/h and kg/s when using $Q$ in kW. *(2 marks penalty)*.
> 2. **Ignoring BPE & Hydrostatic Head** when asked about *real* ΔT distribution in multi-effect systems. *(2–3 marks penalty)*.
> 3. **Confusing steam economy with capacity** — they are inverse-correlated in multi-effect systems, not identical. *(1–2 marks penalty)*.
> 4. **Not sketching** when asked for a "neat sketch" question — even a basic block diagram earns partial credit. *(up to 3 marks penalty)*.
> 5. **Writing $|x|$** as plain pipes inside the answer sheet table — use $\vert x \vert$ to avoid row breaks. *(No marks lost here, but cleaner scripts are valued)*.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Performance** = **Capacity** + **Economy** + **U**, governed by $Q = U A \Delta T$.
- **Capacity:** $m_w = \dfrac{U A \Delta T_{eff}}{\lambda_w}$.
- **Steam Economy:** $E = \dfrac{m_w}{m_s}$; ideal $E \approx N$ for an $N$-effect system.
- **Effective ΔT** always = (gross ΔT) − (BPE) − (Hydrostatic head loss).
- **Fouling is the #1 enemy** of sustained U; forced circulation is the cure.
- **ΔT, U, A** are the three levers to boost capacity; more effects is the lever to boost economy.
- **LTV evaporator** — most common industrial type; *natural* circulation, long residence.
- **Forced circulation** — for viscous/crystallizing solutions.
- **Falling film** — best for heat-sensitive materials (very low residence time, high U).
- **Forward feed** — preferred when feed is hot; **Backward feed** — preferred when feed is cold and viscous.
- Always check: **latent heat of saturated steam** at the *exact* saturation temperature; tabulate from steam tables, not estimates.
- **BPE grows** with concentration → last effect of multi-effect suffers most.
- **Boiling regime** matters: nucleate boiling gives the highest $h_b$ → the design sweet spot.
- **Operating under vacuum** lowers $T_b$ → increases ΔT → boosts capacity, but costs more in vacuum pump and condenser.

> 🏁 **End of Module 3 Notes — Factors Affecting the Performance of Evaporators.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Liquid Head and Boiling Point Elevation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Effect of Liquid Head and Boiling Point Elevation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Hydrostatic Head (Effect of Liquid Head)

**Formal Definition:**
The **Hydrostatic Head** (also called *Liquid Head Effect*) in an evaporator is the additional pressure exerted by the column of liquid standing above the heating surface (tubes/coils). Because the boiling liquid at the bottom of the tube experiences a pressure greater than the pressure in the free vapor space above it, the liquid at the heating surface must be heated to a temperature **higher** than the saturation temperature corresponding to the vapor space pressure before boiling can commence at the tube wall.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> Students must clearly distinguish between the *actual* boiling temperature at the heating surface and the *secondary steam* temperature leaving the vapor space. These two values differ due to the hydrostatic head.

**Key Numerical Definition:**
For a liquid column of height $h$ (m) and density $\rho_L$ (kg/m³), the hydrostatic head pressure is:

$$P_{hydro} = \rho_L \cdot g \cdot h \quad [Pa]$$

Where **g = 9.81 m/s²** is the acceleration due to gravity.

---

**Conceptual Analogy — The Mountain Climber's Pot:**
Imagine boiling water at the **top of a hill** versus at the **bottom of a deep well**. At the bottom of the well, the column of water above pushes down on the boiling surface. To form a bubble that can rise against this extra "weight" of water, the water at the bottom must be hotter than the water at the surface. This is exactly what happens inside an evaporator tube — the liquid at the heating surface is essentially "boiling at the bottom of a well" of its own liquid. Hence, the true boiling temperature at the wall ($T_w$) is greater than the vapor space saturation temperature ($T_v$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters in industry:** In long-tubed vertical evaporators (e.g., **LTV, Falling Film, Forced Circulation**), the liquid column can be several meters tall, and the hydrostatic head can raise the boiling temperature by **2–5 °C**. Ignoring this effect leads to gross overestimation of heat transfer capacity.

---

### 1.2 Boiling Point Elevation (BPE)

**Formal Definition:**
**Boiling Point Elevation (BPE)** is the increase in the boiling point of a solution relative to the boiling point of the pure solvent (usually water) at the **same absolute pressure**. It arises from the lowering of the vapor pressure of the solvent due to the presence of dissolved, non-volatile solutes (a colligative property governed by **Raoult's Law**).

Mathematically:

$$\text{BPE} = T_{solution} - T_{water} \quad \text{at the same pressure } P$$

Where **T_solution** is the boiling temperature of the solution and **T_water** is the saturation temperature of pure water at the same pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:**
> BPE is **NOT** a constant — it depends on both (i) the **concentration of dissolved solids** and (ii) the **operating pressure**. As pressure decreases, BPE generally increases. This is why multiple-effect evaporators operating at low pressures show pronounced BPE losses.

---

**Conceptual Analogy — Salt Water and Sugar Syrup:**
If you try to boil seawater (or sugar syrup used in jaggery/candy making) in your kitchen, you will notice it takes *longer* to start bubbling vigorously compared to tap water. The dissolved salt/sugar molecules physically block water molecules from escaping into the vapor phase, raising the temperature needed for boiling. The stronger the syrup (more dissolved solids), the harder it is to boil — and the higher the BPE.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Application:** In the manufacture of **caustic soda (NaOH)**, **sugar**, **paper mill black liquor**, and **desalination concentrates**, BPE can be as high as **8–12 °C** in the last effect, severely reducing the effective temperature driving force.

---

### 1.3 Combined Effect on Evaporator Performance

Both effects consume part of the *available* temperature difference ($\Delta T_{total}$) between the heating steam and the secondary vapor. The **Effective ΔT** that actually drives heat transfer is reduced:

$$\Delta T_{effective} = \Delta T_{total} - \text{BPE} - \Delta T_{hydrostatic}$$

This reduction directly lowers the **capacity** (heat transfer rate, Q = U·A·ΔT) of the evaporator.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Does the Hydrostatic Head Matter?

Consider a vertical-tube evaporator with liquid height $h$ above the heating surface:

1. **Vapor Space Pressure:** $P_{vs}$ — measured at the top.
2. **Pressure at Heating Surface:** $P_{hs} = P_{vs} + \rho_L \cdot g \cdot h$
3. **Saturation Temperature at Vapor Space:** $T_{vs} = f(P_{vs})$ — read from steam tables.
4. **Actual Boiling Temperature at Wall:** $T_{hs} = f(P_{hs})$ — also read from steam tables.
5. **Hydrostatic Head Temperature Loss:** $\Delta T_{hh} = T_{hs} - T_{vs}$

The liquid **must** reach $T_{hs}$ before vigorous boiling occurs, even though the secondary vapor leaves at $T_{vs}$. This additional $\Delta T_{hh}$ is "wasted" — it does not contribute to vapor generation.

> [!NOTE]
> In **Falling Film Evaporators**, the liquid film is extremely thin (1–3 mm), so the hydrostatic head is nearly negligible. This is one reason falling-film units are preferred for heat-sensitive materials.

---

### 2.2 Why Does BPE Occur? — Physical Reasoning

For an ideal solution (Raoult's Law):
$$P_{solution} = x_{solvent} \cdot P_{solvent}^{sat}(T)$$

Because $x_{solvent} < 1$ in a solution, the vapor pressure of the solvent above the solution is **lower** than that of pure solvent at the same temperature. To make the solution boil, its temperature must rise until $P_{solution}$ equals the external (system) pressure. Hence the boiling point rises.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Important:** BPE for dilute aqueous solutions can be estimated from **Dühring's Rule**, while for concentrated electrolytes (e.g., NaOH, CaCl₂), empirical correlations or experimental data must be used.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Quantity / Parameter** | **Formula / Expression** | **Units / Notes** |
|:----:|:------------------------|:------------------------|:------------------|
| 1 | Hydrostatic Pressure | $P_{hh} = \rho_L \cdot g \cdot h$ | Pa (or kgf/m²) |
| 2 | Pressure at heating surface | $P_{hs} = P_{vs} + \rho_L \cdot g \cdot h$ | Pa or bar |
| 3 | Hydrostatic Head Temp. Loss | $\Delta T_{hh} = T_{hs} - T_{vs}$ | °C or K |
| 4 | Boiling Point Elevation | $\text{BPE} = T_{soln} - T_{water}$ (at same P) | °C or K |
| 5 | Effective Temperature Difference | $\Delta T_{eff} = \Delta T_{total} - \text{BPE} - \Delta T_{hh}$ | °C or K |
| 6 | Heat Transfer Rate (Capacity) | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{eff}$ | W or kW |
| 7 | Dühring's Rule (linear) | $\dfrac{dT_{soln}}{dT_{water}} = \text{const}$ at fixed concentration | dimensionless slope |
| 8 | Dühring's Two-Point Form | $T_{s2} = T_{s1} + \left(\dfrac{T_{w2} - T_{w1}}{T_{w3} - T_{w1}}\right)(T_{s3} - T_{s1})$ | linear interpolation |
| 9 | BPE dependence on Pressure | $\dfrac{d(\text{BPE})}{dP} < 0$ for most aqueous systems | qualitative |
| 10 | Capacity Reduction Ratio | $\dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{ideal}} = \dfrac{\Delta T_{eff}}{\Delta T_{total}}$ | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always keep track of which **temperature** you are computing. KTU problems typically give *steam temperature*, *vapor space pressure*, *liquid height*, and *solution concentration*. Your job is to compute the **net effective ΔT** before plugging into $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Multiple-Effect Evaporator Design:** The total loss in available ΔT due to BPE + hydrostatic head is the *primary reason* why practical evaporator trains rarely exceed **5 effects** for aqueous NaOH or sugar systems. Each additional effect faces compounded losses.
- **Mechanical Recompression (MVR):** Designers must account for BPE because it determines the compressor discharge temperature required.
- **Seawater Desalination:** BPE of concentrated brine (~6.5 °C at 50,000 ppm) determines the minimum heat input in MED (Multi-Effect Distillation) plants.
- **Process Optimization:** A properly designed *feed preheater* can reclaim some of the "lost" ΔT by bringing the feed close to BPE temperature before it enters the evaporator body.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Hydrostatic Head Temperature Loss ($\Delta T_{hh}$)

**Given:**
- Vapor space absolute pressure: $P_{vs}$ [Pa]
- Height of liquid above heating surface: $h$ [m]
- Liquid density: $\rho_L$ [kg/m³]
- Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81$ [m/s²]

**Step 1:** Compute the hydrostatic pressure exerted by the liquid column.

$$P_{hh} = \rho_L \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 2:** Compute the absolute pressure at the heating surface.

$$P_{hs} = P_{vs} + \rho_L \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 3:** Look up the saturation temperature corresponding to $P_{vs}$ from steam tables.

$$T_{vs} = f_{steam}(P_{vs})$$

**Step 4:** Look up the saturation temperature corresponding to $P_{hs}$ from steam tables.

$$T_{hs} = f_{steam}(P_{hs})$$

**Step 5:** Compute the hydrostatic head temperature loss.

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{hh} = T_{hs} - T_{vs}}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** This $\Delta T_{hh}$ represents additional sensible heat that must be supplied to the liquid at the heating surface before phase change can begin. It does **not** contribute to producing secondary vapor.

---

### 3.2 Application of Dühring's Rule for BPE Estimation

**Dühring's Rule Statement:**
The boiling point of a solution, when plotted against the boiling point of pure water at the **same pressure**, gives a **straight line** for a fixed concentration.

**Mathematical Form:**

Let:
- $T_{w1}$ = boiling point of pure water at pressure $P_1$
- $T_{s1}$ = boiling point of solution at pressure $P_1$
- $T_{w2}$ = boiling point of pure water at pressure $P_2$
- $T_{s2}$ = boiling point of solution at pressure $P_2$ (the unknown)

Then by linearity:

$$\dfrac{T_{s2} - T_{s1}}{T_{w2} - T_{w1}} = \text{constant slope } m$$

Solving for the unknown boiling point:

$$T_{s2} = T_{s1} + m \cdot (T_{w2} - T_{w1})$$

Or, if a *third* reference point $(P_3, T_{w3}, T_{s3})$ is available (cross-checked from data tables):

$$T_{s2} = T_{s1} + \left(\dfrac{T_{w2} - T_{w1}}{T_{w3} - T_{w1}}\right)(T_{s3} - T_{s1})$$

**Derivation from Raoult's Law (qualitative underpinning):**
For an ideal solution at low pressure,
$$P = x_s \cdot P_w^{sat}(T_s)$$
Taking the derivative w.r.t. $T$ on a log scale, we see that $\ln P_w^{sat}(T_s)$ is approximately linear in $T_s$ over modest ranges (Clausius–Clapeyron approximation), which makes $T_s$ a linear function of $T_w$ at constant $x_s$ — the foundation of Dühring's rule.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Board Style)

**Problem Statement (KTU ESE Pattern):**
A single-effect evaporator operates with:
- Heating steam temperature, $T_s = 120$ °C
- Vapor space absolute pressure, $P_{vs} = 0.40$ bar
- Liquid height on heating tubes, $h = 1.5$ m
- Solution (NaOH 20% by mass) BPE at 0.40 bar, $\text{BPE} = 4.2$ °C
- Liquid density, $\rho_L = 1180$ kg/m³
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- Overall heat transfer coefficient, $U = 1800$ W/(m²·K)
- Heat transfer area, $A = 25$ m²

Compute:
(a) Hydrostatic head temperature loss, $\Delta T_{hh}$
(b) Effective ΔT
(c) Actual capacity of the evaporator, $Q$
(d) Capacity if hydrostatic head were neglected

---

**Solution:**

**Part (a): Hydrostatic Head Loss**

Step 1 — Hydrostatic pressure:
$$P_{hh} = \rho_L \cdot g \cdot h = 1180 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 1.5$$
$$P_{hh} = 17{,}363.7 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.1736 \text{ bar}$$

Step 2 — Pressure at heating surface:
$$P_{hs} = P_{vs} + P_{hh} = 0.40 + 0.1736 = 0.5736 \text{ bar}$$

Step 3 — Saturation temperatures from steam tables:
- At $P_{vs} = 0.40$ bar: $T_{vs} = 75.87$ °C
- At $P_{hs} = 0.5736$ bar: $T_{hs} \approx 85.51$ °C

Step 4 — Hydrostatic head loss:
$$\Delta T_{hh} = T_{hs} - T_{vs} = 85.51 - 75.87 = 9.64 \text{ °C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A loss of ~10 °C is huge! This is why forced circulation evaporators are used for viscous, concentrated solutions — they keep the liquid velocity high, reducing the effective hydrostatic head.

---

**Part (b): Effective ΔT**

Total available ΔT:
$$\Delta T_{total} = T_{steam} - T_{vs} = 120 - 75.87 = 44.13 \text{ °C}$$

Effective ΔT:
$$\Delta T_{eff} = \Delta T_{total} - \text{BPE} - \Delta T_{hh}$$
$$\Delta T_{eff} = 44.13 - 4.2 - 9.64$$
$$\Delta T_{eff} = 30.29 \text{ °C}$$

---

**Part (c): Actual Capacity**

$$Q_{actual} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{eff}$$
$$Q_{actual} = 1800 \cdot 25 \cdot 30.29$$
$$\boxed{Q_{actual} \approx 1.363 \times 10^{6} \text{ W} \approx 1363 \text{ kW}}$$

---

**Part (d): Capacity if Hydrostatic Head is Neglected**

$$\Delta T_{eff}^{*} = \Delta T_{total} - \text{BPE} = 44.13 - 4.2 = 39.93 \text{ °C}$$

$$Q_{ideal} = 1800 \cdot 25 \cdot 39.93 \approx 1.797 \times 10^{6} \text{ W} \approx 1797 \text{ kW}$$

**Percentage error in capacity estimation:**
$$\%\text{Error} = \dfrac{Q_{ideal} - Q_{actual}}{Q_{actual}} \times 100 = \dfrac{1797 - 1363}{1363} \times 100$$
$$\%\text{Error} \approx 31.8\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Red Flag:** Ignoring hydrostatic head can lead to a **>30%** overestimation of evaporator capacity. Always account for both BPE and hydrostatic head in design problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Temperature Distribution in a Vertical-Tube Evaporator

The figure below depicts the thermal "staircase" experienced by the heat as it flows from the steam side, through the tube wall, into the boiling liquid, and finally emerges as secondary vapor. Each "step down" represents an irreversibility or temperature loss.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["STEAM CHEST T_s = 120 C"]:::steam --> B["TUBE METAL WALL T_w1"]:::wall
    B --> C["LIQUID AT HEATING SURFACE T_hs = 85.51 C"]:::hotLiquid
    C --> D["BPE LOSS ZONE T_soln = 81.31 C"]:::bpeLoss
    D --> E["VAPOR SPACE T_vs = 75.87 C"]:::vaporSpace
    E --> F["CONDENSER EXIT T_cond"]:::condenser

    G["HYDROSTATIC HEAD LOSS delta_T_hh = 9.64 C"]:::hydroLoss --> C
    H["BOILING POINT ELEVATION BPE = 4.20 C"]:::bpeLabel --> D

    classDef steam fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef wall fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef hotLiquid fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000
    classDef bpeLoss fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#553388,color:#000
    classDef vaporSpace fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef condenser fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5016,color:#000
    classDef hydroLoss fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000
    classDef bpeLabel fill:#d4e1f5,stroke:#2a4d8f,color:#000
```

**How to read this diagram (KTU exam trick):**
- The **temperature drops** progressively from steam ($T_s = 120$ °C) to vapor ($T_{vs} = 75.87$ °C).
- The gap between the tube wall and the actual solution bulk = **Hydrostatic Head Loss** (because liquid at the wall is hotter).
- The gap between the *pure solvent* boiling point and the *solution* boiling point = **BPE**.
- The **Net Useful ΔT** for vapor generation = $\Delta T_{total} - \text{BPE} - \Delta T_{hh}$.

---

### 4.2 Process Flow Showing ΔT Allocation Across an Evaporator Body

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Available delta T_total = 44.13 C"]:::start --> P2["Less Hydrostatic Head Loss 9.64 C"]:::loss1
    P2 --> P3["Less Boiling Point Elevation 4.20 C"]:::loss2
    P3 --> P4["EFFECTIVE delta T = 30.29 C"]:::useful
    P4 --> P5["Q = U x A x delta T_eff"]:::output

    classDef start fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000
    classDef loss1 fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000
    classDef loss2 fill:#fad7ac,stroke:#b46504,color:#000
    classDef useful fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6,color:#000
```

**Engineering Insight:**
Notice how **~31%** of the available temperature driving force is "consumed" by these two losses. This is a hallmark of evaporators handling concentrated, viscous solutions — and explains why evaporator design always starts by carefully quantifying these losses *before* sizing the heat transfer area.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**  
Define **Boiling Point Elevation (BPE)**. How does it affect the effective temperature difference in an evaporator?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Boiling Point Elevation is the difference between the boiling point of a solution and that of the pure solvent (water) at the same pressure. It occurs because dissolved non-volatile solutes lower the vapor pressure of the solvent (Raoult's Law).

$$\text{BPE} = T_{solution} - T_{water} \quad \big\vert_{P = \text{constant}}$$

**Effect on ΔT (1.5 marks):** The total available temperature difference in the evaporator is reduced because part of it is consumed in overcoming the BPE:

$$\Delta T_{effective} = \Delta T_{total} - \text{BPE} - \Delta T_{hh}$$

This reduces the heat transfer rate $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$, thereby lowering the **capacity** of the evaporator. As concentration of solute increases (along the evaporator train), BPE increases progressively.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
Explain the term **"Hydrostatic Head Effect"** in a vertical-tube evaporator. How does it influence the boiling temperature at the heating surface?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Concept (1.5 marks):** The hydrostatic head refers to the pressure exerted by the column of liquid standing above the heating tubes. This adds to the vapor space pressure, so the liquid at the tube wall experiences a pressure greater than the vapor space pressure.

**Effect on Boiling (1.5 marks):** Because boiling occurs at a higher pressure, the saturation temperature at the heating surface ($T_{hs}$) is greater than the vapor space temperature ($T_{vs}$). The difference $\Delta T_{hh} = T_{hs} - T_{vs}$ represents a temperature "loss" that does not produce vapor. The effective ΔT becomes:

$$\Delta T_{effective} = \Delta T_{total} - \text{BPE} - \Delta T_{hh}$$

In long-tube vertical evaporators, $\Delta T_{hh}$ can be as high as 8–10 °C.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice:** Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style, Adapted]*

A single-effect evaporator is used to concentrate a sugar solution from 10% to 50% by mass. The following data are available:

- Heating steam temperature: $T_s = 110$ °C
- Vapor space absolute pressure: $P_{vs} = 0.30$ bar
- Liquid column height above heating tubes: $h = 2.0$ m
- Liquid density (average): $\rho_L = 1250$ kg/m³
- Boiling Point Elevation (BPE) at exit concentration: $\text{BPE} = 3.8$ °C
- Overall heat transfer coefficient: $U = 1400$ W/(m²·K)
- Heat transfer area: $A = 30$ m²
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Calculate the (i) Hydrostatic head pressure, (ii) Hydrostatic head temperature loss, and (iii) Effective temperature difference.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Calculate the actual capacity of the evaporator in kW. Also, determine by what percentage the capacity would be overestimated if both hydrostatic head and BPE were neglected.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a):**

**(i) Hydrostatic head pressure:**
$$P_{hh} = \rho_L \cdot g \cdot h = 1250 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 2.0$$
$$P_{hh} = 24{,}525 \text{ Pa} = 0.24525 \text{ bar}$$

**(ii) Pressure at heating surface:**
$$P_{hs} = P_{vs} + P_{hh} = 0.30 + 0.24525 = 0.54525 \text{ bar}$$

**Saturation temperatures from steam tables:**
- At $P_{vs} = 0.30$ bar: $T_{vs} = 69.10$ °C
- At $P_{hs} = 0.54525$ bar: $T_{hs} \approx 83.20$ °C (interpolated)

**Hydrostatic head temperature loss:**
$$\Delta T_{hh} = T_{hs} - T_{vs} = 83.20 - 69.10 = 14.10 \text{ °C}$$

**(iii) Effective ΔT:**

Total ΔT:
$$\Delta T_{total} = T_s - T_{vs} = 110 - 69.10 = 40.90 \text{ °C}$$

Effective ΔT:
$$\Delta T_{eff} = 40.90 - 3.8 - 14.10 = 23.00 \text{ °C}$$

---

**Part (b):**

**Actual capacity:**
$$Q_{actual} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{eff} = 1400 \cdot 30 \cdot 23.00$$
$$Q_{actual} = 966{,}000 \text{ W} = 966 \text{ kW}$$

**Capacity if both effects are neglected:**
$$\Delta T_{neglected} = \Delta T_{total} = 40.90 \text{ °C}$$
$$Q_{neglected} = 1400 \cdot 30 \cdot 40.90 = 1{,}717{,}800 \text{ W} = 1717.8 \text{ kW}$$

**Percentage overestimation:**
$$\%\text{Overest.} = \dfrac{Q_{neglected} - Q_{actual}}{Q_{actual}} \times 100$$
$$= \dfrac{1717.8 - 966}{966} \times 100 \approx 77.8\%$$

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

A forced-circulation evaporator concentrates a NaOH solution. The following data are given:

- Feed: 15% NaOH by mass, enters at 30 °C
- Product: 45% NaOH by mass
- Steam temperature: $T_s = 135$ °C
- Vapor space absolute pressure: $P_{vs} = 0.20$ bar
- Liquid column height on heating tubes: $h = 1.2$ m
- Liquid density: $\rho_L = 1300$ kg/m³
- BPE of 45% NaOH at 0.20 bar: $\text{BPE} = 6.5$ °C
- Feed rate: $F = 5000$ kg/h
- Latent heat of vaporization (water): $\lambda_v = 2358$ kJ/kg
- Overall heat transfer coefficient: $U = 2000$ W/(m²·K)
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- Steam tables: At 0.20 bar, $T_{sat} = 60.06$ °C; at 0.35 bar, $T_{sat} = 72.69$ °C; at 0.50 bar, $T_{sat} = 81.33$ °C

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Calculate the (i) Hydrostatic head temperature loss using interpolation from the steam table, and (ii) Effective ΔT.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Calculate (i) the rate of water evaporated (using mass balance), and (ii) the required heat transfer area $A$ using the effective ΔT. Take sensible heating of feed up to boiling point into account (assume $C_{p,feed} = 3.6$ kJ/(kg·K)).

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a):**

**(i) Hydrostatic head temperature loss:**

Hydrostatic pressure:
$$P_{hh} = 1300 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 1.2 = 15{,}303.6 \text{ Pa} = 0.1530 \text{ bar}$$

Pressure at heating surface:
$$P_{hs} = 0.20 + 0.1530 = 0.3530 \text{ bar}$$

Interpolating from steam table (between 0.20 bar and 0.50 bar; values given at 0.20, 0.35, 0.50):
- At 0.3530 bar, very close to 0.35 bar: $T_{hs} \approx 72.69$ °C (use 0.35 bar directly since data point provided)
- $T_{vs} = 60.06$ °C

$$\Delta T_{hh} = 72.69 - 60.06 = 12.63 \text{ °C}$$

**(ii) Effective ΔT:**

$$\Delta T_{total} = 135 - 60.06 = 74.94 \text{ °C}$$
$$\Delta T_{eff} = 74.94 - 6.5 - 12.63 = 55.81 \text{ °C}$$

---

**Part (b):**

**(i) Water evaporated (mass balance on solute):**

Let $V$ = kg/h of water evaporated.

$$F \cdot x_F = (F - V) \cdot x_P$$
$$5000 \cdot 0.15 = (5000 - V) \cdot 0.45$$
$$750 = 2250 - 0.45 V$$
$$0.45 V = 1500$$
$$V = 3333.33 \text{ kg/h}$$

**(ii) Required heat transfer area:**

Heat required = sensible heating + latent heat:
$$Q = F \cdot C_{p,feed} \cdot (T_{boil} - T_{feed}) + V \cdot \lambda_v$$

The boiling temperature of the solution = $T_{vs} + \text{BPE} = 60.06 + 6.5 = 66.56$ °C.

$$Q_{sensible} = 5000 \cdot 3.6 \cdot (66.56 - 30) = 5000 \cdot 3.6 \cdot 36.56$$
$$Q_{sensible} = 658{,}080 \text{ kJ/h}$$

Convert to kW: $658{,}080 / 3600 = 182.80$ kW

$$Q_{latent} = 3333.33 \cdot 2358 = 7{,}861{,}994 \text{ kJ/h}$$

Convert to kW: $7{,}861{,}994 / 3600 = 2183.89$ kW

Total:
$$Q_{total} = 182.80 + 2183.89 = 2366.69 \text{ kW} = 2{,}366{,}690 \text{ W}$$

Heat transfer area:
$$A = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{eff}} = \dfrac{2{,}366{,}690}{2000 \cdot 55.81}$$
$$A = \dfrac{2{,}366{,}690}{111{,}620} \approx 21.21 \text{ m}^2$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Many students convert $\Delta T_{hh}$ in °C but $P_{hh}$ in mm Hg or kgf/cm² — leading to a 10× or 9.81× error. Always work in **SI units throughout**.
> 2. **Steam Table Blindness:** Forgetting to look up saturation temperatures at **both** $P_{vs}$ and $P_{hs}$. The hydrostatic head temperature loss is the *difference*, not the saturation temperature itself.
> 3. **BPE Confusion:** Some students add BPE to the steam temperature instead of subtracting it from $\Delta T_{total}$. Remember: BPE *reduces* the effective driving force.
> 4. **Forgetting Sensible Heat:** In Part (b) of Question B, neglecting feed preheating can lead to 7–10% error in area calculation — typically a 1–2 mark deduction.
> 5. **Interpolation Errors:** When $P_{hs}$ falls between steam table entries, *linear interpolation* on temperature (NOT on pressure) is the KTU-expected method.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Effect of Liquid Head & BPE**
>
> ✅ **Hydrostatic Head** = Pressure of liquid column = $\rho_L \cdot g \cdot h$; increases pressure at heating surface → higher boiling temperature there.
>
> ✅ **Hydrostatic Head Loss** = $\Delta T_{hh} = T_{sat}(P_{vs} + P_{hh}) - T_{sat}(P_{vs})$; **always non-negative**.
>
> ✅ **BPE** = $T_{solution} - T_{water}$ at the same pressure; arises from Raoult's Law (vapor pressure lowering).
>
> ✅ **Dühring's Rule** gives a linear relationship between $T_{soln}$ and $T_{water}$ at fixed concentration; use it to estimate BPE at any pressure from one known data point.
>
> ✅ **Effective ΔT** = $\Delta T_{total} - \text{BPE} - \Delta T_{hh} - \Delta T_{losses}$
>
> ✅ **Capacity formula:** $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{eff}$
>
> ✅ **BPE increases** with (i) increasing concentration, (ii) decreasing pressure → most pronounced in the **last effect** of a multiple-effect train.
>
> ✅ **Hydrostatic Head is mitigated** by (i) forced circulation, (ii) thin falling films, (iii) reducing liquid height.
>
> ✅ **Typical magnitudes:** BPE 2–10 °C, Hydrostatic Head Loss 3–15 °C for long-tube evaporators; combined can consume 30–50% of available ΔT.
>
> ✅ **KTU Exam Tip:** When both BPE and hydrostatic head are given/asked, always compute them **separately first**, then subtract from $\Delta T_{total}$. This order is what the KTU answer key expects.
>
> ✅ **Quick sanity check:** $\Delta T_{eff}$ must be **less than** $\Delta T_{total}$ — if your answer violates this, you have a sign error.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Material and Energy Balances for Single Effect Evaporator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Material and Energy Balances for Single Effect Evaporator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Single Effect Evaporator** is a unit operation device in which a volatile solvent (usually water) is vaporized from a non-volatile solute (e.g., NaOH, sugar, salt) using a single heating surface (calandria), and the resulting vapor is removed from a single vapor–liquid separation chamber. The system receives steam on the shell side and the feed solution on the tube side (or vice-versa, depending on type), and the process is governed simultaneously by the **Overall Mass Balance**, the **Component Mass (Solute) Balance**, and the **Steady-State Energy (Enthalpy) Balance**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> The KTU 2024 Scheme specifically requires students to:
> (i) Write the overall and component material balances for a continuous single-effect evaporator.
> (ii) Write and apply the energy balance equation to compute steam consumption.
> (iii) Define **Capacity** and **Economy**, and explain how boiling point elevation (BPE) reduces them.
> (iv) Compute the heat transfer area $A$ using $Q = U A \Delta T$, with $\Delta T$ corrected for BPE and other losses.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine boiling milk in a deep steel pan on a gas stove to make *khoa* (thickened milk for sweets):
- **The pan** is the evaporator body (vapor–liquid separator).
- **The gas flame** = steam (energy input).
- **The milk** = dilute feed solution.
- **The thick paste that remains** = concentrated liquor.
- **The water vapor that rises and escapes** = solvent vapor leaving the system.

A *single effect* means we use **one pan and one heat source**. If we used the steam that escaped from the first pan to heat a *second* pan, that would be a *multiple-effect* evaporator — which is studied later in Module 3.

So in short: **we burn fuel (or use steam) to drive off water, and we want to know exactly how much water leaves, how much steam we need, and how big the pan (heat transfer area) must be.**

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Two performance indices are central to every KTU numerical on evaporators:
> - **Capacity ($C$)**: Rate of solvent (water) vaporized, in **kg of water evaporated per hour**.
> - **Economy ($E$)**: kg of water vaporized per kg of steam supplied. For a single effect, $E$ typically lies between **0.8 and 0.9** for aqueous systems.

The standard numerical conditions assumed (unless otherwise stated in the question) are:
- Saturated steam is used as the heating medium.
- The vapor leaving the evaporator is saturated at chamber pressure.
- Steady-state, no heat loss to surroundings ($Q_{loss} = 0$).
- Heat of vaporization of water at the operating pressure: $\lambda_v$ in **kJ/kg**.
- Latent heat of saturated steam: $\lambda_s$ in **kJ/kg**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 System Description and Stream Definition

Consider a vertical-tube, natural-circulation evaporator operating at steady state.

**Input Streams:**
- $F$ = feed rate, **kg/h**, entering at temperature $T_F$, with mass fraction of solute $x_F$.
- $m_s$ = saturated steam rate, **kg/h**, entering the shell at temperature $T_s$, with latent heat $\lambda_s$.

**Output Streams:**
- $L$ = concentrated liquor (product) rate, **kg/h**, leaving at temperature $T_L = T_b$ (boiling point of solution at chamber pressure), with mass fraction $x_L$.
- $V$ = secondary (solvent) vapor rate, **kg/h**, leaving at temperature $T_v = T_s'$ (saturation temperature at chamber pressure), with latent heat $\lambda_v$.
- Condensate leaving at $T_s$ (saturated liquid) — not counted as a separate balance stream since its enthalpy is part of the steam-side energy accounting.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

**Step 1 — Overall Mass Balance (Conservation of Total Mass):**
Matter cannot be created or destroyed, so what comes in equals what goes out.

**Step 2 — Solute (Component) Mass Balance:**
The solute is non-volatile, so it never leaves with the vapor. Therefore, the entire solute in the feed must appear in the liquor.

**Step 3 — Energy (Enthalpy) Balance:**
First Law of Thermodynamics: rate of enthalpy in = rate of enthalpy out. We account for the enthalpy carried in by steam and feed, the enthalpy carried out by vapor, liquor, and condensate.

**Step 4 — Heat Transfer Rate Equation:**
The rate of heat transferred through the heating surface is given by Fourier-type conduction $Q = U A \Delta T$. Here $U$ is the overall heat transfer coefficient, $A$ is the heat transfer area, and $\Delta T$ is the *effective* temperature difference corrected for Boiling Point Elevation (BPE) and hydrostatic head.

**Step 5 — Capacity and Economy Definitions:**
Once steam consumption and water-vaporized are known, capacity and economy follow directly.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table. Almost every 14-mark Part B question in Module 3 is solved using these equations.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Overall mass balance | $F = L + V$ | kg/h | Steady-state, no reaction |
| 2 | Solute balance | $F x_F = L x_L$ | kg solute/h | Solute is non-volatile |
| 3 | Solvent (vapor) rate | $V = F \left(1 - \dfrac{x_F}{x_L}\right) = F \left(\dfrac{x_L - x_F}{x_L}\right)$ | kg/h | Direct from balance |
| 4 | Concentrated liquor | $L = \dfrac{F x_F}{x_L}$ | kg/h | Always less than $F$ |
| 5 | Capacity | $C = V$ | kg water/h | Numerical value of vapor rate |
| 6 | Economy | $E = \dfrac{V}{m_s}$ | kg vapor / kg steam | Single effect: 0.8 – 0.9 |
| 7 | Steam rate (cold feed) | $m_s = \dfrac{V \lambda_v + F C_{pF}(T_b - T_F) + Q_{loss}}{\lambda_s}$ | kg/h | Full enthalpy balance |
| 8 | Steam rate (preheated feed at $T_b$) | $m_s = \dfrac{V \lambda_v}{\lambda_s}$ | kg/h | Idealized case |
| 9 | Heat duty | $Q = m_s \lambda_s = U A \Delta T_{eff}$ | W or kJ/h | Couple mass and energy balances |
| 10 | Effective ΔT | $\Delta T_{eff} = T_s - T_s' - \text{BPE} - \text{(friction + static head losses)}$ | K or °C | Use $\Delta T = T_s - T_b$ as compact form |
| 11 | BPE (Dühring form) | $\Delta' = T_b - T_s'$ | K | $T_s'$ = saturation T at chamber pressure |
| 12 | Dühring linear rule | $T_b = a + b\, T'$ | K | $T'$ = water boiling T at same pressure |
| 13 | Economy with cold feed | $E = \dfrac{\lambda_s}{\lambda_v + \dfrac{F}{V} C_{pF}(T_b - T_F)}$ | dimensionless | $< 1$ when feed is cold |
| 14 | Area | $A = \dfrac{Q}{U \,\Delta T_{eff}} = \dfrac{m_s \lambda_s}{U \,\Delta T_{eff}}$ | m² | Final design quantity |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU Notation Rule:** In KTU question papers, $V$ and $W$ are often used interchangeably for the water-vaporized rate, and $D$ (or $S$) is used for steam rate. Always read the stream-notation block at the top of the question before substituting.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sugar industry:** Concentrating cane juice from ~15 % solids to ~70 % syrup before crystallization.
- **Dairy industry:** Milk concentration for transport cost reduction.
- **Caustic soda plants:** NaOH concentration from 10 % to 50 %.
- **Desalination:** Pre-stage for multi-effect distillation (MED) and thermal desalination plants.
- **Pulp & paper:** Black liquor concentration before the recovery boiler.

The **economy** $E$ directly translates to **operating cost** (more steam = more fuel), while the **area** $A$ translates to **capital cost** (more area = more heat exchanger tubes, larger shell). Engineers optimize between these two.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Overall and Component Material Balance

Consider a control volume enclosing the entire evaporator (calandria + vapor body + separator). At steady state:

$$
\left(\dot{m}_{in}\right) = \left(\dot{m}_{out}\right)
$$

**Step 1 — Total Mass In = Total Mass Out:**

Feed $F$ and steam $m_s$ enter; liquor $L$, vapor $V$, and condensate $m_s$ leave. The steam and its condensate cancel on both sides, leaving:

$$
F = L + V
$$

**Step 2 — Solute Mass Balance:** Let $x_F$ and $x_L$ be the mass fractions of the solute in feed and liquor respectively. The vapor $V$ is pure solvent (solute is non-volatile, by definition of evaporation).

$$
F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L + V \cdot 0
$$

$$
\therefore \quad F x_F = L x_L
$$

**Step 3 — Eliminate $L$:** Substitute $L = F - V$ into the solute balance:

$$
F x_F = (F - V) x_L
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $V$:**

$$
F x_F = F x_L - V x_L
$$

$$
V x_L = F x_L - F x_F = F (x_L - x_F)
$$

$$
\therefore \quad V = F \left( \frac{x_L - x_F}{x_L} \right) = F \left( 1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L} \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $L$:**

$$
L = F - V = F - F \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right) = \frac{F x_F}{x_L}
$$

These are the **three foundational material-balance equations** of evaporator analysis.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Steady-State Energy (Enthalpy) Balance

Choose a reference state of liquid water at $0\,°C$. All enthalpies are referenced to this. At steady state:

$$
\sum H_{in} = \sum H_{out}
$$

**Streams Entering:**
- Feed: $F$ kg/h at $T_F$, enthalpy $H_F = F \, C_{pF} (T_F - 0)$ (sensible, liquid).
- Saturated steam: $m_s$ kg/h, enthalpy $H_s = m_s \, \lambda_s$ (latent + saturated liquid reference).

**Streams Leaving:**
- Concentrated liquor: $L$ kg/h at $T_b$, enthalpy $H_L = L \, C_{pL} (T_b - 0)$.
- Secondary vapor: $V$ kg/h, saturated at $T_s'$, enthalpy $H_V = V \, \lambda_v$ (latent only if we use saturated-liquid reference; or $V (\lambda_v + C_{pv} T_s')$ if dry-saturated basis).
- Condensate: $m_s$ kg/h at $T_s$, enthalpy $H_c = m_s \, C_{pw} (T_s - 0)$.

**Energy Balance Equation (full form):**

$$
m_s \lambda_s + F C_{pF} T_F = V \lambda_v + L C_{pL} T_b + m_s C_{pw} T_s + Q_{loss}
$$

**Step 1 — Simplify the steam side:** The enthalpy of saturated steam relative to its own saturated liquid is $\lambda_s$. The enthalpy of the condensate (saturated liquid at $T_s$) is $C_{pw} T_s$. So the *net* enthalpy delivered by the steam is:

$$
m_s (\lambda_s - C_{pw} T_s)
$$

**Step 2 — Adopt the common KTU assumption:** Use saturated liquid at the same temperature as the steam as the reference. Then the steam enthalpy is just $m_s \lambda_s$ and the condensate enthalpy is zero. The energy balance reduces to:

$$
m_s \lambda_s + F C_{pF} T_F = V \lambda_v + L C_{pL} T_b + Q_{loss}
$$

**Step 3 — Special Case 1: Feed enters at boiling point ($T_F = T_b$):** No sensible heat is needed to bring the feed to boiling. The equation simplifies to:

$$
m_s \lambda_s = V \lambda_v + Q_{loss}
$$

If $Q_{loss} = 0$ (idealized case most often used in KTU):

$$
\therefore \quad m_s = \frac{V \lambda_v}{\lambda_s}
$$

**Step 4 — Special Case 2: Cold feed ($T_F < T_b$):** Sensible heat must be supplied to bring the feed to $T_b$. The energy balance becomes:

$$
m_s \lambda_s = V \lambda_v + (F C_{pF} - V C_{pv}) (T_b - T_F) + Q_{loss}
$$

A widely used engineering approximation (valid for dilute aqueous solutions where $C_{pF} \approx C_{pL} \approx C_{pw} = 4.187$ kJ/(kg·K)) is:

$$
m_s \lambda_s \approx V \lambda_v + F C_{pw} (T_b - T_F)
$$

These are the **two energy-balance equations** you must know by heart for KTU.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Capacity and Economy

**Capacity** is defined as the rate at which the solvent is vaporized:

$$
C \equiv V \quad \text{[kg water/h]}
$$

**Economy** is defined as the kg of water vaporized per kg of steam used:

$$
E \equiv \frac{V}{m_s}
$$

Substituting the energy-balance result for the case of feed at boiling point:

$$
E = \frac{V}{V \lambda_v / \lambda_s} = \frac{\lambda_s}{\lambda_v}
$$

For water at moderate pressures, $\lambda_s \approx \lambda_v$, so $E \approx 1$. With cold feed, $E < 1$.

**Effect of BPE on Capacity and Economy:**

When the solution has a boiling point elevation $\Delta' = T_b - T_s'$, the effective ΔT drops:

$$
\Delta T_{eff} = T_s - T_b < T_s - T_s'
$$

For a fixed heat-transfer area $A$ and overall coefficient $U$, the heat duty $Q = U A \Delta T_{eff}$ decreases. Since $Q = V \lambda_v$, the **capacity $V$ drops proportionally**. The steam rate $m_s$ also drops, but not as fast, so **economy can be slightly affected**. The BPE thus **reduces both capacity and effective driving force** — a critical insight that KTU questions often test.

## 3.4 Analytical Solution Matrix — "What to Compute From What"

| Given in the problem | Use this equation first | Then compute |
|---|---|---|
| $F, x_F, x_L$ | $V = F (1 - x_F/x_L)$ | $L = F - V$ |
| $F, V, T_F, T_b$ | $m_s = [V \lambda_v + F C_{pw}(T_b - T_F)] / \lambda_s$ | $E = V/m_s$ |
| $m_s, V$ | $E = V / m_s$ | — |
| $Q, U, \Delta T_{eff}$ | $A = Q / (U \Delta T_{eff})$ | — |
| $T_s$ (steam), $T_s'$ (chamber), $\Delta'$ (BPE) | $\Delta T_{eff} = T_s - T_s' - \Delta'$ | $A = m_s \lambda_s / (U \Delta T_{eff})$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The value of $U$ for a standard vertical-tube natural-circulation evaporator with water-like feed is typically **U ≈ 1000 – 3000 W/(m²·K)**. For viscous or fouling solutions (e.g., sugar juice, NaOH), $U$ may drop to **500 – 1500 W/(m²·K)**. Always read the question carefully — KTU often gives $U$ explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Single Effect Evaporator (Block Flow)

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **functional flow of mass and energy** through a single-effect evaporator. All stream labels are in clean uppercase alphanumeric text (no markdown formatting inside node labels), and every node ID is alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to avoid Mermaid parser conflicts.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INLET["INLET STREAMS"]
        F1["F kg/h FEED xF TF degC"]
        S1["ms kg/h SAT STEAM Ts degC LAMBDA s kJkg"]
    end

    subgraph CORE["EVAPORATOR CORE"]
        EV["CALANDRIA HEAT EXCHANGER Q UA deltaT eff"]
        VB["VAPOR LIQUID SEPARATOR CHAMBER P chamber"]
    end

    subgraph OUTLET["OUTLET STREMS"]
        L1["L kg/h CONC LIQUOR xL Tb degC"]
        V1["V kg/h SEC VAPOR Ts prime degC LAMBDA v kJkg"]
        C1["ms kg/h CONDENSATE Ts degC"]
    end

    F1 --> EV
    S1 --> EV
    EV -- "BOILING MIXTURE" --> VB
    VB --> L1
    VB --> V1
    EV --> C1

    classDef inNode fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef coreNode fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef outNode fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    class F1,S1 inNode
    class EV,VB coreNode
    class L1,V1,C1 outNode
```

## 4.2 Stream Interaction Block Diagram

The diagram below clarifies how the **three balance equations** map to the **three blocks** of the evaporator system — the calandria (heat transfer), the chamber (phase change), and the outlet streams.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BAL["THREE GOVERNING BALANCES"]
        M1["MASS BAL F equals L plus V"]
        M2["SOLUTE BAL F xF equals L xL"]
        E1["ENERGY BAL ms LAMBDA s equals V LAMBDA v plus F Cp dT"]
    end

    subgraph OUT["DESIGN OUTPUTS"]
        O1["CAPACITY V kg water per h"]
        O2["ECONOMY E equals V over ms"]
        O3["AREA A equals Q over U deltaT eff"]
    end

    M1 --> O1
    M2 --> O1
    E1 --> O1
    E1 --> O2
    E1 --> O3

    classDef balNode fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef outNode2 fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    class M1,M2,E1 balNode
    class O1,O2,O3 outNode2
```

## 4.3 Temperature Profile Along the Evaporator

A vertical temperature profile showing the **effective temperature driving force** is essential for understanding why BPE reduces the area. The diagram below represents the temperature drop from steam temperature down to the secondary vapor temperature, with the solution's elevated boiling point marked.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PROF["TEMPERATURE LADDER along evaporator"]
        T1["Ts STEAM TEMP 130 degC"]
        T2["Twall CALANDRIA WALL 125 degC"]
        T3["Tb SOLUTION BOILING 108 degC"]
        T4["Ts prime SEC VAPOR SATN 105 degC"]
    end

    T1 -- "dT1 5 K FILM" --> T2
    T2 -- "dT2 17 K WALL plus NON VOLATILE" --> T3
    T3 -- "BPE DELTA prime 3 K" --> T4

    classDef tNode fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    class T1,T2,T3,T4 tNode
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- $\Delta T_{total} = T_s - T_s' = 130 - 105 = 25$ K (the *gross* available driving force).
- $\Delta' = T_b - T_s' = 108 - 105 = 3$ K (Boiling Point Elevation — the *lost* driving force due to the solute).
- $\Delta T_{eff} = T_s - T_b = 130 - 108 = 22$ K (the *net* driving force actually available for heat transfer).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** BPE consumes a portion of the gross ΔT that cannot be used for evaporation. For a 50 % NaOH solution, BPE can exceed 40 K, dramatically reducing the effective ΔT and forcing the design of a much larger heat transfer area.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
> *Define the terms (i) **Capacity** and (ii) **Economy** of an evaporator. For a single-effect evaporator operating with cold feed, write down the energy balance equation and show how economy is obtained from it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Capacity:** The capacity of an evaporator is defined as the **number of kilograms of water (solvent) vaporized per hour**, i.e., $C = V$ in kg/h. (1 mark)
- **Economy:** Economy is the **kg of water vaporized per kg of steam supplied**, i.e., $E = V / m_s$. It is dimensionless. (1 mark)
- **Energy balance (cold feed):** $m_s \lambda_s = V \lambda_v + F C_{pF} (T_b - T_F)$. Dividing both sides by $m_s \lambda_v$ and rearranging gives:

$$
E = \frac{V}{m_s} = \frac{\lambda_s}{\lambda_v + (F/V)\, C_{pF} (T_b - T_F)}
$$

(1 mark)

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *What is **Boiling Point Elevation (BPE)**? Explain with a neat sketch how it reduces the effective temperature difference available for heat transfer in a single-effect evaporator.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **BPE Definition:** Boiling Point Elevation is the difference between the boiling point of the solution and the boiling point of pure water at the same pressure. It arises because the dissolved (non-volatile) solute lowers the vapor pressure of the solvent, requiring a higher temperature to boil. (1 mark)
- **Effective ΔT reduction:** In a single-effect evaporator, the total temperature difference is $T_s - T_s'$ (steam temperature minus secondary vapor saturation temperature). Because the solution boils at $T_b = T_s' + \Delta'$ (where $\Delta'$ is the BPE), the effective ΔT available for heat transfer is:

$$
\Delta T_{eff} = T_s - T_b = T_s - T_s' - \Delta'
$$

(2 marks for the expression and the explanation that the BPE consumes a part of the gross driving force.)

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part B questions in KTU 2024 ESE offer internal choice. You must attempt **one** of the two alternatives. Each Part B question is worth 14 marks and is split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Modified Model Paper]**
> A single-effect evaporator is used to concentrate a **feed of 10,000 kg/h of a 10 % (by mass) NaOH solution to 50 % (by mass)**. Saturated steam is supplied at **3.0 bar abs** (steam temperature $T_s = 133.5\,°C$, $\lambda_s = 2164$ kJ/kg) and the evaporator operates at **atmospheric pressure** (chamber saturation temperature $T_s' = 100\,°C$). The feed enters at **30 °C**. The boiling point elevation of 50 % NaOH at 1 atm is **Δ' = 8 K**. The overall heat transfer coefficient is **U = 1500 W/(m²·K)**. Assume the specific heat of the dilute feed $C_{pF} = 3.77$ kJ/(kg·K).
>
> **(a)** Calculate the **rate of water evaporated**, the **concentrated liquor rate**, and the **steam consumption rate**. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Calculate the **capacity**, **economy**, and the **required heat transfer area**. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Material balance to find $V$ and $L$:**
Given: $F = 10{,}000$ kg/h, $x_F = 0.10$, $x_L = 0.50$.

$$
V = F \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right) = 10000 \left(1 - \frac{0.10}{0.50}\right) = 10000 \times 0.80 = 8000 \text{ kg/h}
$$

(2 marks)

$$
L = F - V = 10000 - 8000 = 2000 \text{ kg/h}
$$

(1 mark)

**Step 2 — Boiling point of solution:**
$T_b = T_s' + \Delta' = 100 + 8 = 108\,°C$ (1 mark)

**Step 3 — Energy balance to find $m_s$:**
$\lambda_v$ at 1 atm (100 °C) = 2257 kJ/kg.

$$
m_s = \frac{V \lambda_v + F C_{pF} (T_b - T_F)}{\lambda_s}
$$

$$
m_s = \frac{8000 \times 2257 + 10000 \times 3.77 \times (108 - 30)}{2164}
$$

$$
m_s = \frac{18{,}056{,}000 + 2{,}940{,}600}{2164} = \frac{20{,}996{,}600}{2164} = 9702.7 \text{ kg/h}
$$

(3 marks)

#### Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Capacity:**
$C = V = 8000$ kg water/h. (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Economy:**

$$
E = \frac{V}{m_s} = \frac{8000}{9702.7} = 0.8245
$$

(1 mark)

**Step 3 — Effective temperature difference:**

$$
\Delta T_{eff} = T_s - T_b = 133.5 - 108 = 25.5 \text{ K}
$$

(2 marks)

**Step 4 — Heat transfer area:**

$$
Q = m_s \lambda_s = 9702.7 \times 2164 = 21{,}000{,}644 \text{ kJ/h} = 5{,}833{,}512 \text{ W}
$$

$$
A = \frac{Q}{U \, \Delta T_{eff}} = \frac{5{,}833{,}512}{1500 \times 25.5} = \frac{5{,}833{,}512}{38{,}250} = 152.5 \text{ m}^2
$$

(3 marks)

**Final Answers (Part a):** $V = 8000$ kg/h, $L = 2000$ kg/h, $m_s = 9702.7$ kg/h.
**Final Answers (Part b):** Capacity $= 8000$ kg/h, Economy $= 0.8245$, Area $= 152.5$ m².

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> A single-effect evaporator concentrates **5,000 kg/h of a 5 % (by mass) salt solution to 25 % (by mass)**. The evaporator operates at **0.3 bar abs** (chamber saturation $T_s' = 69.1\,°C$, $\lambda_v = 2334$ kJ/kg) and is heated by **saturated steam at 1.5 bar abs** ($T_s = 111.4\,°C$, $\lambda_s = 2226$ kJ/kg). The feed enters at its **boiling point** under chamber conditions, and BPE is negligible. The overall heat transfer coefficient is **U = 2000 W/(m²·K)**.
>
> **(a)** Calculate the **steam rate**, **capacity**, and **economy** of the evaporator. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Determine the **required heat transfer area**. State **two reasons** why the actual installed area is usually 10–20 % larger than this calculated value. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Material balance:**
$F = 5000$ kg/h, $x_F = 0.05$, $x_L = 0.25$.

$$
V = 5000 \left(1 - \frac{0.05}{0.25}\right) = 5000 \times 0.80 = 4000 \text{ kg/h}
$$

(2 marks)

**Step 2 — Energy balance (feed at boiling point, BPE negligible):**

$$
m_s = \frac{V \lambda_v}{\lambda_s} = \frac{4000 \times 2334}{2226} = \frac{9{,}336{,}000}{2226} = 4193.6 \text{ kg/h}
$$

(3 marks)

**Step 3 — Capacity and Economy:**
Capacity $C = V = 4000$ kg water/h. (1 mark)

$$
E = \frac{V}{m_s} = \frac{4000}{4193.6} = 0.954
$$

(1 mark)

#### Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Effective ΔT (BPE = 0):**

$$
\Delta T_{eff} = T_s - T_b = T_s - T_s' = 111.4 - 69.1 = 42.3 \text{ K}
$$

(2 marks)

**Step 2 — Heat duty:**

$$
Q = m_s \lambda_s = 4193.6 \times 2226 = 9{,}337{,}000 \text{ kJ/h} = 2{,}593{,}600 \text{ W}
$$

(2 marks)

**Step 3 — Heat transfer area:**

$$
A = \frac{Q}{U \, \Delta T_{eff}} = \frac{2{,}593{,}600}{2000 \times 42.3} = \frac{2{,}593{,}600}{84{,}600} = 30.66 \text{ m}^2
$$

(1 mark)

**Step 4 — Reasons for design oversizing (10–20 %):**
1. **Fouling and scaling** on the heat transfer surface reduces the actual $U$ over time. The installed area is oversized to compensate.
2. **Heat losses to surroundings** ($Q_{loss} > 0$) require additional area for the same evaporation duty.
3. **Startup and turndown operation** — the evaporator must perform at loads lower than design; extra area provides flexibility.
(2 marks — any two reasons accepted.)

**Final Answers (Part a):** $m_s = 4193.6$ kg/h, Capacity $= 4000$ kg/h, Economy $= 0.954$.
**Final Answers (Part b):** $A = 30.66$ m²; reasons: fouling/scaling, heat losses, turndown flexibility.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losses:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch (–2 marks):** Mixing kJ/h with W (watts). Always convert: $1$ W $= 1$ J/s $= 3.6$ kJ/h. Or convert W to kJ/h by multiplying by 3.6.
> 2. **Forgetting BPE in ΔT (–2 marks):** If BPE is given in the data, you *must* subtract it from the gross ΔT. Many students write $\Delta T = T_s - T_s'$ instead of $\Delta T = T_s - T_b$.
> 3. **Wrong denominator in $E$:** $E = V/m_s$ (not $m_s/V$). This is a 1-mark slip.
> 4. **Using $C_p$ of water everywhere:** For NaOH or sugar solutions, $C_{pF}$ is significantly lower than 4.187 kJ/(kg·K). Use the value given in the problem.
> 5. **Not converting latent heat units:** Some problems give $\lambda$ in kcal/kg. Convert: $1$ kcal/kg $= 4.187$ kJ/kg.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Card — Material and Energy Balances for Single Effect Evaporator**

- **Three governing equations (always):**
  - $F = L + V$ (overall mass)
  - $F x_F = L x_L$ (solute mass)
  - $m_s \lambda_s = V \lambda_v + F C_{pF}(T_b - T_F) + Q_{loss}$ (energy)

- **Vapor rate formula (memorize):**

$$
V = F \left(\frac{x_L - x_F}{x_L}\right)
$$

- **Two key performance indices:**
  - **Capacity** $C = V$ (kg/h)
  - **Economy** $E = V / m_s$ (single effect: 0.8 – 0.9)

- **Effective ΔT rule (BPE correction is mandatory):**

$$
\Delta T_{eff} = T_s - T_s' - \Delta' = T_s - T_b
$$

- **Heat duty couples mass and energy:**

$$
Q = m_s \lambda_s = V \lambda_v = U A \Delta T_{eff}
$$

- **Area formula (final design output):**

$$
A = \frac{m_s \lambda_s}{U \Delta T_{eff}}
$$

- **BPE impact:**
  - BPE ↑ → $\Delta T_{eff}$ ↓ → for fixed $A, U$, $Q$ ↓ → $V$ ↓ → **Capacity drops**.
  - BPE is large for concentrated, ionic solutions (NaOH, sugar) and small for dilute organics.

- **Dühring's rule (empirical BPE):**

$$
T_b = a + b T' \quad \text{where } T' = \text{water b.p. at chamber P}
$$

- **Feeding temperature cases:**
  - **Cold feed** ($T_F < T_b$): full energy balance with sensible-heat term; $E < 1$.
  - **Boiling-point feed** ($T_F = T_b$): $m_s \lambda_s = V \lambda_v$; $E = \lambda_s / \lambda_v \approx 1$.
  - **Superheated feed** ($T_F > T_b$): flash evaporation occurs; $E > 1$ (rarely used in problems).

- **Standard latent-heat values to remember (water):**
  - At 1 atm (100 °C): $\lambda_v = 2257$ kJ/kg
  - At 3 bar (133.5 °C): $\lambda_s = 2164$ kJ/kg
  - At 0.3 bar (69.1 °C): $\lambda_v = 2334$ kJ/kg

- **Unit conversions (always check before substituting):**
  - $1$ kW $= 3600$ kJ/h $= 1$ kJ/s
  - $1$ kcal/kg $= 4.187$ kJ/kg
  - $1$ m² = $10.76$ ft² (if ever required)

- **Typical $U$ values for vertical-tube natural-circulation evaporator:**
  - Dilute aqueous (clean): 1500 – 3000 W/(m²·K)
  - Viscous/fouling: 500 – 1500 W/(m²·K)
  - Forced-circulation: 2000 – 5000 W/(m²·K)

- **Common KTU 14-mark question pattern (MEMORIZE the flow):**
  1. Compute $V$ and $L$ from given $F$, $x_F$, $x_L$.
  2. Compute $T_b$ from given $T_s'$ and BPE.
  3. Apply energy balance to find $m_s$.
  4. Compute $E = V/m_s$ and $C = V$.
  5. Compute $\Delta T_{eff}$.
  6. Compute $A = m_s \lambda_s / (U \Delta T_{eff})$.

- **One-line engineering takeaway:** *A single-effect evaporator trades steam economy (always less than 1) for simplicity. To boost economy, one must go to multiple effects — which is the natural next step in your KTU syllabus.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Numerical Problems of Practical Interest
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Numerical Problems of Practical Interest

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Numerical Problems of Practical Interest** in Heat Transfer Operations refer to the application of fundamental heat transfer principles—**conduction**, **convection**, and **radiation**—to solve real-world industrial and engineering design problems. These problems integrate physical laws (Fourier's Law, Newton's Law of Cooling, Stefan-Boltzmann Law) with practical constraints such as material properties, geometry, boundary conditions, time-dependence (steady vs. unsteady state), and economic factors to determine unknown temperatures, heat duties, surface areas, time of operation, or system efficiencies.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, this topic is positioned as a *capstone application area* in Module 3, where students apply all the theoretical concepts learned in the earlier modules (Modes of Heat Transfer, Conduction Equations, Convective Heat Transfer, Heat Exchangers, Evaporators) to multi-faceted industrial problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Board Examiner's Perspective:**
> Numerical problems in Module 3 are **integrative** — they test whether the student can *identify* the right governing equation, *apply* correct assumptions, and *execute* the mathematics with proper units. Marks are awarded in the ratio **2 (identification) : 3 (calculation) : 2 (interpretation)**.

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Plumber's Toolkit for Heat Flow"**
> Imagine you are a **thermal plumber**. Heat is "water" flowing through "pipes" (materials). Some pipes are thick (insulators), some are thin (conductors). The "pressure" driving heat is the **temperature difference**, and the "flow rate" is the **heat flux**. Practical numerical problems in heat transfer are like diagnosing a complex piping network: you must identify every joint (boundary), measure diameters (thicknesses), know the material (k-value), and calculate the flow rate (Q). Just as a plumber cannot fix leaks without understanding the *whole network*, a chemical engineer cannot solve heat transfer problems without a **holistic view of the system geometry, properties, and operating conditions**.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold Emphasis)

- **Stefan-Boltzmann constant** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}^4$
- **Universal Gas Constant** $R = 8.314 \text{ J/mol}\cdot\text{K}$
- **Standard atmospheric pressure** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$
- **Reference temperature for properties** $T_{ref} = \frac{T_s + T_\infty}{2}$ (**film temperature**)
- **Standard heat transfer units:** W (watts), J (joules), kW (kilowatts), m² (area), K or °C (temperature, interchangeable for *differences*)

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Board Exams:** Always quote the final numerical answer with its **unit and a sensible physical interpretation**. A bare number with no unit is **guaranteed to lose 1 mark** in the valuation.

---

### Categories of Practical Numerical Problems (Module-3 Specific)

The KTU syllabus highlights the following practical problem archetypes:

1. **Steady-state conduction through composite geometries** (plane walls, cylinders, spheres — series and parallel arrangements).
2. **Unsteady-state (transient) heat transfer** with phase change (e.g., freezing of food, solidification of metals, ice formation).
3. **Heat transfer with combined modes** (conduction + convection + radiation acting simultaneously, e.g., furnace walls, insulated steam pipes).
4. **Heat Exchanger design and rating problems** (LMTD method, $\varepsilon$–NTU method, correction factor $F$).
5. **Evaporator calculations** (single-effect, multiple-effect, capacity, economy, steam economy).
6. **Fin analysis** (fin efficiency, fin effectiveness, optimum fin dimensions).
7. **Radiation network problems** between multiple surfaces (view factors, radiosity, electrical analogy).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Logic Flow — How to Approach a Practical Heat Transfer Problem

A KTU Board examiner expects a student to follow this structured thought process:

**Step 1: System Identification**
- Classify the problem as *steady* or *unsteady* state.
- Identify the geometry: *plane wall*, *cylindrical*, or *spherical*.

**Step 2: Mode Classification**
- Determine which modes of heat transfer are active: conduction, convection, radiation, or combinations.

**Step 3: Assumption Listing**
- Constant properties ($k$, $\rho$, $c_p$, $h$).
- One-dimensional (1D) heat flow.
- Negligible radiation (or include it if specified).
- Lumped vs. distributed parameter (check Biot number).

**Step 4: Governing Equation Selection**
- Fourier's Law for conduction.
- Newton's Law of Cooling for convection.
- Stefan-Boltzmann Law for radiation.
- Overall Heat Transfer Equation (OHTE) for composite systems.

**Step 5: Mathematical Solution**
- Apply boundary conditions.
- Solve for the unknown variable.

**Step 6: Verification & Interpretation**
- Check orders of magnitude.
- Verify against physical intuition.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Module 3)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **must-memorize** cheat sheet for KTU Board Exams. All formulas are presented in their most commonly tested form.

#### A. Steady-State Conduction (Plane Wall, Cylinder, Sphere)

| Geometry | Heat Transfer Rate $Q$ | Thermal Resistance $R$ |
|----------|------------------------|-------------------------|
| Plane Wall (1 layer) | $Q = \dfrac{kA \Delta T}{L}$ | $R = \dfrac{L}{kA}$ |
| Plane Wall (n layers) | $Q = \dfrac{A \Delta T}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i / k_i}$ | $R_{total} = \sum \dfrac{L_i}{k_i A}$ |
| Hollow Cylinder (1 layer) | $Q = \dfrac{2\pi k L \Delta T}{\ln(r_o/r_i)}$ | $R = \dfrac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{2\pi k L}$ |
| Composite Cylinder (n layers) | $Q = \dfrac{2\pi L \Delta T}{\sum_{i} \dfrac{\ln(r_{i+1}/r_i)}{k_i}}$ | $R = \sum \dfrac{\ln(r_{i+1}/r_i)}{2\pi k L}$ |
| Hollow Sphere (1 layer) | $Q = \dfrac{4\pi k \Delta T}{\dfrac{1}{r_i} - \dfrac{1}{r_o}}$ | $R = \dfrac{(1/r_i - 1/r_o)}{4\pi k}$ |

#### B. Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (OHTC) and Combined Convection–Conduction

$$ \frac{1}{U_o A_o} = \frac{1}{h_o A_o} + \frac{L}{k A_{lm}} + \frac{1}{h_i A_i} $$

where $A_{lm}$ for a cylinder is the **log-mean area**:

$$ A_{lm} = \frac{A_o - A_i}{\ln(A_o / A_i)} = 2\pi L \cdot \frac{r_o - r_i}{\ln(r_o / r_i)} $$

#### C. Critical Radius of Insulation (Cylindrical)

$$ r_{cr} = \frac{k}{h_o} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insulation paradox:** Adding insulation to a *small* radius pipe ($r < r_{cr}$) **increases** heat loss. The maximum heat loss occurs at $r = r_{cr}$.

#### D. Unsteady-State Heat Transfer (Transient)

**Biot Number** (lumped system validity):

$$ Bi = \frac{h L_c}{k} $$

where $L_c$ is the **characteristic length** $= V / A_s$.

**Fourier Number** (dimensionless time):

$$ Fo = \frac{\alpha \, t}{L_c^2} $$

**Lumped Capacitance Method** (valid for $Bi \leq 0.1$):

$$ \frac{T - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{hA_s}{\rho V c_p} \, t\right) = \exp(-Bi \cdot Fo) $$

**Time for a body to reach a temperature $T$:**

$$ t = \frac{\rho V c_p}{hA_s} \ln\!\left(\frac{T_i - T_\infty}{T - T_\infty}\right) $$

#### E. Fin Analysis

**Fin Efficiency:**

$$ \eta_f = \frac{Q_{fin}}{Q_{max}} = \frac{\tanh(m L_c)}{m L_c} $$

where $m = \sqrt{\dfrac{h P}{k A_c}}$.

**Fin Effectiveness:**

$$ \varepsilon_f = \frac{Q_{fin}}{Q_{no\,fin}} = \frac{\tanh(m L_c)}{\sqrt{hP/(kA_c)}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{kA_c}{hP}} \;\;(\text{simplified form}) $$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** A fin is justified if $\varepsilon_f \geq 2$. Always report whether a fin's use is "economical" or not.

#### F. Heat Exchanger Calculations

**Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD):**

$$ \Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} $$

**Heat Exchanger Duty (with correction factor $F$):**

$$ Q = U A F \cdot \Delta T_{lm} $$

**Effectiveness ($\varepsilon$) – NTU Method:**

$$ \varepsilon = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max,possible}} = \frac{1 - \exp\!\left[-NTU\,(1 - C_r)\right]}{1 - C_r \exp\!\left[-NTU\,(1 - C_r)\right]} \quad \text{(parallel flow)} $$

$$ \varepsilon = 1 - \exp\!\left[-\frac{NTU\,(1 + C_r) \text{ terms}}{1}\right] \quad \text{(counter-flow, simplified)} $$

**Capacity Ratio:**

$$ C_r = \frac{C_{min}}{C_{max}} $$

**Number of Transfer Units:**

$$ NTU = \frac{U A}{C_{min}} $$

#### G. Evaporator Calculations

| Quantity | Formula |
|----------|---------|
| Capacity | $C = \text{mass of water evaporated per hour (kg/h)}$ |
| Economy | $E = \dfrac{\text{kg water evaporated}}{\text{kg steam consumed}}$ |
| Steam Economy (n-effect) | $E_n = 0.85 \times n$ (approximate) |
| Heat Duty (single effect) | $Q = m_s \lambda_s = m_w \lambda_{latent}$ |
| Material Balance (n-effect, feed forward) | $V_1 + V_2 + \dots + V_n = V_{total}$ |

#### H. Radiation Between Surfaces

**Net Radiation Heat Transfer (two-surface enclosure):**

$$ Q_{12} = \frac{A_1 \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1\right)} $$

**Electrical Network Analogy:**

$$ Q = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{R_{total}} $$

where $R_{total}$ is a series-parallel combination of surface and space resistances (analogous to electrical resistors).

---

### 2.3 Real-World Industrial Utility

These numerical problems form the **backbone of chemical plant design**. For example:
- **Refinery furnace design** combines radiation from flames with conduction through refractory bricks and convection to the process fluid.
- **Cold storage and food processing** rely on unsteady-state heat transfer with phase change (freezing).
- **Power plant condensers** are designed using OHTC and LMTD principles.
- **Multiple-effect evaporators** in sugar, paper, and desalination industries are designed using the economy and capacity equations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations and worked examples are **solved in the exact style expected by KTU Board Examiners** — every step, every substitution, every unit conversion is explicitly shown.

---

### 3.1 DERIVATION: Steady-State Conduction Through a Composite Cylindrical Wall

**Statement:** A steam pipe of inner radius $r_1$ is covered with two layers of insulation. The first layer (thickness $r_2 - r_1$, conductivity $k_2$) is magnesia, and the second (thickness $r_3 - r_2$, conductivity $k_3$) is asbestos. The inner surface is at $T_i$ and the outer surface is at $T_o$. Show that the heat loss per unit length is:

$$ \frac{Q}{L} = \frac{2\pi (T_i - T_o)}{\dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k_2} + \dfrac{\ln(r_3/r_2)}{k_3}} $$

**Step 1 — Apply Fourier's Law in cylindrical coordinates (steady-state, 1D radial):**

$$ Q = -k (2\pi r L) \frac{dT}{dr} $$

Rearranging and separating variables:

$$ \frac{Q \, dr}{2\pi r L} = -k \, dT $$

**Step 2 — Integrate across each layer (each with its own $k$):**

For the first insulation (magnesium) — limits $r_1$ to $r_2$:

$$ \int_{r_1}^{r_2} \frac{Q \, dr}{2\pi r L \, k_2} = -\int_{T_i}^{T_{12}} dT $$

$$ \frac{Q}{2\pi L k_2} \ln\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) = T_i - T_{12} $$

For the second insulation (asbestos) — limits $r_2$ to $r_3$:

$$ \frac{Q}{2\pi L k_3} \ln\!\left(\frac{r_3}{r_2}\right) = T_{12} - T_o $$

**Step 3 — Add the two equations (intermediate temperature $T_{12}$ cancels out):**

$$ \frac{Q}{2\pi L k_2} \ln\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) + \frac{Q}{2\pi L k_3} \ln\!\left(\frac{r_3}{r_2}\right) = T_i - T_o $$

**Step 4 — Factor out $Q$ and solve:**

$$ \frac{Q}{2\pi L} \left[ \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k_2} + \frac{\ln(r_3/r_2)}{k_3} \right] = T_i - T_o $$

Rearranging:

$$ \frac{Q}{L} = \frac{2\pi (T_i - T_o)}{\dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k_2} + \dfrac{\ln(r_3/r_2)}{k_3}} \quad \blacksquare $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The composite cylinder equation is the **direct thermal analog of resistors in series** — each layer contributes a thermal resistance $R_i = \dfrac{\ln(r_{i+1}/r_i)}{2\pi k_i L}$. This allows the engineer to add resistances just like in an electrical circuit.

---

### 3.2 WORKED EXAMPLE 1: Composite Plane Wall with Convection on Both Sides

**Problem:** A furnace wall consists of three layers:
- Fireclay brick: $L_1 = 0.10$ m, $k_1 = 1.05$ W/m·K
- Insulating brick: $L_2 = 0.10$ m, $k_2 = 0.20$ W/m·K
- Red brick: $L_3 = 0.10$ m, $k_3 = 0.70$ W/m·K

The inside air is at $T_{\infty,i} = 1200$ °C with $h_i = 100$ W/m²·K, and the outside air is at $T_{\infty,o} = 30$ °C with $h_o = 20$ W/m²·K. Calculate the heat loss per unit area and the interface temperatures.

**Step 1 — List thermal resistances (per unit area):**

$$ R_i^{conv} = \frac{1}{h_i} = \frac{1}{100} = 0.0100 \;\text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W} $$

$$ R_1^{cond} = \frac{L_1}{k_1} = \frac{0.10}{1.05} = 0.0952 \;\text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W} $$

$$ R_2^{cond} = \frac{L_2}{k_2} = \frac{0.10}{0.20} = 0.5000 \;\text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W} $$

$$ R_3^{cond} = \frac{L_3}{k_3} = \frac{0.10}{0.70} = 0.1429 \;\text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W} $$

$$ R_o^{conv} = \frac{1}{h_o} = \frac{1}{20} = 0.0500 \;\text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W} $$

**Step 2 — Sum the resistances (series circuit):**

$$ R_{total} = 0.0100 + 0.0952 + 0.5000 + 0.1429 + 0.0500 = 0.7981 \;\text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W} $$

**Step 3 — Compute heat flux:**

$$ q = \frac{\Delta T}{R_{total}} = \frac{1200 - 30}{0.7981} = \frac{1170}{0.7981} \approx 1465.9 \;\text{W/m}^2 $$

**Step 4 — Compute interface temperatures by traversing from the inside outward:**

Inner surface temperature:

$$ T_{s,i} = T_{\infty,i} - q R_i^{conv} = 1200 - 1465.9 \times 0.0100 = 1200 - 14.66 = 1185.3 \text{ °C} $$

Fireclay–insulating brick interface:

$$ T_{1,2} = T_{s,i} - q R_1^{cond} = 1185.3 - 1465.9 \times 0.0952 = 1185.3 - 139.5 = 1045.8 \text{ °C} $$

Insulating–red brick interface:

$$ T_{2,3} = T_{1,2} - q R_2^{cond} = 1045.8 - 1465.9 \times 0.5000 = 1045.8 - 732.95 = 312.85 \text{ °C} $$

Red brick–outer surface interface:

$$ T_{3,o} = T_{2,3} - q R_3^{cond} = 312.85 - 1465.9 \times 0.1429 = 312.85 - 209.4 = 103.45 \text{ °C} $$

**Verification:**

$$ T_{\infty,o} = T_{3,o} - q R_o^{conv} = 103.45 - 1465.9 \times 0.0500 = 103.45 - 73.30 = 30.15 \text{ °C} \;\approx 30 \text{ °C} \;\checkmark $$

> [!TIP]
> **Answer Summary:** $q \approx 1466$ W/m². Note that the largest temperature drop occurs in the **insulating brick** ($q \times R_2^{cond} = 733$ °C), which is the engineering reason for using low-$k$ materials as the principal thermal barrier.

---

### 3.3 WORKED EXAMPLE 2: Unsteady-State Cooling of a Steel Ball (Lumped Analysis)

**Problem:** A steel ball of diameter $D = 5$ cm is heated to a uniform temperature of $T_i = 800$ °C. It is then quenched in a large oil bath at $T_\infty = 100$ °C. The convective heat transfer coefficient is $h = 500$ W/m²·K. Steel properties: $k = 45$ W/m·K, $\rho = 7800$ kg/m³, $c_p = 480$ J/kg·K. Calculate the time required for the ball's center temperature to reach $T = 200$ °C.

**Step 1 — Check Biot Number for lumped capacitance validity:**

Characteristic length:

$$ L_c = \frac{V}{A_s} = \frac{(4/3)\pi r^3}{4\pi r^2} = \frac{r}{3} = \frac{0.025}{3} = 0.00833 \;\text{m} $$

Biot number:

$$ Bi = \frac{h L_c}{k} = \frac{500 \times 0.00833}{45} = \frac{4.167}{45} = 0.0926 $$

Since $Bi < 0.1$, the **lumped capacitance method is valid**.

**Step 2 — Apply the lumped capacitance equation:**

$$ \frac{T - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{hA_s}{\rho V c_p} t\right) $$

Substituting values:

$$ \frac{200 - 100}{800 - 100} = \frac{100}{700} = 0.1429 $$

Note: $\dfrac{hA_s}{\rho V c_p} = \dfrac{h}{\rho c_p L_c} = \dfrac{500}{7800 \times 480 \times 0.00833} = \dfrac{500}{31{,}189} = 0.01603 \;\text{s}^{-1}$

**Step 3 — Solve for $t$:**

$$ 0.1429 = \exp(-0.01603 \, t) $$

$$ \ln(0.1429) = -0.01603 \, t $$

$$ -1.9446 = -0.01603 \, t $$

$$ t = \frac{1.9446}{0.01603} \approx 121.3 \;\text{seconds} \approx 2.02 \;\text{minutes} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The ball's center cools from 800 °C to 200 °C in about 2 minutes. This rapid cooling is characteristic of quenching operations used in metallurgy to produce desired microstructures.

---

### 3.4 WORKED EXAMPLE 3: Counter-Flow Heat Exchanger Using LMTD

**Problem:** A counter-flow double-pipe heat exchanger heats cold oil (CP = 2.0 kW/K) from 30 °C to 80 °C using hot oil (CP = 3.0 kW/K) which cools from 150 °C to 100 °C. The overall heat transfer coefficient is $U = 500$ W/m²·K. Determine: (a) Heat duty, (b) LMTD, (c) Heat transfer area.

**Step (a) — Heat Duty:**

$$ Q = C_{p,cold} (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}) = 2.0 \times (80 - 30) = 100 \;\text{kW} $$

**Verification (energy balance on hot stream):**

$$ Q = C_{p,hot} (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = 3.0 \times (150 - 100) = 150 \;\text{kW} $$

**Discrepancy!** The values differ (100 vs. 150 kW). 

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Examiner Pitfall:** Inconsistent values of $Q$ indicate the problem is either **underspecified** (missing data) or the **ratings must be reconciled**. In a real exam, the student should *state the inconsistency* and proceed with the *energy balance from the more accurate stream* (often the one with no phase change). For demonstration, we will use the **arithmetic mean** $Q = (100+150)/2 = 125$ kW as a textbook exercise, but note the discrepancy explicitly.

**Step (b) — Log Mean Temperature Difference:**

For counter-flow, the temperature differences at the two ends are:

$$ \Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 150 - 80 = 70 \;\text{°C} $$

$$ \Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 100 - 30 = 70 \;\text{°C} $$

When $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$, the LMTD equals the AMTD:

$$ \Delta T_{lm} = 70 \;\text{°C} $$

**Step (c) — Heat Transfer Area:**

Using $Q = 125$ kW $= 125{,}000$ W (mean value):

$$ A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} = \frac{125{,}000}{500 \times 70} = \frac{125{,}000}{35{,}000} = 3.57 \;\text{m}^2 $$

---

### 3.5 WORKED EXAMPLE 4: Multiple-Effect Evaporator (Steam Economy)

**Problem:** A triple-effect forward-feed evaporator concentrates 10,000 kg/h of a 10% solution to 50% solids. Each effect has the same heat transfer area. Estimate the steam economy and total steam requirement. (Assume economy ≈ 0.85 × number of effects.)

**Step 1 — Total water to be evaporated:**

Initial solids: $10{,}000 \times 0.10 = 1000$ kg/h.
Final solids are still 1000 kg/h (inlet solids = outlet solids).
Final solution: $1000 / 0.50 = 2000$ kg/h.
