Water evaporated: $V = 10{,}000 - 2000 = 8000$ kg/h.

**Step 2 — Steam Economy:**

$$ E_3 = 0.85 \times 3 = 2.55 \;\frac{\text{kg water evaporated}}{\text{kg steam}} $$

**Step 3 — Steam Required:**

$$ S = \frac{V}{E_3} = \frac{8000}{2.55} \approx 3137 \;\text{kg/h} $$

**Step 4 — Water evaporated per effect (equal area, equal ΔT approximation):**

$$ V_1 \approx V_2 \approx V_3 \approx \frac{8000}{3} \approx 2667 \;\text{kg/h per effect} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Doubling the number of effects (from 1 to 2) almost *doubles* the economy, but the *capital cost* also rises. There is an **optimum** number of effects where the annual operating savings balance the capital cost. For sugar and paper industries, this optimum is usually 4–6 effects.

---

### 3.6 WORKED EXAMPLE 5: Fin Efficiency and Effectiveness

**Problem:** A circular fin of diameter $D = 2.5$ cm and length $L = 5$ cm is attached to a surface at $T_s = 200$ °C. The surrounding air is at $T_\infty = 25$ °C, with $h = 30$ W/m²·K. The fin has $k = 200$ W/m·K. Compute: (a) fin parameter $m$, (b) fin efficiency, (c) fin effectiveness. Is the fin justified?

**Step (a) — Fin parameter $m$:**

Perimeter: $P = \pi D = \pi \times 0.025 = 0.0785$ m.
Cross-sectional area: $A_c = \pi D^2 / 4 = \pi \times (0.025)^2 / 4 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4}$ m².

$$ m = \sqrt{\frac{h P}{k A_c}} = \sqrt{\frac{30 \times 0.0785}{200 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-4}}} = \sqrt{\frac{2.356}{0.0982}} = \sqrt{23.99} = 4.898 \;\text{m}^{-1} $$

**Step (b) — Fin Efficiency (using $L_c = L + D/4$ for correction of insulated tip):**

Corrected length:

$$ L_c = L + \frac{D}{4} = 0.05 + \frac{0.025}{4} = 0.05625 \;\text{m} $$

$$ m L_c = 4.898 \times 0.05625 = 0.2755 $$

Fin efficiency:

$$ \eta_f = \frac{\tanh(m L_c)}{m L_c} = \frac{\tanh(0.2755)}{0.2755} = \frac{0.2688}{0.2755} = 0.9757 \approx 97.6\% $$

**Step (c) — Fin Effectiveness:**

$$ \varepsilon_f = \eta_f \cdot \frac{A_{fin}}{A_c} \cdot \text{... [alternative compact form]} $$

For a long fin ($mL \to \infty$): $\varepsilon_{f,\infty} = \sqrt{\dfrac{kP}{hA_c}}$:

$$ \varepsilon_f = \eta_f \cdot \frac{\tanh(mL_c) \cdot A_{fin} / A_c}{1} $$

**Simpler textbook form:**

$$ \varepsilon_f = \eta_f \times \frac{A_{fin}}{A_c} = 0.976 \times \frac{2 \times (P/2) \times L_c}{A_c} $$

$$ = 0.976 \times \frac{2 \times 0.0393 \times 0.05625}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.976 \times \frac{4.42 \times 10^{-3}}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 8.78 $$

**Interpretation:** Since $\varepsilon_f \approx 8.78 \geq 2$, the fin is **justified** (it transfers about 9× more heat than the bare surface).

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Rule:** A high fin efficiency (97.6%) and a moderate effectiveness (8.8) indicate a *short, thick, highly conductive fin* — typical of copper or aluminum cooling fins in compact heat exchangers.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Flowchart — Problem-Solving Strategy

The following Mermaid diagram presents the **decision-making tree** a student should follow when encountering any practical heat transfer numerical problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read the problem carefully] --> B{Steady or Unsteady State?}
    B -- Steady --> C{Geometry?}
    B -- Unsteady --> D{Check Biot Number}
    C -- Plane Wall --> E[Use plane wall resistance formulas]
    C -- Cylinder --> F[Use cylindrical log-mean area]
    C -- Sphere --> G[Use spherical resistance]
    D -- Bi less than 0.1 --> H[Lumped Capacitance Method]
    D -- Bi greater than 0.1 --> I[Use Heisler Charts or 1D transient solution]
    E --> J{Active Modes?}
    F --> J
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J -- Conduction only --> K[Apply Fourier Law]
    J -- Convection included --> L[Add film resistance 1 over h]
    J -- Radiation included --> M[Add surface and space resistances]
    K --> N[Compute Q, T, or A]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Verify units and physical sense]
    O --> P[Report answer with units and interpretation]
```

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Composite Heat Transfer System (Furnace Wall)

This diagram illustrates the **thermal resistance network** for a typical industrial furnace wall exposed to hot gases on one side and ambient air on the other. The block layout maps the flow of heat from source to sink, with each block representing a thermal resistance.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hot Gas T_infty_i] -- R_conv_i 1 over h_i --> B[Inner Wall Surface T_si]
    B -- R_cond_1 L1 over k1 --> C[Interface 1 T_12]
    C -- R_cond_2 L2 over k2 --> D[Interface 2 T_23]
    D -- R_cond_3 L3 over k3 --> E[Outer Wall Surface T_so]
    E -- R_conv_o 1 over h_o --> F[Ambient Air T_infty_o]
    A2[Side Note: Heat Flux q equals Delta T divided by R_total] -.-> A
```

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Multiple-Effect Evaporator (Triple Effect, Forward Feed)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Steam Supply S kg per h] --> B[Effect 1 Vapor V1]
    B -- Vapor acts as heating medium --> C[Effect 2 Vapor V2]
    C -- Vapor acts as heating medium --> D[Effect 3 Vapor V3]
    B1[Dilute Feed F] --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E[Concentrated Product L3]
    F[Condenser] --> D
    F2[Vacuum Pump] --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** In *forward feed*, the dilute solution flows from **Effect 1 → Effect 2 → Effect 3** in the *same direction* as the heating vapor. In *backward feed*, the flow direction is *reversed* for higher-viscosity feeds (the most concentrated solution contacts the hottest surface to improve heat transfer).

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Lumped System Cooling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hot Solid at T_i] --> B{Check Bi number}
    B -- Bi less than 0.1 --> C[Uniform Temperature Assumption Valid]
    B -- Bi greater than 0.1 --> D[Use Transient Charts or 1D Model]
    C --> E[Apply Exponential Decay Equation]
    E --> F[Compute Time t or Final Temperature T]
    D --> F
    F --> G[Physical Interpretation: Quenching, Annealing, Cooling]
```

---

### 4.5 Block Diagram — Radiation Network (Two-Surface Enclosure)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Surface 1 at T1, Emissivity e1] -- Surface Resistance 1 minus e1 over e1 A1 sigma --> B[Node Blackbody]
    B -- Space Resistance 1 over A1 F12 --> C[Surface 2 at T2, Emissivity e2]
    C -- Surface Resistance 1 minus e2 over e2 A2 sigma --> D[Heat Transfer Q12]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Electrical analogy reminder:** Surface resistance $R_s = \dfrac{1 - \varepsilon}{\varepsilon A \sigma}$, Space resistance $R_{sp} = \dfrac{1}{A_1 F_{12} \sigma}$. The net heat flow equals $\dfrac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{R_{total}}$ — exactly analogous to Ohm's law.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution Reminder (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> - **Part A (Module Level):** 3 marks, no choice, short answer.
> - **Part B (Module End):** 14 marks, **internal choice** between two sub-questions. Each sub-part is 7 marks.
> - **Cognitive Levels Tested (Revised Bloom's Taxonomy):** Apply (Level 3), Analyze (Level 4), Evaluate (Level 5).

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Define the term "critical radius of insulation" for a cylindrical pipe. State its significance.**

**Model Answer:**

The **critical radius of insulation** $r_{cr}$ is the outer radius of insulation at which the heat transfer rate from the insulated cylinder is **maximum**, given by:

$$ r_{cr} = \frac{k_{insulation}}{h_{outside}} $$

**Significance:**
- For a bare pipe with $r_i < r_{cr}$, *adding* insulation actually *increases* the heat loss because the additional surface area $A_o$ dominates the added resistance.
- For $r_i > r_{cr}$, adding insulation always reduces heat loss, as expected.
- The phenomenon is a result of the **competition** between increased surface area and increased conductive resistance, governed by the Biot-like ratio $k/h$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Markers' Comment:** Most students lose 1 mark by omitting the *mathematical* condition. Always write the equation.

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sample]

**A steam pipe is insulated first with magnesium and then with asbestos. Why? Justify the order.**

**Model Answer:**

The **inner layer (high $k$, e.g., magnesium)** provides a smooth, hot surface, accommodates thermal expansion, and reduces the temperature at the outer surface to a manageable value. The **outer layer (low $k$, e.g., asbestos)** is the principal thermal barrier.

In a composite cylinder, the **layer with the lower $k$ should be placed on the outside** (where surface area is larger). This is because the heat loss is proportional to $A_o / R_{total}$, and putting the low-$k$ material on the outside ensures the **maximum temperature gradient** occurs across the *thicker* material with the *largest* area for heat dissipation. This ordering minimizes the total heat loss and protects personnel from burns.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for Numerical Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** (cm → m, kW → W, °C → K for *absolute* temperatures only — differences are fine in °C).
> 2. **Using the wrong $A$ in OHTC** — the area must correspond to the chosen reference (use $A_o$ consistently).
> 3. **Applying LMTD without the correction factor $F$** for multi-pass shell-and-tube exchangers.
> 4. **Confusing LMTD method with $\varepsilon$–NTU method** — they are *not* interchangeable without re-derivation.
> 5. **Forgetting to check Biot number** before applying lumped capacitance.
> 6. **Using $\ln$ when $\log_{10}$ is required** (or vice versa) — LMTD always uses natural logarithm.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** A 5 cm outer diameter pipe carries steam at 200 °C. It is insulated with a 4 cm thick layer of $k = 0.05$ W/m·K insulation, followed by a 2 cm thick layer of $k = 0.10$ W/m·K insulation. The outside convective coefficient is $h = 10$ W/m²·K and ambient air is at 30 °C. The inner surface temperature of the pipe is 198 °C. Calculate the heat loss per metre length of pipe and the temperature at the interface between the two insulation layers.

**(b)** A 2.5 cm diameter steel ball ($\rho = 7800$ kg/m³, $c_p = 480$ J/kg·K, $k = 45$ W/m·K) is initially at 600 °C. It is suddenly plunged into a large bath of oil at 100 °C, where $h = 600$ W/m²·K. Determine the time required for the ball to cool to 200 °C. State the assumption you make and verify it.

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $r_1 = 0.025$ m, $r_2 = 0.025 + 0.04 = 0.065$ m, $r_3 = 0.065 + 0.02 = 0.085$ m.
$T_{s,i} = 198$ °C, $T_\infty = 30$ °C, $h = 10$ W/m²·K.

**Step 1 — Thermal resistances (per metre length, $L = 1$ m):**

$$ R_1 = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k_1 L} = \frac{\ln(0.065/0.025)}{2\pi \times 0.05 \times 1} = \frac{\ln(2.6)}{0.3142} = \frac{0.9555}{0.3142} = 3.041 \;\text{K/W} $$

$$ R_2 = \frac{\ln(r_3/r_2)}{2\pi k_2 L} = \frac{\ln(0.085/0.065)}{2\pi \times 0.10 \times 1} = \frac{\ln(1.3077)}{0.6283} = \frac{0.2683}{0.6283} = 0.427 \;\text{K/W} $$

$$ R_{conv} = \frac{1}{h \cdot 2\pi r_3 L} = \frac{1}{10 \times 2\pi \times 0.085 \times 1} = \frac{1}{5.341} = 0.1872 \;\text{K/W} $$

**Step 2 — Total resistance and heat loss:**

$$ R_{total} = 3.041 + 0.427 + 0.187 = 3.655 \;\text{K/W} $$

$$ \frac{Q}{L} = \frac{T_{s,i} - T_\infty}{R_{total}} = \frac{198 - 30}{3.655} = \frac{168}{3.655} \approx 45.96 \;\text{W/m} $$

**Step 3 — Interface temperature $T_{12}$ (between the two insulation layers):**

Temperature drop across $R_2$ and $R_{conv}$:

$$ \Delta T_{(R_2 + R_{conv})} = \frac{Q}{L} \times (R_2 + R_{conv}) = 45.96 \times (0.427 + 0.187) = 45.96 \times 0.614 = 28.22 \;\text{°C} $$

$$ T_{12} = T_\infty + \Delta T_{(R_2 + R_{conv})} = 30 + 28.22 = 58.22 \;\text{°C} $$

**Verification:** Temperature drop across $R_1$:

$$ \Delta T_{R_1} = 45.96 \times 3.041 = 139.78 \;\text{°C} $$

$$ T_{s,i} = T_{12} + \Delta T_{R_1} = 58.22 + 139.78 = 198.0 \;\text{°C} \;\checkmark $$

**Answer:** Heat loss $\approx 45.96$ W/m; Interface temperature $T_{12} \approx 58.2$ °C.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Check Biot Number for lumped analysis validity:**

Characteristic length: $L_c = r/3 = 0.0125/3 = 0.00417$ m (using $r = 0.0125$ m).

$$ Bi = \frac{h L_c}{k} = \frac{600 \times 0.00417}{45} = \frac{2.50}{45} = 0.0556 $$

Since $Bi = 0.0556 < 0.1$, the **lumped capacitance method is valid**. ✓

**Step 2 — Time constant:**

$$ \tau = \frac{\rho V c_p}{h A_s} = \frac{\rho c_p L_c}{h} = \frac{7800 \times 480 \times 0.00417}{600} = \frac{15{,}604}{600} = 26.01 \;\text{s} $$

**Step 3 — Time to reach 200 °C:**

$$ \frac{T - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{t}{\tau}\right) $$

$$ \frac{200 - 100}{600 - 100} = \frac{100}{500} = 0.20 $$

$$ \ln(0.20) = -\frac{t}{26.01} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; -1.6094 = -\frac{t}{26.01} $$

$$ t = 26.01 \times 1.6094 \approx 41.86 \;\text{seconds} \approx 0.70 \;\text{minutes} $$

**Answer:** Time $\approx 41.9$ s (about 42 seconds). The lumped assumption is valid because $Bi < 0.1$.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** A counter-flow double-pipe heat exchanger is used to cool 5000 kg/h of an oil (specific heat = 2.5 kJ/kg·K) from 150 °C to 50 °C using water that enters at 25 °C and leaves at 70 °C. The overall heat transfer coefficient based on the outer tube area is 600 W/m²·K. Calculate:
1. The mass flow rate of water.
2. The LMTD.
3. The heat transfer area.

**(b)** A fin of rectangular cross-section (1 cm × 5 mm) and length 6 cm is attached to a wall at 250 °C. The fin material has $k = 50$ W/m·K, and the surrounding air is at 30 °C with $h = 25$ W/m²·K. Calculate the rate of heat dissipation per fin, and the fin efficiency. Is the use of the fin justified?

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Heat duty:**

$$ Q = m_{oil} c_{p,oil} \Delta T_{oil} = \frac{5000}{3600} \times 2.5 \times (150 - 50) = 1.389 \times 2.5 \times 100 = 347.2 \;\text{kW} $$

**Step 2 — Mass flow rate of water ($c_{p,water} = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K):**

$$ m_{water} = \frac{Q}{c_{p,water} \Delta T_{water}} = \frac{347.2}{4.18 \times (70 - 25)} = \frac{347.2}{188.1} = 1.846 \;\text{kg/s} = 6644 \;\text{kg/h} $$

**Step 3 — LMTD (counter-flow):**

$$ \Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 150 - 70 = 80 \;\text{°C} $$

$$ \Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 50 - 25 = 25 \;\text{°C} $$

$$ \Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = \frac{80 - 25}{\ln(80/25)} = \frac{55}{\ln(3.2)} = \frac{55}{1.1632} = 47.28 \;\text{°C} $$

**Step 4 — Heat transfer area:**

$$ A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} = \frac{347{,}200}{600 \times 47.28} = \frac{347{,}200}{28{,}370} = 12.24 \;\text{m}^2 $$

**Answer:** $m_{water} = 6644$ kg/h, $\Delta T_{lm} = 47.28$ °C, $A = 12.24$ m².

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $w = 1$ cm $= 0.01$ m, $t = 5$ mm $= 0.005$ m, $L = 6$ cm $= 0.06$ m, $T_s = 250$ °C, $T_\infty = 30$ °C, $h = 25$ W/m²·K, $k = 50$ W/m·K.

**Step 1 — Geometry:**

$$ A_c = w \cdot t = 0.01 \times 0.005 = 5 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{m}^2 $$

$$ P = 2(w + t) = 2(0.01 + 0.005) = 0.030 \;\text{m} $$

**Step 2 — Fin parameter $m$:**

$$ m = \sqrt{\frac{h P}{k A_c}} = \sqrt{\frac{25 \times 0.030}{50 \times 5 \times 10^{-5}}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.75}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}} = \sqrt{300} = 17.32 \;\text{m}^{-1} $$

**Step 3 — Corrected length and $mL_c$:**

$$ L_c = L + \frac{t}{2} = 0.06 + \frac{0.005}{2} = 0.0625 \;\text{m} $$

$$ mL_c = 17.32 \times 0.0625 = 1.0825 $$

**Step 4 — Fin efficiency:**

$$ \eta_f = \frac{\tanh(mL_c)}{mL_c} = \frac{\tanh(1.0825)}{1.0825} = \frac{0.7893}{1.0825} = 0.7292 \approx 72.9\% $$

**Step 5 — Heat dissipation rate:**

Surface area of fin (perimeter × corrected length):

$$ A_{fin} = P \cdot L_c = 0.030 \times 0.0625 = 1.875 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^2 $$

$$ Q_{fin} = \eta_f \cdot h \cdot A_{fin} \cdot (T_s - T_\infty) = 0.729 \times 25 \times 1.875 \times 10^{-3} \times 220 $$

$$ Q_{fin} = 0.729 \times 25 \times 0.001875 \times 220 = 7.52 \;\text{W} $$

**Step 6 — Fin effectiveness and justification:**

$$ \varepsilon_f = \eta_f \cdot \frac{A_{fin}}{A_c} = 0.729 \times \frac{1.875 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.729 \times 37.5 = 27.34 $$

Since $\varepsilon_f = 27.3 \geq 2$, the fin is **highly justified**.

**Answer:** Heat dissipation $\approx 7.52$ W per fin; $\eta_f \approx 72.9\%$; $\varepsilon_f \approx 27.3$ (fin highly justified).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students frequently forget the **tip correction** ($L_c = L + t/2$ for a rectangular fin) and use $L$ directly. This overestimates $mL_c$ and *underestimates* the efficiency. Always include the correction for KTU Board Exams — it shows conceptual understanding.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Flashcard (for KTU Board Exams):**

- [x] **Composite Resistance:** $\dfrac{1}{U A} = \sum \dfrac{L_i}{k_i A_i} + \dfrac{1}{h_i A_i} + \dfrac{1}{h_o A_o}$ — series circuit.
- [x] **LMTD** = $\dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$; when $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$, use $\Delta T$ directly.
- [x] **Correction factor $F$** needed for cross-flow / multi-pass shell-and-tube exchangers — **never** skip it.
- [x] **$\varepsilon$–NTU method** preferred when *outlet temperatures are unknown*; LMTD preferred when *outlet temperatures are known*.
- [x] **Lumped analysis** is valid only if $Bi \leq 0.1$. Always state the assumption explicitly.
- [x] **Critical radius of insulation:** $r_{cr} = k/h_o$. If $r_i < r_{cr}$, insulation *increases* heat loss.
- [x] **Fin efficiency** $\eta_f = \dfrac{\tanh(mL_c)}{mL_c}$ with $mL_c$ using **corrected** length $L_c$.
- [x] **Fin effectiveness** $\varepsilon_f \geq 2 \Rightarrow$ **fin justified** (engineering rule of thumb).
- [x] **Evaporator economy** $E \approx 0.85 \times n$ for $n$ effects (forward feed, aqueous solutions).
- [x] **Radiation:** Use $T$ in **Kelvin** (absolute). Stefan-Boltzmann $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴.
- [x] **Units discipline:** Convert all dimensions to **SI** (m, W, K, s) before computation. Loss of units = automatic 1-mark penalty.
- [x] **Final answer** must include: (i) numerical value, (ii) unit, (iii) one-line physical interpretation.
- [x] **Energy balance check:** Always verify $Q_{hot} = Q_{cold}$ for heat exchangers. A discrepancy indicates a typo or missing data.
- [x] **Order of insulation layers:** Low $k$ on the *outside* (larger area) for cylindrical systems.
- [x] **Order of layers in furnaces:** Highest temperature-resistant material (firebrick) on the *inside*, low-$k$ insulator in the *middle*, structural material on the *outside*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Board Examiner's Mantra for Module 3:**
> *"Read the problem → Identify mode → Choose formula → Substitute with units → Solve → Verify with energy balance → Interpret physically."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiple Effect Evaporators: Material and Energy Balances
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Multiple Effect Evaporators: Material and Energy Balances

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — MULTIPLE EFFECT EVAPORATORS: MATERIAL AND ENERGY BALANCES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> A **Multiple Effect Evaporator (MEE)** is an evaporation system in which the vapor generated in one effect (stage) is used as the heating medium for the next effect, operating at a successively lower pressure. The system thereby reuses the latent heat of vaporization, dramatically improving **steam economy** while consuming the same amount of steam as a single effect to evaporate the same quantity of water.

**Key Operating Principle:** The pressure is progressively reduced from the first effect to the last effect, so that the vapor leaving effect *i* can boil the liquid in effect *(i+1)*, because the saturation temperature of the vapor from effect *i* is higher than the boiling point of the solution in effect *(i+1)*.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Boiling Water Cascade"

Imagine a **series of kettles stacked on a staircase**, where each step is slightly lower than the previous one. The first kettle is heated directly by a gas burner (steam). The steam rising from kettle-1 is channeled to heat kettle-2, the steam from kettle-2 heats kettle-3, and so on. Since each kettle is at a lower pressure (lower step), the temperature is also lower, so the vapor from the upper kettle can boil the water in the lower kettle. The heat energy "cascades" downward, doing useful work at every step. This is exactly what a multiple effect evaporator does — a **single payment of steam, recovered multiple times**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metrics (Highlighted):**
> - **Capacity of an Evaporator:** Total mass of water evaporated per unit time, **kg water/hr**.
> - **Steam Economy:** Mass of water evaporated per unit mass of steam supplied, **kg water/kg steam** (dimensionless).
> - **Steam Consumption:** Mass of steam required per unit time, **kg steam/hr**.
> - **Effect:** One stage (body) of the evaporator.
> - **Number of Effects (n):** Number of stages connected in series.

### Types of Multiple Effect Evaporators (KTU Classification)

1. **Forward Feed** — Feed and steam/vapor flow in the **same direction** (Feed → 1 → 2 → 3).
2. **Backward Feed** — Feed and steam/vapor flow in **opposite directions** (Feed → 3 → 2 → 1).
3. **Parallel Feed** — Fresh feed is supplied **separately to each effect**; common for crystallizing solutions.
4. **Mixed Feed** — A combination of forward and backward feed to optimize the operation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Use Multiple Effects? (The "Why" Behind the Concept)

- In a **single effect evaporator**, the latent heat of the vapor leaving the body is **wasted** (carried away to the condenser).
- In an **n-effect evaporator**, the vapor from effect-1 is reused as the heating medium for effect-2, that vapor from effect-2 for effect-3, and so on.
- **Result:** Roughly **n times more water is evaporated per kg of steam**, although total heat transfer area must increase.
- **Trade-off:** The economy improves with *n*, but the **total ΔT available is fixed**; dividing it among *n* effects means smaller driving force per effect, which **reduces the rate of evaporation per effect** and hence **capacity**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** A student is expected to (i) draw the material balance, (ii) derive the energy balance, (iii) compute the capacity and economy, and (iv) compare feed arrangements.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of Balances

**Step 1 — Overall Total Mass Balance (on the whole evaporator):**

Total input mass = Total output mass.

**Step 2 — Solute (Component) Mass Balance:**

Solute is non-volatile — it neither evaporates nor condenses. The solute entering in the feed must equal the solute leaving in the concentrated liquor.

**Step 3 — Water Balance:**

The difference between the total and the solute balance gives the water evaporated. The water evaporated is the sum of vapors leaving all effects.

**Step 4 — Energy Balance on the First Effect:**

Steam entering carries latent heat → heat transferred to boiling liquid → vapor leaves with latent heat + liquor leaves with sensible heat. By ignoring heat losses and small sensible heat terms, the **latent heat input equals latent heat output**.

**Step 5 — Energy Balance Cascade:**

Since vapor from effect *i* heats effect *(i+1)*, the energy balance for each effect (under ideal assumptions) gives equal latent heat transferred at each stage. This leads to **equal evaporation per effect**.

**Step 6 — Capacity & Economy Definitions:**

Capacity = total water evaporated; Economy = water evaporated per kg steam.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application |
|---|---|
| **Sugar** | Concentration of cane juice from ~15% to 70% solids. |
| **Dairy** | Concentrating milk from 12% to 50% total solids. |
| **Paper & Pulp** | Black liquor concentration in Kraft process. |
| **Caustic Soda** | Concentrating NaOH from electrolytic cell liquor. |
| **Desalination** | Multi-stage flash (related principle). |
| **Pharma** | API crystallization mother liquor concentration. |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Used:** F = feed rate, L = final concentrated liquor rate, W = total water evaporated, S = steam supplied, V_i = vapor from effect i, x_F = feed mass fraction solute, x_L = final liquor mass fraction solute, λ_s = latent heat of steam, λ_i = latent heat of vapor from effect i, n = number of effects, e = economy, C = capacity.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Overall Mass Balance | $F = L + W$ | kg/hr |
| 2 | Solute Balance | $F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L$ | kg solute/hr |
| 3 | Final Liquor Rate | $L = F \cdot \dfrac{x_F}{x_L}$ | kg/hr |
| 4 | Total Water Evaporated | $W = F - L = F \left(1 - \dfrac{x_F}{x_L}\right)$ | kg/hr |
| 5 | Water Balance (alternate) | $W = F(1 - x_F) - L(1 - x_L)$ | kg/hr |
| 6 | Vapor from i-th effect (equal evap.) | $V_i = \dfrac{W}{n}$ | kg/hr |
| 7 | Energy Balance (ideal) | $S \cdot \lambda_s = V_1 \cdot \lambda_1 = V_2 \cdot \lambda_2 = \dots = V_n \cdot \lambda_n$ | kJ/hr |
| 8 | Energy Balance (λ ≈ const.) | $S \approx V_1 \approx V_2 \approx \dots \approx V_n$ | kg/hr |
| 9 | Steam Required (ideal) | $S = \dfrac{W}{n}$ | kg steam/hr |
| 10 | Capacity | $C = W$ | kg water/hr |
| 11 | Steam Economy | $e = \dfrac{W}{S}$ | kg water / kg steam |
| 12 | Economy for *n* effects (ideal) | $e = n$ | dimensionless |
| 13 | Steam Consumption | $S = \dfrac{W}{e} = \dfrac{F(x_L - x_F)}{n \cdot x_L}$ | kg/hr |
| 14 | Area of i-th Effect | $A_i = \dfrac{Q_i}{U_i \cdot \Delta T_i}$ | m² |
| 15 | Total Heat Transfer Area | $A_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i$ | m² |
| 16 | Capacity-Economy Ratio | $C/e = S$ | links the two metrics |
| 17 | Boiling Point Elevation | $\Delta T_{BPE} = T_{solution} - T_{water}$ | °C or K |
| 18 | Available ΔT per effect (approx.) | $\Delta T_i = \dfrac{\Delta T_{total} - \sum \Delta T_{BPE}}{n}$ | °C or K |

> [!WARNING]
> **Use `\vert` for absolute value / division bars in equation notes** (e.g., $\vert x_F \vert$). In the table above, all `|` separators are markdown table pipes, not equation bars.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Overall Material Balance for an *n*-Effect Evaporator

**Given System:** An *n*-effect evaporator with feed *F* (kg/hr) entering effect-1 and final concentrated liquor *L* (kg/hr) leaving effect-*n*. Vapors *V_1, V_2, …, V_n* leave the respective effects and go to either the next effect (as heating medium) or to a condenser.

**Assumption:** Steady-state, no accumulation.

**Step 1:** Write the total mass balance on the entire evaporator system boundary.

$$
\text{Mass In} = \text{Mass Out}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the streams.

$$
F = L + (V_1 + V_2 + \dots + V_n)
$$

**Step 3:** Define the total water evaporated *W*.

$$
W = \sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i = V_1 + V_2 + \dots + V_n
$$

**Step 4:** Therefore, the overall total mass balance is:

$$
F = L + W \quad \text{...(i)}
$$

**Step 5:** Now perform the **solute (solid) balance**. Solute is non-volatile, so it appears only in liquid streams.

$$
F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L \quad \text{...(ii)}
$$

**Step 6:** Solve equation (ii) for *L*.

$$
L = F \cdot \frac{x_F}{x_L}
$$

**Step 7:** Substitute *L* into equation (i) to obtain the total water evaporated *W*.

$$
W = F - L = F - F \cdot \frac{x_F}{x_L}
$$

**Step 8:** Factor out *F*.

$$
W = F \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right)
$$

**Step 9:** Combine numerator over common denominator.

$$
W = F \left(\frac{x_L - x_F}{x_L}\right) \quad \text{...(iii)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Equation (iii) is the **headline KTU result** for total water evaporated in any multiple effect evaporator. It is independent of *n* — depends only on feed concentration, final concentration, and feed rate.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy Balance for an *n*-Effect Evaporator (Ideal Case)

**Assumptions for the Ideal Model:**

1. Steady state.
2. No boiling point elevation (ΔT_BPE = 0).
3. All latent heats are approximately equal: $\lambda_s \approx \lambda_1 \approx \lambda_2 \approx \dots \approx \lambda_n$.
4. Heat losses to surroundings are negligible.
5. Sensible heat of feed and liquor are negligible compared to latent heats.

**Step 1:** Write the energy balance on the **first effect** alone.

$$
\underbrace{S \cdot \lambda_s}_{\text{Steam energy in}} = \underbrace{V_1 \cdot \lambda_1}_{\text{Vapor energy out}}
$$

(The feed enters at its boiling point; sensible heat terms are neglected.)

**Step 2:** For the **second effect**, the heating medium is the vapor *V_1* from effect-1.

$$
V_1 \cdot \lambda_1 = V_2 \cdot \lambda_2
$$

**Step 3:** Similarly, for the **i-th effect**:

$$
V_{i-1} \cdot \lambda_{i-1} = V_i \cdot \lambda_i
$$

**Step 4:** Cascade these equalities from effect-1 to effect-*n*:

$$
S \cdot \lambda_s = V_1 \cdot \lambda_1 = V_2 \cdot \lambda_2 = \dots = V_n \cdot \lambda_n \quad \text{...(iv)}
$$

**Step 5:** Apply the assumption $\lambda_s \approx \lambda_i$ for all *i*. Then equation (iv) reduces to:

$$
S \approx V_1 \approx V_2 \approx \dots \approx V_n
$$

**Step 6:** Since the total water evaporated is the sum of vapors:

$$
W = V_1 + V_2 + \dots + V_n
$$

**Step 7:** If all *V_i* are equal:

$$
W = n \cdot V_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_i = \frac{W}{n}
$$

**Step 8:** Combining with *S* ≈ *V_i*:

$$
S = \frac{W}{n} \quad \text{...(v)}
$$

**Step 9:** Define the **Steam Economy** *e*:

$$
e = \frac{W}{S} = \frac{W}{W/n} = n
$$

**Step 10:** The general result for an *n*-effect evaporator under ideal conditions:

$$
\boxed{\; e = n \quad \text{and} \quad S = \frac{W}{n} \;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The steam economy of an *n*-effect evaporator is ideally equal to the number of effects. In practice (with BPE and heat losses), economy is **less than** *n*, typically 0.7 *n* to 0.9 *n* for well-designed systems.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Capacity and Steam Consumption

**Step 1:** Capacity is the total rate of water evaporation:

$$
C = W = F \left(\frac{x_L - x_F}{x_L}\right)
$$

**Step 2:** Steam consumption using *S = W/n*:

$$
S = \frac{W}{n} = \frac{F}{n} \left(\frac{x_L - x_F}{x_L}\right)
$$

**Step 3:** Steam consumption using economy *e*:

$$
S = \frac{W}{e}
$$

**Step 4:** Combining with the solute balance result:

$$
S = \frac{F(x_L - x_F)}{n \cdot x_L}
$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Problem:** A **triple-effect forward feed evaporator** concentrates a dilute solution from 10% to 50% by weight at a feed rate of 20,000 kg/hr. Assuming equal evaporation in each effect and equal latent heats, compute: (i) total water evaporated per hour, (ii) steam economy, (iii) steam required per hour, (iv) liquor leaving the last effect.

**Given Data Matrix:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Feed rate | F | 20,000 | kg/hr |
| Feed concentration | x_F | 0.10 | mass fraction |
| Final concentration | x_L | 0.50 | mass fraction |
| Number of effects | n | 3 | — |

**Solution Step-by-Step:**

**Part (i) — Water Evaporated:**

$$
W = F \left(\frac{x_L - x_F}{x_L}\right) = 20000 \cdot \left(\frac{0.50 - 0.10}{0.50}\right)
$$

$$
W = 20000 \cdot \left(\frac{0.40}{0.50}\right) = 20000 \cdot 0.80 = 16000 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

**Part (ii) — Steam Economy (ideal):**

$$
e = n = 3 \text{ kg water / kg steam}
$$

**Part (iii) — Steam Required:**

$$
S = \frac{W}{n} = \frac{16000}{3} = 5333.33 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

**Part (iv) — Final Liquor Rate:**

$$
L = F - W = 20000 - 16000 = 4000 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

**Verification using Solute Balance:**

$$
L \cdot x_L = 4000 \cdot 0.50 = 2000 \text{ kg solids/hr}
$$

$$
F \cdot x_F = 20000 \cdot 0.10 = 2000 \text{ kg solids/hr} \;\;\checkmark
$$

| Output | Value |
|---|---|
| Total water evaporated, W | **16,000 kg/hr** |
| Steam economy, e | **3.0** |
| Steam required, S | **5,333.33 kg/hr** |
| Final liquor rate, L | **4,000 kg/hr** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Forward Feed Multiple Effect Evaporator

In **forward feed**, the feed enters the **first (hottest) effect** and flows in the **same direction** as the steam/vapor (i.e., toward lower pressure). Vapors from each effect are used to heat the next effect.

```mermaid
graph LR
    S_IN["Steam S from Boiler"] --> HX1["Steam Chest Effect 1"]
    HX1 --> EF1["Effect 1 Body"]
    F_IN["Feed F at xF"] --> EF1
    EF1 --> V1["Vapor V1 to Effect 2"]
    EF1 --> L1["Liquor L1 to Effect 2"]
    V1 --> HX2["Steam Chest Effect 2"]
    L1 --> EF2["Effect 2 Body"]
    HX2 --> EF2
    EF2 --> V2["Vapor V2 to Effect 3"]
    EF2 --> L2["Liquor L2 to Effect 3"]
    V2 --> HX3["Steam Chest Effect 3"]
    L2 --> EF3["Effect 3 Body"]
    HX3 --> EF3
    EF3 --> V3["Vapor V3 to Condenser"]
    EF3 --> L_OUT["Final Liquor L at xL"]
    V1 -.-> COND1["Partial Condenser"]
    V3 --> COND3["Final Condenser"]
    COND1 --> CW["Cooling Water Out"]
    COND3 --> CW
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The feed *F* enters effect 1, where it is heated by live steam *S*.
- Vapor *V₁* from effect 1 acts as the heating medium for effect 2.
- Liquor *L₁* cascades into effect 2, gets concentrated further, and so on.
- The pressure sequence is: P₁ > P₂ > P₃ (effect 1 is hottest, effect 3 is coldest).
- The temperature sequence is: T₁ > T₂ > T₃.

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Backward Feed Multiple Effect Evaporator

In **backward feed**, the feed enters the **last (coldest) effect** and flows **opposite** to the steam/vapor (i.e., toward higher pressure). Pumps are required between effects to push the liquor against the pressure gradient.

```mermaid
graph LR
    S_IN["Steam S from Boiler"] --> HX1["Steam Chest Effect 1"]
    HX1 --> EF1["Effect 1 Body Hottest"]
    EF1 --> V1["Vapor V1 to Effect 2"]
    EF1 --> L_OUT["Final Liquor L at xL"]
    V1 --> HX2["Steam Chest Effect 2"]
    HX2 --> EF2["Effect 2 Body"]
    EF2 --> V2["Vapor V2 to Effect 3"]
    EF2 --> L2["Liquor L2 to Effect 1 via Pump P1"]
    L2 --> EF1
    V2 --> HX3["Steam Chest Effect 3 Coldest"]
    HX3 --> EF3["Effect 3 Body"]
    EF3 --> V3["Vapor V3 to Condenser"]
    EF3 --> L3["Liquor L3 to Effect 2 via Pump P2"]
    L3 --> EF2
    F_IN["Feed F at xF"] --> EF3
    V3 --> COND3["Final Condenser"]
    COND3 --> CW["Cooling Water Out"]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The cold feed enters effect 3 (lowest T and P).
- Liquor is pumped *against* the pressure gradient (P2 and P1) using pumps P1 and P2.
- Final concentrated liquor leaves effect 1 at the **highest temperature**, so the **viscosity is low** at the most concentrated stage → **better heat transfer coefficient**.

---

### 4.3 Comparison Matrix — Feed Arrangements

| Feature | Forward Feed | Backward Feed | Parallel Feed |
|---|---|---|---|
| Feed & vapor direction | Same | Opposite | Independent to each effect |
| Pumping cost | Low (gravity flow) | High (pumps required) | Low |
| Viscosity at final stage | High (poor HTC) | Low (good HTC) | Moderate |
| Best for | Dilute, non-viscous feeds | Viscous final liquors | Crystallizing solutions |
| Steam economy | Slightly higher | Slightly lower | Similar |
| Thermal degradation | Less (short residence at high T) | More (longer at high T) | Less |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1]** `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]`

**Q1.** Define **steam economy** of an evaporator. State the ideal steam economy of a single, double, and triple effect evaporator.

**Model Answer:**

Steam economy is defined as the **mass of water evaporated per unit mass of steam supplied** to the evaporator. Mathematically,

$$
e = \frac{\text{Mass of water evaporated (kg/hr)}}{\text{Mass of steam supplied (kg/hr)}} = \frac{W}{S}
$$

Under ideal conditions (no heat losses, no boiling point elevation, equal latent heats), the economy of an *n*-effect evaporator is numerically equal to the number of effects:

| Type | Ideal Economy |
|---|---|
| Single Effect | e = 1 |
| Double Effect | e = 2 |
| Triple Effect | e = 3 |

**Mark Distribution:** Definition (1 mark), Formula (1 mark), Values table (1 mark).

---

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q2.** State **any three advantages** of backward feed arrangement over forward feed arrangement in a multiple effect evaporator.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Improved Heat Transfer Coefficient:** The most concentrated liquor (highest viscosity) is in the **coldest effect** in forward feed, leading to poor heat transfer. In backward feed, the most concentrated liquor is in the **hottest effect**, where viscosity is lowest → **higher overall heat transfer coefficient (U)**.

2. **Higher Capacity for Viscous Liquors:** Because of the better U-value, the **capacity of the evaporator is higher** for viscous final liquors in backward feed.

3. **Less Scaling and Fouling:** In backward feed, the dilute feed enters the coldest effect and gets progressively heated; this means lower temperature difference for the dilute solution and reduced tendency for scale formation on heat transfer surfaces in early effects.

4. *(Bonus point)* Suitable for heat-sensitive materials when residence time at high temperature is acceptable.

**Mark Distribution:** 1 mark per advantage (any 3).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Style, Module 3]**

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** A **triple-effect forward feed evaporator** is used to concentrate a solution from **8% to 40% by weight** of solids. The feed rate is **15,000 kg/hr**. Assuming equal evaporation in each effect and equal latent heat of vaporization, calculate:
   1. Total water evaporated per hour.
   2. Steam economy.
   3. Steam required per hour.
   4. Final liquor rate.    **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **multiple effect evaporator**. State the limitations of the forward feed arrangement.    **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a):

**Given:**

- $F = 15000$ kg/hr, $x_F = 0.08$, $x_L = 0.40$, $n = 3$.

**Step 1 — Final Liquor Rate (using solute balance):**

$$
L = F \cdot \frac{x_F}{x_L} = 15000 \cdot \frac{0.08}{0.40} = 15000 \cdot 0.20 = 3000 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Water Evaporated:**

$$
W = F - L = 15000 - 3000 = 12000 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

Alternative check using the standard formula:

$$
W = F \left(\frac{x_L - x_F}{x_L}\right) = 15000 \cdot \left(\frac{0.40 - 0.08}{0.40}\right) = 15000 \cdot 0.80 = 12000 \text{ kg/hr} \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Step 3 — Steam Economy (ideal, equal latent heats):**

$$
e = n = 3 \text{ kg water / kg steam}
$$

**Step 4 — Steam Required:**

$$
S = \frac{W}{n} = \frac{12000}{3} = 4000 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

**Final Answer Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Final liquor rate, L | **3,000 kg/hr** |
| Total water evaporated, W | **12,000 kg/hr** |
| Steam economy, e | **3.0** |
| Steam required, S | **4,000 kg/hr** |

**Mark Split (7 marks):** Part 1 (2), Part 2 (1), Part 3 (2), Part 4 (2).

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b):

**Working of a Multiple Effect Evaporator (MEE):**

A multiple effect evaporator consists of *n* evaporators (bodies/effects) connected in series. The pressure is graded such that the **first effect operates at the highest pressure and temperature**, and the last effect at the **lowest**. Live steam is fed only to the first effect; its latent heat is transferred to the boiling liquid, generating vapor *V₁*. This vapor *V₁* serves as the heating medium for the second effect. The vapor from the second effect, *V₂*, similarly heats the third effect, and so on. The vapor from the final effect is sent to a condenser, and the condensate from each effect is recovered.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    STM["Steam S"] --> E1["Effect 1 High P and T"]
    F["Feed F"] --> E1
    E1 --> V1["Vapor V1"]
    E1 --> L1["Liquor L1"]
    V1 --> E2["Effect 2"]
    L1 --> E2
    E2 --> V2["Vapor V2"]
    E2 --> L2["Liquor L2"]
    V2 --> E3["Effect 3 Low P and T"]
    L2 --> E3
    E3 --> V3["Vapor V3"]
    E3 --> LOUT["Final Liquor L"]
    V3 --> COND["Condenser"]
```

**Limitations of Forward Feed:**

1. **Low HTC in the last effect:** The most viscous liquor is in the coldest effect, giving the lowest U-value exactly where it matters most.
2. **Reduced capacity for viscous liquors** (e.g., concentrated sugar syrups, paper mill black liquor).
3. **Higher tendency for scale formation** in early effects since the highest temperature difference acts on the most concentrated liquor.
4. **Higher pumping costs are avoided (advantage)** but thermal efficiency is lower than backward feed for viscous systems.

**Mark Split (7 marks):** Working explanation (3), Diagram (2), Limitations (2).

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**(a)** Derive the equations for:
   1. Overall material balance
   2. Solute (component) balance
   3. Energy balance (ideal case) for a multiple effect evaporator with *n* effects.    **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **double effect evaporator** is used to concentrate a solution from **5% to 25% solids**. The feed rate is **5,000 kg/hr** at 25 °C. Steam is supplied at **1.5 bar absolute** with latent heat **2,200 kJ/kg**. Assuming equal evaporation in each effect, calculate: (i) Capacity, (ii) Steam economy, (iii) Steam consumption per hour, (iv) Heat transfer area per effect (take U = 1,500 W/m²·K, average ΔT = 30 K per effect).    **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a):

**1. Overall Material Balance:**

For steady-state, total mass in = total mass out.

$$
F = L + W
$$

where $W = \sum V_i$.

**2. Solute (Component) Balance:**

Solute is non-volatile.

$$
F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L
$$

Rearranging:

$$
L = F \cdot \frac{x_F}{x_L}
$$

Therefore:

$$
W = F - L = F \left(\frac{x_L - x_F}{x_L}\right)
$$

**3. Energy Balance (Ideal):**

Neglecting sensible heat effects and heat losses, the latent heat supplied by the steam in the first effect equals the latent heat carried by the vapor leaving it.

$$
S \cdot \lambda_s = V_1 \cdot \lambda_1
$$

This same vapor *V₁* becomes the heating medium for effect 2:

$$
V_1 \cdot \lambda_1 = V_2 \cdot \lambda_2
$$

Cascading through all effects:

$$
S \cdot \lambda_s = V_1 \cdot \lambda_1 = V_2 \cdot \lambda_2 = \dots = V_n \cdot \lambda_n
$$

With the assumption of equal latent heats:

$$
S = V_1 = V_2 = \dots = V_n = \frac{W}{n}
$$

Hence the **steam economy**:

$$
e = \frac{W}{S} = n
$$

**Mark Split (7 marks):** Part 1 (2), Part 2 (2), Part 3 (3).

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b):

**Given:**

- $F = 5000$ kg/hr, $x_F = 0.05$, $x_L = 0.25$, $n = 2$.
- $\lambda_s = 2200$ kJ/kg, $U = 1500$ W/m²·K, $\Delta T = 30$ K per effect.

**Step 1 — Water Evaporated (Capacity):**

$$
W = F \left(\frac{x_L - x_F}{x_L}\right) = 5000 \cdot \left(\frac{0.25 - 0.05}{0.25}\right) = 5000 \cdot 0.80 = 4000 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

So **Capacity C = 4000 kg water/hr**.

**Step 2 — Steam Economy (ideal):**

$$
e = n = 2 \text{ kg water / kg steam}
$$

**Step 3 — Steam Consumption:**

$$
S = \frac{W}{n} = \frac{4000}{2} = 2000 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

**Step 4 — Heat Transfer Area per Effect:**

The heat transferred per effect (using steam rate) is:

$$
Q = S \cdot \lambda_s = 2000 \text{ kg/hr} \cdot 2200 \text{ kJ/kg} = 4{,}400{,}000 \text{ kJ/hr}
$$

Convert to kW: $1 \text{ kJ/hr} = \frac{1}{3600} \text{ kW}$

$$
Q = \frac{4{,}400{,}000}{3600} = 1222.22 \text{ kW} = 1.222 \times 10^6 \text{ W}
$$

Area per effect:

$$
A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{1.222 \times 10^6}{1500 \cdot 30} = \frac{1.222 \times 10^6}{45000} = 27.16 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Final Answer Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Capacity, C | **4,000 kg/hr** |
| Steam economy, e | **2.0** |
| Steam consumption, S | **2,000 kg/hr** |
| Area per effect, A | **27.16 m²** |

**Mark Split (7 marks):** Capacity (2), Economy (1), Steam (1), Area (3).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert kJ/hr → W (or kW):** Many students write $Q = 4.4 \times 10^6$ kJ/hr and plug it directly into $A = Q/(U \cdot \Delta T)$, producing a wrong answer by a factor of **3600**. Always convert heat duty to Watts before dividing by U (in W/m²·K).
> 2. **Mixing up capacity and economy:** Capacity is **W** (kg/hr), economy is **W/S** (dimensionless). Don't interchange them.
> 3. **Writing the solute balance wrong:** Some students write $F = L \cdot x_F + L \cdot x_L$ — this is dimensionally inconsistent. Always use $F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L$.
> 4. **Forgetting to add 7+7 in the 14-mark question:** KTU's 14-mark Part B questions have two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Allocate time and length accordingly.
> 5. **Assuming economy = n always:** This is **only true under ideal conditions** (no BPE, no heat losses). Mention the assumption explicitly in the answer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Overall mass balance:** $F = L + W$.
- [ ] **Solute balance:** $F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L$ (solute is non-volatile).
- [ ] **Water evaporated:** $W = F \left(\dfrac{x_L - x_F}{x_L}\right)$.
- [ ] **Capacity** = total water evaporated per hour (*W* in kg/hr).
- [ ] **Steam economy** *e* = W/S (dimensionless). For *n*-effect ideal: **e = n**.
- [ ] **Steam consumption:** $S = W/n$ (ideal) = $W/e$ (general).
- [ ] **Energy balance (ideal, equal latent heats):** $S \cdot \lambda_s = V_1 \cdot \lambda_1 = \dots = V_n \cdot \lambda_n$.
- [ ] **Forward feed:** feed & vapor → same direction, no pumps, last effect has highest viscosity.
- [ ] **Backward feed:** feed & vapor → opposite direction, pumps required, best for viscous liquors.
- [ ] **Parallel feed:** fresh feed to each effect, ideal for crystallizers.
- [ ] **Mixed feed:** hybrid of forward and backward; used in large industrial plants.
- [ ] **BPE (Boiling Point Elevation):** $\Delta T_{BPE} = T_{solution} - T_{water}$; reduces effective ΔT per effect.
- [ ] **Dühring's rule:** boiling point of solution is a linear function of the boiling point of pure water at the same pressure (used to estimate BPE).
- [ ] **Real economy** is always less than *n* (typically 0.7*n* to 0.9*n*) due to BPE, sensible heat, and heat losses.
- [ ] **Area of each effect:** $A_i = Q_i / (U_i \cdot \Delta T_i)$ — the bottleneck effect is the one with the largest *A_i*.
- [ ] **Capacity-to-economy ratio:** *C/e* gives the steam consumption directly.
- [ ] Always state the assumptions (steady state, no heat loss, equal latent heat) when solving energy balance problems.
- [ ] For KTU 14-mark questions, **split your answer into 7+7 sub-parts** and show **each calculation step** — the valuation key rewards stepwise working.
- [ ] **Final concentrated liquor rate:** $L = F \cdot x_F / x_L$ — use this to cross-check your water evaporated value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module 3 Topic: Multiple Effect Evaporators — Material and Energy Balances.** Students should practice at least 3 numerical problems covering (i) finding *W*, *S*, *e* for given *F*, *x_F*, *x_L*, *n*, and (ii) heat transfer area calculation using $A = Q / (U \cdot \Delta T)$ for a 14-mark KTU-style question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Different Feeding Arrangements in Multiple Effect Evaporators – Their Merits and Demerits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Different Feeding Arrangements in Multiple Effect Evaporators – Their Merits and Demerits # MODULE 3 | TOPIC: FEEDING ARRANGEMENTS IN MULTIPLE EFFECT EVAPORATORS ## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Multiple Effect Evaporator (MEE)** is an energy-efficient evaporation system in which the latent heat of vapor liberated in one effect (evaporator unit) is utilized as the heating medium for the next effect, thereby achieving substantial steam economy. The arrangement by which the **liquid feed (dilute solution)** is introduced into and passed through the successive effects is termed the **Feeding Arrangement**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The feeding arrangement in a multiple effect evaporator refers to the **direction of flow of the feed (liquid to be concentrated) relative to the direction of flow of the heating steam/vapor** through the series of evaporator effects. According to KTU Board Examiners' standard convention, there are **FOUR (4)** principal feeding arrangements: 1. **Forward Feed (Co-current Feed)** 2. **Backward Feed (Counter-current Feed)** 3. **Parallel Feed** 4. **Mixed Feed (Combined Forward and Backward Feed)** --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Student-Friendly Intuition) > [!NOTE] > **The Staircase Analogy for Multiple Effect Evaporators** > > Imagine a staircase with three steps going **downward**: > - **Steam enters at the TOP step (highest pressure)** and as vapor gives up its latent heat, it cools down and moves down. > - **Feed (dilute solution)** is like a person walking on the staircase. > - The **direction in which the person walks** — with the steam, against the steam, or in some mixed path — defines the feeding arrangement. > > 🔹 **Forward Feed** = Person walks **downward, in the same direction** as the steam/vapor. > 🔹 **Backward Feed** = Person walks **upward, against** the direction of steam/vapor. > 🔹 **Parallel Feed** = Each person climbs a separate staircase independently. > 🔹 **Mixed Feed** = Person walks partway down, then back up, then down again. This staircase visualization helps immediately grasp why different feed arrangements exist — they optimize **pressure, temperature, viscosity, heat transfer, and pumping cost** simultaneously. --- ### 1.3 Standard Pressure and Temperature Profile in MEE In any multiple effect evaporator system operating under standard vacuum/pressure cascade: | Effect Number | Pressure | Temperature | Role | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---| | Effect 1 | **Highest** | **Highest** | Steam supply point | | Effect 2 | Intermediate | Intermediate | Vapor from Effect 1 = heating medium | | Effect 3 | **Lowest** | **Lowest** | Vapor from Effect 2 = heating medium; connects to condenser | The **pressure gradient** decreases from Effect 1 → Effect N. This natural pressure gradient is the key physical driver behind the choice of feed arrangement. --- ### 1.4 Quick-Reference Schematic Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Quick Mnemonic — "F-B-P-M"** stands for **F**orward, **B**ackward, **P**arallel, **M**ixed. > These are the ONLY four feeding arrangements you need to remember for KTU 2024 examinations. # 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 ARRANGEMENT 1: FORWARD FEED (CO-CURRENT FEED) ### 2.1.1 Operating Principle In **forward feed**, both the **feed (liquid)** and the **steam/vapor** flow in the **same direction** — from Effect 1 → Effect 2 → Effect 3 → … → Effect N. - The **dilute feed enters Effect 1** (hottest). - The **concentrated liquor leaves Effect N** (coldest). - **Steam enters Effect 1**; its vapor goes to heat Effect 2, and so on. ### 2.1.2 Why Forward Feed Works Without Pumps The **natural pressure gradient** in MEE ensures: - Pressure in Effect 1 (P₁) > Pressure in Effect 2 (P₂) > Pressure in Effect 3 (P₃) - This pressure difference naturally pushes liquid from Effect 1 → 2 → 3. - **No pumps are required** between successive effects — this is the major economic advantage. ### 2.1.3 Step-by-Step Flow Logic 1. **Fresh steam (S)** is admitted to the steam chest of Effect 1. 2. **Dilute feed (F)** at low concentration $x_F$ enters Effect 1. 3. Due to heating, water evaporates → vapor (V₁) rises and flows to the steam chest of Effect 2. 4. Liquid (now at concentration $x_1 > x_F$) flows by pressure drop to Effect 2. 5. The vapor V₁ condenses on the heating tubes of Effect 2, releasing latent heat. 6. Process repeats through Effect 3. 7. **Concentrated liquor (L)** leaves Effect 3 at concentration $x_L$. 8. **Final vapor (V₃)** goes to the **condenser**. ### 2.1.4 Merits of Forward Feed | # | Merit | Engineering Significance | |:---:|:---|:---| | 1 | **No pumps required between effects** | Lower capital cost; simpler operation | | 2 | **Simplest arrangement** | Easy to design, install, operate, and maintain | | 3 | **Suitable for non-viscous, dilute solutions** | Common in sugar refining, salt production | | 4 | **Steam economy** improves with number of effects | $\text{Steam Economy} = \dfrac{W_{\text{total}}}{S}$ | | 5 | **Lowest operating complexity** | Preferred for small to medium plants | ### 2.1.5 Demerits of Forward Feed | # | Demerit | Reason | |:---:|:---|:---| | 1 | **Cold feed meets hottest surface in Effect 1** | Causes **sudden, vigorous boiling** → scale formation | | 2 | **Concentrated (viscous) liquor leaves the COLDEST effect** | High viscosity → poor heat transfer coefficient in last effect | | 3 | **Lowest temperature difference is in last effect** | Reduces overall capacity | | 4 | **Not suitable for heat-sensitive, viscous, or scaling liquids** | Examples: milk, caustic, fruit juices, calcium chloride | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** A common error students make is stating that "forward feed gives the highest heat transfer coefficient in the last effect." This is **WRONG**. The last effect in forward feed handles the MOST viscous liquor at the LOWEST temperature — both factors REDUCE the heat transfer coefficient. --- ## 2.2 ARRANGEMENT 2: BACKWARD FEED (COUNTER-CURRENT FEED) ### 2.2.1 Operating Principle In **backward feed**, the **feed enters the LAST (coldest) effect** and flows **opposite** to the direction of steam/vapor. - Feed enters Effect N (coldest, lowest pressure). - Liquid moves: Effect N → Effect N-1 → … → Effect 1 (hottest). - Steam still enters Effect 1, vapor flows 1 → 2 → 3 → condenser. ### 2.2.2 Why Pumps ARE Required The **feed flows AGAINST the pressure gradient** — i.e., from low pressure to high pressure. Gravity and pressure difference CANNOT drive this flow. Therefore, **pumps must be installed between successive effects** to move the liquid backward against the rising pressure profile. ### 2.2.3 Step-by-Step Flow Logic 1. **Dilute feed (F)** enters Effect N (coldest, lowest pressure). 2. Slight concentration occurs in Effect N. 3. A **pump** transfers the liquid from Effect N → Effect N-1 (higher pressure). 4. The liquid continues getting concentrated as it moves toward Effect 1. 5. **Concentrated liquor (L)** leaves Effect 1 (hottest). 6. The hot, concentrated liquor in Effect 1 has **LOW viscosity** at the highest temperature → excellent heat transfer. ### 2.2.4 Merits of Backward Feed | # | Merit | Engineering Significance | |:---:|:---|:---| | 1 | **Cold feed enters coldest effect → no sudden boiling** | **No scale formation** in initial stages | | 2 | **Concentrated liquor leaves HOTTEST effect (Effect 1)** | Low viscosity → **highest heat transfer coefficient** in Effect 1 | | 3 | **Best suited for viscous, heat-sensitive, scaling liquids** | Example: glycerin, caustic soda, milk products | | 4 | **Better capacity and heat transfer efficiency** | Higher overall $U$ values | | 5 | **Reduced fouling** | Lower maintenance and downtime | ### 2.2.5 Demerits of Backward Feed | # | Demerit | Reason | |:---:|:---|:---| | 1 | **Pumps required between every two effects** | High capital and operating cost | | 2 | **More complex piping and instrumentation** | Higher maintenance | | 3 | **Pump failure can disrupt entire plant** | Reliability concern | | 4 | **Higher energy consumption** for pumping | Increases auxiliary power requirement | | 5 | **Not economical for small plants** | Pump costs outweigh benefits | --- ## 2.3 ARRANGEMENT 3: PARALLEL FEED ### 2.3.1 Operating Principle In **parallel feed**, **fresh feed is supplied SEPARATELY to each effect**, and **concentrated product is withdrawn SEPARATELY from each effect**. - Each effect operates **independently**. - Steam still flows in series: Effect 1 → 2 → 3 → condenser. - Feed flows **independently** to all effects simultaneously. ### 2.3.2 Operating Logic 1. Fresh feed F is split into F₁, F₂, F₃ … distributed to Effects 1, 2, 3. 2. Each effect concentrates its share of feed to the same final concentration. 3. Concentrated product streams L₁, L₂, L₃ are combined and withdrawn. 4. Vapor V₁ from Effect 1 heats Effect 2, V₂ heats Effect 3, V₃ goes to condenser. ### 2.3.3 Merits of Parallel Feed | # | Merit | Engineering Significance | |:---:|:---|:---| | 1 | **Simple operation; no liquid transfer between effects** | No pumps, no transfer lines | | 2 | **Suitable for crystallizing solutions** | Crystals can be removed independently from each effect | | 3 | **Each effect can be controlled independently** | Better product quality control | | 4 | **Used in salt manufacturing (NaCl, sugar)** | Crystallization with controlled supersaturation | ### 2.3.4 Demerits of Parallel Feed | # | Demerit | Reason | |:---:|:---|:---| | 1 | **Lowest steam economy** | Feed in each effect is dilute — requires more heat per kg water evaporated | | 2 | **High steam consumption** | Dilute feed in last effect requires significant heating | | 3 | **Uneven concentration** | Difficult to maintain uniform concentration across effects | --- ## 2.4 ARRANGEMENT 4: MIXED FEED (COMBINED FORWARD AND BACKWARD) ### 2.4.1 Operating Principle In **mixed feed**, the feed flows **forward** through some effects and **backward** through others. - Typical configuration in a 6-effect MEE: forward feed through Effects 1→2→3, then backward feed through Effects 3→4→5→6 (or vice versa). - Provides the **benefits of both forward and backward feed** while minimizing individual disadvantages. ### 2.4.2 Merits and Demerits | Aspect | Detail | |:---|:---| | **Merit 1** | Compromise between simplicity of forward feed and efficiency of backward feed | | **Merit 2** | Better heat transfer than forward feed | | **Merit 3** | Less pumping cost than pure backward feed | | **Demerit 1** | Complex piping arrangement | | **Demerit 2** | Difficult to control and operate | | **Demerit 3** | Used only in **large plants with 4+ effects** | | **Demerit 4** | Highest instrumentation cost | --- ## 2.5 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas required for KTU 2024 Board Exam (Module 3 — Evaporators)** | # | Formula | Symbol Legend | Units (SI) | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | $\theta = \dfrac{T_1 - T_N}{N}$ | $\theta$: temperature drop per effect; $T_1$: temp in Effect 1; $T_N$: temp in last effect; $N$: number of effects | K | | 2 | $\Delta T_{\text{effect}} = \Delta T_{\text{total}} / N$ | Equal area assumption; $\Delta T_{\text{total}}$ = overall $\Delta T$ | K | | 3 | $A_i = \dfrac{Q_i}{U_i \, \Delta T_i}$ | $A_i$: heat transfer area of effect $i$; $Q_i$: heat duty; $U_i$: overall HTC | m² | | 4 | $W = \dfrac{Q_i}{\lambda_i}$ | $W$: water evaporated per effect; $\lambda_i$: latent heat | kg/s | | 5 | $\text{Steam Economy} = \dfrac{W_{\text{total}}}{S}$ | $W_{\text{total}}$: total water evaporated; $S$: steam supplied | kg water / kg steam | | 6 | $\text{Capacity} = W_{\text{total}}$ | Total evaporation rate | kg/h | | 7 | $\text{Economy}_{\text{N-effect}} \approx 0.8 \, N$ | Approximate steam economy for $N$-effect MEE | — | | 8 | $\text{BPE} = f(\text{concentration}, \text{pressure})$ | Boiling Point Elevation correction | K | | 9 | $T_i^{\text{actual}} = T_i^{\text{sat}} + \text{BPE}_i$ | Actual temperature in effect $i$ | K | | 10 | $F \, x_F = L \, x_L = \sum (L_i \, x_{L_i})$ | Mass balance on solute | kg solute/h | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Specific Note:** The selection of feeding arrangement **does not change the total heat duty** (for the same feed and final concentration), but it **dramatically alters the heat transfer area distribution**, **pumping cost**, and **scale formation tendency** between individual effects. --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications | Industry | Preferred Feed | Reason | |:---|:---:|:---| | **Sugar refining** | Forward or Mixed | Avoid local overheating of sucrose | | **Salt (NaCl) production** | Parallel | Crystals form in each effect | | **Caustic soda (NaOH)** | Backward | Highly viscous, requires high-temp low-viscosity final stage | | **Milk/dairy** | Backward | Heat-sensitive; prevents protein coagulation | | **Paper & pulp (black liquor)** | Mixed | High viscosity, multiple effects | | **Fruit juice concentration** | Backward | Heat-sensitive, viscous concentrates | | **Desalination (seawater)** | Forward | Simplicity, low scaling with proper pretreatment | # 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation: Steam Economy and Capacity of a Triple-Effect Forward Feed Evaporator ### 3.1.1 Problem Setup Consider a **triple-effect forward feed evaporator** with the following given data: | Parameter | Symbol | Value | |:---|:---:|:---:| | Feed rate | $F$ | 10,000 kg/h | | Feed concentration | $x_F$ | 10% (by mass) | | Final liquor concentration | $x_L$ | 50% (by mass) | | Latent heat of vapor (assumed constant) | $\lambda$ | 2,260 kJ/kg | | Steam economy (assumed 0.8 × N) | — | 2.4 | ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution **STEP 1 — Total Mass Balance** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total water evaporated, } W &= F \left( 1 - \dfrac{x_F}{x_L} \right) \\ \end{aligned} $$ Substituting values: $$ \begin{aligned} W &= 10{,}000 \times \left( 1 - \dfrac{0.10}{0.50} \right) \\ W &= 10{,}000 \times (1 - 0.20) \\ W &= 10{,}000 \times 0.80 \\ W &= 8{,}000 \text{ kg/h} \end{aligned} $$ **STEP 2 — Steam Required** $$ \begin{aligned} S &= \dfrac{W}{\text{Steam Economy}} = \dfrac{W}{0.8 \, N} \\ S &= \dfrac{8{,}000}{0.8 \times 3} = \dfrac{8{,}000}{2.4} \\ S &= 3{,}333.33 \text{ kg/h} \end{aligned} $$ **STEP 3 — Steam Economy and Capacity Definitions** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Steam Economy} &= \dfrac{W}{S} = \dfrac{8{,}000}{3{,}333.33} = 2.4 \, \text{kg water/kg steam} \end{aligned} $$ **STEP 4 — Water Distribution in Forward Feed** In forward feed with equal evaporation per effect (assumed): $$ \begin{aligned} W_1 = W_2 = W_3 &= \dfrac{W_{\text{total}}}{N} = \dfrac{8{,}000}{3} = 2{,}666.67 \text{ kg/h per effect} \end{aligned} $$ **STEP 5 — Concentration After Each Effect** $$ \begin{aligned} x_1 &= \dfrac{F \cdot x_F}{F - W_1} = \dfrac{10{,}000 \times 0.10}{10{,}000 - 2{,}666.67} = \dfrac{1{,}000}{7{,}333.33} = 0.1364 \; (13.64\%) \\ \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} x_2 &= \dfrac{F \cdot x_F}{F - W_1 - W_2} = \dfrac{1{,}000}{10{,}000 - 2{,}666.67 - 2{,}666.67} = \dfrac{1{,}000}{4{,}666.67} = 0.2143 \; (21.43\%) \\ \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} x_3 &= \dfrac{F \cdot x_F}{F - W_1 - W_2 - W_3} = \dfrac{1{,}000}{10{,}000 - 8{,}000} = \dfrac{1{,}000}{2{,}000} = 0.50 \; (50\%) \;\; \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Observation:** Concentration rises progressively from Effect 1 → 3, but temperature decreases Effect 1 → 3. Therefore, **viscosity of the liquor RISES** as it approaches Effect 3 in forward feed — confirming the **demerit** of forward feed for viscous solutions. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Forward vs Backward vs Parallel vs Mixed) > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD COMPARISON TABLE — KTU 2024 EXAM FAVORITE** > Memorize this matrix — questions worth 7–14 marks often ask for a comparison. | Parameter | Forward Feed | Backward Feed | Parallel Feed | Mixed Feed | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **Feed direction** | Same as steam (1→N) | Opposite to steam (N→1) | Independent to each effect | Combined forward + backward | | **Feed entry effect** | Effect 1 (hottest) | Effect N (coldest) | All effects simultaneously | Variable | | **Pump requirement** | **No** | **Yes** (between every two effects) | **No** | Partial | | **Capital cost** | **Lowest** | **Highest** | Low | High | | **Operating complexity** | **Simplest** | Complex | Simple | Most complex | | **Viscosity handling** | Poor (conc. liquor in cold effect) | Excellent (conc. liquor in hot effect) | Moderate | Good | | **Scale formation** | High in Effect 1 (cold feed, hot surface) | Low (cold feed, cold surface) | Moderate | Moderate | | **Suitable solutions** | Dilute, non-viscous, non-scaling | Viscous, scaling, heat-sensitive | Crystallizing solutions | Large plants, 4+ effects | | **Heat transfer coefficient** | Decreases Effect 1 → N | Increases Effect 1 → N | Variable | Compromise | | **Steam economy** | Same total | Same total | Lower | Same total | | **Typical applications** | Sugar refining, salt | Caustic, milk, glycerin | Salt crystallization | Paper, large chemical plants | --- ## 3.3 Numerical Illustration — Effect of Feed Direction on Temperature Distribution Consider a **triple-effect evaporator** with overall temperature range from **T₁ = 120 °C (Effect 1)** to **T₃ = 60 °C (Effect 3)**. ### Case A: Forward Feed (Equal Area Assumption) $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2 = \Delta T_3 &= \dfrac{T_1 - T_3}{N} \\ &= \dfrac{120 - 60}{3} = 20 \, °C \end{aligned} $$ ### Case B: Backward Feed (Different ΔT Distribution) In backward feed, due to higher $U$ in Effect 1 (because viscosity is low at high temperature), the area required in Effect 1 is small for the same heat duty, allowing **redistribution of ΔT**. For backward feed, the **effective ΔT** in each effect can be derived from the equal-area condition: $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta T_1 : \Delta T_2 : \Delta T_3 &= \dfrac{1}{U_1} : \dfrac{1}{U_2} : \dfrac{1}{U_3} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > In backward feed, $U_1 > U_2 > U_3$ (since viscosity decreases with temperature rise). This forces $\Delta T_1 < \Delta T_2 < \Delta T_3$. Therefore, the temperature driving force is **LARGER in the LAST effect**, which exactly compensates for the lower $U_3$ — yielding a **smaller total heat transfer area** compared to forward feed. --- ## 3.4 Key Design Equation for Area in Each Effect $$ \begin{aligned} A_i &= \dfrac{Q_i}{U_i \, \Delta T_i} \quad \text{where} \quad Q_i = W_i \, \lambda_i \end{aligned} $$ The **total area** of all effects is equal for forward and backward feed (same $W$, same $Q$), but the **per-effect distribution** differs significantly due to varying $U_i$ and $\Delta T_i$. This is the **fundamental engineering insight** that drives the choice between forward and backward feed in industrial practice. # 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Forward Feed (Co-current) Arrangement — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR Steam1((Steam Supply)) Feed1((Dilute Feed F)) Effect1[Effect 1
Hot High Pressure] Effect2[Effect 2
Intermediate] Effect3[Effect 3
Cold Low Pressure] Condenser((Condenser)) VaporV3((Vapor Out)) LiquidL((Concentrated Product L)) Steam1 --> Effect1 Feed1 --> Effect1 Effect1 -- Vapor V1 --> Effect2 Effect1 -- Liquid by pressure drop --> Effect2 Effect2 -- Vapor V2 --> Effect3 Effect2 -- Liquid by pressure drop --> Effect3 Effect3 --> VaporV3 VaporV3 --> Condenser Effect3 --> LiquidL style Effect1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style Effect2 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000 style Effect3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` **Reading the diagram:** Feed (F) and steam BOTH enter Effect 1 → flow together toward Effect 3 → concentrated product exits from Effect 3 (coldest). --- ## 4.2 Backward Feed (Counter-current) Arrangement — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR Steam1((Steam Supply)) Feed3((Dilute Feed F)) Effect1[Effect 1
Hot High Pressure] Effect2[Effect 2
Intermediate] Effect3[Effect 3
Cold Low Pressure] Condenser((Condenser)) VaporV3((Vapor Out)) LiquidL((Concentrated Product L)) Pump12((Pump)) Pump23((Pump)) Steam1 --> Effect1 Feed3 --> Effect3 Effect3 -- Pump 3 to 2 --> Pump23 Pump23 --> Effect2 Effect2 -- Pump 2 to 1 --> Pump12 Pump12 --> Effect1 Effect1 --> LiquidL Effect1 -- Vapor V1 --> Effect2 Effect2 -- Vapor V2 --> Effect3 Effect3 --> VaporV3 VaporV3 --> Condenser style Effect1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style Effect2 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000 style Effect3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 style Pump12 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 style Pump23 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` **Reading the diagram:** Feed (F) enters Effect 3 (coldest) → pumped against pressure gradient to Effect 2 → pumped to Effect 1 → hot concentrated liquor exits from Effect 1. Vapor still flows 1 → 2 → 3 → condenser. --- ## 4.3 Parallel Feed Arrangement — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR Steam1((Steam Supply)) FeedSplit((Feed F split)) Effect1[Effect 1] Effect2[Effect 2] Effect3[Effect 3] Condenser((Condenser)) ProductMix((Combined Concentrate)) Steam1 --> Effect1 FeedSplit --> Effect1 FeedSplit --> Effect2 FeedSplit --> Effect3 Effect1 --> ProductMix Effect2 --> ProductMix Effect3 --> ProductMix Effect1 -- Vapor V1 --> Effect2 Effect2 -- Vapor V2 --> Effect3 Effect3 --> Condenser style Effect1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style Effect2 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000 style Effect3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` **Reading the diagram:** Fresh feed is split into 3 equal (or unequal) portions → each effect gets its own feed → each produces its own concentrate → all concentrates are combined as final product. --- ## 4.4 Mixed Feed (Combined Forward + Backward) Arrangement ```mermaid flowchart LR Steam1((Steam Supply)) Feed3((Feed F)) Effect1[Effect 1
Hottest] Effect2[Effect 2] Effect3[Effect 3
Forward Segment] Effect4[Effect 4] Effect5[Effect 5] Effect6[Effect 6
Coldest] Condenser((Condenser)) PumpA((Pump)) Steam1 --> Effect1 Feed3 --> Effect3 Effect3 --> Effect2 Effect2 --> Effect1 Effect1 -- Vapor V1 --> Effect2 Effect2 -- Vapor V2 --> Effect3 Effect3 -- Vapor V3 --> Effect4 Effect3 --> PumpA PumpA --> Effect4 Effect4 --> Effect5 Effect5 --> Effect6 Effect6 --> Condenser style Effect1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style Effect2 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000 style Effect3 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000 style Effect4 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 style Effect5 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 style Effect6 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` **Reading the diagram:** Feed enters at Effect 3 → flows forward to Effects 2 and 1 (forward segment) → then is pumped to Effect 4 → flows backward through Effects 5 and 6 (backward segment). Hybrid arrangement used in large installations. --- ## 4.5 Functional Architecture — Decision Tree for Selecting Feed Arrangement ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([New MEE Plant Design]) --> Q1{Is feed
viscous?} Q1 -- No --> Q2{Does solution
form scale?} Q1 -- Yes --> Q3{Is feed
heat-sensitive?} Q2 -- No --> FF[FORWARD FEED
Simplest, no pumps] Q2 -- Yes --> MX[MIXED FEED
Balance scale and viscosity] Q3 -- Yes --> BF[BACKWARD FEED
Best for viscous/heat-sensitive] Q3 -- No --> Q4{Crystals
formed?} Q4 -- Yes --> PF[PARALLEL FEED
Independent crystallization] Q4 -- No --> BF style Start fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#000 style FF fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 style BF fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000 style PF fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style MX fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ## PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Q: Differentiate between forward feed and backward feed multiple effect evaporators with respect to pump requirement and feed entry point.** #### Model Answer (3 Marks — Word Limit: 80–100 words): > **Forward Feed:** The feed enters **Effect 1** (hottest effect) and flows in the **same direction as steam/vapor**. Since the liquid flows along the **natural pressure gradient** (high → low pressure), **no pumps are required** between effects. This makes it the simplest and most economical arrangement. > > **Backward Feed:** The feed enters the **last (coldest) effect** and flows **opposite to the direction of steam**. Liquid must be moved **against the pressure gradient** (low → high pressure), so **pumps are required** between every two successive effects. This increases capital and operating cost but improves heat transfer for viscous solutions. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **Q: List any THREE situations where backward feed evaporator is preferred over forward feed.** #### Model Answer (3 Marks — One mark per point): 1. **Viscous solutions:** Concentrated liquor leaves the hottest effect where its viscosity is lowest → highest heat transfer coefficient. 2. **Scaling/heat-sensitive materials:** Cold feed entering the cold last effect prevents sudden boiling and reduces fouling/decomposition. 3. **High-viscosity final products** (e.g., glycerin, caustic soda, milk products): Backward feed maintains low viscosity throughout the concentration process, improving overall capacity. --- ## PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `Option 1` **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Q: Explain in detail the different feeding arrangements used in multiple effect evaporators. Compare their merits and demerits with neat sketches. State the specific industrial applications of each arrangement.** #### Sub-Part (a) — 7 Marks **Explain the four feeding arrangements with diagrams.** #### Model Answer — Sub-Part (a): **1. Forward Feed (Co-current Feed):** In this arrangement, the feed and the steam/vapor flow in the same direction from Effect 1 to Effect N. The dilute feed enters the hottest effect (Effect 1), and the concentrated liquor leaves the coldest effect (Effect N). The pressure gradient naturally drives the liquid flow, so no pumps are required between effects. The steam economy is approximately 0.8N, and this arrangement is the simplest and cheapest. **2. Backward Feed (Counter-current Feed):** Here, the feed enters the last (coldest) effect and flows in a direction opposite to that of steam/vapor, ultimately leaving as concentrate from the first (hottest) effect. Pumps are mandatory because the liquid must flow against the natural pressure gradient. Although capital cost is higher, it provides superior heat transfer for viscous, scaling, or heat-sensitive solutions. **3. Parallel Feed:** Fresh feed is introduced independently into each effect, and concentrated product is withdrawn separately from each effect. The effects operate independently. This arrangement is preferred for crystallizing solutions where individual crystal removal is required (e.g., salt manufacturing). **4. Mixed Feed (Combined Feed):** This arrangement combines forward and backward feed principles. For instance, in a 6-effect evaporator, the feed may move forward through effects 1-3 and then backward through 4-6. It is used in large industrial plants with 4 or more effects where the disadvantages of pure forward or backward feed are unacceptable. #### Sub-Part (b) — 7 Marks **Comparison table + industrial applications.** | Criterion | Forward | Backward | Parallel | Mixed | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | Pump requirement | None | Required (every effect) | None | Partial | | Capital cost | Lowest | Highest | Low | High | | Suitable solution type | Dilute, non-viscous | Viscous, scaling | Crystallizing | Large plants | | Heat transfer trend | Decreases 1→N | Increases 1→N | Independent | Compromise | | Scale formation risk | High (Effect 1) | Low | Moderate | Moderate | | Steam economy | 0.8N | 0.8N (same) | Lower | 0.8N | | Typical industry | Sugar, water | Caustic, milk, juice | Salt (NaCl) | Paper, large chemical | **Industrial Applications:** - **Forward feed:** Sugar industry (clarified juice concentration), seawater desalination, weak black liquor (after pretreatment). - **Backward feed:** Caustic soda concentration, milk products, fruit juices (heat-sensitive), glycerin, Glauber's salt. - **Parallel feed:** Sodium chloride crystallization, sugar vacuum pans with crystal formation. - **Mixed feed:** Pulp and paper (black liquor recovery boilers), large fertilizer plants, multi-effect desalination with 4+ effects. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `Option 2 / Internal Choice` **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **Q: A triple-effect forward feed evaporator concentrates a dilute caustic solution. The feed rate is 12,000 kg/h at 8% (by mass) NaOH, and the final concentrated liquor is 40% NaOH. The latent heat of vaporization may be taken as 2,260 kJ/kg for all effects. Calculate:** **(a)** Total water evaporated, steam economy (assume 0.8 × N), and steam required. **(b)** Concentration leaving each effect (assuming equal water evaporation per effect). #### Sub-Part (a) — 7 Marks **Step 1: Total water evaporated** $$ \begin{aligned} W_{\text{total}} &= F \left( 1 - \dfrac{x_F}{x_L} \right) \\ &= 12{,}000 \left( 1 - \dfrac{0.08}{0.40} \right) \\ &= 12{,}000 \times 0.80 = 9{,}600 \text{ kg/h} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Steam Economy (assumed 0.8 × N)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Steam Economy} &= 0.8 \times N = 0.8 \times 3 = 2.4 \text{ kg water/kg steam} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Steam required** $$ \begin{aligned} S &= \dfrac{W_{\text{total}}}{\text{Steam Economy}} = \dfrac{9{,}600}{2.4} = 4{,}000 \text{ kg/h} \end{aligned} $$ #### Sub-Part (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1: Equal water evaporation per effect** $$ \begin{aligned} W_1 = W_2 = W_3 &= \dfrac{9{,}600}{3} = 3{,}200 \text{ kg/h per effect} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Concentration after Effect 1** $$ \begin{aligned} x_1 &= \dfrac{F \cdot x_F}{F - W_1} = \dfrac{12{,}000 \times 0.08}{12{,}000 - 3{,}200} = \dfrac{960}{8{,}800} = 0.1091 \; (10.91\%) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Concentration after Effect 2** $$ \begin{aligned} x_2 &= \dfrac{F \cdot x_F}{F - W_1 - W_2} = \dfrac{960}{12{,}000 - 6{,}400} = \dfrac{960}{5{,}600} = 0.1714 \; (17.14\%) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Concentration after Effect 3 (Verification)** $$ \begin{aligned} x_3 &= \dfrac{F \cdot x_F}{F - W_1 - W_2 - W_3} = \dfrac{960}{12{,}000 - 9{,}600} = \dfrac{960}{2{,}400} = 0.40 \; (40\%) \;\; \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** Although the calculation works mathematically, in **actual industrial practice, caustic soda is NEVER concentrated in forward feed** because caustic is highly viscous at high concentration and would form scale in Effect 1. A **backward feed** arrangement is the engineering-correct choice for this duty. --- ## KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S PITFALL CALLOUT — COMMON MARK-LOSING MISTAKES** > > **1. Steam Economy vs. Capacity Confusion (Loses 2–3 marks):** > Students often confuse the terms *steam economy* (kg water evaporated per kg steam) with *capacity* (total kg water evaporated per hour). Remember: > - **Capacity** = depends on total $\Delta T$ and $A$ → approximately **constant** regardless of feed arrangement. > - **Steam Economy** = depends on $N$ and feed arrangement → improves with more effects. > > **2.
