Direction of Vapor Flow vs. Feed Flow (Loses 1–2 marks):** > - In **all arrangements**, **steam/vapor flows Effect 1 → Effect 2 → Effect 3 → Condenser**. > - What changes between arrangements is **only the direction of the FEED (liquid)** flow. > > **3. Pump Location in Backward Feed (Loses 1 mark):** > Pumps are placed **between successive effects** to overcome the pressure rise, NOT at the inlet or outlet. > > **4. Why Forward Feed Does NOT Need Pumps:** > Many students wrongly state "because of vacuum at the end." The correct reason is **the natural pressure gradient** (P₁ > P₂ > P₃) creates a **natural pressure difference** that drives the liquid. > > **5. Mixed Feed Notation:** > In the KTU 2024 answer sheet, **always sketch the arrows for both steam/vapor AND feed** — examiners deduct marks if only one direction is shown. > > **6. Steam Economy of Mixed Feed:** > Some students incorrectly claim mixed feed gives **higher** steam economy than forward/backward. **WRONG** — the **total economy is the same** (≈ 0.8N); only the **per-effect heat transfer distribution** differs. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **📌 RAPID REVISION — MUST REMEMBER FOR KTU 2024 EXAMS** ### ⭐ Core Concept - Feeding arrangement = direction of **feed flow** relative to **steam/vapor** flow in a multiple effect evaporator. ### ⭐ Four Arrangements 1. **Forward** — Feed 1→N (same direction as steam). No pumps. Best for dilute, non-viscous. 2. **Backward** — Feed N→1 (opposite steam). Pumps required. Best for viscous, scaling, heat-sensitive. 3. **Parallel** — Independent feed to each effect. Best for crystallization. 4. **Mixed** — Forward + Backward hybrid. Best for large plants with 4+ effects. ### ⭐ Critical Comparisons | Parameter | Forward | Backward | |:---|:---:|:---:| | Pumps | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | | Capital cost | Lower | Higher | | Viscosity in final effect | High (problematic) | Low (advantageous) | | Scale in Effect 1 | High risk | Low risk | ### ⭐ Key Equations - $W = F(1 - x_F/x_L)$ - Steam Economy ≈ 0.8 × N - $S = W_{\text{total}}$ / Economy - $A_i = Q_i / (U_i \cdot \Delta T_i)$ ### ⭐ Industrial Mapping (Memorize!) - **Sugar** → Forward - **Salt** → Parallel - **Caustic/Milk/Juice** → Backward - **Paper/Large chemicals** → Mixed ### ⭐ Quick Mnemonic > **"FoBaPaM"** — **Fo**rward, **Ba**ckward, **Pa**rallel, **M**ixed — The 4 feeding arrangements in MEE. ### ⭐ Examiner's Quick Sanity Checks - Forward feed = **No pumps, simple, cheapest.** - Backward feed = **Pumps, expensive, but best for viscous.** - Parallel feed = **Independent feeds, used for crystals.** - Mixed feed = **Hybrid, used for large plants.** - **Vapor ALWAYS flows 1 → N → Condenser in all arrangements.**

topics.title: Problem Solving in Boiling and Evaporation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Problem Solving in Boiling and Evaporation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boiling** is a convective heat transfer process in which a liquid is vaporized at a heated surface where the temperature of the heated surface is greater than the saturation temperature of the liquid. Vapor is formed directly at the heat transfer surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Evaporation** is a unit operation in which a volatile solvent (usually water) is vaporized from a non-volatile solute (e.g., NaOH, sugar, salt) using a heated surface, with the objective of concentrating the solution. The vapor is subsequently condensed (often) and the thickened liquor is the product.

### 1.2 The Two Are NOT the Same — A Critical Distinction

| Aspect | Boiling | Evaporation |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Generation of vapor / heat transfer study | Concentration of a solution |
| Solute present? | Generally pure fluid (or solute is irrelevant) | Solute (non-volatile) is the *reason* for the operation |
| Vapor recovered? | Not necessarily | Often condensed to recover solvent |
| Equipment | Kettle, boiler | Evaporator (calandria, falling film, forced circulation) |
| Typical ΔT | Often small, surface-driven | Often large, sustained |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Two Pots on the Stove"

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine you keep two pots on the stove.**
> - **Pot 1** has plain water and you only want steam (say, to drive a turbine or humidify a room). You measure how fast heat enters the water and how vigorous the bubbles are. This is **boiling** — the study of heat transfer rates at the wall.
> - **Pot 2** has sugar-water and you want to make syrup. The sugar stays behind; only water leaves as vapor. You measure how much water is removed and how much steam you had to supply. This is **evaporation** — a mass transfer + energy balance operation.
> - When you boil sugar-water, both phenomena happen *together* — the physical mechanism (nucleate boiling at the tube wall) is identical, but in evaporation we also worry about the changing solution concentration, boiling point rise, and economy of steam.

### 1.4 Why This Topic is a KTU High-Yield Module

- Direct numerical problems on **pool boiling correlations** (Rohsenow, Zuber, Berenson).
- Mass and energy balance problems on **single and multiple effect evaporators**.
- Problems asking for **capacity, economy, steam consumption, and area**.
- Both 3-mark (definition/numerical) and 14-mark (full design problem) questions are repeatedly asked.

**Key physical constants to remember (in bold):**
- Latent heat of vaporization of water at 100 °C ≈ **2257 kJ/kg**
- Saturation pressure of water at 100 °C = **101.325 kPa (1 atm)**
- g = **9.81 m/s²**
- σ_water at 100 °C ≈ **0.0589 N/m**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Pool Boiling Curve (Nukiyama Curve) — The Heart of Boiling

When a horizontal submerged surface is progressively heated, the heat flux and surface temperature behave in a very characteristic way. This is called the **Nukiyama Curve (1934)**.

**The Six Regimes (left to right on the heat flux axis):**

1. **Natural Convection (A→B):** ΔT small, no bubbles. Heat transfer by single-phase natural convection. q increases gently with ΔT.

2. **Nucleate Boiling (B→C):** Discrete bubbles nucleate at active cavities on the surface. This is the **designer’s paradise** — high heat flux with small ΔT.

3. **Transition (or Partial Film) Boiling (C→D):** Vapor blanketing begins; heat flux DROPS drastically even as ΔT rises. **Instability zone** — avoid operating here.

4. **Stable Film Boiling (D→E):** A continuous vapor film insulates the surface. Heat transfer again rises with ΔT but at lower rates than nucleate boiling.

5. **Critical Heat Flux (CHF) — Point C:** Also called *burnout point* or *q_max*. Going past this destroys the heating surface in water (the tube melts).

6. **Leidenfrost Point (D):** The minimum point on the curve. Beyond this, the surface is always covered with a stable film.

### 2.2 Why Does Nucleate Boiling Give Such High h?

- Latent heat of vaporization is **enormous** compared to sensible heat.
- Bubble formation and departure produces intense **micro-convection** that disturbs the thermal boundary layer.
- Liquid moves in to replace the bubble — bringing fresh, hot liquid to the surface.

### 2.3 The Three Classic Correlations for Boiling Problems

#### (a) Rohsenow Correlation (Nucleate Boiling, q < q_max)
This is the most-asked nucleate boiling correlation in KTU problems.

$$h_{b} \;=\; \mu_{l}\,h_{fg}\,\left[\dfrac{g(\rho_{l}-\rho_{v})}{\sigma}\right]^{1/2}\,\left[\dfrac{c_{pl}\,\Delta T_{e}}{C_{sf}\,h_{fg}\,Pr_{l}^{n}}\right]^{3}$$

where
- $h_b$ = nucleate boiling heat transfer coefficient, **W/m²·K**
- $\mu_l$ = liquid dynamic viscosity, **Pa·s**
- $h_{fg}$ = latent heat, **J/kg**
- $\rho_l, \rho_v$ = liquid and vapor densities, **kg/m³**
- $\sigma$ = surface tension, **N/m**
- $c_{pl}$ = liquid specific heat, **J/kg·K**
- $\Delta T_e = T_w - T_{sat}$ = excess temperature, **K**
- $C_{sf}$ = surface-fluid constant (dimensionless) — from tables (e.g., water-copper ≈ 0.0130, water-stainless steel ≈ 0.0132)
- $n = 1.0$ for water, 1.7 for other liquids
- $Pr_l$ = liquid Prandtl number

> [!NOTE]
> **Rohsenow cannot be used directly to find h** when only ΔT is known — it must be solved **iteratively** because both $h_b$ and $\Delta T_e$ appear. The typical KTU exam shortcut: solve the implicit equation for $\Delta T_e$ by trial (or graphically) once q or h is given.

#### (b) Zuber Correlation (Critical Heat Flux)
$$q_{max} \;=\; 0.149\,h_{fg}\,\rho_{v}\left[\dfrac{\sigma\,g\,(\rho_{l}-\rho_{v})}{\rho_{v}^{2}}\right]^{1/4}$$

This gives the upper bound of nucleate boiling. It is independent of the surface material — purely fluid-property driven.

#### (c) Berenson Correlation (Film Boiling)
For film boiling on a horizontal plate or tube:

$$h_{film} \;=\; 0.425\left[\dfrac{k_{v}^{3}\,\rho_{v}\,(\rho_{l}-\rho_{v})\,g\,h_{fg}^{\,\prime}}{D\,\mu_{v}\,\Delta T_{w}}\right]^{1/4}$$

where $h_{fg}^{\,\prime} = h_{fg} + 0.68\,c_{pv}\,\Delta T_w$, $D$ is tube diameter, and $\Delta T_w = T_w - T_{sat}$.

### 2.4 Evaporation — The Balance Equations

For a **single-effect evaporator** with feed F, thick liquor L, vapor V, and saturated steam as heating medium:

**Total mass balance:**
$$F \;=\; L + V$$

**Solute (solids) balance:**
$$F\,x_{F} \;=\; L\,x_{L}$$

**Energy balance** (steam at T_S, feed enters at T_F, vapor leaves at T_sat of liquid, condensate leaves at T_S):
$$F\,h_{F} + S\,H_{S} \;=\; L\,h_{L} + V\,H_{V} + S\,h_{S} + Q_{loss}$$

Neglecting $Q_{loss}$ and using $H_S - h_S = \lambda_S$ (latent heat of steam):
$$\boxed{\,S\,\lambda_{S} \;=\; L\,h_{L} + V\,H_{V} - F\,h_{F}\,}$$

The **heat transfer rate** required:
$$Q \;=\; S\,\lambda_{S} \;=\; U\,A\,\Delta T_{overall}$$
where $\Delta T_{overall} = T_{steam} - T_{liquid\ boiling\ (adjusted\ for\ BPE)}$.

### 2.5 Boiling Point Elevation (BPE)

A solution boils at a temperature **higher** than the pure solvent's saturation temperature at the same pressure. This is called **Boiling Point Elevation (BPE)**.
$$T_{solution} \;=\; T_{sat\ water} + \text{BPE}$$

It is caused by the solute lowering the vapor pressure of the solvent (Raoult's law effect). For dilute solutions, BPE ≈ K_b · m (Dühring-style rule).

### 2.6 Capacity and Economy

| Term | Definition | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Capacity** | Rate of water (solvent) evaporated per hour | kg/hr or kg/s |
| **Economy** | kg of water evaporated per kg of steam supplied | dimensionless |

$$\text{Economy} \;=\; \dfrac{V}{S}$$
$$\text{Capacity} \;=\; V$$

For a **multiple-effect evaporator** (n effects) operating with **equal area and no BPE corrections**:
$$\Delta T_{total} \;=\; T_{S} - T_{vapor\ last\ effect}$$
$$\Delta T_{each\ effect} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta T_{total}}{n}$$

Steam economy rises (≈ 0.8·n) at the cost of lower capacity per effect.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Master Formula Table — Memorize Before Going to the Exam Hall**

| # | Equation | What It Gives | Typical Use in KTU |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $q_{max}=0.149\,h_{fg}\rho_{v}[\sigma g(\rho_l-\rho_v)/\rho_v^2]^{1/4}$ | Critical heat flux | "Find CHF of water at 1 atm" |
| 2 | $h_{b}=\mu_l h_{fg}\sqrt{g(\rho_l-\rho_v)/\sigma}\left[\dfrac{c_{pl}\Delta T_e}{C_{sf}h_{fg}Pr_l^n}\right]^{3}$ | Nucleate boiling h | Iterative solve for ΔT_e or h |
| 3 | $h_{film}=0.425\left[\dfrac{k_v^3\rho_v(\rho_l-\rho_v)g\,h_{fg}^{\,\prime}}{D\mu_v\Delta T_w}\right]^{1/4}$ | Film boiling h | "Film boiling on tube" |
| 4 | $F = L + V$ | Total mass balance | Evaporator material balance |
| 5 | $Fx_F = Lx_L$ | Solute balance | Finding L or x_L |
| 6 | $S\lambda_S = L h_L + V H_V - F h_F$ | Steam economy | Single effect energy balance |
| 7 | $Q = U A \Delta T$ | Heat transfer area | Finding A or U |
| 8 | $\Delta T_{each}=\Delta T_{total}/n$ | Per-effect ΔT | Multiple-effect with equal area |
| 9 | Economy = V/S | Performance | Comparison of schemes |
| 10 | BPE = T_solution − T_water(sat) | Boiling point rise | Correcting ΔT for solutions |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-Truncation Mandate:** Every algebraic and numerical transition below is written out **explicitly**. No "…" substitutions.

### 3.1 Derivation: The Rohsenow Correlation — From Bubble Mechanics to a Closed Form

Rohsenow (1952) reasoned that the heat transfer coefficient in nucleate boiling is governed by the rate at which bubbles depart from the heated surface. The bubble departure diameter $D_b$ is set by a force balance between buoyancy pulling the bubble up and surface tension holding it:

$$\text{Buoyancy force} \;=\; \frac{\pi D_b^{3}}{6}\,g\,(\rho_{l}-\rho_{v})$$

$$\text{Surface tension force} \;=\; \pi D_{b}\,\sigma$$

Setting them equal at departure:
$$\frac{\pi D_{b}^{3}}{6}\,g\,(\rho_{l}-\rho_{v}) \;=\; \pi D_{b}\,\sigma$$

Solving for $D_b$:
$$D_{b} \;=\; \left[\dfrac{6\sigma}{g(\rho_{l}-\rho_{v})}\right]^{1/2}\tag{3.1}$$

Rohsenow then assumed the bubble departure frequency is proportional to the thermal boundary-layer timescale, leading to an **energy balance at the surface**:

$$\text{Heat supplied per unit area per unit time} \;\propto\; \rho_l\,c_{pl}\,u^{*}\,\Delta T_e$$

where $u^{*}$ is a friction velocity related to bubble-induced micro-convection. Eliminating the bubble physics variables and rearranging, one arrives at:

$$h_{b}\,\left[\dfrac{\sigma}{g(\rho_{l}-\rho_{v})}\right]^{1/2}\dfrac{1}{k_{l}} \;=\; \left[\dfrac{c_{pl}\,\Delta T_e}{C_{sf}\,h_{fg}\,Pr_{l}^{n}}\right]^{3}$$

Multiplying through by $\dfrac{k_l}{\sqrt{\sigma/[g(\rho_l-\rho_v)]}}$ and recognizing that $\dfrac{k_l}{\mu_l c_{pl}} = 1/Pr_l$ gives the **final Rohsenow form**:

$$\boxed{\,h_{b} \;=\; \mu_{l}\,h_{fg}\,\sqrt{\dfrac{g(\rho_{l}-\rho_{v})}{\sigma}}\;\left[\dfrac{c_{pl}\,\Delta T_{e}}{C_{sf}\,h_{fg}\,Pr_{l}^{n}}\right]^{3}\,}\tag{3.2}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Zuber CHF from Helmholtz Instability

Zuber (1959) considered the vapor blanket just before it collapses the liquid contact with the surface. The vapor escapes in **Helmholtz waves** of wavelength $\lambda$. The Taylor-Helmholtz instability gives the fastest-growing wavelength as:

$$\lambda \;=\; 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{3\sigma}{g(\rho_l-\rho_v)}}\tag{3.3}$$

The vapor velocity required to remove all vapor generated at $q_{max}$:

$$\rho_v\,u_v \;=\; \dfrac{q_{max}}{h_{fg}}\tag{3.4}$$

The Helmholtz instability condition links $u_v$ and $\lambda$:

$$u_v^{2}\,\rho_v \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi\sigma}{\lambda}\,(1-\rho_v/\rho_l)\tag{3.5}$$

Substituting $\lambda$ from (3.3) into (3.5):
$$u_v^{2}\,\rho_v \;=\; \dfrac{g(\rho_l-\rho_v)}{3}$$

Then substituting $u_v = q_{max}/(h_{fg}\rho_v)$:
$$\dfrac{q_{max}^{2}}{h_{fg}^{2}\rho_v} \;=\; \dfrac{g(\rho_l-\rho_v)}{3}$$

Solving for $q_{max}$:

$$q_{max} \;=\; h_{fg}\,\rho_v\,\sqrt{\dfrac{g(\rho_l-\rho_v)}{3}}\tag{3.6}$$

Zuber found the exact hydrodynamic coefficient to be 0.149 rather than $1/\sqrt{3}=0.577$, by including **Taylor wavelength** (2D instability correction) instead of 1D Kelvin-Helmholtz. So:

$$\boxed{\,q_{max} \;=\; 0.149\,h_{fg}\,\rho_{v}\left[\dfrac{\sigma g(\rho_l-\rho_v)}{\rho_v^{2}}\right]^{1/4}\,}\tag{3.7}$$

### 3.3 Numerical Problem 1 — Critical Heat Flux of Water

**Problem (KTU-style):** Saturated water at 1 atm boils on a polished copper plate. Find the maximum heat flux and the minimum value of h above CHF.
Given (water at 100 °C, 1 atm): $h_{fg} = 2257\times10^3$ J/kg, $\rho_v = 0.596$ kg/m³, $\rho_l = 958$ kg/m³, $\sigma = 0.0589$ N/m, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Write Zuber equation:
$$q_{max}=0.149\,h_{fg}\,\rho_v\left[\dfrac{\sigma g(\rho_l-\rho_v)}{\rho_v^{2}}\right]^{1/4}$$

Step 2 — Compute the term inside the bracket:
$$\rho_l-\rho_v \;=\; 958-0.596 \;=\; 957.404\ \text{kg/m}^3$$

Step 3 — Compute $\sigma g(\rho_l-\rho_v)/\rho_v^{2}$:
$$\dfrac{0.0589\times9.81\times957.404}{(0.596)^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{553.05}{0.355} \;\approx\; 1557.6$$

Step 4 — Take the fourth root:
$$(1557.6)^{1/4} \;=\; \sqrt{\sqrt{1557.6}} \;=\; \sqrt{39.47} \;\approx\; 6.283$$

Step 5 — Multiply all pieces:
$$q_{max} \;=\; 0.149\times(2257\times10^{3})\times0.596\times6.283$$

Step 6 — Numerical evaluation:
$$0.149\times2257\times10^{3} \;=\; 336.3\times10^{3}$$
$$336.3\times10^{3}\times0.596 \;=\; 200.4\times10^{3}$$
$$200.4\times10^{3}\times6.283 \;\approx\; 1.259\times10^{6}\ \text{W/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{q_{max}\;\approx\;1.26\ \text{MW/m}^2}$$

### 3.4 Numerical Problem 2 — Nucleate Boiling h by Rohsenow

**Problem:** Water is boiling nucleately at 1 atm on a stainless steel surface at $T_w = 110\ ^\circ$C. Find the heat transfer coefficient. Use Rohsenow with $C_{sf}=0.0132$, $n=1.0$. Fluid data at 100 °C: $\mu_l=2.82\times10^{-4}$ Pa·s, $\rho_l=958$ kg/m³, $\rho_v=0.596$ kg/m³, $\sigma=0.0589$ N/m, $c_{pl}=4216$ J/kg·K, $h_{fg}=2257\times10^3$ J/kg, $Pr_l=1.76$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Excess temperature:
$$\Delta T_e \;=\; 110 - 100 \;=\; 10\ \text{K}$$

Step 2 — Bracket A:
$$A \;=\; \dfrac{g(\rho_l-\rho_v)}{\sigma} \;=\; \dfrac{9.81\times957.404}{0.0589} \;\approx\; 1.594\times10^{5}$$

Step 3 — $\sqrt{A}$:
$$\sqrt{A} \;\approx\; 399.3$$

Step 4 — Bracket B:
$$B \;=\; \dfrac{c_{pl}\,\Delta T_e}{C_{sf}\,h_{fg}\,Pr_l^{n}} \;=\; \dfrac{4216\times10}{0.0132\times2257\times10^{3}\times1.76}$$

Compute denominator: $0.0132\times2257\times10^3\times1.76 \approx 52437$

So $B = 42160/52437 \approx 0.8041$

Step 5 — $B^3$:
$$B^{3} \;\approx\; (0.8041)^{3} \;\approx\; 0.520$$

Step 6 — Assemble $h_b$:
$$h_b \;=\; \mu_l\,h_{fg}\,\sqrt{A}\,B^{3} \;=\; (2.82\times10^{-4})\times(2257\times10^{3})\times399.3\times0.520$$

Step 7 — Evaluate:
$$2.82\times10^{-4}\times2257\times10^{3} \;=\; 636.5$$
$$636.5\times399.3 \;\approx\; 254,213$$
$$254,213\times0.520 \;\approx\; 132,191$$

$$\boxed{h_b\;\approx\;1.32\times10^{5}\ \text{W/m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{K}}$$

### 3.5 Numerical Problem 3 — Single Effect Evaporator

**Problem:** A single-effect evaporator concentrates 10,000 kg/hr of a 10 wt% NaOH solution to 30 wt%. Feed enters at 30 °C, steam is supplied at 3.5 bar (sat. T ≈ 138.9 °C, $\lambda_S = 2147$ kJ/kg). The boiling temperature inside the evaporator is 105 °C (due to BPE). Find (a) capacity V, (b) thick liquor flow L, (c) steam supplied S, (d) economy. Take $c_{p,feed}\approx4.0$ kJ/kg·K, $h_V \approx 2680$ kJ/kg (steam at 105 °C), $h_L \approx 380$ kJ/kg (approx, 30% NaOH at 105 °C).

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Solute balance:
$$F\,x_F \;=\; L\,x_L \;\Rightarrow\; 10{,}000\times0.10 \;=\; L\times0.30$$

$$L \;=\; \dfrac{1000}{0.30} \;=\; 3333.33\ \text{kg/hr}$$

Step 2 — Total mass balance (capacity):
$$V \;=\; F - L \;=\; 10{,}000 - 3333.33 \;=\; 6666.67\ \text{kg/hr}$$

Step 3 — Energy balance:
$$S\,\lambda_S \;=\; L\,h_L + V\,H_V - F\,h_F$$

Compute $h_F$ (feed as water-like with $c_p=4$):
$$h_F \;=\; 4.0\times(30-0) \;=\; 120\ \text{kJ/kg}$$

Step 4 — Right side:
$$RHS \;=\; 3333.33\times380 + 6666.67\times2680 - 10{,}000\times120$$

$$= 1{,}266{,}667 + 17{,}866{,}676 - 1{,}200{,}000$$

$$= 17{,}933{,}343\ \text{kJ/hr}$$

Step 5 — Steam required:
$$S \;=\; \dfrac{17{,}933{,}343}{2147\times10^3} \;=\; \dfrac{17{,}933{,}343}{2{,}147{,}000} \;\approx\; 8.35\ \text{kg/s of equiv.}$$

Convert to kg/hr: $S = 8.35\times3600 \approx 30,063$ kg/hr. Wait — check units.

> [!NOTE]
> When the energy balance is written per hour with enthalpies in kJ/kg, $\lambda_S$ should be in kJ/kg. The right side gives total kJ/hr. Then $S$ comes out in kg/hr directly.

So redo: $\lambda_S = 2147$ kJ/kg.
$$RHS \;=\; 17{,}933{,}343\ \text{kJ/hr}$$

$$S \;=\; \dfrac{17{,}933{,}343}{2147} \;\approx\; 8353\ \text{kg/hr}$$

Step 6 — Economy:
$$\text{Economy} \;=\; \dfrac{V}{S} \;=\; \dfrac{6666.67}{8353} \;\approx\; 0.798$$

$$\boxed{V=6666.67\ \text{kg/hr},\ L=3333.33\ \text{kg/hr},\ S\approx8353\ \text{kg/hr},\ \text{Economy}\approx0.80}$$

### 3.6 Numerical Problem 4 — Triple-Effect Equal-Area ΔT

**Problem:** Steam at 164.9 °C (6 bar) feeds a triple-effect evaporator. The last effect operates at 0.2 atm (vapor at ~60 °C). BPE is negligible. Find ΔT per effect.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Total ΔT:
$$\Delta T_{total} \;=\; T_S - T_{v,last} \;=\; 164.9 - 60 \;=\; 104.9\ \text{K}$$

Step 2 — Divide equally:
$$\Delta T_{each} \;=\; \dfrac{104.9}{3} \;\approx\; 34.97\ \text{K}$$

Step 3 — Operating temperatures:
- Steam side (effect 1): $T_1 = 164.9$ °C
- Vapor of effect 1 = boiling liquid of effect 2: $T_2 = 164.9 - 34.97 = 129.93$ °C
- Vapor of effect 2 = boiling liquid of effect 3: $T_3 = 129.93 - 34.97 = 94.96$ °C
- Vapor of effect 3 (to condenser): $T_3 = 60$ °C (theoretical last)

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{each}\approx34.97\ \text{K}}$$

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Problem Statement | Correlation / Equation | Common Trap |
|---|---|---|
| "Max heat flux before burnout" | Zuber (CHF) | Forgetting to raise to the 1/4 power correctly |
| "Nucleate boiling h on copper" | Rohsenow | Forgetting iterative nature — actually solvable if ΔT given |
| "Film boiling on tube" | Berenson | Using $h_{fg}$ instead of $h_{fg}^{\,\prime}$ |
| "Steam supplied for n-effect" | $\lambda_S$ balance per effect | Forgetting steam economy gain (≈ 0.8n) |
| "Concentration from feed" | Solute + total balance | Forgetting to convert wt% carefully |
| "Equal-area multiple effect" | $\Delta T_{each}=\Delta T_{total}/n$ | If BPE nonzero, must subtract it first |
| "Boiling point rise" | Dühring-line / data | Using P_solution instead of P_water |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flowchart — Boiling Regime Identification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Submerged horizontal surface] --> B[Heating begins]
    B --> C{Excess temp DeltaT_e?}
    C -->|DeltaT_e less than 4 K| D[Natural Convection Regime]
    C -->|DeltaT_e between 4 and 30 K| E[Nucleate Boiling B to C]
    C -->|DeltaT_e beyond CHF| F[Transition Regime C to D]
    C -->|Beyond Leidenfrost point D| G[Stable Film Boiling D to E]
    D --> H[Low heat flux - quiet]
    E --> I[Highest h - designer mode]
    F --> J[UNSTABLE - avoid]
    G --> K[Low h - vapor film insulator]
    H --> L[Single-phase like]
    I --> M[q_max approx 1.26 MW per sqm for water]
    J --> N[Burnout risk]
    K --> O[Increase q only with DeltaT_w^1.25]
    E --> P[Use Rohsenow correlation]
    G --> Q[Use Berenson correlation]
    E --> R[CHF by Zuber at point C]
    style E fill:#90EE90
    style F fill:#FFB6C1
    style R fill:#FFD700
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*State the difference between nucleate boiling and film boiling. Mention the role of the critical heat flux.*

**Model Answer:**
- **Nucleate boiling:** Discrete bubbles form at active cavities on the heated surface and detach into the bulk liquid. Heat transfer coefficient is **high (h ~ 10⁴–10⁵ W/m²·K)** because of intense micro-convection. The Rohsenow correlation applies in this regime.
- **Film boiling:** The heated surface is blanketed by a continuous vapor film that insulates the liquid from the wall. Heat transfer is **much lower** (h ~ 200–500 W/m²·K) and is governed by conduction through the vapor. Berenson's correlation is used.
- **Critical Heat Flux (CHF, $q_{max}$):** The maximum heat flux the nucleate boiling regime can sustain. Beyond it the surface "burns out". Given by Zuber's correlation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Insight:** Examiner gives full 3 marks only if all three (nucleate, film, CHF) are explained in 2–3 lines each. A one-line answer will lose 1 mark.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define: (i) Capacity, (ii) Economy of an evaporator.*

**Model Answer:**
- **Capacity:** The total mass of solvent (usually water) evaporated per unit time. Units: kg/hr or kg/s.
- **Economy:** The ratio of mass of water evaporated to the mass of steam supplied to the evaporator.
$$\text{Economy} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{kg water evaporated}}{\text{kg steam supplied}}$$

For a single-effect evaporator, economy ≈ 0.8 to 0.9. For a triple-effect, it can approach ≈ 2.4 to 2.7.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Choice Questions (14 Marks each)

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Boiling-Focused**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 style]**

**(a)** [7 marks] Saturated water at atmospheric pressure boils on a polished copper surface. Using Zuber’s correlation, calculate the critical heat flux. Given for water at 100 °C:
- $h_{fg} = 2257$ kJ/kg, $\rho_l = 958$ kg/m³, $\rho_v = 0.596$ kg/m³, $\sigma = 0.0589$ N/m, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**(b)** [7 marks] Using Rohsenow's correlation, compute the nucleate boiling heat transfer coefficient for the same surface if the excess temperature is 10 K. Use $C_{sf}=0.0130$, $n=1.0$, $\mu_l=2.82\times10^{-4}$ Pa·s, $c_{pl}=4216$ J/kg·K, $Pr_l=1.76$.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Critical Heat Flux**
Using Zuber:
$$q_{max}=0.149\,h_{fg}\,\rho_v\left[\dfrac{\sigma g(\rho_l-\rho_v)}{\rho_v^{2}}\right]^{1/4}$$

Compute term-by-term:
- $\rho_l - \rho_v = 957.404$ kg/m³
- $\sigma g(\rho_l-\rho_v) = 0.0589\times9.81\times957.404 = 553.05$ (N·kg/m²·s²)
- Divide by $\rho_v^2 = 0.3552$ → $1557.6$
- Fourth root → $6.283$
- Multiply: $0.149\times2257\times10^3\times0.596\times6.283 = 1.26\times10^6$ W/m²

**$q_{max}\approx1.26$ MW/m²**

**(b) Rohsenow h_b**
$$h_{b} \;=\; \mu_{l}\,h_{fg}\,\sqrt{\dfrac{g(\rho_{l}-\rho_{v})}{\sigma}}\;\left[\dfrac{c_{pl}\,\Delta T_{e}}{C_{sf}\,h_{fg}\,Pr_{l}^{n}}\right]^{3}$$

- $\sqrt{g(\rho_l-\rho_v)/\sigma} = \sqrt{9.81\times957.404/0.0589} = \sqrt{159419} = 399.3$
- $c_{pl}\Delta T_e = 4216\times10 = 42160$
- $C_{sf}h_{fg}Pr_l^n = 0.0130\times2257\times10^3\times1.76 = 51{,}651$
- Bracket ratio = 0.816, cubed = 0.544
- $h_b = 2.82\times10^{-4}\times2257\times10^3\times399.3\times0.544$
- $= 636.5\times399.3\times0.544 \approx 1.38\times10^5$ W/m²·K

**$h_b\approx 1.38\times10^5$ W/m²·K**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Evaporator-Focused**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 style]**

**(a)** [7 marks] A single-effect evaporator is used to concentrate 5,000 kg/hr of a 10 wt% NaOH solution to 40 wt%. The feed enters at 40 °C. Saturated steam at 1.5 bar ($\lambda_S = 2227$ kJ/kg) is used. The liquor boils at 110 °C (BPE = 10 K). Assume $h_F \approx c_p \cdot T_F = 4.0\times40 = 160$ kJ/kg, $H_V \approx 2696$ kJ/kg, $h_L \approx 410$ kJ/kg (estimated for 40% NaOH at 110 °C). Calculate (i) thick liquor rate, (ii) capacity, (iii) steam rate, (iv) economy.

**(b)** [7 marks] The same steam source (at $T_S = 110$ °C equivalent for that pressure) is used in a **double-effect** evaporator with negligible BPE. The last effect is maintained at 50 °C. Assuming equal heat transfer area and equal U, find ΔT per effect and the boiling temperatures of the two effects.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Single Effect**

(i) **Thick liquor**:
$$L = \dfrac{Fx_F}{x_L} = \dfrac{5000\times0.10}{0.40} = 1250\ \text{kg/hr}$$

(ii) **Capacity**:
$$V = F - L = 5000 - 1250 = 3750\ \text{kg/hr}$$

(iii) **Steam**:
$$S\lambda_S = L h_L + V H_V - F h_F$$
$$RHS = 1250\times410 + 3750\times2696 - 5000\times160$$
$$= 512{,}500 + 10{,}110{,}000 - 800{,}000 = 9{,}822{,}500\ \text{kJ/hr}$$
$$S = \dfrac{9{,}822{,}500}{2227} \approx 4410\ \text{kg/hr}$$

(iv) **Economy**:
$$E = \dfrac{V}{S} = \dfrac{3750}{4410} \approx 0.85$$

**Answers:** L = 1250 kg/hr, V = 3750 kg/hr, S ≈ 4410 kg/hr, Economy ≈ 0.85

**(b) Double Effect**

$$\Delta T_{total} = T_S - T_{last} = 110 - 50 = 60\ \text{K}$$

$$\Delta T_{each} = \dfrac{60}{2} = 30\ \text{K}$$

- Effect 1: T₁ = 110 °C (boiling at steam side; pressure higher)
- Effect 2: T₂ = 110 − 30 = 80 °C

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{each}=30\ \text{K},\ T_{1}=110\ ^\circ\text{C},\ T_{2}=80\ ^\circ\text{C}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callouts — Where You Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch** in energy balance: $\lambda_S$ in kJ/kg vs J/kg — convert before plugging.
> 2. **Forgetting BPE** when the problem says "liquor boils at 105 °C" but pure water would boil at 100 °C.
> 3. **Rohsenow iterations**: don't claim to solve it in one step — show a guess and a check.
> 4. **Last effect vapor T**: must come from a stated pressure, not assumed.
> 5. **Negative ΔT** in multiple-effect: if $T_S \leq T_{last}$, you have either inverted your system or the question has a trap.
> 6. **Economy without subtracting condensate enthalpy** if asked for "live steam".

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print This Before the Exam**

- [ ] Nukiyama curve has 6 regimes: Natural convection → Nucleate boiling → Transition → Film boiling → CHF (burnout) → Leidenfrost.
- [ ] **Nucleate boiling** uses Rohsenow; **film boiling** uses Berenson; **CHF** uses Zuber.
- [ ] Rohsenow: implicit in ΔT_e; iterative or graphical solution; constants $C_{sf}$ and $n$ are tabulated.
- [ ] Zuber CHF for water at 1 atm ≈ **1.26 MW/m²** — a benchmark number.
- [ ] Evaporator **mass balance**: $F = L + V$.
- [ ] Evaporator **solute balance**: $Fx_F = Lx_L$.
- [ ] Evaporator **energy balance**: $S\lambda_S = Lh_L + VH_V - Fh_F$.
- [ ] **Economy** = $V/S$; single-effect ≈ 0.8–0.9; n-effect ideal ≈ $0.8n$.
- [ ] **Capacity** = V (kg water evaporated per unit time).
- [ ] **BPE** raises the boiling point of the solution; subtract BPE from ΔT before sizing area.
- [ ] **Multiple-effect equal-area ΔT**: $\Delta T_{each} = (T_S - T_{last} - \sum \text{BPE}_i)/n$.
- [ ] **q_max is a property of the fluid, not the surface** (Zuber is surface-independent).
- [ ] Single-effect evaporators: high steam cost but simple; multiple-effect: more capex but better economy.
- [ ] KTU usually asks **one numerical** (boiling h or q_max) and **one design** (evaporator sizing with balance).
- [ ] Always write **units explicitly** — examiners are picky on kJ/kg vs J/kg.
- [ ] Memorize the **6 standard formula cards** above; they cover ~90% of exam problems on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaporator Design Using Excel/Matlab/Scilab/Python (Only for Self-Study/Microproject/Assignment)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Evaporator Design Using Excel/Matlab/Scilab/Python (Only for Self-Study/Microproject/Assignment)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Evaporator Design Using Computational Tools** refers to the systematic, iterative numerical procedure employed to size and rate the heat-transfer surface area, steam economy, and operating parameters of single-effect and multiple-effect evaporators. Because most real-world evaporator problems involve a set of coupled, non-linear algebraic equations (mass balances, energy balances, and heat-transfer rate equations) in which the unknown temperatures in each effect must be solved simultaneously, a closed-form analytical solution is generally impossible. Hence, **iterative numerical techniques** — such as the **Method of Successive Substitution (MSS)**, **Newton–Raphson method**, or matrix-based simultaneous solvers — are implemented in computational environments (spreadsheets or numerical computing platforms) to converge upon the correct temperature distribution, liquid-vapor split, and required heat-transfer area.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, this topic is designated as a **self-study / microproject / assignment module**, meaning that the learner is expected to (i) formulate the governing equations, (ii) construct a clear algorithm, (iii) implement it in a chosen tool, and (iv) validate the numerical output against a known analytical or literature benchmark.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** This topic carries no direct End-Semester Examination (ESE) weightage. Marks are assessed through **Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE)** components such as assignments, microproject reports, and viva-voce. The student is expected to demonstrate competence in problem formulation, tool usage, and interpretation of results.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have **three buckets** stacked vertically, each connected by a pipe. Hot steam enters the top bucket and boils the dilute solution inside. The vapor produced flows into the next bucket below as its heating medium, which in turn boils a slightly more concentrated solution, and so on. The challenge is: *you do not know the temperature in any of the three buckets before you start the calculation*. The temperature in effect 2 depends on the temperature in effect 1, which depends on the temperature in effect 2 (through the boiling-point elevation, vapor splits, and area constraint). It is a circular dependency — a "snake biting its own tail."

**Computational tools act like a smart thermostat that keeps adjusting each room's temperature** until the heat flowing in equals the heat flowing out at every point. The tool performs this "adjust-recalculate-adjust" cycle hundreds of times in a fraction of a second until the answer no longer changes. That iterative loop is the *numerical engine* of evaporator design.

## 1.3 Why Computational Tools Are Indispensable in Evaporator Design

| Reason | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|
| **Coupled non-linear equations** | Temperature, Boiling-Point Elevation (BPE), and area are inter-dependent. |
| **Iterative convergence** | No closed-form solution exists for multi-effect systems. |
| **Sensitivity analysis** | Engineers must study how output changes with feed rate, pressure, or concentration. |
| **Optimization studies** | Capital cost vs. steam economy trade-offs require rapid scenario testing. |
| **Process integration** | Outputs feed into downstream design (crystallizer, dryer). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard metric in KTU module**: Steam economy = (kg water evaporated) / (kg steam supplied). A typical 3-effect evaporator has an economy of ≈ **2.0 to 2.5** for a feed-forward configuration.

## 1.4 Scope of the Computational Exercise

The student is expected to develop a working model for at least the following cases:

1. **Single-effect evaporator** with given feed, product concentration, steam pressure, and vacuum. Solve for steam consumption, area, and economy.
2. **Triple-effect evaporator** (forward feed, backward feed, or parallel feed) where the temperature distribution and area must be iterated.
3. **Optional extension:** Effect of feed preheating, vapor bleeding, or additional effects on overall economy.

All cases may be coded in **Excel (using iterative calculation toggled ON)**, **Scilab/Matlab (using `fsolve` or custom loop)**, or **Python (using `scipy.optimize`)** — but in line with KTU non-CS engineering discipline norms, the *algorithmic formulation and mathematical correctness* are assessed, not language-specific syntax.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Governing Equations Recap

A multi-effect evaporator with **N** effects is governed by **3N independent equations** that must be satisfied simultaneously:

1. **Material (mass) balance on solute in each effect** (dilute → concentrated).
2. **Overall mass balance on total liquid** (solvent evaporation).
3. **Energy (enthalpy) balance on each effect**.
4. **Rate equation** of heat transfer: $Q_i = U_i A_i \Delta T_i$.
5. **Constraint:** Total area is equal in all effects (for standard design) or specified individually.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU module places strong emphasis on the **equality-of-area constraint** $\left(A_1 = A_2 = \ldots = A_N\right)$, which is the principal reason an iterative procedure becomes mandatory.

## 2.2 Assumptions Standard to KTU Module Problems

To keep the design tractable, the following simplifications are universally adopted (and explicitly stated in model solutions):

- **Negligible sensible-heat change of the heating steam (condensate leaves as saturated liquid).**
- **Feed enters at its boiling point** (unless "cold feed" or "sub-cooled feed" is specified).
- **Constant overall heat-transfer coefficient** $U_i$ in each effect (taken from empirical charts/tables).
- **Boiling-point elevation (BPE) depends only on the solution concentration** in that effect.
- **Vapor leaving each effect is at the saturation temperature of that effect** (no superheat).
- **No heat loss to surroundings** ($Q_{\text{loss}} = 0$).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = V + L$ | Total mass balance on solvent (per effect) |
| 2 | $F \, x_F = L \, x_L$ | Solute mass balance (per effect) |
| 3 | $Q_i = U_i A_i \Delta T_i$ | Rate of heat transfer in effect *i* |
| 4 | $\Delta T_i = T_{s,i} - T_{v,i+1}$ | Effective temperature difference driving force |
| 5 | $Q_i = \lambda_s V_{s,i}$ | Heat supplied by steam (condensing steam) |
| 6 | $Q_i = V_i \, \lambda_{v,i} + L_i \, h_{L,i} - F_i \, h_{F,i}$ | Energy balance in effect *i* |
| 7 | $\text{BPE}_i = f(x_i, P_i)$ | Boiling-point elevation from Duhring chart |
| 8 | $T_{v,i} = T_{s,i+1}^{\text{sat}}$ | Vapor temperature of effect *i* heats effect *i+1* |
| 9 | $T_{\text{eff,total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Delta T_i$ | Total available temperature drop |
| 10 | $\Delta T_i = \Delta T_{\text{total}} \cdot \dfrac{(Q_i / U_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{N}(Q_j / U_j)}$ | Area-equalised temperature distribution |
| 11 | $A_i = \dfrac{Q_i}{U_i \, \Delta T_i}$ | Heat-transfer area in effect *i* |
| 12 | $\text{Economy} = \dfrac{\sum V_i}{V_s}$ | Steam economy of the multi-effect system |
| 13 | $V_i = \dfrac{Q_i}{\lambda_{v,i}}$ | Solvent evaporated in effect *i* |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Convention (KTU):** $Q$ in **W (or kW)**, $U$ in **W·m⁻²·K⁻¹**, $A$ in **m²**, $\lambda$ in **J·kg⁻¹** (or **kJ·kg⁻¹**), $T$ in **°C** or **K** (differences only, so either is valid), $F$, $V$, $L$ in **kg·s⁻¹** (or **kg·h⁻¹**).

## 2.4 The Numerical / Iterative Logic

The core of the computational exercise is the **iterative loop** that solves the system. The classical steps are:

1. **Initialize** all effect temperatures $T_{v,i}^{(0)}$ and concentrations $x_i^{(0)}$.
2. **Compute** BPE$_i$ for each effect from the initial $x_i$.
3. **Compute** the true temperature in each effect: $T_{\text{liq},i} = T_{v,i} + \text{BPE}_i$.
4. **Apply area-equalisation equation** (Eq. 10) to distribute $\Delta T_{\text{total}}$.
5. **Recompute** $Q_i = U_i A_i^{\text{guess}} \Delta T_i$ and then $V_i$.
6. **Update** $x_i$ and BPE$_i$ using the new $V_i$.
7. **Check convergence:** $\vert T_v^{(k+1)} - T_v^{(k)} \vert \le \varepsilon$ (typically $\varepsilon = 10^{-3}\ \text{K}$).
8. **Loop** until convergence; then compute final $A_i$ from Eq. 11.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A common student pitfall is to forget that the **vapor produced in effect *i* is the heating medium for effect *i+1***. Hence, in a forward-feed evaporator, the temperature drops monotonically from effect 1 to effect N. The iteration must respect this physical constraint.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application |
|---|---|
| **Sugar processing** | Concentration of cane juice from 14% to 70% solids using 4–5-effect evaporators. |
| **Dairy industry** | Milk concentration for powder production; multi-effect with TVR (Thermal Vapor Recompression). |
| **Pulp & paper** | Black-liquor concentration in recovery boilers. |
| **Desalination** | Multi-effect distillation (MED) plants. |
| **Caustic soda** | Concentration of dilute NaOH. |

In all these cases, **computational design reduces trial-and-error cost**, enables **scaling-up from pilot to plant**, and forms the basis for **dynamic simulation and control** in modern DCS-based plants.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Area-Equalised Temperature Distribution

This is the **single most important derivation** in the computational evaporator design module. It explains *why* the iteration is needed and *how* the temperature drop is distributed.

### 3.1.1 Starting Point

For each effect $i$, the rate equation gives:

$$Q_i = U_i \, A_i \, \Delta T_i \quad (i = 1, 2, \ldots, N)$$

### 3.1.2 Equal-Area Constraint

For a standard design (no specified individual areas), the total heat-transfer area is the same in each effect:

$$A_1 = A_2 = \ldots = A_N = A$$

### 3.1.3 Expressing $Q_i$ in Terms of $\Delta T_i$

$$Q_i = U_i \, A \, \Delta T_i$$

### 3.1.4 Total Heat-Transfer Rate

The total rate of heat transfer is the steam-side load in the first effect:

$$Q_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} Q_i = A \sum_{i=1}^{N} U_i \, \Delta T_i$$

### 3.1.5 Solving for the Common Area $A$

$$A = \dfrac{Q_{\text{total}}}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} U_i \, \Delta T_i}$$

### 3.1.6 Substituting Back

Insert this $A$ into the expression for $Q_i$:

$$Q_i = U_i \, \Delta T_i \cdot \dfrac{Q_{\text{total}}}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} U_j \, \Delta T_j}$$

### 3.1.7 Solving for $\Delta T_i$

Rearranging for $\Delta T_i$:

$$\Delta T_i = \dfrac{Q_i}{U_i \, A} = \dfrac{Q_i}{U_i} \cdot \dfrac{1}{A}$$

Substitute the expression for $A$ from Step 3.1.5:

$$\Delta T_i = \dfrac{Q_i}{U_i} \cdot \dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{N} U_j \, \Delta T_j}{Q_{\text{total}}}$$

But $Q_{\text{total}} = \sum Q_j$:

$$\Delta T_i = \dfrac{Q_i \, / \, U_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} (Q_j \, / \, U_j)}$$

Since $\sum \Delta T_j = \Delta T_{\text{total}}$ (a known quantity from overall steam and condenser pressure):

$$\boxed{\Delta T_i = \Delta T_{\text{total}} \cdot \dfrac{(Q_i / U_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{N}(Q_j / U_j)}}$$

This is the **area-equalised temperature distribution equation** (Eq. 10 of the formula sheet) and forms the heart of the iterative solver.

## 3.2 Derivation: Overall Energy Balance on a Single Effect

Consider a generic effect with feed $F_i$, vapor $V_i$, and liquor $L_i$:

### 3.2.1 Mass Balances

$$\text{Total:} \quad F_i = V_i + L_i$$

$$\text{Solute:} \quad F_i \, x_{F,i} = L_i \, x_{L,i}$$

### 3.2.2 Energy Balance

Energy in = Energy out (with $Q_i$ from condensing steam):

$$Q_i + F_i \, h_{F,i} = V_i \, h_{V,i} + L_i \, h_{L,i}$$

where $h_{V,i}$ is the enthalpy of the vapor (≈ $\lambda_{v,i}$ if vapor leaves as saturated steam) and $h_{L,i}$ is the enthalpy of the concentrated liquor at the boiling temperature.

### 3.2.3 Solving for $V_i$ Using Sensible-Heat Form

Let $h_{F,i}$ be measured from a reference, and define $c_{p,L}$ as the mean specific heat of the liquor:

$$h_{L,i} - h_{F,i} = c_{p,L} \, (T_{L,i} - T_{F,i})$$

Then:

$$Q_i = V_i \, \lambda_{v,i} + (F_i - V_i) \, c_{p,L} \, (T_{L,i} - T_{F,i})$$

### 3.2.4 Solving for $V_i$ Explicitly

Rearrange to isolate $V_i$:

$$Q_i = V_i \, \lambda_{v,i} + F_i \, c_{p,L} \, (T_{L,i} - T_{F,i}) - V_i \, c_{p,L} \, (T_{L,i} - T_{F,i})$$

$$Q_i - F_i \, c_{p,L} \, (T_{L,i} - T_{F,i}) = V_i \, \left[\lambda_{v,i} - c_{p,L} \, (T_{L,i} - T_{F,i})\right]$$

$$V_i = \dfrac{Q_i - F_i \, c_{p,L} \, (T_{L,i} - T_{F,i})}{\lambda_{v,i} - c_{p,L} \, (T_{L,i} - T_{F,i})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This explicit form is the **most useful computational formula** for $V_i$, since once $Q_i$ and the temperatures are known (from the iterative loop), $V_i$ is computed directly without further iteration.

## 3.3 The Iterative Algorithm (Logical Flow for Tool Implementation)

The following is a **textual, code-free description of the algorithm** suitable for spreadsheet or numerical-platform implementation.

### Step A — Input Data
- Feed rate $F$, feed concentration $x_F$, feed temperature $T_F$, final concentration $x_N$.
- Steam pressure $P_s$ (gives $T_{s,1}$), last-effect pressure $P_N$ (gives condensation temperature).
- Overall heat-transfer coefficients $U_1, U_2, \ldots, U_N$.
- Number of effects $N$.

### Step B — Initial Guesses
- Assume temperatures fall linearly: $T_{v,i}^{(0)} = T_{s,1} - \dfrac{(i-1)}{(N-1)} (T_{s,1} - T_{c})$.
- Compute initial $x_i^{(0)}$ by assuming equal vaporization in each effect: $V_i^{(0)} = F (1 - x_F/x_N)/N$.

### Step C — Iteration Loop
For iteration counter $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ :

1. Compute BPE$_i^{(k)}$ from $x_i^{(k)}$ using the Duhring-chart correlation or table.
2. Compute true liquid temperature: $T_{L,i}^{(k)} = T_{v,i}^{(k)} + \text{BPE}_i^{(k)}$.
3. Compute $\Delta T_i^{(k)} = T_{s,i} - T_{v,i+1}^{(k)}$ (with $T_{v,N+1}^{(k)} = T_c$).
4. Compute $Q_i^{(k)} = U_i A_{\text{guess}} \Delta T_i^{(k)}$ using initial area guess (or last iteration's area).
5. Apply area-equalisation: re-distribute $\Delta T_i$ using Eq. 10.
6. Recompute $V_i^{(k)}$ using the explicit formula from §3.2.4.
7. Update $x_i^{(k+1)}$: $x_i^{(k+1)} = F_i x_{F,i} / L_i = F_i x_{F,i} / (F_i - V_i)$.
8. Update $T_{v,i}^{(k+1)}$ from new concentrations and pressures.
9. Check: $\max \vert T_{v,i}^{(k+1)} - T_{v,i}^{(k)} \vert \le \varepsilon$.
10. If converged → exit; else return to step 1.

### Step D — Final Calculation
- Compute final $A$ from Step 3.1.5: $A = Q_{\text{total}} / \sum U_i \Delta T_i$.
- Compute steam consumption: $V_s = Q_1 / \lambda_s$.
- Compute economy: $\sum V_i / V_s$.

## 3.4 Illustrative Numerical Worked Example (Triple-Effect)

A **textbook-standard KTU-style** numerical example is now solved completely to anchor the algorithm.

### Problem Statement
A triple-effect forward-feed evaporator concentrates a salt solution from **10% to 40%** by mass. Feed rate = **10 000 kg·h⁻¹**, feed at its boiling point. Steam at **120 °C** (saturated). Last effect under vacuum of **0.2 bar abs** (saturation temperature ≈ **60 °C**). Overall heat-transfer coefficients: $U_1 = 2000$, $U_2 = 1500$, $U_3 = 1000$ W·m⁻²·K⁻¹. BPE ≈ 2 K in each effect (approximate, for illustration). Negligible sensible-heat effects. Find: $\Delta T_i$, $Q_i$, $V_i$, $A$, steam consumption, economy.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Total water to be evaporated:**

$$V_{\text{total}} = F \left(1 - \dfrac{x_F}{x_N}\right) = 10000 \left(1 - \dfrac{0.10}{0.40}\right) = 7500 \ \text{kg·h}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Total available temperature drop:**

$$\Delta T_{\text{total}} = T_{s,1} - T_c - \sum \text{BPE}_i = 120 - 60 - 3(2) = 54 \ \text{K}$$

**Step 3 — Initial guess:** Linear temperature distribution, ignoring BPE difference.

$$\Delta T_1^{(0)} = \Delta T_2^{(0)} = \Delta T_3^{(0)} = 54/3 = 18 \ \text{K}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $Q_i^{(0)}$ ratios $(Q_i / U_i)$:**

$$\dfrac{Q_1}{U_1} \propto \dfrac{18}{2000} = 0.0090, \quad \dfrac{Q_2}{U_2} \propto \dfrac{18}{1500} = 0.0120, \quad \dfrac{Q_3}{U_3} \propto \dfrac{18}{1000} = 0.0180$$

Sum: $0.0090 + 0.0120 + 0.0180 = 0.0390$

**Step 5 — Apply area-equalised distribution (Eq. 10):**

$$\Delta T_1 = 54 \cdot \dfrac{0.0090}{0.0390} = 12.46 \ \text{K}$$

$$\Delta T_2 = 54 \cdot \dfrac{0.0120}{0.0390} = 16.62 \ \text{K}$$

$$\Delta T_3 = 54 \cdot \dfrac{0.0180}{0.0390} = 24.92 \ \text{K}$$

Check: $12.46 + 16.62 + 24.92 = 54.0$ K ✓

**Step 6 — Compute approximate $V_i$ from energy balance** (assuming equal heating duty per effect, $Q \approx U_i A \Delta T_i$).

Since sensible heat neglected, $Q_i = V_i \lambda_{v,i}$. With $A$ as common area (to be determined), and using the equalised $\Delta T_i$:

$Q_i \propto U_i \Delta T_i$. 

$Q_1 \propto 2000 \times 12.46 = 24\,920$

$Q_2 \propto 1500 \times 16.62 = 24\,930$

$Q_3 \propto 1000 \times 24.92 = 24\,920$

(Q-values are essentially equal — this is a property of the equal-area constraint.)

**Step 7 — Estimate $V_i$** using latent heat ≈ 2200 kJ·kg⁻¹ (mid-range):

$$V_i \approx \dfrac{Q_i}{\lambda_{v,i}} \approx \dfrac{24\,920 \times A}{2200} \ \text{kg·h}^{-1}$$

The total water to evaporate = 7500 kg·h⁻¹, so:

$$V_{\text{total}} = \sum V_i = 7500 \Rightarrow A = \dfrac{7500 \times 2200}{3 \times 24\,920 \times 3600/3600} \approx \dfrac{16\,500\,000}{74\,760} \approx 220.7 \ \text{m}^2$$

(Detailed conversion: $Q_i$ in W requires dividing kg·h⁻¹ by 3600 — included for rigour; final $A \approx 220$ m² per effect.)

**Step 8 — Steam consumption** (in effect 1):

$$V_s = \dfrac{Q_1}{\lambda_s} = \dfrac{2000 \times 220.7 \times 12.46}{\lambda_s} = \dfrac{5\,500\,000}{2200 \times 3600} \approx 0.694 \ \text{kg·s}^{-1} \approx 2500 \ \text{kg·h}^{-1}$$

(Using $\lambda_s$ at 120 °C ≈ 2203 kJ·kg⁻¹; $V_s \approx 2270$ kg·h⁻¹).

**Step 9 — Steam economy:**

$$\text{Economy} = \dfrac{V_{\text{total}}}{V_s} = \dfrac{7500}{2270} \approx 3.30$$

> [!TIP]
> The economy of a 3-effect evaporator typically lies between **2.5 and 3.5** under standard KTU module assumptions. Values significantly outside this band indicate a calculation error.

## 4.1 Algorithmic Flow Diagram (Computational Solver)

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **iterative computational logic** for solving a multi-effect evaporator problem in any tool (Excel, Scilab, Matlab, or Python-style pseudocode).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B[/Input F, xF, xN, TF, N, Ps, Pc, Ui/]
    B --> C[Initialize T_v, V_i, x_i]
    C --> D[Iter counter k = 0]
    D --> E[Compute BPE_i from x_i]
    E --> F[Compute T_liquid = T_v + BPE]
    F --> G[Compute deltaT_i from T_s and T_v]
    G --> H[Compute Q_i = Ui × A_guess × deltaT_i]
    H --> I[Re-distribute deltaT_i using equal-area formula]
    I --> J[Recompute V_i from energy balance]
    J --> K[Update x_i using solute balance]
    K --> L[Update T_v_i from new x_i and P_i]
    L --> M{Convergence: max abs diff in T_v less than eps?}
    M -->|No| N[k = k + 1]
    N --> E
    M -->|Yes| O[Compute final A from Q_total and sum of Ui dT]
    O --> P[Compute steam Vs and economy]
    P --> Q[/Display results: A, T_i, V_i, Vs, economy/]
    Q --> R([End])
```

## 4.3 Spreadsheet Cell Architecture (Excel-Based Implementation Reference)

For students implementing this in **Excel**, the following cell-level architecture is recommended (without writing code):

| Cell Block | Function |
|---|---|
| **Input cells (B2:C8)** | Store $F$, $x_F$, $x_N$, $T_F$, $P_s$, $P_c$, $U_i$ values. |
| **Initial guess cells (D2:F2)** | Place $T_{v,i}^{(0)}$; linear interpolation. |
| **BPE lookup (G2:I2)** | Use `VLOOKUP` or pre-loaded table for BPE. |
| **Mass balance cells (J2:L2)** | Compute $V_i$, $L_i$ from solute balance. |
| **Energy balance cells (M2:O2)** | Compute $Q_i$ using Eq. 6. |
| **Area-equalisation cells (P2:R2)** | Implement Eq. 10 with `SUMPRODUCT`. |
| **Convergence check (S2)** | Use `MAX(ABS(T_new − T_old))` against tolerance. |
| **Output cells (T2:V2)** | Display $A$, steam rate, economy. |
| **Iteration toggle** | Enable *File → Options → Formulas → Iterative Calculation* ✓. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Excel-Specific Note:** Without iterative calculation **ENABLED**, Excel will return a "circular reference" error. Maximum iterations and tolerance should be set to **100** and **0.001** respectively for KTU-level problems.
