- **Single-Pass (1-1) Arrangement:** All channels of hot fluid are in parallel; all channels of cold fluid are in parallel. Adjacent channels flow in opposite directions → **true counter-current**.
- **Multi-Pass Arrangement:** Channels are grouped into passes using intermediate dividers, giving an **N-M** configuration (e.g., 2-4 means hot has 2 passes, cold has 4 passes). This is less efficient thermally because it mixes some co-current flow with counter-current.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pure counter-current operation is one of the BIGGEST advantages of plate exchangers over shell-and-tube.** It permits the cold fluid outlet temperature to **exceed** the hot fluid outlet temperature in true counter-current operation, which is impossible in a single-pass shell-and-tube exchanger.

## 2.3 Heat Transfer Area & Channel Geometry

For each plate of effective length **L_w** and width **W**:
$$A_{plate} = L_w \times W$$

Total heat transfer area of the exchanger:
$$A = N \times A_{plate}$$

where **N** is the total number of plates.

The cross-sectional flow area per channel is:
$$a_c = W \times b$$

where **b** is the inter-plate gap (channel depth), typically **2 mm to 5 mm**.

The **hydraulic diameter** of a chevron channel is approximately:
$$D_h = \frac{2b}{\sqrt{1 + (1.5 \tan\beta)^2 / 3}}$$

> **Key Insight:** A smaller channel gap **b** gives a smaller hydraulic diameter, which **increases** the Reynolds number at a given flow rate, boosting the heat transfer coefficient dramatically.

## 2.4 Heat Transfer Coefficient Correlations

The standard KTU Board correlation for the Nusselt number in chevron-angled PHE channels is:

$$Nu = C \cdot Re^n \cdot Pr^{1/3} \left(\frac{\mu}{\mu_w}\right)^{0.17}$$

Typical values of C and n depend on the chevron angle β:

| Chevron Angle (β) | C | n | Re Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| β ≤ 30° (low) | 0.718 | 0.349 | 10 < Re < 400 |
| 30° < β ≤ 60° (medium) | 0.360 | 0.310 | 10 < Re < 400 |
| β > 60° (high) | 0.300 | 0.263 | 10 < Re < 400 |
| β > 60° (high) | 0.180 | 0.235 | Re > 400 |

> [!NOTE]
> Higher chevron angle → lower exponent **n**, meaning the heat transfer coefficient becomes **less sensitive to flow rate** but provides a higher baseline value.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table — these are the HIGH-YIELD formulas that appear every KTU exam:**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of channels | $N_c = N - 1$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Heat transfer area | $A = N \times L_w \times W$ | m² |
| 3 | Channel flow area | $a_c = W \times b$ | m² |
| 4 | Hydraulic diameter | $D_h = 2b \;\vert\; \text{approx. for chevron channel}$ | m |
| 5 | Channel velocity | $u = \dot{m} / (\rho \times a_c)$ | m/s |
| 6 | Reynolds number | $Re = \rho u D_h / \mu$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Nusselt number | $Nu = h D_h / k$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | LMTD | $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | K or °C |
| 9 | LMTD with F-factor | $\Delta T_m = F \times \Delta T_{lm}$ | K or °C |
| 10 | Heat duty | $Q = U A \Delta T_m$ | W (or kW) |
| 11 | Heat capacity rate | $C = \dot{m} c_p$ | W/K |
| 12 | Capacity rate ratio | $C_r = C_{min} / C_{max}$ | dimensionless |
| 13 | NTU | $NTU = U A / C_{min}$ | dimensionless |
| 14 | Effectiveness (1-1 PHE) | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp\!\left[-NTU(1 + C_r)\right]}{1 + C_r}$ | dimensionless (counter-current, C_r ≤ 1) |
| 15 | Friction factor (Darcy) | $f = C_f \cdot Re^{n_f}$ | dimensionless |
| 16 | Pressure drop | $\Delta P = 4 f \dfrac{L_w}{D_h} \dfrac{\rho u^2}{2}$ | Pa |

> **Engineering Utility Note:** The F-factor correction is often **1.0 (or very close to 1)** for true 1-1 plate exchangers because of the inherent counter-current flow. This is a key *advantage* cited in KTU numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Number of Channels from Number of Plates

**Statement:** A plate heat exchanger with **N** plates has exactly **N − 1** channels.

**Step-by-step derivation:**

Consider plates stacked in series. Between any two adjacent plates, exactly one flow channel exists.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } N &= 1, \quad \text{then number of channels} = 0 \\
\text{If } N &= 2, \quad \text{then number of channels} = 1 \\
\text{If } N &= 3, \quad \text{then number of channels} = 2 \\
\vdots \\
\text{If } N &= N, \quad \text{then number of channels} = N - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Every additional plate added to the pack creates **one new channel** between it and its neighbour. The relationship is therefore **linear** with a slope of 1 and a y-intercept of −1.

$$
\boxed{N_c = N - 1}
$$

For a single-pass PHE where both fluids share channels equally:

$$
\boxed{N_{c,hot} = N_{c,cold} = \frac{N-1}{2}}
$$

> This is a recurring 2-mark derivation in KTU Module 4. **Memorize it.**

## 3.2 Derivation: Heat Transfer Area for a Plate Pack

Each plate contributes area equal to the effective wetted length times the effective width:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{plate} &= L_w \times W \\
A_{total} &= N \times A_{plate} \\
A_{total} &= N \times L_w \times W
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- **L_w** = effective length of one plate (m), typically **0.5 m to 2.0 m**
- **W** = effective width of one plate (m), typically **0.2 m to 1.0 m**
- **N** = total number of plates

## 3.3 Derivation: LMTD for 1-1 Counter-Current Plate Exchanger

**Setup:** Let hot fluid enter at $T_{h,i}$ and leave at $T_{h,o}$. Let cold fluid enter at $T_{c,i}$ and leave at $T_{c,o}$. In **counter-current** flow, the temperature difference at the two ends of the exchanger is:

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} \quad \text{(hot inlet end)}
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i} \quad \text{(hot outlet end)}
$$

The logarithmic mean temperature difference is:

$$
\boxed{\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}}
$$

**Derivation logic:** For a differential element dA, the rate of heat transfer is:

$$
dQ = U \, dA \, \Delta T
$$

The cold fluid absorbs heat:

$$
dQ = -C_c \, dT_c
$$

The hot fluid releases heat:

$$
dQ = C_h \, dT_h
$$

For counter-current flow, $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$, so:

$$
d(\Delta T) = dT_h - dT_c = -\frac{dQ}{C_h} - \left(-\frac{dQ}{C_c}\right) = -dQ \left(\frac{1}{C_h} - \frac{1}{C_c}\right)
$$

Rearranging and integrating from end 1 to end 2 yields the LMTD expression.

## 3.4 Derivation: Effectiveness for a 1-1 Plate Heat Exchanger

For a single-pass counter-current plate exchanger (assumed $C_{min} = C_c$, $C_{max} = C_h$):

$$
C_r = \frac{C_{min}}{C_{max}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= C_{min} \left( T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} \right) = C_{max} \left( T_{h,i} - T_{h,o} \right) \\
\varepsilon &= \frac{Q}{Q_{max}} = \frac{T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}}{T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}} = \frac{T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}}{T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}}
\end{aligned}
$$

After integrating the differential heat balance and applying boundary conditions:

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon = \frac{1 - \exp\!\left[-NTU\,(1 - C_r)\right]}{1 - C_r \cdot \exp\!\left[-NTU\,(1 - C_r)\right]}}
$$

For a **pure counter-current** exchanger (which the 1-1 PHE approximates):

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon = \frac{1 - \exp\!\left[-NTU\,(1 + C_r)\right]}{1 + C_r} \quad (C_r \leq 1)}
$$

The second form appears in KTU textbooks. For PHE problems where $C_r$ is small, a useful approximation is:

$$
\varepsilon \approx \frac{NTU}{1 + NTU}
$$

## 3.5 Complete Worked Example (Board-Marked Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical KTU Numerical (12–14 marks):**

**Problem:** A plate heat exchanger with **120 plates**, each of effective area **0.25 m²**, is used to condense steam at **120 °C** and preheat water from **30 °C to 80 °C**. The overall heat transfer coefficient is **U = 2500 W/(m²·K)**. The water flow rate is **3.0 kg/s** and $c_p = 4.18$ kJ/(kg·K). Determine: (a) the heat duty, (b) the LMTD, (c) the actual log-mean temperature difference using F-factor, (d) whether the exchanger is adequate.

### Solution:

**Step (a) — Heat Duty Q:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dot{m} \, c_p \, (T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}) \\
Q &= 3.0 \times 4180 \times (80 - 30) \\
Q &= 627{,}000 \; \text{W} = 627 \; \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (b) — LMTD Calculation:**

Steam condenses at constant temperature 120 °C. Cold water enters at 30 °C and leaves at 80 °C.

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} = 120 - 80 = 40 \; °\text{C}
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i} = 120 - 30 = 90 \; °\text{C}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_{lm} &= \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = \frac{40 - 90}{\ln(40/90)} \\
\Delta T_{lm} &= \frac{-50}{\ln(0.4444)} = \frac{-50}{-0.8109} \\
\Delta T_{lm} &= 61.66 \; °\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (c) — Correction Factor F:**

For a 1-1 PHE with one fluid undergoing phase change (condensation), $C_{max} \to \infty$ and $C_r = 0$. The F-factor simplifies to **F = 1** for all practical geometries. Therefore:

$$
\Delta T_m = F \times \Delta T_{lm} = 1 \times 61.66 = 61.66 \; °\text{C}
$$

**Step (d) — Required Area and Adequacy:**

$$
A_{available} = 120 \times 0.25 = 30 \; \text{m}^2
$$

$$
A_{required} = \frac{Q}{U \, \Delta T_m} = \frac{627{,}000}{2500 \times 61.66} = \frac{627{,}000}{154{,}150} = 4.07 \; \text{m}^2
$$

Since $A_{available} = 30 \; \text{m}^2 \gg A_{required} = 4.07 \; \text{m}^2$, **the exchanger is grossly over-sized**. The number of plates required can be reduced to:

$$
N_{required} = \frac{4.07}{0.25} = 16.3 \Rightarrow 17 \; \text{plates (with safety factor)}
$$

> **Engineering Conclusion:** The original 120-plate design is heavily overdesigned. Approximately **17 plates** would suffice. The remaining capacity is reserve for future fouling.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Plate Heat Exchanger

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FRAME["FIXED PRESSURE PLATE & FRAME"]
        direction TB
        FP["Fixed Pressure Plate"]
        MP["Movable Pressure Plate"]
        TB1["Tie Bar 1"]
        TB2["Tie Bar 2"]
        TB3["Tie Bar 3"]
        GB1["Guide Bar Top"]
        GB2["Guide Bar Bottom"]
    end

    subgraph PACK["PLATE PACK ASSEMBLY"]
        direction LR
        P1["Plate 1 (Cold In)"]
        P2["Plate 2 (Hot Flow)"]
        P3["Plate 3 (Cold Flow)"]
        P4["Plate 4 (Hot Flow)"]
        P5["Plate 5 (Cold Flow)"]
        P6["Plate N (Hot In)"]
    end

    subgraph INLETS["FLUID CONNECTIONS"]
        HI["Hot Fluid Inlet Port"]
        HO["Hot Fluid Outlet Port"]
        CI["Cold Fluid Inlet Port"]
        CO["Cold Fluid Outlet Port"]
    end

    HI -- Hot enters -- P2
    P2 -- transfers heat -- P3
    P3 -- receives heat -- P4
    P4 -- continues flow -- P5
    P5 -- final channel -- P6
    P6 -- Hot exits -- HO

    CI -- Cold enters -- P1
    P1 -- Cold flows -- P3
    P3 -- Cold flows -- P5
    P5 -- Cold exits -- CO

    TB1 --- PACK
    TB2 --- PACK
    TB3 --- PACK
    GB1 --- PACK
    GB2 --- PACK

    FP --- PACK
    MP --- PACK
```

## 4.2 Internal Flow Pattern in a Single Channel Pair

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CHANNEL_A["HOT FLUID CHANNEL (Plate 2 and 4)"]
        direction TB
        H1["Hot In: 120 C"]
        H2["Corrugated Flow Path"]
        H3["Chevron Angle Beta"]
        H4["Hot Out: 119 C"]
        H1 --> H2 --> H3 --> H4
    end

    subgraph PLATE_WALL["HEAT TRANSFER PLATE (0.5 mm Stainless Steel)"]
        direction TB
        PW1["Thin Metal Wall"]
        PW2["k = 16 W per m K"]
        PW3["Thermal Resistance Rp"]
        PW1 --> PW2 --> PW3
    end

    subgraph CHANNEL_B["COLD FLUID CHANNEL (Plate 3 and 5)"]
        direction TB
        C1["Cold In: 30 C"]
        C2["Counter-Current Direction"]
        C3["Turbulent Flow"]
        C4["Cold Out: 80 C"]
        C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4
    end

    H4 -- Convective Heat Transfer -- PW1
    PW3 -- Convective Heat Transfer -- C1
```

## 4.3 Process Flow Diagram: Complete Plate Heat Exchanger System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Hot Fluid Source Tank"] --> B["Hot Inlet Pump"]
    B --> C["Hot Inlet Port of PHE"]
    C --> D["Plate Pack N Plates"]
    D --> E["Hot Outlet Port"]
    E --> F["Hot Fluid Receiver"]

    G["Cold Fluid Source Tank"] --> H["Cold Inlet Pump"]
    H --> I["Cold Inlet Port of PHE"]
    I --> J["Adjacent Channel in Plate Pack"]
    J --> K["Cold Outlet Port"]
    K --> L["Cold Fluid Receiver"]

    D -. Counter-Current Heat Transfer .-> J

    M["Control Unit"] -. monitors .-> D
    N["Pressure Gauges"] -. feedback .-> M
    O["Temperature Sensors"] -. feedback .-> M
```

## 4.4 Chevron Pattern Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Chevron Angle Beta"] --> B["Low Angle below 30 deg"]
    A --> C["Medium Angle 30 to 60 deg"]
    A --> D["High Angle above 60 deg"]

    B --> E["Gentle Turbulence"]
    B --> F["Low Pressure Drop"]
    B --> G["Suitable for Viscous Fluids"]

    C --> H["Balanced Turbulence"]
    C --> I["Moderate Pressure Drop"]
    C --> J["General Industrial Use"]

    D --> K["Strong Turbulence"]
    D --> L["High Heat Transfer Coeff"]
    D --> M["High Pressure Drop"]
    D --> N["Suitable for Clean Low Visc Fluids"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always mention the **chevron angle** in your answer when asked about the "design feature that promotes turbulence" in a PHE. This is a guaranteed 1- to 2-mark fetch.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

> Explain the construction and working of a plate heat exchanger with a neat sketch. List any **two advantages** over a shell-and-tube exchanger.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **plate heat exchanger (PHE)** consists of a series of **corrugated metal plates** (typically stainless steel) clamped together between a fixed and a movable pressure plate using **tie bars** and **guide bars**. Each plate is fitted with an **elastomeric gasket** that seals the plate and directs the two fluids into alternate flow channels.

The hot fluid enters through its dedicated port and flows through every alternate channel, while the cold fluid flows counter-currently through the remaining channels. Heat is transferred from the hot fluid to the cold fluid through the thin plate walls (typically 0.5–0.8 mm thick). The corrugations induce high turbulence, increasing the heat transfer coefficient significantly.

**Two advantages over shell-and-tube:**
1. **Higher heat transfer area per unit volume** (200–1000 m²/m³) → much more compact.
2. **True counter-current flow** is achievable in a single pass → higher thermal effectiveness.
3. *Bonus (optional):* Easy to add/remove plates for capacity changes; easy to clean.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

> A plate heat exchanger has **40 plates**, each of effective area **0.30 m²**. Determine the **number of channels** and the **total heat transfer area** if the exchanger is single-pass.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of channels: } N_c &= N - 1 = 40 - 1 = 39 \\
\text{Channels per fluid (1-1 arrangement): } N_{c,fluid} &= \frac{39}{2} = 19.5 \approx 20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total heat transfer area:**

$$
A_{total} = N \times A_{plate} = 40 \times 0.30 = 12.0 \; \text{m}^2
$$

> Note: In practice, with 39 channels one fluid gets 20 channels and the other gets 19, because 39 is odd.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

> **A plate heat exchanger is to be designed to cool 5 kg/s of oil ($c_p$ = 2.0 kJ/kg·K) from 90 °C to 50 °C using cooling water available at 25 °C. The water flow rate is 2.5 kg/s with $c_p$ = 4.18 kJ/kg·K. The overall heat transfer coefficient is U = 1500 W/(m²·K). Each plate has an effective area of 0.20 m². Use the LMTD method with F-factor = 0.95.**
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Calculate the heat duty, outlet temperature of water, and LMTD.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Determine the total number of plates required and the number of channels.

#### Part (a) — Model Solution [7 marks]

**Step 1: Heat duty from oil side**

$$
Q = \dot{m}_{oil} \times c_{p,oil} \times (T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) = 5.0 \times 2000 \times (90 - 50) = 400{,}000 \; \text{W} = 400 \; \text{kW}
$$

**Step 2: Outlet temperature of water**

$$
T_{c,o} = T_{c,i} + \frac{Q}{\dot{m}_w \, c_{p,w}} = 25 + \frac{400{,}000}{2.5 \times 4180} = 25 + 38.28 = 63.28 \; °\text{C}
$$

**Step 3: LMTD Calculation**

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} = 90 - 63.28 = 26.72 \; °\text{C}
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i} = 50 - 25 = 25.00 \; °\text{C}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{26.72 - 25.00}{\ln(26.72/25.00)} = \frac{1.72}{0.0666} = 25.83 \; °\text{C}
$$

**Step 4: Apply F-factor**

$$
\Delta T_m = F \times \Delta T_{lm} = 0.95 \times 25.83 = 24.54 \; °\text{C}
$$

#### Part (b) — Model Solution [7 marks]

**Step 5: Required heat transfer area**

$$
A_{req} = \frac{Q}{U \times \Delta T_m} = \frac{400{,}000}{1500 \times 24.54} = \frac{400{,}000}{36{,}810} = 10.87 \; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 6: Number of plates required**

$$
N = \frac{A_{req}}{A_{plate}} = \frac{10.87}{0.20} = 54.35 \Rightarrow 55 \; \text{plates}
$$

**Step 7: Number of channels**

$$
N_c = N - 1 = 55 - 1 = 54 \; \text{channels total}
$$

$$
N_{c,fluid} = \frac{54}{2} = 27 \; \text{channels per fluid}
$$

**Final Answer:** Use 55 plates; total channels = 54 (27 hot, 27 cold).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> **A plate heat exchanger handles 2.0 kg/s of hot fluid ($c_p$ = 2.5 kJ/kg·K) being cooled from 100 °C to 60 °C. The cold fluid has $C_{min} = 4000$ W/K and enters at 30 °C. Assume the exchanger is single-pass counter-current.**
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Calculate the heat capacity rates, the capacity rate ratio, and the NTU.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Calculate the effectiveness and the actual cold fluid outlet temperature.

#### Part (a) — Model Solution [7 marks]

**Step 1: Hot side heat capacity rate**

$$
C_h = \dot{m}_h \times c_{p,h} = 2.0 \times 2500 = 5000 \; \text{W/K}
$$

**Step 2: Cold side heat capacity rate**

$$
C_c = C_{min} = 4000 \; \text{W/K}
$$

**Step 3: Identify $C_{min}$ and $C_{max}$**

$$
C_{min} = 4000 \; \text{W/K} \quad (\text{cold side}), \quad C_{max} = 5000 \; \text{W/K} \quad (\text{hot side})
$$

**Step 4: Capacity rate ratio**

$$
C_r = \frac{C_{min}}{C_{max}} = \frac{4000}{5000} = 0.8
$$

**Step 5: Heat transfer area (assume 80 plates of 0.25 m² each)**

$$
A = 80 \times 0.25 = 20 \; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 6: Overall U (assume given U = 2000 W/m²·K)**

$$
NTU = \frac{U \times A}{C_{min}} = \frac{2000 \times 20}{4000} = 10
$$

#### Part (b) — Model Solution [7 marks]

**Step 7: Effectiveness for counter-current flow**

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{1 - \exp\!\left[-NTU\,(1 - C_r)\right]}{1 - C_r \cdot \exp\!\left[-NTU\,(1 - C_r)\right]}
$$

$$
NTU \times (1 - C_r) = 10 \times (1 - 0.8) = 10 \times 0.2 = 2
$$

$$
\exp(-2) = 0.1353
$$

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{1 - 0.1353}{1 - 0.8 \times 0.1353} = \frac{0.8647}{1 - 0.1083} = \frac{0.8647}{0.8917} = 0.9697
$$

**Step 8: Actual heat transferred**

$$
Q_{actual} = \varepsilon \times C_{min} \times (T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}) = 0.9697 \times 4000 \times (100 - 30) = 271{,}516 \; \text{W}
$$

**Step 9: Cold fluid outlet temperature**

$$
T_{c,o} = T_{c,i} + \frac{Q_{actual}}{C_{min}} = 30 + \frac{271{,}516}{4000} = 30 + 67.88 = 97.88 \; °\text{C}
$$

**Engineering Observation:** The cold fluid leaves at **97.88 °C**, which is **higher** than the hot fluid outlet temperature of 60 °C. This is the **classic advantage of true counter-current flow** that is uniquely possible in plate heat exchangers and shell-and-tube exchangers with multiple passes.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Channel counting error:** Writing **N** channels instead of **N − 1** is the most common mistake. Always subtract 1.
> 2. **Forgetting the F-factor:** Even when a 1-1 PHE has F ≈ 1, the problem statement may give a slightly different F (e.g., 0.95). Always **multiply** the LMTD by F.
> 3. **Confusing parallel and counter-current formulas:** The effectiveness formula for **counter-current** differs from the parallel-flow formula. Use the **counter-current** formula for plate exchangers unless explicitly told otherwise.
> 4. **Rounding 0.5 to 1 wrongly:** When the number of channels is odd (e.g., 39), the distribution is **20 + 19**, not 19.5 + 19.5. State this explicitly.
> 5. **Unit mismatch:** Always convert kW to W (multiply by 1000) when calculating area using U in W/m²·K. Conversely, if U is in kW/m²·K, keep Q in kW.
> 6. **Skipping the gaskets note:** A full-mark answer must mention the role of **gaskets** and **chevron corrugations** in the construction description.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Plate-Type Heat Exchangers)**

- **Definition:** Plate heat exchanger = thin corrugated plates clamped in a frame, alternating hot and cold channels separated by gaskets.
- **Components:** Pressure plates, tie bars, guide bars, gasketed plate pack, inlet/outlet ports.
- **Number of channels rule:** $N_c = N - 1$. For 1-1 arrangement, channels per fluid = $(N-1)/2$.
- **Heat transfer area:** $A = N \times L_w \times W$ where $L_w$ is effective length and $W$ is effective width of one plate.
- **Channel velocity:** $u = \dot{m} / (\rho \times W \times b)$, where $b$ = inter-plate gap.
- **Hydraulic diameter:** Approximately $2b$ for chevron channels (with correction factor involving $\tan\beta$).
- **Nusselt correlation:** $Nu = C \cdot Re^n \cdot Pr^{1/3} \cdot (\mu/\mu_w)^{0.17}$ — values of C and n depend on chevron angle.
- **Chevron angle effect:** Higher β → higher baseline $h$ → higher pressure drop. β = 30°–60° is the **industrial sweet spot**.
- **LMTD formula:** $\Delta T_{lm} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2) / \ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)$.
- **F-factor for 1-1 PHE:** Usually **F ≈ 1** because of inherent counter-current flow. When given, **always apply** it: $\Delta T_m = F \times \Delta T_{lm}$.
- **Counter-current effectiveness:** $\varepsilon = [1 - \exp(-NTU(1-C_r))] / [1 - C_r \exp(-NTU(1-C_r))]$.
- **True counter-current advantage:** $T_{c,out}$ can **exceed** $T_{h,out}$ — impossible in single-pass shell-and-tube.
- **Pressure drop:** $\Delta P = 4 f (L_w / D_h) (\rho u^2 / 2)$, where $f = C_f \cdot Re^{n_f}$.
- **Advantages over shell-and-tube:** Compactness, high area density (200–1000 m²/m³), easy maintenance, true counter-current in single pass, easily expandable.
- **Disadvantages:** Gasket limitations (temperature, pressure, chemical compatibility), lower pressure rating (~25 bar typical), not suitable for very high temperature or two-phase mixtures with large pressure drops.
- **Design procedure:** (i) Calculate Q from energy balance, (ii) Calculate $\Delta T_{lm}$ and apply F, (iii) Calculate $A = Q/(U \Delta T_m)$, (iv) Calculate $N = A / A_{plate}$, (v) Calculate $N_c = N - 1$, (vi) Verify pressure drop is acceptable.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Extended Surface Heat Exchangers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Extended Surface Heat Exchangers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Extended Surface Heat Exchanger:** An extended surface heat exchanger is a compact heat transfer device that incorporates **fins (extended surfaces)** on either the hot or cold fluid side (or both) to deliberately increase the effective heat transfer area ($A_s$) beyond the bare primary surface area. By doing so, the overall thermal conductance ($UA$) is enhanced, allowing higher heat duties ($Q$) to be achieved within the same volumetric envelope.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, an extended surface is defined as a **secondary surface** attached to a primary heat-transfer wall to augment the rate of convective heat transfer between the wall and an adjoining fluid when the convective heat transfer coefficient on that side is significantly lower than on the other side.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — The Motorcycle Engine Fins:**
> Imagine the engine cylinder of an old Royal Enfield motorcycle. The cylinder itself (the primary surface) is too small in area to dump heat into the passing air fast enough — the engine would overheat. So, engineers bolt on **corrugated metal fins** all around the cylinder. These fins don't generate heat, but they provide thousands of square centimetres of extra surface area for cool air to brush against. The heat from the cylinder flows *out through the fin material* by conduction and then gets blown away by convection. This is exactly what an extended surface heat exchanger does — it provides a "highway" for heat to escape into the weaker fluid stream.

The same engineering principle powers:
- **Refrigerator condenser coils** (finned tubes with air blown across).
- **Air-cooled engines, transformers, and CPU heat sinks** (pin fins on a flat base).
- **Economisers and air preheaters** in power plants (annular fins on boiler tubes).

### 1.3 When Are Extended Surfaces Necessary?

Fins are added to the side with the **lower convective heat transfer coefficient** ($h$). This is dictated by the thermal resistance network:

$$R_{tot} = \frac{1}{h_i A_i} + \frac{\Delta x}{k A_w} + \frac{1}{h_o A_o}$$

If $h_o \ll h_i$, then $\frac{1}{h_o A_o}$ dominates, and increasing $A_o$ via fins drastically reduces $R_{tot}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** Forcing more area on the **high-$h$** side (e.g., a condensing steam side) is wasteful — the heat transfer coefficient is already so high that adding area gives little benefit. Always fin the **low-$h$** (gas-side) surface.

### 1.4 Types of Fins (Extended Surface Geometry)

| Fin Type | Geometry | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Straight Rectangular Fin** | Constant cross-section slab | Electronic cooling, flat walls |
| **Triangular Fin** | Linearly tapered slab | Convective heaters, transformer tanks |
| **Pin (Cylindrical) Fin** | Circular cross-section rod | CPU heat sinks, nuclear fuel rods |
| **Annular Fin** | Disc surrounding a tube | Shell-and-tube exchangers, condensers |
| **Pin-Fin Array** | Cluster of pin fins on a plate | Gas turbine blade cooling |

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Thermal conductivity** of fin material, $k$ — typically **Aluminium ($k \approx 205 \;\mathrm{W/m\cdot K}$)** or **Copper ($k \approx 385 \;\mathrm{W/m\cdot K}$)**.
- **Convective heat transfer coefficient**, $h$ — typically **$5\text{–}250 \;\mathrm{W/m^2\cdot K}$** for gases; **$500\text{–}20{,}000 \;\mathrm{W/m^2\cdot K}$** for condensing/vapourising liquids.
- **Fin parameter (fin modulus)** $m = \sqrt{\dfrac{hP}{kA_c}}$ — has units of $\mathrm{m^{-1}}$; the inverse $1/m$ is the *fin characteristic length*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Governing Fin Differential Equation

Consider a one-dimensional fin of uniform cross-section $A_c$, perimeter $P$, exposed to a fluid at bulk temperature $T_\infty$. The base is maintained at $T_b$. An energy balance on a differential element $dx$ yields:

- Heat in at $x$: $q_x$
- Heat out at $x+dx$: $q_x + \dfrac{dq_x}{dx}dx$
- Heat lost by convection from sides: $h P \,(T - T_\infty)\,dx$

Steady-state balance:

$$\frac{dq_x}{dx} = h P \,(T - T_\infty)$$

Substituting Fourier's law $q_x = -k A_c \dfrac{dT}{dx}$:

$$\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(-k A_c \frac{dT}{dx}\right) = hP\,(T - T_\infty)$$

Assuming constant $k$, $A_c$, $P$, $h$:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{d^2 \theta}{dx^2} - m^2 \theta = 0\;}$$

where $\theta(x) = T(x) - T_\infty$ is the **temperature excess**, and

$$m^2 = \frac{hP}{k A_c}$$

### 2.2 General Solution of the Fin Equation

The general solution is:

$$\theta(x) = C_1 e^{mx} + C_2 e^{-mx}$$

### 2.3 The Four Canonical Boundary Cases

| Case | Tip Condition | Constants $C_1,\,C_2$ | Temperature Distribution $\theta(x)/\theta_b$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A — Long Fin (Infinitely Long)** | $\theta(L)\to 0$ | $C_1=0$ | $e^{-mx}$ |
| **B — Insulated Tip** | $\dfrac{d\theta}{dx}\big\vert_{L}=0$ | $C_1,C_2$ symmetric | $\dfrac{\cosh m(L-x)}{\cosh mL}$ |
| **C — Fin of Finite Length, Convection at Tip** | $-k\dfrac{d\theta}{dx}\big\vert_L = h\,\theta(L)$ | Mixed | $\dfrac{\cosh m(L-x) + (h/mk)\sinh m(L-x)}{\cosh mL + (h/mk)\sinh mL}$ |
| **D — Fixed Temperature at Tip** | $\theta(L)=\theta_L$ | Both nonzero | Linear-combination form |

### 2.4 Heat Transfer Rate from a Fin

For each case, the heat dissipated $q_f$ is evaluated at the base:

$$q_f = -k A_c \left.\frac{d\theta}{dx}\right|_{x=0}$$

- **Long fin:** $\;q_f = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b$
- **Insulated tip:** $\;q_f = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b\,\tanh(mL)$
- **Convection tip:** $\;q_f = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b\,\dfrac{\tanh mL + (h/mk)}{1 + (h/mk)\tanh mL}$

### 2.5 Fin Efficiency $\eta_f$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Board Examiner's Wording):** *Fin efficiency is the ratio of the actual heat transfer rate through the fin to the heat that would be transferred if the entire fin surface were at the base temperature.*

$$\boxed{\;\eta_f = \frac{q_f}{h\,A_s\,\theta_b} = \frac{q_f}{h\,P L\,\theta_b}\;}$$

For an insulated tip fin:

$$\boxed{\;\eta_f = \frac{\tanh(mL)}{mL}\;}$$

For a convection tip fin:

$$\eta_f = \frac{\tanh mL + (h/mk)}{mL\left[\,1 + (h/mk)\tanh mL\,\right]}$$

### 2.6 Fin Effectiveness $\varepsilon_f$

$$\boxed{\;\varepsilon_f = \frac{q_f}{h A_c \theta_b} = \sqrt{\frac{kP}{hA_c}}\;\tanh(mL)\;}$$

For an insulated tip rectangular fin ($P = 2(w+t)$, $A_c = wt$):

$$\varepsilon_f = \sqrt{\frac{2k(w+t)}{h\,w\,t}}\;\tanh(mL)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Rule of Thumb:** A fin is considered *worth adding* if $\varepsilon_f \geq 2$. Anything below 1.5 wastes material and pressure drop.

### 2.7 Overall Surface Efficiency $\eta_o$ (Finned Surface)

When only part of a heat exchanger wall is finned, define:

$$\eta_o = 1 - \frac{A_f}{A_t}\,(1 - \eta_f)$$

where $A_f$ = finned area, $A_t$ = total (finned + unfinned bare) area.

### 2.8 Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient for Finned Exchangers

$$U_o = \frac{1}{\dfrac{A_o}{h_i A_i} + \dfrac{A_o}{kA_w} + \dfrac{1}{\eta_o h_o}}$$

### 2.9 Corrected LMTD for Cross-Flow (Both Fluids Unmixed)

When a finned cross-flow exchanger has neither fluid passing back and forth, the simple LMTD under-predicts the true mean temperature difference. The correction factor $F$ depends on $P$ and $R$:

$$P = \frac{T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}}{T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}}, \qquad R = \frac{T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}}{T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}}$$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta T_{m,\text{corrected}} = F \cdot \Delta T_{lm}\;}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Board Pitfall:** If $F < 0.75$, the heat exchanger is thermodynamically infeasible for the chosen flow arrangement — you must re-specify the unit.

### 2.10 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Units |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m = \sqrt{hP/kA_c}$ | Fin parameter $[\mathrm{m^{-1}}]$ |
| 2 | $\theta(x)/\theta_b = \dfrac{\cosh m(L-x)}{\cosh mL}$ | Insulated-tip rectangular fin |
| 3 | $q_f = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b\,\tanh(mL)$ | Heat dissipation, insulated tip $[\mathrm{W}]$ |
| 4 | $\eta_f = \dfrac{\tanh(mL)}{mL}$ | Insulated-tip fin efficiency (dimensionless) |
| 5 | $\eta_o = 1 - \dfrac{A_f}{A_t}(1-\eta_f)$ | Overall surface efficiency |
| 6 | $\varepsilon_f = \dfrac{q_f}{hA_c \theta_b}$ | Fin effectiveness |
| 7 | $\dfrac{1}{UA_o} = \dfrac{1}{\eta_o h_o A_o} + R_{wall} + \dfrac{1}{h_i A_i}$ | Composite resistance for finned side |
| 8 | $\Delta T_{m,corr} = F\,\Delta T_{lm}$ | Corrected LMTD for cross-flow |
| 9 | $q_{f,\text{bare}} = hA_b\theta_b$ | Heat transfer if no fin existed |
| 10 | $A_s = PL + A_t$ | Total external surface area |

### 2.11 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compact automotive radiators:** pin/serpentine fins drop overall size by ~70 % versus bare tube radiators.
- **Cryogenic LNG heat exchangers:** brazed aluminium plate-fin cores achieve $\beta = 1000\text{–}5000\;\mathrm{m^2/m^3}$.
- **Air-conditioning evaporators/condensers:** $F$-factor cross-flow correction mandatory per ASHRAE.
- **Electronics thermal management:** fin optimisation governed by $\varepsilon_f \geq 2$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Insulated-Tip Rectangular Fin

#### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider a straight rectangular fin of width $w$, thickness $t$, and length $L$, attached to a primary wall of base temperature $T_b$. The fin is exposed to ambient fluid at $T_\infty$. Assume:
1. Steady state.
2. One-dimensional conduction ($T = T(x)$ only).
3. Constant material properties ($k$, $h$).
4. Uniform $h$ over the fin surface.
5. Negligible radiation.
6. Tip is **insulated** ($dq/dx|_L = 0$).

#### 3.1.2 Energy Balance on Element $dx$

Conduction in at $x$:

$$q_x = -k A_c \frac{dT}{dx}$$

Conduction in at $x + dx$:

$$q_{x+dx} = -k A_c \frac{dT}{dx}\bigg|_{x+dx}$$

Convection loss along $dx$:

$$d q_{conv} = h\,P\,(T - T_\infty)\,dx$$

Energy conservation: $q_x = q_{x+dx} + dq_{conv}$

$$-k A_c \frac{dT}{dx}\bigg|_{x} = -k A_c \frac{dT}{dx}\bigg|_{x+dx} + h P (T - T_\infty)\,dx$$

Rearrange:

$$k A_c \left[\frac{dT}{dx}\bigg|_{x+dx} - \frac{dT}{dx}\bigg|_{x}\right] = h P (T - T_\infty)\,dx$$

Divide by $dx$ and let $dx \to 0$:

$$k A_c \frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} = h P (T - T_\infty)$$

Define $\theta = T - T_\infty$. Because $T_\infty$ is constant, $d^2T/dx^2 = d^2\theta/dx^2$:

$$k A_c \frac{d^2 \theta}{dx^2} = h P \,\theta$$

Rearrange into canonical form:

$$\frac{d^2 \theta}{dx^2} - \frac{h P}{k A_c}\,\theta = 0$$

$$\boxed{\;\frac{d^2 \theta}{dx^2} - m^2 \theta = 0,\quad m^2 = \frac{hP}{kA_c}\;}$$

#### 3.1.3 Solution of the ODE

Characteristic equation: $r^2 - m^2 = 0 \Rightarrow r = \pm m$. Hence:

$$\theta(x) = C_1 e^{mx} + C_2 e^{-mx}$$

Equivalently in hyperbolic form:

$$\theta(x) = A \cosh(mx) + B \sinh(mx)$$

#### 3.1.4 Apply Boundary Conditions

- **BC 1:** Base temperature — at $x=0$, $\theta = \theta_b$.
  $$\theta_b = A \cosh(0) + B \sinh(0) = A \Rightarrow A = \theta_b$$
- **BC 2:** Insulated tip — at $x=L$, $\dfrac{d\theta}{dx}=0$.
  $$\left.\frac{d\theta}{dx}\right|_L = m\theta_b \sinh(mL) + mB \cosh(mL) = 0$$
  $$B = -\theta_b \tanh(mL)$$

#### 3.1.5 Final Temperature Distribution

$$\theta(x) = \theta_b \left[\cosh(mx) - \tanh(mL)\sinh(mx)\right]$$

Using the identity $\cosh(mx)\cosh(mL) - \sinh(mx)\sinh(mL) = \cosh\big(m(L-x)\big)$:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{\theta(x)}{\theta_b} = \frac{\cosh\big(m(L-x)\big)}{\cosh(mL)}\;}$$

#### 3.1.6 Heat Dissipation $q_f$

Heat dissipated = conduction at the base:

$$q_f = -k A_c \left.\frac{d\theta}{dx}\right|_{x=0} = -k A_c \,m \theta_b \sinh(mL)\big[0\big]\big/cosh(mL)\;\text{...}$$

Differentiating the expression:

$$\frac{d\theta}{dx} = -\theta_b \cdot m \cdot \frac{\sinh\big(m(L-x)\big)}{\cosh(mL)}$$

At $x = 0$:

$$\left.\frac{d\theta}{dx}\right|_{0} = -m\theta_b\,\frac{\sinh(mL)}{\cosh(mL)} = -m\theta_b\tanh(mL)$$

Therefore:

$$q_f = -k A_c \big(-m\theta_b\tanh(mL)\big) = k A_c m \theta_b \tanh(mL)$$

Substituting $m = \sqrt{hP/kA_c}$:

$$\boxed{\;q_f = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\;\theta_b \tanh(mL)\;}$$

#### 3.1.7 Fin Efficiency $\eta_f$

Total surface area $A_s = PL$ (insulated tip neglects $A_c$). Maximum possible heat = $hA_s\theta_b$:

$$\eta_f = \frac{q_f}{hA_s\theta_b} = \frac{\sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b\tanh(mL)}{hPL\theta_b} = \frac{\sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\tanh(mL)}{hPL}$$

Since $m = \sqrt{hP/kA_c}$, we have $\sqrt{hPkA_c} = m k A_c$. Also $hPL = m^2 k A_c L$. Hence:

$$\eta_f = \frac{m k A_c \tanh(mL)}{m^2 k A_c L} = \frac{\tanh(mL)}{mL}$$

$$\boxed{\;\eta_f = \frac{\tanh(mL)}{mL}\;}$$

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A straight rectangular aluminium fin ($k = 205 \;\mathrm{W/m\cdot K}$) of length $L = 25\;\mathrm{mm}$, thickness $t = 3\;\mathrm{mm}$, and width $w = 100\;\mathrm{mm}$ is attached to a wall at $T_b = 120\;^\circ\mathrm{C}$. It dissipates heat by natural convection to air at $T_\infty = 25\;^\circ\mathrm{C}$ with $h = 15\;\mathrm{W/m^2\cdot K}$. Assume insulated tip. Compute (i) $mL$, (ii) $\eta_f$, (iii) $q_f$, (iv) $\varepsilon_f$.

**Solution.**

Step 1: Geometry.

$$A_c = w t = 0.100 \times 0.003 = 3.0\times 10^{-4}\;\mathrm{m^2}$$

$$P = 2(w + t) = 2(0.100 + 0.003) = 0.206\;\mathrm{m}$$

Step 2: Fin parameter.

$$m = \sqrt{\frac{hP}{kA_c}} = \sqrt{\frac{15\times 0.206}{205\times 3.0\times 10^{-4}}}$$

$$m = \sqrt{\frac{3.09}{0.0615}} = \sqrt{50.244} = 7.088\;\mathrm{m^{-1}}$$

Step 3: $mL$.

$$mL = 7.088 \times 0.025 = 0.1772$$

Step 4: Fin efficiency.

$$\eta_f = \frac{\tanh(0.1772)}{0.1772} = \frac{0.1759}{0.1772} = 0.9925$$

Step 5: Heat dissipation.

$$\theta_b = 120 - 25 = 95\;\mathrm{K}$$

$$q_f = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\;\theta_b\tanh(mL)$$

$$\sqrt{hPkA_c} = \sqrt{15\times 0.206 \times 205 \times 3.0\times 10^{-4}} = \sqrt{1.896} = 1.377\;\mathrm{W/K}$$

$$q_f = 1.377 \times 95 \times 0.1759 = 23.01\;\mathrm{W}$$

Step 6: Fin effectiveness.

$$\varepsilon_f = \frac{q_f}{h A_c \theta_b} = \frac{23.01}{15\times 3.0\times 10^{-4}\times 95} = \frac{23.01}{0.4275} = 53.8$$

Since $\varepsilon_f \gg 2$, the fin is **highly effective**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

| Boundary Condition | Temperature Distribution $\theta/\theta_b$ | Heat Dissipation $q_f$ | Efficiency $\eta_f$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Long fin $(mL \to \infty)$ | $e^{-mx}$ | $\sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b$ | $\to 0$ |
| Insulated tip | $\cosh[m(L-x)]/\cosh(mL)$ | $\sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b\tanh(mL)$ | $\tanh(mL)/(mL)$ |
| Convection at tip | $\dfrac{\cosh m(L-x)+(h/mk)\sinh m(L-x)}{\cosh mL+(h/mk)\sinh mL}$ | $\sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b\,\dfrac{\tanh mL + h/mk}{1 + (h/mk)\tanh mL}$ | Approx. $\approx \dfrac{\tanh mL}{mL}\cdot\dfrac{1}{1 - 0.05/h}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Fin Heat-Transfer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hot Primary Surface at Tb] --> B[Fin Base at x=0]
    B --> C[Fin Body Region 0 to L]
    C --> D[Fin Tip Condition at x=L]
    D --> D1[Insulated dTdx=0]
    D --> D2[Convection h theta]
    D --> D3[Fixed Temperature TL]

    C --> E[Conduction along fin k Ac d2T/dx2]
    E --> F[Convection loss hP T minus Tinf]
    F --> G[Energy Balance yields ODE]
    G --> H[theta double prime minus m squared theta = 0]
    H --> I[Solution theta x = C1 emx + C2 e minus mx]
    I --> J[Apply BCs]
    J --> K[Compute qf base]
    K --> L[Compute eta f = qf over h As theta b]
    L --> M[Compute epsilon f]
    M --> N{Is epsilon f greater than 2?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Fin Justified]
    N -- No --> P[Remove or Redesign Fin]

    O --> Q[Compute eta o composite]
    Q --> R[Compute UA overall]
    R --> S[Apply LMTD with F factor]
    S --> T[Final Heat Duty Q]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cross-Flow Finned Heat Exchanger Functional Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Hot_Inlet
        HI[Hot Fluid Inlet Thi]
    end
    subgraph Hot_Outlet
        HO[Hot Fluid Outlet Tho]
    end
    subgraph Cold_Inlet
        CI[Cold Fluid Inlet Tci]
    end
    subgraph Cold_Outlet
        CO[Cold Fluid Outlet Tco]
    end

    HI --> X[Fin Tube Bank]
    X --> HO
    CI --> X
    X --> CO

    X --> Y[Calculate P and R]
    Y --> Z[Read F from chart]
    Z --> AA[Corrected LMTD F times delta T lm]
    AA --> AB[Use U from eta o h o]
    AB --> AC[Compute Q UA times delta T m corr]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Fin Effectiveness Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[New Heat Exchanger Design] --> side{Which side has lower h?}
    side -- Gas side --> finGas[Add fins to gas side]
    side -- Liquid side --> finLiq[Add fins to liquid side]
    side -- Both equal --> finNone[No fins needed]

    finGas --> mat[Choose fin material high k]
    mat --> geom[Choose fin geometry]
    geom --> rect[Rectangular Straight Fin]
    geom --> tri[Triangular Tapered Fin]
    geom --> pin[Pin Cylindrical Fin]
    geom --> ann[Annular Fin]

    rect --> calc[Compute m, eta f, epsilon f]
    tri --> calc
    pin --> calc
    ann --> calc

    calc --> check{epsilon f at least 2?}
    check -- Yes --> designOK[Approve design]
    check -- No --> redesign[Increase L or reduce thickness]
    redesign --> calc
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above render as functional architectural flowcharts. For physical fin geometry, sketch the standard rectangular cross-section with $A_c$ labelled and $P$ on the perimeter — these conventions are reproduced verbatim in KTU problem statements.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**  
Define *fin efficiency* and *fin effectiveness*. State the condition under which the addition of a fin is justified.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Fin efficiency** $\eta_f$ is the ratio of the heat actually transferred by the fin to the heat that would have been transferred if the entire fin surface were at the base temperature.
> $$\eta_f = \frac{q_f}{h A_s \theta_b}$$
> **Fin effectiveness** $\varepsilon_f$ is the ratio of the heat transferred by the fin to the heat that would be transferred by the same area of bare primary surface (i.e., without the fin).
> $$\varepsilon_f = \frac{q_f}{h A_c \theta_b}$$
> A fin is **justified** if $\varepsilon_f \geq 2$. This means the fin transfers at least twice as much heat as the bare wall area it displaces, justifying the added material cost and pressure drop.

*(3 marks — 1 mark each for the two definitions + 1 mark for the justifying condition.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
Explain the meaning of the **overall surface efficiency** $\eta_o$ for a finned surface. Why is it used instead of the individual fin efficiency?

**Model Answer:**

> Overall surface efficiency $\eta_o$ accounts for the fact that a finned tube bank has both finned area ($A_f$) and unfinned bare tube area ($A_b$). It is given by:
> $$\eta_o = 1 - \frac{A_f}{A_t}\,(1 - \eta_f)$$
> where $A_t = A_f + A_b$. It is used in the overall $UA$ calculation because it represents an *average* thermal performance of the entire composite surface, not just one isolated fin. Using a single $\eta_f$ overestimates heat transfer by ignoring the bare areas between fins, where the wall is at the base temperature.

*(3 marks — 1.5 marks for definition/equation + 1.5 marks for the explanation of necessity.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks). [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a)** Starting from the general fin differential equation, derive the temperature distribution and fin efficiency for a **straight rectangular fin with an insulated tip**. State clearly all the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A stainless-steel pin fin ($k = 16\;\mathrm{W/m\cdot K}$) of diameter $D = 8\;\mathrm{mm}$ and length $L = 40\;\mathrm{mm}$ is exposed to air at $T_\infty = 30\;^\circ\mathrm{C}$ with $h = 22\;\mathrm{W/m^2\cdot K}$. The fin base is at $T_b = 150\;^\circ\mathrm{C}$. Treating the tip as insulated, determine: **(i)** the fin parameter $m$, **(ii)** fin efficiency $\eta_f$, **(iii)** heat dissipated per fin, and **(iv)** fin effectiveness. Comment on whether the fin is justified. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution.**

**(a) Derivation** — *Reproduce in your answer script* the derivation from Section 3.1 of these notes:
1. Write the energy balance on element $dx$ and obtain $\dfrac{d^2\theta}{dx^2} - m^2\theta = 0$, where $m^2 = hP/kA_c$.
2. Solve: $\theta(x) = C_1 e^{mx} + C_2 e^{-mx}$.
3. Apply BCs: $\theta(0)=\theta_b$ and $d\theta/dx|_L = 0$.
4. Obtain $\theta(x)/\theta_b = \cosh[m(L-x)]/\cosh(mL)$.
5. Compute $q_f = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b\tanh(mL)$.
6. Derive $\eta_f = \tanh(mL)/(mL)$.

**Assumptions to list** (1 mark): 1-D steady conduction, constant $k$ and $h$, negligible radiation, uniform cross-section, no internal heat generation, convection only on external surfaces.

**(b) Numerical Computation.**

Given: $D = 0.008\;\mathrm{m}$, $L = 0.040\;\mathrm{m}$, $k = 16\;\mathrm{W/m\cdot K}$, $h = 22\;\mathrm{W/m^2\cdot K}$, $T_b = 150\;^\circ\mathrm{C}$, $T_\infty = 30\;^\circ\mathrm{C}$.

$$\theta_b = 150 - 30 = 120\;\mathrm{K}$$

Geometry of a pin fin:

$$A_c = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi(0.008)^2}{4} = 5.027\times 10^{-5}\;\mathrm{m^2}$$

$$P = \pi D = \pi(0.008) = 0.02513\;\mathrm{m}$$

**(i) Fin parameter $m$:**

$$m = \sqrt{\frac{hP}{kA_c}} = \sqrt{\frac{22 \times 0.02513}{16 \times 5.027\times 10^{-5}}}$$

$$m = \sqrt{\frac{0.5529}{8.043\times 10^{-4}}} = \sqrt{687.5} = 26.22\;\mathrm{m^{-1}}$$

**(ii) Fin efficiency:**

$$mL = 26.22 \times 0.040 = 1.049$$

$$\eta_f = \frac{\tanh(1.049)}{1.049} = \frac{0.7818}{1.049} = 0.7453 \;=\; 74.53\%$$

**(iii) Heat dissipated per fin:**

$$\sqrt{hPkA_c} = \sqrt{0.5529 \times 16 \times 5.027\times 10^{-5}} = \sqrt{4.448\times 10^{-4}} = 0.02109\;\mathrm{W/K}$$

$$q_f = 0.02109 \times 120 \times 0.7818 = 1.978\;\mathrm{W}$$

**(iv) Fin effectiveness:**

$$\varepsilon_f = \frac{q_f}{h A_c \theta_b} = \frac{1.978}{22 \times 5.027\times 10^{-5} \times 120} = \frac{1.978}{0.1327} = 14.91$$

**Comment:** Since $\varepsilon_f = 14.91 \gg 2$, the pin fin is **highly justified**. Even though efficiency is only $\sim 75\%$, effectiveness is very large because the fin perimeter is much greater than the bare cross-sectional area.

*(14 marks total — 7 for part (a) derivation, 7 for part (b) numerical.)*

---

**Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, derive an expression for the **heat dissipation and efficiency of a fin with a convective tip** (i.e., heat is lost by convection from the fin tip as well). Show how the insulated-tip result is recovered as a special case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cross-flow finned heat exchanger handles $5000\;\mathrm{kg/h}$ of hot oil ($C_p = 2.1\;\mathrm{kJ/kg\cdot K}$) cooled from $120\;^\circ\mathrm{C}$ to $80\;^\circ\mathrm{C}$ by air entering at $25\;^\circ\mathrm{C}$ and leaving at $65\;^\circ\mathrm{C}$. The overall heat transfer coefficient based on the outside (fin) area is $U_o = 45\;\mathrm{W/m^2\cdot K}$. For a single-pass cross-flow arrangement with **both fluids unmixed**, calculate the required surface area using the **$F$-correction LMTD method**. Take $F = 0.92$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution.**

**(a) Convective-Tip Fin Derivation.**

The general ODE solution is retained:

$$\theta(x) = C_1 e^{mx} + C_2 e^{-mx}$$

**Boundary conditions**:
- At $x = 0$: $\theta = \theta_b \Rightarrow C_1 + C_2 = \theta_b$.
- At $x = L$: convection balance (heat conducted to tip = heat convected from tip):
  $$-k A_c \left.\frac{d\theta}{dx}\right|_L = h A_c \theta(L)$$
  Substituting:
  $$-k A_c m(C_1 e^{mL} - C_2 e^{-mL}) = h A_c (C_1 e^{mL} + C_2 e^{-mL})$$

Let $H = h/(mk)$. After algebraic solution:

$$\theta(x) = \theta_b\,\frac{\cosh m(L-x) + H\sinh m(L-x)}{\cosh mL + H\sinh mL}$$

Heat dissipated:

$$q_f = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\;\theta_b\;\frac{\tanh mL + H}{1 + H\tanh mL}$$

Efficiency:

$$\eta_f = \frac{\tanh mL + H}{mL\,(1 + H\tanh mL)}$$

**Special case:** If $H \to 0$ (i.e., tip is insulated because $h \to 0$ or $k\to\infty$), then $\sinh mL \to \sinh mL$, $\tanh mL$ remains, and:

$$\eta_f \to \frac{\tanh mL}{mL}$$

which **recovers the insulated-tip formula** exactly. ✔

**(b) Cross-Flow Numerical Problem.**

Given:
- $\dot{m}_h = 5000\;\mathrm{kg/h} = 1.389\;\mathrm{kg/s}$
- $C_{p,h} = 2.1\;\mathrm{kJ/kg\cdot K} = 2100\;\mathrm{J/kg\cdot K}$
- $T_{h,i} = 120\;^\circ\mathrm{C}$, $T_{h,o} = 80\;^\circ\mathrm{C}$
- $T_{c,i} = 25\;^\circ\mathrm{C}$, $T_{c,o} = 65\;^\circ\mathrm{C}$
- $U_o = 45\;\mathrm{W/m^2\cdot K}$, $F = 0.92$

**Step 1: Heat duty.**

$$Q = \dot{m}_h C_{p,h}(T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) = 1.389 \times 2100 \times (120 - 80)$$

$$Q = 1.389 \times 2100 \times 40 = 116{,}694\;\mathrm{W} \approx 116.7\;\mathrm{kW}$$

**Step 2: Counter-flow LMTD.**

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i} = 80 - 25 = 55\;^\circ\mathrm{C}$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} = 120 - 65 = 55\;^\circ\mathrm{C}$$

Since $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$, this is the **balanced cross-flow** case:

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{55 - 55}{\ln(55/55)} \rightarrow \text{use identity} \rightarrow \Delta T_{lm} = 55\;^\circ\mathrm{C}$$

**Step 3: Corrected LMTD.**

$$\Delta T_{m,corr} = F \cdot \Delta T_{lm} = 0.92 \times 55 = 50.6\;^\circ\mathrm{C}$$

**Step 4: Required area.**

$$A_o = \frac{Q}{U_o\,\Delta T_{m,corr}} = \frac{116{,}694}{45 \times 50.6}$$

$$\boxed{\;A_o = \frac{116{,}694}{2277} = 51.25\;\mathrm{m^2}\;}$$

*(14 marks — 7 for part (a) derivation + 7 for part (b) numerical; 1 mark for balanced case identity, 1 mark for $F$-factor application.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $T_b$ to temperature excess** $\theta_b = T_b - T_\infty$. Many students plug $T_b$ directly into efficiency formulas and lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up $h A_c$ vs $h A_s$** in the effectiveness formula. Effectiveness uses $A_c$ (bare area), efficiency uses $A_s$ (total fin area).
> 3. **Treating the tip as insulated** when the problem says nothing — read carefully! If the tip is small, insulated tip is justified; if length is large, use the convection-tip formula.
> 4. **Forgetting the $F$-factor** in cross-flow exchangers. Without it, LMTD overestimates true mean ΔT and the calculated area will be too small (unsafe design).
> 5. **Not computing $mL$ explicitly** — many students write $\eta_f = \tanh(mL)/(mL)$ but skip the numerical evaluation, losing partial marks.
> 6. **Skipping the assumption list** in derivations — KTU examiners award at least 1 mark for stating the six standard fin assumptions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Fin efficiency** $\eta_f = \tanh(mL)/(mL)$ for insulated tip; $\eta_f \to 1$ as $mL \to 0$; $\eta_f \to 1/(mL)$ as $mL \to \infty$.
- **Fin parameter** $m = \sqrt{hP/kA_c}$; higher $h$, larger $P$, smaller $k$, smaller $A_c$ all increase $m$ → lower $\eta_f$.
- **Fin effectiveness** $\varepsilon_f = q_f / (h A_c \theta_b)$; $\varepsilon_f \geq 2$ is the design-justification threshold.
- **Overall surface efficiency** $\eta_o = 1 - (A_f/A_t)(1-\eta_f)$; never use just $\eta_f$ in a finned-tube-bank $UA$ calculation.
- **Finned-side $UA$ formula**: $\dfrac{1}{UA_o} = \dfrac{1}{\eta_o h_o A_o} + \dfrac{A_o}{kA_w} + \dfrac{A_o}{h_i A_i}$.
- **Corrected LMTD**: $\Delta T_{m,corr} = F\,\Delta T_{lm}$; always use $F$ for cross-flow exchangers.
- **Always add fins to the low-$h$ side**; fins on a high-$h$ condensing surface waste material.
- **Aluminium ($k=205$) and Copper ($k=385$)** are the standard fin materials; stainless steel ($k=16$) gives very low $\eta_f$.
- **Balanced cross-flow** (where $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$): $\Delta T_{lm} = \Delta T_1$ directly (limit of L'Hôpital).
- **Six assumptions**: steady state, 1-D conduction, constant $k$, constant $h$, no radiation, no internal generation.
- **Tip conditions ranked by accuracy**: insulated tip < convection tip < actual measured (use convection tip as default).
- **Long-fin limit** ($mL > 2.65$): $\tanh(mL) \to 1$, so $q_f \to \sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b$.
- **Pin fin formulas**: $A_c = \pi D^2/4$, $P = \pi D$, $m = \sqrt{4h/kD}$.
- **Annular fin formulas**: $A_c = 2\pi r_o t$, $P = 2\pi (2r_o - t)$, $m = \sqrt{2h/[k(r_o - r_i)]}$ (note: outer surface convection only).
- **Compactness ratio** $\beta = A/V$ in $\mathrm{m^2/m^3}$ is the metric used to compare finned vs. plain exchangers.
- **Design iteration order**: pick material → choose geometry → compute $m$ → pick $L$ so $mL \approx 1$–$2$ → verify $\varepsilon_f \geq 2$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification According to Flow Arrangements: Single-Pass Exchangers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Classification According to Flow Arrangements: Single-Pass Exchangers # Classification According to Flow Arrangements: Single-Pass Exchangers ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal KTU Syllabus Definition A **Single-Pass Heat Exchanger** (also called a **1-1 Exchanger** or **Once-Through Exchanger**) is the simplest configuration in the family of shell-and-tube heat exchangers in which **both the shell-side and tube-side fluids traverse the heat exchanger only once**, in a single continuous pass, without any change in flow direction or subdivision into multiple parallel streams. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition:** "In a single-pass (1-1) heat exchanger, both the hot and cold fluids flow through the entire length of the exchanger in one pass only. The fluid may flow in concurrent (parallel) or counter-current arrangement, but it does not reverse direction." The heat exchange occurs between two fluids separated by a **cylindrical tube wall** (the heat transfer surface), with the tube-side fluid flowing inside the tubes and the shell-side fluid flowing across the outside of the tubes within the shell. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a long, straight corridor in a building. A warm air duct runs from one end of the corridor to the other, blowing hot air into the rooms along the way. As the hot air moves through the duct, it transfers heat to the surrounding rooms (cold air) ONCE, then exits at the other end. The cold air enters the corridor from the opposite end and flows along the outside of the duct, picking up heat as it goes. **In this analogy:** - The duct = tube carrying hot fluid - The corridor = shell carrying cold fluid - The wall of the duct = tube wall (heat transfer surface) - Each fluid enters ONCE and exits ONCE = **Single Pass** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Intuition:** A single-pass exchanger is the *baseline* design. Everything more complex (1-2, 2-4, cross-flow) is built by *modifying* this simple arrangement. Master the 1-1, and you understand the foundation of all heat exchanger design. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Nomenclature | Symbol | Quantity | Typical SI Unit | |--------|----------|-----------------| | $A$ | Heat transfer area | $\text{m}^2$ | | $U$ | Overall heat transfer coefficient | $\text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$ | | $Q$ | Heat duty / Heat transfer rate | $\text{W}$ | | $\Delta T_{lm}$ | Log mean temperature difference | $\text{K}$ or $^\circ\text{C}$ | | $N_t$ | Number of tubes | dimensionless | | $D_o, D_i$ | Outer and inner tube diameters | $\text{m}$ | | $L$ | Tube length | $\text{m}$ | | $F$ | LMTD correction factor | dimensionless (= 1 for 1-1) | > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** In the KTU 2024 scheme, single-pass exchangers are critical because they form the **reference case** for deriving the LMTD correction factor $F_T$ used in multi-pass exchangers. ### 1.4 Position in the Classification Hierarchy Heat exchangers classified by flow arrangement fall into three primary families: 1. **Single-Pass (1-1)** ← *This topic* 2. **Multi-Pass (1-2, 2-4, etc.)** — fluid reverses direction 3. **Cross-Flow** — fluids cross perpendicular to each other Within single-pass exchangers, two sub-arrangements exist: - **Parallel Flow (Co-current)** — both fluids enter at the same end, flow in the *same direction* - **Counter Flow (Counter-current)** — fluids enter at *opposite ends*, flow in *opposite directions* --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Physical Construction of a 1-1 Exchanger The fundamental components of a single-pass shell-and-tube heat exchanger are: - **Shell**: A large-diameter cylindrical pressure vessel that confines the shell-side fluid. - **Tubes**: A bundle of parallel tubes (often arranged in triangular or square pitch) connecting two tube sheets. - **Tube Sheets**: Solid plates that hold the tubes in place and separate shell-side from tube-side. - **Baffles**: NOT present in true 1-1 exchangers (or only one or two for support) — baffles create multi-pass flow. - **Inlet/Outlet Nozzles**: Four openings — two on the shell (for shell-side fluid in/out) and two on the channel heads (for tube-side fluid in/out). ### 2.2 Operating Principle — Why Heat Transfer Occurs Heat transfer is governed by **Fourier's Law of Conduction** across the tube wall, combined with **Newton's Law of Cooling** on each fluid side. The driving force is the **temperature difference** between the two fluids at every point along the exchanger. The three thermal resistances in series are: $$R_{total} = \frac{1}{h_i A_i} + \frac{\ln(D_o/D_i)}{2\pi k_w L N_t} + \frac{1}{h_o A_o}$$ Where: - $h_i$ = inside (tube-side) heat transfer coefficient - $h_o$ = outside (shell-side) heat transfer coefficient - $k_w$ = thermal conductivity of tube wall material The reciprocal of the total resistance is the **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient** $U$: $$\frac{1}{U A} = \frac{1}{h_i A_i} + R_{wall} + \frac{1}{h_o A_o}$$ ### 2.3 Temperature Profiles — The Heart of Heat Exchanger Analysis For a differential element of area $dA$ in a single-pass exchanger, the rate of heat transfer is: $$dQ = U \cdot (T_h - T_c) \cdot dA$$ The energy balance on the hot fluid (cooling from $T_{h,in}$ to $T_{h,out}$): $$dQ = -\dot{m}_h C_{p,h} \, dT_h$$ The energy balance on the cold fluid (heating from $T_{c,in}$ to $T_{c,out}$): $$dQ = +\dot{m}_c C_{p,c} \, dT_c$$ Combining these with the differential heat transfer equation yields the **Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)** expression. ### 2.4 The Two LMTD Forms **Parallel Flow (Co-current):** $$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,in} \qquad \Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,out}$$ **Counter Flow (Counter-current):** $$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} \qquad \Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$$ **Unified LMTD Formula (valid for both):** $$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Critical Rule:** When $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$ (rare special case), use $\Delta T_{lm} = \Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$ to avoid division by zero. ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $Q = U A \Delta T_{lm}$ | Heat transfer rate equation (1-1) | $\text{W}$ | | 2 | $Q = \dot{m}_h C_{p,h} (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})$ | Hot fluid energy balance | $\text{W}$ | | 3 | $Q = \dot{m}_c C_{p,c} (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$ | Cold fluid energy balance | $\text{W}$ | | 4 | $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$ | Log Mean Temperature Difference | $\text{K}$ | | 5 | $A = \pi D_o L N_t$ | Heat transfer surface area (tube outside) | $\text{m}^2$ | | 6 | $N_u = 0.023 \, Re^{0.8} \, Pr^{0.4}$ (heating) | Dittus-Boelter equation (turbulent) | dimensionless | | 7 | $N_u = 0.023 \, Re^{0.8} \, Pr^{0.3}$ (cooling) | Dittus-Boelter (cooling variant) | dimensionless | | 8 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D_i}{\mu} = \dfrac{4 \dot{m}}{\pi D_i \mu}$ | Reynolds number (tube-side) | dimensionless | | 9 | $P = \dfrac{T_{h,out} - T_{h,in}}{T_{c,in} - T_{h,in}}$ | Thermal effectiveness parameter P | dimensionless | | 10 | $R = \dfrac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}}$ | Capacity ratio R | dimensionless | | 11 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \dfrac{(T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})}{(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})} \cdot R$ | NTU-Effectiveness for counter-flow | dimensionless | | 12 | $F_T = 1$ for 1-1 | LMTD correction factor (single-pass) | dimensionless | ### 2.6 Real-World Utility Single-pass exchangers are the workhorse in: - **Oil refineries**: pre-heating crude oil - **Power plants**: condensers and feedwater heaters - **Chemical plants**: simple heating/cooling duties - **Food industry**: pasteurization (milk, juice) - **HVAC**: chilled water systems > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Reality:** While multi-pass designs achieve higher $U$ (better heat transfer coefficient) due to higher velocities, single-pass designs are preferred when **low pressure drop** is critical, when the **fluids are dirty or fouling**, or when **thermal stress** must be minimized. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the LMTD for Counter-Current Single-Pass Exchanger **Starting Point:** Take a differential element $dA$ of the heat transfer surface. The heat transferred across this element is: $$dQ = U (T_h - T_c) \, dA$$ **Hot fluid energy balance** (the hot fluid cools by $dT_h$): $$dQ = -\dot{m}_h C_{p,h} \, dT_h \quad \Rightarrow \quad dT_h = -\frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}}$$ **Cold fluid energy balance** (the cold fluid heats by $dT_c$): $$dQ = \dot{m}_c C_{p,c} \, dT_c \quad \Rightarrow \quad dT_c = \frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}}$$ **Temperature difference** $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$. Differentiate: $$d(\Delta T) = dT_h - dT_c = -\frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}} - \frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}}$$ Factor out $dQ$: $$d(\Delta T) = -dQ \left( \frac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}} \right)$$ Substitute $dQ = U (T_h - T_c) \, dA = U \Delta T \, dA$: $$d(\Delta T) = -U \Delta T \, dA \left( \frac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}} \right)$$ Separate variables: $$\frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U \left( \frac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}} \right) dA$$ Integrate from inlet ($A=0$, $\Delta T = \Delta T_1$) to outlet ($A=A$, $\Delta T = \Delta T_2$): $$\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U \left( \frac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}} \right) \int_0^A dA$$ Left side: $$\ln\left( \frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1} \right)$$ Right side: $$-U A \left( \frac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}} \right)$$ Set them equal: $$\ln\left( \frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1} \right) = -U A \left( \frac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}} \right)$$ Now use the total heat duty: $Q = \dot{m}_h C_{p,h} (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = \dot{m}_c C_{p,c} (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$. The temperature span of the hot fluid is $T_{h,in} - T_{h,out} = \dfrac{Q}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}}$. For counter-current, the cold fluid enters where the hot fluid exits: $$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} \qquad \Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$$ So: $$\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2 = (T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}) - (T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}) = (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) + (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$$ $$\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2 = \frac{Q}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}} + \frac{Q}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}} = Q \left( \frac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}} \right)$$ Therefore: $$\frac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{Q}$$ Substitute back into the integrated equation: $$\ln\left( \frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1} \right) = -U A \cdot \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{Q}$$ Rearrange: $$Q = \frac{U A (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)}{-\ln(\Delta T_2/\Delta T_1)} = \frac{U A (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$$ Define LMTD: $$\boxed{\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{Q = U A \Delta T_{lm}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** This derivation is a *favorite* in KTU exams. You must show all integral steps clearly. Marks are awarded for: (1) the differential energy balance, (2) the substitution of $dQ$, (3) the integration, and (4) the recognition of $\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2$ in terms of $Q$. ### 3.2 Worked Example — Counter-Current 1-1 Exchanger **Given:** - Hot fluid: oil, $\dot{m}_h = 2.0 \, \text{kg/s}$, $C_{p,h} = 2100 \, \text{J/kg}\cdot\text{K}$, $T_{h,in} = 150^\circ\text{C}$ - Cold fluid: water, $\dot{m}_c = 1.0 \, \text{kg/s}$, $C_{p,c} = 4180 \, \text{J/kg}\cdot\text{K}$, $T_{c,in} = 30^\circ\text{C}$ - $U = 500 \, \text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$ - Configuration: 1-1 counter-current **Step 1: Heat duty from energy balance on hot fluid** $$Q = \dot{m}_h C_{p,h} (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})$$ We need $T_{h,out}$. Use cold fluid energy balance. From capacity ratio: $$R = \frac{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}} = \frac{1.0 \times 4180}{2.0 \times 2100} = \frac{4180}{4200} \approx 0.995 \approx 1$$ **Step 2: Use iterative assumption.** If $R = 1$, then for a counter-current exchanger of infinite area, $T_{h,out} \to T_{c,in} = 30^\circ\text{C}$ and $T_{c,out} \to T_{h,in} = 150^\circ\text{C}$. Assume nearly so: **Step 3: Calculate temperature differences (counter-current)** $$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 150 - 149 = 1^\circ\text{C}$$ $$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 31 - 30 = 1^\circ\text{C}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Pitfall:** When $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$, the LMTD equals the arithmetic mean. Do NOT attempt to compute the logarithm. **Step 4: LMTD** $$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 + \Delta T_2}{2} = \frac{1 + 1}{2} = 1^\circ\text{C}$$ **Step 5: Area required** $$A = \frac{Q}{U \Delta T_{lm}} = \frac{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h} (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})}{U \Delta T_{lm}} = \frac{2.0 \times 2100 \times (150 - 31)}{500 \times 1}$$ $$A = \frac{4200 \times 119}{500} = \frac{499{,}800}{500} = 999.6 \, \text{m}^2$$ ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Parallel vs. Counter-Flow | Property | Parallel Flow | Counter-Flow | |----------|---------------|--------------| | $\Delta T_1$ | $T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}$ | $T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$ | | $\Delta T_2$ | $T_{h,out} - T_{c,out}$ | $T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$ | | $\Delta T_{lm}$ magnitude | Smaller (usually) | Larger (more efficient) | | Max cold outlet temp | Cannot exceed $T_{h,in}$ | Can exceed $T_{h,out}$, can approach $T_{h,in}$ | | Temperature "pinch" risk | Yes, fluids approach same temp | Reduced, especially if $C_{min}$ on hot side | | Thermodynamic efficiency | Lower | Higher (preferred) | | Use when | Temperature cross must be avoided | Maximum heat recovery is the goal | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Single-Pass Exchanger System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Hot Fluid Reservoir
T_h_in] --> B[Hot Pump] B --> C[Shell Inlet Nozzle] C --> D[Shell Side Annulus
Single Pass] D --> E[Tube Bundle
N_t parallel tubes] E --> F[Shell Outlet Nozzle
T_h_out] F --> G[Hot Fluid Cooler Outlet] H[Cold Fluid Reservoir
T_c_in] --> I[Cold Pump] I --> J[Channel Head Inlet] J --> K[Tube Sheet] K --> E E --> L[Opposite Tube Sheet] L --> M[Channel Head Outlet] M --> N[Cold Fluid Heated Outlet
T_c_out] E -. Heat Transfer .-> D D -. Heat Transfer .-> E styleA fill: ffcccc, stroke: cc0000 styleA font: 12px Arial styleH fill: cceeff, stroke: 003366 styleH font: 12px Arial styleE fill: ffffcc, stroke: 996600 styleE font: 12px Arial styleD fill: ffffcc, stroke: 996600 styleD font: 12px Arial ``` ### 4.2 Functional Flow Diagram — Counter-Current vs. Parallel ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Hot In
150 C] --> A2[Tube Side Flow] A2 --> A3[Hot Out
50 C] subgraph Counter1 direction LR B1[Cold In 30 C] --> B2[Shell Flow Opposite] --> B3[Cold Out 130 C] end A2 -. Heat Exchanger .-> B2 C1[Hot In 150 C] --> C2[Tube Side Flow] C2 --> C3[Hot Out 50 C] subgraph Parallel1 direction LR D1[Cold In 30 C] --> D2[Shell Flow Same Direction] --> D3[Cold Out 90 C] end C2 -. Heat Exchanger .-> D2 styleA1 fill: ff9999 styleA3 fill: 99ccff styleB1 fill: 99ccff styleB3 fill: ff9999 styleC1 fill: ff9999 styleC3 fill: 99ccff styleD1 fill: 99ccff styleD3 fill: ffcc66 ``` ### 4.3 Calculation Flow for a 1-1 Exchanger Design Problem ```mermaid flowchart TD stepA[Step 1: Identify given data
m_h, m_c, Cp_h, Cp_c, T_in] --> stepB[Step 2: Compute Q from energy balance
if one outlet is given] stepB --> stepC[Step 3: Determine R and P parameters] stepC --> stepD[Step 4: Find F_T = 1 for 1-1 exchanger] stepD --> stepE[Step 5: Compute Delta T_1 and Delta T_2
based on flow arrangement] stepE --> stepF[Step 6: Calculate LMTD] stepF --> stepG[Step 7: Compute Area A = Q over U times LMTD] stepG --> stepH[Step 8: Choose tubes and bundle layout] stepH --> stepI[Step 9: Verify pressure drop acceptable] stepI --> stepJ[Step 10: Iterate if area infeasible] styleA fill: fff0e0 styleB fill: fff0e0 styleC fill: fff0e0 styleD fill: fff0e0 styleE fill: fff0e0 styleF fill: fff0e0 styleG fill: fff0e0 styleH fill: fff0e0 styleI fill: fff0e0 styleJ fill: fff0e0 ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define a single-pass (1-1) heat exchanger. Distinguish between parallel flow and counter-current flow arrangements with a neat sketch. **Model Answer:** A **single-pass (1-1) heat exchanger** is one in which both the shell-side and tube-side fluids pass through the exchanger only once, without any change in direction. The heat transfer surface is a single tube bundle connecting two tube sheets. **Parallel Flow:** Both hot and cold fluids enter at the *same end* of the exchanger and flow in the *same direction*. The hot fluid cools and the cold fluid heats as they move together, and they tend to approach a common temperature at the outlet. **Counter-Current Flow:** The hot and cold fluids enter at *opposite ends* of the exchanger and flow in *opposite directions*. This arrangement provides a more uniform temperature driving force along the length and allows the cold fluid outlet temperature to exceed the hot fluid outlet temperature. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** State the Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD) formula. Why is it preferred over the Arithmetic Mean Temperature Difference (AMTD)? **Model Answer:** $$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$ The LMTD is preferred because it represents the **true mean temperature driving force** for heat transfer along the length of the exchanger. The temperature difference between the fluids varies exponentially (not linearly) along the exchanger length, and the LMTD correctly accounts for this logarithmic distribution. The AMTD, being a simple arithmetic average, overestimates the driving force and leads to under-sized exchangers. --- ### Part B Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4 Internal Choice]** **(a)** A 1-1 counter-current shell-and-tube heat exchanger is used to cool $5.0 \, \text{kg/s}$ of oil ($C_p = 2100 \, \text{J/kg}\cdot\text{K}$) from $160^\circ\text{C}$ to $60^\circ\text{C}$ using water ($C_p = 4180 \, \text{J/kg}\cdot\text{K}$) available at $30^\circ\text{C}$. The overall heat transfer coefficient is $U = 600 \, \text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$. Calculate: (i) the water flow rate, (ii) the water outlet temperature, and (iii) the heat transfer area. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the LMTD expression for a single-pass counter-current heat exchanger starting from the differential energy balance. **(7 marks)** --- **Solution to (a):** **Given:** - $\dot{m}_h = 5.0 \, \text{kg/s}$, $C_{p,h} = 2100 \, \text{J/kg}\cdot\text{K}$, $T_{h,in} = 160^\circ\text{C}$, $T_{h,out} = 60^\circ\text{C}$ - $C_{p,c} = 4180 \, \text{J/kg}\cdot\text{K}$, $T_{c,in} = 30^\circ\text{C}$ - $U = 600 \, \text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$ **Step 1: Total heat duty** $$Q = \dot{m}_h C_{p,h} (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = 5.0 \times 2100 \times (160 - 60)$$ $$Q = 5.0 \times 2100 \times 100 = 1{,}050{,}000 \, \text{W} = 1050 \, \text{kW}$$ **Step 2: Water outlet temperature from cold-side balance** $$Q = \dot{m}_c C_{p,c} (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$$ $$T_{c,out} = T_{c,in} + \frac{Q}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}}$$ Assume $R = 1$ initially, so: $$\dot{m}_c C_{p,c} = \dot{m}_h C_{p,h} = 5.0 \times 2100 = 10{,}500 \, \text{W/K}$$ $$T_{c,out} = 30 + \frac{1{,}050{,}000}{10{,}500} = 30 + 100 = 130^\circ\text{C}$$ > **Check:** $T_{c,out} = 130^\circ\text{C} < T_{h,in} = 160^\circ\text{C}$. This is consistent with the 2nd law of thermodynamics, so the design is feasible. **Step 3: Calculate water flow rate** Since we set $R = 1$: $$\dot{m}_c = \frac{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}}{C_{p,c}} = \frac{5.0 \times 2100}{4180} = 2.512 \, \text{kg/s}$$ **Step 4: LMTD (counter-current)** $$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 160 - 130 = 30^\circ\text{C}$$ $$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 60 - 30 = 30^\circ\text{C}$$ **Special case:** $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2 = 30^\circ\text{C}$, so: $$\Delta T_{lm} = 30^\circ\text{C}$$ **Step 5: Heat transfer area** $$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} = \frac{1{,}050{,}000}{600 \times 30} = \frac{1{,}050{,}000}{18{,}000} = 58.33 \, \text{m}^2$$ **Final Answers:** - (i) $\dot{m}_c = 2.512 \, \text{kg/s}$ - (ii) $T_{c,out} = 130^\circ\text{C}$ - (iii) $A = 58.33 \, \text{m}^2$ --- **Solution to (b):** See the **complete derivation in Section 3.1** of these notes. Summary outline for the answer script: 1. Start with $dQ = U (T_h - T_c) dA$ 2. Hot fluid: $dT_h = -dQ / (\dot{m}_h C_{p,h})$ 3. Cold fluid: $dT_c = +dQ / (\dot{m}_c C_{p,c})$ 4. Combine: $d(\Delta T) = dT_h - dT_c$ 5. Substitute: $d(\Delta T) / \Delta T = -U(1/\dot{m}_h C_{p,h} + 1/\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}) dA$ 6. Integrate from inlet to outlet. 7. Recognize $\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2 = Q(1/\dot{m}_h C_{p,h} + 1/\dot{m}_c C_{p,c})$ 8. Rearrange to obtain $Q = U A \Delta T_{lm}$. --- ### Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 Internal Choice]** **(a)** Explain the temperature profiles for (i) parallel flow and (ii) counter-current flow single-pass exchangers. In a counter-current exchanger, the cold fluid can be heated to a temperature higher than the hot fluid outlet temperature. Justify. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 1-1 parallel-flow heat exchanger is used to condense steam at $120^\circ\text{C}$ (saturated) using cooling water entering at $25^\circ\text{C}$ and leaving at $80^\circ\text{C}$. The heat transfer area is $15 \, \text{m}^2$ and $U = 1200 \, \text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$. Determine: (i) the heat transfer rate, and (ii) the steam condensation rate if the latent heat is $2200 \, \text{kJ/kg}$. **(7 marks)** --- **Solution to (a):** **Parallel Flow Temperature Profile:** Both fluids enter at the same end. The hot fluid temperature decreases monotonically from $T_{h,in}$ to $T_{h,out}$, and the cold fluid temperature increases monotonically from $T_{c,in}$ to $T_{c,out}$. The two temperature curves approach each other asymptotically but never cross. The cold fluid outlet temperature can never exceed the hot fluid outlet temperature in parallel flow. **Counter-Current Flow Temperature Profile:** Fluids enter at opposite ends. The hot fluid cools from $T_{h,in}$ to $T_{h,out}$, and the cold fluid heats from $T_{c,in}$ to $T_{c,out}$. Since the cold fluid at any point meets the *hottest* portion of the hot fluid at one end and the *coolest* portion at the other, the temperature difference is more uniform. **Justification:** In counter-current flow, the cold fluid at the outlet end meets the *incoming* hot fluid, which is at its maximum temperature $T_{h,in}$. Therefore: $$T_{c,out} = T_{h,in} - \Delta T_1$$ If $\Delta T_1$ is small (good design), then $T_{c,out}$ can approach $T_{h,in}$, which is *always greater* than $T_{h,out}$ (since the hot fluid is cooling). Thus $T_{c,out}$ can exceed $T_{h,out}$. This is impossible in parallel flow. --- **Solution to (b):** **Given:** - $T_{h,in} = T_{h,out} = 120^\circ\text{C}$ (condensing steam — isothermal) - $T_{c,in} = 25^\circ\text{C}$, $T_{c,out} = 80^\circ\text{C}$ - $A = 15 \, \text{m}^2$, $U = 1200 \, \text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$ - Latent heat $\lambda = 2200 \, \text{kJ/kg} = 2{,}200{,}000 \, \text{J/kg}$ **Step 1: LMTD calculation (parallel flow)** $$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,in} = 120 - 25 = 95^\circ\text{C}$$ $$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,out} = 120 - 80 = 40^\circ\text{C}$$ $$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{95 - 40}{\ln(95/40)} = \frac{55}{\ln(2.375)} = \frac{55}{0.8649} = 63.58^\circ\text{C}$$ **Step 2: Heat transfer rate** $$Q = U A \Delta T_{lm} = 1200 \times 15 \times 63.58 = 1{,}144{,}500 \, \text{W} \approx 1144.5 \, \text{kW}$$ **Step 3: Steam condensation rate** $$Q = \dot{m}_{steam} \times \lambda$$ $$\dot{m}_{steam} = \frac{Q}{\lambda} = \frac{1{,}144{,}500}{2{,}200{,}000} = 0.5202 \, \text{kg/s}$$ **Final Answers:** - (i) $Q = 1144.5 \, \text{kW}$ - (ii) $\dot{m}_{steam} = 0.5202 \, \text{kg/s} \approx 1873 \, \text{kg/h}$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on This Topic:** > 1. **Sign convention errors:** Always maintain $T_{h,in} > T_{h,out} > T_{c,out} > T_{c,in}$ for counter-current flow. A negative temperature difference in your final LMTD means you've swapped $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$. > 2. **ln argument sign:** $\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$ requires both $\Delta T$'s positive. If negative, take absolute values. > 3. **Forgetting $F_T$:** For 1-1 exchangers, $F_T = 1$. For multi-pass exchangers, you MUST include the correction factor. Students often forget this in Module 5 problems. > 4. **Unit consistency:** Heat capacity in $\text{kJ/kg}\cdot\text{K}$ and area in $\text{m}^2$ requires $U$ in $\text{kW/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$ or convert everything to $\text{W}$. > 5. **Logical impossibility:** If calculated $T_{c,out} > T_{h,in}$, the problem is infeasible; you must increase the hot fluid flow rate or reduce the cold fluid duty. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Single-Pass Exchangers** - ✅ A **1-1 exchanger** has one shell pass and one tube pass — the simplest configuration. - ✅ **Parallel flow**: both fluids enter at the same end. Counter-current: fluids enter at opposite ends. - ✅ Counter-current flow is **thermodynamically superior** — more uniform $\Delta T$ and higher $T_{c,out}$ possible. - ✅ The LMTD formula is: $\Delta T_{lm} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2) / \ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$. - ✅ When $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$, LMTD equals AMTD (use arithmetic mean to avoid $0/0$). - ✅ For counter-current: $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$, $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$. - ✅ For parallel flow: $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}$, $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,out}$. - ✅ The governing design equation is $Q = U A \Delta T_{lm}$ (for 1-1, $F_T = 1$). - ✅ Area: $A = \pi D_o L N_t$ (outer tube area) or $A = \pi D_i L N_t$ (inner tube area), whichever matches your $U$ basis. - ✅ Dittus-Boelter correlation (turbulent tube-side): $Nu = 0.023 \, Re^{0.8} \, Pr^{0.4}$ (heating). - ✅ Reynolds number (tube-side): $Re = 4\dot{m} / (\pi D_i \mu)$. - ✅ The capacity ratio $R = \dot{m}_c C_{p,c} / \dot{m}_h C_{p,h}$ and effectiveness parameter $P$ are useful for NTU analysis. - ✅ Single-pass exchangers are preferred for **low pressure drop**, **fouling fluids**, and **simple construction**. - ✅ In KTU exams, always show the LMTD derivation — it's a guaranteed 7-mark question. - ✅ For condensing/boiling fluids (isothermal hot side), $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ are simple to compute because $T_{h,in} = T_{h,out}$.

topics.title: Multi-Pass Exchangers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Multi-Pass Exchangers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
