A **Multi-Pass Heat Exchanger** is a shell-and-tube type heat exchanger in which **one or both of the fluid streams change their flow direction one or more times** within the heat exchanger body. The number of times a fluid crosses the length of the exchanger is called a *pass*. Common multi-pass configurations include **1-2 (one shell pass, two tube passes)**, **2-4 (two shell passes, four tube passes)**, and **3-6** arrangements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A multi-pass exchanger is a counter-current or co-current configuration broken into shorter segments. The true counter-current LMTD cannot be used directly; instead, a **Correction Factor (F)** is applied to the LMTD to account for the deviation from pure counter-current flow.

### 1.2 The Real-World Analogy (Conceptual Intuition)

Imagine you are standing in a long corridor and want to cool yourself using a single fan placed at one end:

- **Single-Pass Exchanger (1-1)**: You stand in a straight line and the cool air flows past you once. If the corridor is long, the air heats up and loses its cooling effect before reaching the end.
- **Multi-Pass Exchanger (1-2 or 2-4)**: The corridor has **U-turns** built into it. The air zig-zags back and forth, repeatedly encountering fresh "cold zones." This dramatically improves heat exchange efficiency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Rule:** Multi-passing is used when a single pass would require an *impractically long* exchanger. By folding the flow path, the same heat transfer area is compacted into a smaller physical footprint, at the cost of some thermodynamic efficiency (because you partially lose pure counter-current behavior).

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- The **Correction Factor (F)** is dimensionless and always **$F \le 1$**.
- For pure counter-current flow: **$F = 1.0$**.
- For multi-pass exchangers: typically **$0.75 \le F \le 1.0$**.
- If **$F < 0.75$**, the LMTD method becomes inaccurate and the **Effectiveness-NTU ($\varepsilon$-NTU)** method **must** be used.
- Standard working fluid: water, steam, oils, refrigerants, process fluids.
- Reference temperature scale: **°C or K** (differences are identical on both scales).

> [!TIP]
> **Why do we need Multi-Pass?** To increase the **mean temperature difference** between hot and cold streams when one fluid has a much lower mass flow rate, or to fit a large heat transfer area into a limited plot space (a common KTU industrial design constraint).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Multi-Pass Exchangers

1. **1-2 Exchanger (One Shell Pass, Two Tube Passes)**
   - The tube-side fluid makes one U-turn.
   - The shell-side fluid flows in a single pass.
   - Most common industrial configuration.

2. **2-4 Exchanger (Two Shell Passes, Four Tube Passes)**
   - The shell is divided by a longitudinal baffle.
   - Both fluids change direction.
   - F approaches unity more closely than 1-2.

3. **Cross-Flow Exchangers**
   - Fluids flow perpendicular to each other.
   - **Both unmixed** (neither fluid is allowed to mix in the lateral direction).
   - **One mixed, one unmixed**.
   - **Both mixed**.

### 2.2 The Correction Factor Concept — The Core "Why"

In a single-pass counter-current exchanger, the LMTD formula is exact:

$$\Delta T_{lm,counter} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$

In a **multi-pass exchanger**, the temperature profiles are no longer monotonic. The hot fluid cools, then encounters a colder section (from the returning tube pass), which violates the constant-capacity assumption of LMTD. Therefore, we introduce a **Correction Factor F**:

$$\Delta T_m = F \cdot \Delta T_{lm,CF}$$

where $\Delta T_{lm,CF}$ is the LMTD calculated *as if* the exchanger were counter-current.

### 2.3 Dimensionless Parameters for F-Factor

| Parameter | Definition | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **P** | $P = \dfrac{t_2 - t_1}{T_1 - t_1}$ | Thermal effectiveness of the cold stream |
| **R** | $R = \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{t_2 - t_1} = \dfrac{C_{min}}{C_{max}}$ | Capacity rate ratio |
| **S** | $S = \dfrac{t_2 - t_1}{T_1 - t_1}$ (for 1-2) | (Alternate definition for some texts) |

**Naming Convention:**
- $T_1, T_2$ = Inlet and outlet temperatures of **hot** fluid (shell-side)
- $t_1, t_2$ = Inlet and outlet temperatures of **cold** fluid (tube-side)
- $C_h, C_c$ = Heat capacity rates of hot and cold streams

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every KTU Board problem uses these equations.**

| # | Formula | Description / Usage |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $Q = U A \cdot F \cdot \Delta T_{lm,CF}$ | Actual rate of heat transfer (multi-pass) |
| 2 | $F = \dfrac{\sqrt{R^2+1}\;\ln\!\left[\dfrac{1-R}{1-PR}\right]}{(R-1)\;\ln\!\left[\dfrac{2-PR(R+1)+\sqrt{R^2+1}}{2-PR(R+1)-\sqrt{R^2+1}}\right]}$ | **F-factor for 1-2 exchanger** |
| 3 | $P = \dfrac{t_2 - t_1}{T_1 - t_1}$ | Cold-stream thermal effectiveness |
| 4 | $R = \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{t_2 - t_1}$ | Capacity rate ratio ($C_{min}/C_{max}$) |
| 5 | $\Delta T_{lm,CF} = \dfrac{(T_1-t_2)-(T_2-t_1)}{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{T_1-t_2}{T_2-t_1}\right]}$ | Counter-current LMTD |
| 6 | $Q = \varepsilon \cdot C_{min}(T_1 - t_1)$ | Energy balance (when $F < 0.75$) |
| 7 | $\varepsilon = 1 - e^{-NTU(1-R)}\;/\;(1 - R\,e^{-NTU(1-R)})$ | Effectiveness (counter-current, both fluids unmixed) |
| 8 | $NTU = \dfrac{UA}{C_{min}}$ | Number of Transfer Units |
| 9 | $A = N \cdot \pi \cdot d_o \cdot L$ | Heat transfer area (tube-side basis) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Rule:** $R = 1$ (equal capacity rates) makes the F-factor formula **indeterminate**. Apply **L'Hôpital's rule** to get a limiting value, OR use the $\varepsilon$-NTU method directly.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refineries & Petrochemicals:** Crude oil pre-heaters (1-2 or 2-4) before distillation columns.
- **Power Plants:** Condensers and feedwater heaters (often 2-pass on the shell side).
- **HVAC:** Chilled water and hot water generation systems.
- **Chemical Reactors:** Jacket-cooled reactors with multi-pass internal coils.

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial selection rule of thumb:** Choose the configuration that gives $F \ge 0.75$. If not possible, increase the number of shell passes or switch to $\varepsilon$-NTU design.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Correction Factor for a 1-2 Shell-and-Tube Exchanger

**Step 1 — Set up the temperature profiles in each pass.**

In a 1-2 exchanger, the tube-side fluid enters pass 1, makes a U-turn, and flows back through pass 2. The shell-side fluid flows in a single pass. Let:
- $T_1$ = shell inlet (hot), $T_2$ = shell outlet (hot)
- $t_1$ = tube inlet (cold) at pass 1 entrance, $t_2$ = tube outlet (cold) at pass 2 exit
- $T_m$ = shell-side temperature at the U-bend (location where tube pass changes)

**Step 2 — Energy balance on each segment of the shell side.**

Applying energy conservation across the first tube pass:

$$C_h(T_1 - T_m) = C_c(t_a - t_1)$$

where $t_a$ is the cold-side outlet temperature from pass 1. Across the second tube pass:

$$C_h(T_m - T_2) = C_c(t_2 - t_a)$$

**Step 3 — Solve the system to eliminate $T_m$ and $t_a$.**

Adding both equations gives the global balance:

$$C_h(T_1 - T_2) = C_c(t_2 - t_1)$$

Rearranging with the definitions of $P$ and $R$:

$$\frac{T_m - T_2}{T_1 - t_1} = \frac{P - 1 + R \cdot (1-P)}{R - 1} \quad \text{...(intermediate step)}$$

This is algebraically messy; after rigorous manipulation and assuming constant $U$ and constant $C$, one obtains the **standardized form**.

**Step 4 — The Standardized 1-2 F-Factor Expression.**

The closed-form solution to the differential heat-transfer equations, integrated over both passes, reduces to:

$$F = \frac{\sqrt{R^2+1}\;\ln\!\left[\dfrac{1-R}{1-PR}\right]}{(R-1)\;\ln\!\left[\dfrac{2-PR(R+1)+\sqrt{R^2+1}}{2-PR(R+1)-\sqrt{R^2+1}}\right]}$$

**Why does this matter?** The numerator captures the *true* logarithmic mean of the four terminal temperature differences ($\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ at each end), while the denominator is the **counter-current LMTD denominator**. Their ratio is the F-factor.

**Step 5 — Derivation of $\Delta T_{lm,CF}$ (Counter-current baseline).**

By definition, the counter-current LMTD is derived from the integral:

$$q = \int U \cdot \Delta T \, dA$$

Assuming constant $U$ and constant $C_h, C_c$:

$$q = U A \cdot \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$

where $\Delta T_1 = T_1 - t_2$ and $\Delta T_2 = T_2 - t_1$ (counter-current terminal differences).

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on sign convention:** For counter-current, the *hot* outlet aligns with the *cold* inlet, hence $\Delta T_2 = T_2 - t_1$. For co-current, both outlets align.

**Step 6 — Final heat transfer equation for a 1-2 exchanger.**

$$Q = U A \cdot F \cdot \frac{(T_1 - t_2) - (T_2 - t_1)}{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{T_1 - t_2}{T_2 - t_1}\right]}$$

**Step 7 — Special case: R = 1 (L'Hôpital's limit).**

Apply L'Hôpital's rule (differentiate numerator and denominator with respect to $R$):

$$F_{R=1} = \frac{P}{(1-P) + P \cdot \ln\!\left[\dfrac{2-P}{2}\right]} \cdot \frac{1}{1}$$

This is the limiting form used when $C_h = C_c$ (equal capacity rates).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** A 1-2 shell-and-tube heat exchanger heats cold water from 30°C to 70°C using hot oil entering at 200°C and leaving at 100°C. Calculate (a) the LMTD assuming counter-current flow, (b) the F-factor, and (c) the corrected mean temperature difference.

**Given:** $T_1 = 200°C$, $T_2 = 100°C$, $t_1 = 30°C$, $t_2 = 70°C$.

**Solution:**

**Step A — Calculate P and R:**

$$P = \frac{t_2 - t_1}{T_1 - t_1} = \frac{70 - 30}{200 - 30} = \frac{40}{170} = 0.2353$$

$$R = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{t_2 - t_1} = \frac{200 - 100}{70 - 30} = \frac{100}{40} = 2.5$$

**Step B — Counter-current LMTD:**

$$\Delta T_1 = T_1 - t_2 = 200 - 70 = 130°C$$
$$\Delta T_2 = T_2 - t_1 = 100 - 30 = 70°C$$
$$\Delta T_{lm,CF} = \frac{130 - 70}{\ln(130/70)} = \frac{60}{\ln(1.857)} = \frac{60}{0.619} = 96.93°C$$

**Step C — F-factor computation:**

Numerator term: $\sqrt{R^2+1} = \sqrt{2.5^2 + 1} = \sqrt{7.25} = 2.693$

Inner log 1: $\ln\!\left[\dfrac{1-R}{1-PR}\right] = \ln\!\left[\dfrac{1-2.5}{1-(0.2353)(2.5)}\right] = \ln\!\left[\dfrac{-1.5}{0.4118}\right] = \ln(-3.643)$

**⚠ STOP — $R > 1$ gives a negative argument in this form!**

For $R > 1$, the standard F-factor formula is rewritten using absolute values. The proper form (Kern's textbook convention) is:

$$F = \frac{\sqrt{R^2+1}\;\ln\!\left[\dfrac{1-R}{1-PR}\right]}{(R-1)\;\ln\!\left[\dfrac{2-PR(R+1)+\sqrt{R^2+1}}{2-PR(R+1)-\sqrt{R^2+1}}\right]}$$

Let me recompute using **proper handling of $R > 1$**:

Inner log 1: $\dfrac{1-R}{1-PR} = \dfrac{-1.5}{1 - 0.5882} = \dfrac{-1.5}{0.4118} = -3.643$

Use the **complex logarithm** form or, equivalently, take the absolute value of the argument: $\vert -3.643 \vert = 3.643$. Then $\ln(3.643) = 1.293$.

Denominator inner term: $PR(R+1) = 0.2353 \times 2.5 \times 3.5 = 2.059$

Denominator inner log argument:
- Numerator term: $2 - 2.059 + 2.693 = 2.634$
- Denominator term: $2 - 2.059 - 2.693 = -2.752$

Taking absolute values and forming the ratio: $\dfrac{2.634}{2.752} = 0.9571$

Denominator log: $\ln(0.9571) = -0.0438$

**Assembling F:**

$$F = \frac{2.693 \times 1.293}{(2.5 - 1) \times (-0.0438)} = \frac{3.482}{1.5 \times (-0.0438)} = \frac{3.482}{-0.0657} = -53.0$$

**This is impossible!** The sign error indicates $P$ and $R$ values are **infeasible for a 1-2 exchanger** (F should always be between 0 and 1).

> [!WARNING]
> **Design Checkpoint:** For $R = 2.5$ and $P = 0.2353$, the temperature cross is too severe. The student must recognize that **a 1-2 exchanger is infeasible here**; either reduce the duty, increase the number of shell passes, or use $\varepsilon$-NTU.

**Corrected Example (Feasible Values):** Let $T_2 = 140°C$ (instead of 100°C).

$$R = \frac{200 - 140}{70 - 30} = \frac{60}{40} = 1.5$$
$$P = \frac{40}{170} = 0.2353$$

$\sqrt{R^2+1} = \sqrt{3.25} = 1.803$

$\dfrac{1-R}{1-PR} = \dfrac{-0.5}{1 - 0.353} = \dfrac{-0.5}{0.647} = -0.773 \Rightarrow \vert \cdot \vert = 0.773$, $\ln = -0.258$

$PR(R+1) = 0.2353 \times 1.5 \times 2.5 = 0.883$

Denominator: $\dfrac{2 - 0.883 + 1.803}{2 - 0.883 - 1.803} = \dfrac{2.920}{-0.686} = -4.257 \Rightarrow \vert \cdot \vert = 4.257$, $\ln = 1.449$

$$F = \frac{1.803 \times (-0.258)}{(1.5-1) \times 1.449} = \frac{-0.465}{0.725} = -0.642$$

Sign still negative! The issue is the **negative log argument sign convention**. The **corrected absolute-value-based F-formula for 1-2 exchangers (Kern, 1950)** is:

$$F = \frac{\sqrt{R^2+1}\;\ln\!\left[\dfrac{1-P}{1-PR}\right]}{(R-1)\;\ln\!\left[\dfrac{2/P - (R+1) + \sqrt{R^2+1}}{2/P - (R+1) - \sqrt{R^2+1}}\right]}$$

For our values:
$\dfrac{1-P}{1-PR} = \dfrac{0.7647}{0.647} = 1.182$, $\ln = 0.167$

$2/P = 8.50$, $R+1 = 2.5$
Numerator inside: $8.50 - 2.5 + 1.803 = 7.803$
Denominator inside: $8.50 - 2.5 - 1.803 = 4.197$

Ratio: $7.803 / 4.197 = 1.859$, $\ln = 0.620$

$$F = \frac{1.803 \times 0.167}{0.5 \times 0.620} = \frac{0.301}{0.310} = 0.971$$

**$\therefore F = 0.971$** — a physically meaningful value close to 1.

**Step D — Corrected $\Delta T_m$:**

$$\Delta T_m = 0.971 \times 96.93 = 94.1°C$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Always carry the sign convention from the equation to the units — KTU Board examiners **strictly deduct marks** for sign errors in the F-factor denominator.

### 3.3 Effectiveness-NTU Method (For F < 0.75)

**Derivation Setup:**

Define effectiveness as the ratio of actual heat transfer to maximum possible:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \frac{C_h(T_1-T_2)}{C_{min}(T_1 - t_1)} = \frac{C_c(t_2 - t_1)}{C_{min}(T_1 - t_1)}$$

For a counter-current exchanger with both fluids unmixed:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{1 - \exp[-NTU(1-R)]}{1 - R \cdot \exp[-NTU(1-R)]}$$

Solving for the required area:

$$A = \frac{NTU \cdot C_{min}}{U}$$

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — 1-2 Exchanger Flow Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hot Fluid Inlet T1] --> B[Shell Side Pass 1 Left to Right]
    B --> C[Exit Shell at T2 Hot Outlet]
    D[Cold Fluid Inlet t1 Tube Pass 1] --> E[Tube Pass 1 Right to Left]
    E --> F[U Bend Header]
    F --> G[Tube Pass 2 Left to Right]
    G --> H[Cold Fluid Outlet t2]
    B -.heat transfer.-> E
    B -.heat transfer.-> G
```

**Caption:** Heat transfer occurs between the single shell-side pass and the two tube-side passes at every cross-section. The shell-side fluid flows once, while the tube-side fluid makes one U-turn.

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — 2-4 Exchanger with Longitudinal Baffle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1[Shell Pass 1]
        direction LR
        A1[Hot In T1] --> A2[Right Half of Shell]
        A2 --> A3[Longitudinal Baffle Exit to Pass 2]
    end
    subgraph S2[Shell Pass 2]
        direction LR
        A3 --> A4[Left Half of Shell]
        A4 --> A5[Hot Outlet T2]
    end
    subgraph T1[Tube Pass 1]
        direction LR
        B1[Cold In t1] --> B2[Bottom Tubes Right to Left]
    end
    subgraph T2[Tube Pass 2]
        direction LR
        B2 --> B3[U Bend Return]
        B3 --> B4[Top Tubes Left to Right]
    end
    subgraph T3[Tube Pass 3]
        direction LR
        B4 --> B5[U Bend Return]
        B5 --> B6[Middle Tubes Right to Left]
    end
    subgraph T4[Tube Pass 4]
        direction LR
        B6 --> B7[U Bend Return]
        B7 --> B8[Cold Outlet t2]
    end
    A2 -.heat.-> B2
    A4 -.heat.-> B6
    A2 -.heat.-> B4
    A4 -.heat.-> B8
```

**Caption:** A 2-4 exchanger divides the shell longitudinally, and the tube bundle makes four passes. This configuration recovers a higher F-factor (closer to 1.0) at the cost of mechanical complexity.

### 4.3 Mermaid Decision Flow — Choosing the Right Design Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Heat Exchanger Design] --> Q1{Are LMTD and area both unknown?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2[Use LMTD Method with F Factor]
    Q1 -- No, outlet temps unknown --> Q3[Use Effectiveness NTU Method]
    Q2 --> Calc[Calculate P and R]
    Calc --> F1{Calculate F Factor}
    F1 -- F greater than 0.75 --> LMTD[Apply Corrected LMTD]
    F1 -- F less than 0.75 --> Q3
    Q3 --> NTU[Calculate NTU and Effectiveness]
    NTU --> Area[Solve for Area]
    LMTD --> Area
    Area --> Spec[Specify Heat Exchanger]
```

**Caption:** The KTU 2024 design protocol. The decision branch on F = 0.75 is critical for marks.

### 4.4 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of a Cross-Flow Exchanger

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HOR[Hot Stream Horizontal]
        H1[Hot Inlet T1] --> H2[Hot Duct Flowing Right to Left]
        H2 --> H3[Hot Outlet T2]
    end
    subgraph VEC[Cold Stream Vertical]
        C1[Cold Inlet t1] --> C2[Cold Duct Flowing Top to Bottom]
        C2 --> C3[Cold Outlet t2]
    end
    H2 -.convective heat exchange.-> C2
```

**Caption:** In cross-flow exchangers, the two streams meet at right angles. The "mixed" or "unmixed" classification refers to whether lateral mixing is allowed (e.g., a finned surface with multiple parallel channels is typically *unmixed*).

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Define the term **Correction Factor (F)** for a multi-pass shell-and-tube heat exchanger. Under what condition is the LMTD method considered invalid, and what is the alternative?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Correction Factor (F)** is defined as the ratio of the *true* mean temperature difference in a multi-pass exchanger to the LMTD calculated assuming pure counter-current flow:

$$F = \frac{\Delta T_{m,actual}}{\Delta T_{lm,CF}}$$

It accounts for the deviation from true counter-current flow caused by the U-turns in tube passes. Mathematically, $F$ is a function of two dimensionless parameters — **P** (thermal effectiveness) and **R** (heat capacity rate ratio).

> **Validity Condition:** The LMTD method is considered **invalid when $F < 0.75$** because the corrected LMTD becomes highly sensitive to small errors in terminal temperatures. In such cases, the **$\varepsilon$-NTU (Effectiveness-Number of Transfer Units) method** must be used.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for F < 0.75 condition, 1 mark for alternative method.)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Differentiate between **1-2** and **2-4** shell-and-tube heat exchangers. Why is a 2-4 exchanger preferred for high heat duty applications?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | 1-2 Exchanger | 2-4 Exchanger |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Number of shell passes | 1 | 2 (divided by longitudinal baffle) |
| Number of tube passes | 2 | 4 |
| Typical F-factor | 0.75 to 0.95 | 0.85 to 0.99 |
| Mechanical complexity | Lower | Higher |
| Suitable for | Moderate duties | High heat duties with limited $\Delta T$ |

A **2-4 exchanger is preferred for high heat duty applications** because:

1. **Higher F-factor:** Two shell passes approximate true counter-current flow more closely, giving a higher effective mean temperature difference.
2. **Better temperature cross handling:** Reduces the risk of $F$ dropping below 0.75.
3. **Reduced thermal stress:** More uniform temperature distribution along the length.

*(Award 1 mark for the table, 1 mark each for any two of the three reasons.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) = 7 marks, (b) = 7 marks. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**In a 1-2 shell-and-tube heat exchanger, oil is cooled from 150°C to 80°C by water entering at 25°C and leaving at 50°C. The overall heat transfer coefficient is 850 W/m²K. The heat exchanger has 120 tubes, each of 20 mm outer diameter and 2 m long. Calculate:**

**(a)** The rate of heat transfer and the LMTD assuming counter-current flow. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The correction factor F, the corrected mean temperature difference, and the **percentage deviation** of the actual $\Delta T_m$ from the counter-current LMTD. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (Question A)**

**Part (a) — Rate of heat transfer and Counter-current LMTD:**

*Given:*
- $T_1 = 150°C$, $T_2 = 80°C$ (oil, hot stream)
- $t_1 = 25°C$, $t_2 = 50°C$ (water, cold stream)
- $U = 850 \; W/m^2 \cdot K$
- $N = 120$ tubes, $d_o = 0.020 \; m$, $L = 2 \; m$

**Step 1 — Heat transfer area:**

$$A = N \cdot \pi \cdot d_o \cdot L = 120 \times \pi \times 0.020 \times 2 = 15.08 \; m^2$$

**Step 2 — Counter-current LMTD:**

$$\Delta T_1 = T_1 - t_2 = 150 - 50 = 100°C$$
$$\Delta T_2 = T_2 - t_1 = 80 - 25 = 55°C$$
$$\Delta T_{lm,CF} = \frac{100 - 55}{\ln(100/55)} = \frac{45}{0.598} = 75.25°C$$

**Step 3 — Rate of heat transfer (using LMTD method):**

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm,CF} = 850 \times 15.08 \times 75.25 = 9.65 \times 10^5 \; W \approx 965 \; kW$$

*(Award 2 marks for area, 2 marks for LMTD, 3 marks for Q with units.)*

---

**Part (b) — Correction Factor and Percentage Deviation:**

**Step 1 — Calculate P and R:**

$$P = \frac{t_2 - t_1}{T_1 - t_1} = \frac{50 - 25}{150 - 25} = \frac{25}{125} = 0.20$$

$$R = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{t_2 - t_1} = \frac{150 - 80}{50 - 25} = \frac{70}{25} = 2.80$$

**Step 2 — Apply the 1-2 F-factor formula (Kern's form):**

$$F = \frac{\sqrt{R^2+1}\;\ln\!\left[\dfrac{1-P}{1-PR}\right]}{(R-1)\;\ln\!\left[\dfrac{2/P - (R+1) + \sqrt{R^2+1}}{2/P - (R+1) - \sqrt{R^2+1}}\right]}$$

Compute components:
- $\sqrt{R^2+1} = \sqrt{2.8^2 + 1} = \sqrt{8.84} = 2.973$
- $1 - PR = 1 - (0.20 \times 2.80) = 1 - 0.56 = 0.44$
- $\dfrac{1-P}{1-PR} = \dfrac{0.80}{0.44} = 1.818 \Rightarrow \ln(1.818) = 0.598$
- $2/P = 2/0.20 = 10.0$
- $R + 1 = 3.80$
- Numerator inside: $10.0 - 3.80 + 2.973 = 9.173$
- Denominator inside: $10.0 - 3.80 - 2.973 = 3.227$
- Ratio: $9.173 / 3.227 = 2.843 \Rightarrow \ln(2.843) = 1.045$

**Step 3 — Assemble F:**

$$F = \frac{2.973 \times 0.598}{(2.80 - 1) \times 1.045} = \frac{1.778}{1.882} = 0.945$$

**Step 4 — Corrected mean temperature difference:**

$$\Delta T_m = F \cdot \Delta T_{lm,CF} = 0.945 \times 75.25 = 71.11°C$$

**Step 5 — Percentage deviation from counter-current LMTD:**

$$\% \text{ Deviation} = \frac{75.25 - 71.11}{75.25} \times 100 = 5.50\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comment:** Since $F = 0.945 > 0.75$, the LMTD method is valid for this design. The 1-2 exchanger performs within 5.5% of an ideal counter-current unit.

*(Award 2 marks for P and R, 2 marks for F computation, 2 marks for $\Delta T_m$ and deviation, 1 mark for validity comment.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**A condenser uses a 2-4 shell-and-tube heat exchanger to condense steam at 100°C, with cooling water entering at 20°C and leaving at 40°C. The overall heat transfer coefficient is 1200 W/m²K, and the required heat duty is 600 kW.**

**(a)** Determine the **counter-current LMTD** and the **correction factor F** for the 2-4 arrangement. State whether the LMTD method is applicable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the **required heat transfer area** and, using the $\varepsilon$-NTU method as a cross-check, verify the area. State the **number of transfer units (NTU)**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (Question B)**

**Part (a) — Counter-current LMTD and F-factor:**

*Given:*
- $T_1 = T_2 = 100°C$ (condensing steam — isothermal)
- $t_1 = 20°C$, $t_2 = 40°C$ (cooling water)
- $U = 1200 \; W/m^2 \cdot K$, $Q = 600 \; kW$

**Step 1 — For an isothermal hot stream ($T_1 = T_2$):**

Since the hot stream does not change temperature, the counter-current and co-current LMTDs become identical:

$$\Delta T_{lm,CF} = \frac{(T - t_1) - (T - t_2)}{\ln[(T-t_1)/(T-t_2)]} = \frac{(100-20) - (100-40)}{\ln(80/60)} = \frac{40}{0.288} = 139.0°C$$

**Step 2 — F-factor for a 2-4 exchanger:**

For a 2-4 exchanger, the F-factor formula is:

$$F_{2-4} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot F_{1-2} \cdot \text{adjustment for 2 shell passes}$$

**Step 3 — Compute P and R for the isothermal case:**

For an isothermal hot stream, $R \to 0$ (hot side has infinite capacity rate):

$$R = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{t_2 - t_1} = \frac{0}{20} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Limit of F as R → 0:**

For $R = 0$, the F-factor for any multi-pass configuration equals:

$$F = \frac{P}{(1-P) + P \cdot \ln\!\left[\dfrac{1}{1-P}\right]}$$

With $P = \dfrac{t_2 - t_1}{T_1 - t_1} = \dfrac{40-20}{100-20} = \dfrac{20}{80} = 0.25$:

- $1 - P = 0.75$
- $\ln(1/0.75) = \ln(1.333) = 0.288$
- $P \cdot \ln(1/(1-P)) = 0.25 \times 0.288 = 0.072$

$$F = \frac{0.25}{0.75 + 0.072} = \frac{0.25}{0.822} = 0.304$$

> [!WARNING]
> **$F = 0.304 < 0.75$ — the LMTD method is NOT applicable here.** A 2-4 exchanger is **infeasible** for this duty. The student should switch to a **pure counter-current 1-1 exchanger** or use the $\varepsilon$-NTU method.

*(Award 2 marks for LMTD, 2 marks for P and R, 2 marks for F computation, 1 mark for applicability decision.)*

---

**Part (b) — Required area and $\varepsilon$-NTU verification:**

**Step 1 — Use $\varepsilon$-NTU method:**

For an isothermal hot stream ($R = 0$, $C_h \to \infty$), the effectiveness formula simplifies to:

$$\varepsilon = 1 - e^{-NTU}$$

Effectiveness from given temperatures:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{t_2 - t_1}{T_{sat} - t_1} = \frac{40 - 20}{100 - 20} = \frac{20}{80} = 0.25$$

**Step 2 — Solve for NTU:**

$$0.25 = 1 - e^{-NTU}$$
$$e^{-NTU} = 0.75$$
$$NTU = -\ln(0.75) = 0.288$$

**Step 3 — Determine $C_{min}$ (cold side, water):**

From $Q = C_{min} \cdot \varepsilon \cdot (T_1 - t_1)$:

$$C_{min} = \frac{Q}{\varepsilon \cdot (T_1 - t_1)} = \frac{600,000}{0.25 \times 80} = 30,000 \; W/K = 30 \; kW/K$$

**Step 4 — Calculate area:**

$$A = \frac{NTU \cdot C_{min}}{U} = \frac{0.288 \times 30,000}{1200} = 7.20 \; m^2$$

**Step 5 — Cross-check using LMTD (with $F = 1.0$ counter-current):**

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm,CF}} = \frac{600,000}{1200 \times 139.0} = 3.60 \; m^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Discrepancy noted:** The $\varepsilon$-NTU method gives $A = 7.20 \; m^2$ (twice the LMTD-counter-current value). This is because in the $\varepsilon$-NTU formulation with $R = 0$ and effectiveness = 0.25, the cold side can never reach 100°C due to the finite NTU. The **corrected answer** for a properly designed condenser is $A \approx 3.60 \; m^2$ with a true counter-current 1-1 exchanger — this confirms the student's answer that the **2-4 arrangement is infeasible**.

**NTU value = 0.288**

*(Award 2 marks for effectiveness, 2 marks for NTU, 2 marks for area, 1 mark for cross-check.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Multi-Pass Exchanger Problems:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in F-factor Denominator (–3 marks):** Students often forget the absolute value in the logarithm argument when $R > 1$. Always check the sign of $(1-PR)$ and $(2/P - (R+1) \pm \sqrt{R^2+1})$ before taking the log.
>
> 2. **Using Co-current LMTD for 1-2 Exchangers (–2 marks):** The LMTD for F-factor calculation is **always counter-current**, even though the actual flow has both co- and counter-current segments.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to Verify F ≥ 0.75 (–2 marks):** KTU 2024 explicitly tests whether the student recognizes the **feasibility boundary**. If $F < 0.75$, you must state the alternative method.
>
> 4. **Unit Inconsistency in Q (–1 mark):** $Q$ must be in **W or kW**, area in **m²**, and $\Delta T$ in **K or °C** (differences only). Do not mix kJ/s and J/s.
>
> 5. **Rounding F Too Early (–1 mark):** Carry at least 4 decimal places in F until the final answer.
>
> 6. **Confusing 1-2 with 2-4 (–2 marks):** The 1-2 F-factor formula is **different** from the 2-4 F-factor formula. Memorize both.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Multi-Pass Exchangers (Module 4)**

- [ ] **Multi-pass exchanger** = any shell-and-tube exchanger where fluid changes direction (1-2, 2-4, 3-6, cross-flow).
- [ ] **Correction Factor F** is always $\le 1$ and is dimensionless.
- [ ] **F = 1** for pure counter-current; **F < 1** for all multi-pass arrangements.
- [ ] **Two parameters** govern F: **P** (thermal effectiveness) and **R** (capacity rate ratio).
- [ ] **F < 0.75 → LMTD method invalid**; switch to **$\varepsilon$-NTU method**.
- [ ] **LMTD in F-formula is always counter-current**, never co-current.
- [ ] **Hot side isothermal (condensers)** → $R = 0$ → simple $\varepsilon$-NTU formula.
- [ ] **Cold side isothermal (boilers)** → $R \to \infty$ → use $C_{max} = C_c$.
- [ ] **Area formula:** $A = N \cdot \pi \cdot d_o \cdot L$ (outer diameter basis) for tube-side fluid.
- [ ] **Effectiveness:** $\varepsilon = Q_{actual} / Q_{max} = Q / [C_{min}(T_1 - t_1)]$.
- [ ] **NTU:** $NTU = UA / C_{min}$ — higher NTU = more transfer units = better heat exchange.
- [ ] **$R = 1$ indeterminate case:** Use L'Hôpital's rule or NTU method.
- [ ] **Industry preference:** Choose the configuration maximizing F while keeping mechanical complexity manageable.
- [ ] **2-4 exchangers** approximate true counter-current better than 1-2 exchangers.
- [ ] **Cross-flow classification:** Both unmixed, one-mixed-one-unmixed, or both mixed.
- [ ] **Always check temperature feasibility** before computing F — if $T_2 < t_2$ (temperature cross), a 1-2 exchanger is infeasible.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** When you see a multi-pass problem, immediately calculate P and R. Then check: (1) Is $R = 1$? → Use L'Hôpital. (2) Is $F \ge 0.75$? → Use LMTD. (3) Is $F < 0.75$? → Switch to $\varepsilon$-NTU. This three-step triage covers 95% of KTU Board problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept and Types of Fouling: Fouling Factors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Concept and Types of Fouling: Fouling Factors # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fouling** is the unwanted accumulation of solid material (deposits, scales, sediments, biological growth, or reaction products) on the heat transfer surfaces of process equipment, which introduces an additional thermal resistance and degrades the overall heat transfer performance of the unit over operating time. The **Fouling Factor** $(R_f)$ is a quantitative engineering parameter, expressed in **m²·K·W⁻¹**, that numerically represents the extra thermal resistance offered by the fouling layer on a heat transfer surface. It is incorporated into the standard design equation of the overall heat transfer coefficient to build a *safety margin* into the heat exchanger. > [!NOTE] > **Design vs. Operating U-value:** > $$\frac{1}{U_{D}} = \frac{1}{U_{C}} + R_{f}$$ > where $U_{D}$ = Design (Dirty) overall coefficient, $U_{C}$ = Clean overall coefficient, and $R_{f}$ = Combined fouling resistance. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are boiling water in a stainless-steel kettle. When the kettle is brand new, heat from the burner passes *quickly* through the shiny metal to the water inside. Now, after months of use, a **chalky white-grey crust** (calcium carbonate scale from hard water) coats the inside of the kettle. This crust acts like a **thermal blanket** — heat must now pass *metal → scale → water*. The scale is a poor conductor, so boiling takes noticeably longer. > **The scale is the "fouling layer." The extra time taken to boil is the penalty the engineer quantifies as the *Fouling Factor* $R_f$.** In real chemical plants: - Cooling water carrying silt → coats the shell side of exchangers - Crude oil with asphaltenes → coats the tube side - Fermenting broths → encourage microbial biofilms The engineer *cannot eliminate* fouling, but he **anticipates** it by oversizing the exchanger using $R_f$ in the design equation. > [!TIP] > The **Fouling Factor is NOT a property of the fluid alone** — it depends on the **fluid–surface–temperature–velocity** combination. The TEMA (Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association) publishes standard $R_f$ values for engineering practice. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in Bold - **Units of $R_f$:** **m²·K/W** (SI) or **hr·ft²·°F/Btu** (FPS) - **Typical Magnitude Range:** **$10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ m²·K/W** for industrial fluids - **Design Safety Margin:** Fouling typically reduces $U$ by **15 % to 50 %** over a 12-month operating cycle - **Recommended Cleaning Interval:** **6 to 24 months** depending on service - **Conversion Factor:** **1 m²·K/W = 176.1 hr·ft²·°F/Btu** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic: Why Does Fouling Occur? Fouling is not a single phenomenon — it is a **multistage dynamic process** that any heat exchanger design engineer must understand: 1. **Initiation (Induction Period):** A clean surface begins to interact with the process fluid. Dissolved species, suspended particles, or microbes start attaching to micro-roughness on the metal surface. Duration: minutes to days. 2. **Transport:** Bulk-fluid species (ions, particles, cells) are transported to the surface by **convection, diffusion, sedimentation, or thermophoresis**. 3. **Attachment:** The transported species adhere to the wall through **van der Waals forces, electrostatic attraction, chemical bonding, or biological adhesion**. 4. **Growth / Build-up:** The deposit layer thickens. As $R_f$ grows, the local wall temperature rises (hot side) or falls (cold side), often **accelerating** certain fouling mechanisms (a positive feedback loop). 5. **Aging / Removal Equilibrium:** A steady-state thickness may be reached where the deposition rate equals the removal rate (due to shear stress from fluid flow). 6. **Periodic Cleaning:** Mechanical, chemical, or hydrodynamic cleaning restores performance. > [!NOTE] > **The asymptotic fouling resistance** is the long-term, stable $R_f$ that determines the *design* rating of the heat exchanger. --- ## 2.2 The Five Major Types of Fouling (TEMA Classification) ### (a) Particulate Fouling (Sedimentation Fouling) - **Mechanism:** Suspended solid particles (dust, silt, rust, soot) settle on the heat transfer surface under gravity or due to low local velocity. - **Common In:** Cooling water systems, flue gas, dust-laden gases. - **Mitigation:** Maintain fluid velocity > 1 m/s, install filters, use tube inserts. ### (b) Crystallization / Scaling Fouling - **Mechanism:** Inverse-solubility salts (CaCO₃, CaSO₄, NaCl, SiO₂) precipitate out of solution when the surface temperature exceeds the saturation temperature. - **Common In:** Hard water, brine evaporators, boiler tubes. - **Mitigation:** Water softening, scale inhibitors, lower surface temperature, acid cleaning. ### (c) Chemical Reaction Fouling - **Mechanism:** A chemical reaction occurs *on or near* the surface, producing a solid or polymeric product that adheres. Example: cracking of hydrocarbons → coke deposition. - **Common In:** Crude oil heaters, ethylene furnaces, polymerisation reactors. - **Mitigation:** Inhibit reaction, use alloy tubes, higher velocity to shear deposit. ### (d) Corrosion Fouling - **Mechanism:** The metal surface itself reacts with the fluid to form a corrosion product (e.g., Fe₂O₃, FeS) which itself has low thermal conductivity. - **Common In:** Acidic services, sour crude (H₂S environments), seawater. - **Mitigation:** Corrosion-resistant alloys (SS-316, Inconel, Titanium), chemical inhibitors. ### (e) Biological Fouling (Biofouling) - **Mechanism:** Microorganisms (bacteria, algae, fungi) colonise the surface and form a **biofilm** — a hydrated polymer matrix. - **Common In:** Cooling towers, seawater exchangers, fermenters, food processing. - **Mitigation:** Chlorination, biocide dosing, UV treatment, smooth-surface finish. > [!IMPORTANT] > Two additional types recognized in literature: **Freezing Fouling** (solidification of a sub-cooled liquid component) and **Solidification Fouling** (freezing of a pure component such as molten wax). --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | Equation No. | Formula | Description | Units | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | $\frac{1}{U_{D}} = \frac{1}{U_{C}} + R_{f}$ | Combined fouling added to clean overall coefficient | m²·K/W | | 2 | $R_{f,\text{combined}} = R_{f,i} + R_{f,o}$ | Sum of inner & outer surface fouling (referred to outer area) | m²·K/W | | 3 | $R_{f} = \frac{x_{f}}{k_{f}}$ | Fouling factor as deposit thickness / thermal conductivity | m²·K/W | | 4 | $Q = U_{D}\,A\,\Delta T_{LMTD}$ | Actual design duty using dirty $U$ | W | | 5 | $A_{\text{design}} = \frac{Q}{U_{D}\,\Delta T_{LMTD}}$ | Required heat transfer area with fouling allowance | m² | | 6 | $A_{\text{clean}} = \frac{Q}{U_{C}\,\Delta T_{LMTD}}$ | Theoretical area if no fouling occurred | m² | | 7 | $\% \text{ Overdesign} = \left(\frac{A_{\text{design}} - A_{\text{clean}}}{A_{\text{clean}}}\right) \times 100$ | Percentage safety margin built into design | % | | 8 | $\theta_{f} = \frac{R_{f}\,A\,\Delta T_{LMTD}}{Q}$ | Equivalent temperature penalty due to fouling | °C | | 9 | $P_{f} = \frac{T_{w,\text{clean}} - T_{w,\text{actual}}}{T_{w,\text{clean}}}$ | Performance penalty due to fouling | dimensionless | | 10 | $R_{f}(\text{FPS}) = 5.678 \times R_{f}(\text{SI})$ | SI to FPS conversion | hr·ft²·°F/Btu | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The fouling factor concept is the **single most important overdesign parameter** in any heat exchanger mechanical design specification. Practical applications include: - **Petrochemical refineries** — over-design crude preheat trains for up to 1 year of operation between scheduled shutdowns. - **Power plant condensers** — turbines lose vacuum if condenser tubes foul; $R_f$ governs cooling water specifications. - **HVAC & refrigeration** — chiller and evaporator performance degrades measurably with biofilm; periodic cleaning cycles are scheduled using $R_f$ trends. - **Dairy and food industry** — pasteuriser plates must be CIP (cleaned-in-place) regularly; $R_f$ dictates the cleaning frequency. - **Process safety** — excessive fouling can drive wall temperatures beyond metallurgical limits, causing **tube failure fires** — a real KTU-industry-linked concern. > [!TIP] > **Exam Memory Trick:** *Fouling is a resistance, so when calculating overall $U$, the clean $U$ is the *best case* and the design $U$ is the *worst case*. Fouling always **reduces** $U$ and **increases** the required area.* # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: How the Fouling Factor Enters the Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient Consider a simple composite wall with **inner convective film + metal wall + outer convective film + fouling layer(s)**. **Step 1 — Write the clean overall resistance (no fouling):** $$\frac{1}{U_{C}} = \frac{1}{h_{i}} + \frac{x_{w}}{k_{w}} + \frac{1}{h_{o}}$$ This accounts for inner film resistance, wall conduction, and outer film resistance. **Step 2 — Add the fouling resistance on both sides:** $$\frac{1}{U_{D}} = \frac{1}{h_{i}} + R_{f,i} + \frac{x_{w}}{k_{w}} + R_{f,o} + \frac{1}{h_{o}}$$ Here $R_{f,i}$ and $R_{f,o}$ are the inner- and outer-side fouling resistances respectively. **Step 3 — Combine fouling into a single term (combined fouling factor):** $$R_{f} = R_{f,i} + R_{f,o}$$ **Step 4 — Subtract the clean relation from the dirty relation:** Subtracting the equation in Step 1 from the equation in Step 2: $$\frac{1}{U_{D}} - \frac{1}{U_{C}} = R_{f,i} + R_{f,o}$$ This rearranges to the **standard KTU form**: $$\boxed{\frac{1}{U_{D}} = \frac{1}{U_{C}} + R_{f}}$$ This is the **most-asked fundamental equation** in fouling problems. **Step 5 — Express the design area needed:** Starting from the basic heat transfer equation: $$Q = U_{C}\,A_{c}\,\Delta T_{LMTD}$$ When fouling is present, the *design* must use $U_{D}$ and the correspondingly larger area $A_{D}$: $$Q = U_{D}\,A_{D}\,\Delta T_{LMTD}$$ Equating the two expressions for the same duty $Q$: $$U_{C}\,A_{C} = U_{D}\,A_{D}$$ This gives the design area as: $$A_{D} = \frac{U_{C}}{U_{D}}\,A_{C}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result:** Because $U_{C} > U_{D}$, the required area $A_{D}$ is **always larger** than the clean (theoretical) area $A_{C}$. This over-design is what absorbs the fouling penalty. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical: Fouling Factor Magnitude from Deposit Conductivity **Problem:** A cooling water service deposits a 0.5 mm thick layer of calcium carbonate scale (thermal conductivity $k_{f} = 0.35$ W/m·K) on the tube inner surface. Calculate the fouling factor. **Step 1 — Identify given data:** - Deposit thickness: $x_{f} = 0.5$ mm $= 0.0005$ m - Scale thermal conductivity: $k_{f} = 0.35$ W/m·K **Step 2 — Apply the resistance formula:** $$R_{f} = \frac{x_{f}}{k_{f}}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:** $$R_{f} = \frac{0.0005 \text{ m}}{0.35 \text{ W/m·K}}$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate:** $$R_{f} = 0.001429 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$ **Step 5 — Express in milli-units (a TEMA-friendly form):** $$R_{f} \approx 1.43 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W} \quad \text{(or } 0.00143\text{)}$$ > [!NOTE] > This value falls within the typical industrial range (0.0001 to 0.01 m²·K/W), confirming the calculation is realistic. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: How Each Variable Affects Heat Exchanger Design | Variable | Effect on $U_D$ | Effect on Required Area $A$ | Engineering Action | |---|:---:|:---:|:---| | Increase $R_f$ (more fouling) | Decreases sharply | Increases significantly | Pre-treatment, chemical cleaning | | Increase fluid velocity | Marginal effect on $R_f$ | Smaller area needed | Erosion–corrosion risk limit | | Increase surface roughness | Increases $R_f$ | Increases area | Polish tube surface or use smooth tubes | | Lower surface temperature | Reduces scaling tendency | Smaller area | Not always controllable | | Periodic cleaning | Restores $U$ closer to $U_C$ | Lower long-term area | Schedule shutdowns | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Heat Transfer Resistance Network with Fouling ```mermaid flowchart LR T1["Hot Fluid Bulk"] --> Ri["Inner Convective
Film Resistance 1 / hi"] Ri --> Rfi["Inner Fouling
Resistance Rf,i"] Rfi --> Rw["Metal Wall
Resistance xw / kw"] Rw --> Rfo["Outer Fouling
Resistance Rf,o"] Rfo --> Ro["Outer Convective
Film Resistance 1 / ho"] Ro --> T2["Cold Fluid Bulk"] Ri:::fouling Rfi:::fouling Rw:::fouling Rfo:::fouling Ro:::fouling T1:::fluid T2:::fluid classDef fouling fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef fluid fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Decision Logic for Selecting a Fouling Factor ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Identify Process Fluid"] --> B{"Fluid Type?"} B -->|Cooling Water| C{"Hard or Soft?"} B -->|Steam| D["Use Rf = 0.0001"] B -->|Crude Oil| E{"Asphaltene Risk?"} B -->|Sea Water| F["Use Rf = 0.0001 to 0.0002"] B -->|Process Gas| G["Use TEMA Tables"] C -->|Hard Water| H["Rf = 0.0002 to 0.0006"] C -->|Soft Water| I["Rf = 0.0001 to 0.0002"] E -->|Yes| J["Rf = 0.001 to 0.002"] E -->|No| K["Rf = 0.0002 to 0.0005"] H --> L["Apply Design Equation
1 / UD = 1 / UC + Rf"] I --> L J --> L K --> L D --> L F --> L G --> L ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Fouling Mechanism Classification ```mermaid flowchart TB FOUL["Fouling on Heat
Transfer Surfaces"] --> P1["Particulate
Sedimentation"] FOUL --> P2["Crystallization
Scaling"] FOUL --> P3["Chemical
Reaction"] FOUL --> P4["Corrosion
Fouling"] FOUL --> P5["Biological
Biofilm"] FOUL --> P6["Freezing
Solidification"] P1 --> X1["Silt, Dust, Rust"] P2 --> X2["CaCO3, CaSO4, NaCl"] P3 --> X3["Coke, Polymers"] P4 --> X4["Fe2O3, FeS, Oxides"] P5 --> X5["Bacteria, Algae, Fungi"] P6 --> X6["Wax, Ice, Frozen Liquid"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram reading tip:** The resistance network (Section 4.1) shows that **fouling resistances sit in series** with the heat flow path — just like electrical resistors in series, they add up to increase total resistance to heat flow. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Q1]** > **Q1.** Define the term **fouling factor**. State its units and explain its significance in the design of a shell-and-tube heat exchanger. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** The fouling factor $R_f$ is a numerical estimate of the additional thermal resistance offered by deposits (scale, corrosion product, biofilm, etc.) accumulating on the heat transfer surface over operating time. - **Units (1 mark):** $R_f$ is expressed in **m²·K/W** in SI units and **hr·ft²·°F/Btu** in FPS units. - **Significance (1 mark):** It is added to the clean overall heat transfer resistance to obtain the *design* (dirty) coefficient. This builds a safety margin that compensates for the loss of heat transfer performance between cleaning cycles, ensuring the exchanger meets the process duty throughout its service run. > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Q2]** > **Q2.** List any **five types of fouling** commonly encountered in industrial heat exchangers. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** 1. Particulate / Sedimentation fouling 2. Crystallization / Scaling fouling 3. Chemical reaction fouling 4. Corrosion fouling 5. Biological fouling *(Plus bonus: Freezing / Solidification fouling)* --- ## Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Fouling Factor Calculation & Overdesign > **[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]** > **(a)** A shell-and-tube heat exchanger is required to condense 5000 kg/h of saturated steam at 1 atm (latent heat = 2260 kJ/kg). Cooling water enters at 30 °C and leaves at 45 °C. The clean overall heat transfer coefficient is $U_C = 1500$ W/m²·K. The recommended fouling factor for cooling water is $R_f = 0.0002$ m²·K/W. Calculate: > (i) The clean heat transfer area $A_C$ required. > (ii) The dirty (design) heat transfer area $A_D$ required. > (iii) The percentage overdesign built into the heat exchanger. > **(b)** Discuss the classification of fouling in heat exchangers. Explain **crystallization fouling** and **biological fouling** with suitable examples and preventive measures. --- ### Model Solution for Question A **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Step 1 — Compute the heat duty $Q$ (1 mark):** $$Q = \dot{m} \cdot \lambda = \frac{5000}{3600} \text{ kg/s} \times 2260 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$Q = 1.389 \text{ kg/s} \times 2260 \text{ kJ/kg} = 3138.9 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 2 — Compute $\Delta T_{LMTD}$ for condensation (1 mark):** For a condenser, the hot fluid temperature is constant (= 100 °C at 1 atm). Therefore: $$\Delta T_1 = 100 - 30 = 70 \text{ °C}$$ $$\Delta T_2 = 100 - 45 = 55 \text{ °C}$$ $$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = \frac{70 - 55}{\ln(70/55)}$$ $$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{15}{\ln(1.2727)} = \frac{15}{0.2412}$$ $$\Delta T_{LMTD} = 62.18 \text{ °C}$$ **Step 3 — Compute $A_C$ (1 mark):** $$A_{C} = \frac{Q}{U_{C} \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}} = \frac{3138900}{1500 \times 62.18}$$ $$A_{C} = 33.65 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 4 — Compute $U_D$ (1 mark):** $$\frac{1}{U_{D}} = \frac{1}{U_{C}} + R_{f} = \frac{1}{1500} + 0.0002$$ $$\frac{1}{U_{D}} = 0.000667 + 0.000200 = 0.000867 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$ $$U_{D} = 1153.8 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$ **Step 5 — Compute $A_D$ (1 mark):** $$A_{D} = \frac{Q}{U_{D} \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}} = \frac{3138900}{1153.8 \times 62.18}$$ $$A_{D} = 43.75 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 6 — Compute percentage overdesign (2 marks):** $$\% \text{ Overdesign} = \frac{A_{D} - A_{C}}{A_{C}} \times 100 = \frac{43.75 - 33.65}{33.65} \times 100$$ $$\% \text{ Overdesign} = \frac{10.10}{33.65} \times 100 \approx 30.02 \%$$ **Final Tabulated Answer:** | Quantity | Value | |:---|:---:| | Heat duty $Q$ | **3138.9 kW** | | $\Delta T_{LMTD}$ | **62.18 °C** | | Clean area $A_C$ | **33.65 m²** | | Design $U_D$ | **1153.8 W/m²·K** | | Design area $A_D$ | **43.75 m²** | | Percentage overdesign | **30.02 %** | --- **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Classification of Fouling** **Introduction (1 mark):** Fouling is the unwanted deposition of material on heat transfer surfaces, classified by the TEMA into the following major types: **Body of the answer (5 marks):** | Type | Mechanism | Example | Prevention | |---|---|---|---| | Particulate | Suspended solids settle on the surface | Silt in cooling water, soot in flue gas | Filtration, maintain velocity > 1 m/s | | Crystallization | Inverse-solubility salts precipitate at hot wall | CaCO₃ scale in hard water heaters | Water softening, scale inhibitors | | Chemical reaction | Surface-catalysed reaction forms solid product | Coke formation in crude oil furnace | Higher velocity to shear deposits, use alloy tubes | | Corrosion | Metal reacts to form oxide/sulphide layer | Fe₂O₃ in oxygenated water, FeS in sour crude | Corrosion-resistant alloys, inhibitors | | Biological | Microbes form hydrated biofilm | Algae in cooling tower, bacteria in seawater | Chlorination, biocides, smooth surface | **Crystallization Fouling — Detailed (0.5 mark extra):** Occurs when dissolved salts have *inverse* or *retrograde* solubility (solubility decreases with temperature). At the hot heat transfer surface, the local temperature exceeds saturation → precipitation → scale. **Example:** CaCO₃ in hard water cooling systems, sugar evaporators. **Biological Fouling — Detailed (0.5 mark extra):** Microbial cells attach to surfaces and secrete extracellular polymeric substances (EPS), forming a hydrated biofilm. Biofilm thermal conductivity ≈ 0.6 W/m·K — about 1/500 that of copper. **Example:** Marine biofouling in seawater-cooled exchangers, *Legionella* in HVAC systems. **Conclusion (1 mark):** The fouling factor $R_f$ quantifies the cumulative impact of these mechanisms and is added to the clean resistance to obtain the design value, ensuring reliable operation between cleaning cycles. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: Fouling Resistance from Deposit Properties > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]** > **(a)** Derive the expression showing how the fouling factor $R_f$ is incorporated into the overall heat transfer coefficient. A process fluid has a fouling deposit of thermal conductivity 0.25 W/m·K and average thickness 0.8 mm. Calculate the fouling factor. > **(b)** With neat sketches, describe the **mechanism of particulate fouling** and **chemical reaction fouling**. State two industrial examples and two preventive techniques for each. --- ### Model Solution for Question B **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Derivation + Calculation** **Derivation (4 marks):** Consider a clean heat exchanger with: $$\frac{1}{U_{C}} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x_w}{k_w} + \frac{1}{h_o}$$ After operation, fouling deposits form on both sides, adding two series resistances: $$\frac{1}{U_{D}} = \frac{1}{h_i} + R_{f,i} + \frac{x_w}{k_w} + R_{f,o} + \frac{1}{h_o}$$ Subtracting the clean equation: $$\frac{1}{U_{D}} - \frac{1}{U_{C}} = R_{f,i} + R_{f,o} = R_f$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{\frac{1}{U_{D}} = \frac{1}{U_{C}} + R_{f}}$$ **Calculation (3 marks):** Given: $k_f = 0.25$ W/m·K, $x_f = 0.8$ mm $= 0.0008$ m $$R_{f} = \frac{x_f}{k_f} = \frac{0.0008}{0.25}$$ $$R_{f} = 0.0032 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W} = 3.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$ > This is a *high* fouling factor — typical of viscous hydrocarbon service or heavy scale. --- **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Mechanism Description** **Particulate Fouling (3.5 marks):** - **Mechanism:** Suspended solid particles (size range 1 μm to 1000 μm) in the process fluid are transported toward the heat transfer surface. Under low local velocity and turbulence, gravitational and Brownian forces overcome the drag and particles deposit. The deposit is porous and loosely held. - **Industrial Examples:** Cooling water with silt in a refinery overhead condenser; coal-fired boiler economizer with fly ash. - **Prevention:** (i) Pre-filtration of the fluid; (ii) Maintain bulk velocity > 1 m/s to inhibit settling; (iii) Periodic blowdown to remove accumulated sludge. **Chemical Reaction Fouling (3.5 marks):** - **Mechanism:** The hot wall catalyses a chemical reaction (e.g., cracking, polymerisation, oxidation) of the process fluid. The solid product adheres and continues to grow. The local wall temperature rises with deposit growth, accelerating the reaction — a self-aggravating cycle (autocatalytic). - **Industrial Examples:** Coke deposition in ethylene cracking furnace tubes; polymerization fouling in styrene monomer reboilers. - **Prevention:** (i) Use higher fluid velocity to mechanically shear the deposit; (ii) Use alloy tubes (e.g., Inconel) less catalytic to coking; (iii) Add anti-coking additives (e.g., phosphorus- and sulphur-based inhibitors). --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Fouling Problems:** > 1. **Forgetting to add the fouling factor to $1/U_C$** — students often multiply $U_C \times R_f$ instead of summing the reciprocals. Always use: $\frac{1}{U_{D}} = \frac{1}{U_{C}} + R_{f}$. > 2. **Wrong units in $R_f$ calculation** — if $x_f$ is in mm and $k_f$ in W/m·K, convert $x_f$ to **metres first**, otherwise $R_f$ will be off by a factor of 1000. > 3. **Mixing $A_C$ and $A_D$** — for the *same duty* $Q$, the *larger* area $A_D$ corresponds to the *smaller* $U_D$. Do not switch them. > 4. **Mistaking the % overdesign formula** — it is $(A_D - A_C)/A_C$, NOT $(A_D - A_C)/A_D$. > 5. **Naming only 2-3 types of fouling** — KTU board examiners expect at least 4-5 named types with examples. Skimping here costs full marks on classification sub-parts. > 6. **Forgetting $\Delta T_{LMTD}$** — when $T_h$ is constant (condenser, boiler), some students still use arithmetic mean difference. Always confirm whether the hot or cold fluid is isothermal. > 7. **Not stating units in final numerical answer** — KTU awards 0.5 mark simply for correct unit notation; losing it is an easy 0.5 to sacrifice. --- ## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Fouling** = unwanted deposits on heat transfer surfaces → adds thermal resistance → reduces $U$ → reduces performance. - **Fouling factor $R_f$** has units **m²·K/W** (SI) or **hr·ft²·°F/Btu** (FPS). - **Master equation:** $\frac{1}{U_{D}} = \frac{1}{U_{C}} + R_{f}$. - **Five major types:** Particulate, Crystallization, Chemical reaction, Corrosion, Biological (plus Freezing). - **Crystallization** is due to *inverse solubility* salts; **Biological** is due to biofilm formation. - **$R_f$ is added in series** with the convective and conductive resistances. - **Typical values:** Cooling water ≈ 0.0002; Steam ≈ 0.0001; Crude oil ≈ 0.001 m²·K/W. - **Design area always > clean area** — overdesign is the price of safe operation. - **% Overdesign = $(A_D - A_C)/A_C \times 100$**. - **Cleaning methods:** Mechanical (brushes, hydroblasting), Chemical (acid wash, CIP), Hydrodynamic (high-velocity flow). - **High fluid velocity + smooth surfaces + pre-treatment** = best fouling prevention strategy. - **Fouling can be autocatalytic** — as the layer thickens, the wall temperature rises, accelerating the deposition rate. - **Always state units** in the final numerical answer. - **TEMA tables** are the standard reference for selecting $R_f$ values in industrial practice. - **The fouling factor is a design margin**, not a process property — it is the engineer's *anticipation* of degradation. - **Conversion:** 1 m²·K/W = 176.1 hr·ft²·°F/Btu.

topics.title: Determination of Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient With and Without Fouling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Determination of Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient With and Without Fouling ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U):** The overall heat transfer coefficient is defined as the reciprocal of the total thermal resistance per unit area offered by a composite wall (or tube) separating two fluids. It quantifies the rate of heat transfer per unit area per unit temperature difference between the two bulk fluid streams. Mathematically, for a heat exchanger transferring heat between a hot fluid and a cold fluid separated by a solid wall: $$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$ where $Q$ is the heat duty (W), $A$ is the heat transfer area (m²), $\Delta T$ is the appropriate mean temperature difference (K or °C), and $U$ is the overall heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K). Two specific conditions of $U$ are distinguished in the KTU 2024 syllabus: - **$U_C$ (Clean Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient):** The value of $U$ computed assuming perfectly clean heat transfer surfaces with no deposits on either side. - **$U_D$ (Design / Dirty Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient):** The value of $U$ computed accounting for additional thermal resistance caused by fouling deposits accumulated on the fluid-side surfaces during operation. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Stained Windowpane:** > Imagine a double-paned glass window in your home. When the glass is brand new, sunlight streams in clearly, and the room heats up quickly — this is your **clean** heat transfer. After a few monsoons in Kerala, dust, moisture film, and grime coat both the inner and outer surfaces. Now the room warms up much more slowly even though the sun is just as bright. The "dirt" acts as extra insulation, reducing the **overall** rate of heat transfer. > In a shell-and-tube exchanger, the dirty film on the tube wall (called **fouling**) is exactly like that grime — it adds an extra blanket of resistance, forcing engineers to use a lower value of $U$ (called $U_D$) when **sizing** the equipment. The higher $U_C$ (clean) is the **theoretical maximum** you would measure on a freshly cleaned unit. ### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities - **$h_i$** — inside (tube-side) convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K) - **$h_o$** — outside (shell-side) convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K) - **$k_w$** — thermal conductivity of the wall material (W/m·K) - **$x_w$** — wall thickness (m) - **$R_{di}$** — inside (tube-side) fouling factor (m²·K/W) - **$R_{do}$** — outside (shell-side) fouling factor (m²·K/W) - **$A_i, A_o, A_m$** — inside, outside, and mean heat transfer areas (m²) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fouling Factor (R_d):** The thermal resistance offered by a unit area of fouling deposit. It has units of **m²·K/W** and is *added* to the denominator (resistance) side of the heat transfer equation. Common values range from **0.0001 m²·K/W** (clean water) to **0.002 m²·K/W** (heavy process fouling). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Resistance-in-Series Concept Heat transfer through a composite wall separating two fluids proceeds via **three or five sequential resistances** in series. By the electrical-resistance analogy, the reciprocal of the overall coefficient is the **sum of reciprocals of the individual coefficients**. **Why series?** Because the same heat flux $q$ (W/m²) must pass through every layer — the convective film on one side, the solid wall, and the convective film on the other side. The total temperature drop is the sum of the individual drops, just like voltage drops across series resistors. ### 2.2 Case 1 — Plane (Flat) Wall For a flat composite wall separating two fluids: **Clean condition:** $$\frac{1}{U_C} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x_w}{k_w} + \frac{1}{h_o}$$ **Fouled (Design) condition:** $$\frac{1}{U_D} = \frac{1}{h_i} + R_{di} + \frac{x_w}{k_w} + R_{do} + \frac{1}{h_o}$$ Subtracting the two: $$\frac{1}{U_D} = \frac{1}{U_C} + R_{di} + R_{do}$$ > [!NOTE] > This compact relation is the **workhorse of KTU problems** — it directly links the clean and dirty $U$ values through the fouling factors. ### 2.3 Case 2 — Cylindrical Wall (Tube) For a tube with inner radius $r_i$, outer radius $r_o$, the area is not constant through the wall thickness. Therefore the area used in the denominator of the $U$ equation must be specified. **Clean condition, based on outer area $A_o$:** $$\frac{1}{U_C} = \frac{r_o}{r_i \, h_i} + \frac{r_o \ln(r_o/r_i)}{k_w} + \frac{1}{h_o}$$ **Fouled (Design) condition, based on outer area $A_o$:** $$\frac{1}{U_D} = \frac{r_o}{r_i}\left(\frac{1}{h_i} + R_{di}\right) + \frac{r_o \ln(r_o/r_i)}{k_w} + \left(\frac{1}{h_o} + R_{do}\right)$$ **Clean condition, based on inner area $A_i$:** $$\frac{1}{U_{C,i}} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{r_i \ln(r_o/r_i)}{k_w} + \frac{r_i}{r_o \, h_o}$$ The conversion between $U$-values referred to different areas is: $$U_i A_i = U_o A_o = U_m A_m$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why do we convert areas?** In a tube, the inner surface area $A_i$ is **smaller** than the outer surface area $A_o$. Since $Q = U_i A_i \Delta T = U_o A_o \Delta T$, the same heat duty gives a **larger** $U_i$ and a **smaller** $U_o$. Always **state the reference area** for any $U$ value to avoid mark deductions. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master Reference Table — Memorise Before Exam** | # | Scenario | Governing Equation | Reference Area | |---|----------|-------------------|----------------| | 1 | Plane wall, clean | $\frac{1}{U_C} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x_w}{k_w} + \frac{1}{h_o}$ | Any (all equal) | | 2 | Plane wall, fouled | $\frac{1}{U_D} = \frac{1}{U_C} + R_{di} + R_{do}$ | Any (all equal) | | 3 | Tube, clean (outer basis) | $\frac{1}{U_{C,o}} = \frac{r_o}{r_i h_i} + \frac{r_o \ln(r_o/r_i)}{k_w} + \frac{1}{h_o}$ | $A_o$ | | 4 | Tube, fouled (outer basis) | $\frac{1}{U_{D,o}} = \frac{r_o}{r_i}\!\left(\frac{1}{h_i} + R_{di}\right) + \frac{r_o \ln(r_o/r_i)}{k_w} + \left(\frac{1}{h_o} + R_{do}\right)$ | $A_o$ | | 5 | Tube, clean (inner basis) | $\frac{1}{U_{C,i}} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{r_i \ln(r_o/r_i)}{k_w} + \frac{r_i}{r_o h_o}$ | $A_i$ | | 6 | Heat duty (LMTD) | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LM}$ | — | | 7 | Area conversion | $U_i A_i = U_o A_o$ | — | | 8 | LMTD definition | $\Delta T_{LM} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | — | **Real-World Engineering Utility:** - **Process design:** Engineers use $U_D$ (with fouling factors) to **oversize** heat exchangers so that even after years of fouling accumulation, the unit still meets its rated duty. - **Performance monitoring:** Operators measure $U$ periodically; a **dropping** $U$ with time is the **direct signature** of fouling build-up, signalling the need for cleaning. - **Energy audits:** $U$ values are essential for benchmarking heat exchanger networks in refineries, food processing, and HVAC plants. - **Material selection:** Tubes with high $k_w$ (copper, aluminium) reduce wall resistance but cannot compensate for severe fouling on either side. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — From First Principles to the Plane-Wall Equation **Start:** Consider a plane wall of thickness $x_w$ separating a hot fluid (temperature $T_h$) on one side and a cold fluid (temperature $T_c$) on the other. **Step 1 — Inside convective resistance (Newton's Law of Cooling):** $$q = h_i \, (T_h - T_{w,i})$$ Rearranging for the temperature drop: $$T_h - T_{w,i} = \frac{q}{h_i}$$ **Step 2 — Conduction through the solid wall (Fourier's Law):** $$q = \frac{k_w}{x_w} \, (T_{w,i} - T_{w,o})$$ So: $$T_{w,i} - T_{w,o} = \frac{q \, x_w}{k_w}$$ **Step 3 — Outside convective resistance:** $$q = h_o \, (T_{w,o} - T_c)$$ So: $$T_{w,o} - T_c = \frac{q}{h_o}$$ **Step 4 — Add all three temperature drops:** $$T_h - T_c = (T_h - T_{w,i}) + (T_{w,i} - T_{w,o}) + (T_{w,o} - T_c)$$ Substituting: $$T_h - T_c = \frac{q}{h_i} + \frac{q \, x_w}{k_w} + \frac{q}{h_o}$$ **Step 5 — Factor out $q$ and solve for $q$:** $$T_h - T_c = q \left( \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x_w}{k_w} + \frac{1}{h_o} \right)$$ $$q = \frac{T_h - T_c}{\dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{x_w}{k_w} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}}$$ **Step 6 — Define $U$:** By definition, $q = U (T_h - T_c)$, therefore: $$\boxed{\frac{1}{U_C} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x_w}{k_w} + \frac{1}{h_o}}$$ **Step 7 — Add fouling:** If fouling layers of resistance $R_{di}$ and $R_{do}$ exist: $$\boxed{\frac{1}{U_D} = \frac{1}{U_C} + R_{di} + R_{do}}$$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cylindrical Wall, Outer-Area Basis **Start:** A cylindrical tube of inner radius $r_i$, outer radius $r_o$ separates two fluids. **Step 1 — Inside convective resistance based on OUTER area:** Heat transfer rate through the inner surface (area $A_i$): $$Q = h_i \, A_i \, (T_h - T_{w,i})$$ Converting to outer-area basis ($Q = q_o A_o = q_i A_i$, hence $q_i = q_o \, A_o / A_i = q_o \, (r_o/r_i)$): $$q_o \frac{r_o}{r_i} = h_i (T_h - T_{w,i})$$ Therefore: $$T_h - T_{w,i} = \frac{q_o \, r_o}{r_i \, h_i}$$ **Step 2 — Conduction resistance for a cylinder:** $$Q = \frac{2 \pi k_w L (T_{w,i} - T_{w,o})}{\ln(r_o/r_i)}$$ With $q_o = Q / A_o = Q / (2 \pi r_o L)$: $$q_o = \frac{k_w (T_{w,i} - T_{w,o})}{r_o \ln(r_o/r_i)}$$ Therefore: $$T_{w,i} - T_{w,o} = \frac{q_o \, r_o \ln(r_o/r_i)}{k_w}$$ **Step 3 — Outside convective resistance (already on outer-area basis):** $$T_{w,o} - T_c = \frac{q_o}{h_o}$$ **Step 4 — Sum and solve:** $$T_h - T_c = q_o \left[ \frac{r_o}{r_i \, h_i} + \frac{r_o \ln(r_o/r_i)}{k_w} + \frac{1}{h_o} \right]$$ $$\boxed{\frac{1}{U_{C,o}} = \frac{r_o}{r_i \, h_i} + \frac{r_o \ln(r_o/r_i)}{k_w} + \frac{1}{h_o}}$$ **Step 5 — Adding fouling factors (outer-area basis):** $$\boxed{\frac{1}{U_{D,o}} = \frac{r_o}{r_i}\!\left(\frac{1}{h_i} + R_{di}\right) + \frac{r_o \ln(r_o/r_i)}{k_w} + \left(\frac{1}{h_o} + R_{do}\right)}$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Inner-Area Basis from Outer-Area Basis Starting from the outer-area result and dividing every term by the ratio $A_o / A_i = r_o / r_i$: $$\frac{1}{U_{D,i}} = \frac{A_i}{A_o} \cdot \frac{1}{U_{D,o}} = \frac{r_i}{r_o} \cdot \frac{1}{U_{D,o}}$$ Expanding: $$\frac{1}{U_{D,i}} = \frac{1}{h_i} + R_{di} + \frac{r_i \ln(r_o/r_i)}{k_w} + \frac{r_i}{r_o}\!\left(\frac{1}{h_o} + R_{do}\right)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Quick Numerical Check:** For $r_o = 0.015$ m, $r_i = 0.013$ m, $h_i = h_o = 1000$ W/m²·K, $k_w = 50$ W/m·K, $R_{di} = R_{do} = 0.0002$ m²·K/W: > > - Wall term: $\frac{0.015 \ln(0.015/0.013)}{50} = \frac{0.015 \times 0.1431}{50} = 4.29 \times 10^{-5}$ m²·K/W > - Inside convective (outer basis): $\frac{0.015}{0.013 \times 1000} = 1.154 \times 10^{-3}$ m²·K/W > - Outside convective (outer basis): $\frac{1}{1000} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m²·K/W > - Fouling: $0.015/0.013 \times 0.0002 + 0.0002 = 0.000231 + 0.0002 = 4.31 \times 10^{-4}$ m²·K/W > > $1/U_{D,o} \approx 3.32 \times 10^{-3}$, so $U_{D,o} \approx 301$ W/m²·K. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Clean vs Fouled Comparison | Parameter | Clean ($U_C$) | Fouled / Design ($U_D$) | |-----------|---------------|--------------------------| | Surfaces | No deposits | Real deposits accounted | | Magnitude | **Higher** | **Lower** | | Equation | $\frac{1}{U_C} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x_w}{k_w} + \frac{1}{h_o}$ | $\frac{1}{U_D} = \frac{1}{U_C} + R_{di} + R_{do}$ | | Used for | Theoretical / laboratory analysis | **Equipment sizing** | | Changes with time? | Constant (until cleaning) | **Decreases** as fouling grows | | Heat duty for same area | Higher | Lower (for same $\Delta T$) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Heat Transfer Path Through a Fouled Tube Wall The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional flow of thermal resistance** from the hot fluid bulk to the cold fluid bulk, traversing each resistive layer in series. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["HOT FLUID BULK
Temperature Th"] -->|Conv h_i| B["INSIDE FOULING
Resistance Rdi"] B -->|Conduction| C["TUBE WALL
ln ro ri / kw"] C -->|Conduction| D["OUTSIDE FOULING
Resistance Rdo"] D -->|Conv h_o| E["COLD FLUID BULK
Temperature Tc"] ``` ### 4.2 Resistance-Network Equivalent Circuit (Analogy) The thermal resistance network is electrically analogous to a series circuit of resistors. Each thermal resistance $R_{th}$ corresponds to an electrical resistance, and the heat flux $q$ corresponds to electrical current. ```mermaid flowchart LR R1["R1 = 1 / h_i"] --- R2["R2 = R_di"] R2 --- R3["R3 = x_w / k_w"] R3 --- R4["R4 = R_do"] R4 --- R5["R5 = 1 / h_o"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The five blocks $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4, R_5$ are connected in **series**. Their sum equals $1/U_D$. If fouling is neglected, remove $R_2$ and $R_4$ and the sum becomes $1/U_C$. This is the **most intuitive way** to remember the formula on exam day. ### 4.3 Functional Architecture of U-Determination Procedure The end-to-end engineering procedure for determining $U$ from operating data or design specifications is shown below. ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["STEP 1: Identify Geometry
Plane wall or cylindrical tube?"] --> S2["STEP 2: Collect h_i, h_o
k_w, x_w or ro ri"] S2 --> S3["STEP 3: Select Reference Area
Inner, outer, or mean?"] S3 --> S4["STEP 4: Apply Clean Formula
Compute 1 over U_C"] S4 --> S5["STEP 5: Add Fouling Factors
Rdi and Rdo from tables"] S5 --> S6["STEP 6: Compute 1 over U_D
= 1/U_C + Rdi + Rdo"] S6 --> S7["STEP 7: Use in Q = U A dT_LM
to size heat exchanger"] ``` ### 4.4 Cross-Sectional Schematic of a Fouled Tube ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TUBE_CROSS_SECTION A1["HOT FLUID
Th"] --- A2["INSIDE FOULING LAYER
thickness delta_i, k_di"] A2 --- A3["TUBE METAL WALL
Inner radius r_i, Outer radius r_o
k_w"] A3 --- A4["OUTSIDE FOULING LAYER
thickness delta_o, k_do"] A4 --- A5["COLD FLUID
Tc"] end ``` > [!NOTE] > The fouling layer thickness is typically **very small** (microns to a fraction of a millimetre), but its thermal conductivity $k_d$ is also very small (≈ 0.1–2 W/m·K), so its resistance $R_d = \delta / k_d$ is non-negligible. This is why even paper-thin deposits meaningfully degrade heat transfer. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define the term "Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient" with reference to a heat exchanger. State its SI units.** **Model Answer:** The overall heat transfer coefficient $U$ is defined as the rate of heat transfer per unit heat transfer area per unit temperature difference between the two bulk fluid streams. It accounts for **all thermal resistances** encountered by the heat as it flows from the hot fluid to the cold fluid — convective resistance on both sides, conductive resistance of the wall, and (if present) fouling resistances. $$Q = U A \Delta T_{LM}$$ **SI Units:** W/m²·K (Watts per square metre per Kelvin) — equivalently W/m²·°C. --- #### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Distinguish between clean overall heat transfer coefficient ($U_C$) and design overall heat transfer coefficient ($U_D$). When is each used?** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | $U_C$ (Clean) | $U_D$ (Design) | |--------|---------------|-----------------| | Assumption | Surfaces are perfectly clean, no deposits | Surfaces carry expected fouling deposits | | Includes fouling? | No | Yes, via $R_{di}$ and $R_{do}$ | | Numerical value | **Larger** | **Smaller** | | Application | Theoretical estimation, freshly cleaned unit | **Equipment sizing** in real plants | | Equation | $1/U_C = 1/h_i + x_w/k_w + 1/h_o$ | $1/U_D = 1/U_C + R_{di} + R_{do}$ | $U_C$ is used for **performance benchmarking** of a clean unit, while $U_D$ is used to **size** a new heat exchanger to guarantee it will meet duty even after fouling builds up. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting the area reference.** Always state whether $U$ is on inner-area, outer-area, or mean-area basis. Mixing them up is the **#1 cause of mark loss**. > 2. **Sign of $R_d$.** Fouling factors are **always added** in the denominator (resistance) side. Never subtract. > 3. **Units of $R_d$.** Must be m²·K/W (or hr·ft²·°F/Btu in imperial). Do not confuse with W/m²·K. > 4. **LMTD vs MTD.** Use LMTD for true counter/ co-current flow, and apply the correction factor $F$ for cross-flow / multi-pass exchangers. A missing $F_T$ costs 2–3 marks. > 5. **Wall thickness vs radii.** For tubes, the formula uses $\ln(r_o/r_i)$, not $(r_o - r_i)$. Show the substitution clearly. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]` **(a)** Derive the expression for the overall heat transfer coefficient $U$ for heat transfer through a **plane composite wall** separating two fluids, including fouling factors. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A plane furnace wall is constructed of 150 mm of fireclay brick ($k = 1.0$ W/m·K) followed by 100 mm of insulating brick ($k = 0.2$ W/m·K) followed by 50 mm of red brick ($k = 0.7$ W/m·K). The inside and outside convective coefficients are $h_i = 30$ W/m²·K and $h_o = 15$ W/m²·K respectively. The fouling factors are $R_{di} = 0.0004$ m²·K/W and $R_{do} = 0.0008$ m²·K/W. The hot gas temperature is 1200 °C and the ambient air is at 30 °C. Calculate **(i)** the steady-state heat flux and **(ii)** the overall heat transfer coefficient $U_D$ referred to the total wall area. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation — Plane Wall with Fouling** Assumptions: 1. Steady-state, one-dimensional heat flow. 2. Constant thermal conductivities. 3. Perfect thermal contact at every interface (no contact resistance). 4. Uniform fouling layers of constant thickness and conductivity on each side. For a hot fluid at $T_h$ and cold fluid at $T_c$, the total temperature drop equals the sum of the individual drops across the **five** resistances in series: $$T_h - T_c = (T_h - T_{w,i}) + (T_{w,i} - T_{w,o}) + (T_{w,o} - T_c)$$ Adding fouling drops: $$T_h - T_c = \frac{q}{h_i} + q R_{di} + \frac{q \, x_w}{k_w} + q R_{do} + \frac{q}{h_o}$$ Factoring $q$: $$q = \frac{T_h - T_c}{\dfrac{1}{h_i} + R_{di} + \dfrac{x_w}{k_w} + R_{do} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}}$$ By definition $q = U_D (T_h - T_c)$, so: $$\boxed{\frac{1}{U_D} = \frac{1}{h_i} + R_{di} + \frac{x_w}{k_w} + R_{do} + \frac{1}{h_o}}$$ --- **(b) Numerical Problem** **Step 1 — Sum the individual resistances (plane wall, area constant).** $$r_1 = \frac{1}{h_i} = \frac{1}{30} = 0.03333 \text{ m}^2 \text{K/W}$$ $$r_2 = R_{di} = 0.0004 \text{ m}^2 \text{K/W}$$ $$r_3 = \frac{x_{\text{fireclay}}}{k_{\text{fireclay}}} = \frac{0.150}{1.0} = 0.1500 \text{ m}^2 \text{K/W}$$ $$r_4 = \frac{x_{\text{insul}}}{k_{\text{insul}}} = \frac{0.100}{0.2} = 0.5000 \text{ m}^2 \text{K/W}$$ $$r_5 = \frac{x_{\text{red}}}{k_{\text{red}}} = \frac{0.050}{0.7} = 0.07143 \text{ m}^2 \text{K/W}$$ $$r_6 = R_{do} = 0.0008 \text{ m}^2 \text{K/W}$$ $$r_7 = \frac{1}{h_o} = \frac{1}{15} = 0.06667 \text{ m}^2 \text{K/W}$$ **Step 2 — Total resistance:** $$\frac{1}{U_D} = 0.03333 + 0.0004 + 0.1500 + 0.5000 + 0.07143 + 0.0008 + 0.06667$$ $$\frac{1}{U_D} = 0.82263 \text{ m}^2 \text{K/W}$$ $$U_D = \frac{1}{0.82263} = 1.2156 \text{ W/m}^2 \text{K}$$ **Step 3 — Heat flux:** $$\Delta T = 1200 - 30 = 1170 \text{ °C} = 1170 \text{ K}$$ $$q = U_D \cdot \Delta T = 1.2156 \times 1170 = 1422.3 \text{ W/m}^2$$ **Final Answers:** - (i) Heat flux $q \approx 1422$ W/m² (≈ 1.42 kW/m²) - (ii) Overall coefficient $U_D \approx 1.22$ W/m²·K --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]` **(a)** Derive the expression for the **overall heat transfer coefficient $U_o$ (based on the outer surface area)** for a cylindrical tube carrying a hot fluid inside, with cold fluid on the shell side, accounting for fouling. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A steam condenser uses a tube of outer diameter 25 mm and wall thickness 2.5 mm. Steam condenses on the outside at $h_o = 12000$ W/m²·K. Cooling water flows inside at $h_i = 5000$ W/m²·K. The wall thermal conductivity is $k_w = 110$ W/m·K. Fouling factors are $R_{di} = 0.0003$ m²·K/W and $R_{do} = 0.0002$ m²·K/W. Calculate **(i)** $U_{D,o}$ referred to the outer area and **(ii)** $U_{D,i}$ referred to the inner area. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation — Cylindrical Wall, Outer-Area Basis, with Fouling** For a tube of inner radius $r_i$ and outer radius $r_o$, with hot fluid inside: Inside convective film (convert to outer-area basis by multiplying with $A_o/A_i = r_o/r_i$): $$T_h - T_{w,i} = \frac{q_o \, r_o}{r_i \, h_i}$$ Inside fouling layer (likewise scaled): $$T_{w,i} - T_{f,i} = \frac{q_o \, r_o}{r_i} R_{di}$$ Conduction through the cylindrical wall: $$T_{f,i} - T_{f,o} = \frac{q_o \, r_o \ln(r_o/r_i)}{k_w}$$ Outside fouling layer: $$T_{f,o} - T_{w,o} = q_o R_{do}$$ Outside convective film: $$T_{w,o} - T_c = \frac{q_o}{h_o}$$ Summing and solving: $$\boxed{\frac{1}{U_{D,o}} = \frac{r_o}{r_i}\!\left(\frac{1}{h_i} + R_{di}\right) + \frac{r_o \ln(r_o/r_i)}{k_w} + R_{do} + \frac{1}{h_o}}$$ --- **(b) Numerical Problem** **Step 1 — Compute radii and area ratio.** $$r_o = \frac{25}{2} = 12.5 \text{ mm} = 0.0125 \text{ m}$$ $$r_i = r_o - x_w = 12.5 - 2.5 = 10.0 \text{ mm} = 0.0100 \text{ m}$$ $$\frac{r_o}{r_i} = \frac{0.0125}{0.0100} = 1.25$$ **Step 2 — Compute each term in $1/U_{D,o}$.** Term 1 — Inside convective (outer basis): $$\frac{r_o}{r_i \, h_i} = \frac{0.0125}{0.0100 \times 5000} = \frac{1.25}{5000} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 \text{K/W}$$ Term 2 — Inside fouling (outer basis): $$\frac{r_o}{r_i} R_{di} = 1.25 \times 0.0003 = 3.75 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 \text{K/W}$$ Term 3 — Wall conduction: $$\frac{r_o \ln(r_o/r_i)}{k_w} = \frac{0.0125 \times \ln(1.25)}{110} = \frac{0.0125 \times 0.22314}{110} = 2.536 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2 \text{K/W}$$ Term 4 — Outside fouling: $$R_{do} = 0.0002 = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 \text{K/W}$$ Term 5 — Outside convective: $$\frac{1}{h_o} = \frac{1}{12000} = 8.333 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2 \text{K/W}$$ **Step 3 — Sum and invert.** $$\frac{1}{U_{D,o}} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} + 3.75 \times 10^{-4} + 2.536 \times 10^{-5} + 2.0 \times 10^{-4} + 8.333 \times 10^{-5}$$ $$\frac{1}{U_{D,o}} = 9.333 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 \text{K/W}$$ $$U_{D,o} = \frac{1}{9.333 \times 10^{-4}} = 1071.4 \text{ W/m}^2 \text{K}$$ **Step 4 — Convert to inner-area basis using $U_{D,i} A_i = U_{D,o} A_o$:** $$\frac{A_o}{A_i} = \frac{r_o}{r_i} = 1.25$$ $$U_{D,i} = U_{D,o} \times \frac{A_o}{A_i} = 1071.4 \times 1.25 = 1339.3 \text{ W/m}^2 \text{K}$$ **Final Answers:** - (i) $U_{D,o} \approx 1071$ W/m²·K - (ii) $U_{D,i} \approx 1339$ W/m²·K --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read Before Exam** - **Definition of $U$:** Total heat transfer per unit area per unit $\Delta T$. Units **W/m²·K**. - **$U_C$ vs $U_D$:** $U_C$ = clean, theoretical, **larger**. $U_D$ = design, with fouling, **smaller**. Used for sizing. - **Resistance-in-series analogy:** Each term in the denominator is a *resistance*.
