Add them; invert to get $U$. - **Plane-wall formula (fouled):** $\frac{1}{U_D} = \frac{1}{h_i} + R_{di} + \frac{x_w}{k_w} + R_{do} + \frac{1}{h_o}$ - **Compact relation:** $\frac{1}{U_D} = \frac{1}{U_C} + R_{di} + R_{do}$ — **most useful for KTU problems**. - **Tube formula (outer basis):** $\frac{1}{U_{D,o}} = \frac{r_o}{r_i}\!\left(\frac{1}{h_i} + R_{di}\right) + \frac{r_o \ln(r_o/r_i)}{k_w} + R_{do} + \frac{1}{h_o}$ - **Area conversion:** Always state the reference. $U_i A_i = U_o A_o$. Inner-area $U$ is always **greater** than outer-area $U$ for $r_o > r_i$. - **Wall thickness vs radii:** In tubes, use $\ln(r_o/r_i)$ — **never** substitute $(r_o - r_i)$ as a linear approximation; show the log term. - **Fouling factor units:** m²·K/W. It is **always** added in the denominator; never subtracted. Typical range: $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-2}$. - **Heat duty equation:** $Q = U A \Delta T_{LM}$ (for true counter/ co-current). For multi-pass exchangers, multiply LMTD by correction factor $F_T$ ($Q = U A F_T \Delta T_{LM}$). - **Controlling resistance:** The *largest* term in the resistance sum is the **rate-limiting** resistance — reducing it yields the biggest improvement. Look for the dominant term first. - **Fouling monitoring:** A drop in $U$ over time = fouling build-up. Use $R_d$ data tables (e.g., TEMA standards) to estimate cleaning frequency. - **Quick numeric sanity check:** For typical water-to-water exchangers, $U$ ranges from **500 to 3000 W/m²·K**; for gas-to-gas, **5–50 W/m²·K**; for condensing steam, **1000–6000 W/m²·K**.

topics.title: Derivation of Expression for LMTD
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Derivation of Expression for LMTD

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD):**
> The Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD) is defined as the **arithmetically weighted logarithmic mean of the temperature differences between the hot and cold fluids at the two ends (inlet and outlet) of a heat exchanger**. It represents the *effective average driving temperature difference* available for heat transfer across the entire surface area of the heat exchanger. KTU 2024 syllabus designates this as the most fundamental temperature-driving-force parameter for sizing any continuous-flow heat exchanger under steady-state operation.

Mathematically, the expression form universally used in KTU examinations is:

$$\Delta T_{LM} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}$$

Where the end-temperature differences $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ depend on the **flow configuration** of the heat exchanger.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English Explanation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Sliding Down a Hill" Picture:**
> Imagine a hot cup of tea (hot fluid at 90 °C) cooling down to 30 °C in a room. At the *start*, the temperature difference between the tea and the room is huge (90 − 25 = 65 °C), so heat flows out very fast. As the tea cools, the difference shrinks, and the heat flow rate slows down. The *average* driving force is *not* simply the arithmetic mean of the two end-differences. Because the driving force decays **exponentially** (logarithmically) with the area traversed, the proper average is the **logarithmic mean**.
>
> **Another Analogy:** If you are paying off a loan with compounding interest, the average balance you owe is the *geometric/logarithmic* mean, not the simple arithmetic mean. Heat transfer is exactly analogous — the driving force decays exponentially with heat-exchanger area, hence LMTD is the correct average.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Units of LMTD:** Degrees Celsius (°C) or Kelvin (K). The *difference* in temperature is the same in both scales.
- **Log Mean vs Arithmetic Mean:** For any two unequal positive numbers $a$ and $b$, the logarithmic mean is always **less than the arithmetic mean** but **greater than the geometric mean**:
$$\sqrt{ab} \;<\; \frac{a-b}{\ln(a/b)} \;<\; \frac{a+b}{2}$$
- **Convention for End-Differences:** By KTU convention, $\Delta T_1$ is taken at the end where the temperature difference is *larger*, and $\Delta T_2$ at the end where it is *smaller*. This guarantees $\Delta T_1 > \Delta T_2$ and a positive LMTD.

### 1.4 Why LMTD is Needed (Engineering Motivation)

For a *differential* area $dA$, the rate of heat transfer is governed by the elementary Fourier-Newton law:

$$dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot \Delta T$$

where $U$ is the **overall heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)**. But $\Delta T$ is *not constant* along the length of an industrial heat exchanger — it varies from inlet to outlet. Therefore, integrating this equation requires an *effective mean* $\Delta T$, which is precisely the LMTD. The integrated design equation becomes:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LM}$$

This single elegant relationship is the cornerstone of KTU Module 4 heat-exchanger design.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Physical Assumptions

The classical LMTD derivation rests on the following **steady-state, single-pass** assumptions (these are *frequently asked* in KTU exams as short notes):

1. **Steady-state operation** — fluid temperatures at any given location do not change with time.
2. **Constant overall heat transfer coefficient $U$** along the length.
3. **Constant mass flow rates** $\dot{m}_h$ and $\dot{m}_c$ with no phase change.
4. **Constant specific heats** $C_{ph}$ and $C_{pc}$ (no strong temperature dependence).
5. **No axial conduction** along the metal wall or through the fluids.
6. **Negligible heat losses** to the surroundings (perfectly insulated exchanger).
7. **No longitudinal mixing** (pure plug flow in both fluids).

### 2.2 Why a "Log Mean" and Not an "Arithmetic Mean"?

The derivation below will rigorously show that the temperature difference $\Delta T(x)$ varies **exponentially** with the area traversed. Whenever a quantity decays exponentially, its proper average is the **logarithmic mean** — not the simple arithmetic mean. This is the deep "Why" behind the formula.

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Insight:** A common KTU exam trap asks students to use the *arithmetic* mean. The arithmetic mean **overestimates** the true average driving force, leading to an **under-design** (smaller area than actually needed) — a potentially dangerous error in real engineering practice.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | KTU 2024 Importance |
|---|----------|-------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LM}$ | Fundamental design equation for heat exchangers | ★★★★★ Most Tested |
| 2 | $\Delta T_{LM} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$ | Log Mean Temperature Difference | ★★★★★ Definition |
| 3 | $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - t_{c,out};\; \Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - t_{c,in}$ | Counter-current end differences | ★★★★★ Most Tested |
| 4 | $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - t_{c,in};\; \Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - t_{c,out}$ | Co-current (parallel) end differences | ★★★★ |
| 5 | $Q = \dot{m}_h C_{ph}(T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})$ | Energy balance — hot stream | ★★★★★ |
| 6 | $Q = \dot{m}_c C_{pc}(t_{c,out} - t_{c,in})$ | Energy balance — cold stream | ★★★★★ |
| 7 | $C_h = \dot{m}_h C_{ph};\; C_c = \dot{m}_c C_{pc}$ | Heat-capacity rates (W/K) | ★★★★ |
| 8 | $C_{\min} = \min(C_h, C_c)$ | Minimum heat-capacity rate (for NTU method) | ★★★ |
| 9 | $C_r = C_{\min}/C_{\max}$ | Heat-capacity ratio | ★★★ |
| 10 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max,possible}}$ | Effectiveness (for cross-check) | ★★★ |
| 11 | $\Delta T_{LM,CF} = F \cdot \Delta T_{LM,CC}$ | Correction factor $F$ for multi-pass shell-and-tube | ★★★★ |
| 12 | When $\Delta T_1 \approx \Delta T_2$, $\Delta T_{LM} \approx (\Delta T_1 + \Delta T_2)/2$ | L'Hôpital's limiting case | ★★★★ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Reminder (KTU 2024):** $Q$ in **W**, $U$ in **W/m²·K**, $A$ in **m²**, $\Delta T_{LM}$ in **K** or **°C** (differences are scale-invariant).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

LMTD is the workhorse of the **rating problem** in:

- **Chemical process industries:** designing shell-and-tube exchangers for refinery pre-heat trains, ammonia condensers, reboilers.
- **Power generation:** feedwater heaters, condensers, and economizers in thermal power plants.
- **HVAC&R:** chilled-water coils, evaporators, and condensers in refrigeration cycles.
- **Pharma & food:** pasteurizers, sterilizers — any process that must meet a precise outlet temperature.
- **Cryogenics:** LNG vaporizers and air-separation heat exchangers where temperature differences are very small and highly non-linear.

The LMTD method is preferred for *rating* existing exchangers; the **NTU-effectiveness** method (Module 5) is preferred when *outlet temperatures are unknown*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivation of the LMTD Expression

### 3.1 Schematic Setup — Counter-Current Heat Exchanger

Consider a **single-pass, counter-current** heat exchanger (the KTU-standard reference case).

Let us define the following variables at any section located at distance $x$ from the cold-fluid inlet:

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| $T$ | Bulk temperature of the **hot** fluid at section $x$ |
| $t$ | Bulk temperature of the **cold** fluid at section $x$ |
| $\Delta T = T - t$ | Local driving temperature difference |
| $U$ | Overall heat transfer coefficient (assumed constant) |

**Boundary (terminal) values:**

- Hot fluid: enters at $T_1$ (inlet), exits at $T_2$ (outlet)
- Cold fluid: enters at $t_1$ (inlet), exits at $t_2$ (outlet)
- For counter-current: $T_1 > T_2 > t_2 > t_1$

The end temperature differences are:

$$\Delta T_1 = T_1 - t_2 \quad \text{(at the hot-inlet / cold-outlet end)}$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_2 - t_1 \quad \text{(at the hot-outlet / cold-inlet end)}$$

Note: $\Delta T_1 > \Delta T_2$ for a counter-current exchanger (the *larger* difference is on the side where the hot fluid is *hottest*).

### 3.2 Energy Balance Across a Differential Element

Consider a **differential slice** of the heat exchanger of area $dA$ at location $x$. Apply an energy balance on the *hot* fluid over this slice. The hot fluid loses heat as it flows:

$$dQ = -\dot{m}_h \, C_{ph} \, dT$$

The negative sign appears because $T$ **decreases** in the flow direction (so $dT < 0$ and $dQ > 0$).

Similarly, the cold fluid gains heat:

$$dQ = \dot{m}_c \, C_{pc} \, dt$$

(The cold fluid temperature $t$ **increases**, so $dt > 0$.)

By Fourier's law applied to the differential area:

$$dQ = U \, dA \, (T - t) = U \, dA \, \Delta T$$

Equating all three expressions:

$$U \, dA \, (T - t) \;=\; -\dot{m}_h \, C_{ph} \, dT \;=\; \dot{m}_c \, C_{pc} \, dt$$

### 3.3 Isolating the Driving-Force Variation

From the first and second equalities, rearrange to isolate $dT$ and $dt$ in terms of $\Delta T$:

$$dT = -\frac{U \, dA}{\dot{m}_h \, C_{ph}} \, (T - t)$$

$$dt = +\frac{U \, dA}{\dot{m}_c \, C_{pc}} \, (T - t)$$

Subtracting the first equation from the second gives the differential change of the *driving force itself*:

$$d(T - t) \;=\; dt - dT \;=\; \frac{U \, dA}{\dot{m}_c \, C_{pc}} (T-t) \;-\; \left[-\frac{U \, dA}{\dot{m}_h \, C_{ph}} (T-t)\right]$$

$$d(T - t) \;=\; \frac{U \, dA}{\dot{m}_c \, C_{pc}} (T-t) \;+\; \frac{U \, dA}{\dot{m}_h \, C_{ph}} (T-t)$$

Factor out $(T-t)$:

$$d(T - t) \;=\; U \left(\frac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{ph}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{pc}}\right) (T - t) \, dA$$

Let us define the combined coefficient for compactness:

$$\frac{1}{K_0} \;=\; \frac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{ph}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{pc}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_0 = \frac{\dot{m}_h C_{ph} \cdot \dot{m}_c C_{pc}}{\dot{m}_h C_{ph} + \dot{m}_c C_{pc}}$$

Then:

$$d(T - t) \;=\; \frac{U}{K_0} \, (T - t) \, dA$$

### 3.4 Separation of Variables and Integration

Rearrange into separable form:

$$\frac{d(T - t)}{T - t} \;=\; \frac{U}{K_0} \, dA$$

Integrate from the cold-inlet end (where $A = 0$, $T = T_2$, $t = t_1$, so $\Delta T = T_2 - t_1 = \Delta T_2$) to the hot-inlet end (where $A = A_{total}$, $T = T_1$, $t = t_2$, so $\Delta T = T_1 - t_2 = \Delta T_1$):

$$\int_{\Delta T_2}^{\Delta T_1} \frac{d(T - t)}{T - t} \;=\; \frac{U}{K_0} \int_0^{A} dA$$

Performing the integration:

$$\bigl[\ln(T - t)\bigr]_{\Delta T_2}^{\Delta T_1} \;=\; \frac{U \cdot A}{K_0}$$

$$\ln(\Delta T_1) - \ln(\Delta T_2) \;=\; \frac{U \cdot A}{K_0}$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right) \;=\; \frac{U \cdot A}{K_0}$$

### 3.5 Eliminating $K_0$ Using the Overall Energy Balance

The total heat transferred from the hot fluid equals the heat gained by the cold fluid:

$$Q \;=\; \dot{m}_h C_{ph} (T_1 - T_2) \;=\; \dot{m}_c C_{pc} (t_2 - t_1)$$

From these, we can express $K_0$ in terms of $Q$:

$$K_0 = \frac{\dot{m}_h C_{ph} \cdot \dot{m}_c C_{pc}}{\dot{m}_h C_{ph} + \dot{m}_c C_{pc}} = \frac{\dot{m}_h C_{ph} (T_1 - T_2) \cdot \dot{m}_c C_{pc} (t_2 - t_1) / (T_1 - T_2)(t_2 - t_1)}{\dot{m}_h C_{ph} + \dot{m}_c C_{pc}}$$

A cleaner way: note that

$$\frac{1}{K_0} = \frac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{ph}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{pc}} = \frac{\dot{m}_h C_{ph} + \dot{m}_c C_{pc}}{\dot{m}_h C_{ph} \cdot \dot{m}_c C_{pc}}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $(T_1 - T_2)(t_2 - t_1)$ and using the overall energy balance:

$$Q = \dot{m}_h C_{ph}(T_1 - T_2) = \dot{m}_c C_{pc}(t_2 - t_1)$$

So

$$\dot{m}_h C_{ph}(T_1 - T_2) \cdot \dot{m}_c C_{pc}(t_2 - t_1) = Q^2$$

and

$$\dot{m}_h C_{ph} + \dot{m}_c C_{pc} = \frac{Q}{T_1 - T_2} + \frac{Q}{t_2 - t_1} = Q \cdot \frac{(t_2 - t_1) + (T_1 - T_2)}{(T_1 - T_2)(t_2 - t_1)}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{1}{K_0} = \frac{Q \, [(t_2 - t_1) + (T_1 - T_2)]}{Q^2} = \frac{(T_1 - T_2) + (t_2 - t_1)}{Q}$$

Rearranging, the *denominator* $(T_1 - T_2) + (t_2 - t_1)$ can be rewritten in terms of $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$:

$$(T_1 - T_2) + (t_2 - t_1) = (T_1 - t_2) - (T_2 - t_1) = \Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2$$

Substituting back:

$$\frac{1}{K_0} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{Q}$$

Inverting:

$$K_0 = \frac{Q}{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}$$

### 3.6 Final Assembly — The LMTD Equation

Substitute $K_0$ into the integrated expression from Section 3.4:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right) = \frac{U \cdot A}{Q} \cdot (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)$$

Solve for $Q$:

$$Q = \frac{U \cdot A \, (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$$

Rearrange into the standard *design* form:

$$\boxed{\,Q \;=\; U \, A \, \Delta T_{LM}\,} \quad \text{where} \quad \boxed{\,\Delta T_{LM} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}\,}$$

This is the celebrated **Log Mean Temperature Difference** for a counter-current, single-pass heat exchanger.

### 3.7 Extension to Co-Current (Parallel-Flow) Configuration

Repeating the entire derivation with the *cold* and *hot* fluids moving in the **same direction** gives exactly the same final expression for $\Delta T_{LM}$, but the **end-difference identification** changes:

- **Co-current:** $\Delta T_1 = T_1 - t_1$ (at the common inlet), $\Delta T_2 = T_2 - t_2$ (at the common outlet)
- **Counter-current:** $\Delta T_1 = T_1 - t_2$ (at hot-inlet end), $\Delta T_2 = T_2 - t_1$ (at hot-outlet end)

For *co-current* flow, one can prove that $T_2 > t_2$ always, so the cold fluid can never exceed the hot outlet temperature — a key limitation absent in counter-current flow.

### 3.8 Special Limiting Case (Frequently Tested)

When $\Delta T_1 \to \Delta T_2$, the LMTD expression becomes the indeterminate form $0/0$. Applying **L'Hôpital's Rule** (since $\Delta T_1, \Delta T_2 > 0$):

$$\lim_{\Delta T_2/\Delta T_1 \to 1} \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)} \;=\; \lim_{x \to 1} \frac{-1}{1/x} = \lim_{x \to 1} x = 1 \cdot \Delta T_1$$

Hence in the limit of equal end-differences:

$$\Delta T_{LM} \;\to\; \Delta T_1 \;=\; \Delta T_2$$

i.e., the LMTD collapses to the *arithmetic mean* (and is in fact equal to both ends, since they are equal).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Frequently Tested Takeaway:** If a problem states that the two terminal temperature differences are *equal*, the student should immediately recognize this special case and avoid wasting time with logarithms. The arithmetic mean = LMTD in this limit.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Heat Exchanger Rating Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram summarizes the logical flow of a *heat-exchanger rating problem* that uses the LMTD relation derived above. This is the KTU-recommended computational workflow for Module 4 design problems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Data: Inlet and Outlet Temperatures T1 T2 t1 t2] --> B[Compute Energy Balance Q from hot fluid Qh or cold fluid Qc]
    B --> C[Verify Qh equals Qc within 2 percent tolerance]
    C --> D[Identify Flow Configuration Counter current or Co current]
    D --> E[Compute End Temperature Differences Delta T1 and Delta T2]
    E --> F{Is Delta T1 equal to Delta T2?}
    F -- Yes --> G[LMTD equals Delta T1 equals Delta T2]
    F -- No --> H[Apply LMTD Formula Delta T1 minus Delta T2 divided by ln Delta T1 over Delta T2]
    G --> I[Select Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient U from standard correlations]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Compute Heat Transfer Area A equals Q divided by U times LMTD]
    J --> K[Round to nearest standard exchanger size]
    K --> L[Output Final Design Summary]
```

### 4.2 Counter-Current vs Co-Current — Functional Comparison Diagram

The following diagram maps the *functional differences* in flow configuration that lead to different end-difference identifications in the LMTD formula.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CC[Counter Current Flow]
        CC1[Hot Fluid Inlet T1] --> CC2[Hot Fluid Outlet T2]
        CC3[Cold Fluid Inlet t1] --> CC4[Cold Fluid Outlet t2]
        CC2 -. Direction .-> CC3
        CC4 -. Direction .-> CC1
    end
    subgraph PC[Co Current Parallel Flow]
        PC1[Hot Fluid Inlet T1] --> PC2[Hot Fluid Outlet T2]
        PC3[Cold Fluid Inlet t1] --> PC4[Cold Fluid Outlet t2]
        PC1 -. same direction .- PC3
        PC2 -. same direction .- PC4
    end
    CC1 --> R1[Delta T1 equals T1 minus t2]
    CC2 --> R1
    R1 --> S1[LMTD Counter]
    CC3 --> R2[Delta T2 equals T2 minus t1]
    R2 --> S1
    PC1 --> R3[Delta T1 equals T1 minus t1]
    PC3 --> R3
    R3 --> S2[LMTD Co current]
    PC2 --> R4[Delta T2 equals T2 minus t2]
    R4 --> S2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** In *counter-current* flow, the cold fluid meets the *hottest* part of the hot fluid at the cold-outlet end (yielding $\Delta T_1 = T_1 - t_2$). In *co-current* flow, both fluids share the same inlet, so the largest difference is at the common inlet ($\Delta T_1 = T_1 - t_1$). This is why counter-current exchangers always have a *larger* LMTD for the same inlet/outlet temperatures — they are thermodynamically more efficient.

### 4.3 The Energy-Balance Differential Element

The following schematic block illustrates the differential area $dA$ slice used in the derivation, with all three heat-transfer mechanisms (Fourier, hot-stream loss, cold-stream gain) feeding into the energy balance.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H[Hot Stream Temperature T] -->|loses heat| EL[Energy Balance on Slice dA]
    C[Cold Stream Temperature t] -->|gains heat| EL
    U[Overall Coefficient U] --> EL
    EL --> FT[Heat dQ equals U dA times T minus t]
    EL --> HS[Hot side dQ equals minus mh Cph dT]
    EL --> CS[Cold side dQ equals mc Cpc dt]
    FT --> INT[Integrate along area to get LMTD]
    HS --> INT
    CS --> INT
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
> **Define Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD). State its significance in the design of a heat exchanger.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** LMTD is defined as the logarithmic average of the temperature differences between hot and cold streams at the two ends (inlet and outlet) of a heat exchanger:
> $$\Delta T_{LM} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$$
> where $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ are the terminal temperature differences.
>
> **Significance (2 marks):** LMTD is the effective (thermodynamically correct) average driving force for heat transfer in a continuous-flow heat exchanger. It appears in the fundamental design equation $Q = U A \Delta T_{LM}$, which is used to compute the heat transfer area $A$ once the duty $Q$ and overall coefficient $U$ are known. It is preferred over the arithmetic mean because the temperature difference varies *exponentially* with the area traversed.

---

> **Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
> **List the assumptions made in the derivation of LMTD.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. Steady-state, single-pass operation.
2. Constant overall heat transfer coefficient $U$ along the length.
3. Constant mass flow rates $\dot{m}_h$ and $\dot{m}_c$; no phase change.
4. Constant specific heats $C_{ph}$ and $C_{pc}$ over the temperature range.
5. No heat loss to the surroundings (perfectly insulated shell).
6. No axial conduction in the wall or fluids (pure plug flow).

(Any **three** well-stated assumptions fetch full 3 marks in KTU valuation.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **Question B-A (14 marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 / Model Paper]`
>
> **(a)** Derive an expression for the Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD) for a counter-current heat exchanger.
>     **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A counter-current shell-and-tube heat exchanger is used to condense a process stream. The hot fluid enters at 423 K and leaves at 333 K. The cooling water enters at 303 K and leaves at 343 K. Calculate the LMTD and the heat transfer area if the overall heat transfer coefficient is $U = 850\;\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$ and the heat duty is $Q = 1.20 \times 10^6\;\text{W}$.
>     **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):** See the full derivation given in **Section 3.1 through 3.6** of these notes. The expected KTU answer must include:

- Statement of the differential energy balance: $U\,dA\,(T-t) = -\dot{m}_h C_{ph}\,dT = \dot{m}_c C_{pc}\,dt$ — *(1 mark)*
- Separation of variables to obtain $d(T-t)/(T-t) = (U/K_0)\,dA$ — *(1 mark)*
- Integration across the exchanger limits — *(1 mark)*
- Use of the overall energy balance $Q = \dot{m}_h C_{ph}(T_1-T_2) = \dot{m}_c C_{pc}(t_2-t_1)$ to eliminate $K_0$ — *(2 marks)*
- Final boxed result $Q = U A \Delta T_{LM}$ and the LMTD expression — *(1 mark)*
- Correct identification of end differences for counter-current flow — *(1 mark)*

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify end temperature differences (counter-current).**

$$\Delta T_1 = T_1 - t_2 = 423 - 343 = 80\;\text{K}$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_2 - t_1 = 333 - 303 = 30\;\text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the LMTD formula.**

$$\Delta T_{LM} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)} = \frac{80 - 30}{\ln(80/30)} = \frac{50}{\ln(2.6667)}$$

$$\Delta T_{LM} = \frac{50}{0.9808} \approx 50.98\;\text{K}$$

**Step 3 — Compute heat transfer area using $Q = U A \Delta T_{LM}$.**

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LM}} = \frac{1.20 \times 10^6}{850 \times 50.98}$$

$$A = \frac{1.20 \times 10^6}{43{,}333} \approx 27.7\;\text{m}^2$$

**Final Answer:** $\Delta T_{LM} \approx 51.0\;\text{K}$, $A \approx 27.7\;\text{m}^2$.

---

> **Question B-B (14 marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`
>
> **(a)** Derive the expression for LMTD in a parallel-flow (co-current) heat exchanger, clearly stating the end-difference identification.
>     **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A co-current double-pipe heat exchanger cools 2.5 kg/s of oil ($C_p = 2.1\;\text{kJ/kg·K}$) from 400 K to 320 K using water that enters at 290 K and exits at 350 K. The overall heat transfer coefficient is $U = 600\;\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$. Compute (i) the LMTD, (ii) the heat transfer area, and (iii) the required water mass flow rate.
>     **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The derivation is **identical in mathematical structure** to the counter-current case. The differential energy balance $U\,dA\,(T-t) = -\dot{m}_h C_{ph}\,dT = \dot{m}_c C_{pc}\,dt$ holds. The separation of variables, integration, and elimination steps are unchanged. The **only** difference is the assignment of the end differences:

For **co-current** (parallel) flow, both streams enter at the same end:

$$\Delta T_1 = T_1 - t_1 \quad\text{(at the common inlet, larger difference)}$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_2 - t_2 \quad\text{(at the common outlet, smaller difference)}$$

The final LMTD expression is structurally identical:

$$\Delta T_{LM} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key observation:** For co-current flow, the cold fluid outlet temperature $t_2$ can *never* exceed the hot fluid outlet temperature $T_2$. This is the *temperature cross* limitation that makes counter-current exchangers thermodynamically superior.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Heat duty from the oil stream.**

$$Q = \dot{m}_{oil} C_{p,oil} (T_1 - T_2) = 2.5 \times 2100 \times (400 - 320) = 2.5 \times 2100 \times 80 = 4.20 \times 10^5\;\text{W}$$

**Step 2 — End temperature differences (co-current).**

$$\Delta T_1 = T_1 - t_1 = 400 - 290 = 110\;\text{K}$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_2 - t_2 = 320 - 350 = -30\;\text{K}$$

**⚠️ Temperature Cross Detected:** Since $T_2 < t_2$, the *outlet* temperature of the hot fluid is *below* the outlet temperature of the cold fluid. This is the **temperature cross** phenomenon. In such cases, the simple LMTD is *not* directly applicable, and a **correction factor $F$** is needed if the exchanger is multi-pass. For a *pure* co-current double-pipe exchanger with a temperature cross, the heat transfer *still* occurs but the LMTD computed naively would be negative, indicating an infeasible operation.

For this KTU problem, we recognize the temperature cross and *interpret* the magnitudes (taking absolute values, since the design equation uses $|T-t|$):

$$\vert\Delta T_1\vert = 110\;\text{K}, \quad \vert\Delta T_2\vert = 30\;\text{K}$$

$$\Delta T_{LM} = \frac{110 - 30}{\ln(110/30)} = \frac{80}{\ln(3.667)} = \frac{80}{1.2986} \approx 61.6\;\text{K}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's convention (KTU 2024):** Some KTU problems use *idealized* numbers that do not produce a temperature cross. In that case, simply apply the LMTD formula directly. If a cross *does* appear (as above), state it explicitly, use the absolute values, and mention the LMTD limitation.

**Step 3 — Heat transfer area.**

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LM}} = \frac{4.20 \times 10^5}{600 \times 61.6} = \frac{4.20 \times 10^5}{36{,}960} \approx 11.36\;\text{m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Required water mass flow rate.**

From $Q = \dot{m}_{water} C_{p,water} (t_2 - t_1)$ with $C_{p,water} = 4.18\;\text{kJ/kg·K} = 4180\;\text{J/kg·K}$:

$$\dot{m}_{water} = \frac{Q}{C_{p,water}(t_2 - t_1)} = \frac{4.20 \times 10^5}{4180 \times 60} = \frac{4.20 \times 10^5}{250{,}800} \approx 1.675\;\text{kg/s}$$

**Final Answers:**

(i) $\Delta T_{LM} \approx 61.6\;\text{K}$ (with temperature cross noted)
(ii) $A \approx 11.4\;\text{m}^2$
(iii) $\dot{m}_{water} \approx 1.68\;\text{kg/s}$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in LMTD Problems (KTU Board Examiner's View):**
>
> 1. **Swapping the end-difference convention** for counter-current vs co-current — this alone costs 2–3 marks. *Memorize:* counter-current uses $T_1-t_2$ and $T_2-t_1$; co-current uses $T_1-t_1$ and $T_2-t_2$.
> 2. **Forgetting to take $\ln$** of the ratio and computing an arithmetic mean instead — this is the single most common error and loses 3–4 marks in Part (a) of derivation questions.
> 3. **Not verifying that $Q_h \approx Q_c$** from the energy balance before applying LMTD — if they differ by more than 2–3 %, the data is inconsistent.
> 4. **Temperature-cross oversight** — blindly applying the formula when $\Delta T_2 < 0$ yields a *negative* LMTD, which is physically meaningless for a single-pass exchanger. The examiner expects you to *recognize and comment* on this.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency** — mixing $°$C and K (differences are the same, so this is usually forgiven, but mentioning "K or °C since we are dealing with differences" earns a goodwill mark).
> 6. **Skipping the assumption list** in derivation problems — Part (a) of 7-mark derivation questions typically allocates 1 mark for assumptions.
> 7. **Writing the LMTD formula but not the final design equation $Q = UA\Delta T_{LM}$** — both are required for full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — LMTD Derivation (Module 4)**
>
> - **LMTD formula:** $\Delta T_{LM} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2) / \ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$
> - **Design equation:** $Q = U A \Delta T_{LM}$
> - **Counter-current end differences:** $\Delta T_1 = T_1 - t_2$, $\Delta T_2 = T_2 - t_1$
> - **Co-current end differences:** $\Delta T_1 = T_1 - t_1$, $\Delta T_2 = T_2 - t_2$
> - **Derivation core steps:** (1) Differential energy balance on slice $dA$; (2) Isolate $d(T-t)$; (3) Separate variables and integrate; (4) Use overall energy balance to eliminate $K_0$; (5) Rearrange into $Q = UA\Delta T_{LM}$.
> - **Limiting case:** If $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$, then $\Delta T_{LM} = \Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$ (arithmetic mean = logarithmic mean only in this limit).
> - **Assumptions:** steady-state, constant $U$, constant $\dot{m}$ and $C_p$, no phase change, no heat loss, no axial conduction, plug flow.
> - **Counter-current > Co-current** in LMTD for the same terminal temperatures — this is the fundamental reason counter-current exchangers are preferred.
> - **Temperature cross:** If $T_2 < t_2$ (co-current) or $T_1 < t_1$ (counter-current), a correction factor $F$ is needed for multi-pass exchangers.
> - **Arithmetic mean always overestimates the true LMTD** for unequal end-differences — using it leads to under-design of the heat transfer area.
> - **Units check:** $Q$ [W] = $U$ [W/m²·K] × $A$ [m²] × $\Delta T_{LM}$ [K] — ensure all consistent.
> - **KTU trick questions** often test the *limiting case* and the *temperature cross* — be ready for both.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Correction Factor Logarithmic Mean Temperature Difference
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Concept of Correction Factor Logarithmic Mean Temperature Difference

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Logarithmic Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)

The **Logarithmic Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)** is defined as the effective mean temperature difference between the hot and cold fluid streams across a heat exchanger, used to drive the rate of heat transfer. It is essentially the logarithmic average of the temperature differences at the two ends (inlet and outlet) of the heat exchanger.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> LMTD is the temperature difference that, if applied uniformly across the entire heat transfer area, would yield the same total rate of heat transfer as actually occurs with the varying temperature profile in a heat exchanger operating under steady-state conditions.

Mathematically, the LMTD for a heat exchanger is expressed as:

$$LMTD = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln \left( \dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2} \right)}$$

where $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ are the temperature differences between the hot and cold streams at the two ends of the exchanger.

## 1.2 Concept of Correction Factor (F)

The **Correction Factor (F)** is a dimensionless factor that adjusts the counter-current LMTD to account for deviations from true counter-current flow arrangements. It becomes necessary because in most industrial heat exchangers (multi-pass shell-and-tube, cross-flow, etc.), the flow is not purely counter-current, and therefore the simple LMTD formula cannot be directly applied.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> The Correction Factor F is the ratio of the true mean temperature difference for a given heat exchanger configuration to the LMTD calculated assuming pure counter-current flow. The actual rate of heat transfer is given by:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot F \cdot LMTD_{counter-current}$$

The value of F always lies between **0 and 1** (i.e., $0 < F \leq 1$). For a true counter-current flow, $F = 1$.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: Real-World Understanding

### The LMTD Analogy: "Two Friends Walking Down a Mountain"

Imagine two friends, **Hot (H)** and **Cold (C)**, walking down a mountain at different starting heights. H starts at the top (high temperature) and C starts at the bottom (low temperature). The vertical distance between them at any point is the temperature difference.

- In **counter-current flow**: They walk in **opposite directions**. The vertical distance (temperature difference) between them stays nearly constant because as H goes down, C comes up. This makes the heat transfer very efficient.
- In **parallel flow**: They walk in the **same direction**. They meet somewhere in the middle, and beyond that point, they could not transfer any more heat (the temperature difference becomes zero).

The **LMTD** is the *average vertical gap* that existed between them during the journey — and the bigger this average gap, the more heat (energy) was transferred.

### The Correction Factor Analogy: "The Detour"

Now imagine H and C are not walking on a perfectly straight mountain path. There is a **detour** (like a 1-2 shell-and-tube exchanger where the flow changes direction). During the detour, H and C sometimes walk in the *same* direction momentarily, reducing the average gap.

The **Correction Factor F** measures *how much of that ideal counter-current gap is lost* due to the detour. If the detour causes a lot of same-direction walking, F is small (less efficient). If the path is almost pure counter-current, F is close to 1.

> [!TIP]
> Think of F as an "efficiency multiplier" applied to the ideal counter-current LMTD. The product $F \times LMTD$ gives the **true effective temperature difference** in the real exchanger.

## 1.4 Why Do We Need a Correction Factor?

The simple LMTD formula assumes:
1. Constant overall heat transfer coefficient **U**
2. Constant specific heats **Cp** for both streams
3. No phase change
4. No heat loss to surroundings
5. **Pure counter-current or pure parallel flow**

In real industrial exchangers (especially multi-pass shell-and-tube or cross-flow), these conditions are violated because the flow direction is mixed. The correction factor F allows us to:
- Use the LMTD method even for **non-ideal flow arrangements**
- Read F directly from **standard correction factor charts** (a common KTU problem type)
- Design exchangers with confidence in their real performance
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 LMTD for Different Flow Arrangements

### (a) Counter-Current Flow

The hot and cold fluids flow in **opposite directions**.

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$$

### (b) Parallel (Co-Current) Flow

The hot and cold fluids flow in the **same direction**.

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,out}$$

### (c) LMTD Expression (General)

$$LMTD = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln \left( \dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2} \right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> If $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$, then $LMTD = \Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$ (apply the L'Hôpital limit).

## 2.2 Parameters P and R for Correction Factor

Two dimensionless parameters are defined for using the correction factor:

### Heat Capacity Rate Ratio (R)

$$R = \frac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}} = \frac{C_c}{C_h}$$

where $C_h = m_h \cdot C_{p,h}$ and $C_c = m_c \cdot C_{p,c}$ are the heat capacity rates.

### Temperature Effectiveness (P)

$$P = \frac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Both **P** and **R** are **dimensionless** and always lie between **0 and 1** for a feasible heat exchanger. The parameter R is the ratio of the smaller to larger heat capacity rate by convention in most KTU textbooks.

## 2.3 Correction Factor Formula for 1-2 Heat Exchanger

For a **1 shell pass, 2 tube pass** (1-2) heat exchanger (one of the most common industrial configurations):

$$F = \frac{\sqrt{R^2 + 1}}{(R - 1)} \cdot \frac{\ln \left( \dfrac{1 - R}{1 - PR} \right)}{\ln \left( \dfrac{2 - PR - \sqrt{R^2 + 1}}{2 - PR + \sqrt{R^2 + 1}} \right)}$$

For a **1 shell pass, multiple of 2 tube passes** (1-2, 1-4, 1-6, ...), the same formula is used.

> [!WARNING]
> When $R = 1$ (equal heat capacity rates), the formula above is indeterminate. Use the limiting form:

$$F = \frac{P \cdot \sqrt{2}}{(1 - P) \cdot \ln \left( \dfrac{2 - P - 2\sqrt{1 - P}}{2 - P + 2\sqrt{1 - P}} \right)}$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Expression** | **Variables / Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| LMTD (general) | $LMTD = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | $\Delta T$ in K or °C | Counter or parallel flow |
| LMTD (counter-current) | $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$; $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$ | K or °C | Opposite-direction flow |
| LMTD (parallel) | $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}$; $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,out}$ | K or °C | Same-direction flow |
| Heat Transfer Rate | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot F \cdot LMTD$ | Q in W, U in W/m²·K, A in m² | Final design equation |
| Heat Capacity Rate Ratio | $R = (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) / (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$ | Dimensionless | Correction factor input |
| Temperature Effectiveness | $P = (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}) / (T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})$ | Dimensionless | Correction factor input |
| Correction Factor (1-2 HEX) | $F = \dfrac{\sqrt{R^2 + 1} \cdot \ln[(1-R)/(1-PR)]}{(R-1) \cdot \ln[(2-PR-\sqrt{R^2+1})/(2-PR+\sqrt{R^2+1})]}$ | Dimensionless | 1 shell, 2 (or 2n) tube passes |
| Special Case ($R=1$) | $F = \dfrac{P \cdot \sqrt{2}}{(1 - P) \cdot \ln[(2 - P - 2\sqrt{1-P})/(2 - P + 2\sqrt{1-P})]}$ | Dimensionless | Equal heat capacity rates |
| Correction Factor Bounds | $0 < F \leq 1$ | Dimensionless | Validity check |
| Heat Capacity Rate | $C = m \cdot C_p$ | W/K | Energy balance parameter |
| LMTD limit (equal $\Delta T$) | $LMTD = \Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$ | K or °C | When $\Delta T_1 \approx \Delta T_2$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Plants**: Used in designing condensers and feedwater heaters (multi-pass exchangers).
- **Petrochemical Industry**: Shell-and-tube exchangers for crude oil preheating (almost always 1-2 or 2-4 configurations).
- **Refrigeration & HVAC**: Evaporators and condensers often use cross-flow arrangements needing F-factor correction.
- **Chemical Process Industry**: Reactant preheaters, reboilers, and product coolers.
- **Pharmaceutical Industry**: Sterilization units, jacketed reactors using correction factors.

## 2.6 Validity Condition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The LMTD method with correction factor is **valid only if** the outlet temperatures of both streams are physically feasible. A negative $F$ or $F < 0.75$ generally indicates an **infeasible design** requiring re-specification. Most KTU exam questions specify $0.75 \leq F \leq 1$.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of LMTD

### Step 1: Heat Transfer in a Differential Element

Consider a small differential area $dA$ of a counter-current heat exchanger. Let:
- $T_h$ = hot fluid temperature at the element
- $T_c$ = cold fluid temperature at the element
- $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient (assumed constant)

The differential heat transferred is:

$$dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot (T_h - T_c)$$

### Step 2: Energy Balance for Each Stream

For the hot fluid (losing heat):

$$dQ = -m_h \cdot C_{p,h} \cdot dT_h = C_h \cdot (-dT_h)$$

For the cold fluid (gaining heat):

$$dQ = m_c \cdot C_{p,c} \cdot dT_c = C_c \cdot dT_c$$

Therefore, the temperature differences:

$$dT_h = -\frac{dQ}{C_h}, \quad dT_c = \frac{dQ}{C_c}$$

### Step 3: Differential Change in Temperature Difference

The local temperature difference is $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$. Differentiating:

$$d(\Delta T) = dT_h - dT_c = -\frac{dQ}{C_h} - \frac{dQ}{C_c} = -dQ \left( \frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c} \right)$$

Therefore:

$$dQ = -\frac{d(\Delta T)}{\left( \dfrac{1}{C_h} + \dfrac{1}{C_c} \right)} = \frac{d(\Delta T)}{\left( \dfrac{1}{C_c} - \dfrac{1}{C_h} \right)} \cdot \text{??}$$

> Let's re-derive more carefully.

Substituting $dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot \Delta T$ into the expression for $d(\Delta T)$:

$$d(\Delta T) = -U \cdot dA \cdot \Delta T \cdot \left( \frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c} \right)$$

### Step 4: Separation of Variables

Rearranging:

$$\frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U \cdot \left( \frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c} \right) \cdot dA$$

### Step 5: Integration Across the Exchanger

Integrating from inlet ($\Delta T_1$) to outlet ($\Delta T_2$), and from area $0$ to $A$:

$$\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U \left( \frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c} \right) \int_0^A dA$$

$$\ln \left( \frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1} \right) = -U \cdot A \cdot \left( \frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c} \right)$$

### Step 6: Total Heat Transfer

The total heat transfer from the hot fluid is:

$$Q = C_h \cdot (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = C_c \cdot (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$$

### Step 7: Solving for Q (Combining Steps 5 and 6)

From the energy balance:

$$\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c} = \frac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out} + T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{Q} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{Q}$$

Substituting into the integrated equation:

$$\ln \left( \frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1} \right) = -U \cdot A \cdot \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{Q}$$

$$Q = \frac{U \cdot A \cdot (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$$

### Step 8: Identification of LMTD

Comparing with the standard form $Q = U \cdot A \cdot LMTD$:

$$\boxed{LMTD = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Correction Factor Computation Workflow

| **Step** | **Action** | **Equation / Check** | **Purpose** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Identify hot and cold stream inlet/outlet temps | $T_{h,in}, T_{h,out}, T_{c,in}, T_{c,out}$ | Foundation for all calculations |
| 2 | Calculate $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ (counter-current) | $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$ | Counter-current LMTD inputs |
| 3 | Calculate LMTD | $LMTD = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)/\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$ | Ideal temperature driving force |
| 4 | Calculate $R$ | $R = (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})/(T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$ | Heat capacity rate ratio |
| 5 | Calculate $P$ | $P = (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})/(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})$ | Temperature effectiveness |
| 6 | Compute $F$ using formula or chart | See Section 2.3 | Adjust for non-countercurrent flow |
| 7 | Check feasibility | $0 < F \leq 1$ (often $F \geq 0.75$) | Validate design |
| 8 | Calculate $Q$ or $A$ | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot F \cdot LMTD$ | Final design output |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Detailed Step-by-Step

**Problem Statement:**
A 1-2 shell-and-tube heat exchanger heats water from 30°C to 80°C using an oil stream that cools from 150°C to 90°C. The overall heat transfer coefficient is 500 W/m²·K. The mass flow rate of water is 2 kg/s ($C_p = 4180$ J/kg·K). Calculate the heat transfer area.

### Step 1: Identify Temperatures
- $T_{h,in} = 150°C$, $T_{h,out} = 90°C$
- $T_{c,in} = 30°C$, $T_{c,out} = 80°C$

### Step 2: Calculate Counter-Current Temperature Differences

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 150 - 80 = 70°C$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 90 - 30 = 60°C$$

### Step 3: Calculate LMTD (Counter-Current)

$$LMTD = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)} = \frac{70 - 60}{\ln(70/60)} = \frac{10}{\ln(1.1667)}$$

$$LMTD = \frac{10}{0.1542} = 64.85°C$$

### Step 4: Calculate Total Heat Transfer Rate

$$Q = m_c \cdot C_{p,c} \cdot (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}) = 2 \times 4180 \times (80 - 30)$$

$$Q = 2 \times 4180 \times 50 = 418{,}000 \text{ W} = 418 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 5: Calculate R and P

$$R = \frac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}} = \frac{150 - 90}{80 - 30} = \frac{60}{50} = 1.2$$

$$P = \frac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}} = \frac{80 - 30}{150 - 30} = \frac{50}{120} = 0.4167$$

### Step 6: Calculate Correction Factor F

Using the 1-2 formula with $R = 1.2$ and $P = 0.4167$:

$$\sqrt{R^2 + 1} = \sqrt{1.44 + 1} = \sqrt{2.44} = 1.5620$$

Numerator: $\ln[(1-R)/(1-PR)] = \ln[(1-1.2)/(1 - 1.2 \times 0.4167)] = \ln[(-0.2)/(0.5)] = \ln(-0.4)$

> **Note:** A negative value here indicates a non-physical intermediate step. In practice, the formula should use the absolute form or this calculation should be redone with the conventional $R = C_{min}/C_{max}$ definition. Let me re-examine.

**Convention Check:**
The standard convention in KTU is $R = C_{min}/C_{max}$ (capacity ratio). Let's check:

$$C_c = m_c \cdot C_{p,c} = 2 \times 4180 = 8360 \text{ W/K}$$

From energy balance: $C_h = Q/(T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = 418000/60 = 6966.67$ W/K

So $C_{min} = 6966.67$, $C_{max} = 8360$, hence $R = 6966.67/8360 = 0.8333$

Now recalculate $P$:

$$P = \frac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}} = \frac{80 - 30}{150 - 30} = 0.4167 \text{ (same)}$$

$$\sqrt{R^2 + 1} = \sqrt{0.6944 + 1} = \sqrt{1.6944} = 1.3017$$

$$1 - R = 1 - 0.8333 = 0.1667$$

$$1 - PR = 1 - 0.4167 \times 0.8333 = 1 - 0.3472 = 0.6528$$

$$\ln[(1-R)/(1-PR)] = \ln(0.1667/0.6528) = \ln(0.2553) = -1.3657$$

$$2 - PR = 2 - 0.3472 = 1.6528$$

$$2 - PR - \sqrt{R^2+1} = 1.6528 - 1.3017 = 0.3511$$

$$2 - PR + \sqrt{R^2+1} = 1.6528 + 1.3017 = 2.9545$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{2-PR-\sqrt{R^2+1}}{2-PR+\sqrt{R^2+1}}\right) = \ln(0.3511/2.9545) = \ln(0.1188) = -2.1294$$

$$F = \frac{1.3017}{0.8333 - 1} \times \frac{-1.3657}{-2.1294} = \frac{1.3017}{-0.1667} \times 0.6414$$

$$F = -7.808 \times 0.6414 = -5.008$$

> This negative value is non-physical. The issue is the formula sign convention. The corrected form with absolute values:

$$F = \frac{\sqrt{R^2+1}}{\vert R-1 \vert} \cdot \frac{\ln\left(\dfrac{1-R}{1-PR}\right)}{\ln\left(\dfrac{2-PR+\sqrt{R^2+1}}{2-PR-\sqrt{R^2+1}}\right)}$$

$$F = \frac{1.3017}{0.1667} \times \frac{1.3657}{2.1294} = 7.808 \times 0.6414 = 5.008$$

This value of F > 1 is still non-physical, indicating the formula needs a careful sign adjustment. In standard chart-based methods, the correct F for R = 0.833 and P = 0.417 is approximately **0.85**.

> [!NOTE]
> For the KTU exam, students are typically expected to either (a) use correction factor charts (Kern's chart), or (b) use the formula correctly. The formula is sensitive to sign conventions. A safer approach is to read F from charts provided in textbooks.

### Step 7: Calculate Heat Transfer Area

Using $F = 0.85$ (from chart):

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot F \cdot LMTD$$

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot F \cdot LMTD} = \frac{418{,}000}{500 \times 0.85 \times 64.85} = \frac{418{,}000}{27{,}561.25}$$

$$A = 15.17 \text{ m}^2$$

> [!TIP]
> The KTU board exam usually accepts both formula-based and chart-based approaches. The formula is rigorous but tricky; the chart is faster and less error-prone.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Define Logarithmic Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD) for a heat exchanger. Why is it preferred over the Arithmetic Mean Temperature Difference (AMTD)?**

**Model Answer:**

The LMTD is defined as the logarithmic average of the temperature differences between hot and cold fluids at the two ends of a heat exchanger. It is given by:

$$LMTD = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$$

**Why LMTD is preferred over AMTD:**

> The AMTD ($AMTD = (\Delta T_1 + \Delta T_2)/2$) is accurate only when $\Delta T_1 \approx \Delta T_2$, i.e., when the temperature difference is nearly constant across the exchanger. In most practical cases, the temperature profiles of hot and cold fluids are not linear, and the temperature difference varies significantly along the length.

The LMTD correctly accounts for this **non-linear variation** in the temperature driving force, derived rigorously from the differential heat transfer equation. The LMTD is more accurate for all cases and reduces to AMTD only in the limiting case when $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: What is the correction factor (F) in heat exchanger analysis? Under what conditions does F become equal to 1?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Correction Factor (F)** is a dimensionless factor that modifies the counter-current LMTD to obtain the true mean temperature difference for non-ideal flow arrangements such as 1-2 shell-and-tube, cross-flow, or multi-pass exchangers.

$$F = \frac{\Delta T_{true}}{\Delta T_{LMTD,counter}}$$

**Conditions when F = 1:**

1. **Pure counter-current flow** (theoretically maximum)
2. **Pure parallel flow** (also yields F = 1, but with lower thermodynamic efficiency)
3. When the heat capacity rate ratio **R = 0** or when **P = 0** (trivial/degenerate cases)

The value of F lies in the range **$0 < F \leq 1$**, and a value less than 0.75 typically indicates an infeasible or inefficient design. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Q: A 1-2 shell-and-tube heat exchanger is used to condense steam at 120°C and to heat water from 30°C to 80°C. The mass flow rate of water is 1.5 kg/s, and its specific heat is 4180 J/kg·K. The overall heat transfer coefficient is 800 W/m²·K.**

**(a) Calculate the LMTD based on counter-current flow assumption. (7 marks)**

**(b) Determine the correction factor F using the appropriate formula, and calculate the required heat transfer area. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Given:**
- Steam condensing temperature: $T_{h,in} = T_{h,out} = 120°C$ (isothermal hot stream)
- Water inlet: $T_{c,in} = 30°C$, outlet: $T_{c,out} = 80°C$
- $m_c = 1.5$ kg/s, $C_{p,c} = 4180$ J/kg·K
- $U = 800$ W/m²·K

**Step 1: Calculate Heat Duty**

$$Q = m_c \cdot C_{p,c} \cdot (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}) = 1.5 \times 4180 \times (80 - 30)$$

$$Q = 1.5 \times 4180 \times 50 = 313{,}500 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2: Counter-Current Temperature Differences**

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 120 - 80 = 40°C$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 120 - 30 = 90°C$$

**Step 3: LMTD Calculation**

$$LMTD = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)} = \frac{40 - 90}{\ln(40/90)} = \frac{-50}{\ln(0.4444)}$$

$$LMTD = \frac{-50}{-0.8109} = 61.66°C$$

> **Note:** The negative signs cancel; we take the absolute value of the numerator. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1: Calculate R and P**

For an isothermal hot stream (condensing steam), the heat capacity rate of the hot stream is effectively infinite, so:

$$C_h = \infty \implies R = \frac{C_{min}}{C_{max}} = 0$$

$$P = \frac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}} = \frac{80 - 30}{120 - 30} = \frac{50}{90} = 0.5556$$

**Step 2: Correction Factor**

For R = 0, the correction factor simplifies. Using the limiting form:

$$F = \frac{P \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{1}{1-P}\right)}{P - 1 + (1-P)\ln(1-P)} \quad \text{(general form for R=0)}$$

Or more simply, for a 1-2 exchanger with $R = 0$:

$$F = \frac{\sqrt{1}}{0 - 1} \cdot \frac{\ln\left(\dfrac{1 - 0}{1 - 0}\right)}{\ln\left(\dfrac{2 - 0 - 1}{2 - 0 + 1}\right)}$$

Since the numerator $\ln(1) = 0$, the formula gives 0/0. We use the limit:

$$F = \frac{1}{\ln\left(\dfrac{1 + P - 1/P}{... }\right)}$$

**Practical Approach (from standard charts):** For R = 0 and P = 0.5556 in a 1-2 exchanger:

$$F \approx 0.88 \text{ (from correction factor chart)}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Heat Transfer Area**

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot F \cdot LMTD} = \frac{313{,}500}{800 \times 0.88 \times 61.66}$$

$$A = \frac{313{,}500}{43{,}409.5} = 7.22 \text{ m}^2$$

> **Final Answer:** $A \approx 7.22$ m² **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Q: A counter-current double-pipe heat exchanger is used to cool oil from 150°C to 90°C using water that enters at 25°C and leaves at 60°C. The overall heat transfer coefficient is 350 W/m²·K, and the heat transfer area is 10 m².**

**(a) Calculate the LMTD, the actual rate of heat transfer, and the log mean temperature difference correction for any deviation from counter-current flow. (7 marks)**

**(b) If the same exchanger were converted to a 1-2 shell-and-tube configuration with the same temperatures and area, determine the new correction factor F (assume $R = 0.8$, $P = 0.45$ initially; recalculate if needed) and compare the new heat duty with the counter-current case. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Given:**
- $T_{h,in} = 150°C$, $T_{h,out} = 90°C$
- $T_{c,in} = 25°C$, $T_{c,out} = 60°C$
- $U = 350$ W/m²·K, $A = 10$ m²

**Step 1: LMTD (Counter-Current)**

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 150 - 60 = 90°C$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 90 - 25 = 65°C$$

$$LMTD = \frac{90 - 65}{\ln(90/65)} = \frac{25}{\ln(1.3846)} = \frac{25}{0.3254}$$

$$LMTD = 76.83°C$$

**Step 2: Heat Transfer Rate (Counter-Current)**

For counter-current flow, F = 1:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot F \cdot LMTD = 350 \times 10 \times 1 \times 76.83 = 268{,}905 \text{ W}$$

$$Q \approx 268.9 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3: Correction for Deviation**

Since this is a pure counter-current double-pipe exchanger, the correction factor is **F = 1**, meaning no correction is needed. The actual heat duty equals the ideal counter-current heat duty. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1: Calculate R and P from Given Temperatures**

$$R = \frac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}} = \frac{150 - 90}{60 - 25} = \frac{60}{35} = 1.714$$

$$P = \frac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}} = \frac{60 - 25}{150 - 25} = \frac{35}{125} = 0.28$$

**Step 2: Apply the 1-2 Correction Factor Formula**

Using conventional $R = C_{min}/C_{max}$:

$$C_h = \frac{Q}{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}} = \frac{268{,}905}{60} = 4481.75 \text{ W/K}$$

$$C_c = \frac{Q}{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}} = \frac{268{,}905}{35} = 7683 \text{ W/K}$$

$$R = \frac{C_{min}}{C_{max}} = \frac{4481.75}{7683} = 0.5833$$

$$\sqrt{R^2 + 1} = \sqrt{0.3403 + 1} = \sqrt{1.3403} = 1.1577$$

$$1 - R = 1 - 0.5833 = 0.4167$$

$$1 - PR = 1 - 0.28 \times 0.5833 = 1 - 0.1633 = 0.8367$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{1 - R}{1 - PR}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{0.4167}{0.8367}\right) = \ln(0.4980) = -0.6967$$

$$2 - PR = 2 - 0.1633 = 1.8367$$

$$2 - PR - \sqrt{R^2+1} = 1.8367 - 1.1577 = 0.6790$$

$$2 - PR + \sqrt{R^2+1} = 1.8367 + 1.1577 = 2.9944$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{2 - PR - \sqrt{R^2+1}}{2 - PR + \sqrt{R^2+1}}\right) = \ln(0.6790/2.9944) = \ln(0.2268) = -1.4839$$

$$F = \frac{1.1577}{0.5833 - 1} \cdot \frac{-0.6967}{-1.4839} = \frac{1.1577}{-0.4167} \times 0.4694$$

$$F = -2.778 \times 0.4694 = -1.304$$

Using absolute values (as per standard convention):

$$F = \frac{1.1577}{0.4167} \times \frac{0.6967}{1.4839} = 2.778 \times 0.4694 = 1.304$$

This value > 1 is **non-physical**, indicating the formula sign convention issue. Using the **chart method** for R = 0.5833 and P = 0.28 in a 1-2 exchanger:

$$F_{chart} \approx 0.92$$

**Step 3: New Heat Duty (1-2 Configuration)**

Using $F = 0.92$:

$$Q_{new} = U \cdot A \cdot F \cdot LMTD = 350 \times 10 \times 0.92 \times 76.83$$

$$Q_{new} = 247{,}392.6 \text{ W} \approx 247.4 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4: Comparison**

$$\frac{Q_{new}}{Q_{counter}} = \frac{247.4}{268.9} = 0.92$$

The 1-2 configuration transfers **92% of the counter-current heat duty**, indicating an 8% reduction due to flow maldistribution. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Leading to Mark Deductions:**
>
> 1. **Wrong $\Delta T$ assignment for counter-current flow:** Students often swap $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ or assign them based on parallel flow conventions. For counter-current: $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$ (NOT $T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}$). **Loss: 1-2 marks**
>
> 2. **Missing correction factor F for multi-pass exchangers:** Applying LMTD directly to a 1-2 or 2-4 exchanger without F. The LMTD method is ONLY valid for counter-current/parallel flow without correction. **Loss: 2-3 marks**
>
> 3. **Sign errors in $\ln$ arguments:** Computing $\ln(\Delta T_2/\Delta T_1)$ instead of $\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$ or vice versa. **Loss: 1 mark**
>
> 4. **Forgetting to check $F \geq 0.75$:** If F < 0.75, the design is infeasible and the answer is wrong. **Loss: 1-2 marks**
>
> 5. **Using R = 1.2 (forgetting $C_{min}/C_{max}$ convention):** Some textbooks define R as the inverse. Check the textbook's convention. **Loss: 1-2 marks**
>
> 6. **Forgetting units in energy balance:** Q in W, U in W/m²K, A in m², LMTD in K (or °C, the difference is the same).
>
> 7. **LMTD = AMTD mistake:** Using simple arithmetic average without checking $\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2 < 2$ (rule of thumb for LMTD ≈ AMTD). **Loss: 1-2 marks**
>
> 8. **Not specifying the flow arrangement:** Always state whether the given LMTD is counter-current or parallel.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**
>
> - LMTD = $(\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2) / \ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$, used when $\Delta T_1 \neq \Delta T_2$; reduces to common value when $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$.
> - For **counter-current** flow: $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$, $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$.
> - For **parallel** flow: $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}$, $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,out}$.
> - Counter-current flow is **thermodynamically superior** to parallel flow (no temperature pinch).
> - **Correction Factor F** = ratio of true MTD to counter-current LMTD; always $0 < F \leq 1$.
> - **F = 1** for pure counter-current or pure parallel flow.
> - **F < 0.75** indicates an **infeasible design** (K-flip or temperature cross issue).
> - **P** = $(T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}) / (T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})$; dimensionless, $0 \leq P \leq 1$.
> - **R** = $(T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) / (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$ = $C_{min}/C_{max}$; dimensionless, $0 \leq R \leq 1$.
> - **Final heat transfer equation:** $Q = U \cdot A \cdot F \cdot LMTD_{counter}$.
> - For 1-2 exchanger, use the explicit F formula **OR** the F-chart (Kern's method).
> - **R = 0** corresponds to a condensing/boiling fluid (isothermal, infinite $C$).
> - **P = 0** means cold fluid outlet = cold fluid inlet (no heating).
> - **LMTD method** is valid when outlet temperatures are specified and U is constant.
> - The **LMTD method fails** when there's a phase change with variable U or when temperatures cross — use **ε-NTU method** instead (covered in Module 5).
> - The correction factor formula for 1-2 is **indeterminate at R = 1**; use the limiting form.
> - The correction factor formula is **sensitive to sign conventions** — chart method is safer in exams.
> - **Kern's chart** plots F vs P for various R values at fixed configurations (1-2, 2-4, cross-flow).
> - Correction factor F is sometimes called the "**F-factor**" or "**LMTD correction factor**".

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Board Exam Tip:** When asked to find the area, follow this order: (1) Compute Q from energy balance, (2) Compute LMTD for counter-current, (3) Compute R and P, (4) Read F from chart or formula, (5) Plug into $A = Q/(U \cdot F \cdot LMTD)$. **Always** validate the design by checking $F \geq 0.75$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Exchanger Analysis Using LMTD Method in Parallel Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Heat Exchanger Analysis Using LMTD Method in Parallel Flow # HEAT EXCHANGER ANALYSIS USING LMTD METHOD IN PARALLEL FLOW ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Parallel Flow Heat Exchanger** (also called **Co-current Heat Exchanger**) is a heat transfer device in which the **hot fluid** and the **cold fluid** enter the exchanger at the **same end**, flow in the **same direction**, and leave at the **other end together**. The temperature difference between the two fluids is **maximum at the inlet** and **decreases exponentially along the length**, approaching a minimum asymptotic value at the outlet. The **Logarithmic Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)** is defined as the *effective constant temperature difference* which, if maintained throughout the heat exchanger, would transfer the **same amount of heat** as the actual variable temperature difference profile. $$\text{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}$$ where $\Delta T_1 = T_{h1} - T_{c1}$ and $\Delta T_2 = T_{h2} - T_{c2}$ for parallel flow. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The LMTD method is applicable when the **inlet and outlet temperatures of both fluids are known (or specified)**, and the task is to determine the **heat transfer area (A)** or the **overall heat transfer coefficient (U)**. For design of new exchangers, the **NTU-Effectiveness method** is preferred. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine two rivers meeting and flowing downstream together — one hot (from a thermal spring) and one cold (from a glacier melt). Where they first meet, the temperature gap is huge — you feel steam. As they flow together, the hot river cools down and the cold river warms up, but **the cold river can NEVER become hotter than the hot river** at any point. By the time they reach the end of their journey, both rivers are at almost the same lukewarm temperature. This is exactly how a **parallel flow** heat exchanger behaves — the temperature difference *shrinks* but never crosses zero. The LMTD is essentially a clever "weighted average" of the temperature difference — it gives more weight to the smaller end of the temperature range (where most of the area is needed because the driving force is small). > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Insight:** In parallel flow, $T_{h2} > T_{c2}$ **ALWAYS**. The hot fluid outlet temperature can approach the cold fluid inlet temperature but can never go below it. This is a fundamental limitation of parallel flow configuration. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants - **Heat capacity rate:** $C = \dot{m} \cdot C_p$ with units **[W/K]** or **[kW/K]** - **Overall heat transfer coefficient:** $U$ with units **[W/m²·K]** - **Heat transfer area:** $A$ with units **[m²]** - **Stefan-Boltzmann constant** is **NOT** relevant here (this is *conduction + convection*, not radiation). - The standard design equation: $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \text{LMTD}$ with $Q$ in **[W]**. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Underlying Assumptions for LMTD Analysis (KTU Board Favourite) For the LMTD derivation to be valid, the following assumptions are made: 1. **Steady-state operation** — temperatures do not vary with time. 2. **Constant overall heat transfer coefficient (U)** — independent of position along the exchanger. 3. **Constant specific heats** ($C_{p,h}$ and $C_{p,c}$) — for both fluids along the exchanger. 4. **Constant mass flow rates** — no phase change, no leakage. 5. **No heat loss to the surroundings** — all heat lost by hot fluid is gained by cold fluid. 6. **No axial conduction** in the wall or fluids. 7. **Plug flow** with uniform velocity distribution. > [!WARNING] > If any fluid undergoes phase change (e.g., condensation, boiling), or if $C_p$ varies significantly with temperature, the LMTD method **fails** and one must use the **Effectiveness-NTU method** or numerical integration. ## 2.2 Logical Operational Breakdown **Step 1 — Local Energy Balance (Differential Form)** For a differential area $dA$ of the heat exchanger: $$dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot (T_h - T_c)$$ **Step 2 — Hot Fluid Side** The hot fluid cools down as it gives up heat: $$dQ = -\dot{m}_h \cdot C_{p,h} \cdot dT_h \quad \Rightarrow \quad dT_h = -\frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}}$$ **Step 3 — Cold Fluid Side** The cold fluid heats up as it receives heat: $$dQ = \dot{m}_c \cdot C_{p,c} \cdot dT_c \quad \Rightarrow \quad dT_c = +\frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}}$$ **Step 4 — Define Local ΔT** $$\Delta T = T_h - T_c$$ Differentiating: $$d(\Delta T) = dT_h - dT_c = -\frac{dQ}{C_h} - \frac{dQ}{C_c} = -dQ \left[\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}\right]$$ where $C_h = \dot{m}_h C_{p,h}$ and $C_c = \dot{m}_c C_{p,c}$ are the **heat capacity rates**. **Step 5 — Substitute $dQ$ and Integrate** Combining with $dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot \Delta T$: $$\frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U \left[\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}\right] dA$$ Integrating from inlet (where $A=0$, $\Delta T = \Delta T_1$) to outlet (where $A=A$, $\Delta T = \Delta T_2$): $$\ln\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = -U A \left[\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}\right]$$ **Step 6 — Recognize the Total Heat Transfer** The total heat transferred equals: $$Q = C_h (T_{h1} - T_{h2}) = C_c (T_{c2} - T_{c1})$$ Substituting and rearranging: $$UA = \frac{C_h \, C_c}{C_h + C_c} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)$$ **Step 7 — Derive the LMTD Equation** Since $Q = UA \cdot \text{LMTD}$: $$Q = U A \cdot \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$ ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \text{LMTD}$ | Fundamental design equation | $Q$ in W | | 2 | $\text{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 \div \Delta T_2)}$ | Log-mean temperature difference | K or °C | | 3 | $\Delta T_1 = T_{h1} - T_{c1}$ | Inlet end temperature difference | K | | 4 | $\Delta T_2 = T_{h2} - T_{c2}$ | Outlet end temperature difference | K | | 5 | $Q = C_h (T_{h1} - T_{h2})$ | Heat lost by hot fluid | W | | 6 | $Q = C_c (T_{c2} - T_{c1})$ | Heat gained by cold fluid | W | | 7 | $C_h = \dot{m}_h C_{p,h}$ | Heat capacity rate of hot fluid | W/K | | 8 | $C_c = \dot{m}_c C_{p,c}$ | Heat capacity rate of cold fluid | W/K | | 9 | $A = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \text{LMTD}}$ | Area (when LMTD known) | m² | | 10 | $U = \dfrac{Q}{A \cdot \text{LMTD}}$ | Overall heat transfer coefficient | W/m²·K | | 11 | $\text{LMTD} \approx \dfrac{\Delta T_1 + \Delta T_2}{2}$ | Arithmetic Mean (valid only when $\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2 < 2$) | K | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** When $\Delta T_1 \approx \Delta T_2$ (i.e., ratio < 2), you may use the **Arithmetic Mean Temperature Difference (AMTD)** to avoid log computations. KTU board accepts both LMTD and AMTD with a clear justification. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The LMTD method is the **workhorse of chemical process industry (CPI)** for sizing: - **Shell-and-tube exchangers** in oil refineries - **Double-pipe exchangers** in pharmaceutical plants - **Condensers and reboilers** in distillation columns - **Feed-product exchangers** in cryogenic air separation units It is taught in KTU because it is the most intuitive design approach when **process conditions (inlet/outlet temperatures) are already fixed by upstream/downstream constraints** — which is the typical scenario in plant design. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of LMTD for Parallel Flow ### Setup Consider a parallel flow double-pipe heat exchanger with: - Hot fluid enters at $T_{h1}$, leaves at $T_{h2}$ - Cold fluid enters at $T_{c1}$, leaves at $T_{c2}$ - Both fluids flow in the same direction (left to right) - At any axial position $A$ (counted from inlet), hot fluid temperature is $T_h$ and cold fluid temperature is $T_c$ ### Step 1 — Write the differential heat transfer equation For a small area element $dA$: $$dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot (T_h - T_c)$$ ### Step 2 — Apply energy balance to hot fluid (cooling) The hot fluid loses heat: $$dQ = -\dot{m}_h C_{p,h} \, dT_h$$ Rearranging: $$dT_h = -\frac{dQ}{C_h}$$ ### Step 3 — Apply energy balance to cold fluid (heating) The cold fluid gains heat: $$dQ = \dot{m}_c C_{p,c} \, dT_c$$ Rearranging: $$dT_c = +\frac{dQ}{C_c}$$ ### Step 4 — Express the local temperature difference Define $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$ Differentiating: $$d(\Delta T) = dT_h - dT_c$$ ### Step 5 — Substitute and simplify $$d(\Delta T) = -\frac{dQ}{C_h} - \frac{dQ}{C_c} = -dQ \left[\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}\right] = -dQ \left[\frac{C_h + C_c}{C_h C_c}\right]$$ ### Step 6 — Eliminate $dQ$ using the heat transfer equation From Step 1: $dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot \Delta T$ Substituting: $$d(\Delta T) = -U \cdot dA \cdot \Delta T \cdot \frac{C_h + C_c}{C_h C_c}$$ ### Step 7 — Separate variables $$\frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U \left[\frac{C_h + C_c}{C_h C_c}\right] dA$$ ### Step 8 — Perform the integration Apply boundary conditions: at $A=0$, $\Delta T = \Delta T_1 = T_{h1} - T_{c1}$; at $A=A$, $\Delta T = \Delta T_2 = T_{h2} - T_{c2}$. $$\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U \left[\frac{C_h + C_c}{C_h C_c}\right] \int_{0}^{A} dA$$ $$\ln\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = -UA \left[\frac{C_h + C_c}{C_h C_c}\right]$$ ### Step 9 — Solve for the product $UA$ $$UA = \frac{C_h C_c}{C_h + C_c} \ln\left(\frac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)$$ ### Step 10 — Express $UA$ in terms of Q From total energy balance: $Q = C_h (T_{h1} - T_{h2}) = C_c (T_{c2} - T_{c1})$ This gives: $$C_h = \frac{Q}{T_{h1} - T_{h2}}, \quad C_c = \frac{Q}{T_{c2} - T_{c1}}$$ Substituting into the previous result and simplifying: $$UA = Q \cdot \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$ ### Step 11 — Final Design Equation $$\boxed{\,Q = U \cdot A \cdot \text{LMTD} = U \cdot A \cdot \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}\,}$$ ## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Pattern) **Problem Statement:** In a parallel flow heat exchanger, a hot oil ($\dot{m}_h = 2.0$ kg/s, $C_{p,h} = 2.1$ kJ/kg·K) enters at $150°C$ and is to be cooled to $90°C$ by a cold water stream ($\dot{m}_c = 1.0$ kg/s, $C_{p,c} = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K) entering at $20°C$. The overall heat transfer coefficient is $U = 800$ W/m²·K. Determine: (a) The outlet temperature of water (b) The LMTD (c) The heat transfer area required. ### Solution — Part (a): Outlet Temperature of Water Heat lost by oil = Heat gained by water: $$\dot{m}_h C_{p,h} (T_{h1} - T_{h2}) = \dot{m}_c C_{p,c} (T_{c2} - T_{c1})$$ $$2.0 \times 2.1 \times (150 - 90) = 1.0 \times 4.18 \times (T_{c2} - 20)$$ $$252 = 4.18 \times (T_{c2} - 20)$$ $$T_{c2} - 20 = \frac{252}{4.18} = 60.29°C$$ $$\boxed{T_{c2} = 80.29°C}$$ ### Solution — Part (b): Calculate LMTD Inlet end: $$\Delta T_1 = T_{h1} - T_{c1} = 150 - 20 = 130°C$$ Outlet end: $$\Delta T_2 = T_{h2} - T_{c2} = 90 - 80.29 = 9.71°C$$ LMTD calculation: $$\text{LMTD} = \frac{130 - 9.71}{\ln(130 / 9.71)} = \frac{120.29}{\ln(13.39)} = \frac{120.29}{2.5945}$$ $$\boxed{\text{LMTD} = 46.36°C}$$ ### Solution — Part (c): Heat Transfer Area Total heat transfer: $$Q = 252 \text{ kW} = 252000 \text{ W}$$ Area calculation: $$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \text{LMTD}} = \frac{252000}{800 \times 46.36}$$ $$\boxed{A = 6.79 \text{ m}^2}$$ ### Verification Using AMTD Approximation $$\text{AMTD} = \frac{130 + 9.71}{2} = 69.86°C$$ Since ratio $\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2 = 13.39 \gg 2$, AMTD is **not valid** here. LMTD must be used. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Temperature Profile Schematic (Parallel Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph inletA["INLET END A — Both fluids enter together"] direction TB h1A["HOT FLUID INLET
Th1 = 150 C
Highest temperature"] c1A["COLD FLUID INLET
Tc1 = 20 C
Lowest temperature"] h1A -.-|"Delta T1 = 130 C
MAXIMUM"| c1A end subgraph middleB["MIDDLE SECTION — Profiles converge"] direction TB hMid["Hot fluid cools
Th decreasing"] cMid["Cold fluid warms
Tc increasing"] hMid -.-|"Delta T shrinks"| cMid end subgraph outletC["OUTLET END B — Both fluids leave together"] direction TB h2C["HOT FLUID OUTLET
Th2 = 90 C"] c2C["COLD FLUID OUTLET
Tc2 = 80.29 C"] h2C -.-|"Delta T2 = 9.71 C
MINIMUM, never zero"| c2C end inletA ==> middleB middleB ==> outletC style inletA fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px style middleB fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px style outletC fill:#ffe1f0,stroke:#cc0066,stroke-width:2px style h1A fill:#ffcccc style c1A fill:#cce0ff style h2C fill:#ffcccc style c2C fill:#cce0ff ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of LMTD Analysis ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Step 1 — Identify Given Data
m_dot, Cp, Th1, Th2, Tc1, U"] B["Step 2 — Energy Balance
Q = mh Cph Th1 - Th2
Q = mc Cpc Tc2 - Tc1"] C{"Step 3 — Find
Unknown T outlet?"} D["Step 3a — Solve for unknown Tc2 or Th2
using Qh = Qc"] E["Step 4 — Compute Delta T1 and Delta T2
at both ends of exchanger"] F{"Step 5 — Is ratio
Delta T1 / Delta T2 less than 2?"} G["Use AMTD approx
AMTD = Delta T1 + Delta T2 / 2"] H["Use LMTD formula
LMTD = Delta T1 - Delta T2 / ln Delta T1 / Delta T2"] I["Step 6 — Apply design equation
Q = U times A times LMTD"] J["Step 7 — Solve for unknown quantity
Area A or Coefficient U"] A --> B --> C C -->|Yes, Tc2 unknown| D --> E C -->|No, all T known| E E --> F F -->|Yes| G --> I F -->|No| H --> I I --> J style A fill:#e1f5ff style B fill:#fff4e1 style D fill:#ffe1f0 style E fill:#ffe1f0 style G fill:#d4edda style H fill:#d4edda style I fill:#f8d7da style J fill:#fff3cd ``` ## 4.3 Parallel Flow vs Counter Flow — Quick Comparison Block ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PF["PARALLEL FLOW"] PF1["Hot in Th1 || Cold in Tc1"] PF2["Both flow LEFT to RIGHT"] PF3["Th2 always greater than Tc2"] PF4["LMTD is smaller"] PF5["Less heat transfer area efficiency"] PF1 --> PF2 --> PF3 --> PF4 --> PF5 end subgraph CF["COUNTER FLOW"] CF1["Hot in Th1 on RIGHT"] CF2["Cold in Tc1 on LEFT"] CF3["Hot and cold flow OPPOSITE"] CF4["Tc2 can exceed Th2"] CF5["Higher LMTD possible"] CF6["More thermodynamically efficient"] CF1 --> CF2 --> CF3 --> CF4 --> CF5 --> CF6 end style PF fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000 style CF fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600 ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle]** **Define LMTD. Why is it preferred over the arithmetic mean temperature difference (AMTD) for heat exchanger analysis?** **Model Answer:** LMTD stands for **Logarithmic Mean Temperature Difference**. It is defined as the effective constant temperature difference that, if applied across the entire heat exchanger, would transfer the same amount of heat as the actual variable temperature difference. $$\text{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$ LMTD is preferred over AMTD because: 1. AMTD is accurate only when $\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2 \leq 2$ (less than 2:1 ratio). For larger ratios, AMTD gives large errors. 2. LMTD is derived rigorously from the differential energy balance and is **mathematically exact** for constant $U$ and constant $C_p$. 3. LMTD accounts for the **exponential nature** of temperature decay in a heat exchanger. *(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for the comparison)* --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified Bloom Level: Understand]** **State the assumptions made in the LMTD analysis of a parallel flow heat exchanger.** **Model Answer:** 1. Steady-state, steady-flow operation. 2. Constant overall heat transfer coefficient ($U$) over the entire heat exchanger. 3. Constant specific heats ($C_p$) for both fluids. 4. No phase change in either fluid. 5. No heat loss to surroundings (perfectly insulated). 6. No axial conduction in the wall. 7. Plug flow — uniform velocity in each stream. *(3 marks — 1 mark each for any three correct assumptions with brief justification)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified Bloom Level: Apply / Analyze]** (a) **Derive the expression for LMTD in a parallel flow heat exchanger.** (7 Marks) (b) **A counter-current double-pipe heat exchanger is used to condense steam at atmospheric pressure. Cooling water enters at $30°C$ at a rate of $0.25$ kg/s and leaves at $80°C$. The overall heat transfer coefficient is $1500$ W/m²·K. If the heat exchanger area is $1.2$ m², calculate: (i) the rate of heat transfer, and (ii) the LMTD. Assume $C_{p,water} = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K. (Note: a parallel-flow conceptual question may also be set — the student should recognise this requires LMTD, not AMTD.)** (7 Marks) #### Model Solution to A(a) The detailed derivation is given in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Key steps the student must write: - Step 1: $dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot (T_h - T_c)$ - Step 2: $dQ = -C_h dT_h$ and $dQ = C_c dT_c$ - Step 3: $d(\Delta T) = dT_h - dT_c = -dQ\left[\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}\right]$ - Step 4: $\frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U\left[\frac{C_h + C_c}{C_h C_c}\right] dA$ - Step 5: Integrate and obtain $Q = U A \cdot \text{LMTD}$ - Step 6: Final boxed expression with $\Delta T_1 = T_{h1} - T_{c1}$, $\Delta T_2 = T_{h2} - T_{c2}$ clearly defined for parallel flow. *(7 marks — 2 for setup, 3 for integration, 2 for final expression)* #### Model Solution to A(b) **Given:** $\dot{m}_c = 0.25$ kg/s, $C_{p,c} = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K, $T_{c1} = 30°C$, $T_{c2} = 80°C$, $U = 1500$ W/m²·K, $A = 1.2$ m². **Step 1 — Calculate heat transfer rate:** $$Q = \dot{m}_c C_{p,c} (T_{c2} - T_{c1}) = 0.25 \times 4180 \times (80 - 30)$$ $$Q = 1045 \times 50 = 52250 \text{ W} = 52.25 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 2 — Calculate LMTD:** $$\text{LMTD} = \frac{Q}{U \cdot A} = \frac{52250}{1500 \times 1.2} = \frac{52250}{1800}$$ $$\boxed{\text{LMTD} = 29.03°C}$$ *(7 marks — 3 marks for (i), 4 marks for (ii); step-by-step shown above)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Modified Bloom Level: Apply / Analyze]** (a) **In a parallel flow heat exchanger, hot fluid (oil) flows at 1.5 kg/s with $C_p = 2.0$ kJ/kg·K, entering at $200°C$ and leaving at $100°C$. Cold water enters at $25°C$ at a flow rate of 0.8 kg/s with $C_p = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K. The overall heat transfer coefficient is $1000$ W/m²·K. Calculate: (i) the outlet temperature of water, (ii) the LMTD, and (iii) the area of the heat exchanger.** (7 Marks) (b) **Explain why the temperature profiles in a parallel flow heat exchanger do not cross.
