What is the maximum possible temperature the cold fluid can reach in a parallel flow exchanger?** (7 Marks) #### Model Solution to B(a) **Given:** $\dot{m}_h = 1.5$ kg/s, $C_{p,h} = 2000$ J/kg·K, $T_{h1} = 200°C$, $T_{h2} = 100°C$, $\dot{m}_c = 0.8$ kg/s, $C_{p,c} = 4180$ J/kg·K, $T_{c1} = 25°C$, $U = 1000$ W/m²·K. **Part (i) — Outlet Temperature of Water:** Heat lost by oil = Heat gained by water: $$\dot{m}_h C_{p,h} (T_{h1} - T_{h2}) = \dot{m}_c C_{p,c} (T_{c2} - T_{c1})$$ $$1.5 \times 2000 \times (200 - 100) = 0.8 \times 4180 \times (T_{c2} - 25)$$ $$300000 = 3344 \times (T_{c2} - 25)$$ $$T_{c2} - 25 = 89.71°C$$ $$\boxed{T_{c2} = 114.71°C}$$ **Part (ii) — LMTD:** Inlet end: $\Delta T_1 = T_{h1} - T_{c1} = 200 - 25 = 175°C$ Outlet end: $\Delta T_2 = T_{h2} - T_{c2} = 100 - 114.71 = -14.71°C$ > [!WARNING] > **CRITICAL OBSERVATION:** $\Delta T_2$ is **NEGATIVE**! This means our assumption of parallel flow is **PHYSICALLY IMPOSSIBLE** for these conditions. The cold fluid would heat up to a temperature higher than the hot fluid outlet — which violates the Second Law of Thermodynamics. **The student must explicitly state this conclusion in the exam to get full marks.** **Conclusion for B(a):** Parallel flow is not feasible for these inlet temperatures. The problem must be solved as **counter-flow** configuration. $$\boxed{\text{For feasible parallel flow: } T_{c2} \leq T_{h2}}$$ *(7 marks: Part i = 3, Part ii = 2, Part iii conclusion = 2)* #### Model Solution to B(b) **Why temperature profiles do not cross in parallel flow:** In a parallel flow heat exchanger, both fluids enter at the same end and move in the same direction. At any cross-section, the local rate of heat transfer is: $$dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot (T_h - T_c)$$ Heat can flow **only** from hot to cold, which requires $T_h > T_c$ at every point. As both fluids move in the same direction, the hot fluid continuously cools down and the cold fluid continuously heats up. The temperature difference $(T_h - T_c)$ shrinks monotonically along the length but **must remain positive** for heat to transfer. **Maximum possible temperature of cold fluid:** The cold fluid can NEVER exceed the hot fluid outlet temperature. In the limit of an **infinitely long** parallel flow heat exchanger, $T_{c2} \to T_{h2}$ asymptotically. Therefore: $$\boxed{T_{c2,\max} = T_{h2} \quad \text{(theoretical limit, requires } A \to \infty\text{)}}$$ **Practical implication:** This is why counter-flow exchangers are preferred when you need to heat a cold fluid to a temperature higher than the hot fluid outlet temperature — counter-flow has no such thermodynamic ceiling. *(7 marks: 3 for non-crossing explanation with reference to second law, 2 for the limit, 2 for practical implication)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **TOP 5 PLACES STUDENTS LOSE MARKS IN LMTD PARALLEL FLOW QUESTIONS:** > > 1. **Forgetting to identify $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ correctly** — they are the temperature differences at the **two ENDS of the exchanger**, not inlet/outlet of a single fluid. Mixing these up loses 2–3 marks. > > 2. **Using AMTD when ratio > 2** — this gives wrong answer and the examiner deducts 2 marks for not justifying the use of LMTD. > > 3. **Not checking feasibility of parallel flow** — if $T_{c2} > T_{h2}$, the problem is physically impossible in parallel flow; this MUST be flagged. Examiners give full credit only when the student recognises this. > > 4. **Unit inconsistency** — mixing kW with W, kJ with J, etc. Always convert to SI base units (W, J, m, K) before substituting. KTU examiners are strict about this. > > 5. **Missing the assumption list** — when asked to "derive LMTD", many students skip the assumptions. Always begin the derivation by listing the assumptions — this fetches 1 mark easily. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Parallel Flow + LMTD** > > - **Definition:** Parallel flow = both fluids flow in the **same direction**, enter at the same end. > - **Key sign rule:** $\Delta T_1$ is at the **common inlet**; $\Delta T_2$ is at the **common outlet**; for parallel flow $\Delta T_1 > \Delta T_2 > 0$ always. > - **Master formula:** $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \text{LMTD}$ where $\text{LMTD} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2) / \ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)$. > - **AMTD shortcut:** valid only when $\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2 < 2$ (always verify before using). > - **Heat capacity rate:** $C = \dot{m} \cdot C_p$ — appears throughout the derivation; remember the harmonic sum $1/C_h + 1/C_c$. > - **Thermodynamic limit of parallel flow:** $T_{c2}$ can NEVER exceed $T_{h2}$. > - **Assumptions list (must memorize):** steady-state, constant $U$, constant $C_p$, no phase change, no heat loss, no axial conduction, plug flow. > - **Practical use:** for **sizing** heat exchangers when inlet/outlet temperatures are specified. > - **When NOT to use LMTD:** phase change, variable $C_p$, temperature-dependent viscosity, condensation with non-condensables — switch to **NTU method**. > - **Counter-flow is more efficient:** when you need high cold-outlet temperature, counter-flow is the only choice. Parallel flow is used when **thermal stress** on the wall must be minimised (smallest $\Delta T$ at outlet). > - **Examiner's favourite question pattern:** "Calculate the area for the given conditions" — practice at least 5 numerical problems before KTU ESE.

topics.title: Counter Flow
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Counter Flow

---

# 🔄 COUNTER FLOW HEAT EXCHANGERS — A Complete KTU 2024 Study Module

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic falls under **Module 4 – Heat Exchangers** (Course Code: PCCHT402). It is a **high-weightage, frequently repeated topic** in KTU University examinations and is directly linked to **CO4**: *Apply the principles of heat transfer to design and analyze heat exchangers.*

---

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Counter Flow Heat Exchanger (CFHE):** A heat exchanger configuration in which the **hot fluid and the cold fluid flow in mutually opposite directions** (anti-parallel flow) along the length of the heat transfer surface, such that the hot fluid outlet is located adjacent to the cold fluid inlet, and the hot fluid inlet is located adjacent to the cold fluid outlet.

**KTU 2024 Precise Terminology:**
- The fluid with the **higher capacity rate (Cmax)** is typically placed in the tube side, while the fluid with the lower capacity rate is placed on the shell side — but this is a design choice, not a rule.
- The Greek letter **C = Ch/Cc** denotes the **heat capacity rate ratio**, where C ≤ 1 by convention.
- The **Correction Factor (F)** for a counter-flow exchanger is always **F = 1.0** for all capacity ratios — this is its key advantage.

---

## 1.2 The Intuitive "Train Station" Analogy 🚂

Imagine **two conveyor belts moving in opposite directions** through a long insulated tunnel:

- One belt carries **hot parcels** (hot fluid) entering from the **left**.
- The other belt carries **cold parcels** (cold fluid) entering from the **right**.
- As they pass each other, the hot parcels gradually cool down while the cold parcels gradually warm up.

> [!TIP]
> **The Big Insight:** Because the parcels travel in opposite directions, **every hot parcel meets a cold parcel that is "even colder" at every point along the tunnel**. This maintains a **strong, persistent temperature driving force** along the entire length — unlike parallel flow, where the two fluids eventually reach the same temperature and the driving force vanishes.

This sustained temperature difference makes the counter-flow exchanger **thermodynamically the most efficient** of all flow arrangements.

---

## 1.3 Why Counter Flow is Special — The KTU Highlight

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Reasons KTU Examiners Love This Topic:**
> 1. It yields the **highest possible LMTD** for given inlet/outlet temperatures.
> 2. It is the **only configuration that can handle C > 1** (when Ch > Cc) without violating the Second Law.
> 3. It can theoretically achieve **100% heat exchanger effectiveness (ε → 1)** if the surface area is sufficiently large.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

---

## 2.1 Temperature Distribution Along the Length

Let the counter-flow exchanger have a total heat transfer area **A** and overall heat transfer coefficient **U**.

**Nomenclature (KTU Standard):**

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Units |
|:------:|:--------|:-------------:|
| Th,in | Hot fluid inlet temperature | K or °C |
| Th,out | Hot fluid outlet temperature | K or °C |
| Tc,in | Cold fluid inlet temperature | K or °C |
| Tc,out | Cold fluid outlet temperature | K or °C |
| Ch | Heat capacity rate of hot fluid (ṁh·cph) | W/K |
| Cc | Heat capacity rate of cold fluid (ṁc·cpc) | W/K |
| C | Capacity rate ratio = Cmin/Cmax ≤ 1 | dimensionless |
| UA | Product of overall coefficient and area | W/K |
| NTU | Number of Transfer Units = UA/Cmin | dimensionless |
| ε | Heat exchanger effectiveness | dimensionless |
| F | LMTD correction factor | dimensionless |

---

## 2.2 Key Theoretical Relationships

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For a counter-flow exchanger, the temperature profiles of both fluids are exponential curves (not straight lines), and they approach each other asymptotically without ever meeting.**

**Step 1 — Energy Balance (steady state):**

$$q = Ch \cdot (Th,in - Th,out) = Cc \cdot (Tc,out - Tc,in)$$

**Step 2 — Overall Heat Transfer Rate:**

$$q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LM,CF}$$

**Step 3 — Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD) for Counter Flow:**

$$\Delta T_{LM,CF} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$

where for counter flow:

$$\Delta T_1 = Th,in - Tc,out$$

$$\Delta T_2 = Th,out - Tc,in$$

> [!TIP]
> **Critical KTU Rule:** For counter flow, **ΔT₁ is always the difference at one end** (hot inlet / cold outlet end) and **ΔT₂ at the other end** (hot outlet / cold inlet end). This is the **opposite** of parallel flow conventions in some textbooks — verify the diagram carefully!

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — The Counter Flow Cheat Table

> [!NOTE]
> **Save this table — it covers 80% of numerical problems on Counter Flow in KTU exams.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Application |
|:-:|:------------------|:-----------|:------------|
| 1 | Heat duty | $q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Energy balance |
| 2 | LMTD (counter flow) | $\Delta T_{LM} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Area calculation |
| 3 | Area relation | $A = \dfrac{q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LM}}$ | Sizing |
| 4 | NTU definition | $NTU = \dfrac{UA}{C_{min}}$ | Effectiveness method |
| 5 | Effectiveness (general) | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{q_{actual}}{q_{max}} = \dfrac{q}{C_{min}(Th,in - Tc,in)}$ | Performance |
| 6 | Effectiveness for CF, C ≠ 1 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-NTU(1-C)]}{1 - C \cdot \exp[-NTU(1-C)]}$ | Most-used |
| 7 | Effectiveness for CF, C = 1 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{NTU}{1 + NTU}$ | Special case |
| 8 | LMTD Correction Factor | $F_{CF} = 1.0$ | Always for counter flow |
| 9 | Outlet temperature (hot) | $Th,out = Th,in - \varepsilon \cdot C \cdot (Th,in - Tc,in)$ | Performance |
| 10 | Outlet temperature (cold) | $Tc,out = Tc,in + \varepsilon \cdot (Th,in - Tc,in)$ | Performance |

---

## 2.4 Physical Significance in Engineering

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Applications of Counter Flow Design:**
> - **Power plant condensers** (steam on one side, cooling water on the other, in counter flow)
> - **Petroleum refinery crude pre-heaters** using hot product streams
> - **Air preheaters** in blast furnaces
> - **Milk pasteurizers** (continuous flow, counter-current heating/cooling)
> - **Nuclear reactor steam generators** (where thermal efficiency is critical)

The reason counter flow dominates industry: it gives the **largest mean temperature driving force**, hence the **smallest required heat transfer area**, hence the **lowest capital cost** for the same duty.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. EXHAUSTIVE STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS

---

## 3.1 Derivation of LMTD for Counter Flow

**Given:** A counter-flow heat exchanger of area A, with hot fluid entering at temperature **Th,in** and leaving at **Th,out**, while cold fluid enters at **Tc,in** and leaves at **Tc,out**.

**Setup:** Consider a differential element of area **dA** at a distance **x** from the hot inlet end.

**Step 1 — Heat transferred through the differential area:**

$$dq = U \cdot dA \cdot \Delta T(x)$$

**Step 2 — Differential temperature drop in hot fluid:**

Since $dq = -Ch \cdot dTh$, we have:

$$dTh = -\frac{U \cdot dA \cdot \Delta T(x)}{Ch}$$

**Step 3 — Differential temperature rise in cold fluid:**

Since $dq = Cc \cdot dTc$ (cold fluid flows in opposite direction, so its temperature rises as we move from its inlet), but in counter flow, the cold fluid *temperature gradient is opposite in sign to the hot fluid gradient along the length*. Let $\Delta T = Th - Tc$:

$$d(\Delta T) = dTh - dTc = -\frac{dq}{Ch} - \frac{dq}{Cc} = -dq \cdot \left(\frac{1}{Ch} + \frac{1}{Cc}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute dq from Step 1:**

$$d(\Delta T) = -U \cdot dA \cdot \Delta T \cdot \left(\frac{1}{Ch} + \frac{1}{Cc}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Separate variables and integrate:**

$$\frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U \left(\frac{1}{Ch} + \frac{1}{Cc}\right) dA$$

Integrating from inlet (A = 0) where ΔT = Th,in − Tc,out, to outlet (A = A) where ΔT = Th,out − Tc,in:

$$\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U\left(\frac{1}{Ch} + \frac{1}{Cc}\right) \int_{0}^{A} dA$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = -U \cdot A \cdot \left(\frac{1}{Ch} + \frac{1}{Cc}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Use the energy balance relation:**

From energy balance: $Ch(Th,in - Th,out) = Cc(Tc,out - Tc,in) = q$

Therefore:

$$\frac{1}{Ch} + \frac{1}{Cc} = \frac{Th,in - Th,out + Tc,out - Tc,in}{q} = \frac{(\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)}{q}$$

where $\Delta T_1 = Th,in - Tc,out$ and $\Delta T_2 = Th,out - Tc,in$.

**Step 7 — Combine results:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = -U \cdot A \cdot \frac{(\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)}{q}$$

Rearranging for q:

$$q = U \cdot A \cdot \frac{(\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$

**Step 8 — Final Result:**

$$\boxed{q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LM,CF}}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{LM,CF} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note on Cross-Multiplication:** If the numerator (ΔT₁ − ΔT₂) is zero, then the LMTD reduces to a common temperature difference (ΔT₁ = ΔT₂). This happens when the heat capacity rates of both fluids are equal (Ch = Cc) — the temperatures vary linearly.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Effectiveness for Counter Flow (C < 1)

**Given:** Effectiveness-NTU method for counter flow.

**Step 1 — Write the outlet temperatures in terms of effectiveness:**

$$Th,out = Th,in - \varepsilon \cdot \frac{C_{min}}{C_h} \cdot (Th,in - Tc,in)$$

$$Tc,out = Tc,in + \varepsilon \cdot \frac{C_{min}}{C_c} \cdot (Th,in - Tc,in)$$

Since C = Cmin/Cmax, for the case Ch > Cc (so Cmin = Cc):

$$Th,out - Tc,out = (Th,in - Tc,in) \left[1 - \varepsilon \cdot C - \varepsilon\right] = (Th,in - Tc,in)(1 - \varepsilon)(1+C)$$

Wait — more carefully, define $T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}$ as the maximum inlet temperature difference.

**Step 2 — Apply the LMTD-integral relation:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{Th,out - Tc,in}{Th,in - Tc,out}\right) = -NTU(1 - C)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the effectiveness-based expressions:**

For counter flow, the inlet of cold fluid is at the same end as outlet of hot fluid, so:

$$\frac{Th,out - Tc,in}{Th,in - Tc,out} = \frac{(Th,in - Tc,in) - \varepsilon \cdot (Th,in - Tc,in)[1 + C] / (1 + C) \cdot \text{(simplified)}}{...}$$

A more direct route uses the relation:

$$Th,out - Tc,in = (Th,in - Tc,in)(1 - \varepsilon)(1 - C)$$  ❌

The correct substitution (after algebra) gives:

$$\frac{Th,out - Tc,in}{Th,in - Tc,out} = \frac{1 - \varepsilon}{1 - \varepsilon C}$$

**Step 4 — Equate to the LMTD-derived ratio:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{1 - \varepsilon}{1 - \varepsilon C}\right) = -NTU(1 - C)$$

$$\frac{1 - \varepsilon}{1 - \varepsilon C} = \exp[-NTU(1 - C)]$$

**Step 5 — Solve for ε:**

$$1 - \varepsilon = (1 - \varepsilon C) \cdot \exp[-NTU(1 - C)]$$

$$1 - \varepsilon = \exp[-NTU(1 - C)] - \varepsilon C \cdot \exp[-NTU(1 - C)]$$

$$\varepsilon \left[1 - C \cdot \exp[-NTU(1 - C)]\right] = 1 - \exp[-NTU(1 - C)]$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{CF} = \frac{1 - \exp[-NTU(1 - C)]}{1 - C \cdot \exp[-NTU(1 - C)]} \quad \text{for } C \neq 1}$$

**Step 6 — Special case (C = 1):**

Taking the limit $C \to 1$ using L'Hôpital's rule:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{CF} = \frac{NTU}{1 + NTU} \quad \text{for } C = 1}$$

---

## 3.3 KTU Numerical Worked Example

**Problem Statement:** In a counter-flow heat exchanger, hot fluid enters at **500 K** and leaves at **400 K**. Cold fluid enters at **300 K** and leaves at **450 K**. The overall heat transfer coefficient is **500 W/m²K** and the heat duty is **50 kW**. Calculate:
(a) The LMTD, and
(b) The required heat transfer area.

**Given:**
- Th,in = 500 K, Th,out = 400 K
- Tc,in = 300 K, Tc,out = 450 K
- U = 500 W/m²K, q = 50,000 W

**Step 1 — Identify ΔT₁ and ΔT₂ for counter flow:**

ΔT₁ = Th,in − Tc,out = 500 − 450 = **50 K** (hot inlet / cold outlet end)

ΔT₂ = Th,out − Tc,in = 400 − 300 = **100 K** (hot outlet / cold inlet end)

**Step 2 — Apply LMTD formula:**

$$\Delta T_{LM} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = \frac{50 - 100}{\ln(50 / 100)}$$

$$\Delta T_{LM} = \frac{-50}{\ln(0.5)} = \frac{-50}{-0.6931}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{LM} = 72.13 \text{ K}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convention Note:** The **sign** of ΔT_LM is always taken as **positive**. The negative sign in the numerator cancels with the negative logarithm of a number less than 1.

**Step 3 — Calculate the required area:**

$$A = \frac{q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LM}} = \frac{50{,}000}{500 \times 72.13}$$

$$\boxed{A = 1.386 \text{ m}^2}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

---

## 4.1 Counter-Flow Temperature Profile Diagram

The diagram below illustrates how the temperatures of both fluids vary along the length of a counter-flow heat exchanger.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HE["COUNTER-FLOW HEAT EXCHANGER"]
        direction LR
        endA["END A: Hot INLET  Cold OUTLET"]
        endB["END B: Hot OUTLET  Cold INLET"]
        Tprof["Temperature Profile Along Length"]
    end

    Tprof --> P1["At End A  Th,in = 500 K  Tc,out = 450 K"]
    Tprof --> P2["At End B  Th,out = 400 K  Tc,in = 300 K"]
    Tprof --> P3["Delta T1 at End A = 50 K"]
    Tprof --> P4["Delta T2 at End B = 100 K"]
    Tprof --> P5["LMTD = 72.13 K"]
    Tprof --> P6["Sustained driving force across entire length"]
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Counter Flow Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IN_H["HOT FLUID INLET  Th,in"] --> HEX_CF["COUNTER-FLOW HEAT EXCHANGER CORE"]
    IN_C["COLD FLUID INLET  Tc,in"] --> HEX_CF
    HEX_CF --> OUT_H["HOT FLUID OUTLET  Th,out"]
    HEX_CF --> OUT_C["COLD FLUID OUTLET  Tc,out"]
    HEX_CF --> Q_OUT["Heat Duty q  kW"]
    HEX_CF --> UA_OUT["Area Times Coefficient  UA  W per K"]
    HEX_CF --> LMTD_OUT["LMTD Calculation"]
    UA_OUT --> LMTD_OUT
    Q_OUT --> LMTD_OUT
    LMTD_OUT --> AREA_RESULT["Required Area  A  sq m"]
```

---

## 4.3 Comparison Block: Counter Flow vs Parallel Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CF["COUNTER FLOW  Anti Parallel Direction"]
        CF1["Hot enters left  Cold enters right"]
        CF2["Driving force remains positive throughout"]
        CF3["F factor equals 1.0 always"]
        CF4["Highest LMTD for same duties"]
        CF5["Can achieve any effectiveness up to 100 percent"]
    end

    subgraph PF["PARALLEL FLOW  Co Current Direction"]
        PF1["Hot and Cold enter same end"]
        PF2["Driving force decays toward common temperature"]
        PF3["F factor less than 1.0 generally"]
        PF4["Lower LMTD than counter flow"]
        PF5["Effectiveness limited below 50 percent"]
    end

    CF1 -.compared with.-> PF1
    CF2 -.compared with.-> PF2
    CF3 -.compared with.-> PF3
    CF4 -.compared with.-> PF4
    CF5 -.compared with.-> PF5
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** 
Define a counter-flow heat exchanger. State **any two advantages** it has over a parallel-flow heat exchanger.

**Model Answer:**

> A **counter-flow heat exchanger** is one in which the two fluids flow in **opposite directions**, such that the hot fluid outlet is located at the same end as the cold fluid inlet, and vice versa.

**Two Advantages:**
1. It provides a **higher Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)** for the same inlet and outlet temperatures, thereby requiring a **smaller heat transfer area**.
2. The temperature of the cold fluid at the outlet can theoretically **exceed the outlet temperature of the hot fluid**, which is impossible in parallel flow. This enables **higher thermal effectiveness (ε → 1)**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** 
For a counter-flow heat exchanger, write the expression for **LMTD correction factor (F)** and state its value.

**Model Answer:**

For a counter-flow heat exchanger, the LMTD correction factor:

$$F = \frac{\ln\left[\dfrac{1 - R}{1 - R \cdot C}\right]}{(R - 1) \cdot \ln\left[\dfrac{2 - R(R+1+C)}{(2 - R)(R+1+C - 2R)}\right]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> However, for an **ideal counter-flow heat exchanger with both fluids unmixed**, this factor simplifies to a constant value:
>
> $$\boxed{F_{counter-flow} = 1.0}$$
>
> This means no correction is needed for true counter-flow configuration in the LMTD method.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically divided into sub-parts (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**. Answer **ANY ONE** of the two full questions provided.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]**

**(a)** A counter-flow heat exchanger is used to condense steam at atmospheric pressure. Cooling water enters at **30 °C** with a mass flow rate of **2.0 kg/s** and leaves at **70 °C**. The steam side (condensing) has a latent heat of **2257 kJ/kg** and condenses at a rate of **0.05 kg/s**. Calculate:
   - (i) The rate of heat transfer. **(3 marks)**
   - (ii) The LMTD. **(2 marks)**
   - (iii) The required heat transfer area if U = 2000 W/m²K. **(2 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **temperature distribution** for a counter-flow heat exchanger with **C < 1** (heat capacity rates unequal). Sketch the temperature profile. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

**Part (a)(i): Rate of heat transfer**

The hot fluid is condensing steam. Heat released by steam:

$$q = \dot{m}_{steam} \cdot \lambda = 0.05 \times 2257 \times 10^3 = 112{,}850 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{q = 112.85 \text{ kW}}$$

**Part (a)(ii): LMTD calculation**

For counter-flow heat exchanger:

- Hot side: Th,in = 100 °C (saturated steam at 1 atm), Th,out = 100 °C (condensation, isothermal)
- Cold side: Tc,in = 30 °C, Tc,out = 70 °C

ΔT₁ = Th,in − Tc,out = 100 − 70 = **30 °C**

ΔT₂ = Th,out − Tc,in = 100 − 30 = **70 °C**

$$\Delta T_{LM} = \frac{30 - 70}{\ln(30/70)} = \frac{-40}{\ln(0.4286)} = \frac{-40}{-0.8473}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{LM} = 47.21 \text{ °C}}$$

**Part (a)(iii): Required area**

$$A = \frac{q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LM}} = \frac{112{,}850}{2000 \times 47.21}$$

$$\boxed{A = 1.195 \text{ m}^2}$$

**Part (b): Temperature distribution explanation**

For a counter-flow heat exchanger with C = Cmin/Cmax < 1:

- The hot fluid (Cmax) temperature changes **slowly** along the length.
- The cold fluid (Cmin) temperature changes **rapidly**, rising sharply at first.
- The cold fluid outlet temperature **can exceed** the hot fluid outlet temperature.
- The temperature difference **ΔT** remains positive throughout the length.
- The temperature profiles are **exponential curves** approaching each other asymptotically.
- The cold fluid exit can theoretically approach the hot fluid inlet temperature as area → ∞, achieving effectiveness close to unity.

**Sketch of Temperature Profile:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    X["Position along exchanger"] --> Y["Temperature"]
    A1["Hot fluid enters at Th,in = high"] --> A2["Hot fluid exits at Th,out = moderate"]
    B1["Cold fluid enters at Tc,in = low"] --> B2["Cold fluid exits at Tc,out = can exceed Th,out"]
    A2 -.exponential decay.-> A1
    B1 -.exponential rise.-> B2
```

The two curves are mirror images of each other and never intersect.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Alternate]**

**(a)** Using the **ε-NTU method**, derive the effectiveness relation for a counter-flow heat exchanger when the capacity rate ratio **C < 1**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A counter-flow heat exchanger handles **5 kg/s** of oil (cp = 2.0 kJ/kg·K) being cooled from **150 °C** to **80 °C** by water entering at **25 °C** (cp = 4.18 kJ/kg·K). The overall heat transfer coefficient is **800 W/m²K**. Calculate:
   - (i) The water mass flow rate. **(3 marks)**
   - (ii) The effectiveness. **(2 marks)**
   - (iii) The NTU and required area. **(2 marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

**Part (a): Derivation of effectiveness for counter-flow (C < 1)**

> [!TIP]
> **Refer to Section 3.2 above** for the complete step-by-step derivation.

The final result:

$$\varepsilon_{CF} = \frac{1 - \exp[-NTU(1 - C)]}{1 - C \cdot \exp[-NTU(1 - C)]}$$

The derivation proceeds from the LMTD-integral relation, expressing the temperature ratio in terms of effectiveness, equating, and solving for ε. (Full algebra shown in **Section 3.2**.)

**Part (b)(i): Water mass flow rate**

Heat duty:
$$q = \dot{m}_{oil} \cdot c_{p,oil} \cdot (Th,in - Th,out) = 5 \times 2.0 \times (150 - 80) = 700 \text{ kW}$$

By energy balance:
$$\dot{m}_w = \frac{q}{c_{p,w} \cdot (Tc,out - Tc,in)}$$

First find Tc,out — but C must be identified first:

$$C_{oil} = 5 \times 2.0 = 10 \text{ kW/K}$$

We need Tc,out. Since this requires an iterative approach (or assumption), the standard approach is to assume both fluids have comparable capacity rates and compute iteratively. For this problem, **assume cold water capacity rate is also 10 kW/K**:

$$\dot{m}_w = \frac{10}{4.18} = 2.39 \text{ kg/s}$$

So Cc = 2.39 × 4.18 ≈ 10 kW/K.

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_w \approx 2.39 \text{ kg/s}}$$

**Part (b)(ii): Effectiveness**

$$C = \frac{C_{min}}{C_{max}} = \frac{10}{10} = 1.0$$

Using the special case formula for C = 1:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{Tc,out - Tc,in}{Th,in - Tc,in} = \frac{q}{C_{min}(Th,in - Tc,in)}$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{700}{10 \times (150 - 25)} = \frac{700}{1250} = 0.56$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = 0.56}$$

**Part (b)(iii): NTU and Area**

For C = 1: $\varepsilon = \dfrac{NTU}{1 + NTU}$

$$0.56 = \frac{NTU}{1 + NTU}$$

$$0.56 + 0.56 \cdot NTU = NTU$$

$$0.56 = 0.44 \cdot NTU$$

$$\boxed{NTU = 1.273}$$

Area:

$$A = \frac{NTU \cdot C_{min}}{U} = \frac{1.273 \times 10{,}000}{800}$$

$$\boxed{A = 15.91 \text{ m}^2}$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose 3–5 Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Swapping ΔT₁ and ΔT₂:** In counter flow, the convention is ΔT₁ = Th,in − Tc,out and ΔT₂ = Th,out − Tc,in. Reversing them gives a numerically identical answer (since LMTD is symmetric), but **writing the wrong labels costs method marks.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting F = 1.0:** Several students apply a correction factor for counter flow. Remember: **F = 1.0 always** for true counter-flow — write this explicitly in your answer.
>
> 3. **Using Parallel Flow Formula:** When asked for **counter-flow effectiveness**, using the parallel-flow equation $\varepsilon_{PF} = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-NTU(1+C)]}{1 + C}$ will be marked **completely wrong**.
>
> 4. **Sign Error in Logarithm:** $\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)$ becomes negative when $\Delta T_2 > \Delta T_1$. The numerator is also negative. The **negative signs cancel** — do not leave a negative LMTD.
>
> 5. **Not Stating the Assumption of Constant U and cp:** Examiners award 1 mark for stating assumptions explicitly.

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section — it covers 90% of what you need for the KTU exam on Counter Flow.**

- ✅ Counter flow = fluids move in **opposite directions** along the heat transfer surface.
- ✅ LMTD formula: $\Delta T_{LM} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2) / \ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)$.
- ✅ For counter flow: $\Delta T_1 = Th,in - Tc,out$ and $\Delta T_2 = Th,out - Tc,in$.
- ✅ **Correction factor F = 1.0 always** for counter-flow configuration.
- ✅ Effectiveness for C < 1: $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-NTU(1-C)]}{1 - C \cdot \exp[-NTU(1-C)]}$.
- ✅ Effectiveness for C = 1: $\varepsilon = \dfrac{NTU}{1 + NTU}$.
- ✅ Effectiveness for C > 1 in counter flow: **achievable up to 100%** (uniquely among all configurations).
- ✅ NTU = UA / Cmin — dimensionless driving-force units.
- ✅ Counter flow gives **highest LMTD** and **highest effectiveness** for given area.
- ✅ Cold fluid outlet temperature can **exceed** hot fluid outlet temperature.
- ✅ When Ch = Cc (C = 1), temperature profiles are **linear**, not exponential.
- ✅ Always check whether the problem uses **ε-NTU method or LMTD method** before solving.
- ✅ Inlet/outlet end identification is the **#1 source of mistakes** — label your sketch clearly.
- ✅ For **condensing or boiling** (isothermal hot side): ΔT at hot inlet = ΔT at hot outlet, so ΔT₁ ≠ ΔT₂ — use the standard LMTD formula, it still works.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cross Flow and Multi-Pass Heat Exchangers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cross Flow and Multi-Pass Heat Exchangers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Cross Flow Heat Exchanger** is a configuration in which the two fluid streams move perpendicular to each other (typically at a **90° angle**). One fluid flows through a bundle of parallel tubes while the second fluid flows across the tubes, being deflected by the tube baffles. Such units are compact and used extensively in air-cooled condensers, automotive radiators, and HVAC coils.

A **Multi-Pass Heat Exchanger** is a shell-and-tube type arrangement in which one or both of the fluids are forced to change direction and traverse the heat-transfer surface more than once before exiting. The most common industrial configurations are the **1-2 (one shell pass, two tube passes)** and **2-4 (two shell passes, four tube passes)** arrangements. They combine the mechanical advantages of shell-and-tube construction with thermal performance approaching true counter-current flow.

For both configurations, the true log-mean temperature difference (LMTD) deviates from the ideal counterflow LMTD, so a **Temperature Difference Correction Factor (F)** is introduced.

$$Q = U A \, F \, \Delta T_{lm,CF}$$

where $F \le 1$ is the correction factor read from a Bowman-Mueller-Nagle chart, $U$ is the **overall heat transfer coefficient** (W/m²·K), and $A$ is the heat transfer area (m²).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive:** Students must master the **Correction Factor Method** for cross flow and multi-pass exchangers, and understand the construction/usage of the F-factor chart with parameters $P$ and $R$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine a city intersection where pedestrians (Fluid A) walk straight across a road, while cyclists (Fluid B) ride perpendicular to them.

- The **crossing zone** is the heat transfer area — energy (heat) is exchanged only where the two streams physically overlap.
- If a pedestrian is "slowed" by having to cross multiple lanes, more opportunity for heat exchange is created — this mimics a **multi-pass** arrangement, where fluid A is forced to traverse the exchanger multiple times.
- Because the streams don't move in perfectly opposite directions, the temperature "driving force" is reduced at the corners — this is why we need a **correction factor $F < 1$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Metrics Used:**
> - Heat transfer coefficient $U$: typically **200 – 1500 W/m²·K** for liquid-liquid, **30 – 300 W/m²·K** for gas-side exchangers.
> - Correction factor $F$ is dimensionless and lies in the range **0 ≤ F ≤ 1**.
> - Effectiveness $\varepsilon$ is dimensionless and lies in the range **0 ≤ ε ≤ 1**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why We Need the Correction Factor F

In an **ideal counterflow** exchanger, the temperature profile on the hot and cold sides is parallel, yielding the maximum possible LMTD. In **cross flow** and **multi-pass** units, this parallelism is broken:

- The terminal temperatures are not co-linear, causing the local ΔT to drop significantly near the outlet.
- Using the simple counterflow LMTD would **overestimate** the true mean temperature difference, leading to an undersized exchanger.

Hence a **correction factor F** is applied:

$$\Delta T_m = F \cdot \Delta T_{lm,CF}$$

where $\Delta T_{lm,CF}$ is the log-mean temperature difference calculated **as if** the exchanger were a pure counterflow unit.

## 2.2 Construction of the F-Factor Chart

The correction factor depends on two dimensionless temperature ratios:

$$P = \frac{t_2 - t_1}{T_1 - t_1} \quad \text{(cold fluid temperature effectiveness)}$$

$$R = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{t_2 - t_1} \quad \text{(capacity rate ratio)} = \frac{C_{hot}}{C_{cold}}$$

where:
- $T_1, T_2$ = hot fluid inlet and outlet temperatures (K)
- $t_1, t_2$ = cold fluid inlet and outlet temperatures (K)
- $C = \dot{m} C_p$ = heat capacity rate (W/K)

For the KTU 2024 exam, you must memorize the **chart selection table**:

| Exchanger Type | Chart to Use |
|---|---|
| 1 shell pass, 2, 4, 6... tube passes | F vs P, with R as parameter |
| 2 shell passes, 4, 8... tube passes | Modified chart (F vs P, R) |
| Cross flow, both fluids unmixed | Chart 1 |
| Cross flow, one fluid mixed, other unmixed | Chart 2 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Utility:** The F-factor method is used worldwide in the design of process heat exchangers in **refineries, ammonia plants, power plant condensers, and LNG preheaters**. Engineers at KTU-affiliated process industries (FACT, BPCL-Kochi, MRPL) routinely use this technique via software like **HTRI** and **Aspen Exchanger Design**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \dot{m}_h C_{ph}(T_1 - T_2) = \dot{m}_c C_{pc}(t_2 - t_1)$ | Energy balance on each stream | W |
| 2 | $\Delta T_{lm,CF} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log-mean temperature difference (counterflow) | K |
| 3 | $\Delta T_1 = T_1 - t_2$, $\Delta T_2 = T_2 - t_1$ | Terminal temperature differences | K |
| 4 | $P = (t_2 - t_1)/(T_1 - t_1)$ | Cold fluid temperature effectiveness | – |
| 5 | $R = (T_1 - T_2)/(t_2 - t_1)$ | Capacity rate ratio | – |
| 6 | $\Delta T_m = F \cdot \Delta T_{lm,CF}$ | True mean ΔT for cross/multi-pass | K |
| 7 | $Q = U A \Delta T_m$ | Heat duty via overall coefficient | W |
| 8 | $C_{min} = \min(\dot{m}_h C_{ph},\; \dot{m}_c C_{pc})$ | Minimum heat capacity rate | W/K |
| 9 | $C_{max} = \max(\dot{m}_h C_{ph},\; \dot{m}_c C_{pc})$ | Maximum heat capacity rate | W/K |
| 10 | $C_r = C_{min}/C_{max}$ | Heat capacity ratio | – |
| 11 | $\text{NTU} = U A / C_{min}$ | Number of Transfer Units | – |
| 12 | $\varepsilon = Q_{actual} / Q_{max} = (T_1 - T_2)/(T_1 - t_1) \cdot C_{min}/C_h$ | Effectiveness | – |
| 13 | $\varepsilon = 1 - \exp[-\text{NTU}\,(1 - C_r)]$ for **cross flow, both unmixed** (approx) | ε-NTU relation | – |
| 14 | $\varepsilon = 1 - \exp\left\{-\text{NTU}\,\left[1 - C_r \exp(-\text{NTU})\right]\right\}$ for **one mixed, one unmixed** | ε-NTU relation | – |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Rule:** Never use absolute value symbols `|x|` inside markdown tables — use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ to prevent table-rendering failures.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

## 3.1 Derivation: LMTD in Counterflow Exchanger

Consider a counterflow heat exchanger with hot fluid cooling from $T_1$ to $T_2$ and cold fluid heating from $t_1$ to $t_2$.

Take a differential element of area $dA$. The heat transferred across this element is:

$$dQ = U \, dA \, \Delta T$$

where $\Delta T = T - t$ at that location. By energy balance on the hot stream over the differential element:

$$dQ = -\dot{m}_h C_{ph} \, dT = C_h \, (-\, dT)$$

Equating the two expressions for $dQ$:

$$U \, dA \, (T - t) = -C_h \, dT$$

The temperature difference $(T - t)$ varies along the length. For the cold stream, applying energy balance gives $C_c \, dt = C_h \, (-dT)$ if $C_h = C_c$, so $(T-t)$ is constant.

In the general case, the integrated form yields the **log-mean temperature difference**:

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$

where $\Delta T_1 = T_1 - t_2$ and $\Delta T_2 = T_2 - t_1$ (counterflow convention).

> [!NOTE]
> This LMTD is rigorously valid **only for counterflow**. For all other configurations, the correction factor $F$ must be applied.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1: 1-2 Shell-and-Tube Exchanger (14-Mark Standard)

**Problem:** *(KTU University Exam — May 2024 style)*

A 1-2 shell-and-tube heat exchanger is used to condense steam at **T₁ = 410 K** by heating water from **t₁ = 300 K** to **t₂ = 360 K**. The overall heat transfer coefficient is **U = 1000 W/m²·K**. The heat load is **Q = 100 kW**. Determine the heat transfer area required. (Use R = 2.0 and read F from the 1-2 chart; assume F = 0.85 as a typical reading for educational purposes.)

**Step 1 — Energy balance to find hot fluid outlet T₂.**

Since the hot fluid is condensing steam, its temperature does not change during condensation:

$$T_2 = T_1 = 410 \text{ K}$$

(Steam condenses at constant temperature while releasing latent heat.)

**Step 2 — Calculate heat capacity rates and R.**

From the energy balance on the cold stream:

$$C_c = \frac{Q}{t_2 - t_1} = \frac{100{,}000}{360 - 300} = 1666.67 \text{ W/K}$$

For condensing steam, $C_h \to \infty$ (since $T_1 - T_2 = 0$ but heat is released):

$$R = \frac{C_h}{C_c} \to \infty$$

However, the standard chart convention uses $R = (T_1 - T_2)/(t_2 - t_1)$. Here, $T_1 - T_2 = 0$:

$$R = \frac{0}{60} = 0$$

So this is a special case ($R = 0$). For $R = 0$, $F = 1$ (it behaves like counterflow with phase change).

**Step 3 — Calculate counterflow LMTD.**

$$\Delta T_1 = T_1 - t_2 = 410 - 360 = 50 \text{ K}$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_2 - t_1 = 410 - 300 = 110 \text{ K}$$

$$\Delta T_{lm,CF} = \frac{110 - 50}{\ln(110/50)} = \frac{60}{\ln(2.2)} = \frac{60}{0.7885} = 76.09 \text{ K}$$

**Step 4 — Apply correction factor.**

For $R = 0$, the F-factor for a 1-2 exchanger equals 1 (from the Bowman chart at the R = 0 boundary):

$$F = 1.00$$

Therefore:

$$\Delta T_m = F \cdot \Delta T_{lm,CF} = 1.00 \times 76.09 = 76.09 \text{ K}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the heat transfer area.**

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_m} = \frac{100{,}000}{1000 \times 76.09} = 1.314 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\boxed{A \approx 1.31 \text{ m}^2}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2: Cross Flow Exchanger (F-Factor Determination)

**Problem:** *(KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 style)*

In a cross flow heat exchanger with both fluids unmixed, hot oil enters at **T₁ = 423 K** and leaves at **T₂ = 373 K**, while cold water enters at **t₁ = 303 K** and leaves at **t₂ = 343 K**. The overall heat transfer coefficient is **U = 850 W/m²·K** and the heat duty is **Q = 75 kW**. Calculate the area using the F-factor method.

**Step 1 — Compute P and R.**

$$P = \frac{t_2 - t_1}{T_1 - t_1} = \frac{343 - 303}{423 - 303} = \frac{40}{120} = 0.333$$

$$R = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{t_2 - t_1} = \frac{423 - 373}{343 - 303} = \frac{50}{40} = 1.25$$

**Step 2 — Counterflow LMTD.**

$$\Delta T_1 = T_1 - t_2 = 423 - 343 = 80 \text{ K}$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_2 - t_1 = 373 - 303 = 70 \text{ K}$$

$$\Delta T_{lm,CF} = \frac{80 - 70}{\ln(80/70)} = \frac{10}{\ln(1.1429)} = \frac{10}{0.13353} = 74.89 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3 — Read F from the cross flow chart (both unmixed).**

Using the Bowman-Mueller-Nagle chart for cross flow, both fluids unmixed, with **P = 0.333** and **R = 1.25**, we read:

$$F \approx 0.94$$

**Step 4 — Compute true ΔTm.**

$$\Delta T_m = F \cdot \Delta T_{lm,CF} = 0.94 \times 74.89 = 70.40 \text{ K}$$

**Step 5 — Heat transfer area.**

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_m} = \frac{75{,}000}{850 \times 70.40} = \frac{75{,}000}{59{,}840} = 1.253 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\boxed{A \approx 1.25 \text{ m}^2}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3: 2-4 Multi-Pass Exchanger

**Problem:** *(KTU University Exam — Jun 2023 style)*

A 2-4 shell-and-tube exchanger is used to cool a hot process stream. Hot fluid: $T_1 = 500$ K, $T_2 = 400$ K. Cold fluid: $t_1 = 300$ K, $t_2 = 450$ K. Overall U = 600 W/m²·K, Q = 200 kW. Find the required area. (Assume F = 0.75 from the 2-4 chart.)

**Step 1 — Compute P and R.**

$$P = \frac{t_2 - t_1}{T_1 - t_1} = \frac{450 - 300}{500 - 300} = \frac{150}{200} = 0.75$$

$$R = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{t_2 - t_1} = \frac{500 - 400}{450 - 300} = \frac{100}{150} = 0.667$$

**Step 2 — Counterflow LMTD.**

$$\Delta T_1 = T_1 - t_2 = 500 - 450 = 50 \text{ K}$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_2 - t_1 = 400 - 300 = 100 \text{ K}$$

$$\Delta T_{lm,CF} = \frac{100 - 50}{\ln(100/50)} = \frac{50}{\ln 2} = \frac{50}{0.6931} = 72.13 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3 — Apply F = 0.75.**

$$\Delta T_m = 0.75 \times 72.13 = 54.10 \text{ K}$$

**Step 4 — Area.**

$$A = \frac{200{,}000}{600 \times 54.10} = \frac{200{,}000}{32{,}460} = 6.16 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\boxed{A \approx 6.16 \text{ m}^2}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cross Flow Heat Exchanger — Flow Architecture

The diagram below maps the perpendicular interaction between the two streams and the corner-temperature zones where the LMTD deviates most from counterflow behaviour.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Hot Fluid Inlet T1] --> B[Tube Bank - Flow Direction 1]
    B --> C[Hot Fluid Outlet T2]
    D[Cold Fluid Inlet t1] --> E[Tube Bank Crossed at 90 degrees]
    E --> F[Cold Fluid Outlet t2]
    G[Corner Zone High Deviation]:::warn
    H[Central Zone Small Deviation]:::ok
    I[Effective Heat Transfer Area]:::area
    B -. interacts with .-> E
    E -. exits to .-> F
    classDef warn fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef ok fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    classDef area fill:#e1ecff,stroke:#003399,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Multi-Pass Shell-and-Tube (1-2 Configuration) Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Hot Fluid Enters Shell Side T1] --> B[Shell Pass 1 - Baffles Direct Flow]
    B --> C[Hot Fluid Exits Shell T2]
    D[Cold Fluid Enters Tube Inlet Header t1] --> E[Tube Pass 1 Forward]
    E --> F[Return Header Reverses Flow]
    F --> G[Tube Pass 2 Return]
    G --> H[Cold Fluid Exits t2]
    I[Partition Baffle in Return Header]:::baffle
    J[Combined Area A equals Tube Pass 1 plus Tube Pass 2]:::area
    E -. counts as .-> J
    G -. counts as .-> J
    classDef baffle fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef area fill:#e1ecff,stroke:#003399,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram: F-Factor Method Workflow

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Step 1 Identify Inlet and Outlet Temperatures T1 T2 t1 t2] --> S2[Step 2 Compute Heat Duty Q via Energy Balance]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Calculate P and R Dimensionless Groups]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Read F from the Correct Bowman Mueller Nagle Chart]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Compute Counterflow LMTD using Terminal DeltaT]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Multiply by F to get True Mean DeltaTm]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Apply Q equals U A DeltaTm to find A]
    S7 --> S8[Final Result Heat Transfer Area in square metres]:::result
    classDef result fill:#d4f7d4,stroke:#006600,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Comparative Architecture of Exchanger Types

| Configuration | Flow Pattern | Typical F Range | Best Used When |
|---|---|---|---|
| Counterflow (ideal) | Opposite direction | F = 1.00 | Maximum heat recovery needed |
| Parallel flow | Same direction | F < 0.8 | Quick cooling, low ΔT |
| 1-2 shell-and-tube | Cross-cum-counter | 0.75 – 0.95 | Standard process service |
| 2-4 shell-and-tube | Two counter shells | 0.80 – 0.97 | Closer to counterflow performance |
| Cross flow, both unmixed | 90° perpendicular | 0.85 – 0.98 | Air-cooled exchangers, radiators |
| Cross flow, one mixed | Tube-side fully mixed | 0.80 – 0.95 | Compact finned units |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define the correction factor F for a heat exchanger. Why is its value always less than or equal to 1?**

**Model Answer:**
The correction factor F is defined as the ratio of the true mean temperature difference in a heat exchanger to the log-mean temperature difference calculated assuming counterflow:

$$F = \frac{\Delta T_m}{\Delta T_{lm,CF}}$$

F is always ≤ 1 because **only counterflow** produces the maximum parallel temperature profiles, yielding the highest LMTD. In all other arrangements (parallel, cross, multi-pass), the temperature profiles diverge at the exits, reducing the local ΔT and hence the mean ΔT.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between "cross flow with both fluids unmixed" and "cross flow with one fluid mixed, the other unmixed".**

**Model Answer:**
- **Both unmixed:** Neither fluid is redistributed as it crosses the heat exchanger. Each tube-side and shell-side stream retains its inlet/outlet identity. Typical of finned coils and tube banks with deep baffles.
- **One mixed, one unmixed:** One fluid is thoroughly mixed (e.g., by strong crossflow-induced turbulence or by a large plenum), so it has a single uniform bulk temperature. The other fluid moves through discrete unmixed channels. Typical of condensers with header-fed tubes.

The ε-NTU relations differ for the two cases.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Cross Flow Exchanger

**`[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]`**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* A cross flow heat exchanger (both fluids unmixed) heats **2000 kg/h** of oil ($C_p = 2.1$ kJ/kg·K) from **313 K to 353 K** using hot water that enters at **363 K** and leaves at **333 K**. The overall heat transfer coefficient is **U = 350 W/m²·K**. Calculate the heat transfer area.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* If the same exchanger is operated with the oil entering at 323 K (instead of 313 K) with all other conditions identical, determine the new outlet temperature of the oil and comment on the feasibility using the F-factor chart.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

Heat duty:

$$Q = \dot{m}_{oil} C_{p,oil} (T_{out,oil} - T_{in,oil})$$

$$Q = \frac{2000}{3600} \times 2100 \times (353 - 313) = 0.5556 \times 2100 \times 40$$

$$Q = 46{,}667 \text{ W} = 46.67 \text{ kW}$$

Cold fluid (oil) here is the cooler one being heated; we identify P and R using standard notation. Let us rename: cold stream = oil (heating 313 → 353), hot stream = water (363 → 333).

$$P = \frac{t_2 - t_1}{T_1 - t_1} = \frac{353 - 313}{363 - 313} = \frac{40}{50} = 0.80$$

$$R = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{t_2 - t_1} = \frac{363 - 333}{353 - 313} = \frac{30}{40} = 0.75$$

Counterflow LMTD:

$$\Delta T_1 = 363 - 353 = 10 \text{ K}, \quad \Delta T_2 = 333 - 313 = 20 \text{ K}$$

$$\Delta T_{lm,CF} = \frac{20 - 10}{\ln(20/10)} = \frac{10}{0.6931} = 14.43 \text{ K}$$

From the cross flow chart (both unmixed) at P = 0.80, R = 0.75: **F ≈ 0.92**.

$$\Delta T_m = 0.92 \times 14.43 = 13.28 \text{ K}$$

$$\boxed{A = \frac{Q}{U \Delta T_m} = \frac{46{,}667}{350 \times 13.28} = 10.04 \text{ m}^2}$$

**Part (b):**

If oil enters at 323 K, we need to solve for the new oil outlet temperature $t_2'$ using the same area A = 10.04 m² and the F-factor iteration. Apply energy balance:

$$Q' = 0.5556 \times 2100 \times (t_2' - 323) = 1166.67 \times (t_2' - 323)$$

Heat capacity rate of water:

$$C_w = \frac{Q'}{T_1 - T_2'} = \frac{1166.67 (t_2' - 323)}{363 - T_2'}$$

But also from water side energy balance: $C_w (363 - T_2') = Q'$, which is consistent.

We use the F-factor iterative method. The trial solution (iterating on $t_2'$) gives approximately:

$$t_2' \approx 354.6 \text{ K}$$

The exchanger is feasible as long as $P \le P_{max}$ at the given R (a constraint from the F-chart boundary). Here P is well within the feasible region, so the design is valid.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Multi-Pass Shell-and-Tube Exchanger

**`[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* A **1-2 shell-and-tube heat exchanger** condenses **5000 kg/h** of saturated steam at **2 bar (T = 393 K)**. Cooling water enters at **298 K** and leaves at **318 K**. The overall heat transfer coefficient is **U = 2000 W/m²·K**. Calculate the heat transfer area.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Explain with a neat sketch the construction of a 2-4 shell-and-tube exchanger and state two advantages over a 1-2 exchanger.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

Latent heat of vaporization of steam at 2 bar ≈ **2206 kJ/kg** (from steam tables).

$$Q = \dot{m}_{steam} \times \lambda = \frac{5000}{3600} \times 2206 \times 10^3$$

$$Q = 1.389 \times 2206 \times 10^3 = 3.064 \times 10^6 \text{ W} = 3064 \text{ kW}$$

Since steam condenses, $T_1 = T_2 = 393$ K. Therefore $R = 0$ and $F = 1$ for 1-2 exchanger (boundary of chart).

Counterflow LMTD:

$$\Delta T_1 = 393 - 318 = 75 \text{ K}, \quad \Delta T_2 = 393 - 298 = 95 \text{ K}$$

$$\Delta T_{lm,CF} = \frac{95 - 75}{\ln(95/75)} = \frac{20}{0.2364} = 84.6 \text{ K}$$

$$\Delta T_m = F \times \Delta T_{lm,CF} = 1.0 \times 84.6 = 84.6 \text{ K}$$

$$\boxed{A = \frac{Q}{U \Delta T_m} = \frac{3.064 \times 10^6}{2000 \times 84.6} = 18.11 \text{ m}^2}$$

**Part (b):**

A 2-4 shell-and-tube exchanger has **2 shell passes** and **4 tube passes**. The shell is divided longitudinally by a partition, forcing the shell-side fluid to reverse direction once. The tube bundle is split by two return headers, creating four tube passes.

**Advantages over 1-2:**
1. **Higher F-factor (closer to 1)** for the same P and R, meaning the true mean ΔT is closer to the ideal counterflow LMTD, yielding a smaller area.
2. **Better temperature approach** and thermal performance because the shell-side reversal approximates true countercurrent flow.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Using $\Delta T_2 - \Delta T_1$ instead of $\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2$ in the LMTD numerator** — the order matters! Always take (hot end ΔT) − (cold end ΔT) with the appropriate sign.
> 2. **Forgetting to read the correct F-chart** — a 1-2 chart is **NOT** used for cross flow. Mixing up the charts costs 3–4 marks instantly.
> 3. **Using $R = C_{min}/C_{max}$ instead of $(T_1 - T_2)/(t_2 - t_1)$** — these are only equal when both streams have $C_p$ values that yield matching ratios. The KTU exam explicitly tests the (T₁−T₂)/(t₂−t₁) definition.
> 4. **Inlet/outlet temperature transposition** — confusing $t_1$ (cold inlet) with $t_2$ (cold outlet) is the single most common arithmetic error in heat-exchanger problems.
> 5. **Ignoring the constraint P ≤ P_max** — a design with P above the F-chart envelope is physically infeasible (it implies a temperature cross) and will receive zero credit on a design question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Counterflow** is the *only* configuration where $F = 1$ universally; all others require $F < 1$.
- The **LMTD formula** requires positive ΔT at both ends; a "temperature cross" (where ΔT changes sign along the length) is a red flag for the design.
- The **F-factor** is a function of two dimensionless groups, $P$ and $R$, read from a chart specific to the exchanger geometry.
- For **cross flow with both fluids unmixed**, the ε-NTU formula is:
  $$\varepsilon = 1 - \exp[-\text{NTU}\,(1 - C_r)]$$
  (approximate; the exact form involves an infinite series).
- For **cross flow with one fluid mixed, the other unmixed**:
  $$\varepsilon = 1 - \exp\left\{-\text{NTU}\,\left[1 - C_r \exp(-\text{NTU})\right]\right\}$$
- **Multi-pass exchangers** (1-2, 2-4) approach counterflow performance as the number of passes increases; 2-4 typically has $F$ values 0.05–0.15 higher than 1-2 at the same P and R.
- **Phase change on one side** (condensation, evaporation) ⇒ $R = 0$ ⇒ $F = 1$, regardless of geometry. This is a frequent KTU short-answer and is highly scoring.
- The **heat transfer area A** computed from $Q = U A \Delta T_m$ is the **total surface area** of all tube passes combined, not per-pass.
- **NTU = U A / C_min** is the key dimensionless group of the ε-NTU method; $\varepsilon = Q_{actual}/Q_{max}$, where $Q_{max} = C_{min}(T_1 - t_1)$.
- The chart-based F-factor is read at the intersection of $P$ (vertical axis) and $R$ (curve family); many KTU students mis-plot these axes, so practise chart reading carefully.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Temperature-Distance Plots for Different Flow Arrangements in Single and Multi-Pass Heat Exchangers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Temperature-Distance Plots for Different Flow Arrangements in Single and Multi-Pass Heat Exchangers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Exchanger Flow Arrangements** refer to the specific geometric configuration in which the hot and cold fluid streams enter, traverse, and exit the heat transfer device. The **temperature–distance (T vs. x) plot** is a graphical representation showing how the bulk temperature of each fluid changes with respect to the distance travelled along the heat exchanger length, governed by the relative **direction of motion** and the **number of passes**.

In shell-and-tube heat exchangers, KTU 2024 Scheme classifies arrangements based on:
- **Passes on tube side (T)** and **passes on shell side (S)**, denoted as **S–T** notation.
- The most common standard is the **TEMA (Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association)** classification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Single-Pass (1-1) Heat Exchanger** — Both shell-side and tube-side fluids make a single traverse from inlet to outlet. The two sub-cases are **Co-current (parallel) flow** and **Counter-current flow**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Multi-Pass Heat Exchanger** — One or both fluids are forced to change direction multiple times, e.g., **1-2 (one shell pass, two tube passes)**, **2-4**, **1-2-2**, etc. These are characterised by the use of a **Correction Factor (F)** applied to the LMTD.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two trains travelling on parallel tracks:

- **Co-current (parallel) flow**: Both trains travel in the *same direction*. The hot train (furnace) and cold train (refrigerator) start at one end — the hot one gives heat, the cold one receives it. As they move, both temperatures **approach each other** but never cross. The final outlet temperatures of the two streams get closer and closer, but the temperature difference becomes very small near the outlet, limiting heat transfer.

- **Counter-current flow**: The trains travel in *opposite directions*. The hottest part of the hot fluid meets the warmest part of the cold fluid, and the coldest part of the hot fluid meets the coldest part of the cold fluid. This is like a "perfect handshake" — the temperature difference remains **nearly uniform** along the entire length, allowing **maximum possible heat transfer**.

- **Multi-pass (e.g., 1-2)**: Think of the hot fluid going straight through (one shell pass), while the cold fluid weaves back and forth like a snake (two tube passes). The temperature profile looks like a **zig-zag** or **folded** pattern.

The T–x plot is essentially the **fingerprint** of the heat exchanger — it tells you, at a glance, the efficiency and behaviour of that specific arrangement.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Board Examinations

- **LMTD** = **L**og **M**ean **T**emperature **D**ifference (°C or K)
- **Correction Factor, F** (dimensionless, always ≤ 1)
- **Heat Capacity Ratio, R** = (C_min / C_max) (dimensionless)
- **Effectiveness, ε** (dimensionless, 0 to 1)
- **NTU** = Number of Transfer Units = (UA / C_min)
- **P** = Temperature effectiveness of the fluid with minimum heat capacity

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently test whether students can correctly **identify ΔT₁ and ΔT₂** from the given inlet and outlet temperatures for each flow arrangement. Memorising the orientation is worth 3–5 marks per problem.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Breakdown of Each Flow Arrangement

### 2.1.1 Co-current (Parallel) Flow — 1-1 Co-current

- Both fluids enter at the **same end (x = 0)** and flow in the **same direction**.
- Temperature of hot fluid **decreases** monotonically.
- Temperature of cold fluid **increases** monotonically.
- The two temperature curves **approach each other asymptotically** but never cross.
- Maximum possible temperature of cold fluid outlet is limited by the hot fluid inlet temperature.
- Suitable when **outlet temperature of cold stream must not exceed a specific limit**.

### 2.1.2 Counter-current Flow — 1-1 Counter

- Fluids enter at **opposite ends** and flow in **opposite directions**.
- The hot fluid outlet meets the cold fluid inlet at one end; hot fluid inlet meets cold fluid outlet at the other.
- The temperature difference ΔT remains **almost constant** if heat capacity rates are equal.
- Allows the cold fluid outlet to **exceed the hot fluid outlet** in temperature (a unique advantage).
- Thermodynamically the **most efficient** arrangement.

### 2.1.3 Cross Flow

- Fluids flow **perpendicular** to each other (one horizontal, one vertical).
- Common in air-cooled condensers and radiators.
- Each fluid may be mixed (well-mixed) or unmixed (stream-wise).
- The T-x plot is more complex; average temperature differences must be computed using empirical correction factors.

### 2.1.4 Multi-Pass Arrangements (1-2, 1-4, 2-4, 1-2-2)

- **1-2 (one shell pass, two tube passes)**: The shell-side fluid makes one pass; tube-side fluid makes two passes. The T-x plot resembles a **counter-current pattern in segments** with one "crossover" point.
- **1-4, 1-2-2, 2-4, 2-8**: Even number of tube passes ensures both fluids enter and exit on the **same side** of the shell (practical convenience).
- Correction Factor F < 1 reduces the effective LMTD.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Temperature Profile Shapes

| Feature | Co-current | Counter-current |
|---|---|---|
| ΔT at inlet | Large (T_h,in − T_c,in) | Large (T_h,in − T_c,out) |
| ΔT at outlet | Small (T_h,out − T_c,out) | Large (T_h,out − T_c,in) |
| Log-mean ΔT | Smaller | Larger |
| Heat transfer | Less (for equal UA) | More (for equal UA) |
| T_c,out can exceed T_h,out? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Cross-over possible? | No (T_h always > T_c) | Yes (if R ≠ 1) |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **directly testable** in KTU University Exams. Master the table — it covers 80% of Module 4 numericals.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat Duty | $Q = \dot{m}_h \, c_{p,h} \, (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = \dot{m}_c \, c_{p,c} \, (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$ | W or kW | Energy balance |
| 2 | Heat Capacity Rate | $C = \dot{m} \, c_p$ | W/K | $C_h$ and $C_c$ |
| 3 | LMTD — General | $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | K or °C | For $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$, use arithmetic mean |
| 4 | ΔT₁, ΔT₂ — Co-current | $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}$ ; $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,out}$ | K | Both at same end |
| 5 | ΔT₁, ΔT₂ — Counter-current | $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$ ; $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$ | K | At opposite ends |
| 6 | Heat Transfer Rate | $Q = U \, A \, \Delta T_{lm}$ | W | For single-pass |
| 7 | Heat Transfer Rate — Multi-pass | $Q = U \, A \, F \, \Delta T_{lm,CF}$ | W | F = correction factor |
| 8 | Heat Capacity Ratio | $R = \dfrac{C_{min}}{C_{max}} = \dfrac{C_c}{C_h}$ (if C_c < C_h) | — | Dimensionless |
| 9 | Temperature Effectiveness (P) | $P = \dfrac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}}$ | — | Cold-side |
| 10 | Effectiveness | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \dfrac{C_c(T_{c,out}-T_{c,in})}{C_{min}(T_{h,in}-T_{c,in})}$ | — | 0 to 1 |
| 11 | NTU | $\text{NTU} = \dfrac{U A}{C_{min}}$ | — | Number of transfer units |
| 12 | ε–NTU Relation (Counter) | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-\text{NTU}(1-R)]}{1 - R \exp[-\text{NTU}(1-R)]}$ | — | For counter-current |
| 13 | ε–NTU Relation (Parallel) | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-\text{NTU}(1+R)]}{1 + R}$ | — | For co-current |
| 14 | Correction Factor Definition | $F = \dfrac{\Delta T_{m}}{\Delta T_{lm,CF}}$ | — | F ≤ 1 |
| 15 | F for 1-2 (1 shell, 2 tube passes) | $F = \dfrac{\sqrt{R^2+1}\;\ln\!\big[\tfrac{1-P}{1-PR}\big]}{(R-1)\ln\!\big[\tfrac{2-P(R+1-\sqrt{R^2+1})}{2-P(R+1+\sqrt{R^2+1})}\big]}$ | — | TEMA standard |

> [!TIP]
> In the table above, **note that F depends only on R and P**, not on the actual temperatures directly. This is why F-charts (Baker plots) are used in design.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Crude oil pre-heaters** in refineries → counter-current to maximise heat recovery.
- **Milk pasteurisers** → co-current to avoid overheating the product above a critical temperature.
- **Power plant condensers** → counter-current shell-and-tube.
- **Automotive radiators** → cross-flow.
- **LNG vaporisers** → multi-pass with correction factor.
- **Pharmaceutical reactors** → co-current for tight temperature control.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of LMTD for Co-current Flow

### Step 1: Energy Balance on a Differential Element

Consider a small element of heat exchanger of length dx and total area dA.

Let the local temperatures of the hot and cold fluids be $T_h$ and $T_c$ respectively.

The differential heat transferred across dA is:

$$dQ = U \, (T_h - T_c) \, dA$$

The heat lost by the hot fluid (C_h, constant) over dx:

$$dQ = -C_h \, dT_h$$

The heat gained by the cold fluid (C_c, constant) over dx:

$$dQ = C_c \, dT_c$$

### Step 2: Differential Equation for ΔT

Let $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$. Then:

$$d(\Delta T) = dT_h - dT_c = -\dfrac{dQ}{C_h} - \dfrac{dQ}{C_c} = -dQ \left( \dfrac{1}{C_h} + \dfrac{1}{C_c} \right)$$

Substituting $dQ = U \, \Delta T \, dA$:

$$d(\Delta T) = -U \, \Delta T \, dA \left( \dfrac{1}{C_h} + \dfrac{1}{C_c} \right)$$

Define $\beta = U \left( \dfrac{1}{C_h} + \dfrac{1}{C_c} \right)$, a constant (for constant U, C_h, C_c).

Then:

$$\dfrac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -\beta \, dA$$

### Step 3: Integration Over Total Area A

Integrating from inlet (ΔT₁ at A = 0) to outlet (ΔT₂ at A = A):

$$\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \dfrac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -\beta \int_0^A dA$$

$$\ln\!\left( \dfrac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1} \right) = -\beta A = -U A \left( \dfrac{1}{C_h} + \dfrac{1}{C_c} \right)$$

### Step 4: Express βA in Terms of ΔT₁ and ΔT₂

Total heat transferred:

$$Q = C_h (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = C_c (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$$

For co-current flow:

$$T_{h,in} - T_{h,out} = \Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2$$
$$T_{c,out} - T_{c,in} = \Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2$$

So $Q = C_h (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2) = C_c (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)$.

Hence:

$$\dfrac{1}{C_h} + \dfrac{1}{C_c} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{Q}\left( \dfrac{1}{C_h \cdot \text{...}} \right)$$

More cleanly:

$$\beta A = U A \left( \dfrac{1}{C_h} + \dfrac{1}{C_c} \right) = \dfrac{U A (C_h + C_c)}{C_h C_c}$$

But $Q = C_h (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2) = C_c (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)$ → implies $C_h = C_c$ in this special formulation, or more correctly:

$$\beta A = \dfrac{U A}{Q} (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)$$

Substituting back:

$$\ln\!\left( \dfrac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1} \right) = -\dfrac{U A (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)}{Q}$$

Solving for $Q$:

$$Q = U A \cdot \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$

Defining LMTD:

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$

We obtain the **LMTD equation**:

$$Q = U \, A \, \Delta T_{lm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is valid for **both co-current and counter-current** flow, provided ΔT₁ and ΔT₂ are defined consistently with the orientation (i.e., ΔT₁ is the temperature difference at one end and ΔT₂ at the other end, following the fluid flow direction).

## 3.2 Derivation of Effectiveness for Counter-Current Heat Exchanger (Constant C_h, C_c)

### Step 1: Use ε–NTU Formulation

Define the maximum possible heat transfer (using $C_{min}$):

$$Q_{max} = C_{min} (T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})$$

### Step 2: Express Q in Terms of ε

If $C_c = C_{min}$:

$$Q = \varepsilon \, C_{min} (T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}) = C_c (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$$

### Step 3: Derive the ε-NTU Relation

Starting from the counter-current integration result:

$$\dfrac{T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}} = \exp\!\left[ -\text{NTU} \, (1 - R) \right]$$

where $R = C_{min} / C_{max}$.

Define $P = \dfrac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}} = \varepsilon$ (if $C_c = C_{min}$).

Through algebraic manipulation (let $T_{c,in} = 0$ for simplicity):

- $T_{h,in} - T_{h,out} = R(T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$ from energy balance (if $C_h = C_{max}$).
- $T_{c,out} = P \cdot T_{h,in}$
- $T_{h,out} = T_{h,in} - R P T_{h,in} = T_{h,in}(1 - R P)$

Substituting:

$$\dfrac{T_{h,in}(1-RP) - 0}{T_{h,in} - T_{h,in} P} = \exp[-\text{NTU}(1-R)]$$

$$\dfrac{1 - RP}{1 - P} = \exp[-\text{NTU}(1-R)]$$

Let $E = \exp[-\text{NTU}(1-R)]$:

$$1 - RP = E(1 - P)$$
$$1 - RP = E - EP$$
$$P(EP - R) = E - 1$$
$$P = \dfrac{1 - E}{R - E} = \dfrac{1 - E}{R - E} \cdot \dfrac{-1}{-1} = \dfrac{E - 1}{E - R}$$

Since $E = \exp[-\text{NTU}(1-R)]$ and $P = \varepsilon$:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-\text{NTU}(1-R)]}{1 - R \exp[-\text{NTU}(1-R)]}}$$

This is the **ε-NTU equation for counter-current flow**.

> [!TIP]
> The ε-NTU method is more convenient than LMTD when outlet temperatures are not known. KTU 2024 numerical problems often specify UA and inlet temperatures, and ask to compute outlet temperatures — use ε-NTU.

## 3.3 Analytic Comparison Matrix for Flow Arrangements

| Property | 1-1 Co-current | 1-1 Counter | 1-2 (1 shell, 2 tube) | Cross-flow |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| F value | 1 (use ΔT_lm directly) | 1 (use ΔT_lm, counter) | < 1 (use F-chart) | < 1 (use F-chart) |
| Efficiency | Lowest | Highest | Intermediate | Intermediate |
| Counter-flow can be applied? | No (true counter only in 1-1 counter) | Yes | Approximate | No |
| T_c,out can exceed T_h,out? | No | Yes | Yes (sometimes) | Yes |
| Minimum approach ΔT | At outlet | Anywhere | At crossover | Variable |
| Practical use | Pasteurisation, viscous fluids | Process heaters, coolers | Most common industrial | HVAC, radiators |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram of Heat Exchanger Flow Arrangements

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["HEAT EXCHANGER FLOW ARRANGEMENTS"] --> B["SINGLE PASS - 1 by 1"]
    A --> C["MULTI PASS - 1 by 2 / 2 by 4 / etc"]
    A --> D["CROSS FLOW"]

    B --> B1["CO CURRENT PARALLEL FLOW"]
    B --> B2["COUNTER CURRENT FLOW"]

    B1 --> B1A["Hot inlet and Cold inlet at SAME end"]
    B1 --> B1B["Both streams flow in SAME direction"]
    B1 --> B1C["Temperature curves approach but never cross"]
    B1 --> B1D["LMTD applied directly F equals 1"]

    B2 --> B2A["Hot inlet and Cold inlet at OPPOSITE ends"]
    B2 --> B2B["Streams flow in OPPOSITE directions"]
    B2 --> B2C["Nearly constant temperature difference"]
    B2 --> B2D["Highest heat transfer efficiency"]

    C --> C1["ONE SHELL PASS MULTI TUBE PASS"]
    C --> C2["MULTI SHELL MULTI TUBE PASS"]

    C1 --> C1A["Example 1 by 2 arrangement"]
    C1 --> C1B["Tube fluid reverses direction once"]
    C1 --> C1C["Correction factor F less than 1"]
    C1 --> C1D["Use F chart with R and P parameters"]

    D --> D1["Streams flow PERPENDICULAR to each other"]
    D --> D2["Both unmixed or both mixed or one mixed"]
    D --> D3["F obtained from cross flow F chart"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram of LMTD Calculation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["START Identify Flow Arrangement"] --> S2["Is it single pass 1 by 1"]
    S2 -- YES CO CURRENT --> S3a["Delta T1 at Th,in minus Tc,in"]
    S2 -- YES COUNTER CURRENT --> S3b["Delta T1 at Th,in minus Tc,out"]
    S2 -- NO MULTI PASS --> S3c["Calculate Counter LMTD using COUNTER end definitions"]
    S3c --> S3d["Find R = Cmin over Cmax"]
    S3d --> S3e["Find P from outlet temperatures"]
    S3e --> S3f["Look up F from F chart or formula"]
    S3f --> S3g["Q equals U times A times F times Delta T lm CF"]

    S3a --> S4["Calculate LMTD"]
    S3b --> S4
    S3g --> S5["Compute Q equals U times A times LMTD times F"]
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6["END Report Heat Duty Q"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Representation of Temperature Profiles

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph P1["PROFILE 1: CO-CURRENT FLOW"]
        D1A["Th,in: 150 C"] --> D1B["Linear decrease"]
        D1C["Tc,in: 30 C"] --> D1D["Linear increase"]
        D1B --> D1E["Th,out: 80 C"]
        D1D --> D1F["Tc,out: 70 C"]
        D1E -.Gap closes.- D1F
    end

    subgraph P2["PROFILE 2: COUNTER-CURRENT FLOW"]
        D2A["Th,in: 150 C LEFT"] --> D2B["Linear decrease"]
        D2C["Tc,out: 100 C LEFT"] --> D2D["Slight decrease right to left"]
        D2B --> D2E["Th,out: 70 C RIGHT"]
        D2D --> D2F["Tc,in: 30 C RIGHT"]
    end

    subgraph P3["PROFILE 3: ONE SHELL TWO TUBE PASSES 1-2"]
        D3A["Hot: 150 C in, 75 C out"] --> D3B["Monotonic decrease"]
        D3C["Cold Pass 1: 30 C in to 90 C"] --> D3D["Turn around"]
        D3D --> D3E["Cold Pass 2: 90 C to 110 C out"]
    end
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture of a 1-2 Heat Exchanger (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SHELL["SHELL SIDE HOT FLUID PATH"]
        H1["Hot Inlet T_h,in"] --> H2["Traverse length L of shell"]
        H2 --> H3["Hot Outlet T_h,out"]
    end

    subgraph TUBE1["TUBE PASS 1 COLD FLUID"]
        C1["Cold Inlet T_c,in"] --> C2["Traverse tube 1 of length L"]
        C2 --> C3["Return Header"]
    end

    subgraph TUBE2["TUBE PASS 2 COLD FLUID"]
        C3 --> C4["Traverse tube 2 of length L"]
        C4 --> C5["Cold Outlet T_c,out"]
    end

    H2 -. Heat transfer through tube walls .- C2
    H2 -. Heat transfer through tube walls .- C4
```
