# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**With the help of a neat sketch, explain the temperature–distance plot for a co-current (parallel) flow heat exchanger. State any two advantages and disadvantages.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

A co-current (parallel) flow heat exchanger is one in which both the hot and cold fluids enter at the **same end**, flow in the **same direction**, and exit at the **other end** together.

**Temperature profile description (2 marks):**
- The hot fluid temperature **decreases** from $T_{h,in}$ to $T_{h,out}$ as it gives up heat.
- The cold fluid temperature **increases** from $T_{c,in}$ to $T_{c,out}$ as it gains heat.
- The two temperature curves **approach each other asymptotically** but **never cross**, since the hot fluid remains hotter than the cold fluid throughout.
- The temperature difference is **largest at the inlet** ($\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}$) and **smallest at the outlet** ($\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,out}$).

**Two key points (1 mark):**
- *Advantage:* Simple construction; cold fluid outlet temperature cannot exceed hot fluid outlet (safe for heat-sensitive products).
- *Disadvantage:* Lowest heat transfer rate for given area, since the temperature driving force diminishes rapidly along the length.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Define Correction Factor (F) for a multi-pass heat exchanger. Why is its value always less than 1?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

**Definition (2 marks):**
The Correction Factor **F** is defined as the ratio of the **true mean temperature difference** for a multi-pass heat exchanger to the **log-mean temperature difference (LMTD)** calculated as if the exchanger were operating in pure counter-current flow:

$$F = \dfrac{\Delta T_m}{\Delta T_{lm,CF}}$$

where $\Delta T_{lm,CF}$ is the LMTD computed using the counter-current end definitions of $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$.

**Why F < 1 (1 mark):**
In a multi-pass exchanger, the cold fluid changes direction, causing a **reduction in the effective driving force** at the points where the fluids change direction (crossover points). Since the true mean temperature difference can never exceed the ideal counter-current LMTD, the ratio $F \le 1$ always.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, each Part B question carries 14 marks and carries an **internal choice**. Students must attempt **ONE** of the two alternatives.

---

### Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**In a 1-2 shell-and-tube heat exchanger, oil is cooled from 150 °C to 80 °C by water entering at 30 °C and leaving at 50 °C. The overall heat transfer coefficient is 500 W/m²·K. The heat exchanger has 200 tubes, each of 0.02 m inner diameter and 3 m long. Calculate:**

**(a)** The heat duty (Q) and the LMTD for counter-current flow. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The correction factor F (using the formula below) and the total heat transfer area required. **(7 marks)**

$$F = \dfrac{\sqrt{R^2+1}\;\ln\!\left[\dfrac{1-P}{1-PR}\right]}{(R-1)\ln\!\left[\dfrac{2-P(R+1-\sqrt{R^2+1})}{2-P(R+1+\sqrt{R^2+1})}\right]}$$

#### Model Solution:

**Given:**
- $T_{h,in} = 150°\text{C}$, $T_{h,out} = 80°\text{C}$
- $T_{c,in} = 30°\text{C}$, $T_{c,out} = 50°\text{C}$
- $U = 500 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$
- $N = 200$ tubes, $d = 0.02 \text{ m}$, $L = 3 \text{ m}$

**Part (a) — Heat Duty and LMTD (7 marks)**

Step 1: For a 1-2 arrangement, use the **counter-current end definition** for LMTD:
- $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 150 - 50 = 100°\text{C}$
- $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 80 - 30 = 50°\text{C}$

Step 2: Apply LMTD formula:

$$\Delta T_{lm,CF} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)} = \dfrac{100 - 50}{\ln(100/50)} = \dfrac{50}{0.6931}$$

$$\Delta T_{lm,CF} = 72.13 °\text{C}$$

Step 3: Heat duty from energy balance (we will use a basis; here we use the available area method).

Total inner tube area (each pass has all 200 tubes; the heat transfer length is the tube length inside the shell):

$$A_{calc} = N \pi d L = 200 \times \pi \times 0.02 \times 3 = 37.70 \text{ m}^2$$

Step 4: Calculate Q from $Q = U A_{actual} \Delta T_{lm,CF}$ — but this is the actual Q only after applying F. The Q must first be obtained from the given outlet temperatures assuming the mass flow rates are consistent. KTU standard approach: use the formula $Q = U A \Delta T_{lm,CF} F$. Since we don't have a basis mass flow rate given, we calculate Q from a working basis:

For a 1-2 exchanger, we can use the given area to find Q as:

$$Q = U A F \Delta T_{lm,CF}$$

We need to first find F, but to find F we need R and P, which need $C_{min}$ and $C_{max}$ — these need mass flow rates.

**Note to KTU students:** In typical board exam questions, the problem provides enough info to compute $C_h$ and $C_c$ or provides a working fluid with given mass flow rate. The standard KTU answer for Q in such a 1-2 problem with given geometry proceeds by:

Let $C_h = C_{min}$ and $C_c = C_{max}$ — assume oil is hot.

If not specified, compute using $C_h / C_c$ ratio from outlet temperatures. With $T_{h,in} - T_{h,out} = 70$ and $T_{c,out} - T_{c,in} = 20$, by energy balance:

$$C_h (70) = C_c (20) \implies C_h / C_c = 20/70 = 2/7$$

So $R = C_{min}/C_{max} = C_c/C_h = 7/2 \cdot ?$ — wait, since $C_h > C_c$, $C_{min} = C_c$, $C_{max} = C_h$.

$R = C_c / C_h = 20/70 = 0.2857$

$P = \dfrac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}} = \dfrac{50 - 30}{150 - 30} = \dfrac{20}{120} = 0.1667$

Now apply the F formula with $R = 0.2857$ and $P = 0.1667$:

$R^2 + 1 = 0.0816 + 1 = 1.0816$
$\sqrt{R^2+1} = 1.04$

$1 - P = 0.8333$
$1 - PR = 1 - (0.1667 \times 0.2857) = 1 - 0.0476 = 0.9524$
$\ln(0.8333/0.9524) = \ln(0.875) = -0.1335$

Numerator: $1.04 \times (-0.1335) = -0.1388$

$R - 1 = -0.7143$
$R + 1 = 1.2857$
$R + 1 - \sqrt{R^2+1} = 1.2857 - 1.04 = 0.2457$
$R + 1 + \sqrt{R^2+1} = 1.2857 + 1.04 = 2.3257$

$2 - P(R+1-\sqrt{R^2+1}) = 2 - 0.1667 \times 0.2457 = 2 - 0.0410 = 1.9590$
$2 - P(R+1+\sqrt{R^2+1}) = 2 - 0.1667 \times 2.3257 = 2 - 0.3877 = 1.6123$

Ratio inside second ln: $1.9590 / 1.6123 = 1.2150$
$\ln(1.2150) = 0.1948$

Denominator: $(R-1) \times 0.1948 = -0.7143 \times 0.1948 = -0.1392$

$F = -0.1388 / -0.1392 = 0.9971$

Step 5: Calculate the area using $Q = U A F \Delta T_{lm,CF}$:

$$A = \dfrac{Q}{U F \Delta T_{lm,CF}}$$

But we still need Q. Using the actual area given (37.70 m²):

$$Q = 500 \times 37.70 \times 0.9971 \times 72.13 = 1{,}355{,}752 \text{ W} \approx 1356 \text{ kW}$$

**Part (b) — Correction Factor and Total Area (7 marks)**

Correction Factor: $F = 0.997$ (computed above).

The total area from the geometry is $A = 37.70 \text{ m}^2$.

This matches the area required, so the design is **adequate**.

**Heat Duty: Q ≈ 1356 kW**, **LMTD = 72.13 °C**, **F = 0.997**, **A_required ≈ 37.70 m²**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes (Examiner's Pitfall):**
> 1. Using **co-current** end definitions (T_h,in − T_c,in and T_h,out − T_c,out) for a 1-2 exchanger. **Always** use the counter-current end definitions for multi-pass LMTD.
> 2. Confusing R and 1/R. $R = C_{min} / C_{max}$, never inverted.
> 3. Forgetting to apply the correction factor F to the LMTD in multi-pass exchangers.
> 4. Using degrees Celsius in $\ln$ — this is fine for **temperature differences** (which are interval scale), but **never** apply $\ln$ to a temperature ratio in K vs °C mixed.

---

### Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** With the help of temperature–distance plots, compare the performance of single-pass co-current and counter-current flow heat exchangers. Discuss the relative advantages and disadvantages of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A counter-current double-pipe heat exchanger is used to cool 0.5 kg/s of oil ($c_p = 2.1$ kJ/kg·K) from 100 °C to 60 °C using water entering at 25 °C with a flow rate of 0.3 kg/s and $c_p = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K. The overall heat transfer coefficient is 800 W/m²·K. Calculate the LMTD, the heat transfer area, and the effectiveness. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Comparison (7 marks)**

| Feature | Co-current | Counter-current |
|---|---|---|
| Inlet position of fluids | Same end | Opposite ends |
| Direction of flow | Same | Opposite |
| ΔT variation | From large to small | Approximately constant |
| LMTD | Smaller | Larger |
| T_c,out can exceed T_h,out? | No | Yes |
| Efficiency | Lower | Higher |
| Use case | Heat-sensitive products | Maximum heat recovery |

**Co-current Advantages (1 mark):**
- The cold fluid outlet temperature is **self-limiting** — it can never exceed the hot fluid outlet temperature. This is critical for products that degrade above a certain temperature (e.g., milk, pharmaceuticals, heat-sensitive polymers).
- Initial temperature difference is large, ensuring rapid initial heat transfer.

**Co-current Disadvantages (1 mark):**
- The temperature driving force diminishes rapidly; the heat exchanger becomes long and inefficient.
- Cannot achieve the cold outlet temperature higher than the hot outlet temperature.

**Counter-current Advantages (2 marks):**
- The temperature driving force remains **nearly uniform** along the length (for equal C_h and C_c).
- The cold fluid can be heated to a temperature **higher than the cold-side hot fluid outlet** in special cases.
- The most **thermodynamically efficient** arrangement.

**Counter-current Disadvantages (1 mark):**
- Inlet and outlet are at opposite ends, complicating piping.
- If the cold fluid is more viscous at low temperature, the inlet end (where the cold fluid is coldest) may experience laminar flow, reducing U.

**Part (b) — Counter-current Heat Exchanger Calculations (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $\dot{m}_h = 0.5 \text{ kg/s}$, $c_{p,h} = 2.1 \text{ kJ/kg}\cdot\text{K}$, $T_{h,in} = 100°\text{C}$, $T_{h,out} = 60°\text{C}$
- $\dot{m}_c = 0.3 \text{ kg/s}$, $c_{p,c} = 4.18 \text{ kJ/kg}\cdot\text{K}$, $T_{c,in} = 25°\text{C}$
- $U = 800 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$

Step 1: Heat duty from energy balance on hot fluid:

$$Q = \dot{m}_h c_{p,h} (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = 0.5 \times 2100 \times (100 - 60)$$

$$Q = 0.5 \times 2100 \times 40 = 42{,}000 \text{ W} = 42 \text{ kW}$$

Step 2: Outlet temperature of cold fluid (water):

$$T_{c,out} = T_{c,in} + \dfrac{Q}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}} = 25 + \dfrac{42{,}000}{0.3 \times 4180}$$

$$T_{c,out} = 25 + \dfrac{42{,}000}{1254} = 25 + 33.49 = 58.49°\text{C}$$

Step 3: LMTD (counter-current end definitions):

- $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 100 - 58.49 = 41.51°\text{C}$
- $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 60 - 25 = 35°\text{C}$

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{41.51 - 35}{\ln(41.51/35)} = \dfrac{6.51}{\ln(1.186)} = \dfrac{6.51}{0.1707} = 38.13°\text{C}$$

Step 4: Heat transfer area:

$$A = \dfrac{Q}{U \Delta T_{lm}} = \dfrac{42{,}000}{800 \times 38.13} = \dfrac{42{,}000}{30{,}504}$$

$$A = 1.377 \text{ m}^2$$

Step 5: Effectiveness calculation:

- $C_h = \dot{m}_h c_{p,h} = 0.5 \times 2100 = 1050 \text{ W/K}$
- $C_c = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c} = 0.3 \times 4180 = 1254 \text{ W/K}$
- $C_{min} = C_h = 1050 \text{ W/K}$
- $C_{max} = C_c = 1254 \text{ W/K}$
- $Q_{max} = C_{min}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}) = 1050 \times (100 - 25) = 78{,}750 \text{ W}$

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{Q}{Q_{max}} = \dfrac{42{,}000}{78{,}750} = 0.5333 \text{ or } 53.33\%$$

**Final Answers:**
- **LMTD = 38.13 °C**
- **Area A = 1.38 m²**
- **Effectiveness ε = 0.533 (or 53.33%)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse** the hot and cold side: in this problem the oil (hot) is being **cooled** and water (cold) is being **heated**. The water outlet temperature must be **lower** than oil inlet — check: $58.49 < 100$ ✓.
> 2. Many students write $Q_{max} = C_{min}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})$ with the wrong sign or use $C_{max}$ instead of $C_{min}$. **Always** use $C_{min}$.
> 3. LMTD units must be in **K or °C intervals** — but the **ratio $\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2$** must be unitless, so both values must be in the same units.
> 4. For counter-current flow, **never** compute the LMTD using co-current end definitions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 4 – Flow Arrangements & T-x Plots)**

- [ ] **1-1 Co-current**: Fluids enter at the same end; ΔT₁ = T_h,in − T_c,in; ΔT₂ = T_h,out − T_c,out. T-x curves approach but never cross. LMTD applied directly (F = 1).

- [ ] **1-1 Counter-current**: Fluids enter at opposite ends; ΔT₁ = T_h,in − T_c,out; ΔT₂ = T_h,out − T_c,in. Highest efficiency; cold outlet can exceed hot outlet. F = 1.

- [ ] **Multi-pass (1-2, 2-4, etc.)**: Use counter-current end definitions for LMTD, then apply the correction factor F. F depends only on R (= C_min/C_max) and P (cold-side temperature effectiveness).

- [ ] **Cross-flow**: Perpendicular streams; F obtained from standard F-chart. Most common in air-cooled exchangers.

- [ ] **LMTD equation**: $Q = U A F \Delta T_{lm}$ (always multiply by F in multi-pass).

- [ ] **ε–NTU method**: Use when outlet temperatures are unknown. Key equations:
  - Counter: $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - e^{-\text{NTU}(1-R)}}{1 - R e^{-\text{NTU}(1-R)}}$
  - Parallel: $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - e^{-\text{NTU}(1+R)}}{1 + R}$

- [ ] **R and P definitions** (must memorise):
  - $R = C_{min} / C_{max}$ (always ≤ 1)
  - $P = (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}) / (T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})$

- [ ] **Effectiveness range**: $0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$. For a given NTU, counter-current always gives higher ε than co-current.

- [ ] **TEMA classifications**: 1-1, 1-2, 1-4, 2-4, 1-2-2, etc. — number of tube passes is always a multiple of the number of shell passes for the standard TEMA types.

- [ ] **Hot fluid always on the shell side** for shell-and-tube (most common), and **cold fluid on the tube side** for ease of cleaning.

- [ ] **The temperature–distance plot slope** is proportional to the local U and inversely proportional to the local heat capacity rate: $dT/dx \propto -U(T_h - T_c)/C$.

- [ ] **Falling-film and plate exchangers** behave like counter-current; multiple plate passes are denoted as **N1-N2** similar to shell-and-tube.

- [ ] **Common KTU mistakes**: (i) using co-current ΔT definitions for counter; (ii) using degrees Rankine or Fahrenheit in LMTD without conversion; (iii) omitting F in multi-pass problems; (iv) confusing inlet and outlet ends in P.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: NTU Method of Heat Exchanger Calculation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** NTU Method of Heat Exchanger Calculation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Number of Transfer Units (NTU) Method** is an alternative approach to the **Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)** method for analyzing and rating heat exchangers. It is particularly powerful when the **outlet temperatures of the fluid streams are unknown** (i.e., during the design/sizing of a heat exchanger), making the LMTD method unusable because both inlet and outlet temperatures are required to compute ΔT.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> The NTU method expresses the heat transfer performance of a heat exchanger in terms of three dimensionless groups: **(1) NTU (Number of Transfer Units)**, **(2) Effectiveness (ε)**, and **(3) Heat Capacity Ratio (C\*)**. The functional relationship is given by:
> $$\varepsilon = f(\text{NTU},\ C^{*},\text{Flow Configuration})$$

The **Number of Transfer Units (NTU)** is formally defined as a dimensionless measure of the heat transfer capability of a heat exchanger, given by:

$$\text{NTU} = \frac{UA}{C_{\min}}$$

where:
- $U$ = Overall heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $A$ = Heat transfer area (m²)
- $C_{\min}$ = Smaller of the two fluid heat capacity rates (W/K)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from LMTD Method:**
> - **LMTD Method** is used when inlet AND outlet temperatures are known → used for **RATING** (performance check).
> - **NTU Method** is used when only inlet temperatures and outlet temperature of ONE stream are known (or area is to be designed) → used for **SIZING** (designing).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two water tanks connected by a pipe with a controllable valve:

- **Tank A (hot water)** wants to give heat to **Tank B (cold water)**.
- The **valve opening** represents the heat transfer area $A$. A bigger valve = more area = more heat transfer.
- The **pump speed** of the water through the valve represents the flow rate. A faster pump = more fluid rushing through = heat capacity rate $C$.
- **NTU** tells you *how big the "valve" is relative to the "smallest pipe size" (heat capacity rate)*. A high NTU means a generous area, allowing nearly complete thermal equilibration.
- **Effectiveness (ε)** tells you *how close the cold stream gets to the hot stream's inlet temperature*. Did the cold water get warmed up fully (ε → 1) or barely (ε → 0)?

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Effectiveness (ε):** Dimensionless, ranges from **0 to 1** (0% to 100% approach).
- **NTU:** Dimensionless, ranges from **0 to ∞**.
- **Heat Capacity Rate Ratio (C\*):** Always between **0 and 1** (since $C_{\min} \leq C_{\max}$).
- **Typical Industrial Range:** Most industrial heat exchangers operate between **ε = 0.5 to 0.85**, corresponding to **NTU = 1 to 4**.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Rule of Thumb:** Higher NTU ⇒ Higher Effectiveness. The relationship is **monotonically increasing** but **non-linear**, and its shape depends on the flow arrangement.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Building Blocks

### 2.1.1 Heat Capacity Rate ($C$)

The heat capacity rate of a fluid stream is the product of its mass flow rate and specific heat:

$$C = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \quad \text{[W/K]}$$

For sensible heating/cooling (no phase change), $c_p$ is taken at the mean film temperature.

### 2.1.2 Heat Capacity Rate Ratio ($C^*$)

$$C^{*} = \frac{C_{\min}}{C_{\max}} \quad ; \quad 0 \leq C^{*} \leq 1$$

### 2.1.3 Effectiveness ($\varepsilon$) — The Heart of NTU Method

The effectiveness is defined as the ratio of **actual heat transfer** to the **maximum possible heat transfer**:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\max}}$$

The **maximum possible heat transfer** occurs if the stream with minimum heat capacity rate undergoes the maximum possible temperature change — that is, it exits at the inlet temperature of the other stream. Therefore:

$$Q_{\max} = C_{\min} \cdot (T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})$$

Hence:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{C_{\min} \cdot (T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})}$$

## 2.2 Why NTU is Dimensionless but Powerful

NTU can be intuitively understood as the **"size" of the heat exchanger** normalized by the smallest thermal throughput. It is the single most important design parameter in the NTU method because it groups together the three key physical design variables: $U$, $A$, and the operating flow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operational Logic Steps of NTU Method:**
> 1. Identify the two streams (hot and cold). Compute $C_h$ and $C_c$.
> 2. Determine $C_{\min}$, $C_{\max}$, and $C^{*}$.
> 3. Determine the **flow configuration** (parallel, counter, shell-and-tube, cross flow).
> 4. Use the appropriate ε-NTU relationship to find **ε**.
> 5. Compute $Q = \varepsilon \cdot C_{\min} \cdot (T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})$.
> 6. Calculate outlet temperatures: $T_{h,o} = T_{h,i} - \dfrac{Q}{C_h}$ and $T_{c,o} = T_{c,i} + \dfrac{Q}{C_c}$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units / Range** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Heat Capacity Rate | $C = \dot{m} \cdot c_p$ | W/K |
| 2 | Heat Capacity Ratio | $C^{*} = C_{\min}/C_{\max}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \leq C^{*} \leq 1$ |
| 3 | Number of Transfer Units | $\text{NTU} = UA/C_{\min}$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Maximum Possible Heat | $Q_{\max} = C_{\min}(T_{h,i}-T_{c,i})$ | W |
| 5 | Actual Heat Transfer | $Q = \varepsilon \cdot C_{\min}(T_{h,i}-T_{c,i})$ | W |
| 6 | Effectiveness | $\varepsilon = Q / Q_{\max}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \leq \varepsilon \leq 1$ |
| 7 | Hot Outlet Temp | $T_{h,o} = T_{h,i} - Q/C_h$ | K or °C |
| 8 | Cold Outlet Temp | $T_{c,o} = T_{c,i} + Q/C_c$ | K or °C |
| 9 | ε for Parallel Flow | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-\text{NTU}(1+C^{*})]}{1+C^{*}}$ | Always $< 0.5$ |
| 10 | ε for Counter Flow | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-\text{NTU}(1-C^{*})]}{1 - C^{*}\exp[-\text{NTU}(1-C^{*})]}$ | Can approach 1 |
| 11 | ε for Shell & Tube (1 shell pass, 2 tube passes) | $\varepsilon = 2 \left\{ 1+C^{*} + \sqrt{1+C^{*2}} \cdot \dfrac{1+\exp[-\text{NTU}(1+C^{*2})^{0.5}]}{1-\exp[-\text{NTU}(1+C^{*2})^{0.5}]} \right\}^{-1}$ | Corrected with $F$-factor too |
| 12 | ε for Condenser / Evaporator ($C^{*} = 0$) | $\varepsilon = 1 - \exp(-\text{NTU})$ | — |
| 13 | LMTD Relation | $\text{NTU} = \dfrac{UA}{C_{\min}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1+C^{*2}}}\ln\left[\dfrac{2-\varepsilon(C^{*}+1-\sqrt{1+C^{*2}})}{2-\varepsilon(C^{*}+1+\sqrt{1+C^{*2}})}\right]$ | Connects both methods |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** In KTU board exams, students often confuse $C_{\min}$ and $C_{\max}$ in the formula. Always identify the stream with the **smaller product of $\dot{m} \cdot c_p$** as $C_{\min}$. This stream experiences the **largest temperature change**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The NTU method is the **backbone of commercial heat exchanger design software** (HTRI, Aspen Exchanger Design, COMSOL). It is the standard approach used by:
- **Petrochemical industries** for designing crude oil pre-heaters.
- **HVAC engineers** for sizing chilled water coils.
- **Power plant engineers** for designing condensers and feedwater heaters.
- **Food and pharmaceutical industries** for pasteurizers, where phase-change heat transfer is common.

The method is favored because heat exchanger **sizing** (finding $A$) and **performance rating** (finding $\varepsilon$ or $Q$) reduce to a single, configuration-specific chart or equation.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Effectiveness for a Parallel-Flow Heat Exchanger

Consider a parallel-flow (co-current) heat exchanger where both fluids flow in the same direction. Let $T_h(x)$ and $T_c(x)$ be the local temperatures at position $x$ along the exchanger.

### Step 1: Energy balance on the hot stream

Heat lost by hot fluid equals heat gained by cold fluid:

$$\dot{m}_h c_{p,h} \cdot dT_h = -\dot{m}_c c_{p,c} \cdot dT_c = -dQ$$

Equivalently: $C_h \cdot dT_h = -C_c \cdot dT_c$ along the length.

### Step 2: Differential heat transfer equation

By Newton's law of cooling across a differential area $dA$:

$$dQ = U \cdot (T_h - T_c) \cdot dA$$

But $dQ = C_h \cdot dT_h$, so:

$$C_h \cdot dT_h = U \cdot (T_h - T_c) \cdot dA$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{dT_h}{T_h - T_c} = \frac{U \cdot dA}{C_h}$$

### Step 3: Introducing temperature difference $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$

Differentiating $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$:

$$d(\Delta T) = dT_h - dT_c$$

Using $C_h dT_h = -C_c dT_c$, we get $dT_c = -\dfrac{C_h}{C_c} dT_h$, hence:

$$d(\Delta T) = dT_h + \frac{C_h}{C_c} dT_h = dT_h \left( 1 + \frac{C_h}{C_c} \right) = dT_h \left( \frac{C_c + C_h}{C_c} \right)$$

So:

$$dT_h = \frac{C_c}{C_c + C_h} d(\Delta T)$$

### Step 4: Substitution into heat transfer ODE

Substituting this $dT_h$ into $C_h dT_h = U(\Delta T) dA$:

$$\frac{C_h \cdot C_c}{C_c + C_h} \cdot d(\Delta T) = U \cdot \Delta T \cdot dA$$

Let $C_{\min} = C_c$ and $C_{\max} = C_h$ (for parallel flow where $C_c < C_h$ generically), then:

$$\frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = \frac{U \cdot (C_c + C_h)}{C_h \cdot C_c} \cdot dA$$

### Step 5: Integration across the heat exchanger

Integrate from inlet ($A=0$, $\Delta T_i = T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}$) to outlet ($A=A$, $\Delta T_o = T_{h,o} - T_{c,o}$):

$$\int_{\Delta T_i}^{\Delta T_o} \frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = \frac{U}{C_{\min}} \left( 1 + C^{*} \right) \int_0^A dA$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{\Delta T_o}{\Delta T_i}\right) = -\frac{UA}{C_{\min}}(1 + C^{*}) = -\text{NTU}(1 + C^{*})$$

Wait — let me correct sign. Since $C_c + C_h$ is positive and the integration should yield a negative result (ΔT decreases), the proper form is:

$$\frac{\Delta T_o}{\Delta T_i} = \exp[-\text{NTU}(1 + C^{*})]$$

### Step 6: Converting to Effectiveness

For a single-stream effectiveness definition (the stream that undergoes the largest temperature change is the $C_{\min}$ stream — i.e., the cold stream in parallel flow), we have:

$$Q = C_{\min}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,o}) = C_{\min}(T_{h,o} - T_{c,i}) \cdot \text{(NOT equal in parallel)}$$

Actually, the correct relation uses the $C_{\min}$ stream. For parallel flow, $C_{\min}$ could be either. The general relation between $\Delta T_o/\Delta T_i$ and ε is:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{1 - \exp[-\text{NTU}(1+C^{*})]}{1 + C^{*}}$$

**Final Derived Equation for Parallel Flow:**

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{\text{parallel}} = \frac{1 - \exp[-\text{NTU}(1+C^{*})]}{1 + C^{*}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limiting Case Check:** When $C^{*} = 0$ (one fluid has infinite capacity, e.g., condensing steam), the equation reduces to $\varepsilon = 1 - e^{-\text{NTU}}$, which is the classic result for a condenser/evaporator. This is a strong validation of the derivation.

## 3.2 Derivation of Effectiveness for Counter-Flow Heat Exchanger

A similar procedure is applied, but with the streams flowing in **opposite directions**, leading to:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{\text{counter}} = \frac{1 - \exp[-\text{NTU}(1-C^{*})]}{1 - C^{*}\exp[-\text{NTU}(1-C^{*})]}}$$

### Special Case: $C^{*} = 1$ (Equal Heat Capacity Rates)

When $C_h = C_c$, the equation becomes indeterminate (0/0). Applying L'Hôpital's rule:

$$\varepsilon_{\text{counter, C^{*}=1}} = \frac{\text{NTU}}{1 + \text{NTU}}$$

This is a frequently tested special case in KTU exams.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Analytical Problem (Worked Example)

**Problem:** A counter-flow heat exchanger is used to cool oil ($\dot{m}_h = 1.0$ kg/s, $c_{p,h} = 2.1$ kJ/kg·K) from 150°C using water ($\dot{m}_c = 0.5$ kg/s, $c_{p,c} = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K) entering at 30°C. The overall heat transfer coefficient is $U = 500$ W/m²·K and the area is $A = 10$ m². Find the heat transfer rate and outlet temperatures.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Compute heat capacity rates**

$$C_h = 1.0 \times 2.1 = 2.1 \text{ kW/K} = 2100 \text{ W/K}$$

$$C_c = 0.5 \times 4.18 = 2.09 \text{ kW/K} = 2090 \text{ W/K}$$

**Step 2: Identify $C_{\min}$, $C_{\max}$, and $C^{*}$**

$$C_{\min} = C_c = 2090 \text{ W/K}, \quad C_{\max} = C_h = 2100 \text{ W/K}$$

$$C^{*} = \frac{2090}{2100} = 0.9952$$

**Step 3: Compute NTU**

$$\text{NTU} = \frac{UA}{C_{\min}} = \frac{500 \times 10}{2090} = 2.392$$

**Step 4: Compute effectiveness using counter-flow formula**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{1 - \exp[-\text{NTU}(1-C^{*})]}{1 - C^{*}\exp[-\text{NTU}(1-C^{*})]}$$

Compute $1 - C^{*} = 0.00476$:

$$\text{NTU}(1-C^{*}) = 2.392 \times 0.00476 = 0.01139$$

$$\exp(-0.01139) = 0.98867$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{1 - 0.98867}{1 - 0.9952 \times 0.98867} = \frac{0.01133}{1 - 0.98393} = \frac{0.01133}{0.01607} = 0.7050$$

**Step 5: Compute heat transfer rate**

$$Q = \varepsilon \cdot C_{\min} \cdot (T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}) = 0.7050 \times 2090 \times (150 - 30)$$

$$Q = 0.7050 \times 2090 \times 120 = 176{,}814 \text{ W} \approx 176.8 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6: Compute outlet temperatures**

$$T_{h,o} = T_{h,i} - \frac{Q}{C_h} = 150 - \frac{176{,}814}{2100} = 150 - 84.2 = 65.8 \text{ °C}$$

$$T_{c,o} = T_{c,i} + \frac{Q}{C_c} = 30 + \frac{176{,}814}{2090} = 30 + 84.6 = 114.6 \text{ °C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** In a counter-flow exchanger, the cold outlet can exceed the hot outlet. Here $T_{c,o} = 114.6°C > T_{h,o} = 65.8°C$, confirming counter-flow behavior. The cold water can never exceed the hot inlet (150°C) due to the Second Law of Thermodynamics.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: NTU Method Computational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given Inlet Temps, Flow rates, cp, U, A] --> B[Compute Heat Capacity Rates Ch and Cc]
    B --> C{Identify Cmin and Cmax}
    C --> D[Compute Cstar = Cmin / Cmax]
    D --> E[Compute NTU = UA / Cmin]
    E --> F{Identify Flow Configuration}
    F -->|Parallel| G[Apply Parallel Flow epsilon-NTU equation]
    F -->|Counter| H[Apply Counter Flow epsilon-NTU equation]
    F -->|Shell and Tube| I[Apply Shell and Tube epsilon-NTU equation]
    F -->|Cross Flow| J[Apply Cross Flow epsilon-NTU equation]
    G --> K[Compute epsilon]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Q = epsilon times Cmin times Thi minus Tci]
    L --> M[Tho = Thi minus Q over Ch]
    L --> N[Tco = Tci plus Q over Cc]
    M --> O[Output: Q, Tho, Tco]
    N --> O
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Heat Exchanger Flow Configurations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PAR[Parallel Flow Co-current]
        A1[Hot In: 150C] --> A2[Hot Out: 90C]
        B1[Cold In: 30C] --> B2[Cold Out: 75C]
        A1 --- B1
        A2 --- B2
    end
    subgraph CTR[Counter Flow]
        C1[Hot In: 150C] --> C2[Hot Out: 65C]
        D2[Cold Out: 115C] --> D1[Cold In: 30C]
        C1 --- D1
        C2 --- D2
    end
    subgraph SHL[Shell and Tube 1-2]
        E1[Hot In: Shell] --> E2[Hot Out: Shell]
        F2[Cold Out: Tube] --> F1[Cold In: Tube]
    end
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: LMTD vs NTU Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Heat Exchanger Problem] --> Q1{Known: All 4 Temperatures?}
    Q1 -->|Yes, want Q| LMTD[LMTD Method: Q = U A LMTD]
    Q1 -->|No, want to design Area| NTU[NTU Method: Find epsilon first]
    Q1 -->|No, want outlet temps| NTU
    LMTD --> OUT1[Output: Q and Area check]
    NTU --> Q2{Choose Configuration}
    Q2 --> R1[Parallel]
    Q2 --> R2[Counter]
    Q2 --> R3[Shell Tube]
    Q2 --> R4[Cross Flow]
    R1 --> EPS[Compute epsilon from epsilon-NTU]
    R2 --> EPS
    R3 --> EPS
    R4 --> EPS
    EPS --> OUT2[Output: Q, Outlet Temperatures, Area]
```

## 4.4 Effectiveness-NTU Curve (Conceptual Mermaid Representation)

```mermaid
graph LR
    NTU0[NTU = 0] -->|epsilon = 0| EPS0[Zero Effectiveness]
    NTU1[NTU = 1] -->|epsilon ~ 0.5| EPS1[Moderate Heat Transfer]
    NTU2[NTU = 2] -->|epsilon ~ 0.75| EPS2[High Heat Transfer]
    NTU3[NTU = 5] -->|epsilon ~ 0.95| EPS3[Very High Effectiveness]
    NTU4[NTU -> infinity] -->|epsilon -> 1| EPS4[Maximum Approach Infinite Area]
    EPS0 --- EPS1
    EPS1 --- EPS2
    EPS2 --- EPS3
    EPS3 --- EPS4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** As NTU increases, effectiveness approaches unity asymptotically. This is a key insight — you can never reach 100% effectiveness with finite area. Counter-flow exchangers reach the asymptote faster than parallel-flow exchangers.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define effectiveness of a heat exchanger. Why is it always less than unity?**

**Model Answer:**

Effectiveness ($\varepsilon$) of a heat exchanger is defined as the ratio of the actual heat transfer rate to the maximum possible heat transfer rate:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\max}} = \frac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{C_{\min}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})}$$

**Why always less than unity:**
1. The Second Law of Thermodynamics prevents the cold fluid outlet from exceeding the hot fluid inlet temperature.
2. Finite heat transfer area and finite $U$ prevent infinite residence time.
3. Fluid stream outlet temperatures are bounded by the inlet temperatures of the other stream.
4. Heat losses to surroundings further reduce actual heat transfer below the theoretical maximum.

*(3 marks: 1.5 marks for definition + equation, 1.5 marks for any two reasons.)*

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Differentiate between LMTD method and NTU method of heat exchanger analysis.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **LMTD Method** | **NTU Method** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Primary Use | Rating (performance check) | Sizing (design calculation) |
| Required Inputs | Inlet AND outlet temps of both fluids | Inlet temps, $U$, $A$, flow rates |
| Output | Heat transfer rate $Q$ | Effectiveness $\varepsilon$ first, then $Q$ |
| Temperature Dependence | Uses $\Delta T$ (log mean) | Uses dimensionless effectiveness |
| Best For | Operating existing exchangers | Designing new exchangers |

*(3 marks: 1 mark per key difference with example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for effectiveness of a parallel-flow heat exchanger in terms of NTU and heat capacity ratio.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A shell-and-tube heat exchanger (1 shell pass, 2 tube passes) heats 5 kg/s of water from 35°C to 80°C using a hot oil stream entering at 200°C and leaving at 100°C. The water has $c_p = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K, oil has $c_p = 2.5$ kJ/kg·K, $U = 800$ W/m²·K. Calculate:
(i) The heat transfer rate
(ii) The effectiveness
(iii) The area of the heat exchanger
(iv) The NTU

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1:** Set up the differential energy balance for parallel flow. Let $T_h$ and $T_c$ be local temperatures.

$$C_h dT_h = -C_c dT_c = U(T_h - T_c) dA$$

**Step 2:** Define $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$. Differentiating: $d(\Delta T) = dT_h - dT_c = dT_h + (C_h/C_c) dT_h = dT_h \cdot (1 + C_h/C_c)$.

**Step 3:** Substituting and integrating from inlet to outlet:

$$\frac{\Delta T_o}{\Delta T_i} = \exp\left[-\frac{UA}{C_{\min}}(1 + C^{*})\right] = \exp[-\text{NTU}(1+C^{*})]$$

**Step 4:** Using the identity $\Delta T_i - \Delta T_o = (T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})(1 - C^{*}) \cdot \varepsilon / \text{...}$ (after algebraic manipulation involving the $C_{\min}$ stream heat balance), the final result is:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = \frac{1 - \exp[-\text{NTU}(1+C^{*})]}{1 + C^{*}}}$$

**Limiting Cases:**
- $C^{*} = 0$: $\varepsilon = 1 - e^{-\text{NTU}}$ (condenser limit)
- $\text{NTU} \to 0$: $\varepsilon \to 0$ (no heat transfer)
- $\text{NTU} \to \infty$: $\varepsilon \to 1/(1+C^{*}) < 1$ (parallel flow can never exceed $1/(1+C^{*})$)

*(7 marks: 2 for energy balance, 2 for integration setup, 2 for final derivation, 1 for limiting case discussion.)*

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- Water (cold): $\dot{m}_c = 5$ kg/s, $c_{p,c} = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K, $T_{c,i} = 35°C$, $T_{c,o} = 80°C$
- Oil (hot): $T_{h,i} = 200°C$, $T_{h,o} = 100°C$, $c_{p,h} = 2.5$ kJ/kg·K
- $U = 800$ W/m²·K, Shell-and-tube (1-2)

**Step 1: Heat capacity rates**

$$C_c = 5 \times 4.18 = 20.9 \text{ kW/K} = 20900 \text{ W/K}$$

$$C_h = \frac{Q}{T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}} \text{ (need Q first)}$$

**Step 2: Compute $Q$ from water side (cold stream)**

$$Q = C_c \cdot (T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}) = 20900 \times (80 - 35) = 20900 \times 45 = 940{,}500 \text{ W}$$

$$Q = 940.5 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3: Find $C_h$**

$$C_h = \frac{Q}{T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}} = \frac{940{,}500}{200 - 100} = 9405 \text{ W/K}$$

**Step 4: Identify $C_{\min}$, $C_{\max}$, $C^{*}$**

$$C_{\min} = 9405 \text{ W/K (oil)}, \quad C_{\max} = 20900 \text{ W/K (water)}$$

$$C^{*} = \frac{9405}{20900} = 0.4500$$

**Step 5: Effectiveness**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{Q}{C_{\min}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})} = \frac{940{,}500}{9405 \times (200 - 35)} = \frac{940{,}500}{9405 \times 165}$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{940{,}500}{1{,}551{,}825} = 0.6060$$

**Step 6: NTU from shell-and-tube 1-2 formula**

$$\varepsilon = 2 \left\{ 1 + C^{*} + \sqrt{1 + C^{*2}} \cdot \frac{1 + \exp[-\text{NTU}\sqrt{1+C^{*2}}]}{1 - \exp[-\text{NTU}\sqrt{1+C^{*2}}]} \right\}^{-1}$$

With $C^{*} = 0.45$: $\sqrt{1 + 0.2025} = \sqrt{1.2025} = 1.0966$

$$0.6060 = 2 \left\{ 1.45 + 1.0966 \cdot \frac{1 + e^{-1.0966 \cdot \text{NTU}}}{1 - e^{-1.0966 \cdot \text{NTU}}} \right\}^{-1}$$

$$\left\{ 1.45 + 1.0966 \cdot \frac{1 + e^{-1.0966 \cdot \text{NTU}}}{1 - e^{-1.0966 \cdot \text{NTU}}} \right\} = \frac{2}{0.6060} = 3.3003$$

$$1.0966 \cdot \frac{1 + e^{-1.0966 \cdot \text{NTU}}}{1 - e^{-1.0966 \cdot \text{NTU}}} = 1.8503$$

Let $y = e^{-1.0966 \cdot \text{NTU}}$:

$$1.0966 \cdot \frac{1 + y}{1 - y} = 1.8503 \Rightarrow \frac{1+y}{1-y} = 1.6873$$

$$1 + y = 1.6873(1 - y) = 1.6873 - 1.6873y$$

$$y(1 + 1.6873) = 1.6873 - 1 = 0.6873$$

$$y = \frac{0.6873}{2.6873} = 0.2558$$

$$e^{-1.0966 \cdot \text{NTU}} = 0.2558$$

$$-1.0966 \cdot \text{NTU} = \ln(0.2558) = -1.3635$$

$$\text{NTU} = \frac{1.3635}{1.0966} = 1.2434$$

**Step 7: Area**

$$A = \frac{\text{NTU} \cdot C_{\min}}{U} = \frac{1.2434 \times 9405}{800} = \frac{11{,}693.5}{800} = 14.62 \text{ m}^2$$

**Final Answers:**
- $Q = 940.5$ kW
- $\varepsilon = 0.606$
- $\text{NTU} = 1.243$
- $A = 14.62$ m²

*(7 marks: 1 for Q, 1.5 for effectiveness, 3 for NTU derivation from shell-and-tube formula, 1.5 for area.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of Number of Transfer Units (NTU). Discuss the ε-NTU relationship for a counter-flow heat exchanger with a neat temperature profile sketch. State the limiting cases.

**(b) [7 Marks]** In a counter-flow double-pipe heat exchanger, oil ($\dot{m} = 2$ kg/s, $c_p = 2.0$ kJ/kg·K) is cooled from 130°C to 60°C by water entering at 25°C with a flow rate of 0.8 kg/s and $c_p = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K. The overall heat transfer coefficient is 600 W/m²·K. Calculate:
(i) The exit temperature of water
(ii) The effectiveness
(iii) The heat transfer area
(iv) The log mean temperature difference (LMTD) for verification

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Concept of NTU:** Number of Transfer Units (NTU) is a dimensionless parameter that represents the heat transfer capacity of an exchanger relative to the heat throughput of the stream with minimum heat capacity rate. It is given by:

$$\text{NTU} = \frac{UA}{C_{\min}}$$

**Physical Meaning:** It indicates the "size" or "thermal length" of the heat exchanger. A higher NTU means a larger surface area, more residence time, and hence greater approach to thermal equilibrium.

**ε-NTU Relation for Counter-Flow:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{1 - \exp[-\text{NTU}(1-C^{*})]}{1 - C^{*}\exp[-\text{NTU}(1-C^{*})]}$$

**Temperature Profile Sketch (Counter-Flow):**

```
Hot Fluid:  Thi ----Tho (decreases left to right)
              |        |
              |        |
Cold Fluid: Tco ----Tci (increases left to right, opposite direction)
```

In counter-flow, the cold outlet can exceed the hot outlet (but never the hot inlet).

**Limiting Cases:**

1. **$C^{*} = 0$ (e.g., condenser/evaporator):**
$$\varepsilon = 1 - e^{-\text{NTU}}$$

2. **$C^{*} = 1$ (equal capacity rates):** Applying L'Hôpital's rule:
$$\varepsilon = \frac{\text{NTU}}{1 + \text{NTU}}$$

3. **$\text{NTU} \to 0$:** $\varepsilon \to 0$ (no heat transfer)

4. **$\text{NTU} \to \infty$:** $\varepsilon \to 1$ (maximum possible heat transfer; infinite area)

5. **Counter-flow can achieve effectiveness up to 1**, while **parallel-flow is limited to** $1/(1+C^{*})$.

*(7 marks: 2 for NTU concept, 2 for formula and profile, 3 for limiting cases with derivations.)*

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- Oil (hot): $\dot{m}_h = 2$ kg/s, $c_{p,h} = 2.0$ kJ/kg·K, $T_{h,i} = 130°C$, $T_{h,o} = 60°C$
- Water (cold): $\dot{m}_c = 0.8$ kg/s, $c_{p,c} = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K, $T_{c,i} = 25°C$
- $U = 600$ W/m²·K, Counter-flow

**Step 1: Heat capacity rates**

$$C_h = 2 \times 2.0 = 4.0 \text{ kW/K} = 4000 \text{ W/K}$$

$$C_c = 0.8 \times 4.18 = 3.344 \text{ kW/K} = 3344 \text{ W/K}$$

**Step 2: Heat transfer rate**

$$Q = C_h (T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) = 4000 \times (130 - 60) = 4000 \times 70 = 280{,}000 \text{ W} = 280 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3: (i) Exit temperature of water**

$$Q = C_c (T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}) \Rightarrow T_{c,o} = T_{c,i} + \frac{Q}{C_c} = 25 + \frac{280{,}000}{3344} = 25 + 83.73$$

$$T_{c,o} = 108.73°C$$

**Step 4: (ii) Effectiveness**

$C_{\min} = 3344$ W/K (water), $C_{\max} = 4000$ W/K (oil)

$$C^{*} = \frac{3344}{4000} = 0.836$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{Q}{C_{\min}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})} = \frac{280{,}000}{3344 \times (130 - 25)} = \frac{280{,}000}{3344 \times 105} = \frac{280{,}000}{351{,}120} = 0.7974$$

$$\varepsilon = 0.797$$

**Step 5: (iii) Area**

Compute NTU from counter-flow ε equation:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{1 - e^{-\text{NTU}(1-C^{*})}}{1 - C^{*}e^{-\text{NTU}(1-C^{*})}}$$

With $C^{*} = 0.836$, $1 - C^{*} = 0.164$, $\varepsilon = 0.7974$.

Let $x = \text{NTU} \times 0.164$. Then:

$$0.7974 = \frac{1 - e^{-x}}{1 - 0.836 \cdot e^{-x}}$$

Let $u = e^{-x}$:

$$0.7974(1 - 0.836u) = 1 - u$$

$$0.7974 - 0.6666u = 1 - u$$

$$u(1 - 0.6666) = 1 - 0.7974$$

$$0.3334u = 0.2026 \Rightarrow u = 0.6077$$

$$e^{-x} = 0.6077 \Rightarrow x = -\ln(0.6077) = 0.4979$$

$$\text{NTU} = \frac{0.4979}{0.164} = 3.036$$

$$A = \frac{\text{NTU} \cdot C_{\min}}{U} = \frac{3.036 \times 3344}{600} = \frac{10{,}152.4}{600} = 16.92 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 6: (iv) LMTD for verification**

For counter-flow:
- $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} = 130 - 108.73 = 21.27°C$
- $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i} = 60 - 25 = 35°C$

$$\text{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = \frac{21.27 - 35}{\ln(21.27/35)} = \frac{-13.73}{\ln(0.6077)} = \frac{-13.73}{-0.4979} = 27.58°C$$

$$Q_{\text{LMTD}} = U \cdot A \cdot \text{LMTD} = 600 \times 16.92 \times 27.58 = 280{,}000 \text{ W} \checkmark$$

**Final Answers:**
- $T_{c,o} = 108.73°C$
- $\varepsilon = 0.797$
- $A = 16.92$ m²
- $\text{LMTD} = 27.58°C$ (verified)

*(7 marks: 2 for Q and outlet temp, 1.5 for effectiveness, 2 for area via NTU, 1.5 for LMTD verification.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Traps in NTU Method (KTU Board Exam Patterns):**
>
> 1. **Confusing $C_{\min}$ and $C_{\max}$:** Students often blindly use $C_h$ in the denominator of NTU. Always identify which stream has the **smaller** $C$ value. This costs **2-3 marks** typically.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the shell-and-tube correction:** For 1-2 or 2-4 shell-and-tube exchangers, the ε-NTU formula has a more complex form. Using the parallel/counter formula will give a **wrong answer (lose 4-5 marks)**.
>
> 3. **Sign errors in the LMTD verification:** When verifying using LMTD, the temperature differences $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ must be computed correctly. For counter-flow, $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o}$ and $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i}$. Reversing these is a common error.
>
> 4. **Not using the correct limiting case:** For condensers/evaporators, $C^{*} = 0$, and the formula simplifies. Applying the general formula unnecessarily is acceptable but slower.
>
> 5. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing kW/K with W/K or kJ/kg·K with J/kg·K. Always convert to a **single unit system (SI recommended)** before substitution.
>
> 6. **Sanity check missing:** Always verify that $T_{c,o} < T_{h,i}$ (Second Law). If not, your calculation is wrong.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist: NTU Method of Heat Exchanger Calculation**
>
> - **Effectiveness ($\varepsilon$):** Ratio of actual to maximum possible heat transfer. Range: **0 to 1**.
> - **NTU:** $\text{NTU} = UA/C_{\min}$. Measures the "thermal size" of the exchanger.
> - **$C^{*}$:** Always $C_{\min}/C_{\max}$, between 0 and 1.
> - **Maximum heat transfer:** $Q_{\max} = C_{\min}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})$.
> - **Three core equations to memorize:**
>   - Parallel: $\varepsilon = [1 - e^{-\text{NTU}(1+C^{*})}]/(1+C^{*})$
>   - Counter: $\varepsilon = [1 - e^{-\text{NTU}(1-C^{*})}]/[1 - C^{*}e^{-\text{NTU}(1-C^{*})}]$
>   - Condenser ($C^{*}=0$): $\varepsilon = 1 - e^{-\text{NTU}}$
> - **Counter-flow is always more effective** than parallel-flow for the same NTU and $C^{*}$.
> - **Counter-flow can achieve $\varepsilon \to 1$**; parallel-flow is **capped at $1/(1+C^{*})$**.
> - **Special case $C^{*}=1$ counter-flow:** $\varepsilon = \text{NTU}/(1+\text{NTU})$.
> - **NTU is preferred over LMTD** for design calculations (when outlet temps are unknown).
> - **LMTD is preferred over NTU** for rating existing exchangers.
> - **Sanity check:** $T_{c,o} < T_{h,i}$ always (Second Law).
> - **For shell-and-tube 1-2:** Use the complex formula involving $\sqrt{1+C^{*2}}$; do not use parallel/counter formula.
> - **Verification:** $Q = \varepsilon C_{\min} \Delta T_{\max} = U A \text{LMTD}$ for cross-checking.
> - **Typical industrial NTU range:** **1 to 4**; typical effectiveness range: **0.5 to 0.85**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Area
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Determination of Area # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Determination of Area** in Heat Transfer Operations refers to the rigorous engineering calculation of the **required heat transfer surface area (A)** of a heat exchanger (shell-and-tube, plate, double-pipe, or extended-surface type) needed to accomplish a specified thermal duty (Q) under prescribed process and utility conditions, while satisfying the governing rate equation of heat transfer. The fundamental design rate equation is: $$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{m}$$ where the symbols are standardized as per KTU 2024 Heat Transfer Operations syllabus. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Water Heater Wall"** > Imagine you are trying to heat water inside a metal bucket using a hot flame underneath. If the bucket has a **small bottom area**, only a little heat flows up — water takes long to boil. If you **increase the bottom area** (use a wider, shallower pan), heat flows faster, and water boils quicker. The bucket's bottom is your "heat transfer area." > > In industry, instead of boiling water, we cool reactor products, condense vapours, or pre-heat feed streams. The **engineer must mathematically determine the precise area** of metal surface needed so that a given thermal duty (Q) is achieved within the available temperature driving force (ΔT) and the realistic overall heat transfer coefficient (U) of the chosen geometry and materials. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Standard Metric (SI)** — heat transfer area expressed in **m² (square metres)**. - **U (Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient)** — typical industrial range **10 W/(m²·K) for gases to 10,000 W/(m²·K) for condensing steam**; conservative design values for **shell-and-tube exchangers ≈ 500–2000 W/(m²·K)**. - **Fouling Factor (R_f)** — units **m²·K/W**; typical water-side values ≈ **0.0001 to 0.0005 m²·K/W**. - **Design Margin** — KTU and ISPE convention recommends a **safety / over-design factor of 5 % to 25 %** on calculated area. --- ## 1.4 Classification of Area in Heat Exchangers | Type of Area | Description | Typical Use | |---|---|---| | **Bare Area (Primary Surface)** | Smooth, unfinned tube/shell wall | Clean service, high U fluids | | **Extended / Finned Area (Secondary Surface)** | Tubes fitted with transverse or longitudinal fins to amplify area | Gas-side heating/cooling (low h_gas) | | **Effective Area (A_eff)** | Area corrected for fins (A_finned + η_f · A_fin) and fouling | Used in final rate equation | | **Outside Tube Area (A_o)** | Used as the reference for U_o | Standard convention (Kern's method) | | **Inside Tube Area (A_i)** | Used as the reference for U_i | When fluid is on tube side only | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Area Determination is a "Design Closure" Problem Area determination is not a single calculation — it is an **iterative design closure loop** involving five coupled decisions: 1. **Selection of heat exchanger type** (shell-and-tube, plate, finned, etc.). 2. **Selection of U** (initial guess based on service experience tables). 3. **Calculation of true mean temperature difference (ΔT_m)** — pure counter-current **LMTD** or corrected with **LMTD correction factor F_T** for multi-pass / cross-flow arrangements. 4. **Computation of A = Q / (U · ΔT_m)**. 5. **Iteration** — re-check U using the assumed geometry, fouling, and material; repeat until A converges. > [!NOTE] > The **KTU 2024 Scheme** explicitly tests this closure logic — students are expected to outline the iteration, identify the rate equation, and justify the choice of U and ΔT_m. ## 2.2 The General Rate Equation (Design Basis) The basic rate equation forms the **backbone** of every area calculation: $$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_m$$ For shell-and-tube exchangers with multi-pass flow, the corrected form is: $$Q = U \cdot A \cdot F_T \cdot \Delta T_{m,cf}$$ where the subscripts mean: - **LMTD** = Log Mean Temperature Difference (counter-current, true co-current baseline). - **ΔT_m,cf** = Log Mean Temperature Difference based on **counter-current** end temperatures. - **F_T** = LMTD correction factor (function of shell- and tube-side heat capacity rates P and R). - **U** = Overall heat transfer coefficient referenced to a chosen area basis (A_o or A_i). ## 2.3 Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient — Resistance Network For a plain tube (no fins), the resistances in series yield: $$\frac{1}{U_o} = \frac{1}{h_o} + R_{f,o} + \frac{d_o \cdot \ln(d_o/d_i)}{2 \cdot k_w} + R_{f,i} \cdot \frac{d_o}{d_i} + \frac{1}{h_i} \cdot \frac{d_o}{d_i}$$ For a finned tube, the **fin efficiency η_f** reduces the fin contribution, and U based on the bare outside area is: $$\frac{1}{U_o} = \frac{1}{h_o \cdot (\eta_o A_o / A_o)} + R_{f,o} + \frac{d_o \cdot \ln(d_o/d_i)}{2 \cdot k_w} + R_{f,i} \cdot \frac{d_o}{d_i} + \frac{1}{h_i} \cdot \frac{d_o}{d_i}$$ Simplified by defining **A_o = A_bare + η_f · A_fin** : $$\frac{1}{U_o} = \frac{A_o}{h_o \cdot (A_bare + \eta_f \cdot A_{fin})} + R_{f,o} + \frac{d_o \cdot \ln(d_o/d_i)}{2 \cdot k_w} + R_{f,i} \cdot \frac{d_o}{d_i} + \frac{1}{h_i} \cdot \frac{d_o}{d_i}$$ ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations — they are the only ones the KTU 2024 ESE paper expects you to apply for the "Determination of Area" topic.** | # | Formula | Meaning / When to Use | Units (SI) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_m$ | Single-pass / true co-current or counter-current | Q [W], U [W/m²K], A [m²], ΔT [K] | | 2 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot F_T \cdot \Delta T_{m,cf}$ | Shell-and-tube, 1-2 / 2-4 pass with crossflow | dimensionless $F_T \in [0, 1]$ | | 3 | $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Counter-current LMTD | K | | 4 | $P = \dfrac{T_{h,o} - T_{h,i}}{T_{c,i} - T_{c,i}}$ ; $R = \dfrac{T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}}{T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}}$ | Parameters for $F_T$ chart | dimensionless | | 5 | $A = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot F_T \cdot \Delta T_{lm,cf}}$ | Direct area calculation | m² | | 6 | $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_o} + R_{f,o} + \dfrac{d_o \ln(d_o/d_i)}{2 k_w} + R_{f,i} \dfrac{d_o}{d_i} + \dfrac{1}{h_i}\dfrac{d_o}{d_i}$ | Plain tube resistance network | m²K/W | | 7 | $A_{eff} = A_{bare} + \eta_f \cdot A_{fin}$ | Finned-tube effective area | m² | | 8 | $\eta_f = \dfrac{\tanh(m r_c)}{m r_c}$, $m = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 h}{k_{fin} t_{fin}}}$ | Fin efficiency (annular/rectangular fin) | dimensionless | | 9 | $\varepsilon = 1 - \exp\bigl[NTU^{0.22} (\exp(-NTU^{0.78}) - 1)\bigr]$ (crossflow, both unmixed, approx) | Effectiveness for sizing via NTU | dimensionless | | 10 | $A = \dfrac{NTU \cdot C_{min}}{U}$ | NTU-based area | m² | | 11 | $A_{design} = A_{calc} \times (1 + \text{overdesign factor})$ | Practical final area | m² | > [!IMPORTANT] > In the markdown table above, $\vert$ in any expression such as "$\vert \Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2 \vert$" should be written using $\vert$ explicitly. **Do not** use raw `|` inside table cells. ## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering - **Process Plant Design** — sizing condensers, reboilers, feed-effluent exchangers, intercoolers, and waste-heat recovery units in refineries, fertilizer, and LNG plants. - **HVAC & Refrigeration** — selection of chillers, evaporators, and air-cooled finned coils. - **Power Generation** — boiler economizers, feedwater heaters, and air-preheaters. - **Pharma & Food** — plate heat exchangers for pasteurization; tight area control is critical for residence time and product quality. - **Cogeneration & Waste Heat Recovery** — extended-surface (finned) exchangers for flue gas heat recovery. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD) Consider a differential element of a counter-current heat exchanger. Let the hot fluid be cooled from $T_h(x)$ to $T_h(x) - dT_h$ over element $dx$, and the cold fluid be heated from $T_c(x)$ to $T_c(x) + dT_c$. Heat transferred through element: $$dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot (T_h - T_c)$$ Mass and energy balances on the two streams give: $$dQ = -C_h \, dT_h = C_c \, dC_c$$ where $C_h = \dot{m}_h c_{p,h}$ and $C_c = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c}$ are the heat capacity rates. Differentiating $(T_h - T_c)$ along the length: $$d(T_h - T_c) = dT_h - dT_c = -\frac{dQ}{C_h} - \frac{dQ}{C_c} = -dQ \left(\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}\right)$$ Substituting $dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot (T_h - T_c)$: $$\frac{d(T_h - T_c)}{T_h - T_c} = -U\left(\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}\right) dA$$ Integrating from inlet (A = 0) to outlet (A = A): $$\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U\left(\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}\right) \int_0^A dA$$ $$\ln\!\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = -U A\left(\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}\right)$$ Using overall energy balance $Q = C_h (T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) = C_c (T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})$: $$\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c} = \frac{T_{h,i} - T_{h,o} + T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}}{Q} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{Q}$$ Substituting back and solving for Q: $$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$ Therefore, the LMTD is defined as: $$\boxed{\;\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}\;}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Sign convention:** $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ are always taken as **positive**; the sign of heat flow is handled by Q. ## 3.2 Derivation of the NTU-Effectiveness Form for Area Effectiveness ε is defined as: $$\varepsilon = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \frac{Q}{C_{min}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})}$$ Substituting the rate equation $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{m}$: $$\varepsilon = \frac{U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_m}{C_{min}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})}$$ For a counter-current heat exchanger (no phase change), the LMTD can be written as: $$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{(T_{h,i} - T_{c,o}) - (T_{h,o} - T_{c,i})}{\ln\!\left(\frac{T_{h,i} - T_{c,o}}{T_{h,o} - T_{c,i}}\right)}$$ Substituting the relations: $$T_{h,o} = T_{h,i} - \varepsilon \frac{C_{min}}{C_h}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})$$ $$T_{c,o} = T_{c,i} + \varepsilon \frac{C_{min}}{C_c}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})$$ The algebra reduces to the classical ε-NTU relation: $$\varepsilon = \frac{1 - \exp\bigl[-NTU(1 - C_r)\bigr]}{1 - C_r \cdot \exp\bigl[-NTU(1 - C_r)\bigr]}$$ where: $$C_r = \frac{C_{min}}{C_{max}}, \qquad NTU = \frac{U \cdot A}{C_{min}}$$ Solving explicitly for **area**: $$\boxed{\;A = \frac{NTU \cdot C_{min}}{U}\;}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation of the Fin Efficiency (Rectangular Fin) Consider a rectangular fin of length $L$, thickness $t$, perimeter $P$, cross-section $A_c = P \cdot t / 2$ (rectangular), thermal conductivity $k$, and convective coefficient $h$ on both sides. Energy balance on a slice $dx$ from the fin tip: $$\frac{d^2 \theta}{dx^2} - m^2 \theta = 0, \qquad \theta = T - T_\infty, \qquad m^2 = \frac{h P}{k A_c}$$ The general solution is $\theta = C_1 \cosh(mx) + C_2 \sinh(mx)$. For an **adiabatic tip** (long-fin approximation, $L_c = L + t/2$), and base at $\theta(0) = \theta_b$: $$\frac{\theta}{\theta_b} = \frac{\cosh\!\left[m(L - x)\right]}{\cosh(mL)}$$ Heat dissipated by the fin: $$q_f = \sqrt{h P k A_c}\,\theta_b\,\tanh(mL)$$ The fin efficiency is defined as $\eta_f = q_f / (h A_f \theta_b)$ with $A_f = P L$: $$\eta_f = \frac{\tanh(mL)}{mL}$$ > [!NOTE] > For an annular fin, $r_c$ replaces $L$ in the formula, giving $\eta_f = \tanh(m r_c) / (m r_c)$. This is the form KTU prefers for tube-fin analysis. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Single-Pass Counter-Current) **Given:** A counter-current shell-and-tube exchanger cools oil from 150 °C to 80 °C using water entering at 25 °C and leaving at 60 °C. Heat duty $Q = 120 \; kW$. $U = 800 \; W/(m^2 \cdot K)$. **Step 1 — End temperature differences:** $$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} = 150 - 60 = 90 \; °C$$ $$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i} = 80 - 25 = 55 \; °C$$ **Step 2 — LMTD:** $$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{90 - 55}{\ln(90/55)} = \frac{35}{\ln(1.6364)} = \frac{35}{0.4925} = 71.06 \; K$$ **Step 3 — Area from rate equation:** $$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} = \frac{120{,}000 \; W}{800 \cdot 71.06} = \frac{120{,}000}{56{,}846} = 2.111 \; m^2$$ **Step 4 — Applying 20 % over-design factor:** $$A_{design} = 2.111 \times 1.20 = 2.533 \; m^2$$ This is the **final specification area** to be ordered/rated. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for Choosing the Method | Situation | Method to Use | Reason | |---|---|---| | Single-pass, true counter-current | Direct LMTD | $F_T = 1$, no correction needed | | 1-2, 2-4 shell-and-tube | LMTD + $F_T$ chart | Crossflow and pass-mixing invalidates pure LMTD | | Phase change on one side | LMTD (condensation/boiling) | ΔT simplifies; careful with LMTD when one side is isothermal | | Both fluids unmixed (compact exchanger) | ε-NTU | No need for LMTD closure; direct area | | Specified outlet temperature, unknown Q | Energy + ε-NTU | Iterative convergence from target temperature | | Gas-side dominant, low h_gas | LMTD + fin effectiveness | Fin efficiency must be included in U or A_eff | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Area Determination Procedure ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[Process Inputs: Q duty, fluid T, m_dot, cp] --> B0[Step 1: Pick Exchanger Type] B0 --> B1{Shell-and-Tube?} B0 --> B2{Plate Frame?} B0 --> B3{Finned Coil?} B1 --> C1[Reference Area: A_o] B2 --> C2[Reference Area: A_plate] B3 --> C3[Reference Area: A_finned + bare] C1 --> D1[Step 2: Assume U from Tables] C2 --> D2[Step 2: Assume U from Tables] C3 --> D3[Step 2: Compute U from Resistances + Fin Efficiency] D1 --> E1[Step 3: Compute LMTD] D2 --> E2[Step 3: Compute LMTD] D3 --> E3[Step 3: Compute LMTD] E1 --> F1{Multi-pass?} E2 --> F2{Multi-pass?} E3 --> F3[Pure CC, F_T = 1] F1 -- Yes --> G1[Apply F_T from P-R Chart] F1 -- No --> H1[F_T = 1] F2 -- Yes --> G2[Apply F_T from P-R Chart] F2 -- No --> H2[F_T = 1] G1 --> I1[Step 4: A = Q / U F_T LMTD] G2 --> I1 H1 --> I1 H2 --> I1 F3 --> I1 I1 --> J1[Step 5: Apply Fouling and Overdesign Factor] J1 --> K1[Final A_design Specification] D3 --> I1 ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Overall Resistance Network (1/U) ```mermaid flowchart LR R1[1/h_o
Shell Side Convection] --> R2[R_f,o
Shell Side Fouling] R2 --> R3[d_o ln d_o/d_i / 2k_w
Tube Wall Conduction] R3 --> R4[R_f,i times d_o/d_i
Tube Side Fouling] R4 --> R5[d_o/d_i times 1/h_i
Tube Side Convection] R5 --> OUT[1/U_o
Overall Resistance] ``` ## 4.3 Schematic of a Shell-and-Tube Exchanger (TEMA Convention) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SHELL[Shell Side - Hot Fluid] H1[Hot Inlet T_h,i] --> H2[Nozzle Channel D] H2 --> H3[Baffle Region 1] H3 --> H4[Baffle Region 2] H4 --> H5[Nozzle Channel D Prime] H5 --> H6[Hot Outlet T_h,o] end subgraph TUBE[Tube Side - Cold Fluid] C1[Cold Inlet T_c,i] --> C2[Tubesheet 1] C2 --> C3[Tube Pass 1] C3 --> C4[Return Header] C4 --> C5[Tube Pass 2] C5 --> C6[Tubesheet 2] C6 --> C7[Cold Outlet T_c,o] end SHELL -- Heat Through Tube Wall --> TUBE ``` ## 4.4 Finned Tube — Effective Area Concept ```mermaid flowchart LR A[A_total] --> B[A_bare] A --> C[A_fin] C --> D[Multiplier: fin efficiency eta_f] D --> E[eta_f times A_fin] B --> F[A_eff = A_bare + eta_f A_fin] E --> F F --> G[Plug into A = Q / U LMTD] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define the term "Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)" and state its units. How does fouling affect its value during heat exchanger operation?** **Model Answer:** > [!IMPORTANT] > **U (Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient)** is defined as the rate of heat transfer per unit area per unit mean temperature difference across a composite heat transfer surface. > > $$\boxed{\;U = \dfrac{Q}{A \cdot \Delta T_m}\;}$$ > > **Units:** $W/(m^2 \cdot K)$ in SI. > > **Effect of fouling:** Fouling deposits an additional thermal resistance $R_f$ on the heat transfer surfaces (tube inside, tube outside, or both). Since $U$ is the reciprocal of the sum of all resistances, the addition of $R_f$ **decreases U** with time. Freshly commissioned exchangers have the highest $U$; over months of operation, $U$ drops, reducing heat duty. **Overdesign area (5–25 %)** is built in to accommodate this decline. ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **What is the LMTD correction factor $F_T$? Under what conditions is its value taken as unity?** **Model Answer:** > The **LMTD correction factor $F_T$** accounts for the deviation of a multi-pass / crossflow heat exchanger from true counter-current flow. The actual driving force is: > > $$\Delta T_m = F_T \cdot \Delta T_{lm,cf}$$ > > where $\Delta T_{lm,cf}$ is the counter-current LMTD computed using the *terminal* temperatures. > > $F_T = 1$ when: > 1. The exchanger is **single-pass shell-and-tube** with true counter-current flow. > 2. One fluid undergoes **phase change** (e.g., condenser with condensing saturated steam) — temperature on that side is constant, making multi-pass equivalent to counter-current. > 3. The heat capacity rates are equal ($C_{min}/C_{max} = 1$) and the configuration is 1-2 with one fluid experiencing negligible temperature change. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > As per **KTU ESE Regulation 2024 (Module Level Internal Choice Pattern)**, the following is provided as the **OR option pair** for the question of this module. --- ### **Question A** [KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Pattern] — 14 Marks **(a)** Derive the expression for the **Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)** for a counter-current heat exchanger. State clearly the assumptions used. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A shell-and-tube heat exchanger (1-2 configuration) is used to condense 5,000 kg/h of saturated steam at 1.5 bar (saturation temperature = 111.4 °C). Cooling water enters at 25 °C and leaves at 60 °C. The overall heat transfer coefficient is $U = 2{,}500 \; W/(m^2 \cdot K)$. Determine: - (i) The rate of heat transfer **Q**. - (ii) The **LMTD**. - (iii) The required **heat transfer area**. - (iv) The number of tubes required if each tube is 3 m long with 19.05 mm OD. - (v) The required **cold fluid flow rate** of water (assume $c_{p,water} = 4.18 \; kJ/kg\cdot K$). Take latent heat of vaporization of steam at 1.5 bar: $\lambda = 2{,}226 \; kJ/kg$. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Derivation:** *(See Section 3.1 of these notes for the complete derivation. The key steps are:)* - Energy balance on differential element: $dQ = U \, dA \, (T_h - T_c)$. - Mass-energy balances: $dQ = -C_h dT_h = C_c dT_c$. - Differential of temperature difference: $d(\Delta T) = -dQ\left(\dfrac{1}{C_h} + \dfrac{1}{C_c}\right)$. - Substitution, integration from $\Delta T_1$ to $\Delta T_2$, and use of overall energy balance $Q = C_h(T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) = C_c(T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})$ yields: $$\boxed{\;\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}\;}$$ - **Assumptions:** (1) Steady-state operation, (2) Constant $U$ along the length, (3) Constant $c_p$ for each fluid, (4) No phase change, (5) No heat loss to surroundings, (6) No axial conduction in wall. **Part (b) — Numerical:** **(i) Heat duty:** $$Q = \dot{m}_{steam} \times \lambda = \frac{5000}{3600} \times 2226 = 1.389 \times 2226 = 3{,}091.7 \; kW$$ **(ii) LMTD (condensation, so steam side isothermal at 111.4 °C):** $$\Delta T_1 = 111.4 - 60 = 51.4 \; °C$$ $$\Delta T_2 = 111.4 - 25 = 86.4 \; °C$$ $$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{86.4 - 51.4}{\ln(86.4 / 51.4)} = \frac{35.0}{\ln(1.681)} = \frac{35.0}{0.5193} = 67.4 \; K$$ **Since one fluid is condensing (isothermal), $F_T = 1$ (no correction needed).** **(iii) Required area:** $$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} = \frac{3{,}091{,}700 \; W}{2500 \times 67.4} = \frac{3{,}091{,}700}{168{,}500} = 18.35 \; m^2$$ **(iv) Number of tubes:** Surface area of one tube = $\pi d_o L = \pi \times 0.01905 \times 3 = 0.1795 \; m^2$ $$N = \frac{A}{A_{tube}} = \frac{18.35}{0.1795} \approx 102 \; \text{tubes}$$ **(v) Cooling water flow rate:** $$\dot{m}_{water} = \frac{Q}{c_p \Delta T_c} = \frac{3{,}091{,}700 \; W}{4180 \times (60 - 25)} = \frac{3{,}091{,}700}{146{,}300} = 21.13 \; kg/s$$ Or equivalently, $= 21.13 \times 3600 = 76{,}068 \; kg/h \approx 76.1 \; m^3/h$. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** When one fluid is **condensing (isothermal)**, students often wrongly compute LMTD using streamwise temperatures. Instead, take $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ at the two ends of the *condensate side* (water inlet/outlet) using the constant steam temperature. Also, $F_T = 1$ in condensation — do **not** apply a correction factor for a 1-2 condenser. --- ### **Question B** [KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Pattern] — 14 Marks **(a)** Explain the **NTU-Effectiveness method** for heat exchanger analysis. Derive the expression for effectiveness of a counter-current heat exchanger. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A finned-tube air heater is used to heat air from 20 °C to 80 °C using flue gas entering at 250 °C and leaving at 150 °C. The overall heat transfer coefficient (based on bare outside tube area) is $U_o = 60 \; W/(m^2 \cdot K)$. The total heat transfer rate is 80 kW. - (i) Calculate the **LMTD** (assume $F_T = 1$ for a pure crossflow). - (ii) Calculate the **required bare tube area**. - (iii) The tubes are fitted with annular fins, with fin efficiency $\eta_f = 0.85$ and fin area ratio $A_{fin}/A_{bare} = 8$. Find the **effective (total) heat transfer area**. - (iv) Briefly explain why the fin efficiency reduces the effective heat transfer area contribution. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — NTU Method:** > [!NOTE] > The **NTU (Number of Transfer Units) — Effectiveness (ε) method** is preferred when outlet temperatures are not specified or when phase change or complex configurations are involved. > > **Definitions:** > - Effectiveness: $\varepsilon = Q_{actual} / Q_{max}$ > - $Q_{max} = C_{min}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})$ > - $NTU = U A / C_{min}$ > - Heat capacity ratio: $C_r = C_{min} / C_{max}$ > > **Derivation (Counter-current, both fluids single phase):** Using the energy balance and the LMTD equation, after algebraic manipulation: > > $$\varepsilon = \frac{1 - \exp\bigl[-NTU(1 - C_r)\bigr]}{1 - C_r \cdot \exp\bigl[-NTU(1 - C_r)\bigr]}$$ > > Special cases: for $C_r = 0$ (e.g., condensation): $\varepsilon = 1 - \exp(-NTU)$. **Part (b) — Numerical:** **(i) LMTD:** $$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} = 250 - 80 = 170 \; °C$$ $$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i} = 150 - 20 = 130 \; °C$$ $$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{170 - 130}{\ln(170/130)} = \frac{40}{\ln(1.3077)} = \frac{40}{0.2683} = 149.1 \; K$$ **(ii) Bare tube area (without fins):** $$A_{bare} = \frac{Q}{U_o \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} = \frac{80{,}000}{60 \times 149.1} = \frac{80{,}000}{8{,}946} = 8.94 \; m^2$$ **(iii) Effective area with fins:** The fin effectiveness equation: $$A_{eff} = A_{bare} + \eta_f \cdot A_{fin} = A_{bare} \left(1 + \eta_f \cdot \frac{A_{fin}}{A_{bare}}\right)$$ $$A_{eff} = 8.94 \times (1 + 0.85 \times 8) = 8.94 \times (1 + 6.8) = 8.94 \times 7.8 = 69.7 \; m^2$$ **(iv) Why fin efficiency reduces contribution:** > [!NOTE] > A fin's tip region operates at a temperature **closer to the fluid temperature** than its base. Therefore, the local heat flux varies along the fin from maximum at the base to minimum at the tip. The fin efficiency $\eta_f < 1$ quantifies this — the fin dissipates only a fraction $\eta_f$ of the heat that an equivalent bare surface at the **base temperature** would transfer. Hence the effective fin area used in the rate equation is $\eta_f \cdot A_{fin}$, not the geometric $A_{fin}$. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write $A_{eff} = A_{bare} + A_{fin}$ (without $\eta_f$). This is incorrect. The formula is $A_{eff} = A_{bare} + \eta_f A_{fin}$. Forgetting the fin efficiency term costs **2–3 marks** consistently across KTU valuation. Also, do not confuse U based on bare area with U based on effective area — they differ by a factor of $(A_{eff}/A_{bare})$ in the convective resistance term. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision — Memorize Before ESE:** - [x] **Master equation:** $Q = U \cdot A \cdot F_T \cdot \Delta T_{lm,cf}$ — write it the moment a heat exchanger area problem appears. - [x] **LMTD formula:** $\Delta T_{lm} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2) / \ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$ — keep ΔT's **positive**. - [x] **Condensation/boiling:** $F_T = 1$ always, and ΔT is computed using the constant saturation temperature. - [x] **Multi-pass exchangers:** Compute LMTD using **counter-current end temperatures** (even if flow is multi-pass), then multiply by $F_T$ from P-R chart. - [x] **Fouling factor $R_f$:** Always **added** to resistance, never subtracted. Units: **m²·K/W**. Typical water-side: 0.0001–0.0005. - [x] **U decreases with time** due to fouling. The design area is calculated with the **fouled (operating) U**, not the clean U. - [x] **Finned tubes:** $A_{eff} = A_{bare} + \eta_f \cdot A_{fin}$. Forgetting $\eta_f$ is a **common error**. - [x] **Over-design factor** (5–25 %) is applied to the *calculated* A — not to U. - [x] **NTU method:** Use when **outlet temperatures unknown** or **effectiveness specified**; otherwise prefer LMTD method. - [x] **Iterative closure:** Area determination is **iterative** — U assumed → A calculated → geometry assumed → re-calculate U → repeat until A converges. - [x] **Units consistency:** Q in W, U in W/m²K, A in m², ΔT in K. Watch out for kW vs W conversion (×1000). - [x] **Safety check:** If $F_T < 0.75$, KTU expects the student to recommend a **different exchanger configuration** (e.g., 2-4 pass or pure counter-current). - [x] **Heat capacity rate definition:** $C = \dot{m} \cdot c_p$. The minimum $C$ sets $Q_{max}$. - [x] **Wall resistance formula:** $R_{wall} = d_o \ln(d_o/d_i) / (2 k_w)$ — only the **tube** wall; for plates, $R = L_{plate} / k_{plate}$. - [x] **Sanity test:** If $A_{design}$ < 0.5 m², the problem is likely asking for a double-pipe exchanger; if > 50 m², expect shell-and-tube or plate.

topics.title: Length
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Length

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Heat Exchanger Design (Module 4 – PCCHT402)**, the term **"Length"** specifically refers to the **effective tube length (L)** required in a **Shell-and-Tube Heat Exchanger (STHE)** to achieve a specified rate of heat transfer (heat duty, $Q$) between a hot and a cold fluid stream. It is the principal geometric design output obtained from the fundamental heat transfer equation once the heat transfer area, log-mean temperature difference (LMTD), and overall heat transfer coefficient ($U$) are known.

Mathematically, it is the axial distance measured along the tube through which the tube-side fluid flows, providing the surface area necessary for the desired heat exchange.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *"The length of the heat exchanger is the length of the tube bundle required to transfer a specified heat duty at a given overall heat transfer coefficient and mean temperature difference, computed using the relation $L = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm} \cdot F}$ for a single-pass exchanger, extended with appropriate correction factors for multi-pass configurations."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are heating water using a long metal pipe carrying hot oil. If the pipe is **short**, the water does not get enough time in contact with the hot surface — the exit temperature remains low. If the pipe is **very long**, the oil loses most of its heat before reaching the end, and the water becomes very hot. Somewhere in between, there is an **"optimum length"** at which the oil gives up *exactly* the amount of heat the water needs to reach the target temperature.

> **Plain-English Summary:** The *Length* in a heat exchanger is the "stretch of contact" the two fluids need to exchange the right amount of heat — too short and the heat exchange is incomplete, too long and the equipment becomes oversized and uneconomical.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are critical to the length calculation:

- **Heat Duty ($Q$):** Expressed in **W (watts)** or **kW** in SI units. **1 kW = 1000 W**.
- **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient ($U$):** Expressed in **$\mathbf{W/m^2 \cdot K}$**.
- **Log-Mean Temperature Difference ($\Delta T_{lm}$):** Expressed in **K** or **°C** (numerically equivalent for differences).
- **Outer Tube Diameter ($d_o$):** Expressed in **m** or **mm**.
- **Correction Factor ($F$):** Dimensionless, ranging between **0 and 1**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Engineering Practice:** Tube lengths in industrial STHEs are usually standardized at **8 ft (2.44 m), 12 ft (3.66 m), 16 ft (4.88 m), and 20 ft (6.10 m)**. The calculated length is rounded up to the next standard size.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic Behind Length Calculation

The design of a heat exchanger is essentially a **back-calculation problem** in which the engineer is given a required heat duty (from process needs) and must determine the *physical size* of the equipment. The logic proceeds in a strict hierarchical order:

1. **Step 1 – Mass & Energy Balance:** Compute the heat duty $Q$ from the mass flow rates and specific heats:
   $Q = \dot{m}_h c_{ph} (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = \dot{m}_c c_{pc} (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$

2. **Step 2 – Estimate the Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient ($U$):** $U$ is obtained from a combination of individual convective resistances on both the tube and shell sides, plus the conduction resistance of the tube wall (and any fouling layers).

3. **Step 3 – Compute the Log-Mean Temperature Difference ($\Delta T_{lm}$):** This is the *effective* temperature driving force for a counter-current or co-current arrangement:
   $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$

4. **Step 4 – Apply the Correction Factor ($F$):** For multi-pass and cross-flow exchangers, the true mean temperature difference is $\Delta T_m = F \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$. A value of $F < 0.75$ generally indicates an uneconomical design.

5. **Step 5 – Compute the Required Heat Transfer Area ($A$):** Using the rate equation:
   $A = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_m}$

6. **Step 6 – Back-Calculate the Length ($L$):** Using the geometric relation $A = \pi d_o L N_t$, solve for $L$:
   $L = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \pi d_o N_t \cdot \Delta T_m}$
   where $N_t$ is the number of tubes per pass.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why $U$ comes first:** $U$ depends on fluid velocities, properties, and tube geometry, which must be assumed *before* sizing.
- **Why $\Delta T_{lm}$ is logarithmic:** The temperature difference between the fluids varies continuously along the exchanger; the logarithmic mean properly weights this variation.
- **Why $F$ is needed:** In a multi-pass exchanger, the true flow pattern is neither purely counter-current nor purely co-current; $F$ corrects for this deviation.
- **Why the length is back-calculated:** The area is the more fundamental design quantity, and the length is simply the area "unrolled" along the tube axis.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q = \dot{m} \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | Sensible heat duty | W |
| 2 | $Q = U \, A \, \Delta T_m$ | Overall rate equation | W |
| 3 | $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log-mean temperature difference | K or °C |
| 4 | $\Delta T_m = F \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$ | Corrected MTD for multi-pass | K or °C |
| 5 | $A = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_m}$ | Required heat transfer area | $\text{m}^2$ |
| 6 | $A = \pi \, d_o \, L \, N_t$ | Area of $N_t$ parallel tubes | $\text{m}^2$ |
| 7 | $\boxed{L = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \pi \, d_o \, N_t \cdot \Delta T_m}}$ | **Tube length (primary design equation)** | m |
| 8 | $L/D$ ratio check | L/D should typically be between 4 and 10 | dimensionless |
| 9 | $F = \dfrac{\sqrt{(R^2+1) \, \ln\!\left[\dfrac{1-S}{1-RS}\right]}}{(R-1) \, \ln\!\left[\dfrac{2-S(R+1-\sqrt{R^2+1})}{2-S(R+1+\sqrt{R^2+1})}\right]}$ | Correction factor (1-2 STHE) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $R = \dfrac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}$, $S = \dfrac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}}$ | Auxiliary parameters for $F$ | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When writing the length equation, *always* explicitly state that $A = \pi d_o L N_t$ and substitute into $A = Q / (U \cdot \Delta T_m)$. Skipping the substitution step is the single most common reason students lose 1–2 marks.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Petrochemical & Refining Industry:** Sizing crude oil pre-heaters, overhead condensers, and reboilers.
- **Power Plants:** Condensers, feedwater heaters, and oil coolers.
- **HVAC & Refrigeration:** Evaporator and condenser coil sizing.
- **Chemical Process Plants:** Reactor cooling/heating coils and inter-stage coolers.

> **Critical Design Constraint:** The calculated length should be checked against the **L/D ratio** constraint (typically 4 ≤ L/D ≤ 10). If violated, the number of tubes $N_t$ must be adjusted and the calculation re-iterated.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Tube Length Formula

We start from the **fundamental rate equation of heat transfer**:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_m$$

where $Q$ is the heat duty, $U$ is the overall heat transfer coefficient, $A$ is the total heat transfer area, and $\Delta T_m$ is the corrected mean temperature difference.

For a shell-and-tube heat exchanger with $N_t$ number of tubes per pass, each of outer diameter $d_o$ and length $L$, the total heat transfer area (neglecting the contribution of the inner surface for simplicity, or assuming $d_i \approx d_o$) is the lateral surface of all tubes:

$$A = (\text{surface area of one tube}) \times N_t = (\pi \, d_o \, L) \cdot N_t$$

Substituting this expression for $A$ into the rate equation:

$$Q = U \cdot (\pi \, d_o \, L \, N_t) \cdot \Delta T_m$$

Rearranging to isolate $L$ on the left-hand side:

$$\boxed{L = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \pi \, d_o \, N_t \cdot \Delta T_m}}$$
