> **Derivation Note:** This is the *core* design equation. Every other variant (e.g., using inner diameter $d_i$ with $h_i$ on the inside) follows the same algebraic structure but with $U$ re-defined on an inner-area basis.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Log-Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)

Consider a counter-flow heat exchanger. Let $T$ be the temperature of the hot fluid and $t$ be the temperature of the cold fluid at any axial position $x$. The local temperature differences at the two ends are:

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} \quad \text{(hot fluid in - cold fluid out)}$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} \quad \text{(hot fluid out - cold fluid in)}$$

For a differential element $dx$, the heat transferred is $dQ = U \cdot (\pi d_o N_t) \cdot (T - t) \cdot dx$. Equating this with the energy balance on each fluid, integrating across the exchanger, and applying the boundary conditions, we obtain:

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}$$

> **Key Property:** The LMTD is always **less than the arithmetic mean** but **greater than the smaller of** $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$. If $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$ (constant temperature difference, e.g., one fluid undergoing phase change), the LMTD equals that common value.

## 3.3 Comprehensive Worked Example (Model KTU-Style Problem)

> **Problem:** A 1-2 shell-and-tube heat exchanger is to heat $20{,}000\ \text{kg/h}$ of an oil (specific heat $c_{p} = 2.0\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$) from $30\ \text{°C}$ to $90\ \text{°C}$ using condensing steam at $120\ \text{°C}$. The overall heat transfer coefficient is $U = 1500\ \text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$. The heat exchanger uses tubes of outer diameter $d_o = 25\ \text{mm}$ arranged as **2 tubes per pass per shell, 4 passes total, in a single shell**. Determine the **tube length $L$**.

### Solution:

**Step 1 – Compute the Heat Duty ($Q$):**

$$Q = \dot{m} \, c_p \, \Delta T = \left(\dfrac{20{,}000}{3600}\right) \times 2000 \times (90 - 30)$$

$$Q = 5.556 \times 2000 \times 60 = 666{,}666.67\ \text{W} \approx 666.67\ \text{kW}$$

**Step 2 – Compute the Log-Mean Temperature Difference ($\Delta T_{lm}$):**

Since the hot fluid (steam) is condensing, its temperature is constant at $120\ \text{°C}$. The cold fluid (oil) goes from $30\ \text{°C}$ to $90\ \text{°C}$.

$$\Delta T_1 = 120 - 30 = 90\ \text{°C}$$
$$\Delta T_2 = 120 - 90 = 30\ \text{°C}$$

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{90 - 30}{\ln(90/30)} = \dfrac{60}{\ln 3} = \dfrac{60}{1.0986} = 54.61\ \text{°C}$$

**Step 3 – Apply the Correction Factor ($F$):**

For a 1-2 STHE with one fluid undergoing phase change ($R = 0$ — but undefined for $R=0$, conventionally taken as $F = 1$ when one fluid is isothermal):

$$F = 1.0$$

Therefore $\Delta T_m = F \cdot \Delta T_{lm} = 54.61\ \text{°C}$.

**Step 4 – Compute the Required Heat Transfer Area ($A$):**

$$A = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_m} = \dfrac{666{,}666.67}{1500 \times 54.61}$$

$$A = \dfrac{666{,}666.67}{81{,}915} = 8.138\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 5 – Compute the Tube Length ($L$):**

Total number of tubes = $N_t \times \text{passes} = 2 \times 4 = 8$ tubes. The "tubes per pass" $N_t$ in the formula is 2.

$$L = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \pi \, d_o \, N_t \cdot \Delta T_m} = \dfrac{666{,}666.67}{1500 \times \pi \times 0.025 \times 2 \times 54.61}$$

$$L = \dfrac{666{,}666.67}{12{,}872.55} = 51.79\ \text{m}$$

**Step 6 – Length per Pass and Design Verification:**

Since the heat exchanger has 4 passes, the **length per pass** is:

$$L_{\text{per pass}} = \dfrac{51.79}{4} = 12.95\ \text{m}$$

Standardize to $L = 16\ \text{ft} = 4.88\ \text{m}$ per pass × 4 passes = $19.52\ \text{m}$ total, which exceeds the required $51.79\ \text{m}$ ✓. Alternatively, $L = 12\ \text{m}$ per pass × 4 passes = $48\ \text{m}$, which is too short; therefore $L = 4.88\ \text{m}$ × 4 passes is the correct choice, or reduce the number of passes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Note:** The final step of standardizing to a commercial tube length is **often skipped in KTU answer sheets**, but examiners often award 1 mark for "design verification / standard size selection." Always include it.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Length Calculation

The following block diagram maps the **sequential dependency** of the inputs required to compute the length of a shell-and-tube heat exchanger. Each block represents a calculation stage, and the arrows represent the data flow between stages.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Process Inputs: Flow rates, Inlet and Outlet Temperatures] --> B[Mass and Energy Balance]
    B --> C[Heat Duty Q in W]
    D[Estimated Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient U] --> G[Area Calculation]
    E[Inlet and Outlet Temperatures Hot and Cold] --> F[LMTD Computation]
    F --> H[Correction Factor F from Chart]
    H --> I[Corrected MTD Delta T m]
    C --> J[Length Computation]
    G --> J
    I --> J
    K[Tube Geometry: do and Number of Tubes per Pass Nt] --> J
    J --> L[Tube Length L in metres]
    L --> M[L per D Ratio Check]
    M --> N[Standardize to Commercial Length: 8, 12, 16, 20 ft]
```

## 4.2 Schematic of a 1-2 Shell-and-Tube Heat Exchanger (Conceptual Block View)

The diagram below abstracts a real shell-and-tube unit into labeled functional blocks, showing how the **hot and cold streams traverse the unit in a 1-shell-pass, 2-tube-pass arrangement**, and where the **length parameter $L$** physically manifests.

```mermaid
graph LR
    InletHot[Hot Fluid Inlet T hin] --> ShellA[Shell Side Pass 1]
    ShellA --> ShellB[Shell Side Pass 2]
    ShellB --> OutletHot[Hot Fluid Outlet T hout]
    InletCold[Cold Fluid Inlet t cin] --> TubeA[Tube Side Pass 1 of Length L]
    TubeA --> Turnaround[Header Turnaround]
    Turnaround --> TubeB[Tube Side Pass 2 of Length L]
    TubeB --> OutletCold[Cold Fluid Outlet t cout]
    ShellA -.transfers Q.-> TubeA
    ShellB -.transfers Q.-> TubeB
```

## 4.3 Component-Level Architecture of Length-Dependent Design

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Q from Energy Balance] --> P2[Numerator of Length Formula]
    P3[U Estimated] --> P4[Denominator Term 1]
    P5[pi times do times Nt] --> P6[Denominator Term 2]
    P7[F times Delta T lm] --> P8[Denominator Term 3]
    P4 --> P9[Final Length L]
    P6 --> P9
    P8 --> P9
    P2 --> P9
    P9 --> P10[Compare with Standard Tube Lengths]
    P10 --> P11[Final L Selected: Round Up]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid for Students:** The three denominator terms represent the three physical "knobs" an engineer can turn to reduce the length: (1) increase $U$ (better heat transfer enhancement), (2) increase $d_o$ or $N_t$ (more area), or (3) increase $\Delta T_m$ (better flow arrangement to raise $F$).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1:** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the term "Length of a heat exchanger." What is its physical significance in the design of a shell-and-tube exchanger?**

**Model Answer:**
The *length* of a heat exchanger refers to the effective length $L$ of the tubes in a shell-and-tube exchanger through which heat transfer between the hot and cold fluids takes place. It is computed from the relation $L = Q / (U \cdot \pi \, d_o \, N_t \cdot \Delta T_m)$. Physically, it represents the axial distance over which the two fluids must remain in thermal contact to exchange the required heat duty. A longer length provides more area, but at higher capital cost, so $L$ is a critical design optimization parameter that must be rounded up to the nearest standard commercial tube length (typically 8, 12, 16, or 20 ft).

### **Question A2:** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Why is the LMTD multiplied by a correction factor $F$ in the design of multi-pass heat exchangers?**

**Model Answer:**
The log-mean temperature difference (LMTD) is strictly valid only for **pure counter-current or pure co-current** flow. In a multi-pass or cross-flow heat exchanger, the actual flow pattern is a hybrid, and the true mean temperature driving force is smaller than the counter-current LMTD. The correction factor $F$ (≤ 1) accounts for this deviation. The design equation therefore becomes $Q = U \cdot A \cdot F \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$. If $F$ falls below 0.75, the design is considered economically unattractive, and the engineer must either change the flow arrangement or add more shells in series.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> *Per KTU 2024 ESE Module Convention, students must answer ONE of TWO internal choices. Both alternatives are provided below.*

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

**A 1-2 shell-and-tube heat exchanger is to be designed to cool $50{,}000\ \text{kg/h}$ of a hydrocarbon liquid (specific heat $c_p = 2.5\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$) from $150\ \text{°C}$ to $40\ \text{°C}$ using cooling water that enters at $25\ \text{°C}$ and leaves at $40\ \text{°C}$. The overall heat transfer coefficient is $U = 800\ \text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$. Use tubes of outer diameter $25\ \text{mm}$ with 100 tubes arranged in 2 passes.**

**(a) Calculate the heat duty and the log-mean temperature difference.** **(7 Marks)**

**(b) Calculate the correction factor and the required tube length. Comment on the L/D ratio.** **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for B-A:**

**Part (a) – Heat Duty and LMTD:**

**Heat Duty ($Q$):**

$$Q = \dot{m} \, c_p \, \Delta T = \left(\dfrac{50{,}000}{3600}\right) \times 2500 \times (150 - 40)$$

$$Q = 13.889 \times 2500 \times 110 = 3{,}819{,}444\ \text{W} \approx 3.82\ \text{MW}$$

**Log-Mean Temperature Difference:**

For a 1-2 STHE, the *true counter-current* end-differences are:
- $\Delta T_1 = 150 - 40 = 110\ \text{°C}$ (hot in / cold out)
- $\Delta T_2 = 40 - 25 = 15\ \text{°C}$ (hot out / cold in)

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{110 - 15}{\ln(110/15)} = \dfrac{95}{\ln 7.333} = \dfrac{95}{1.9924} = 47.68\ \text{°C}$$

**Part (b) – Correction Factor and Length:**

**Correction Factor Parameters:**

$$R = \dfrac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}} = \dfrac{150 - 40}{40 - 25} = \dfrac{110}{15} = 7.333$$

$$S = \dfrac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}} = \dfrac{40 - 25}{150 - 25} = \dfrac{15}{125} = 0.12$$

**$F$ from the 1-2 STHE correction factor chart** (or by formula):

$$F \approx 0.88 \quad \text{(read from standard chart for } R=7.33, S=0.12\text{)}$$

**Corrected MTD:**

$$\Delta T_m = F \cdot \Delta T_{lm} = 0.88 \times 47.68 = 41.96\ \text{°C}$$

**Tube Length:**

$$L = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \pi \, d_o \, N_t \cdot \Delta T_m} = \dfrac{3{,}819{,}444}{800 \times \pi \times 0.025 \times 100 \times 41.96}$$

$$L = \dfrac{3{,}819{,}444}{2{,}636{,}357} = 1.449\ \text{m per tube, total length per pass}$$

**Length per tube** (since there are 100 tubes in 2 passes, the flow path is $L$ through the entire bundle; if 2 passes means 50 tubes per pass, then per-pass length is $2.898\ \text{m}$):

Assuming the full heat transfer area is on a single continuous tube of length $L$ and 100 tubes operate in parallel:

$$L = 1.45\ \text{m}$$

**L/D Ratio Check:**

$$\dfrac{L}{D} = \dfrac{1.45}{0.025} = 58$$

**Comment:** The L/D ratio of 58 is far in excess of the recommended range (4 to 10). This indicates that the design is over-sized in terms of tube diameter / under-sized in length. The recommendation is to use **more, smaller-diameter tubes** or **multiple shells in series** to bring the L/D ratio into the acceptable range. Alternatively, choose a larger $d_o$ (e.g., 50 mm) to reduce the L/D ratio.

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks) – Internal Choice:** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**A condenser is to condense $5{,}000\ \text{kg/h}$ of saturated steam at $0.2\ \text{bar}$ (latent heat $h_{fg} = 2350\ \text{kJ/kg}$) using cooling water entering at $20\ \text{°C}$ and leaving at $35\ \text{°C}$. The overall heat transfer coefficient based on the outer area of the tubes is $U_o = 4500\ \text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$. The exchanger has 50 tubes of outer diameter $20\ \text{mm}$, arranged in a single pass. Steam condenses at $60\ \text{°C}$ (saturation temperature at $0.2\ \text{bar}$).**

**(a) Calculate the heat duty and the LMTD. Explain why the correction factor $F$ is not required here.** **(7 Marks)**

**(b) Determine the required tube length. Verify whether the L/D ratio is acceptable.** **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for B-B:**

**Part (a) – Heat Duty and LMTD:**

**Heat Duty ($Q$):**

$$Q = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg} = \left(\dfrac{5{,}000}{3600}\right) \times 2{,}350{,}000 = 1.389 \times 2{,}350{,}000$$

$$Q = 3{,}263{,}889\ \text{W} \approx 3.26\ \text{MW}$$

**LMTD Calculation:**

Steam condenses isothermally at $60\ \text{°C}$, so $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$ and:

$$\Delta T_{lm} = 60 - \dfrac{20 + 35}{2} = 60 - 27.5 = 32.5\ \text{°C}$$

**Why $F$ is not required:** When one of the fluids undergoes a phase change at constant temperature (isothermal process), the LMTD is **inherently valid** for any flow arrangement (counter-current, co-current, multi-pass, cross-flow). The correction factor $F$ is required only when **both fluids undergo sensible heat transfer with changing temperatures** in non-counter-flow arrangements. Therefore, $F = 1$ and $\Delta T_m = \Delta T_{lm} = 32.5\ \text{°C}$.

**Part (b) – Tube Length and L/D Check:**

**Tube Length:**

$$L = \dfrac{Q}{U_o \cdot \pi \, d_o \, N_t \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} = \dfrac{3{,}263{,}889}{4500 \times \pi \times 0.020 \times 50 \times 32.5}$$

$$L = \dfrac{3{,}263{,}889}{459{,}490} = 7.10\ \text{m}$$

**L/D Ratio Check:**

$$\dfrac{L}{D} = \dfrac{7.10}{0.020} = 355$$

**Verification:** An L/D ratio of 355 is **grossly unacceptable** (limit: 4 to 10). This indicates the design is impractical. The corrective action is to:
- (i) Increase the number of tubes $N_t$ substantially (e.g., to 200 tubes), which would reduce the required $L$ to $\approx 1.78\ \text{m}$, giving an L/D of 89 — still high.
- (ii) Use **multiple shells in series** (e.g., 2 STHE shells in series) to halve the per-shell length.
- (iii) Use a larger outer diameter tube (e.g., $d_o = 50\ \text{mm}$), which would halve $L$ and bring L/D into an acceptable range.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** A common error is using $d_o$ in mm without converting to m. The resulting length will be off by a factor of 1000. *Always write $d_o = 0.025$ m, not 25 mm, in the formula.*
> 2. **Confusing $N_t$:** Students often use the *total* number of tubes (e.g., 100) instead of the *tubes per pass* (e.g., 50). This halves the calculated length and inflates the L/D ratio. *Read the problem statement carefully: "50 tubes in 2 passes" means 25 tubes per pass.*
> 3. **Forgetting the Correction Factor:** For multi-pass exchangers with sensible heat transfer on both sides, omitting $F$ will lead to a *non-conservative* (under-sized) length. *Always check whether both fluids are sensible or whether one is isothermal.*
> 4. **Skipping the L/D Check:** Examiners award marks for design verification. A correct length without an L/D commentary usually loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Sign Error in $\Delta T_{lm}$:** $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ must be defined consistently (both as "hot − cold" at the same end). Mixing them up leads to negative LMTD.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the "Length" Topic**

- ✅ **Master the 7-step design hierarchy:** $Q \rightarrow U \rightarrow \Delta T_{lm} \rightarrow F \rightarrow A \rightarrow L \rightarrow$ Standardize.
- ✅ **The master equation:** $L = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \pi \, d_o \, N_t \cdot \Delta T_m}$ — memorize it, including the units of every term.
- ✅ **Always include the correction factor $F$** for multi-pass exchangers *unless* one fluid is isothermal (phase change).
- ✅ **$\Delta T_{lm}$ is logarithmic, not arithmetic.** If $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$, then $\Delta T_{lm}$ equals that common value.
- ✅ **Standardize the final length** to a commercial size: 8, 12, 16, or 20 ft.
- ✅ **Verify the L/D ratio** — acceptable range is **4 to 10**. If violated, modify $N_t$ or use multiple shells.
- ✅ **Unit discipline:** $d_o$ in metres, $Q$ in watts, $U$ in $\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$, $\Delta T$ in K or °C.
- ✅ **For condensing/boiling fluids:** $F = 1$ automatically; the LMTD is the true MTD.
- ✅ **Practical design rule of thumb:** If $F < 0.75$, redesign the flow arrangement.
- ✅ **Cross-check using the NTU method** (Module 4, KTU 2024) as an alternative sizing approach to validate the LMTD-based length.
- ✅ **Don't forget to multiply the per-pass length by the number of passes** when reporting the total bundle length.
- ✅ **Effectiveness-NTU relationship:** $\varepsilon = f(\text{NTU}, C_r)$ — if you must cross-verify, use this when outlet temperatures are not specified.
- ✅ **Fouling factor:** In a real design, include a fouling resistance $R_f$ in the calculation of $U$. KTU problems often provide $U$ directly, so this is only relevant for design projects.

> **Final Exam Maxim:** *Length is not just a number — it is the geometric embodiment of the heat duty divided by the integrated thermal driving force. Master this equation, and every heat-exchanger problem in KTU Module 4 will bow down before you.* 🎯
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Number of Tubes Required for a Given Duty in Different Configurations Using LMTD and NTU Method of Analysis
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Number of Tubes Required for a Given Duty in Different Configurations Using LMTD and NTU Method of Analysis

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Exchanger Design Duty (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> The "duty" of a heat exchanger is the **total rate of heat transfer (Q, in Watts or kW)** that must be transferred between the hot and cold streams under specified inlet and outlet temperature conditions. The **Number of Tubes Required for a Given Duty** is the count of parallel heat-transfer tubes (in a shell-and-tube exchanger) whose combined surface area satisfies the fundamental design equation:
> $$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{effective}$$
> where $A = N_t \cdot \pi \cdot d_o \cdot L$, with $N_t$ being the number of tubes, $d_o$ the outer tube diameter, and $L$ the effective tube length.

The "configuration" refers to the **shell-and-tube pass arrangement** (1-1, 1-2, 1-3, 1-4, 2-2, 2-4, 3-6, etc.), which dictates the **correction factor ($F_T$) for LMTD** and the **effectiveness–NTU relationship** for the exchanger.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you need to **cool 1000 people in a hot auditorium** using water sprays:
- **One big spray (1 tube, high flow)** → Some people stay wet, others dry (poor heat distribution).
- **Many small sprays in parallel (many tubes, moderate flow each)** → Everyone gets cooled evenly.

The heat exchanger works the same way. Instead of forcing all fluid through one giant tube, we **split the flow across $N_t$ smaller parallel tubes**, dramatically increasing the **total surface area $A$** available for heat transfer. The **"configuration"** tells us how many times the fluid reverses direction (a 1-2 exchanger has **one shell pass** and **two tube passes**), and this affects how efficiently the hot and cold streams approach each other's temperatures.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Design Logic (KTU 2024 Outcome CO4):**
> Given a duty $Q$, the engineer must back-calculate $N_t$ from $A = Q/(U \cdot \Delta T_{effective})$, where $\Delta T_{effective}$ depends on the configuration chosen.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold Highlights)

- **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient ($U$):** typically **200 – 1500 W/m²·K** for liquid-liquid exchangers.
- **Tube Outer Diameter ($d_o$):** standard values **19.05 mm (¾"), 25.4 mm (1")**.
- **Tube Length ($L$):** usually **2.5 m, 3.0 m, 4.0 m, 6.0 m**.
- **LMTD ($\Delta T_{LM}$):** the **log-mean temperature difference** under pure counter-current flow.
- **Correction Factor $F_T$:** ranges from **0 to 1**, applicable for non-counterflow configurations.
- **NTU (Number of Transfer Units):** dimensionless group governing effectiveness.
- **Effectiveness ($\varepsilon$):** ratio of actual heat transfer to the **maximum possible heat transfer**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

The complete procedure to determine the **number of tubes required** for a shell-and-tube heat exchanger proceeds through these logical stages:

**Step 1 – Enunciate the Duty:**
Given $m_h, m_c, C_{p,h}, C_{p,c}$, and the four terminal temperatures ($T_{h,in}, T_{h,out}, T_{c,in}, T_{c,out}$), compute:
$$Q = m_h \cdot C_{p,h} \cdot (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = m_c \cdot C_{p,c} \cdot (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$$

**Step 2 – Assume/Estimate Overall $U$:**
From empirical correlations (Kern's method, Donohue's correlation) or from standard tables (e.g., **$U = 850 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$** for water-to-water service).

**Step 3 – Compute LMTD for Pure Counter-Current Flow:**
$$\Delta T_{LM} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$
where $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$ and $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$ (counter-current reference).

**Step 4 – Determine the Configuration and $F_T$ Factor:**
Choose configuration (e.g., 1-2, 2-4). Compute $P$ and $R$:
$$P = \frac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}} \quad ; \quad R = \frac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}$$
Read $F_T$ from the standard KTU textbook chart (Fig. 17.13 Holman / Fig. 11.20 Kern) for the chosen number of shell passes.

**Step 5 – Compute the True Mean Temperature Difference:**
$$\Delta T_M = F_T \cdot \Delta T_{LM}$$

**Step 6 – Solve for Required Heat Transfer Area $A$:**
$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_M}$$

**Step 7 – Solve for Number of Tubes $N_t$:**
$$N_t = \frac{A}{\pi \cdot d_o \cdot L}$$

**Step 8 – Apply Tube-Layout Constraints:**
Round $N_t$ up to a value achievable using a standard **tube pitch layout** (triangular 30°, square 90°, rotated square 60°) on the standard shell diameter $D_s$.

**Step 9 – NTU Verification (KTU 2024 CO4 Sub-bullet):**
Independently re-verify using the NTU-effectiveness method:
$$\varepsilon = f(\text{NTU}, C_r, \text{configuration})$$
$$Q = \varepsilon \cdot C_{min} \cdot (T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})$$
$$N_t \text{ from } A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LM,corr}}$$

### 2.2 Why and How Behind Each Step

- **Why Step 3?** LMTD assumes pure counter-current flow, which is the most thermodynamically efficient arrangement. Real exchangers deviate, so $F_T$ corrects this.
- **Why Step 4?** Each shell pass reverses the cold stream's direction. The resulting temperature criss-cross causes **temperature cross** (where $T_{h,out} < T_{c,out}$), reducing $\Delta T_M$.
- **Why NTU verification?** The LMTD method fails when $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$ (phase change at constant temperature). NTU method handles all cases gracefully.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE (Memorize Before Exam)**

| # | Equation | Application | Typical Units |
|---|----------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $Q = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Energy balance | $\text{kW}$ |
| 2 | $\Delta T_{LM} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$ | Pure counter-current LMTD | $\text{K}$ or $^\circ\text{C}$ |
| 3 | $P = \frac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}}$ | Temperature effectiveness ratio | dimensionless |
| 4 | $R = \frac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}$ | Heat capacity ratio parameter | dimensionless |
| 5 | $F_T = f(P, R, N_{sp})$ | Correction factor (chart) | $0 \le F_T \le 1$ |
| 6 | $\Delta T_M = F_T \cdot \Delta T_{LM}$ | True MTD | $\text{K}$ |
| 7 | $A = Q / (U \cdot \Delta T_M)$ | Area from duty | $\text{m}^2$ |
| 8 | $N_t = A / (\pi \cdot d_o \cdot L)$ | Number of tubes | integer |
| 9 | $C_r = C_{min}/C_{max}$ | Capacity ratio (NTU) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\text{NTU} = UA/C_{min}$ | Number of transfer units | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\varepsilon_{1-2} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{4R^2-1}} \cdot \frac{1-\exp[-\text{NTU}\sqrt{4R^2-1}/2]}{1+R}$ | Effectiveness for 1-2 shell-and-tube | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\varepsilon_{\text{counter}} = \frac{1-\exp[-\text{NTU}(1-R)]}{1-R\cdot\exp[-\text{NTU}(1-R)]}$ | Effectiveness for counter-current | dimensionless |
| 13 | $Q_{max} = C_{min} \cdot (T_{h,in}-T_{c,in})$ | Maximum possible duty | $\text{kW}$ |
| 14 | $Q = \varepsilon \cdot Q_{max}$ | Actual duty via NTU method | $\text{kW}$ |
| 15 | $n_c = 0.85 \cdot (D_b/d_p)^2 / (\text{const for pitch})$ | Tube count per shell (Wein's formula) | integer |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall:** When $F_T < 0.75$, the **1-2 configuration is unacceptable** (KTU design rule). Either increase the number of shell passes, use a 2-4 configuration, or shift the temperature approach.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: 1-2 Shell-and-Tube Effectiveness (KTU 2024 Module 4)

We derive the effectiveness equation for a **1-shell pass, 2-tube pass** exchanger. This configuration consists of two identical tube passes, each behaving as a counter-current half, with hot fluid entering and leaving the shell once.

**Start with the differential heat balance on a tube pass half:**

For one of the two tube passes, treat it as counter-current with the hot fluid (mass flow $m_h$ in shell). Let $T_h^*$ be the shell-side temperature at the junction of the two tube passes.

**Heat balance on the lower half (counter-current):**
$$m_c C_{p,c} (T_{c,out} - T_{h,c,junction}) = m_h C_{p,h} (T_{h,in} - T_{h}^*)$$

Defining dimensionless temperatures:
$$\theta_h = \frac{T_h - T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}}, \quad \theta_c = \frac{T_c - T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}}$$

The differential equation for counter-current flow on one side is:
$$\frac{d\theta_h}{d\theta_c} = -\frac{1}{R}$$
where $R = C_{min}/C_{max}$ on the shell side.

**Integrating across the tube-pass half** (assuming constant $U$, $C_p$):
$$\ln\left(\frac{1 - \theta_{c,out}}{1 - \theta_{h,middle}}\right) = \text{NTU}_1 \cdot (1 - R)$$

**Combining both halves** using energy balance symmetry:

The shell-side fluid traverses both tube passes. Define $\text{NTU}_1 = UA/(2 \cdot C_{min})$ for one tube pass.

The cold fluid in pass 1 sees hot fluid at $T_{h,in}$ entering and exiting at some mean $T_{h,m}$. By symmetry of the two passes:
$$T_{h,in} - T_{h,m} = T_{h,m} - T_{h,out}$$

The classical derivation (Holman, Heat Transfer, 10th Ed., Ch. 11) yields the closed-form expression:

$$\varepsilon_{1-2} = \frac{2 \cdot \left[1 + R + \sqrt{1+R^2} \cdot \coth\left(\frac{\text{NTU}\cdot\sqrt{1+R^2}}{2}\right)\right]^{-1} \cdot (1 - e^{-\text{NTU}\sqrt{1+R^2}/2})}{\text{(hyperbolic form — equivalent forms in textbooks)}}$$

Equivalently, in exponential form (most KTU-friendly):

$$\varepsilon_{1-2} = \frac{2 \cdot \left[1 - \exp\left(-\frac{\text{NTU}}{2}\sqrt{1+R^2}\right)\right]}{1 + R + \sqrt{1+R^2} \cdot \frac{1 + \exp(-\text{NTU}\sqrt{1+R^2}/2)}{1 - \exp(-\text{NTU}\sqrt{1+R^2}/2)}}$$

**Hyperbolic simplification:** Using $\coth(x) = (1+e^{-2x})/(1-e^{-2x})$, the result reduces to the textbook form:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{1-2} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{R^2+1}} \cdot \frac{1 - \exp\left[-\text{NTU}\sqrt{R^2+1}/2\right]}{1 + R \cdot \frac{1 + \exp(-\text{NTU}\sqrt{R^2+1}/2)}{1 - \exp(-\text{NTU}\sqrt{R^2+1}/2)}}}$$

**Final Compact Form (Kern's form, used in KTU exams):**

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{1-2} = \frac{1 - \exp[-\text{NTU}(1-R)]}{1 - R \cdot \exp[-\text{NTU}(1-R)]} \cdot \text{(apparent counter-current)} \quad \text{— corrected via } F_T = \frac{\sqrt{R^2+1} \ln\left(\frac{1-R}{1-\varepsilon_{1-2}}\right)}{2R}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Shortcut for KTU Exams:** When solving for $N_t$, students typically use the **LMTD-$F_T$ method** to get area, then $N_t = A/(\pi d_o L)$. NTU is used for **verification** or when phase change makes LMTD undefined.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU-Style, 14-Mark Format)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example: Tubes Required for 1-2 Configuration**
>
> **Given:**
> - Hot oil: $m_h = 2.0 \text{ kg/s}$, $C_{p,h} = 2.1 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$
> - Cold water: $m_c = 1.0 \text{ kg/s}$, $C_{p,c} = 4.18 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$
> - $T_{h,in} = 423 \text{ K}$, $T_{h,out} = 373 \text{ K}$, $T_{c,in} = 303 \text{ K}$
> - Find $T_{c,out}$ from energy balance.
> - Assume $U = 500 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$, tube $d_o = 0.0254 \text{ m}$, $L = 3.0 \text{ m}$.
> - Configuration: **1 shell pass, 2 tube passes**.

**Step 1: Compute the duty $Q$.**

$$Q = m_h C_{p,h} (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$Q = 2.0 \times 2.1 \times (423 - 373) = 2.0 \times 2.1 \times 50 = 210 \text{ kW}$$

This is the **duty** that the exchanger must deliver.

**Step 2: Compute $T_{c,out}$ from energy balance.**

By conservation of energy, the heat lost by the hot stream equals the heat gained by the cold stream:

$$m_h C_{p,h} (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = m_c C_{p,c} (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$$

$$210 = 1.0 \times 4.18 \times (T_{c,out} - 303)$$

Solving for $T_{c,out}$:

$$T_{c,out} - 303 = \frac{210}{4.18} = 50.24 \text{ K}$$

$$T_{c,out} = 303 + 50.24 = 353.24 \text{ K}$$

So the cold water exits at approximately **353.24 K**.

**Step 3: Compute LMTD for pure counter-current flow.**

First, identify the two temperature differences at the ends of the exchanger (counter-current reference):

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 423 - 353.24 = 69.76 \text{ K}$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 373 - 303 = 70.00 \text{ K}$$

Apply the LMTD formula:

$$\Delta T_{LM} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)} = \frac{69.76 - 70.00}{\ln(69.76/70.00)}$$

Numerator:
$$69.76 - 70.00 = -0.24 \text{ K}$$

Denominator:
$$\ln(69.76/70.00) = \ln(0.99657) = -0.003432$$

LMTD:
$$\Delta T_{LM} = \frac{-0.24}{-0.003432} = 69.93 \text{ K}$$

**Step 4: Compute $P$ and $R$ parameters.**

$$P = \frac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}} = \frac{353.24 - 303}{423 - 303} = \frac{50.24}{120} = 0.4187$$

$$R = \frac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}} = \frac{423 - 373}{353.24 - 303} = \frac{50}{50.24} = 0.9952$$

**Step 5: Read $F_T$ from the 1-2 chart (KTU textbook, Fig. 11.20 Kern).**

With $P = 0.4187$ and $R = 0.9952$, on the **1-2 shell pass curve**:

$$F_T \approx 0.88$$

(This is high because the temperature profiles are nearly parallel — both fluids change by similar amounts.)

**Step 6: True mean temperature difference.**

$$\Delta T_M = F_T \cdot \Delta T_{LM} = 0.88 \times 69.93 = 61.54 \text{ K}$$

**Step 7: Required heat transfer area $A$.**

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_M} = \frac{210{,}000}{500 \times 61.54} = \frac{210{,}000}{30{,}770} = 6.825 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 8: Number of tubes.**

$$N_t = \frac{A}{\pi \cdot d_o \cdot L} = \frac{6.825}{\pi \times 0.0254 \times 3.0}$$

Compute denominator:
$$\pi \times 0.0254 \times 3.0 = 0.2394 \text{ m}^2/\text{tube}$$

Then:
$$N_t = \frac{6.825}{0.2394} = 28.51$$

**Round up to 30 tubes** (nearest achievable count for triangular pitch layout on a small shell).

**Step 9: NTU Verification (Optional but recommended in KTU exams).**

For a 1-2 exchanger with $C_r = R = 0.9952$, compute NTU:
$$\text{NTU} = \frac{U \cdot A}{C_{min}} = \frac{500 \times 6.825}{C_{min}}$$

$C_{min} = C_{p,c} \cdot m_c = 4180 \times 1.0 = 4180 \text{ W/K}$

$$\text{NTU} = \frac{500 \times 6.825}{4180} = \frac{3412.5}{4180} = 0.8165$$

Effectiveness predicted:
$$\varepsilon = \frac{Q}{C_{min}(T_{h,in}-T_{c,in})} = \frac{210{,}000}{4180 \times 120} = \frac{210{,}000}{501{,}600} = 0.4187$$

This matches $P = 0.4187$ (definitionally true — confirms energy balance consistency).

**Answer Summary:**

> **Number of tubes required = 30 (rounded from 28.5) for a 1-2 shell-and-tube configuration.**

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix for Different Configurations

| Configuration | $F_T$ Range (typical) | Effectiveness Equation | Use Case |
|---------------|------------------------|------------------------|----------|
| **1-1 (Counter-current)** | $F_T = 1.0$ (by definition) | $\varepsilon = \frac{1-\exp[-\text{NTU}(1-R)]}{1-R\exp[-\text{NTU}(1-R)]}$ | Maximum efficiency |
| **1-2** | $0.75 \le F_T \le 0.95$ | $\varepsilon_{1-2} = \frac{2[1-\exp(-\text{NTU}\sqrt{1+R^2}/2)]}{(1+R)+\sqrt{1+R^2}\coth(\text{NTU}\sqrt{1+R^2}/4)}$ | Most common industrial |
| **1-3, 1-4** | $0.65 \le F_T \le 0.85$ | Multi-segment correction via Bowman chart | Compromise units |
| **2-2, 2-4** | $0.85 \le F_T \le 0.97$ | Treated as two 1-2 units in series | High-recovery duties |
| **3-6, 4-8** | $0.90 \le F_T \le 0.99$ | Multi-shell series approximation | Refinery preheaters |
| **Cross-flow (both unmixed)** | $0.85 \le F_T \le 0.95$ | $\varepsilon = 1 - \exp\{(\text{NTU}^{0.22}/C_r)[\exp(-C_r \text{NTU}^{0.78})-1]\}$ | Air-cooled exchangers |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Number-of-Tubes Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Enunciate Duty] --> B[Step 1: Compute Q from Energy Balance]
    B --> C[Step 2: Estimate U from Tables or Kern Correlation]
    C --> D[Step 3: Calculate LMTM for Pure Counter-Current]
    D --> E[Step 4: Select Configuration 1-2, 2-4, etc.]
    E --> F[Step 5: Compute P and R Parameters]
    F --> G[Step 6: Read F sub T from Chart]
    G --> H{Is F sub T greater than 0.75?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Step 7: Compute Delta T sub M equals F sub T times LMTD]
    H -- No --> J[Step 8: Redesign - Increase Shell Passes]
    J --> E
    I --> K[Step 9: Solve A equals Q divided by U times Delta T sub M]
    K --> L[Step 10: Solve N sub t equals A divided by pi d sub o L]
    L --> M[Step 11: Round to Tube Layout Multiples]
    M --> N[Step 12: NTU Verification]
    N --> O[Final: Confirm Tube Count and Shell Diameter]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Heat Exchanger Configurations

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SP1["1-2 CONFIGURATION"]
        SP1A[Hot In] --> SP1B[Shell with 2 Tube Passes]
        SP1B --> SP1C[Hot Out]
        SP1B <--> SP1D[Cold Path]
        SP1E[Cold In] --> SP1B
        SP1B --> SP1F[Cold Out]
    end

    subgraph SP2["2-4 CONFIGURATION"]
        SP2A[Hot In] --> SP2B[Shell 1 with 2 Tube Passes]
        SP2B --> SP2C[Shell 2 with 2 Tube Passes]
        SP2C --> SP2D[Hot Out]
        SP2E[Cold In] --> SP2B
        SP2F[Cold Out]
    end

    subgraph SP3["1-1 COUNTER-CURRENT"]
        SP3A[Hot In] --> SP3B[Single Tube]
        SP3B --> SP3C[Hot Out]
        SP3E[Cold In] --> SP3B
        SP3B --> SP3F[Cold Out]
    end
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree for Configuration Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Duty Q given] --> S2{Is temperature cross present?}
    S2 -- No --> S3[F sub T close to 1]
    S3 -- Use 1-2 --> Z1[Apply Standard 1-2 Formula]
    S2 -- Yes --> S4[Temperature cross detected]
    S4 --> S5{F sub T acceptable 0.75 or more?}
    S5 -- Yes --> S6[Use 2-4 or 3-6]
    S5 -- No --> S7[Increase passes or add trim heater]
    S6 --> Z2[Apply Multi-shell Series Method]
    S7 --> S8[Redesign Temperature Approach]
```

### 4.4 Functional Flow: LMTD vs. NTU Method (Comparison)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LMTD["LMTD METHOD"]
        L1[Terminal Temperatures] --> L2[Compute LMTD]
        L2 --> L3[Compute P and R]
        L3 --> L4[Read F sub T from Chart]
        L4 --> L5[True MTD]
        L5 --> L6[Area equals Q over U times Delta T]
        L6 --> L7[Number of Tubes]
    end

    subgraph NTU["NTU METHOD"]
        N1[Stream Capacities C sub min, C sub max] --> N2[Compute C sub r]
        N2 --> N3[Guess or compute NTU]
        N3 --> N4[Read epsilon from configuration chart]
        N4 --> N5[Q equals epsilon times C sub min times T sub h in minus T sub c in]
        N5 --> N6[Area equals NTU times C sub min over U]
        N6 --> N7[Number of Tubes]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Q1(a)]**

**Q1(a).** Define the **LMTD correction factor $F_T$** for a shell-and-tube heat exchanger. Why is its value always less than or equal to 1 for a 1-2 configuration?

**Model Answer:**

> The LMTD correction factor $F_T$ is a dimensionless multiplier applied to the counter-current log-mean temperature difference to account for the deviation from pure counter-current flow in multi-pass shell-and-tube exchangers. Mathematically:
> $$\Delta T_M = F_T \cdot \Delta T_{LM}$$
>
> For a 1-2 configuration, the cold fluid reverses direction once per shell pass. The temperature difference along the exchanger varies less uniformly than in pure counter-current flow, leading to a smaller average driving force. Since pure counter-current is the most thermodynamically efficient arrangement (maximum $\Delta T$), any deviation reduces the effective MTD, ensuring $F_T \le 1$.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Q1(b)]**

**Q1(b).** Distinguish between the **LMTD method** and the **NTU method** of heat exchanger analysis. State one advantage of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | LMTD Method | NTU Method |
|--------|-------------|------------|
| Primary Output | Area $A$ for given $Q$ | Effectiveness $\varepsilon$ |
| Key Parameter | $\Delta T_{LM}$ and $F_T$ | $\text{NTU} = UA/C_{min}$ |
| Strength | Direct sizing; simple for counter-current | Handles phase change and unknown outlet $T$ |
| Limitation | Fails when $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$ (e.g., condenser) | Indirect sizing; iterative |

- **Advantage of LMTD:** Direct calculation of area.
- **Advantage of NTU:** Useful when outlet temperatures are not specified (e.g., condensers, evaporators).

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Q4]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): 1-2 Configuration with Detailed LMTD Analysis**

A **1-2 shell-and-tube heat exchanger** is to be designed to cool $25{,}000 \text{ kg/h}$ of oil from $100^\circ\text{C}$ to $50^\circ\text{C}$ using water available at $25^\circ\text{C}$. The water leaves at $40^\circ\text{C}$. Take $C_{p,\text{oil}} = 2.0 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $C_{p,\text{water}} = 4.18 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $U = 500 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$, tube outer diameter $d_o = 25 \text{ mm}$, tube length $L = 3 \text{ m}$.

**Solve:**
- **(a)** [7 Marks] Calculate the number of tubes required for this duty.
- **(b)** [7 Marks] If the same duty must be achieved using a **2-4 configuration** with $F_T = 0.96$, determine the new number of tubes.

---

##### **Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) – 1-2 Configuration:**

**Step 1:** Convert mass flow rate:
$$m_h = 25{,}000/3600 = 6.944 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 2:** Compute duty:
$$Q = m_h \cdot C_{p,h} \cdot (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = 6.944 \times 2000 \times (100 - 50) = 694{,}400 \text{ W} = 694.4 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $T_{c,out}$:
$$m_c = \frac{Q}{C_{p,c}(T_{c,out}-T_{c,in})}; \text{Given } T_{c,out}=40^\circ\text{C}. \text{ Check: } m_c = \frac{694{,}400}{4180 \times 15} = 11.07 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 4:** LMTD (counter-current):
$$\Delta T_1 = 100 - 40 = 60^\circ\text{C}, \quad \Delta T_2 = 50 - 25 = 25^\circ\text{C}$$

$$\Delta T_{LM} = \frac{60-25}{\ln(60/25)} = \frac{35}{\ln(2.4)} = \frac{35}{0.8755} = 39.98^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 5:** Compute $P$ and $R$:
$$P = \frac{40-25}{100-25} = \frac{15}{75} = 0.20$$

$$R = \frac{100-50}{40-25} = \frac{50}{15} = 3.333$$

**Step 6:** Read $F_T$ for 1-2 with $P=0.20$, $R=3.33$:
$$F_T \approx 0.92$$

**Step 7:** True MTD:
$$\Delta T_M = 0.92 \times 39.98 = 36.78^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 8:** Area:
$$A = \frac{694{,}400}{500 \times 36.78} = 37.76 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 9:** Number of tubes:
$$N_t = \frac{37.76}{\pi \times 0.025 \times 3} = \frac{37.76}{0.2356} = 160.3 \approx \mathbf{161 \text{ tubes}}$$

---

**Part (b) – 2-4 Configuration:**

With $F_T = 0.96$ given:
$$\Delta T_M' = 0.96 \times 39.98 = 38.38^\circ\text{C}$$

New area:
$$A' = \frac{694{,}400}{500 \times 38.38} = 36.18 \text{ m}^2$$

New number of tubes:
$$N_t' = \frac{36.18}{0.2356} = 153.6 \approx \mathbf{154 \text{ tubes}}$$

**Comparison:** 2-4 saves about **7 tubes (4.3%)** due to higher $F_T$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Using $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}$ and $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,out}$ (incorrect cross-flow assignment). Always use counter-current reference: $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$ and $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to convert mass flow rate from kg/h to kg/s when $U$ is in W/m²·K.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Not converting $C_p$ from kJ/kg·K to J/kg·K — this leads to a **factor of 1000 error**.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Rounding $N_t$ **down** instead of **up** (you cannot have 160.3 tubes — round to 161).
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Using $F_T = 1.0$ for non-counter-current configurations. Loss: **2 marks minimum**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): NTU Method Verification of Tube Count**

A **shell-and-tube heat exchanger** has $N_t = 200$ tubes, each of outer diameter $25 \text{ mm}$ and length $3 \text{ m}$. The hot stream enters at $150^\circ\text{C}$ and leaves at $90^\circ\text{C}$. The cold stream enters at $30^\circ\text{C}$ and leaves at $70^\circ\text{C}$. Hot stream mass flow $= 5 \text{ kg/s}$, $C_{p,h} = 2.5 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, cold stream $C_{p,c} = 4.18 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $U = 600 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$, configuration: **1 shell, 2 tube passes**.

**Solve:**
- **(a)** [7 Marks] Verify the number of tubes using the **NTU method** (assume 1-2 effectiveness equation) and check whether $N_t = 200$ is sufficient.
- **(b)** [7 Marks] If $C_{p,c}$ were halved (cold flow becomes more viscous), what is the new minimum number of tubes required? Discuss the physical reason.

---

##### **Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) – NTU Verification:**

**Step 1:** Compute capacities:
$$C_h = 5 \times 2500 = 12{,}500 \text{ W/K}$$
$$C_c = \frac{Q}{T_{c,out}-T_{c,in}}$$
$$Q = 12{,}500 \times (150-90) = 750{,}000 \text{ W} = 750 \text{ kW}$$
$$C_c = \frac{750{,}000}{70-30} = 18{,}750 \text{ W/K}$$

**Step 2:** Identify $C_{min}$ and $C_{max}$:
$$C_{min} = 12{,}500 \text{ W/K} = C_h$$
$$C_{max} = 18{,}750 \text{ W/K} = C_c$$
$$C_r = C_{min}/C_{max} = 12{,}500/18{,}750 = 0.6667$$

**Step 3:** Actual area:
$$A = N_t \cdot \pi \cdot d_o \cdot L = 200 \times \pi \times 0.025 \times 3 = 47.12 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4:** Compute NTU:
$$\text{NTU} = \frac{U \cdot A}{C_{min}} = \frac{600 \times 47.12}{12{,}500} = \frac{28{,}272}{12{,}500} = 2.2618$$

**Step 5:** Effectiveness for 1-2 configuration:

Using the KTU formula:
$$\varepsilon_{1-2} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{R^2+1}} \cdot \frac{1 - \exp[-\text{NTU}\sqrt{R^2+1}/2]}{1 + R \cdot \frac{1 + \exp(-\text{NTU}\sqrt{R^2+1}/2)}{1 - \exp(-\text{NTU}\sqrt{R^2+1}/2)}}$$

Substituting $R = C_r = 0.6667$ and $\text{NTU} = 2.2618$:

$$\sqrt{R^2+1} = \sqrt{0.4444+1} = \sqrt{1.4444} = 1.2018$$

$$\frac{\text{NTU}\sqrt{R^2+1}}{2} = \frac{2.2618 \times 1.2018}{2} = 1.3596$$

$$\exp(-1.3596) = 0.2567$$

$$\frac{1-\exp(-\cdot)}{2/\sqrt{R^2+1}} = \frac{1 - 0.2567}{2/1.2018} = \frac{0.7433}{1.6641} = 0.4466$$

$$1 + R \cdot \frac{1+\exp}{1-\exp} = 1 + 0.6667 \cdot \frac{1.2567}{0.7433} = 1 + 0.6667 \cdot 1.6904 = 1 + 1.1270 = 2.1270$$

$$\varepsilon_{1-2} = 0.4466 / 2.1270 = 0.2100$$

**Step 6:** Required effectiveness:
$$\varepsilon_{req} = \frac{Q}{C_{min}(T_{h,in}-T_{c,in})} = \frac{750{,}000}{12{,}500 \times (150-30)} = \frac{750{,}000}{1{,}500{,}000} = 0.50$$

**Step 7:** Compare:
$$\varepsilon_{actual} = 0.21 < \varepsilon_{req} = 0.50$$

**Conclusion:** With $N_t = 200$, the exchanger delivers only **21% effectiveness** but **50% is required**. **N_t is INSUFFICIENT.** Need to increase tubes.

**Required new area (from NTU back-calculation):**
$$A_{req} = \frac{\text{NTU}_{req} \cdot C_{min}}{U}$$

Solve for $\text{NTU}_{req}$ such that $\varepsilon = 0.50$ with $R = 0.6667$:

By iteration, $\text{NTU}_{req} \approx 4.5$ gives $\varepsilon \approx 0.50$.

$$A_{req} = \frac{4.5 \times 12{,}500}{600} = 93.75 \text{ m}^2$$

$$N_{t,req} = \frac{93.75}{0.2356} = 397.9 \approx \mathbf{398 \text{ tubes}}$$

**Answer:** $N_t = 200$ is **insufficient**. Need **at least 398 tubes** for the 1-2 configuration.

---

**Part (b) – Halved $C_{p,c}$:**

If $C_{p,c}$ is halved, then $C_c = 9{,}375 \text{ W/K}$. Now:
$$C_{min} = 9{,}375 \text{ W/K} = C_c$$
$$C_{max} = 12{,}500 \text{ W/K} = C_h$$
$$C_r = 9{,}375/12{,}500 = 0.75$$

The cold stream has less capacity to absorb heat. The hot stream exits at a higher temperature (less cooling). Maximum possible duty:
$$Q_{max} = C_{min}(T_{h,in}-T_{c,in}) = 9{,}375 \times 120 = 1{,}125{,}000 \text{ W}$$

But actual $Q$ stays the same (heat available from hot stream = 750 kW if outlet $T$ unchanged). However, the cold outlet shifts. Required effectiveness:
$$\varepsilon_{req} = \frac{750{,}000}{1{,}125{,}000} = 0.667$$

With $R = 0.75$ and $\varepsilon = 0.667$, NTU from chart $\approx 5.5$:
$$A_{req} = \frac{5.5 \times 9{,}375}{600} = 85.94 \text{ m}^2$$
$$N_{t,req} = \frac{85.94}{0.2356} = 364.8 \approx \mathbf{365 \text{ tubes}}$$

**Physical Reason:** When the cold stream's heat capacity drops, the cold fluid heats up faster, so the temperature approach at the cold-outlet end becomes small, reducing the local $\Delta T$. The exchanger needs more area (more tubes) to maintain the same duty. The temperature cross becomes more pronounced, demanding more surface to compensate.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST**

- ✅ **Duty $Q$** is fixed by energy balance; $Q = m C_p \Delta T$ on either side.
- ✅ **LMTD** assumes pure counter-current: $\Delta T_{LM} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)/\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$.
- ✅ **$F_T$ correction** accounts for non-counter-current flow; always read from chart for the chosen pass arrangement.
- ✅ **$F_T < 0.75$** means **redesign** (increase shell passes or shift temperatures).
- ✅ **Number of tubes** $N_t = A/(\pi d_o L)$ — always round **up** to nearest layout multiple.
- ✅ **NTU method** uses $\text{NTU} = UA/C_{min}$, $C_r = C_{min}/C_{max}$, and configuration-specific $\varepsilon$.
- ✅ **1-2 configuration** is most common; **2-4** gives higher $F_T$ but adds cost.
- ✅ **Temperature cross** (where $T_{h,out} < T_{c,out}$) severely reduces $F_T$; avoid if possible.
- ✅ **Counter-current ($F_T = 1$)** is the **theoretical maximum efficiency** baseline.
- ✅ **Mass flow consistency:** Use SI units throughout (kg/s, W, J/kg·K, m²·K/W).
- ✅ **Effectiveness** is bounded: $\varepsilon \le 1$ for finite exchangers.
- ✅ **Hot fluid generally goes through tubes** (less fouling, easier cleaning) — KTU standard practice.
- ✅ **Tube pitch layout** (triangular 30° gives most tubes; square 90° gives easier cleaning).

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Tip:** When solving for $N_t$ in the exam, write down all **7 steps explicitly** (energy balance → LMTD → $P,R$ → $F_T$ → $\Delta T_M$ → $A$ → $N_t$). Each step carries marks; skipping earns deductions. Show your $F_T$ chart value even if it's approximate.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Problem Solving in Heat Exchanger Design Using Excel/Matlab/Scilab/Python (Only for Self-Study/Microproject/Assignment)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT402 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Problem Solving in Heat Exchanger Design Using Excel/Matlab/Scilab/Python (Only for Self-Study/Microproject/Assignment)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for **PCCHT402 – Heat Transfer Operations**, Module 4 culminates in the computational resolution of **Heat Exchanger Design Problems**. A Heat Exchanger is a unit operation device whose purpose is the controlled transfer of thermal energy between two or more fluids separated by a solid wall. "Problem Solving" in this context refers to the systematic application of the **Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)** method and the **Effectiveness–Number of Transfer Units (ε–NTU)** method — combined with iterative numerical routines — to execute **Rating** (determining outlet temperatures for a specified exchanger) and **Sizing** (determining the heat transfer area and tube count for a specified duty) calculations.

Computational tools such as **Excel** (solvers, Goal Seek, VBA macros), **MATLAB** (scripting, `fzero`, `fsolve`), **Scilab** (open-source equivalent), or **Python** (SciPy, NumPy libraries) are employed to handle the non-linear, iterative calculations that arise due to the temperature dependence of physical properties, the iterative nature of pressure drop corrections, and the use of correction factors for multi-pass shell-and-tube exchangers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Note:** This topic is officially designated as **"Only for Self-Study / Microproject / Assignment"** under the 2024 scheme. Direct coding questions are NOT asked in the End Semester Examination (ESE). However, the design methodology and numerical problem-solving approach form the analytical backbone of the entire Module 4. Therefore, the calculations, equations, and procedures described here are **fully examinable** as Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Thermostat" Intuition

Imagine you are an architect designing a heating system for a swimming pool. You need to install solar panels (the "hot fluid" side) that transfer heat to the pool water (the "cold fluid" side) through pipes (the heat exchanger tubes). Before construction, you must calculate:
1. **How big** the panels should be (Area, A)
2. **How many pipes** you need (Number of tubes, N)
3. **How fast** the water should flow to extract enough heat (Mass flow rate, ṁ)

Doing these calculations by hand for a single pass is straightforward, but the **real-world problem is iterative**: if you change the pipe diameter, the flow velocity changes, which changes the heat transfer coefficient, which changes the required area, which may force you to recalculate the pipe count! Computational tools automate this "trial-and-improve" loop — exactly as a smart thermostat iteratively adjusts a room's temperature by reading the current value and re-evaluating the heating/cooling effort required.

## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics in Heat Exchanger Design

The following standard metrics are central to KTU-level design problems:

- **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)** — typically **300 – 1500 W/m²·K** for liquid-liquid exchangers, **50 – 500 W/m²·K** for gas-liquid, and **500 – 5000 W/m²·K** for condensers/boilers.
- **Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)** — the thermodynamically correct driving force for counter-current flow.
- **Correction Factor (F)** — accounts for the deviation from true counter-current flow in multi-pass shell-and-tube units.
- **Effectiveness (ε)** — the ratio of actual heat transfer to the maximum possible heat transfer.
- **Number of Transfer Units (NTU)** — a dimensionless group that characterizes the heat exchanger's capacity.
- **Pressure Drop (ΔP)** — must satisfy mechanical design constraints (typically **≤ 10 psi for liquid services** in shell, **≤ 2 psi in tubes**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used:**
> - Acceleration due to gravity, **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - Stefan–Boltzmann constant, **σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴** (not used in this convective module but listed for completeness)
> - Universal Gas Constant, **R = 8.314 J/mol·K**
> - Standard atmospheric pressure, **P_atm = 101.325 kPa**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars of Heat Exchanger Design Methodology

The computational problem-solving approach rests on **two fundamental methods** which the KTU examiner expects every student to recognize and apply:

### Pillar A: LMTD Method (used for Sizing)
Applicable when both inlet and outlet temperatures of the two streams are **known or specified**.

1. Compute the **heat duty (Q)** from the energy balance on either the hot or cold stream.
2. Compute the **LMTD** using the counter-current temperature differences.
3. For multi-pass exchangers, compute the **correction factor (F)**.
4. Apply the core design equation: **A = Q / (U × F × LMTD)**.
5. Convert the area to a **tube count and bundle layout**.

### Pillar B: ε–NTU Method (used for Rating)
Applicable when **only inlet temperatures are known** and the exchanger area is specified (i.e., "rating an existing unit").

1. Compute the **heat capacity rates** (C = ṁ × Cp) for both streams.
2. Identify **C_min** and **C_max**; compute the **heat capacity ratio (c = C_min / C_max)**.
3. Compute **NTU = UA / C_min**.
4. Use the appropriate **ε–NTU correlation** (depends on flow arrangement: counter-flow, parallel-flow, shell-and-tube N-shell, cross-flow, etc.) to find **ε**.
5. Compute the **actual heat transfer: Q = ε × C_min × (T_h,in − T_c,in)**.
6. Back-calculate the **outlet temperatures** from the energy balance.

## 2.2 Why Iteration Is Required in Real Design

The LMTD method is **not directly invertible** for rating problems. Moreover, the Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient **U is itself a function of the flow regime (Reynolds number)**, which depends on the **tube velocity**, which depends on the **tube count**, which depends on the **area** you are trying to find! This circular dependency is why computational tools (Excel Solver, MATLAB's `fsolve`, etc.) are essential — they perform the iterative convergence.

The iterative sequence for **detailed design** is:

- **Step 1:** Assume an initial value of U (based on service type from literature).
- **Step 2:** Compute area A from LMTD equation.
- **Step 3:** Convert area to tube count N (assuming tube diameter, pitch, and length).
- **Step 4:** Compute tube-side and shell-side velocities from the flow rates.
- **Step 5:** Compute new Reynolds numbers → new h_i and h_o → new U.
- **Step 6:** Recalculate area with corrected U. Repeat Steps 2–5 until **convergence (tolerance ≈ 1%)** is achieved.
- **Step 7:** Compute pressure drops; verify against allowable limits.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Definition | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \dot{m}_h C_{p,h} \Delta T_h = \dot{m}_c C_{p,c} \Delta T_c$ | Heat duty, mass flow, specific heat | W, kg/s, J/kg·K, K |
| 2 | $Q = U A F \Delta T_{LM}$ | Core LMTD design equation | W, W/m²·K, m², K |
| 3 | $\Delta T_{LM} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log Mean Temperature Difference | K |
| 4 | $F = \dfrac{\sqrt{R^2+1}\,\ln\!\left[\dfrac{1-R}{1-PR}\right]}{(R-1)\,\ln\!\left[\dfrac{2-P(R+1)-\sqrt{R^2+1-P(R+1)+1}}{2-P(R+1)+\sqrt{R^2+1-P(R+1)+1}}\right]}$ | Correction factor (1-2, 2-4 exch.) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $P = \dfrac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}}$, $\quad R = \dfrac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}$ | LMTD parameters | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_o} + \dfrac{d_o \ln(d_o/d_i)}{2 k_w} + \dfrac{d_o}{d_i h_i} + R_{d,o} + R_{d,i}\dfrac{d_o}{d_i}$ | Overall resistance (tube wall + fouling) | m²·K/W |
| 7 | $C = \dot{m} C_p$, $\quad c = C_{min}/C_{max}$ | Heat capacity rate & ratio | W/K, dimensionless |
| 8 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{C_{min}(T_{h,in}-T_{c,in})}$ | Effectiveness | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{NTU} = \dfrac{U A}{C_{min}}$ | Number of Transfer Units | dimensionless |
| 10 | Counter-flow: $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-NTU(1-c)]}{1 - c \exp[-NTU(1-c)]}$ | ε–NTU relation (counter) | dimensionless |
| 11 | Parallel-flow: $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-NTU(1+c)]}{1 + c}$ | ε–NTU relation (parallel) | dimensionless |
| 12 | Shell & tube (1-2): $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1}{c}\!\left[\sqrt{1+c^2}\,\dfrac{1+\exp(-\text{NTU}\sqrt{1+c^2})}{1-\exp(-\text{NTU}\sqrt{1+c^2})} - 1\right] \cdot \dfrac{1}{2}$ | ε–NTU relation (1-2) | dimensionless |
| 13 | $\Delta P_f = 4 f \dfrac{L}{D}\dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ (Darcy–Weisbach form) | Frictional pressure drop | Pa |
| 14 | $N_t = \dfrac{A}{\pi d_o L}$ | Number of tubes from area | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on KTU Examinations:** The F-factor expression in row 4 looks intimidating, but in KTU 2024 scheme problems, the examiner either (a) supplies the F-value directly, (b) gives a 1-2 exchanger with simple temperatures where F can be read from a chart, or (c) tells you to assume **F ≈ 0.9** as a default. Do not waste time deriving F in the exam unless the F value is missing AND the problem explicitly demands its calculation.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Heat exchanger design using computational iteration is the **bread-and-butter of every chemical, petrochemical, and power plant engineer**. Industries relying on this methodology include:

- **Oil refineries** — crude preheat trains (saving 30% furnace fuel cost).
- **Power plants** — condensers, feedwater heaters, and boiler economizers.
- **HVAC systems** — chilled water and cooling coil design.
- **Cryogenic gas processing** — LNG heat exchangers (complex multi-stream units).
- **Food and pharmaceutical industries** — pasteurizers and sterilizers (sanitary design).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the LMTD Expression (Quick Recap for KTU)

The LMTD is derived by integrating the differential heat transfer equation over the length of the exchanger, assuming **constant U** and **constant C_p** along the length. The local differential heat transfer is:

$$
dQ = U\, dA\, \Delta T
$$

For a counter-current exchanger, applying the energy balance to a differential element yields:

$$
d(\Delta T) = -\Delta T\, dA \left(\dfrac{1}{C_h} + \dfrac{1}{C_c}\right)
$$

Integrating between the two ends (where ΔT changes from ΔT_1 to ΔT_2):

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = -UA\left(\dfrac{1}{C_h} + \dfrac{1}{C_c}\right)
$$

Since $Q = U A \Delta T_{LM}$ and $Q = C_h (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = C_c (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$, algebraic rearrangement gives:

$$
\Delta T_{LM} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}
$$

with the inlet-end and outlet-end counter-current temperature differences defined as:

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}, \quad \Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}
$$

## 3.2 KTU Standard Worked Example (Sizing Problem)

This is the **archetypal KTU question** the examiner loves to ask. The computational tool is used in real practice to handle the iterations, but in the exam, one or two iterations by hand are usually sufficient.

> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
> A 1-2 shell-and-tube heat exchanger is to be sized to cool **10,000 kg/h of an oil** (specific heat $C_{p,h} = 2.0$ kJ/kg·K) from **100 °C to 50 °C** using cooling water (specific heat $C_{p,c} = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K) available at **25 °C** with a maximum permissible temperature rise of **10 °C**. The overall heat transfer coefficient based on the outside area is $U_o = 500$ W/m²·K. Use **19.05 mm OD × 16 mm ID** tubes of length **3 m**, arranged on a **25.4 mm triangular pitch**. The correction factor for the 1-2 arrangement is $F = 0.90$. Calculate:
> (a) The heat duty Q
> (b) The mass flow rate of cooling water
> (c) The log mean temperature difference (LMTD)
> (d) The required heat transfer area and number of tubes
> (e) Verify that the correction factor used is consistent (i.e., F ≥ 0.75 acceptable for design).

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step (a): Heat Duty Q**

$$
\dot{m}_h = \dfrac{10{,}000 \text{ kg/h}}{3600 \text{ s/h}} = 2.778 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
Q = \dot{m}_h\, C_{p,h}\, \Delta T_h
$$

$$
Q = 2.778 \times 2.0 \times (100 - 50) = 277.78 \text{ kW} = 277{,}780 \text{ W}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The oil mass flow is given per hour, so we divide by 3600 to convert to SI (kg/s). The specific heat is in kJ/kg·K, so multiplying by 1000 internally is implicit when we report Q in kW.

**Step (b): Cooling Water Mass Flow Rate**

$$
\dot{m}_c = \dfrac{Q}{C_{p,c}\, \Delta T_c} = \dfrac{277.78}{4.18 \times 10} = 6.645 \text{ kg/s}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** $Q$ is in kW (≡ kJ/s), $C_p$ is in kJ/kg·K, ΔT is in K, so the result is in kg/s. The water outlet temperature is $T_{c,out} = 25 + 10 = 35\,°\text{C}$.

**Step (c): LMTD Calculation**

First, identify the four terminal temperatures for **counter-current reference** (this is the KTU trick — always use counter-current as the baseline):

$$
T_{h,in} = 100\,°\text{C}, \quad T_{h,out} = 50\,°\text{C}, \quad T_{c,in} = 25\,°\text{C}, \quad T_{c,out} = 35\,°\text{C}
$$

For a 1-2 exchanger, the true counter-current temperature differences are:

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 100 - 35 = 65\,°\text{C}
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 50 - 25 = 25\,°\text{C}
$$

Apply the LMTD formula:

$$
\Delta T_{LM} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = \dfrac{65 - 25}{\ln(65/25)} = \dfrac{40}{\ln(2.6)}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{LM} = \dfrac{40}{0.9555} = 41.86\,°\text{C}
$$

**Step (d): Heat Transfer Area and Tube Count**

Since this is a 1-2 exchanger, apply the correction factor:

$$
Q = U_o\, A\, F\, \Delta T_{LM}
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{Q}{U_o\, F\, \Delta T_{LM}} = \dfrac{277{,}780}{500 \times 0.90 \times 41.86}
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{277{,}780}{18{,}837} = 14.747 \text{ m}^2
$$

Number of tubes required (using $A = N_t \pi d_o L$):

$$
N_t = \dfrac{A}{\pi d_o L} = \dfrac{14.747}{\pi \times 0.01905 \times 3} = \dfrac{14.747}{0.1795} = 82.16
$$

$$
\boxed{N_t \approx 83 \text{ tubes (round up to integer)}}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** Outer diameter is converted from 19.05 mm to 0.01905 m. The area of a single tube's outer cylindrical surface is $\pi d_o L$. The bundle area is $N_t$ times that.

**Step (e): Verification of F-Factor**

The P and R parameters for the LMTD correction factor formula are:

$$
P = \dfrac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}} = \dfrac{35 - 25}{100 - 25} = \dfrac{10}{75} = 0.1333
$$

$$
R = \dfrac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}} = \dfrac{100 - 50}{35 - 25} = \dfrac{50}{10} = 5.0
$$

For a 1-2 shell-and-tube exchanger, the F-factor can be looked up on a standard chart (Kern's correlation). For $P = 0.133$ and $R = 5.0$, the chart typically gives $F \approx 0.88 - 0.92$, which **matches the assumed F = 0.90** → design is consistent. The value $F \geq 0.75$ is acceptable per TEMA standards.

### 3.2.2 Iteration Discussion (How a Computational Tool Solves This)

The above is a **simplified single-pass design**. In real practice, the following iterations are required:

- **Iteration 1:** Assume $U_o = 500$ W/m²·K → compute $A = 14.75$ m² → $N_t = 83$ tubes.
- **Iteration 2:** Compute tube-side velocity: $v_t = \dot{m}_c / (\rho_c \times N_t \times \pi d_i^2 / 4)$. With $N_t = 83$, $d_i = 0.016$ m, $\rho_c = 995$ kg/m³:
  $$
  v_t = \dfrac{6.645}{995 \times 83 \times \pi \times (0.016)^2 / 4} = \dfrac{6.645}{1.661} = 4.00 \text{ m/s}
  $$
- **Iteration 3:** Re = $\rho v d / \mu$. For water at 30 °C, $\mu \approx 0.8 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s:
  $$
  Re_t = \dfrac{995 \times 4.00 \times 0.016}{0.8 \times 10^{-3}} = 79{,}600 \quad (\text{turbulent flow})
  $$
- **Iteration 4:** Use Dittus–Boelter (heating) or Sieder–Tate correlation to find new $h_i$. With $Re = 79{,}600$ and $Pr \approx 5.4$, $Nu \approx 0.023 Re^{0.8} Pr^{0.4} \approx 0.023 \times 79600^{0.8} \times 5.4^{0.4} \approx 0.023 \times 10{,}775 \times 1.93 \approx 478$.
  $$
  h_i = \dfrac{Nu \cdot k}{d_i} = \dfrac{478 \times 0.615}{0.016} = 18{,}380 \text{ W/m²·K}
  $$
- **Iteration 5:** Re-evaluate $U_o$ using the resistance equation. The result typically shifts U from 500 to perhaps 600 W/m²·K, which would reduce A to 12.3 m² → tube count drops to ~69. The student then re-runs the velocity and U calculations — a loop that converges in 3-5 iterations. **This is precisely where Excel Solver or MATLAB `fsolve` becomes indispensable.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Takeaway:** The KTU examiner may ask you to perform **one iteration** of the U-correction manually to demonstrate you understand the loop. They will NOT ask you to write actual code, but they may ask for the **pseudocode or algorithm** of the iterative loop as a 3-mark Part A question.

## 3.3 Derivation of Effectiveness (ε) for Counter-Flow Exchanger

This is a **high-yield derivation** for KTU, often appearing in the 7-mark sub-part of Part B questions.

**Starting energy balance for a counter-flow exchanger:**

For a differential element, the heat transferred is:

$$
dQ = -C_h\, dT_h = C_c\, dT_c
$$

The total heat transferred across the entire area is:

$$
Q = C_h (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = C_c (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})
$$

The local temperature difference at any point satisfies:

$$
\dfrac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U\, dA \left(\dfrac{1}{C_h} + \dfrac{1}{C_c}\right)
$$

Integrating with boundary conditions $\Delta T_{in} = T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}$ and $\Delta T_{out} = T_{h,out} - T_{c,out}$:

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_{out}}{\Delta T_{in}}\right) = -UA\left(\dfrac{1}{C_h} + \dfrac{1}{C_c}\right)
$$

Substituting $\Delta T_{in} = T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}$ and $\Delta T_{out} = T_{h,out} - T_{c,out}$, and using $C_{min} = C_h$ and $C_{max} = C_c$ (assume $C_h \leq C_c$):

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_{h,out} - T_{c,out}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}}\right) = -\dfrac{UA}{C_h}\left(1 + \dfrac{C_h}{C_c}\right) = -\text{NTU}(1+c)
$$

Now apply the effectiveness definition: $\varepsilon = \dfrac{C_h (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})}{C_{min}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})} = \dfrac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}}$

By energy balance: $C_h(T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = C_c(T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$, so $T_{c,out} = T_{c,in} + c(T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})$.

Substitute and simplify (this is the **standard textbook manipulation**):

$$
T_{h,out} - T_{c,out} = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} - c(T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})
$$

$$
= (1+c)T_{h,out} - c T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}
$$

After further algebraic manipulation (combining with the integrated log relation) and rearranging:

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-\text{NTU}(1-c)]}{1 - c \exp[-\text{NTU}(1-c)]}}
$$

This is the **counter-flow ε–NTU relation** used universally in KTU problems.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Heat Exchanger Design Workflow

The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates the complete computational workflow a student or engineer follows when solving a heat exchanger design problem using Excel, MATLAB, Scilab, or Python. The diagram intentionally abstracts away from the physical geometry of the exchanger and instead focuses on the **logical/algorithmic flow** of inputs, computations, decision points, and outputs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT BLOCK: Process Specifications]:::input
    B[Hot Stream Data: m_dot_h, Cp_h, Th_in, Th_out]:::input
    C[Cold Stream Data: m_dot_c, Cp_c, Tc_in, Delta_Tc]:::input
    D[Initial Estimate: U_assumed, F_factor, tube specs]:::input

    E{Core Energy Balance}:::core
    F[Compute Q from hot stream Q = m_h Cp_h Delta_Th]:::calc
    G[Compute m_c from cold side m_c = Q / Cp_c Delta_Tc]:::calc

    H{LMTD Method Block}:::core
    I[Compute delta_T1, delta_T2, LMTD]:::calc
    J[Compute area A = Q / U F LMTD]:::calc
    K[Compute tube count Nt = A / pi do L]:::calc

    L{Iteration: Re-evaluate U}:::iter
    M[Compute tube velocity v_t = m_c / rho N pi di2 4]:::calc
    N[Compute Re and Pr, then h_i via Dittus-Boelter]:::calc
    O[Compute new U using resistance equation]:::calc
    P{U converged within tolerance?}:::decide
    Q[YES: Proceed to Pressure Drop Check]:::route
    R[NO: Loop back with new U to LMTD block]:::route

    S{Pressure Drop Check}:::core
    T[Compute tube side delta_Pt]:::calc
    U[Compute shell side delta_Ps]:::calc
    V{delta_P within allowable limits?}:::decide
    W[YES: Finalize Design Specs]:::output
    X[NO: Adjust tube count or length, iterate]:::route

    Y[OUTPUT BLOCK: Final Design Report]:::output
    Z[Area A, Tube Count Nt, Tubes per Pass, Shell Diameter, Pressure Drops]:::output

    A --> B
    A --> C
    A --> D
    B --> E
    C --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    E --> H
    H --> I
    I --> J
    J --> K
    K --> L
    D --> L
    L --> M
    M --> N
    N --> O
    O --> P
    P --> Q
    P --> R
    R --> H
    Q --> S
    S --> T
    T --> U
    U --> V
    V --> W
    V --> X
    X --> L
    W --> Y
    Y --> Z

    classDef input fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#0C4A6E
    classDef core fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#78350F
    classDef calc fill:#D1FAE5,stroke:#047857,stroke-width:2px,color:#064E3B
    classDef decide fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,stroke-width:2px,color:#831843
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree: When to Use LMTD vs ε–NTU

The following **Mermaid decision tree** captures the heuristic the KTU examiner tests in 3-mark conceptual questions. It is **memorize-able** and often appears in the exam as "State with justification whether you would use the LMTD method or the ε–NTU method to solve this problem."

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Problem Statement Given]:::input
    B{Are all four terminal temperatures known?}:::decide
    C[Use LMTD METHOD for SIZING: Find A, Nt]:::route
    D{Are only inlet temperatures AND area known?}:::decide
    E[Use epsilon-NTU METHOD for RATING: Find Q, Th_out, Tc_out]:::route
    F{Is the problem PHASE-CHANGE related?}:::decide
    G[Use LMTD with constant Tsat for condenser / boiler]:::route
    H{Is the problem a multi-stream plate-fin type?}:::decide
    I[Use epsilon-NTU with multi-stream matrix method]:::route

    A --> B
    B -- YES --> C
    B -- NO --> D
    D -- YES --> E
    D -- NO --> F
    F -- YES --> G
    F -- NO --> H
    H -- YES --> I
    H -- NO --> E

    classDef input fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#0C4A6E
    classDef decide fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,stroke-width:2px,color:#831843

```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to Use the Diagram in the Exam:** The Mermaid diagrams above are NOT what you draw in the exam. They are **study aids** to internalize the workflow. In the exam, you would sketch a simple **flowchart** (rectangles for processes, diamonds for decisions, arrows for flow) on paper. The KTU examiner awards 1 mark for logical structure, 1 mark for correct labels, and 1 mark for proper decisions/loops in such flowchart questions.

## 4.3 Physical Schematic Description (Textual)

Since a literal Mermaid drawing of a 1-2 shell-and-tube heat exchanger is impractical, here is the **detailed textual schematic** of the unit being designed in the worked example:

- **Shell-side (hot oil):** Enters at the **top-left nozzle** of the cylindrical shell at $T_{h,in} = 100\,°\text{C}$. Flows horizontally across the tube bundle in **two passes** (hence 1-2 designation), guided by horizontal baffles spaced approximately **0.3 × shell diameter** apart. Exits at the **bottom-left nozzle** at $T_{h,out} = 50\,°\text{C}$.
- **Tube-side (cold water):** Enters at the **front-end header (stationary tube sheet)** at the right side at $T_{c,in} = 25\,°\text{C}$. Flows through the **first pass** of tubes (left-to-right), enters the **rear-end header (channel)** that redirects the flow, returns through the **second pass** of tubes (right-to-left), and exits at $T_{c,out} = 35\,°\text{C}$ from the front-end header.
- **Tube bundle:** 83 tubes, each 19.05 mm OD × 16 mm ID, length 3 m, arranged on a **25.4 mm triangular pitch** with **5 tube passes** per shell pass, on a **tube sheet of diameter ≈ 0.39 m** (estimated from tube count and pitch).
- **Baffles:** Segmental baffles with **25% cut**, providing cross-flow on the shell side.
