# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question, Module 4]**
> **Differentiate clearly between the LMTD method and the ε–NTU method for solving heat exchanger problems. State one situation where each method is preferred.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | LMTD Method | ε–NTU Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary use** | **Sizing** (find area A) | **Rating** (find outlet temperatures) |
| **Required data** | All four terminal temperatures known | Only inlet temperatures + area A |
| **Iteration needed** | Not iterative in basic form | Iterative to find unknown U often |
| **Difficulty with phase change** | Handles phase change well (constant ΔT) | Less convenient for phase change |
| **Preferred when** | Design of a new exchanger; all temps specified | Performance check of existing unit; or for multi-stream / cross-flow / non-counter arrangements |

**Marking scheme (KTU pattern):**
- 1 mark for clear differentiation of primary use.
- 1 mark for stating the required data difference.
- 1 mark for correctly identifying one application scenario for each method.

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question, Module 4]**
> **What is the correction factor (F) in a multi-pass shell-and-tube heat exchanger? Why is it required, and what is the minimum acceptable value for design per TEMA standards?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **correction factor F** is a dimensionless multiplier applied to the LMTD (calculated assuming pure counter-current flow) to account for the deviation from true counter-current behavior in **multi-pass shell-and-tube exchangers** (such as 1-2, 2-4, etc.) where the flow is a mixture of co-current and counter-current within a single shell.

It is **required** because the LMTD formula is derived under the ideal counter-current assumption, but real multi-pass exchangers cannot achieve this ideal. Using the uncorrected LMTD would lead to **over-sizing** the exchanger (predicting a larger area than actually needed).

The **minimum acceptable value** of F for design, per **TEMA (Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association)** standards, is **F ≥ 0.75**. If F falls below 0.75, the heat exchanger configuration is considered thermally inefficient and should be redesigned (e.g., change shell passes, or switch to a pure counter-current or 1-1 unit).

**Marking scheme (KTU pattern):**
- 1 mark for defining F.
- 1 mark for explaining why it is needed.
- 1 mark for stating the F ≥ 0.75 TEMA limit.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Note:** Each Part B question features internal sub-parts **(a) [7 marks]** and **(b) [7 marks]**, following the standard KTU 2024 scheme format.

### Question A (Option 1)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, 14 Marks]**
> **(a) [7 marks]** A counter-current double-pipe heat exchanger is used to condense steam at **120 °C** and cool the condensate to **60 °C** using cooling water entering at **25 °C** and leaving at **45 °C**. The overall heat transfer coefficient is $U = 1000$ W/m²·K. Calculate:
> (i) The log mean temperature difference (LMTD).
> (ii) The heat transfer area required if the heat duty is **50 kW**.
> (iii) The length of pipe required if the inner pipe has an OD of **50 mm**.

> **(b) [7 marks]** Using a computational tool (Excel Solver or equivalent), describe the **step-by-step algorithm** you would use to:
> (i) Verify the LMTD-based area for the above problem.
> (ii) Re-evaluate the area if the actual overall heat transfer coefficient is later found to be **800 W/m²·K** instead of 1000 W/m²·K.
> (iii) Discuss why such iterative procedures are essential in real heat exchanger design.

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Sub-parts (i), (ii), (iii):**

**Sub-part (i): LMTD Calculation**

For a counter-current exchanger with the given temperatures:

- $T_{h,in} = 120\,°\text{C}$ (steam/condensate inlet)
- $T_{h,out} = 60\,°\text{C}$ (condensate outlet)
- $T_{c,in} = 25\,°\text{C}$ (water inlet)
- $T_{c,out} = 45\,°\text{C}$ (water outlet)

Counter-current temperature differences:

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 120 - 45 = 75\,°\text{C}
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 60 - 25 = 35\,°\text{C}
$$

Apply LMTD:

$$
\Delta T_{LM} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = \dfrac{75 - 35}{\ln(75/35)} = \dfrac{40}{\ln(2.1429)}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{LM} = \dfrac{40}{0.7621} = 52.49\,°\text{C}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** Since this is a **pure counter-current** exchanger, **F = 1.0**, and no correction factor is needed. Note that the problem mentions "condensing steam," but the phase change occurs only at the start; the bulk of the problem is sensible cooling, so the LMTD approach is valid.

**Sub-part (ii): Heat Transfer Area**

For a counter-current exchanger, $F = 1$:

$$
A = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LM}} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{1000 \times 52.49} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{52{,}490} = 0.9525 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{A \approx 0.953 \text{ m}^2}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** Q is converted from 50 kW to 50,000 W. The LMTD is in K (numerically equal to °C, so the unit is consistent).

**Sub-part (iii): Pipe Length**

For a double-pipe exchanger, the heat transfer area is the outer surface of the inner pipe:

$$
A = \pi d_o L \implies L = \dfrac{A}{\pi d_o}
$$

$$
L = \dfrac{0.9525}{\pi \times 0.050} = \dfrac{0.9525}{0.1571} = 6.064 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{L \approx 6.07 \text{ m}}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** Outer diameter is converted from 50 mm to 0.050 m. The area formula for a cylinder is $A = \pi d L$.

**Part (b) — Computational Algorithm and Discussion:**

**Sub-part (i): Algorithm to verify the LMTD-based area:**

**Step 1 (Initialize):** Set up the known parameters in a spreadsheet or MATLAB workspace:

- Inputs: $T_{h,in} = 120$, $T_{h,out} = 60$, $T_{c,in} = 25$, $T_{c,out} = 45$, $U = 1000$, $Q = 50{,}000$ W.
- Output variable: $A_{calc} = Q / (U \cdot \Delta T_{LM})$.

**Step 2:** Compute $\Delta T_1$, $\Delta T_2$, and $\Delta T_{LM}$ using the formulas above.

**Step 3:** Set the cell/variable $A_{calc} = 0.9525$ m². Verify it matches the analytical result to within 0.1%.

**Step 4:** Display the result in a formatted report.

**Sub-part (ii): Algorithm to re-evaluate for U = 800 W/m²·K:**

**Step 1:** Change the input parameter U to 800 W/m²·K (one cell in Excel, or reassign the variable in MATLAB).

**Step 2:** Recalculate $A_{calc}$ in the dependent cell.

$$
A_{new} = \dfrac{Q}{U_{new} \cdot \Delta T_{LM}} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{800 \times 52.49} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{41{,}992} = 1.191 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3:** The new length becomes $L_{new} = 1.191 / (\pi \times 0.050) = 7.58$ m.

**Step 4:** Generate a comparison table:

| Parameter | Original (U = 1000) | Revised (U = 800) |
|---|---|---|
| Area (m²) | 0.953 | 1.191 |
| Length (m) | 6.07 | 7.58 |
| % Increase | — | +25% |

**Sub-part (iii): Discussion on necessity of iteration:**

In real heat exchanger design, the value of **U is not constant** — it depends on the **Reynolds number**, which in turn depends on the **tube count and velocity**, which depend on the **area** being calculated. A single calculation of U using assumed values is an approximation. The actual U is found by:

- (a) Assuming U, computing A and tube count.
- (b) Using tube count to compute velocity, then Re, then h_i and h_o (via Dittus–Boelter, Sieder–Tate, or Kern's method).
- (c) Using the resistance equation to compute a new U.
- (d) Recomputing A with the new U. Repeat until U converges to within a tolerance (typically 1-2%).

This iterative procedure ensures that the **final design is self-consistent** and physically realizable. Computational tools like Excel Solver, MATLAB's `fsolve`, or Python's SciPy `root_scalar` are essential because hand-iteration for 5-6 design parameters and multiple iterations is tedious, error-prone, and impractical for industrial design.

**Marking scheme (KTU pattern, 14 marks total):**
- Part (a)(i): 2 marks for correct LMTD.
- Part (a)(ii): 2 marks for correct area.
- Part (a)(iii): 2 marks for correct length with proper unit conversion.
- Part (a) overall: 1 mark for logical flow and units.
- Part (b)(i): 2 marks for clear algorithm steps.
- Part (b)(ii): 2 marks for correct re-calculation and presentation.
- Part (b)(iii): 3 marks for demonstrating depth of understanding of iteration.

### Question B (Option 2 — Internal Choice Alternative)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, 14 Marks]**
> **(a) [7 marks]** A shell-and-tube heat exchanger with **one shell pass and two tube passes** is used to heat **5 kg/s of water** from **30 °C to 80 °C** using hot oil (Cp = 2.5 kJ/kg·K) entering at **150 °C** and leaving at **90 °C**. The overall heat transfer coefficient is $U_o = 800$ W/m²·K. The correction factor is given as $F = 0.92$. Determine:
> (i) The mass flow rate of hot oil.
> (ii) The log mean temperature difference.
> (iii) The heat transfer area and the number of tubes required if **25.4 mm OD × 20 mm ID** tubes of **3 m length** are used.

> **(b) [7 marks]** A counter-current double-pipe heat exchanger has an area of **2.5 m²** and overall heat transfer coefficient of **600 W/m²·K**. Cold water enters at **20 °C** with flow rate **1.5 kg/s** (Cp = 4.18 kJ/kg·K) and hot oil enters at **120 °C** with flow rate **0.8 kg/s** (Cp = 2.0 kJ/kg·K). Using the **ε–NTU method**, determine:
> (i) The heat capacity rates and the heat capacity ratio.
> (ii) The effectiveness from the appropriate ε–NTU correlation.
> (iii) The actual heat transfer rate and the outlet temperatures of both streams.

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Sub-parts (i), (ii), (iii):**

**Sub-part (i): Mass flow rate of hot oil**

Heat duty from water side:

$$
Q = \dot{m}_c C_{p,c} \Delta T_c = 5 \times 4.18 \times (80 - 30) = 5 \times 4.18 \times 50 = 1045 \text{ kW}
$$

From oil side:

$$
\dot{m}_h = \dfrac{Q}{C_{p,h} \Delta T_h} = \dfrac{1045}{2.5 \times (150 - 90)} = \dfrac{1045}{150} = 6.967 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_h \approx 6.97 \text{ kg/s}}
$$

**Sub-part (ii): LMTD**

Terminal temperatures:
- $T_{h,in} = 150\,°\text{C}$, $T_{h,out} = 90\,°\text{C}$
- $T_{c,in} = 30\,°\text{C}$, $T_{c,out} = 80\,\text{C}$

For a 1-2 exchanger, use the **counter-current reference** for LMTD:

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 150 - 80 = 70\,°\text{C}
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 90 - 30 = 60\,°\text{C}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{LM} = \dfrac{70 - 60}{\ln(70/60)} = \dfrac{10}{\ln(1.1667)} = \dfrac{10}{0.1542} = 64.86\,°\text{C}
$$

**Sub-part (iii): Heat Transfer Area and Tube Count**

Apply the LMTD design equation with correction factor:

$$
A = \dfrac{Q}{U_o F \Delta T_{LM}} = \dfrac{1{,}045{,}000}{800 \times 0.92 \times 64.86}
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{1{,}045{,}000}{47{,}737} = 21.89 \text{ m}^2
$$

Number of tubes:

$$
N_t = \dfrac{A}{\pi d_o L} = \dfrac{21.89}{\pi \times 0.0254 \times 3} = \dfrac{21.89}{0.2394} = 91.45
$$

$$
\boxed{N_t \approx 92 \text{ tubes}}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** Q is converted from 1045 kW to 1,045,000 W. OD is converted from 25.4 mm to 0.0254 m. Tube count is rounded up to the next integer.

**Part (b) — Sub-parts (i), (ii), (iii):**

**Sub-part (i): Heat Capacity Rates and Ratio**

$$
C_h = \dot{m}_h C_{p,h} = 0.8 \times 2000 = 1600 \text{ W/K} = 1.6 \text{ kW/K}
$$

$$
C_c = \dot{m}_c C_{p,c} = 1.5 \times 4180 = 6270 \text{ W/K} = 6.27 \text{ kW/K}
$$

Identify minimum and maximum:

$$
C_{min} = C_h = 1.6 \text{ kW/K}, \quad C_{max} = C_c = 6.27 \text{ kW/K}
$$

$$
c = \dfrac{C_{min}}{C_{max}} = \dfrac{1.6}{6.27} = 0.2552
$$

**Sub-part (ii): Effectiveness from ε–NTU correlation**

First compute NTU:

$$
\text{NTU} = \dfrac{U A}{C_{min}} = \dfrac{600 \times 2.5}{1600} = \dfrac{1500}{1600} = 0.9375
$$

For a **counter-current** exchanger:

$$
\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-\text{NTU}(1-c)]}{1 - c \exp[-\text{NTU}(1-c)]}
$$

$$
\text{NTU}(1-c) = 0.9375 \times (1 - 0.2552) = 0.9375 \times 0.7448 = 0.6983
$$

$$
\exp(-0.6983) = 0.4975
$$

$$
\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - 0.4975}{1 - 0.2552 \times 0.4975} = \dfrac{0.5025}{1 - 0.1270} = \dfrac{0.5025}{0.8730} = 0.5756
$$

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon \approx 0.576 \text{ or } 57.6\%}
$$

**Sub-part (iii): Heat Transfer Rate and Outlet Temperatures**

Maximum possible heat transfer:

$$
Q_{max} = C_{min} (T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}) = 1.6 \times (120 - 20) = 160 \text{ kW}
$$

Actual heat transfer:

$$
Q = \varepsilon \times Q_{max} = 0.5756 \times 160 = 92.10 \text{ kW}
$$

Outlet temperature of hot oil:

$$
T_{h,out} = T_{h,in} - \dfrac{Q}{C_h} = 120 - \dfrac{92.10}{1.6} = 120 - 57.56 = 62.44\,°\text{C}
$$

Outlet temperature of cold water:

$$
T_{c,out} = T_{c,in} + \dfrac{Q}{C_c} = 20 + \dfrac{92.10}{6.27} = 20 + 14.69 = 34.69\,°\text{C}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{h,out} \approx 62.4\,°\text{C}, \quad T_{c,out} \approx 34.7\,°\text{C}}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The capacities are in kW/K (equivalent to kJ/s·K), so when we divide kW by kW/K, we get K (temperature difference). The outlet temperatures are added/subtracted from the inlets to give final results in °C.

**Marking scheme (KTU pattern, 14 marks total):**
- Part (a)(i): 2 marks.
- Part (a)(ii): 2 marks.
- Part (a)(iii): 2 marks.
- Part (a) overall flow: 1 mark.
- Part (b)(i): 2 marks.
- Part (b)(ii): 3 marks (careful NTU correlation).
- Part (b)(iii): 2 marks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Top 5 Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the correction factor F for multi-pass exchangers.** A 1-2 or 2-4 exchanger that uses only $\Delta T_{LM}$ (without F) will **over-design** the area by 10–25%. The examiner will deduct 2-3 marks for this.
>
> 2. **Using the wrong temperature pair for ΔT_1 and ΔT_2 in multi-pass exchangers.** Always use the **counter-current pairing** ($T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$ and $T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$), NOT the actual flow direction. Students often reverse this and get LMTD values that are 5-15% off.
>
> 3. **Unit inconsistency between Q and U.** If Q is in kW but U is in W/m²·K, the area will be wrong by a factor of 1000. Always state units explicitly: "$Q = 50{,}000$ W" and "$U = 1000$ W/m²·K."
>
> 4. **Applying the wrong ε–NTU correlation.** Using the parallel-flow formula for a counter-current exchanger (or vice versa) leads to incorrect effectiveness. Memorize at least the counter-flow and 1-2 shell-tube correlations.
>
> 5. **Rounding tube count down instead of up.** You cannot have 82.16 tubes — you must have **83 tubes** (rounded UP). The area must be sufficient to meet the duty, not less. Always round UP for area-related quantities and round DOWN for length-related capacities.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **LMTD Method** is for **SIZING** (find Area). **ε–NTU Method** is for **RATING** (find outlet temps).
- [ ] The **LMTD formula** is $\Delta T_{LM} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2) / \ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)$, with $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$ and $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$ (counter-current reference).
- [ ] The **core design equation** is $A = Q / (U F \Delta T_{LM})$. For pure counter-current, $F = 1$.
- [ ] The **TEMA limit** for the correction factor is $F \geq 0.75$; below this, redesign.
- [ ] **Effectiveness** $\varepsilon = Q_{actual} / Q_{max} = Q_{actual} / [C_{min} (T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})]$.
- [ ] **NTU** $= UA / C_{min}$. Higher NTU = higher effectiveness (closer to 1).
- [ ] **Counter-flow ε–NTU relation**: $\varepsilon = [1 - e^{-NTU(1-c)}] / [1 - c \cdot e^{-NTU(1-c)}]$.
- [ ] **Parallel-flow ε–NTU relation**: $\varepsilon = [1 - e^{-NTU(1+c)}] / (1 + c)$.
- [ ] **Tube count** $N_t = A / (\pi d_o L)$. Always round UP.
- [ ] **Overall resistance** equation: $1/U = 1/h_o + (d_o \ln(d_o/d_i))/(2 k_w) + (d_o/d_i)(1/h_i) + R_{d,o} + R_{d,i}(d_o/d_i)$.
- [ ] Computational tools are used to **iterate on U** because U depends on Re, which depends on the tube count, which depends on A — a circular dependency.
- [ ] For **single-phase sensible heat transfer**, use **Dittus–Boelter** ($Nu = 0.023 Re^{0.8} Pr^n$, $n = 0.4$ for heating, $n = 0.3$ for cooling) or **Sieder–Tate** correlation for large viscosity variations.
- [ ] **Pressure drop** must be checked against allowable limits (typically ≤ 10 psi shell, ≤ 2 psi tube) — iterative adjustment of tube count or length may be needed.
- [ ] **Standard metric units are MANDATORY in KTU exams**: Q in W, A in m², U in W/m²·K, ΔT in K (or °C, since they are equivalent in differences), m in kg/s, Cp in J/kg·K.
- [ ] **Computational tools** (Excel, MATLAB, Scilab, Python) are for **self-study, microprojects, and assignments** — they will NOT appear as direct coding questions in the ESE, but the design methodology they implement IS fully examinable.
- [ ] **Triangular pitch** allows more tubes in the same shell diameter than square pitch, but creates higher shell-side pressure drop. **Square pitch** is preferred for dirty fluids (cleaning lanes between tubes).
- [ ] **Standard tube lengths** are 8, 10, 12, 16, 20 ft (2.44, 3.05, 3.66, 4.88, 6.10 m). Choose from these for realistic design.
